experiments.title: Does the placement of a donation module on specific campaign pages impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module within content for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation module to the content section of a landing page versus adding it to the hero image. We hypothesized that adding it further down the page would allow new users to ingest our content, be motivated by it and then provide an easy option for donating at that point.
We tested this on various campaign pages—in this case campaign refers to specific areas where Save the Children operates (Ukraine, Gaza, South Sudan, etc)
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test compared the donation module's effectiveness in the hero section versus further down the page.
We hypothesized that placing the module lower on the page would give new users time to engage with the content, build motivation, and then encounter a natural opportunity to give.
Key Results:
Desktop conversion: ↑ 186% (91% confidence, not statistically significant)
 	Mobile conversion: ↓ 62% (97% confidence, statistically significant)
What Happened:
The results revealed a sharp split between desktop and mobile behavior.
On desktop, moving the donation module into the content showed a strong (though not statistically significant) lift in conversion. This suggests that desktop users may benefit from more time and context before being asked to give.
Overall, placing the donation module further down benefits desktop users but significantly reduces mobile conversions.

experiments.title: How removing the dollar ($) sign from a gift array impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe removing the dollar sign for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 07/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the dollar sign from the ask array levels on the donation form. We hypothesized that the dollar sign signaled the users brain to enter into transactional mode rather than staying connected to the emotion of the cause. Removing the dollar sign would allow the user to stay connected to the mission and would thus increase their willingness to give, potentially at a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We set out to test if the dollar sign adds friction for donors.
We thought seeing a “$” might feel transactional, deterring emotional giving. Removing it, we believed, could keep donors focused on the mission and increase giving.
What Happened:
The results told a very different story.
Instead of reducing friction, removing the dollar sign significantly hurt performance—especially on desktop. Both conversion rate and revenue per visitor dropped, in some cases dramatically.
This suggests the dollar sign isn’t a distraction. It’s actually doing something helpful: giving users clarity about what’s being asked of them.
Overall Performance:
Across all users, conversion dropped from 40.5% to 33%—a 17% decrease (52% confidence). Revenue per visitor declined even more sharply, falling 64% with 98% statistical confidence.
Desktop Performance:
The desktop saw the most dramatic impact. Conversion rate fell from 48.1% to 18%—a 62% decrease (95% confidence). Revenue per visitor dropped 75% (99% confidence).
It’s worth noting this segment had a small sample size (15 gifts), but the magnitude and consistency of the drop are hard to ignore.
Mobile Performance:
Mobile told a more mixed story. The conversion rate increased from 36.2% to 40.9% (a 13% lift), but with only 29% confidence, it is not statistically reliable.
At the same time, revenue per visitor dropped 58% (90% confidence), indicating that even when more users converted, they gave less.
One-Time Gifts:
For one-time donations, conversion declined from 32.4% to 24.2%—a 25% decrease (62% confidence). Revenue per visitor fell 69% (98% confidence), reinforcing the broader pattern: fewer gifts, and smaller ones.
Recurring Gifts:
Recurring performance showed a slight increase in conversion (8.1% to 9.1%), but with very low confidence (13%) and only 6 total gifts.
Revenue per visitor was essentially flat, decreasing just 0.97% (50% confidence). With such a small sample, these results aren’t directional.
Takeaway:
The main takeaway: keeping the dollar sign significantly improves donor clarity and response.
The dollar sign is not a barrier; it provides important clarity and guides users effectively through the donation process.
Without it, donors may feel uncertain, which can lower both participation and donation amounts.

experiments.title: Can a “Start My Gift” Button on the Main Donation Page Reduce Friction for Year-End Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the main donation page for visitors during the year-end campaign will increase donor conversion by reducing friction for highly motivated donors and allowing them to quickly begin the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/31/2025
During the year-end campaign, many donors arrive on the main donation page after clicking through from ads, emails, and other campaign touchpoints. While the page includes a detailed value proposition explaining the impact of giving, the team was concerned that the longer messaging could introduce friction for highly motivated donors who were already ready to give.
Specifically, visitors had to scroll past the page’s text before reaching the donation form, which could delay donors who simply wanted to give after responding to an appeal.
However, the team also feared that shortening the page would be counterproductive, as many visitors arrive still unconvinced to donate.
To address these concerns, we decided to test a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the donation page. The test variants were:
Control Version: visitors saw the standard donation page experience, where they would scroll down the page to reach the giving form
Treatment Version: a “Start My Gift” button was placed near the top of the page. When clicked, the button automatically scrolled the visitor directly to the donation form, allowing donors to skip the body copy and begin the giving process immediately
The goal of this test was to determine whether reducing friction for highly motivated donors would increase the donation page conversion rate during the year-end campaign. The test ran from December 19 – December 31.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall Conversion
Conversion rates were virtually identical between the two versions — 12.9% for the control and 12.8% for the treatment. Adding the "Start My Gift" button didn't hurt overall conversion, suggesting that a faster path to the form didn't undermine the page's persuasive messaging.
Revenue Per Visitor
There was no significant difference in overall one-time revenue per visitor. However, a meaningful shift emerged among larger donors: for gifts of $1,000 or more, the treatment increased revenue per visitor by 34.9% at 93.5% confidence. This suggests the button reduced friction for highly motivated mid-level and major donors who arrived ready to give and simply wanted a faster way to do it.
Monthly Sustainer Gifts
The clearest tradeoff was in recurring giving. The control generated 50 sustainer gifts compared to 31 for the treatment — a 36.8% decline at 95.8% confidence. A likely explanation: the button allowed donors to skip past the page's value proposition, reducing exposure to messaging that might have inspired a monthly commitment.
Mobile vs. Desktop
No meaningful differences emerged between devices. The button performed similarly across mobile and desktop.
Key Takeaway
Reducing friction helps unlock larger one-time gifts without hurting overall conversion — but it may come at the cost of recurring giving. Further testing should focus on whether a fast-access path can be designed in a way that preserves exposure to the monthly giving ask.

experiments.title: How the placement of a featured gift in a gift catalogue impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the placement of a featured gift for gift catalogue donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2025 - 06/03/2025

Rather than providing the user with an easy donating option, it added an additional decision point and increased the scroll length of the page..
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether the placement of that featured gift made a difference.

experiments.title: Does having a notable figure’s presence in Spring Share content drive more donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that featuring Karl Clauson for Moody Radio Slide out visitors during Spring Share will have no impact on engagement or downstream donor metrics.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/02/2026
To measure the impact of featuring Moody Bible Institute President Dr. Mark Jobe’s transition from leadership, we tested whether highlighting a different staff member would affect user engagement and downstream metrics.
The test ran for approximately 2 weeks, with traffic split evenly between the 2 variants. We tracked clicks and gifts to evaluate the overall performance and impact of each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Karl Clauson's feature on Moody Radio's Spring Share slide-out yielded encouraging early signals:
Clicks: +11.1% (59.8% confidence)
 	Transactions (all traffic): +35.3% (69.7% confidence)
Both results are positive, but do not meet the 95% significance needed for firm conclusions.
Looking Ahead:
Early signs suggest Karl Clauson's presence may boost engagement and giving. More testing is needed to confirm this.
Next steps:
Running the experiment longer or expanding the sample size to build confidence
 	Review for biases or other factors that may affect the results

experiments.title: Does Problem–Solution Messaging Increase Year-End Main Donation Page Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying and sharpening the problem statement on the year-end main donation page will increase donor conversion because clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and their motivation to give. because the clear problem–solution messaging strengthens donors’ understanding of the problem and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 11/25/2025
Recently, we tested stronger problem–solution messaging on the main donation page, clarifying families’ challenges and positioning the donor’s gift as a direct solution. This language led to a 20% increase in gifts, suggesting that clearer articulation of the problem and the donor’s role effectively boosts  motivation to give.
Based on these results, we aimed to test if applying problem–solution framing to the year-end donation page would also increase donor motivation and improve conversion rates.
Control Version: focused on the “Help Deliver Hope and Joy” theme, highlighted the matching gift, and listed ministry activities supported by donations.
Treatment Version: reframed the message to highlight the crisis families face and positioned year-end gifts as ways to restore hope, strengthen marriages, equip parents, and help children experience Christ’s love. It also reinforced the ministry’s impact and directly linked gifts to outcomes.
The test goal was to see if clearer problem framing and a stronger donor-impact message would boost conversion during this critical year-end fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the page generated 119 gifts with a 9.6% conversion rate, compared to 138 gifts and a 10.8% conversion rate on the control, representing an 11.2% decrease in gifts. However, this result did not reach statistical significance (68.4% level of confidence), meaning the two versions of the page performed very similarly overall.

The test also did not reach the required sample size of 4,807 visitors, receiving only 2,513 total visitors before it was paused. Because of this limited traffic, it is difficult to draw a definitive conclusion about the impact of the messaging change on donor conversion.

In terms of donor behavior, both versions performed similarly in generating monthly sustainer gifts (5 on the control vs. 7 on the treatment), suggesting the messaging change did not meaningfully affect donors’ likelihood to start recurring giving.

However, the test was paused after six days due to a concerning trend in revenue performance. While conversion rates were relatively close, the average gift size differed significantly:
Control average gift: $44.95
 	Treatment average gift: $24.38
45.8% less revenue
93.6% level of confidence

Control adjusted average gift: $14.84
 	Treatment adjusted average gift: $12.67
14.6% decrease in average gift

experiments.title: Does refreshing the homepage value prop with topical messaging impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value prop and a donation module for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2025 - 09/22/2025
This test was the second iteration of the value prop box on the homepage. This time, we added the donation module back to the hero image (which is directly above the box) and slightly revised the value prop copy. In the last test, the donate button was pushed below the fold, so we hypothesized that adding the donation module would solve this issue and allow for the topical value prop to be the focus for users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test was the second iteration of the value prop box on the homepage. This time, we added the donation module back to the hero image (which is directly above the box) and slightly changed the value prop copy. In the last test, the donate button was pushed below the fold, so we hypothesized that adding the donation module would solve this issue and allow for the topical value prop to be the focus for users.
Key Results:
35% non-statistically significant decline (88%) in recurring conversion rate.
 	33% non-statistically significant decline (86%) in recurring revenue per visitor. This test showed that a topical value proposition likely created urgency and relevance, making more visitors feel that giving was timely and impactful.
While urgency drove immediate one-time giving, it may have framed the donation as a short-term crisis response rather than an ongoing need. When the value prop emphasizes immediacy, donors are more inclined to act once rather than commit long-term.
Recommendation/Implementation: Given our focus on recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.
Overall performance:

experiments.title: How acknowledging listeners impacts engagement with our web asset during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading with a thank-you to Radio Player listeners will increase engagement and downstream donor metrics because it acknowledges their presence, deepens their connection to Moody Radio, and invites generosity at the moment they’re already engaged.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/09/2026
To increase engagement on Moody Radio’s player, we tested a more direct, listener-focused headline. We believed that showing gratitude upfront would build connection and boost engagement and giving.
Thanking listeners upfront recognizes their presence, reinforces their role, and prompts generosity. This approach meets the audience while they’re already tuned in, making it a good time to invite deeper support.
This test ran in the lead-up to Spring Share. We evenly split traffic across variations to ensure a reliable comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that leading with a thank-you message for Radio Player listeners did not produce a measurable lift in engagement or downstream donor metrics.
While the treatment version saw a 4.8% decrease in Content Offer CTA clicks compared to the control, the result was not statistically significant (33% confidence vs. the 95% threshold). This means we cannot reliably attribute the performance difference to the headline change.
However, looking further down the funnel provides important context. Both the control and treatment groups generated 11 gifts each. This suggests that although the thank-you message drove fewer clicks, the traffic it did generate may have been more qualified or motivated to give.
Therefore, these results should not be used to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the messaging or explore other strategies to increase engagement and donor metrics among Radio Player listeners.

experiments.title: How a donor-centric question impacts engagement on a high urgency campaign slide up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the treatment language for Moody Radio website visitors will increase clicks and downstream donor metrics because leading with a donor-focused question creates immediate emotional resonance and curiosity in the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2026 - 03/14/2026
To optimize our web assets, we tested a donor-centric question in the lower third of Moody’s Spring Share campaign. The control used our standard approach; the treatment asked, “Do you love Moody Radio?”
This test builds on proven elements we’ve already implemented, adding an additional layer of optimization focused on emotional engagement.
Our hypothesis was that the treatment would drive more traffic to the donation page because the short, donor-focused question creates immediate emotional resonance and curiosity.
By inviting listeners to respond internally before fully processing the ask, the message reduces friction and increases the likelihood of a click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the donor-focused question in the treatment drove a 15.8% increase in clicks compared to the control. However, the result did not reach statistical significance (81.1% confidence vs. the 95% threshold), so it should not be used to inform definitive decisions.

More importantly, this increase in clicks did not translate into increased giving. The treatment actually saw a 5.6% decrease in gifts (22.7% confidence), suggesting that while the question sparked curiosity and drove more engagement, it did not attract users who were ready to give.

This indicates a gap between engagement and conversion. The question may have lowered friction for clicking, but it did not sufficiently reinforce motivation to take the next step.

Moving forward, it would be valuable to continue testing donor-centric language while strengthening the connection between the initial hook and the giving opportunity. Refining the message to more clearly tie emotional engagement to impact—and testing across different placements or audience segments—can help determine whether this approach can drive not just clicks, but meaningful donor action.

experiments.title: How updating a callout with value prop on a campaign page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the value prop more topical for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2025 - 09/01/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the current value prop on the homepage from being a general organization focused to topical focused. With changes on the ground in Gaza, we wanted to test whether referencing that situation would make an impact. We hypothesized that this would help the user connect more with the organization by presenting a topic relevant and important to them.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of shifting the hero value proposition from a general, organization-wide focus to a topical, issue-specific focus. The hypothesis was that surfacing a topic personally relevant to the user would strengthen their connection to the organization and drive higher engagement.
Key Results
Overall Conversion Rate: -14% decline (65% confidence) — not statistically significant
 	Revenue Per Visitor: -3% decline (55% confidence) — not statistically significant
Insights
The results suggest that a topical focus may inadvertently alienate users who don't feel personally connected to the featured issue. Rather than a narrow topic, users appear to prefer a holistic snapshot of the organization — who we are, what we do, and the breadth of our impact. A general value prop may better serve the range of motivations users bring when they arrive.
Recommendation & Next Steps:
We plan to retest this module with two adjustments: reintroducing the donation module within the hero and refining the copy. This will help us isolate whether the topical framing itself — or the absence of a clear giving prompt — was the primary driver of the decline.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor goal countdown on a slide-up increase engagement and donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the dynamic donor goal in the campaign lower third for Moody Bible Institute homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream donor conversion because it shows visible progress toward a shared goal, which creates urgency and social proof—signaling that others are giving and that each gift meaningfully moves the mission forward right now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2025 - 12/31/2025
During Moody Bible Institute’s Year-End Campaign, we tested whether adding a donor goal to the website’s lower third would increase clicks and downstream donor conversion. The lower-third goal was calculated using the ministry’s overall revenue goal and average gift amount, then dynamically counted down as gifts were completed through the donation form.
The test ran for the full duration of the campaign, with traffic split evenly between the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic donor goal to the lower third on the Moody Bible Institute homepage showed mixed—but directionally interesting—results. While the treatment group experienced a 9.7% decrease in lower-third clicks compared to the control (42.5% level of confidence), it also saw a 5.5% increase in completed transactions (14.5% level of confidence). Although neither result reached statistical significance, the pattern suggests that the donor goal did not encourage more users to click the lower third, but it may have helped motivate those who did engage to follow through with a donation. This suggests the donor goal may be influencing donor intent and commitment rather than top-of-funnel interactions. Future testing should prioritize larger sample sizes to increase statistical confidence and yield clearer conclusions. In addition, testing alternative donor goal presentations may reveal more effective ways to communicate progress and reinforce donor motivation. While the findings are inconclusive, the directional lift in transactions suggests the donor goal has potential to strengthen conversion and merits further experimentation to better understand its impact.

This test was run before on a different form, so we wanted to see if we got the same results on different forms tied to different SMS messages.

experiments.title: How radically simplifying the email format affects engagement and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile will achieve greater immediate and downstream engagement because readers will perceive greater authenticity and trust, since the email appears to have been sent from an individual rather than a list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2026 - 02/11/2026
Luther Seminary has been sending emails without many design elements to their housefile for years, believing that the more a message looks as if it was sent by an individual, the greater the trust and engagement will be. While their standard email template does not appear to have many design elements, there is still a bit of extra white space and unconventional line spacing that could subconsciously lead a reader to associate the message with marketing.
We wanted to see if fully simplifying email formatting would boost engagement. For the test, we removed all padding. We matched font, size, and color to a typical personal email.
We determined that click-through rate would gauge impact. We also measured offer sign-ups and donations to assess how a front-end change affected downstream behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Top of Funnel: Email Clicks Radically simplifying email formatting for an owned-audience email housefile did not lead to greater immediate engagement. The treatment actually led to a slight -2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control, though the difference was not statistically significant and therefore unreliable.

Mid Funnel: Offer Sign-Ups. The control email yielded 317 offer sign-ups, and the treatment yielded 316. The radically simplified email format had no impact on downstream offer sign-ups for email subscribers.

Bottom of Funnel: Donations. The treatment email resulted in a 40% increase in donations, a 48% increase in overall revenue, and a 5% increase in average gift, compared to the control, at a 85.8% level of confidence.

Key Takeaway: While the experiment did not yield the top-of-funnel increase in click-through rate as predicted, the impact on downstream donations warrants further testing. The greater authenticity of the radically simplified design could affect donor perceptions of the sender and carry through to important downstream actions. More experiments could demonstrate a clearer link and answer additional questions, such as: How does a radically simplified email format impact engagement in other email types, such as a direct appeal or cultivation message?

experiments.title: Does including an author image improve email performance for donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we add an image of a credible Hoover fellow to the email, it will increase engagement and gifts. Recognizable and authoritative figures can increase trust and attention, making readers more likely to engage with the message and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2026
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization dedicated to advancing scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom. For this test, we wanted to see whether adding an image to an email would increase donor acquisition and engagement when promoting a free ebook. Our expected outcome was that the email with an image would result in higher donor responses and engagement than the version without an image. Everything else in the email remained the same; the only difference between the two versions was the addition of an image of Victor Davis Hanson, a Hoover fellow with strong recognition among Hoover’s audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Image)
CTR: 2.27%
 	Signups: 239
 	Donations: 31
 	Revenue: $1,525
 	Click to Donate: 9.69%
 	RPM: $108
Treatment (Image Added)
CTR: 2.13%
 	Signups: 236
 	Donations: 40
 	Revenue: $2,807
 	Click to Donate: 13.25%
 	RPM: $197.86
What we observed
CTR and signups were slightly lower with the image.
 	The image version generated:
 	9 more donations
 	Nearly double the revenue
 	Higher click-to-donate rate
Why do we think this happened?
Donors are already familiar with Hoover’s mission and the work it produces. Because of that, when the content reinforces the type of research and scholarship they support, they may be more inclined to give once they reach the page. While the image did not increase clicks, it may have reinforced credibility and relevance, leading to a higher percentage of donors choosing to give.
Next Steps
Test a Hoover or mission-focused image against an author image to see which better reinforces the work's value.
 	Test language that more clearly connects the scholar to Hoover’s mission, such as framing the author as part of Hoover’s broader research and impact.
 	Test reinforcing this connection on the donation page, helping donors see how the content ties directly to the work their support makes possible.

experiments.title: How changing copy on a button for a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing button copy to be clearer for potential monthly donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 04/29/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to clearly communicate the action and improve conversion rates. The results showed that the CTA change improved user clarity and increased conversion rates. The results showed whether this change improved conversion rates, though specific outcome data is not provided here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It is important to note that the donation sample size (17 total) was very small as we were aiming for monthly donors. This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to set appropriate expectations for the user.

Key Results:

-       +76% non-statistically significant lift (75%) in overall conversion rate.

-       +99% non-statistically significant lift (89%) in overall RPV.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have changed the CTA copy on the landing page to DONATE TODAY.

 

Desktop performance:

Control: 2.3% conversion rate and $0.76 RPV.

Treatment: 3.9% conversion rate, which is a 69% increase with a 44% level of confidence. RPV increased by 29% with a 60% level of confidence.

 

Mobile performance:

Control: 1.5% conversion rate and $0.28 RPV.

Treatment: 2.8% conversion rate, which was an 81% increase with a 69% level of confidence. RPV also increased by 167% with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How testing a premium focused donation page vs a mission focused donation page affects donor acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a missional focused funnel for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor acquisition rate because having the copy be missional focused would encourage the user to give to the overall mission.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/09/2026
Time of Grace has a new premium of the month, and they send emails to the housefile to offer them a chance to donate and receive it.
Usually, the copy on emails and donation pages focuses on the value proposition of the premium being offered. For this test, we wanted to explore a funnel focused on the overall mission of Time of Grace rather than just the premium.
We ran this test on both the email and donation pages, dividing the list into those who received the premium-focused or mission-focused versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that a mission focused funnel for Time of Grace Ministry's housefile did not increase donor acquisition rates. The treatment group actually experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control group. Because statistical confidence is below 95%, these results are not valid for future decision-making. It is essential to keep testing different strategies and funnels to find the most effective way to increase donor acquisition rates. For future tests, carefully design experiments with a clear hypothesis and ensure the sample size is large enough for valid results. Also, test a variety of messaging strategies. Use both premium focused and mission-focused funnels to see which best resonates with the audience and drives donations.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition to reflect the impact of the entire organization—rather than a single brand—affects donor conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the “One Moody” approach for donation page visitors will not change donor conversion rates because most donors support Moody’s mission as a whole rather than a single ministry, making the broader institutional language unlikely to create friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2026
To better understand the impact of consolidating the brands across Moody, we a tested more unified “One Moody” language to see how the Moody Bible Institute’s audience responded to this shift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment suggests that implementing a more unified “One Moody” approach on the donation page was associated with a 22.6% decrease in total donations across all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level was only 69.5%, well below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, indicating the results are not statistically reliable and should not be used to inform future decision-making. While overall donations declined in the treatment, the average gift increased. The control page generated an average gift of $20.50, while the “One Moody” treatment yielded an average gift of $23.79—an increase of approximately 16%. Because the test did not reach statistical significance, additional testing is needed before drawing conclusions about how unified “One Moody” messaging may influence donor behavior. Future experiments should prioritize achieving higher confidence levels and may benefit from further exploration of how branding and messaging influence donor perception, motivation, and giving behavior.

experiments.title: Does adding a credibility block to a content offer increases email acquisition?
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a credibility block highlighting WUNC’s editorial team to the short-copy landing page will increase email sign-ups (primary KPI) and increase donation conversion rate (secondary KPI) by reinforcing trust and perceived value without changing the streamlined structure that previously improved performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2026 - 02/23/2026
WUNC tested whether adding a credibility block to the winning short-copy version of the Get Out Guide landing page would impact performance. The short-copy version (problem–solution narrative, streamlined body copy, no bullets) had previously won and became the new control. The test version held all layout and copy constant while introducing a credibility block highlighting WUNC’s reporters and editorial expertise. The goal was to isolate the effect of the credibility element alone.
experiments.keyLearnings:
🧠 Overall Takeaway
This test does not provide sufficient evidence to adopt or remove the credibility block based on performance impact. The directional split between lower email acquisition and higher donation behavior suggests the block may influence downstream trust signals differently than initial sign-up intent, but the data is not statistically reliable enough to support a firm decision.

experiments.title: Does Making the “Last Name” Field Optional Increase Email Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the “Last Name” field optional on content offer sign-up forms will increase email acquisition because reducing required effort lowers friction at the moment of decision and makes the form feel simpler and less intrusive to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2026
Luther Seminary uses its Illustrated Book of Acts and Enter the Bible Exodus content offers as ongoing acquisition tools to introduce new audiences to its ministry and grow its email list. These resources consistently attract individuals interested in biblical scholarship and theological education and serve as an important entry point into long term engagement.
As the team reviewed the landing page experience for these offers, they identified a potential source of friction within the sign up form. The form required three fields to be completed: first name, last name, and email address. While collecting a full name supports personalization and database management, required fields can introduce subtle resistance, especially for individuals who are early in their relationship with the organization.
This raised an important motivational question. When someone is downloading free theological content, are they willing to provide only the minimum required information, and does requiring a last name create enough additional effort or privacy concern to reduce completion rates?
Rather than removing the last name field entirely, Luther Seminary tested making the field optional while still requiring first name and email address. This approach preserved the opportunity to collect full names from those willing to provide them while lowering the minimum amount of required information needed to complete the form.
Two split URL tests were conducted, one for Illustrated Book of Acts and one for Enter the Bible Exodus. In each case, the page experience was identical except for the required fields on the form.
Control
First name required
Last name required
Email required
Treatment
First name required
Last name optional
Email required
Because both offers tested the same structural change with similar acquisition audiences, the results were aggregated to evaluate the overall impact of reducing required form fields across content campaigns.
The primary metric was email acquisition measured by form completion rate. Secondary metrics included overall transactions, one time gifts, recurring gifts, and revenue to ensure that any improvement in top of funnel performance did not negatively affect downstream donor value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test reached 77.2% statistical confidence, which does not meet the 95% level of confidence threshold for a valid learning. Therefore, we cannot conclude that making the “Last Name” field optional had a meaningful impact on email acquisition. The observed differences between control and treatment may be due to normal variation rather than a true behavioral effect.

Email Acquisition Results

104 conversions out of 434 visitors

11 transactions out of 432 visitors

experiments.title: Does adding a donor goal countdown on the homepage for CYE increase engagement and donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the dynamic donor goal in the campaign lower third for Today in the Word’s Homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks as well as downstream metrics because it shows visible progress toward a shared goal, which creates urgency and social proof—signaling that others are giving and that each gift meaningfully moves the mission forward right now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2025 - 12/31/2025
During Today in the Word’s Year-End Campaign, we tested whether adding a donor goal to the website’s lower third would increase clicks and downstream donor conversion. The lower-third goal was calculated using the ministry’s overall revenue goal and average gift amount, then dynamically counted down as gifts were completed through the donation form.
The test ran for the full duration of the campaign, with traffic split evenly between the control and treatment experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic donor goal to the lower third of the Today in the Word homepage resulted in a 4.4% increase in Lower Third slide-up CTA clicks across all traffic. However, the level of confidence for this result was 30.4%, which falls well below the standard 95% threshold, meaning the finding is not statistically valid and should not be used to inform future decisions on its own.

The treatment group also generated an 18.7% increase in completed transactions at a 55.3% level of confidence. While this result does not validate a definitive lift in clicks or gifts, it is directionally positive and suggests that the presence of a donor goal does not demotivate the Today in the Word audience—and may help reinforce giving behavior among engaged users.

Future experimentation should focus on increasing sample size and extending test duration to improve statistical confidence and account for normal fluctuations in donor behavior.

Overall, while these results are not conclusive, the positive directional trends indicate that further testing is warranted to better understand whether a donor goal in the homepage lower third can meaningfully drive engagement and downstream donor conversion..

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to be the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept, would lead to potential confusion and friction about the message. Therefore, leading the users to the non-tribute page that had more missional language, would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2026 - 01/27/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and was tested on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the donate button links to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors led to a 7.8% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donor conversion rate, potentially reducing confusion and friction for users.
In future experimentation, it is important to note that while the results of this experiment suggest a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended 95%. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution and not used for making definitive decisions.
However, the insights gained from this experiment can guide future testing strategies. It is worth exploring further variations in the donation button links to see if similar positive results can be achieved with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, considering the significant increases in both donations and revenue from the non-tribute donation page, incorporating more missional language and context into donation pages could be a valuable strategy to enhance donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to point to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will increase the donor conversion rate because the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate. Donation buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page, and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the donation buttons on site profiles from leading to the tribute donation page to the non-tribute donation page resulted in an 8.4% decrease in donations across all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experiments, it is important to ensure the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the timing of the experiment, such as avoiding conducting tests during peak donation periods like the Calendar Year End campaign. It's also important to carefully monitor and analyze the impact of any changes on donation metrics before making broader implementations.

experiments.title: How changing the headline on a donation page to be more tangible affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headline for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because having a more tangible ask will help provide clarity to the user as to what the goal is, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 12/26/2025
As part of Time of Grace’s Calendar Year End campaign, our goal was to understand how the messaging on our donation page could influence giving behavior. Specifically, we tested whether a more tangible, outcome-oriented headline would drive higher donor conversion compared to a more general, open-ended ask.
The hypothesis was that clearly articulating the impact of a gift would make the donation opportunity feel more concrete and persuasive, thereby encouraging more visitors to complete a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: Looking at the results, there was a slight decrease in donations on the treatment version. However, looking at the revenue, there was a 141% increase in revenue to the treatment. Control - $8,605 Treatment - $21,830. This suggests that while the tangible ask didn't increase donations, it encouraged users to make larger gifts, resulting in an increase in revenue for TOG during their campaign. Therefore, this campaign was a success.

experiments.title: Does including a specific giving handle in an email appeal lead to an increase in donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a specific giving handle for Email 2 recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion  because  donors are more likely to give when they can quickly see what their generosity will accomplish.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/13/2026 - 01/13/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympic campaign, we tested several approaches to increase donation page traffic and, ultimately, drive more gifts.
In Email 2, we tested a specific giving handle paired with a clear impact statement. The email highlighted a $50 gift and tied it directly to providing 50 booklets of the Gospel of John.
The goal of this test was to determine whether IMB’s housefile audience is more motivated to give when a clear, tangible value is attached to a specific donation amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a specific giving handle in Email 2 was directionally strong, resulting in a 50.1% increase in donations across all traffic. While this lift suggests meaningful donor responsiveness to clearly defined, impact-driven gift amounts, the test did not reach statistical validation. The confidence level of 63.0% falls below the standard 95% benchmark, indicating that the result should be interpreted as directional rather than conclusive.
Even without statistical validation, the outcome provides valuable insight into IMB’s housefile audience. The performance suggests that donors may be more motivated to give when a specific dollar amount is paired with a tangible ministry outcome, reinforcing the importance of clarity and impact in fundraising messaging.
Moving forward, continued testing will be essential to more clearly identify what drives donor conversion. Future experiments should be structured to reach a minimum statistical confidence threshold of 95% to ensure learnings can be acted on with greater certainty. Doing so will allow IMB to make more informed, data-backed decisions that strengthen donation page traffic, increase gift conversion, and ultimately grow the number of donors supporting IMB’s mission.

WUNC is a public media organization serving North Carolina. This experiment tested homepage pop-up messaging for an Education 101 email series content offer. The standard pop-up was compared against a variant that emphasized the credibility and expertise of the WUNC reporter who created the series. The test was prioritized to explore whether authority-based framing could enhance email acquisition without increasing friction or disrupting the page.

experiments.title: How an exit intent with a thermometer and an exit intent with social proof affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because for the donor goal thermometer version, the image would highlight what the user could do to fill the gap, and the social proof image would show how the user could make an impact along with the other people that have already made an impact, encouraging the user to help CB meet the match.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
During their 2-day matches, CaringBridge usually has an exit intent that appears on-site profiles, featuring language that reflects the urgency of the match and how the user can help CaringBridge reach its goal.
During this particular match at the end of the calendar year, we wanted to try an A/B/C test with different exit intents that used varying visuals on the exit intent.
The first treatment, which had an exit intent, featured a thermometer image, showing how the user could help fill the gap.
The second treatment, exit intent, featured an image of people, highlighting how they could help reach the goal among the community of people who had already contributed.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices, as well as on-site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment showed an 8.2% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the visuals used in the exit intent may have had a positive impact on user engagement.

There was an increase in revenue for both mobile and desktop, which indicated that the social proof image variant was a clear winner. For desktop, there was a 19.37% increase in revenue. For mobile, there was a 24% increase in revenue.

Control (desktop) - $7,208

Treatment 1(desktop) - $7,515

Treatment 2 (desktop) - $8,770

Control (mobile) - $9,686

Treatment 1 (mobile) - $10,665

Treatment 2 (mobile) - $12,200

So the conclusion for this test is that while both variants produced an almost similar amount of donations, the variant with the social proof image encouraged users to give higher gifts, resulting in an overall increase in revenue.

experiments.title: How Meta Ad Campaign Optimization Goals Affect the Cost of Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing Meta ads for Maximum ROAS for a mixed acquisition and retargeting audience will achieve higher ROI than optimizing for Purchases, because focusing the algorithm on revenue-per-dollar spent may prioritize higher-value donors, even if it reduces overall conversion volume.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2025 - 12/15/2025
To better understand how Meta campaign optimization goals impact both giving behavior and campaign efficiency, Human Coalition tested two different optimization strategies. The control group optimized for Purchases—Meta’s default goal of maximizing the number of tracked donation conversions. The treatment group optimized for maximum Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)—Meta’s strategy that seeks to maximize the total dollar value returned for every ad dollar spent.
The team hypothesized that ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) optimization might reduce the number of conversions but increase the average gift size enough to improve overall ROI. This trade-off between conversion volume and gift value is central to the donor motivation question: Are there optimization strategies that attract higher-value donors, even at lower volume?
Both groups used the same ad creatives, daily budgets, and targeting parameters, isolating only the optimization goal as the variable under test. This test aimed to surface insights on how optimization settings shape both donor behavior and bottom-line campaign performance.
experiments.keyLearnings: Optimizing for Maximum ROAS did not outperform Purchase optimization in this campaign. While the average gift in the ROAS-optimized group was higher ($49.27 vs. $38.24), the overall performance suffered due to a sharp decline in donation volume, with the treatment group generating only 39 gifts compared to 175 in the control. As a result, the gross cost per donor more than doubled in the treatment group ($213.70 vs. $86.17), and overall ROI decreased. The treatment group saw a 2.5% decrease in Donations for All Traffic, which was not significant at a 12.0% level of confidence. Therefore, the hypothesis that focusing on revenue per dollar spent would attract higher-value donors, potentially improving overall ROI, was not supported by the data. This suggests that Meta’s ROAS optimization may struggle to scale donation volume for nonprofit campaigns, especially when the audience includes a large proportion of new or cold prospects. While ROAS optimization likely prioritized users predicted to give larger gifts, the reduced reach or conversion frequency significantly undercut overall efficiency. We learned that gift value alone cannot offset a steep drop in conversions—at least in this audience and campaign context. Optimizing for total donor volume may remain the more effective strategy when the primary goal is acquisition, particularly if follow-up engagement can increase lifetime value over time. These results prompt further questions: Could ROAS optimization be more effective with warmer audiences or higher average gift campaigns? Are there hybrid approaches (e.g., campaign-level ROAS goals with ad set-level Purchase optimization) that could better balance value and volume?

experiments.title: Can gift array preselection increase revenue for paid social content offers with persuasion-heavy messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom, relying on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that can increase revenue per visitor while maintaining healthy conversion rates across acquisition channels.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection influence conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($ 25, $50, and $100, and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This read focuses on the “Trickle Down” content offer, which typically runs on paid social and attracts a more prospect-heavy audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the “Trickle Down” offer, results were directional and not statistically valid, and performance patterns diverged from aggregate findings.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal thermometer and countdown clock influence donor conversion during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer and countdown clock to the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion, as these elements reinforce urgency and visible progress, helping donors feel both the timeliness and the collective impact of their gift at the moment of decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/25/2025 - 01/01/2026
At the tail end of IMB’s year-end campaign, we tested the isolated impact of adding a countdown clock and goal thermometer to the donation page. The test ran for approximately five days, with traffic evenly split between the two variants.
All copy and value proposition remained consistent across both pages; the only variables introduced were the countdown clock and goal thermometer. This approach enabled us to directly assess the impact of urgency and progress indicators on donor conversion, without confounding changes to messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that adding a countdown clock and goal thermometer to the IMB year-end donation page had no meaningful impact on donor conversion. While the treatment showed a 2.7% decrease in conversion rate, the result carried a low level of confidence (24.2% LOC) and does not suggest a true negative effect.

Under different circumstances, this test would have benefited from a longer run time to achieve greater statistical clarity. However, given the limited window and high stakes of the year-end campaign, extending the test was not feasible. As a result, we conclude that urgency and progress indicators alone were not sufficient to materially influence donor behavior during this phase of the campaign.
