experiments.title: Can a slide-down donation prompt outperform WUNC’s standard slide-up during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing a slide-down donation prompt against our standard slide-up for all homepage visitors would help identify whether a fresh, less familiar user experience could improve engagement and conversions during CYE because repeated exposure to the same slide-up format may be leading to creative fatigue, while a new slide-down interaction could draw renewed attention without major structural or messaging changes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2025 - 12/08/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, funded primarily through listener and reader support. As part of its 2025 year-end growth goals, WUNC focused on optimizing high-traffic homepage fundraising elements while minimizing risk during its most important revenue window.
This experiment, conducted during the CYE period, tested a modest UX variation to assess whether a refreshed interaction pattern could improve performance without altering the messaging or offer.
The team compared WUNC’s standard slide-up donation prompt, used across multiple drives, to a new slide-down version using the same gift array and copy. The test was prioritized to evaluate potential creative fatigue in the existing unit and to explore low-risk ways to diversify on-site fundraising experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The existing slide-up outperformed the slide-down on donation conversion rate, winning directionally with 78% confidence. Results did not reach conventional statistical significance, and the experiment was ended early to prioritize year-end revenue.
📊 Performance Metrics (Primary KPI)
Donation conversion rate (donations / impressions):
Slide up: 26 / 4,370 = 0.60%
 	Slide down: 18 / 4,384 = 0.41%
 	Result: Slide-up leads at 78% confidence (two-proportion z-test; not statistically significant)
✂️ Donor Type Breakdowns (Directional only)
One-time donor conversion rate:
Slide up: 17 / 4,370 = 0.39%
 	Slide down: 12 / 4,384 = 0.27%
 	Confidence: <70% (not statistically valid)
Sustainer conversion rate:
Slide up: 9 / 4,370 = 0.21%
 	Slide down: 6 / 4,384 = 0.14%
 	Confidence: <70% (not statistically valid)
First-time donor conversion rate:
Slide up: 15 / 4,370 = 0.34%
 	Slide down: 11 / 4,384 = 0.25%
 	Confidence: <70% (not statistically valid)
📊 Secondary Metrics
Average gift:
Slide up: $94
 	Slide down: $84
 	Confidence: Not statistically evaluated (insufficient data on variance)
Revenue:
Slide up: $2,450
 	Slide down: $1,520
 	Revenue differences reflect overall conversion volume rather than gift size effects.
Next Steps

experiments.title: How Using Heritage Member Survey Insights to Shape Year-End Messaging Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using Heritage member survey insights to shape more urgent, action-oriented year-end messaging in the sitewide sticky bar will increase donor conversion rates because it reflects supporters’ strongest motivations—fear of wasting this moment and a desire to lock in permanent wins through accountability—making the appeal feel more relevant, concrete, and compelling at the point of decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s calendar year-end fundraising campaign, when urgency, traffic volume, and donor intent were at their highest. It coincided with the release of new qualitative research from Heritage’s own audience, which showed deep anxiety about conservatives “blowing it” by failing to lock in recent political gains. Year-end provided a natural deadline, but the research made clear that what truly motivated this audience was not just time pressure—it was fear of losing momentum and allowing courts, bureaucrats, and future elections to reverse progress.
The sticky bar was tested sitewide on Heritage.org, ensuring persistent visibility across the full donor journey rather than limiting the message to a single landing page.
We wanted to run this experiment because the control message leaned heavily on a standard year-end frame—“give before midnight on December 31”—without fully addressing the deeper emotional drivers revealed in the research. Survey respondents repeatedly expressed fear that conservative leadership would waste a once-in-a-generation window and fail to codify wins. The treatment was designed to test whether explicitly framing a gift as a way to lock in victories, dismantle the deep state, and hold policymakers accountable would better align with those anxieties.
Finally, the sticky bar format itself made this a low-risk, high-impact test. It allowed us to introduce sharper, more militant language drawn directly from donor sentiment without rebuilding core pages mid-campaign. In essence, this experiment tested whether shifting from abstract urgency to permanence, accountability, and action would increase engagement during the most critical fundraising moment of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main takeaway from this experiment is that there was no meaningful difference between the control and treatment in overall transactions or sticky bar–driven clicks. Conversion rate remained flat at 2.4% across both variants, and while the treatment showed slight directional movement in sticky bar clicks, the difference was not statistically meaningful and did not translate into increased giving. Due to the small number of sticky bar–initiated conversions and low confidence levels, the results are not considered valid for drawing performance conclusions.
Moving forward, this test suggests that changing sticky bar messaging alone is unlikely to materially impact donor behavior during year-end, when most conversions are driven by higher-intent paths like email and direct visits. We will use this learning to deprioritize sticky bars as a primary lever for conversion optimization and instead apply survey-informed messaging insights to higher-impact surfaces—such as homepage heroes, landing pages, and email—where message framing has a greater opportunity to influence donor decisions and reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How Using Heritage Member Survey Insights to Reframe Homepage Hero Messaging Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing the homepage hero message using Heritage member survey insights to emphasize permanence, preventing reversal, and turning wins into law will increase donor conversion rates because it aligns the first on-site message donors see with their strongest motivations—fear of lost momentum and a desire for durable, enforceable outcomes—making the case for giving feel more concrete and urgent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment was ran during The Heritage Foundation’s calendar year-end fundraising campaign, when homepage traffic and donor intent were elevated across all channels. At the same time, Heritage had just gathered extensive qualitative feedback from its members revealing deep anxiety about recent conservative wins being reversed by courts, bureaucrats, and future elections. Year-end urgency was already present, but the research suggested donors were especially motivated by the idea of permanence—turning temporary victories into lasting law before political momentum fades.
The test was conducted in the homepage hero block on Heritage.org, specifically within the primary text block layered over the homepage hero image. This placement ensured maximum visibility, as the homepage serves as the front door for email, direct, and organic traffic during year-end. Because this block is often the first message visitors see, it plays a critical role in framing both Heritage’s mission and the purpose of giving during high-traffic moments.
The control homepage message emphasized broad values (“Stand for Conservative Values”) and a generic year-end match framing, which aligned with traditional nonprofit appeals but did not reflect the sharper emotional drivers uncovered in the member survey. Survey respondents expressed frustration with symbolic wins and fear that the Left would undo progress unless reforms were codified quickly and aggressively.
This experiment was designed to test whether shifting from abstract values to concrete outcomes—explicitly naming permanence, lawmaking, and dismantling the deep state—would make the homepage appeal feel more urgent, specific, and aligned with donor expectations. The treatment directly translated member language and priorities into the hero message, positioning a year-end gift as a tool to prevent reversal and secure long-term change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main takeaway from this experiment is that changing the homepage hero messaging did not meaningfully affect overall conversion rates or revenue in a statistically valid way. Conversion rate held steady at 2.4% across control and treatment, and while the treatment showed a lower CTA click rate, none of these differences reached statistical significance. Although the treatment produced a higher average gift and higher revenue per visitor directionally, the experiment did not reach the required sample size or confidence threshold, so these results cannot be treated as conclusive.
Moving forward, this test reinforces that homepage hero copy changes alone are unlikely to drive measurable performance shifts during high-traffic year-end periods, when donor behavior is heavily influenced by prior intent from email and direct channels. We will use this learning to treat homepage hero messaging as a brand- and framing-level lever rather than a primary conversion lever, and continue applying survey-informed language in contexts where it can be paired with clearer calls to action, tighter message-to-donation-page alignment, and tests that can reach validity more efficiently.

experiments.title: How Elevating Matching Gift Messaging on the Donation Page Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that highlighting the matching gift higher on the donation page would increase donor conversion rates because it would immediately reinforce urgency and perceived impact at the moment of decision, encouraging donors to complete their gift while the match was available.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment ran during The Heritage Foundation’s year-end fundraising campaign, when matching gifts were a core component of the overall appeal strategy. At year-end, donors expect urgency and leverage, and the presence of a large match was already baked into campaign messaging. The question was not whether to use the match, but how prominently it should appear once a donor reached the donation page.
At this point in the funnel, visitors had already demonstrated strong intent. The experiment tested whether elevating the match higher on the page—immediately after the headline—would increase conversions by reinforcing urgency and perceived impact, or whether it would be redundant for donors already primed to give.
The test was conducted on the donation page, specifically in the sub-headline area directly beneath the main headline. All donation page visitors were randomly assigned to either the control experience (no sub-headline; match mentioned later in the page copy) or the treatment experience, where the match was surfaced immediately at the top of the page.
Previous tests suggested that donors arriving at the donation page already have high intent, raising an important question: does additional urgency messaging at this stage help—or does it create friction? Because matches are one of the strongest traditional fundraising levers, we wanted to test whether bringing the match forward earlier would reinforce commitment and improve conversion, or whether donors would respond better to a calmer, more streamlined donation experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not produce a statistically valid result, but the directional outcome suggests that bringing the match higher on the donation page did not improve performance. The treatment showed a lower conversion rate than the control (-8.9% relative difference), while revenue per visitor was essentially flat. Although average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, this did not offset the decline in conversions.
These results suggest that for high-intent donors, surfacing the match earlier may be unnecessary—or even distracting—once they have already decided to give.
Moving forward, we will avoid prioritizing prominent match messaging at the very top of the donation page and instead continue placing match details further down the page, where they can reinforce value without interrupting the donation flow. This test supports a broader pattern across year-end experiments: at the point of conversion, clarity and simplicity outperform added urgency.
Match messaging remains a powerful lever earlier in the funnel—emails, landing pages, homepage messaging—but on the donation page itself, the focus should be on reducing friction and making it easy for motivated donors to complete their gift.

experiments.title: Can shortened, problem–solution copy increase Get Out Guide sign-ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing a shortened version of the Get Out Guide landing page for all visitors would increase email sign-ups because quickly establishing the reader’s problem, clearly presenting WUNC’s solution, and simplifying the layout would improve relevance, readability, and trust, encouraging more visitors to complete the sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2025 - 12/18/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, producing local journalism and cultural coverage supported by its audience. As part of its 2025 growth strategy, WUNC is focused on expanding its email audience through content-led offers that introduce new users to its editorial value and create long-term engagement opportunities.
This experiment tested a revised landing page for WUNC’s Get Out Guide, a content offer promoted primarily through Meta ads to both new audiences and existing followers. Building on a previous winning approach from the Education 101 series, the team prioritized this test to evaluate whether shorter, more focused copy could improve email acquisition and downstream donations without changing the core offer. The test compared the existing long-form page with bullet points against a streamlined, problem–solution–driven version designed to improve clarity, flow, and perceived relevance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The shortened copy outperformed the long version on both email acquisition volume and conversion rate, but results did not reach statistical significance. Performance was directionally positive and supports further iteration rather than immediate rollout.

experiments.title: Content Offer 5: Can adjusting gift arrays and preselection improve performance for top-performing, mixed-channel content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and depends on donor support to fund its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that can increase donor acquisition and revenue efficiency while preserving gift value across its strongest-performing content offers.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection affect conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($25/50/100 and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This read focuses on the “History of Socialism and Capitalism” content offer, which is a consistently high-performing offer and received a mix of paid channel traffic during the test period.
Note: this is a drilldown of a larger experiment (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/meR0
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the “History of Socialism and Capitalism” offer, results were directional but not statistically valid, though patterns largely aligned with aggregate program-level findings.
📊 Performance metrics
Total samples: 2,033Total conversions: 133Overall conversion rate: 6.5%Overall average gift: $38.45Control ($50/100/250, no select):Conversion rate: 6.4%Average gift: $44.27$25/50/100, no select:Conversion rate: 8.3% (+30.5% vs. control)Average gift: $31.25Revenue per visitor: $2.60$25/50/100, select 50:Conversion rate: 6.3%Average gift: $37.97$50/100/250, select 100:Conversion rate: 5.3%Average gift: $42.43
✂️ Offer and channel context
This is a top-performing content offerTraffic came from a mix of paid channelsVariants without preselection continued to show higher conversion ratesPreselecting on the lower array generated a higher average gift than the non-preselected lower arrayDespite that lift in AOV, revenue per visitor remained higher on the $25/50/100 array without preselection
The results suggest that even for strong offers, preselection can introduce friction that offsets gains in gift size. The one deviation—higher AOV on the preselected lower array—did not materially change overall revenue efficiency.
✅ Statistical validity
Results did not reach statistical significanceConfidence levels were below the 95% threshold required for validation
Next Steps
If possible, test gift array and preselection strategies within a housefile email audience to determine whether channel and donor familiarity meaningfully affect performance. Otherwise, default to the global recommendation from the aggregated Preselect/Array experiment when applying learnings to this offer.

experiments.title: How a slide-up versus a slide-down performs during a Giving Tuesday high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different slide-up/down tactic for potential donors will lead to an improvement in donation conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2025 - 11/30/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of implementing a slide‑down banner instead of a slide‑up banner during a peak giving moment. We hypothesized that the slide‑down banner would be more visually prominent, capture user attention more effectively, and increase conversion. The slide-up banner was the control in this instance, as we had previously tested it during other time periods, and it proved to be the winner.
In this experiment, the slide-up appeared after the user started scrolling down the page, and the slide-down appeared once the visitor went to the homepage, remaining at the top of the page as the user scrolled. Users could minimize the slide-down banner if they wished; even when minimized, a donation array continued to display at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results show a directional decrease in overall conversion and RPV. This suggests that the slide‑down banner may have been perceived as distracting and less visually appealing, ultimately leading to reduced conversion.

Key Results:

-       -10% non-statistically significant decrease (71%) in overall conversion rate.

-       -2% non-statistically significant decrease (54%) in overall RPV.

Overall analysis:

As show in the statistics above, the slide down treatment version had a 10% decrease in overall transaction conversion rates, with a 70% level of confidence. The treatment also had a slightly lower revenue per visitor, showing a 1.8% decrease with a 54% level of confidence.

 

Desktop versus mobile analysis:

Desktop:

Control had a 6.4% conversion rate

Treatment had a 6.1% conversion rate, which is only a 4.6% decrease with a 27% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased slightly on the treatment version, with a 10% increase and a 67% level of confidence in that result.

Mobile:

Control had a 4.6% conversion rate

Treatment had a 3.7% conversion rate, which is a 19% decrease with an 80% level of confidence. Moreover, revenue per visitor also declined on the treatment version by 39%, with a 97% level of confidence.

 

Recurring versus one-time analysis

Recurring:

Control: 0.69% conversion rate

Treatment: 0.71% conversion rate, which is a 3% increase with only an 8% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also was marginally impacted, with a 1% increase but only a 50% level of confidence.

 

One-time

Control: 4.8% conversion rate

Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 12% decrease with a 75% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also marginally decreased by 1% with a 51% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Moody Radio website radio player visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because  it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Radio Player.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for two weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment showed that adding an image to the Moody Radio Player led to a 20.6% decrease in clicks overall (77.1% level of confidence). Because this outcome was unexpected, we took a deeper look at the data to better understand what may have driven the decline.
To isolate potential drivers, we analyzed click performance by device type.
On mobile, the treatment resulted in a 12.7% decrease in clicks (48% level of confidence).
On desktop, clicks declined by 44.8% (72% level of confidence).
On tablet, clicks dropped by 52.7% (62% level of confidence).
These results suggest that the image had the greatest negative impact on desktop and tablet users, where the decline in engagement was most pronounced.
Transaction data followed a similar pattern. The control generated four gifts, while the treatment generated just one—representing a 75% decrease in transactions (82% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How a Percentage-Based Goal Thermometer Drives Donor Conversion in High-Urgency Campaigns
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a percentage-based goal thermometer to the CYE Giving page will increase donor conversion. By showing progress without emphasizing the total dollar amount, the thermometer helps donors see their gift as a meaningful contribution to a shared goal, motivating them to join in and take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s Year-End campaign, we tested adding a percentage-based goal thermometer. Previous tests with a dollar-based thermometer on giving pages showed less impact on donation lift than expected.
We hypothesized that this was due to the size of the overall goal—individual donors may have felt that their gift wouldn’t make a meaningful impact on progress.
This approach aimed to increase donor engagement, and early results indicated it was successful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is clear. Adding a percentage-based goal thermometer to giving pages increased donations by 20.8% for all traffic. This indicates that displaying progress as a percentage can enhance donor conversion rates.
However, the statistical confidence level was 75.3%, below the 95% threshold. This means the results may not be reliable for future decisions, though they are still directional. Future studies should further investigate the impact of the percentage-based thermometer, possibly by modifying its design or placement. It is also important to ensure that experiments are well-powered and have high statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact statement below the donate button affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic impact statement  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission. because  the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/02/2025
During Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test adding a dynamic impact statement below the donate button for CaringBridge on their donation pages. The statement pulled the number of donors on the donation page during the day of Giving Tuesday. The statement read as follows –
Your gift’s impact will combine with the X other donors who have also stepped forward to DOUBLE their Giving Tuesday gift alongside the $50,000 match!
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic impact statement on the donation page did not have a significant positive impact on transactions during Giving Tuesday. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 31.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
This suggests that the dynamic impact statement did not effectively encourage visitors to make a donation based on the number of donors who had already contributed. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of social proof elements on donation pages to ensure that they resonate with visitors and positively impact their decision to donate.
It is also essential to conduct experiments for a longer duration to gather sufficient data and increase the confidence level of the results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the findings. Additionally, considering different types of social proof elements or adjusting the messaging based on visitor behavior and preferences could be explored in future experiments to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a double impact slideup affects transactions during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors will increase transactions because it highlights the urgency and potential impact the visitor could have, encouraging them to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/03/2025
We have seen that slide-ups have been very successful for CaringBridge during high-urgency campaigns. For Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test a regular slide-up vs a slide-up that highlighted the chance for the user to have their gift doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a double impact slide-up for homepage visitors did not yield the expected increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 45.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making. In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the experiment's duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and sufficient time for the impact of the changes to be accurately measured. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the double impact message or explore other ways to create urgency and highlight the potential impact of donations to see if different strategies yield better results. It is also important to consider the overall messaging and timing of the campaign to ensure it resonates with the target audience.

experiments.title: Does Identity-Based Messaging Outperform Recipient-Based Messaging on a Campaign Landing Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing appeal through the lens of the donor’s identity for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because identity-based messaging increases personal relevance and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/29/2025
This test ran on Bible League Canada’s Transforming India landing page, which aimed to send Bibles to waiting believers. We wanted to explore whether donor identity-based messaging could outperform the standard recipient-based messaging.
The control version emphasized the hardship and urgency experienced by Christians in India, featuring a clear call to action. The treatment led with a direct appeal to the donor’s faith and identity. The narrative remained the same, but the message positioned the donor as an active participant in Bible League’s mission.
This A/B test ran for the month of October and was split 50/50 amongst website traffic. Visitors came from a mix of meta ads, email appeals, and organic website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

This test did not produce a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. Both versions generated nearly identical numbers of donations, with a 0.8% relative decrease in revenue per visitor. The average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, but still not enough to come to a strong conclusion. The biggest difference was the decrease in recurring gifts (5 vs 1), but this wasn't at a statistically-significant level of confidence.

Framing the landing page message around donor identity did not outperform a more traditional, recipient-focused messaging. This may suggest that:
There was not a clear enough difference between the control and treatment.
 	The visitors didn't have much of a preference between the two versions.
 	The donors' faith identity was already assumed through earlier steps in the journey
Implications for Future Experimentation:
Testing the identity-based language earlier in the donor journey
 	Combining identity with stronger urgency or social proof to create a more layered motivation
 	Identity framing language in a colder traffic segment where shared beliefs aren't assumed

experiments.title: Static image vs interactive giving element for driving donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a static image with an interactive gift impact slider for prospective donors  will achieve increased conversions because  showing tangible, real-time impact activates the principle of concreteness and gives donors a sense of agency and efficacy in their giving..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2025 - 09/02/2025
Bible League Canada (BLC) uses story-driven donation pages as part of its digital campaigns, such as DA7 “Transforming Asia,” to engage donors and invite them to place Bibles into the hands of new believers. These campaigns typically pair narrative content with strong visual imagery to create an emotional connection and drive giving.
To test whether making the donor’s impact more tangible would increase conversions, we replaced a static image on the donation page with an interactive slider showing how many Bibles a gift would provide.
Our Control used a static sidebar image of a woman with the handwritten-style caption: “A Bible for every believer.” The Treatment swapped the image for Fundraise Up’s gift array impact slider, which dynamically displayed the number of Bibles placed at each gift level (e.g., $80 = 16 Bibles). The slider was designed to give donors a sense of real-time, visible impact.
The test ran on their Transforming Asia Campaign landing page with a 50/50 traffic split. It reached primarily new donors from facebook advertising, and some warm leads from email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment version using the impact slider intuitively offered donors a clearer picture of what their gift would achieve, the test saw lower conversion performance than the control.
Due to the total sample size falling below the required threshold (3,969) and confidence not reaching 95%, the results are not statistically valid. However, directional insights are still worth reflecting on.
This suggests that, in this context, the emotional storytelling and human imagery may have been more effective at motivating giving than a purely impact-driven interface. One possible interpretation is that the slider introduced a more transactional or analytical tone, which may have weakened the emotional connection that the static image helped to establish.
Additionally, since the campaign itself already emphasized tangible impact in the copy, the slider may have been redundant rather than additive. Future tests could explore hybrid approaches that retain emotional imagery while layering in impact messaging—ensuring the donor’s experience remains both heart-driven and outcome-aware.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a tabbed form donation page affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly gift tab for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because describing what kind of impact the user could make with a monthly gift would encourage the user to give a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2025 - 10/31/2025
Time of Grace’s donation pages feature a tabbed form, allowing users to switch between one-time and monthly donations. We wanted to test adding a monthly callout to the monthly tab to see how that would affect recurring transactions.
The language described how the user could make a tangible impact by sharing Jesus’ love with people all year round through a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding a callout to the monthly gift tab on the donation page for visitors did not have the intended effect of increasing recurring donations. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 49.4% decrease in recurring donations across all traffic, with a 43.0% statistical significance level. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable.
There was an increase in the overall number of transactions.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully analyze the results and assess statistical significance before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on the findings. It is also essential to consider factors such as the potential impact of the proposed changes on user behavior and donation patterns. Additionally, conducting A/B experiments for a longer duration and with a larger sample size may help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: Does More Context Increase Email Sign-Ups for Content Offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that providing more context and credibility through longer copy—including a testimonial, bullet points, and a detailed explanation—would better persuade users to sign up for the email series by building trust and emotional resonance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/02/2025 - 10/13/2025
This test evaluated the impact of copy length on the Education 101 email series landing page, which offers readers a free series covering public education policy in North Carolina.
The long copy version included:
A quote from the author
A longer headline
Bullet points outlining what users would learn
Contextual background on the reporter and WUNC’s editorial credibility
The short copy version removed these supporting elements and focused directly on the offer. The shorter layout also surfaced the reporter’s bio above the fold on most screen sizes.
Note: While this test was part of a broader effort to improve Meta-driven performance, all traffic in this experiment came from Meta ads. That context is useful when interpreting results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
🔍 Main Takeaway
The shorter copy version generated more email signups, but the difference was not statistically significant (58% confidence). This suggests the difference was likely due to specific copy and layout decisions, not just length alone.
Since the two versions differed in multiple ways—headline, presence of a quote, bullet points, and bio visibility—we cannot isolate what caused the performance lift. This test shows that this specific short version outperformed this specific long version, not that short copy always wins.
Performance Metrics and Insights
Email SignupsControl (long copy): 387 signupsTest (short copy): 414 signupsConfidence Level: 58%Conclusion: Not statistically significantDonations from Landing Page: 14 totalDue to the low volume of gifts, the team is reevaluating the Meta audience to improve lead quality and downstream conversion. Initial indicators suggest the launch targeting may have missed the mark.
Next Steps
Refine Meta Targeting: Adjust audiences to improve quality of leads and conversion potential.Refactor Copy Strategically:
Add back bullet points or a testimonial to the short version to see if trust and clarity can be blended effectively.Highlight the reporter’s identity more clearly—especially above the fold.
Address Visual Distractions:
Test removing or modifying the left-hand sidebar, which may distract users from the form.
Form Optimization:
Explore simplifying the form fields to reduce dropoff and improve completion.

experiments.title: How clarity about donor dollars on the homepage impacts engagement and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that transparent “100% of your gift” language for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page. because  it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/14/2025
To strengthen IMB’s homepage value proposition and increase clicks to the donation page, we tested adding a statement to the homepage graphic that clearly explains where donor dollars are used.
This message about how gifts are applied was also featured on the main giving page, creating stronger congruency between the microdecision to click “Donate” and the experience donors encounter once they arrive on the giving page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using transparent language like "100% of your gift" did not yield the expected increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics. Despite the treatment version emphasizing how donor dollars are used, it actually resulted in a 13.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.
Since the statistical level of confidence is only 68.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making. Therefore, it is important not to draw conclusions or make changes based on these results alone.
However, was the treatment more effective at motivating donors?
The short answer: No.
The treatment produced a 23.1% decrease in transactions at a 47% LOC including all transactions made.
However, in order to see the most accurate results for this test, we removed all gifts over $500, and saw the treatment actually has a 60% decrease in overall transactions at a valid 95.1% LOC.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider factors such as the language used, the placement of the message, and the overall messaging strategy. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of the message or to explore other elements of the homepage that could impact engagement and donor behavior. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making significant changes is crucial to making informed decisions.

experiments.title: Does emphasizing collective impact strengthen a donor’s sense of shared mission, or does it unintentionally reduce their sense of personal responsibility to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including collective impact language in the Fall Share Email will help recipients achieve an increase in engagement and downstream conversion, as donors will be invited to see their gift as part of a larger, shared mission.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2025
In an effort to better understand what motivates Moody Radio’s audience to engage with an email and ultimately give, we tested how emphasizing collective impact influences donor behavior. Specifically, we wanted to see whether highlighting the power of “together” encourages greater involvement—or if it unintentionally reduces a donor’s sense of personal responsibility to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that using collective impact language in Fall Share Email 5 led to a 15.5% increase in click engagement across all traffic. However, the results reached only a 65.0% confidence level, which falls below the standard 95% threshold for statistical significance. As such, these findings should be viewed as directional rather than conclusive.

When examining donor conversion, the results were mixed:
Control: 10 transactions, $462 in revenue
 	Treatment: 7 transactions, $905 in revenue
While the treatment generated
fewer gifts overall
, it produced a
higher average gift size
, suggesting that collective impact framing may increase perceived value or motivation among those who choose to give.
Moving forward, continued testing is recommended to validate these initial trends. Future experiments could include
larger sample sizes
,
alternative expressions of collective impact
, or
different audience segments
to understand better how this message framing influences both engagement and giving behavior. Additionally, evaluating the
context and tone
in which collective impact language appears may help determine how best to align this approach with Moody Radio’s audience motivations and communication strategy.

experiments.title: How does transparency in communicating gift allocation affect donor conversion performance?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including explicit language about gift allocation on the Deaflympics donation page will increase donor conversion metrics, as transparency builds trust and reassures donors that their gifts directly support the mission, rather than administrative costs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2025 - 10/05/2025
To improve donor conversion on IMB’s Deaflympics donation page, we introduced more direct, transparent language about how each gift is used.
While we’ve often said, “100% of your gift goes directly to missionaries on the field,” this version takes it a step further—explicitly clarifying that no portion of these funds is used for administrative or overhead expenses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that including explicit language about gift allocation on the Deaflympics donation page resulted in a 15.7% increase in transactions across all traffic. This suggests that transparency regarding how donations are utilized can positively impact donor conversion metrics by fostering trust and ensuring that donors know their gifts directly support the mission. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 45.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for making future decisions. It is also important to note that the treatment received a $60,000 gift, which significantly skewed our revenue metric. In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to generate statistically significant results.

Additionally, considering factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as external events or changes in donor behavior, can help in designing more robust experiments. Overall, the concept of transparency in communicating how donations are used is promising for improving donor conversion rates. Future experiments should continue to explore this idea while also focusing on obtaining valid and reliable results to inform decision-making effectively.

experiments.title: How merging two appeals together impacted donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that intertwining messaging and value proposition around Dr. Carson’s birthday card and the Little Patriots appeal for ACI Housefile subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it ties the celebration of Dr. Carson directly to advancing the mission of ACI, allowing donors to honor him in a more meaningful and impactful way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2025 - 09/26/2025
During ACI’s Fall High Urgency Campaign, we included both an appeal around Dr. Carson’s birthday and an appeal to expand the Little Patriots curriculum nationwide.
To add value for the donor, we tested a combined version for the ACI housefile, weaving the ask for Little Patriots into Dr. Carson’s birthday card appeal. This allowed us to see whether a unified message could deepen engagement and increase response
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1.The treatment, which intertwined messaging around Dr. Carson's birthday card and the Little Patriots appeal, saw a 5.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level for this result was only 24.9%, which is below the 95% threshold for validity.
3. Due to the low statistical confidence level, this experiment's results should not be used for future decision-making.
4. It is essential to ensure that future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. 5. While the initial results show potential for a positive impact on donor conversion by linking Dr. Carson's work with the Little Patriots program, further testing with a larger sample size or different methodology may be necessary to confirm this potential impact.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Guide to the Constitution)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment aimed to increase completed transactions on the Guide to the Constitution donation form by adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time vs. monthly) to the submit button. The existing version of the form used a generic “Make My Gift” button, which provided no clear indication of the commitment donors were making when completing their gift. We hypothesized that this lack of clarity could create hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process.
To address this, we created an A/B test with two versions of the submit button. The control version used the existing generic button, while the treatment version explicitly included the gift type within the button text, such as “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift” depending on the donor’s selection. This approach was designed to reinforce what the donor had chosen earlier in the process and reduce any last-minute confusion before completing the transaction.
The test ran for a little over two weeks, with traffic randomly and evenly allocated between the two page versions to ensure comparable audiences and sessions.The primary metric for the test was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including transactions monthly, one-time and overall conversion rate from page views to completed gifts. By clarifying the commitment on the button itself, we expected to see an increase in donor confidence and a corresponding lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key findings of this experiment are the treatment that added gift type clarity to the submit button resulted in a -28.4% decrease in transactions compared to the control, with a confidence level of only 48%. This is well below the typical threshold for statistical significance, indicating that the observed difference is likely due to random variation rather than a true effect. Rather than improving clarity and increasing conversions, the added text on the button may have created friction or uncertainty for donors at the final step.

These results suggest that clarifying gift type at the point of submission is not an effective strategy for improving conversions and may even discourage donors from completing their gift. Future efforts to improve clarity should focus on earlier points in the giving flow—such as during gift selection or form completion—rather than on the submit button itself.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: Does impact-focused or campaign-focused language lead to more engagement and an increase in downstream metrics?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact-focused language for Moody Radio radio player visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the donors to connect emotionally to the impact of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2025 - 08/07/2025
For Moody Radio’s 48 Hours of Impact campaign, we wanted to test whether campaign-focused copy or impact-focused copy resonated more with donors. Moody has historically been very campaign-focused with their appeals, since their audience is most likely accustomed to this type of appeal. We wanted to know whether the additional value of their gift would be more impactful than traditional language.
Control: Campaign-focused
Treatment: Impact-focused
This test ran on the Moody Radio radio player for about two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact-focused language did not perform better than the campaign-focused language in terms of generating clicks for Moody Radio's 48 Hours of Impact campaign. The treatment actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Furthermore, we used custom metrics in VWO to track gifts that came from the Radio Player.

Control:
13 transactions, $1950
7 transactions, $925

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Moody Radio acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 08/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Moody Radio’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which simplified the opt-in copy and removed the form frame for Moody Radio's acquisition landing page, did not lead to an increase in email opt-ins compared to the control. The treatment actually resulted in a 5.0% decrease in Opt-in checkbox clicked for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 60.2%. Therefore, the hypothesis that reducing friction and decision fatigue would streamline the process for users and increase opt-ins was not supported by the data.

On the positive side, the experiment did show a significant increase in Revenue by 216% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while the simplified messaging did not lead to more opt-ins, it may have had a positive impact on revenue generation.

Moving forward, the insights gained from this experiment suggest that when making changes to acquisition landing pages, it is essential to carefully consider the potential impact on user behavior and to thoroughly test any hypothesis before implementing changes. Additionally, when interpreting results, it is important to focus on statistical significance levels of at least 95% to ensure the validity of the findings. Finally, while an increase in revenue is positive, it should be balanced with other key metrics such as opt-in rates to fully evaluate the effectiveness of a new approach.

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receiver’s will achieve an increase in engagement with the email because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 08/05/2025
IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. The control group received our standard typed sign-off, while the treatment group received the signature block with the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment group, which received the signature block with the sender's image, experienced a decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it is essential to carefully monitor and analyze the data to determine the impact of any changes made to the email templates. While personalizing emails with a signature block may seem like a good idea intuitively, it is important to test this hypothesis rigorously to validate its effectiveness before implementing it on a larger scale. Further experimentation with different variations of personalization elements in emails could help determine the most effective strategies for increasing engagement with the audience.

experiments.title: How adding a signature block with image in a direct appeal to the housefile impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the email signature block for housefile appeal receivers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it adds a personal touch—allowing readers to put a face to the name and therefore connect more with the ask.
experiments.summary:

IMB has been working to optimize the email templates used to communicate with their housefile, with the goal of adding more personal touches to make each message feel like it’s coming from an individual rather than the organization as a whole. To support this effort, we tested adding a signature block with the sender’s photo to a direct appeal email. The control group received our standard typed sign-off, while the treatment group received the signature block with the image.

- The hypothesis that adding a personal touch can improve donor conversion metrics was supported by the results of the experiment.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 58.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

However, we also looked at revenue by version and saw a 58.3% decrease in revenue (77.3% LOC) with the treatment.

experiments.title: How splitting the sticky slide-out donation ask into two steps impacts donor conversion on FamilyLife’s website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation ask into two steps will increase conversion rates. By first asking whether FamilyLife has provided value and then following up with a donation request, we reduce cognitive load and create a smoother, less overwhelming user experience, which will lead to higher engagement and more conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, uses a persistent sticky donation slide-out on article pages to encourage giving. The Control (current version) features a single message asking users if FamilyLife had provided them with $25 worth of valuable resources and, if so, invites them to donate. Users can select “Yes” to proceed to the donation page or “No” to close the slide-out.
We previously tested this dual-ask approach during FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, where the Treatment led to a 50.9% increase in conversions but did not reach statistical significance, achieving only a 68.9% level of confidence. Despite this, the results were promising enough that FamilyLife chose to implement it for the remainder of the campaign due to the urgency of year-end giving. Given the directional improvement, we wanted to retest this approach in a non-urgent campaign setting to further evaluate its impact.
In this experiment, we tested a modified version (Treatment) that split the question into two steps. The first step only asked if FamilyLife had provided $25 worth of value. If users clicked “Yes,” they were then shown the donation request. If they clicked “No,” the slide-out closed immediately.
We hypothesized that this two-step approach would improve conversion rates by reducing cognitive load, making the initial interaction feel less transactional, and increasing engagement before presenting the donation ask. The experiment was structured as an A/B test with a 50/50 traffic split between the Control (current sticky donate button) and the Treatment (split-question version), measuring whether this adjustment led to higher donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 30 days. The Treatment (dual ask) led to a 65% increase in donations compared to the Control (single ask). However, the result reached a 77% level of confidence, meaning it did not fully validate at the typical 95% threshold but showed promising potential.

The Control (standard sticky slide-out) generated 9 transactions, while the Treatment (dual ask) resulted in 15 transactions.

Similar to the previous test conducted during FamilyLife’s high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, we believe the dual ask improved conversions by reducing cognitive overload. By first asking a low-effort question about the value of FamilyLife’s resources, users were more inclined to engage. Those who clicked “Yes” then encountered the donation request in a way that felt more natural and less transactional. The additional step may have also given users more time to process the ask, leading to a higher likelihood of conversion.

Given the positive directional impact across multiple tests, we recommend retesting the dual ask in future campaigns to further validate its effect on conversions. Additionally, there are opportunities to explore other optimizations for the sticky donate button, such as presenting a content offer or premium or updating the copy to enhance the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky stacked navigation bar with a “Donate Monthly” button affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “donate monthly” button on a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in recurring donations because having a separate button would draw more attention to it, resulting in users clicking on the button and making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2025
For this experiment, we added a sticky stacked navigation bar for mobile, but we also added a “Donate Monthly” button to see how that would affect transactions. We had previously tested this with just a donate button, and we had seen that there was an increase in clicks to the donate button when in a stacked navigation bar. This experiment was run for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a "donate monthly" button to a stacked navigation bar for mobile users can potentially increase donations. The treatment group saw a significant increase in donations, indicating that having a separate button for monthly donations could draw more attention and encourage users to donate regularly.

However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means that these results should not be considered reliable for future decision-making.

We did see an increase in clicks to "donate monthly button". We saw a valid increase in clicks.

But ultimately, we saw a decrease in overall transactions, therefore indicating that this had a negative effect on transactions. We saw a 6.3% decrease in transactions. This was not a valid decrease in transactions.

For future experimentation, it is essential to run further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a "donate monthly" button. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donation behavior on mobile devices, such as the placement of the button, messaging, and design. Additionally, conducting usability testing and gathering user feedback can provide valuable insights for optimizing the donation process on mobile platforms.

experiments.title: How using identity-based messaging without a clear problem affected donor conversion on the main donation page

rows.name: Control - Improves Perception of the Problem

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that affirming the donor’s identity and shared values on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people are more likely to take action when it reinforces their self-concept and aligns with their core beliefs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2025 - 08/05/2025
Focus on the Family’s main donation page is a critical point of conversion—but historically, it has resisted optimization, with most tests yielding no improvement. Our goal in this series of experiments is to uncover which messaging strategies can reliably lift donor response by better aligning the value proposition with what motivates giving.
In the previous test, we validated that problem-centric copy—focused on the family crisis and the donor’s role in addressing it—produced a meaningful increase in donor conversion, particularly in recurring gifts. Building on that insight, this second test explored a different psychological lever: identity alignment.
In this A/B test, the Control used the previous winning treatment—copy that framed the family crisis in vivid terms and emphasized the donor’s role in addressing urgent cultural and spiritual problems. The Treatment, by contrast, shifted to language that affirmed the donor’s identity, values, and motivations. It positioned the act of giving not just as a solution to a problem, but as a personal expression of belief and belonging—“You believe in biblical families. So do we.”
The A/B test ran on the primary donation page for users who clicked the main “Donate” button in site navigation. Traffic was evenly split. This is the second in a planned series of tests aimed at refining how Focus on the Family frames its impact—and understanding what kind of value proposition most effectively motivates giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test offered a valuable reminder of a core fundraising principle: donors are more likely to give when they clearly understand the problem and how their gift solves it.
The identity-based Treatment helped affirm the reader's shared beliefs and values, but it failed to clearly establish why support was urgently needed. It lacked the language about critical family struggles and the donor’s role in addressing them that made the Control language so compelling. As a result, recurring donor conversion dropped by 76%, with a 94.9% level of confidence. Other results were statistically flat, indicating no measurable lift in total or one-time giving.
This suggests that psychological distance may have widened in the Treatment. Without a concrete problem to solve, the act of giving may have felt more abstract or optional—especially for recurring support, which requires higher commitment. While identity alignment can enhance message resonance, it appears insufficient on its own to motivate action on a donation page without a compelling “why give now?”
Our key learning here then is that an effective appeal should try and incorporate both identity and clarity of the problem.
Next step:
We’ll test a hybrid value proposition that combines the emotional clarity of problem perception with the motivating power of identity alignment, to see if this blended approach can drive stronger overall conversion and deeper commitment.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array for non-members on a campaign donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array for non-members will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2025
The Heritage Foundation ran their annual President’s Match campaign in July. In three of their emails to non-members, they wanted to test increasing the one-time gift amounts on the donation page to $50, $100, $250 to see if revenue would increase as well.
They changed the gift array for 50% of the traffic.
***The monthly tab’s gift array was also adjusted from $15, $25, $35 to $14, $24, $42, but we do not have the data to split apart recurring from one-time gifts, so this write up includes all transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the gift array for non-members from $25, $50, $100 to $ $50, $100, $250 resulted in a 1.4% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw a 16.8% decrease in transactions on the treatment donation page with a 64.8% level of confidence.

Therefore, while the treatment version did show a slight increase in revenue, the lack of statistical significance means that these results should not be used for future decision-making. It's important to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to validate the impact of changing the gift array on donation revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different gift amounts and messaging to see if there are ways to further optimize donation revenue. Additionally, ensuring that experiments have a sufficient sample size and statistical significance is crucial in order to draw reliable conclusions and make data-driven decisions.

experiments.title: How a two column layout impacts downstream donor metrics on a direct ask email to the housefile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two column approach for iMB housefile contacts will achieve an increase in downstream donor metrics because it offers an additional CTA to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2025
For IMB’s Ghana Training Direct Ask offer, we tested our standard one-column email format vs a two-column format that featured a couple of calls-to-action.
On desktop, the treatment was featured as two columns at about 70/30 between the main content and the additional CTAs. However, on desktop, the “second” column went below the main content of the email. Therefore, it will be imperative to look at gifts based on device type to fully understand the impact of the treatment variation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the two-column format with additional calls-to-action in the email for IMB's Ghana Training Direct Ask offer resulted in a 60.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that providing an extra CTA to give can positively impact donor engagement and conversion rates.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.5%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be solely relied upon for decision making.

These results indicate that having a secondary CTA to give on desktop did in fact increase transactions, but had no impact on mobile.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different layouts and CTAs in emails to optimize donor engagement. It would also be interesting to test our standard layout against a double CTA within a direct ask email to see if it was truly the secondary column or simply the secondary CTA to give.

experiments.title: How changing the placement of an eBook a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because By presenting the offer right away, we reduce friction and maximize clarity, especially for users in scanning mode.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Dueling Populisms”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Further Results:

Transactions:
Control: 38 donations
 	Treatment: 37 donations
 	A 2.63% decrease
Control: $1,434
 	Treatment: $1,399
 	A 2.44% decrease
The treatment showed a modest but meaningful increase (+4.4%) in email acquisition by moving the ebook offer to the top of the page. However, this result was not statistically significant. More testing needs to be done to confirm that above-the-fold visibility enhances user engagement for lead-generation offers, particularly when screen space is limited.
While email acquisition increased slightly, there was no significant difference in downstream revenue or transactions. This suggests that while placement can improve micro-conversions (like lead capture), it doesn’t automatically translate into macro-conversions (like donations or purchases). Additional nurturing may be needed.
This experiment reinforces that even small changes to content hierarchy — without altering the offer itself — can lead to improved performance. This is especially important for high-traffic, high-value landing pages like this one.
Test the size or prominence of the CTA button
 	Try shorter copy on just the mobile audience
 	Trigger a popup if the user has gone idle for a bit... to see if you can remind them to signup.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects donor conversion rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase donor conversion rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction—especially for mobile users—by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with a clear call to action like “Double My Gift” can potentially increase donor conversion rates compared to a plain-text hyperlink. While the treatment did show a 0.6% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for significance.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of small design changes such as CTA formats on user behavior and conversion rates. However, it is crucial to ensure that the statistical significance of the results is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Therefore, when conducting similar tests in the future, it is recommended to continue testing different CTA formats while ensuring a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive decisions based on the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as audience segmentation, messaging variation, and testing on different platforms can further enhance the reliability and applicability of the experiment's findings.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer as apart of a high urgency campaign affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/19/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running their annual President’s Match Challenge campaign to members and non-members of Heritage. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of an ebook offer as apart of the campaign would affect the rate of recurring giving.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
