By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Defaulting to the monthly tab on the donation page resulted in a 0.2% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic, indicating a potential positive impact on recurring conversion rates.

2. The increase in Revenue by 134% with a 100% level of confidence suggests that while the primary metric did not meet the desired level of significance, there was a significant improvement in the total amount of revenue generated.

3. The results should be cautiously interpreted due to the statistical level of confidence being below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the findings may not be 100% reliable.

4. Future experimentation should consider adjusting the duration of the experiment or increasing the sample size to ensure more robust results.

5. Despite the inconclusive nature of the primary metric, the significant increase in Revenue implies that there may be value in further exploring the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab for donation offerings.

6. It is essential to continue testing this hypothesis in future experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact on recurring conversion rates.

In conclusion, while the experiment did not provide a definitive answer regarding the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab on recurring conversion rates, the increase in Revenue suggests that there may be potential benefits to implementing this strategy. Further testing and optimization are recommended to gain a deeper understanding of the effects and to make more informed decisions in the future.

experiments.title: How including matching gift language in a high urgency campaign overlay impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including matching gift language in the EL2 overlay for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will perceive their gift as having twice the impact and feel a heightened urgency to give within the limited-time match window.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/29/2025
During the Navigators’ Eagle Lake 2 campaign, we ran an A/B test to evaluate the impact of matching gift language on donor conversion.
Specifically, we tested whether highlighting a matching opportunity in the overlay would further motivate web visitors to give.
The experiment was live for approximately three weeks, with traffic evenly split between the control (no match mention) and the treatment (match highlighted). Our goal was to determine if this added urgency and incentive would result in more gifts to support Eagle Lake.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, adding matching gift language to the Eagle Lake 2 website overlay led to an 8.3% increase in donations across all traffic. While the treatment group outperformed the control group, the statistical confidence level was only 27.8%, falling well short of the standard 95% benchmark needed to validate results.

Notably, the treatment group also saw a slight decline in revenue of 3.4%, with a confidence level of 53.6%. These low confidence levels indicate that neither the lift in donations nor the revenue change can be considered statistically significant.

The data suggests no conclusive evidence that the presence of matching gift language in the overlay had a positive or negative effect on donor behavior. As such, these results should not be used to inform future strategy or implementation.

Note: Donations over $500 were excluded from this analysis.

experiments.title: How an animated heart and monthly callout impacts giving on Non-Tribute donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated heart and monthly callout for users on the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because it draws attention the monthly option, while preserving the donor’s autonomy, then after being clicked reinforces the decision with an impact callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page (Non-Tribute), we tested an animated heart over the monthly tab, which, once clicked, would display callout copy reinforcing the impact of a recurring gift. We hypothesize that drawing attention to monthly giving, while still preserving the donor’s autonomy, will increase monthly giving. In this experiment, the monthly tab is not pre-selected and the callout is only shown after a user clicks the monthly tab—breaking the steps into two distinct actions: first encouraging the donor to select this tab, then reinforcing the action they’ve taken.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included an animated heart and a monthly callout on the non-tribute donation page, did not lead to an increase in monthly giving. In fact, there was a 15.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 72.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

It is worth noting that this same experience was conducted on both the Non-Tribute donation page as well as CaringBridge's Tribute donation page. In the latter, this experience did not increase monthly giving, but it did result in greater Tribute gifts overall, whereas here the same experience has decreased transactions overall.

This encourages further consideration around how the two donation pages are different, the diverging motivations of traffic on both to where the same experience could have a reverse effect depending on the page.

experiments.title: How does specific value prop for Youtube traffic impact conversion on main donate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “video-specific” value prop for Youtube traffic coming to the main donate page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it acknowledges where they’re coming from and focuses in on an aspect of the mission that they have experienced themselves.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2025 - 04/28/2025
For Time of Grace, we observed an increase in the amount of traffic coming to the main donate page from their YouTube channel. We hypothesized that leading this traffic to a main donate that was “video-specific” acknowledging the type of content they were coming from and focusing on this form of outreach in particular would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the "video-specific" value proposition for YouTube traffic coming to the main donate page did not increase donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 71.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

One way of understanding these results is that perhaps focusing the copy too much on one aspect of the mission, left the overall value prop less effective, indicating that users respond better to copy about video, but also the other missional outreach Time of Grace is doing.

experiments.title: How does adding a button to the May Challenge Grant banner impact donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable button with a clear call-to-action beneath the May Challenge Grant banner for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donations because it eliminates any confusion around the banner being clickable, while also giving a directive to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2025 - 05/13/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, the primary banner lacked a button and call-to-action directly beneath it. We hypothesized that, despite the banner itself being clickable, it may not be immediately obvious to users on the homepage—especially those on mobile who would not have the ability to “hover” over the banner and see it darken as clickable. We believe adding a clear button and direct call-to-action will increase donations
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a clickable button with a clear call-to-action beneath the May Challenge Grant banner did not result in an increase in donations. The treatment group saw a 12.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, however, the statistical confidence level was only 40.4%.

These results indicate that there may not be confusion or friction for users on the homepage, knowing they can click the banner itself. After this test, we instead turned our attention to the ask contained in this banner itself.

experiments.title: How does adding an image of the book in the May Challenge Grant banner impact donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an image of the book premium in the May Challenge Grant banner image for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donations because it provides a stronger visual of what the donor would receive with their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/27/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, we hypothesized that adding an image of the book/premium that donors would receive with their gift would increase the donor conversion rate versus a more general background and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of the book premium in the May Challenge Grant banner on the homepage led to a 10.5% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 40.9%, which is below the 95% threshold needed for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the banner image with the book premium to see if the results can be replicated and validated. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or any external events that may have impacted donor behavior.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is essential to conduct further testing to validate the impact of adding an image of the book premium on donor conversion rates before making any definitive conclusions or changes to future campaigns.

experiments.title: How does a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out the premium, impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out that donors will receive a gifted copy of the latest resources with their donation, for will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/27/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, we hypothesized that a sticky bar calling out the book premium available to every donor would increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar on the donation page emphasizing the book premium available to donors may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. However, the results of the experiment only showed a 3.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 20.1%, which is below the 95% threshold necessary for validity.

Originally, the motivation behind this sticky bar was to call out the premium for the reader, in a way that didn't require them to fully read the donation page. However, it's likely that donors arriving on this page from Facebook ads or the Homepage banner may have already been aware of this thank-you gift—making it a non-unique piece of information to have pinned.

experiments.title: How increasing the gift array on a new donor acquisition page affects conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher gift array for new members will achieve an increase in revenue with no decrease in conversion rate because the differentials in the arrays is not significant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2025 - 06/16/2025
The Heritage Foundation had been testing on their new member acquisition page for years. At some point, the page had been altered to have a lower gift array ($25, $50, $100) than on other pages. They knew that their data showed that donors who started with a gift of $50 traditionally had higher retention rates. They wanted to test a higher gift array, with the caveat that they might reduce conversion rates overall as a tradeoff.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a higher gift array for new members led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 331.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence was 0.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide accurate and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments for a longer duration to capture any potential fluctuations over time.

Based on the results of this experiment, it would be advisable to revisit the hypothesis and potentially retest the impact of a higher gift array on new member acquisition. It is essential to gather more robust data and ensure a higher level of statistical confidence before making any changes to the current strategy. Continuous testing and learning from experiments is crucial for making data-driven decisions and optimizing performance in the long term.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed form on acquisition donation pages impact donor conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed form for users on Time of Grace’s acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it helps remove unnecessary friction and choose how much they’d like to give after clarifying how often they’d like to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/16/2025
We’ve seen a tabbed donation form perform well for Time of Grace on their main donation page. We hypothesized that applying the same experience to their acquisition donation pages, receiving Facebook traffic for book premiums, would result in a similar increase. We believe that a tabbed form removes unnecessary friction, making it clear the type of gift a donor is making, which by extension can better clarify how much they may like to give and how often.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:The tabbed donation form on Time of Grace's acquisition donation pages resulted in a significant increase in transactions, with a 26.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, a larger takeaway is that revenue experienced a substantial 64.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This is a very promising result that shows the potential impact of the tabbed donation form on donor contributions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate the positive impact on donor conversion rates and revenue. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating learnings from this experiment, such as the design elements and user experience of the tabbed form, into future tests can help optimize the donation process and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How does setting the comment box to “Active” impact visitor comments on posts?

rows.name: Focus Comment Box when View All clicked

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting the comment box to “Active” for visitors, after they click “View All Comments” will achieve an increase in comments because it eliminates an extra step and point of friction—allowing the user to begin typing immediately..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2025 - 07/11/2025
A key metric for CaringBridge is increasing Visitor comments on an Author’s post. In the current logic, when a user clicks “View All Comments” on a user’s post, they still have to click into the comment box again to make it “Active,” ensuring they can begin typing and post a reply. We hypothesize that eliminating this extra step, updating the comment box so that a Visitor can immediately begin typing after they click “View All” will increase comments.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved setting the comment box to "Active" for visitors after they click "View All Comments," resulted in a 1.6% decrease in comments for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 69.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and the experiment did not provide conclusive evidence to support the hypothesis. The primary takeaway from this experiment is that it made no difference to the status quo.

experiments.title: How increasing specificity and personalization on the Tribute donate button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treatment copy for the call-to-action on the Tribute donate button will achieve an increase in conversions because the two options leverage either greater specificity around the kind of a gift a user is making, or personalizes with who they’re making the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/17/2025
On CaringBridge’s Tribute donation page, the Donate button’s CTA was simply: Submit Secure Donation. We developed two treatments to improve the conversion rate on this page that targeted this final call-to-action. The first, hypothesized that greater specificity: Submit My Tribute Gift would increase donor conversion—better reflecting the kind of gift the user was making. The second iteration, tested personalization of the CTA button. A tribute gift is a donation to CaringBridge made in honor of a particular person. We believe that displaying that person’s name in the donate button itself would increase conversion rate—reiterating who the tribute was being made to.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which aimed to increase conversions by either providing greater specificity in the call-to-action or personalizing the donation button, did not result in a statistically significant increase in donations. In fact, there was a 1.4% decrease in Tribute Donations with a 19.6% level of confidence. This means that the control was more effective in this case. Additionally, it's worth noting that both treatments resulted in a decrease (with statistical validity) in the average gift versus the control donate button.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page redesign impact email signups for a video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
This organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition for the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.
 	The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the control and treatment pages.
 	Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: How the placement of an ebook image on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive. because  eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2025 - 06/30/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Three Keys to Unlock the American Economy”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings & Takeaways:
Visual Priority May Not Always Drive Action - While it’s often effective to lead with a visual cue—especially for mobile users—the results suggest that immediate exposure to the ebook image did not enhance performance. In fact, conversions were slightly lower in the treatment. It’s possible that the early image displaced other persuasive copy or created a “banner blindness” effect.Perceived Value Comes From Message, Not Just Visual - This experiment suggests that copy structure and headline framing may carry more weight than visuals in persuading users to submit their email. Users might be more compelled by clarity around what they’ll gain from the ebook than the ebook’s image itself.Small Sample Sizes = Direction, Not Decision - With limited traffic and low donation volume, this test doesn’t give us statistically significant answers, but it provides a useful signal that visual-first design changes on mobile should be introduced cautiously and tested against message clarity.
Next Steps & Future Testing:
•	Test Hybrid Layouts: Consider a version where the ebook image is still visible early—but paired more tightly with benefit-driven subhead copy to retain persuasive momentum.
•	Run Similar Tests on Higher-Traffic Offers: This pattern is worth validating at a larger scale, especially on mobile-focused offers with greater reach.

experiments.title: How changing the copy above the comment box affects comments
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy above the comment box for profile visitors will achieve an increase in comments on posts because the copy options encourage users to leave a comment by describing how it would positively affect the user who posted.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2025 - 07/07/2025
CaringBridge has a feature that allows users to leave comments on posts. There is a line of copy that encourages users to leave a comment on a post. For this test, we wanted to test how changing that copy option would affect users leaving comments on posts. This was tested on site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy above the comment box on site profiles resulted in a small increase in comments left for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 46.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is a sufficient sample size and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the copy to see if there is a stronger impact on user behavior. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence user engagement, such as the design of the comment box, the visibility of the call-to-action, or the overall user experience.

experiments.title: How a plain text appeal email affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending out a plain text email that looks similar to emails in someone’s inbox for the housefile will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it would look more humanized as it would look like someone you know is sending you an email, encouraging the user to read through the email and donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/14/2025
Time of Grace sends out appeal emails every month for their new premium. They send out stylized emails with organization branding. For this test, we wanted to see how a plain text email that you see for normal emails in your inbox would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending out a plain text email for donation appeals resulted in a 33.8% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that the more personal and familiar format of the plain text email may have resonated better with recipients, potentially leading to higher engagement and conversion rates.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 55.6%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the treatment showed a positive result, it cannot be considered conclusive or reliable for future decision-making.

We saw an 18% increase in clicks with a 67% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the effectiveness of plain text emails for donation appeals with a larger sample size or different variations to see if the results can be replicated and validated with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, incorporating elements of personalization and humanization in email communications could continue to be explored as a strategy for increasing donor engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How changing the color of the call to action button on tribute widgets affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the tribute widget call to action color for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donor conversion rate because changing the color would provide more visibility to the button and provide more clarity as to what the button is for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/07/2025
For CaringBridge, their website primarily contains their purple brand color. That is the case for the call to action buttons for the tribute widgets as well. That can potentially cause the button to be blended in with the other elements on the website and not be entirely visible for users. For this test, we wanted to test how changing the button to blue would affect tribute donations. This was tested on all of site tribute widgets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the tribute widget call to action color from purple to blue resulted in a 2.8% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic, but with a low statistical confidence level of 38.3%. This indicates that the change may have had a positive impact on donor conversion, but the results are  inconclusive.
 	However, Revenue - Tribute experienced a significant 19% decrease with a high level of confidence (100%). This indicates that while the number of donations may have increased, the total revenue generated from tributes decreased.
The impact of changes on multiple metrics: While an increase in donations is positive, it is essential to also consider the impact on overall revenue. It's important to strike a balance between increasing donations and maintaining or increasing revenue.
 	Statistical confidence level: Results with a low statistical confidence level like the one in this experiment should not be relied upon for decision-making. It's crucial to ensure that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant to make informed decisions.
 	Further testing: To validate the impact of changing the tribute widget call to action color, additional testing with a larger sample size and longer duration may be necessary. This will help in drawing more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a quote from Victor Davis Hanson to the body of the email for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donor conversion. because the Hoover audience is likely familiar with and influenced by Victor Davis Hanson’s thought leadership. Seeing a direct quote may deepen their connection, reinforce credibility, and drive more meaningful engagement.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their donor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age. Given this audience’s familiarity and affinity with VDH’s work, the goal was to explore whether featuring a direct quote from Hanson in the email body would increase engagement—particularly email acquisition and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Below you can find the impact on donor conversion:

Control: 65 donations

Treatment: 56 donations

 

Here are our takeaways:
Familiarity Doesn’t Always Translate to Action - Despite the donor audience’s familiarity with Victor Davis Hanson, simply adding a quote—without new content framing or emphasis—did not improve performance. This suggests that passive recognition is not always a strong enough motivator to change behavior.
 	Subtle Changes Can Sometimes Dilute Impact - The quote was added near the end of the email, without prominent placement or context. It’s possible that the quote blended into the copy too subtly, failing to act as a clear call to action or point of differentiation. Strategic placement and visual emphasis may be critical when incorporating recognizable names or thought leaders.
 	Performance Declines Were Modest but Directional - While the treatment underperformed slightly in both acquisition and donations, the declines were not statistically significant. However, the downward trend is worth noting. It reinforces the idea that every added element must serve a clear persuasive function—especially when engaging a high-performing audience like Hoover’s donors.
• Test Placement and Framing:
• Segment Further by VDH Engagement History:
• Use Quotes in Multichannel Reinforcement:
• Explore Narrative over Quotation:

experiments.title: How an autoresponder sent from a person versus the organization impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the autoresponder coming from a person for Grace Upon Grace eBook requesters will achieve an increase in clicks because people are more likely to engage and trust personalized, human communication.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2025 - 07/09/2025
For Moody Radio, we wanted to test how an autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization versus from the organization directly would impact email engagement.
This test ran for a little over a month with traffic being split equally between the control and treatment versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization resulted in a 2.1% decrease in clicks compared to the autoresponder sent from the organization directly. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 25.9%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not conclusive and should not be used for future decision-making.

While we know that opens are no longer reliable, we did pull the results of both opens and click-to-open rates to understand the range of where each email performed.

Control: 65.48% open rate, 64.93% click to open rate

Treatment: 61.23% open rate, 67.96% click to open rate

These results suggest that the treatment email, coming from Wes Ward, resulted in a slightly lower open rate of the email, but led to a slightly higher click-to-open rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the emails, the messaging, and the audience segmentation could help to identify what truly drives engagement and clicks. It is also important to test different variables in future experiments to gain a better understanding of what resonates with the audience and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a quote from Victor Davis Hanson to the body of the email for Hoover’s nondonor audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donations because Hoover’s non-donor audience may still be familiar with or influenced by VDH’s commentary, and a quote could deepen their interest in downloading the ebook or even making a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their nondonor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age. The goal was to determine whether adding a VDH quote to the body of the email would increase email acquisitions and donations by leveraging name recognition and the intellectual appeal of Hanson’s thought leadership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Donations:
Control - 24
Treatment - 21
Resulting in a -12.5 decrease in donations, with a 61.5% level of confidence.
Key Learnings:
Consistent Pattern Across Audiences - As with the donor test, adding the VDH quote did not improve performance. In both audiences, the control yielded slightly higher email acquisitions and donations, suggesting that the quote, in this placement and form, may not serve as a persuasive enhancement.Familiarity Likely Lower for Non-Donors - While donors likely recognize VDH by name, the non-donor audience may be less familiar with Hoover’s fellows, reducing the quote’s impact. In this context, the quote may have felt disconnected or abstract, lacking the built-in credibility it holds for existing supporters.Clarity and Value Remain Key for Non-Donors - The control version’s clear structure, focused entirely on the value of the content and offer, appears to slightly outperform the version that introduced a more reflective tone via the quote. This reinforces the importance of direct, benefit-driven messaging when engaging cold or newer audiences.
Recommendations for Future Strategy:
•	Tailor Thought Leader Content to Audience Awareness - Before introducing name-based authority elements like quotes, assess whether the audience has adequate familiarity or affinity. If not, it may be more effective to frame thought leaders through introductory or contextual narrative, rather than standalone quotes.
•	Reserve Quotes for Warm Audiences or Use Them as Lead-ins - Rather than placing the quote at the end, test it at the top of the email as a hook or curiosity driver, especially for audiences that already show signs of interest in similar topics.
•	Continue Focusing on Clear Offer Framing for Acquisition - The control’s stronger performance across both experiments suggests that clear, benefit-led messaging consistently works—especially in acquisition campaigns. Personal or philosophical flourishes should be carefully tested before inclusion.
•	Future Test Opportunity - Try using a quote in the landing page or post-download email, where it might provide credibility reinforcement after the initial decision to click has been made.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in engagement and donor conversion metrics because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
We believe that using a gift array on a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment testing a gift array in the lower third footer of Moody Radio's website are as follows: The one-button version resulted in a 4.6% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was only 13.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

While we were unable to reach validity on this test due to the limited time frame, the results do tell us that Moody Radio's audience may be more motivated to give when there is only a single call-to-action button. On the other hand, the results suggest that the gift array version of the footer bar led to a higher average gift.

*Development team added parameters to custom transaction and revenue metrics based on the "src" associated with each variant.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio did not lead to an increase in clicks, as initially hypothesized. In fact, we observed a 4.2% decrease in clicks across all traffic, with a statistical confidence of 36.3%. This is well below the accepted 95% threshold for validity.

Additionally, downstream metrics showed a 4.4% decrease in transactions, with a confidence level of only 13.3%.

These findings suggest that the gift array version of the footer did not effectively drive more users to the donation page and may have resulted in less motivated traffic. While these metrics did not meet the validity criteria, they point to the possibility that there may be more effective ways to encourage donations from Moody Radio listeners beyond the gift array format.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that what may seem like a logical approach to increasing user engagement, such as allowing users to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page, may not always lead to the desired outcome. It is essential to test all changes thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically sound and reach a high level of confidence before drawing conclusions.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that he gift array version of the footer  for Moody Bible website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Bible.
This text ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for the Moody Bible website traffic showed a 4.3% increase in clicks compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.1%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid.
Furthermore, the treatment saw a 26.1% lift in transactions at a 43.7% LOC, meaning that the treatment led to more people engaging with the footer bar as well as led to more motivated traffic to make a gift.
However, the treatment actually resulted in a 20.7% decrease in revenue at a 56.7% level of confidence (pulled out of the number of transactions)
Control: 11 gifts, $3747
Treatment: 14 gifts, $3780
These results indicate that the treatment was more effective at getting people to engage and make a gift, but ultimately, the control produced a higher average of revenue per donor.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before making decisions based on the results. This means conducting the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the experiment design to improve the reliability of the results.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that the addition of a goal thermometer on the FYE donation page led to a 2.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence at 19.1%. This means that while there was a positive impact, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

However, revenue saw a substantial 400% decrease with 100% confidence, indicating a clear negative impact from the goal thermometer. These results suggest that Moody Bible’s audience may be more inclined to make smaller donations when a goal thermometer is present, potentially due to the high fundraising target set for the FYE campaign. Further testing is needed to refine these insights and draw more definitive conclusions. It may also be worth exploring the use of a daily campaign goal, which could help reduce the perceived pressure of reaching a large target and encourage more frequent contributions.

Moving forward, it is important to take into consideration the statistical significance of the results when evaluating the effectiveness of different strategies. While the idea of leveraging social proof through a goal thermometer showed some promise in increasing donor conversion metrics, further testing and optimization are necessary to draw valid conclusions. It is important to refine the messaging and design of the goal thermometer to ensure it effectively communicates progress towards a communal objective without negatively impacting revenue. Future experimentation should focus on testing variations of the goal thermometer to find the most effective approach for driving donor conversions without compromising financial outcomes.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because is creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Today in the Word’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry. Ultimately, the goal was to drive an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while the treatment version with the goal thermometer led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic, it resulted in 1.5% decrease at a 10.8%level of confidence, which is below the threshold of 95% for validity. These results indicate that Today in the Word's audience didn't respond negatively or positively to the presence of the thermometer.

However, revenue saw a 9.6% increase at a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the goal thermometer was effective at motivating donors to give larger amounts. Therefore, we should find ways to incorporate thermometers during other Today in the Word campaigns.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of the goal thermometer on revenue and donor behavior. It may also be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the results, such as the messaging and design of the goal thermometer. Additionally, conducting the experiment for a longer period of time and with a larger sample size could provide more reliable insights.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the goal thermometer can be a valuable tool for driving increased revenue and encouraging larger donations, even if it does not directly impact the number of transactions.

experiments.title: How removing gift arrays for high-value zip codes impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a gift array for potential mid-level donors will achieve an increase in average gift and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 05/02/2025
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether removing the gift array altogether on a donation form that was being used in high-value zip codes would impact conversion rates and average gift.
The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we did not see any statistically-significant impact on either conversion rates or average gift for this experiment. There was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rates across the board, with a 34% level of significance, and a 2.8% decrease in revenue per conversion, with a 54% level of significance.

When breaking down the results by desktop and mobile, we also did not see any large impact:

Desktop:

Control: 30% conversion rate, $73.79 revenue per visitor

Treatment: 29.1% conversion rate (40% level of confidence) and a 2.8% decrease in revenue per visitor (54% level of confidence).

Mobile:

Control: 20.5% conversion rate, $16.72 revenue per visitor

Treatment: 4% decrease in conversion rate (25% level of confidence) and a 6.9% decrease in revenue per visitor (60% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How moving the tribute widget in the Updates Tab affects tribute donations

rows.name: Relocate Tribute Widget in Updates Tab

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the tribute widget in the updates tab for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because moving the tribute widget would reduce the confusion of the widget appearing as a paywall before the posts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2025 - 06/17/2025
For this CaringBridge experiment, we wanted to move the tribute widget in the updates tab to be below the comments to see how that would affect tribute donations. This was similar to an experiment we had done for the single post page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the tribute widget in the updates tab below the comments resulted in a 21.6% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donation conversions.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it might be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data and improve the statistical validity of the findings.

Overall, while the initial results suggest that moving the tribute widget may have a positive effect on donations, further testing is needed to confirm these findings. It is important to approach experimentation with a careful and analytical mindset, considering both statistical significance and practical significance when evaluating the results.

experiments.title: How connecting an offer’s impact to the organizations mission impact email acquisition on a funnel landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “Connecting an offer’s impact to the organization’s mission for Note of Encouragement landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it reinforces the visitor’s sense of purpose and alignment with the organization’s values, increasing the likelihood of engagement and boosting email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/16/2025
To boost email acquisition rates on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested new landing page copy that connected the user’s action to IMB’s mission and the Great Commission. The goal was to present the act as a meaningful partnership in the gospel, rather than just a kind gesture, with the expectation that this would drive higher email acquisition rates.
By clearly linking how a simple note supports missionaries and aligns with IMB’s mission, this version gives users a stronger reason to engage and complete the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that connecting an offer's impact to the organization's mission on the Note of Encouragement landing page did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rates. Despite the hypothesis that reinforcing the visitor's sense of purpose and alignment with the organization's values would boost engagement, the treatment actually saw a 2% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.
 	It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 36.0%, well below the 95% threshold needed for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and messaging to determine what resonates best with users. It may be beneficial to explore other ways of tying the visitor's action to the organization's mission and values to see if a different approach yields more positive results. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that future experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How adding gift equivalencies to each individual ask array button impacts conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift equivalencies for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2025 - 06/12/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding equivalencies to the ask array section, where each gift amount would have a related ‘item’ that it could fund.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Adding gift equivalencies to the ask array section resulted in a 4.6% decrease in donations for all traffic.
 	The statistical level of confidence was only 40.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.
 	Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.
 	It is essential to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions from A/B experiments.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the results, such as the presentation of gift equivalencies and donor preferences.
 	Further testing with a larger sample size and adjustments to the gift equivalencies may be needed to draw more accurate conclusions about their impact on conversion rates and average gift.

experiments.title: Does reducing the amount of gift arrays from 6 to 4 impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing gift array options for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because of the reduction of friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/15/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of reducing the number of ask array options. We hypothesized that reducing the number of options would reduce the amount of friction for the user and thus increase the conversion rate. We tested this on the main evergreen donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reducing the number of gift array options on the donation form resulted in a 20.7% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is sufficiently high before making any conclusive decisions based on the results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the impact of reducing the number of options on the donation form by conducting more tests with a larger sample size to validate these findings. This will help us make more informed decisions on how to optimize the donation process for potential donors in the future.

experiments.title: The Big 5 Personality Traits effect on email fundraising
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a fundraising email for conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness will increase donor response.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/13/2025
During our sustainer invite campaign we tested the use of AI in writing fundraising copy for conservatives using 2 of the 5 Big Personality Traits. Copilot was asked to “Rewrite this fundraising email as if it was going to be sent to a conservative who is low in openness and high in conscientiousness according to the big 5 personality traits. ” The prompt did not include any ask to improve the fundraising appeal, which may have been the reason for the average results. As other testing has shown that prompting AI to rewrite an email to increase the likely-hood someone would donate has increased donations.
Here is the control:
Email 8a—few days until mid-campaign deadline
Sender: Virginia Haggerty
Subject: Deadline approaching/ Tony asked that I follow up with you

Dear <First Name>,
Tony asked me to reach out to let you know that the mid-campaign deadline for our Leaders Club membership drive is rapidly approaching.
When you activate/upgrade your Leaders Club membership today, your increased monthly gift will help protect America’s founding principles, increase prosperity for American families, and stop the leftist establishment’s sabotage of President Trump.
The sooner you act, the greater chance we have of defeating the radical Left and ushering in America’s Golden Age.
Activate/Upgrade your Leaders Club membership before the mid-campaign deadline using the secure link below.
URL

Best,
Virginia Haggerty
Assistant Director, Membership

Here is the treatment:

Subject: Deadline approaching/ Tony asked that I follow up with you
Date: 5/22/25
Dear <First Name>,
Tony asked me to reach out to inform you that the mid-campaign deadline for our Leaders Club membership drive is rapidly approaching.
By activating/upgrading your Leaders Club membership today, your increased monthly gift will help safeguard America’s founding principles, enhance prosperity for American families, and counteract the leftist establishment’s efforts against President Trump.

Virginia Haggerty
Assistant Director, Membership
P.S. Don’t miss the mid-campaign deadline. Activate/Upgrade your Leaders Club membership now to uphold your principles and stop the radical Left’s sabotage of President Trump: [URL]
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. The treatment, which was written for conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness, did not perform better than the control in terms of donation response rate.

2. The treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that the messaging may not have resonated as effectively with the target audience.

3. The statistical level of confidence was below 85%, indicating that the results are not considered valid for future decision making.

How to Use What Was Learned in Future Experimentation:

1. It is essential to carefully consider the characteristics and preferences of the target audience when crafting fundraising messaging.

2. Future experiments should include a prompt for AI to focus on improving the fundraising appeal, as this has shown to increase donation response in previous tests.

3. Conduct further experiments targeting conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness, using different messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with this audience segment.

4. Ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to generate statistically significant results, allowing for accurate conclusions to be drawn.

experiments.title: How a 3 button gift array instead of a 4 button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 3 button gift array for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions and average gift because having a 3 button gift array with the weighted option in the middle would reduce friction and the donor would more likely make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/16/2025
Time of Grace usually has 4 gift array buttons on their donation page. For this test, we wanted to test reducing the gift array to 3 buttons and see how that would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array to 3 buttons on the donation page led to an 8.8% increase in transactions for all traffic. This suggests that the hypothesis that a 3 button gift array would reduce friction and increase donor conversions may have some merit. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 46.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

Therefore, while the results of this experiment are promising, they should not be used for future decision making as they are not considered statistically significant. In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the impact of a 3 button gift array on donor conversion rates, but with a larger sample size or different experimental design to ensure more reliable and conclusive results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor preferences, messaging, and timing when designing future experiments to further optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How adding a money back guarantee statement on the donation page affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a money-back guarantee message to the donation page will increase donor conversion by reducing perceived risk and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2025 - 05/07/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
As part of their commitment to transparency and donor trust, CitizenGO has a policy that allows donors to request a refund of their donation at any time, no questions asked. While this policy has long existed, it had never been clearly communicated on the donation page itself.
We hypothesized that explicitly including a money-back guarantee on the donation page would reduce the perceived risk of donating and increase donor conversion rates. By clearly communicating CitizenGO’s refund policy, we aimed to reassure hesitant visitors and increase their likelihood of completing a donation.
This experiment was launched during CitizenGO’s monthly donation campaign titled “FR MD L Expand our MD base in [COUNTRY] 2025.” To ensure broad applicability across CitizenGO’s global audience, we tested this experiment on donation pages in the following languages: English (EN), French (FR), Italian (IT), German (DE), Spanish (ES), and Portuguese (PT).
Control: No changes were made to the donation page.
Treatment: We added the following money-back guarantee message to the donation page:
“Our Promise to You: If you ever feel that your donation isn’t making the impact you expected to defend fundamental human rights, just let us know, and we’ll refund 100% of your gift—no questions asked.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 27 days and found that overall donor conversion decreased by -9.8%, with a confidence level of 77.9%. While not statistically significant, this directional result suggests that the addition of the money-back guarantee may have introduced doubt or hesitation rather than reassurance for prospective donors. It’s possible that mentioning a refund option on the donation page raised concerns about the organization’s impact or credibility. Further testing could explore alternative placements or softer language to build trust without unintentionally undermining confidence. Here are the results split up by language:
English: Treatment saw a -3.2% relative decrease in conversion compared to control (20.0% confidence).
 	French: Treatment saw a +36.9% relative increase (57.7% confidence).
 	German: Treatment saw a -14.3% relative decrease (59.6% confidence).
 	Italian: Treatment saw a -46.4% relative decrease (90.7% confidence).
 	Spanish: Treatment saw a -15.3% relative decrease (62.5% confidence).
 	Portuguese: Treatment saw a -10.5% relative decrease (21.0% confidence).
Next Steps:
Explore softer, mission-aligned language that emphasizes donor trust without using retail-style guarantees.
 	Test different placements for trust-building language (e.g. near testimonials or impact statements rather than near the gift form).
 	Consider emphasizing positive reinforcement—such as donor impact or satisfaction—rather than offering an opt-out.

experiments.title: How reframing funnel landing page copy as a gospel partnership impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing the funnel landing page copy for “Note of Encouragement” visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it shows how a simple note strengthens the mission of IMB therefore giving the visitor a stronger reason to engage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 06/16/2025
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested into landing page copy that would frame the act as a way to partner in the gospel versus a transactional experience. We wanted their audience to feel like they were truly playing a part in the mission of IMB.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which reframed the landing page copy for "Note of Encouragement" visitors to emphasize partnering in the gospel, resulted in a 2.2% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. However, the statistical confidence level of 40.0% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the messaging and framing of the landing page copy to effectively communicate the value proposition to visitors and encourage email acquisition. A/B testing remains a valuable tool for optimizing conversion rates, but it is crucial to interpret results with caution and rely on statistically significant findings for decision making.

experiments.title: How adding a soft donation ask to an email about a survey impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a soft ask for contacts that received the FYE survey email will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/08/2025
DTS was sending their annual FYE Survey before launching their FYE campaign. We decided to test how adding a line in the email that said “After completing the survey, you’ll also have the opportunity to deepen your partnership with DTS if you choose.” would impact donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a soft ask for donations in the email sent to contacts receiving the FYE survey resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group, which included the line about deepening the partnership with DTS, saw a 95.3% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including a donation ask in communications with contacts. By conducting further tests with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical significance, we can determine more accurately if this strategy is effective in driving donations for Dallas Theological Seminary.

experiments.title: How removing the ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the processing fee option for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 05/31/2025
Many non-profit donation forms have a ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature where a donor can choose to increase their gift to cover any processing fees the non-profit might have to pay their payment providers. For Save the Children US, we wanted to test whether removing this function had an impact on conversion rates and revenue. We ran this one one of their evergreen donation forms, so the results were not influenced by any specific campaign driving traffic to that form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the processing fee option for potential donors resulted in a 5.2% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 41.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
Moreover, when reviewing overall performance based on revenue per visitor, the treatment also had a decrease (this removes large gifts), with a 37% decrease in revenue per visitor with a 99% level of confidence. This, however, makes sense, as the 'tip-up' function adds revenue to the overall gift.
Desktop:
Control: 30% conversion rate
Treatment: 24.6% conversion rate, which is a decrease of 18% with an 88% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 34% with a 95% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 14% conversion rate
Treatment: 17% conversion rate, which is a 20% increase with a 79% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor, however, deceased by 35% with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Do minimalist ads for newsletter signups improve donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that simplified, headline-style ad creative with a clear call to action would outperform a more visually complex layout in generating donations. The assumption was that complexity could reduce persuasive impact in mission-driven creative by requiring more cognitive effort to understand.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2025 - 04/25/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate whether simplified, headline-style ad creative would outperform more complex designs in Meta ads for donor acquisition. The test was inspired by recent behavioral research from the Journal of Consumer Research that found simple, minimalist advertising often outperforms complex designs. While more detailed ads may be perceived as higher effort, simplicity allows messages to be processed more fluently and emotionally, leading to higher engagement.

experiments.title: How aligning donation page copy with giving level impacts average gift during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact bullet to the donation page for a larger amount for April Eagle Lake donation page visitors will achieve an increase in average gift because the donation page copy calls out a higher impact bullet that is more in line with their previous giving levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/04/2025
Every spring The Navigators run an April Eagle Lake campaign. At the beginning of this campaign, we ran a gift array test based on the donor’s lifetime revenue, but didn’t quite see the results that we anticipated.
In an effort to get a valid learning, we tested adding an additional impact bullet to the donation page for the two higher segments of lifetime donors to try to boost their average gift. The idea behind the test is that adding a higher level of impact bullet would lead to larger gifts from a segment of people who are financially dedicated to The Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment group, which included adding an impact bullet at a higher level to the donation page for higher segments of lifetime donors, saw a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 67.3% as well as a 300% increase in transactions at an 89.3% LOC. However, the average gift for the control was $150 and $63 for the treatment. This indicates that The Navigators audience was more likely to give with the higher impact bullet amount added but they were more likely to give smaller gifts.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of emphasizing higher donation levels to significant donors. By refining the experiment design and testing different variables, we can continue to optimize our donation page and enhance donor engagement and revenue in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding an image to an email impacted clicks for a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the offer for email subscribers will achieve a higher click because there are evidentials of what they would receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/12/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV sends many emails throughout the month and so we decided to test adding an image of the resources included in the bundle.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment adding an image of the offer in the email resulted in an 11.6% increase in clicks for all traffic.

We also saw a 28% increase in donations from the treatment version.

experiments.title: How adding an image to an email impacted clicks for a nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the offer for email subscribers will achieve a higher click because there are evidentials of what they would receive. because there are evidentials of what they would receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/12/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV sends many emails throughout the month and so we decided to test adding an image of the resources included in the bundle.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of the offer in the email did not result in a higher click-through rate compared to the control group. Despite seeing a 7.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic in the treatment group, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

We also saw a 56% increase in donation from the treatment version

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency pop-up impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the overlay. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors resulted in a small increase of 0.4% in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 1.1%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

However, after further investigation, the treatment had a negative impact on revenue. The treatment saw a 35.6% decrease in revenue at a 61.8% level of confidence. While these results did not validate they do suggest that the impact callouts were negatively trending.

In future experimentation, we should ensure that we have a sufficiently large sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, we should consider refining the impact messaging in the callouts to potentially make a larger impact on donor conversion rates. It is also important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize the donation experience for our visitors.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency sticky bar impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in click rate and engagement with the pushdown because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make. because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
