experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the pushdown. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors resulted in an 18.4% increase in Clicked CTA button for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.1%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95%. These results indicate that the impact callouts did in fact get more people to engage with the pushdown.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of including impact callouts or other strategies to improve donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How changing the headline from conversational to more analytical impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the headline to a more formal and analytical headline for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition because more people will perceive the content as authoritative and intellectually valuable, appealing to readers interested in economic analysis..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2025 - 05/06/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of landing page headlines for one of their content offers, “Globalization and Its Discontents eBook. If the landing page headline is changed from a conversational phrasing “American Jobs vs. Affordable Goods: Can We Have Both?” to a more formal, analytical one “The Globalization Dilemma: Protecting Jobs vs. Lower Prices—Is There a Balance?”, then more users will perceive the content as authoritative and intellectually valuable, because the revised headline frames the topic as a balanced, data-driven dilemma—appealing to readers interested in serious economic analysis, which may increase their likelihood of submitting their email to access the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Takeaways:
Conversational Headlines May Drive Higher Engagement
The control's conversational tone ("Can We Have Both?") outperformed the more formal treatment in both email acquisition and donations—suggesting that a more accessible, human-centered approach may resonate better with visitors.
Headline Framing Has Measurable Influence on Email Signups
Although the difference in email acquisition was modest (3% decrease), the 83.9% confidence level indicates a likely negative impact from the more analytical, formal headline. Small language shifts can significantly affect user perception and action.
Donation Behavior May Be Sensitive to Perceived Approachability
The treatment saw a 14.2% drop in donation rate and a lower average gift amount, hinting that a formal or abstract headline may reduce emotional connection or urgency—key drivers of giving behavior.
Tone and Framing Should Align with Audience Expectations
While Hoover’s brand is scholarly, this test suggests users may still prefer direct, clear language that frames the issue as personally relevant rather than academic or complex.
Further Testing Ideas:
Position the eBook as a solution - change the offer description to emphasize a takeaway. Maybe something like: “A Practical Guide to Understanding Global Trade and Its Impact on You.Test the CTA "Download Your Copy Now"Test Value-Oriented vs. Curiosity-Oriented HeadlinesValue: “How Globalization Can Strengthen Both Jobs and Prices”Curiosity: “What Happens When Trade Meets Jobs? Find Out Inside”

experiments.title: How adding animation to a donation button in the navigation impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding animation to the donate button for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2024 - 02/15/2024
This test explored the impact of adding an animation to the donate button in the main navigation. The goal was to increase conversion by drawing the user’s eye to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that by adding an animation to the donate button in the navigation, it will increase conversions.
In this experiment we tested 2 different variants of animation– a ‘wiggle’ and a ‘bounce.’
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was an overall increase in engagement with the donate button, but this experiment did not validate either on conversion rate or revenue per donor.

Desktop:

Control: 3.8% click through rate, 3.1% conversion rate.

Treatment B (wiggle): 4.6% click through rate, 3.7% conversion rate (20% increase at 76% level of confidence), 4% increase in revenue per visitor (55% level of confidence).

Treatment C (bounce): 4.3% click through rate, 4% conversion rate (27% increase at an 88% level of confidence), 12% decrease in revenue per visitor (67% level of confidence)

 

Mobile:

Control: 1% click through rate, 3.2% donor conversion rate.

Treatment B (wiggle): 1.6% click through rate, 3.1% conversion rate (3% decrease at 14% level of confidence), 19% decrease in revenue per visitor (79% level of confidence)

Treatment C (bounce): 2% click through rate, 2.7% conversion rate (14% decrease at a 66% level of confidence), 20% decrease in revenue per visitor (79% level of confidence)

experiments.title: How a gift array personalized by giving level impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with lifetime revenue between $100-$499 will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it better aligns with their giving capacity therefore prompting larger gift amounts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
For The Navigators, April Eagle Lake campaign this year, we chose to test into gift arrays that correlated to the users giving level.
Therefore, for individuals with lifetime revenue between $100-$499, we split the recipients in half and tested the following gift arrays. Our goal was that prompting the individuals with a higher gift array would lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics.
Control: $25,$75,$150,$300
Treatment: $75,$150,$300,$450
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with a lifetime revenue between $100-$499 resulted in a 43.8% increase in donations for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on donor conversion metrics, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. These results did not validate, but they are directional.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a 243.9% lift in revenue at a 92.6% level of confidence. This metric did not quote reach validity, but it does show that donors were willing to make larger gifts with a higher set gift array. Furthermore, the control had an average gift of $42.50 and the treatment had an average gift of $101.67.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions, but due to the small list sizes for this campaign, we were unable to reach validity. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different gift arrays or segments of donors to better understand the impact of varying gift amounts on donor conversion metrics. By conducting experiments with higher levels of confidence and testing different variables, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal thermometer impacts email conversion rate on funnel landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the introduction of a goal amount in the copy and a goal thermometer for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it reinforces social proof and urgency on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2025 - 04/17/2025
In an effort to boost email conversions on ACI’s Landing page, we opted to test into a goal thermometer on the page to focus in on urgency and social proof of joining a community of similarly minded individuals.
We have tested this concept on different ACI funnels and seen positive results, so we were hopeful to see the same for the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which introduced a goal amount in the copy and a goal thermometer, resulted in a 0.6% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 21.9% statistical level of confidence. This means that the control version, without the goal amount and thermometer, performed better than the treatment.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results with a high level of confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test variations of the treatment to see if different approaches to presenting the goal amount and thermometer could yield more positive results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to always validate results with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an A/B experiment.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a specific gift on a gift catalogue page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing a specific gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2025 - 04/11/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of highlighting a “priority” product to the top of the gift catalogue all products page for Save the Children. The gift catalogue is something that STC has used over the years to generate support for their mission. In this treatment, we showed one product out of a possible five that we chose to highlight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by highlighting a specific gift in the gift catalogue, there was a decrease in donations for all traffic by 18.1%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 63.0%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, revenue per visitor also saw a decrease of 14%, but only with a 67% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How redesigning content blocks on the homepage impacted clicks.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning content blocks for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks on said blocks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of redesigning the content blocks on the Homepage. The goal was to increase clicks (engagement) and conversion by creating a more enticing and easier to navigate look to the content. We changed copy to be in white box overlaying image, with CTAs presented as links and highlight teal when hovering. CTA links always teal on mobile.
Our hypothesis was that redesigning the content blocks on the homepage will make them more engaging for the visitor and will lead to higher clicks and ultimately higher donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesigned content blocks on the homepage did not lead to more clicks from visitors. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control version. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

This experiment also did not impact donor conversion rates:

Control: 5%

Treatment: 4.9%, which was a 1.3% decrease with a 53% level of confidence.

 

The differences between desktop and mobile were also relatively small:

Desktop

Control: 2.4% click through rate; 5.2% donor conversion rate

Treatment: 2.4% click through rate (1.1% increase with a 21% level of confidence); 5.2% donor conversion rate (0.38% decrease, 11% level of confidence).

 

Mobile

Control: 2.6% click through rate; 4.9% conversion rate.

Treatment: 2.5% click through rate (1.6% decrease with a 34% level of confidence); 4.7% conversion rate (2.3% decrease with a 62% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How adding a wiki-style donation ask impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation header for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2023 - 12/13/2023
This test was run to validate whether or not users on engagement pages are more likely to donate when there is a donation/ask box present at the top of the page. The rationale behind the test was based on the research that shows users who are actively engaging with our content are more likely to donate when directly presented with an ask as this reduces the barrier to entry and provides the user with an easy next step to take with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donation header for potential donors led to a 33.4% increase in donations for all traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence at 75.5% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making.

When digging deeper into the results, we saw:
One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (19.4% more) but only with a 51% level of confidence.
 	Revenue per visitor was 153% higher (albeit only $0.13 per visitor) for the treatment version, with an 88% level of confidence.
 	No recurring gifts on the control, but 4 on the treatment.
 	On desktop:
 	Control had a 0.19% conversion rate with a $0.07 rev/visitor
 	Treatment had a 0.06% conversion rate, which is a 65% decrease with a 99.4% level of confidence. It also had a 9% lower revenue per visitor, with a 57% level of confidence.
 	On mobile:
 	Control had a 0.09% conversion rate, with a $0.01 revenue per visitor.
 	Treatment had a 601% increase in conversion rate (albeit, 4 gifts compared to 29, so not a valid sample size) with a 99% level of confidence. Mobile also had a 2,512% increase in revenue per visitor (but from $0.01 to $0.34) with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (standard) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Standard landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the redesigned landing page did not perform better than the original landing page in terms of email acquisition for all traffic. There was a 0.7% decrease in email signups with a statistical confidence level of 31.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the test are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that the statistical significance meets the minimum threshold before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the test. Additionally, considering other factors like user feedback, usability testing, and further iterations of the redesigned landing page could help in determining what changes need to be made to improve email signups effectively.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (lighthouse) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the organization’s logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 85% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the Control and Treatment pages.

3. Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.

4. In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: Does reducing friction on a premium landing page affect conversion rate for Meta Ads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that removing premium-related fields and focusing the page experience around the donation itself would reduce friction and increase conversion rates and cost efficiency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate whether reducing friction on a landing page would improve donor conversion. The test was run through Meta ads that featured a premium mug. The control ad linked to a general donation page, while the test ad linked to a simplified landing page that included an image of the mug but removed fields and copy related to premiums.

experiments.title: How simplifying copy on a ways to give page impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/07/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
The ‘ways to give’ page is a popular page on PTV’s website, and it traditionally offers different methods where supporters of PTV can donate.
In this experiment, we simplified the amount of copy underneath each ‘box’ that promoted different ways to give, with the goal of reducing friction and increasing conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the copy underneath each 'box' on the 'ways to give' page led to a 2.1% increase in transactions for all traffic. While this result shows a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence was only 19.6%, which is too low to consider the results valid for future decision making.

Moreover, the number of clicks on each variant was flat, while time on page was 45% longer on the control version. Despite this longer time spent on page, the conversion rate was less, indicating that people that are visiting that page are already doing so with the intent to donate.

experiments.title: Does a visually disruptive donation ask generate more donations than a contextually integrated one?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that placing the donation ask at the end of the article, with visual contrast and a gift array, would increase donor conversion because users would be more likely to consider donating after consuming the article content and be more inclined to act with a clearer path to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the effectiveness of in-article versus end-of-article donation asks on article pages. The “Dear Reader” style messages were designed to prompt donations in a low-friction, editorial-style format.

experiments.title: How using an open amount field on a donation form impacts mid-level conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for potential mid level donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to see whether mid-level donors would respond more to a donation form with an open field instead of the standard gift array. The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for mid-level donors did not result in an increase in conversion rates and average gift as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in donations with a statistical confidence level of 34.5%, indicating that these results are not reliable or valid for decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequately large to increase the statistical confidence level. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of donation forms, including those with ask array amounts and open fields, to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations from mid-level donors.

Overall, it is crucial to interpret results carefully and avoid making decisions based on inconclusive data. Further testing and refinement of donation forms may yield more reliable insights for optimizing fundraising efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How does the placement of an employer match widget impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro ask for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2024 - 07/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into the employer match component on donation forms. Our hypothesis was that adding a micro-ask decision to the donation form for the employer match section will increase revenue per visitor and overall matching funds. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling. Incorporating a micro-ask can make users more involved and attentive toward the employer match.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into the employer match component on donation forms. Key results from the analysis include:

+4% non-statistically significant lift (68%) in overall conversion rate.

+9% non-statistically significant lift (87%) in overall revenue per visitor.

Directionally, we saw a lift in overall performance, but more importantly, we do not see a negative impact. We will be implementing this feature across all donation forms.

Results in detail:

Desktop:

Control: 14.5% conversion rate. Revenue per visitor of $20.06.

Treatment: 16.2% conversion rate, 96.5% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor of $19.79, a decrease of 1.3% with a 55% level of confidence.

 

Mobile:

Desktop: 7.1% conversion rate, $4.13 revenue per visitor.

Treatment: 6.8% conversion rate, $5.25 revenue per visitor, an increase of 27% with a 98% level of confidence

experiments.title: How the placement of resources on a ‘listen now’ page impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the most recent broadcasts for those looking to catch up will achieve a change in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/07/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to listen past sermons and TV broadcasts.
Building upon the initial test of a grid layout, in this version we wanted to test whether moving the positioning of the resource of the month, as well as the station locator, impacted engagement with the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that moving the positioning of the resource of the month and station locator on the Pathway to Victory website did not have a significant impact on engagement with the page. Although the treatment group saw a 7.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
One of the goals of this experiment was to see if moving the featured resource in-line with the top grid, rather than a big call-out, led to more gifts. Overall, the treatment version had 3 more gifts in total, which is a 32% increase in conversion rates, but only at a 50% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding other ways to give for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2024 - 09/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of adding additional ways to give, specifically Crypto, on donation forms. We added some language to the bottom of the form, under the ‘donate’ button, that gave donors links to be able to give through their preferred vehicle.
Our hypothesis was that adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form will make it easier for people to donate in their preferred method. This will decrease friction for the users looking for these options without impacting the overall conversion of the donation form for all other users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding additional ways to give at the bottom of a donation form did not result in the intended outcome of increasing conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical confidence level was not high enough to draw definitive conclusions.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of adding new elements to a donation form and to carefully assess whether these changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and conclusive results.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to conduct further research and testing to better understand the impact of offering different ways to donate on conversion rates. This could include gathering feedback from donors, conducting surveys, and running additional A/B tests with a larger sample size and more rigorous statistical analysis.

experiments.title: Does adding options to donate by crypto and DAF impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding crypto and DAF giving options for donation page visitors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2024 - 12/02/2024
This test explored the impact of adding DAF and Crypto buttons to the donation form. The goal was to increase the use of these payment methods without detracting from regular form conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding crypto and DAF giving options to the donation page did not have a significant impact on conversion rates. The treatment actually saw a slight decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 44.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid for making future decisions. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to provide reliable results and that the statistical significance threshold is met before drawing conclusions. It may also be worth exploring other variables or factors that could potentially impact donation conversion rates and testing them in a controlled manner to determine their effectiveness.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor countdown on the donation page help conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown on a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page will achieve more transactions because the donor countdown will provide extra social proof, showing that others are doanting too. It also gives an exact number needed which can lead into exclusivity and ultimately increasing motivation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio just wrapped up their annual Spring Share campaign.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if we could include a donor countdown on the main campaign donation page to reinforce the need and provide extra motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the donor countdown on a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing transactions. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.5% decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 54.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the design of the donor countdown feature before drawing any conclusions. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the campaign, messaging, or external events. Additionally, incorporating qualitative feedback from donors and testing different variations of the donor countdown feature can provide valuable insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: Will adding a timer on the donation page increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign will achieve more transactions because because the added element will increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio recently wrapped up their Annual Spring Share campaign.
This campaign drives a lot of traffic to the donation pages from on-air, paid social and paid search, as well as organic. This campaign is about a month long which can seem like a long time for a donor to have the opportunity to give to where they can push it off.
We wanted to see if we could increase the urgency to give on the donation page instead of waiting by adding a countdown time to the end of the campaign. To make sure this was the most effective, we waited to launch this until the last 4 days of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign did result in a 6.7% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.8%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficiently large to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to capture more data and allow for more conclusive findings.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to take caution in interpreting the results and to continue conducting rigorous experiments to drive decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: Is increasing urgency the best way to convert donors on a lower third?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer on the lower third banner for website content pages will achieve more transactions because adding the countdown timer to the end of the campaign will increase the urgency for a user to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio just concluded its annual Spring Share campaign.
During this campaign, we tested adding several elements across all parts of their website.
For this specific test, we wanted to see if we could increase the urgency on the lower third banner that shows on the content pages of moodyradio.org
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a countdown timer on the lower third banner for website content pages did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donations during the Spring Share campaign. The treatment actually resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, which indicates that the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on user engagement and conversion.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 57.6%, which means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making. It is crucial to always ensure a high level of confidence (at least 95%) in the results of A/B experiments before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on those results.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully design and implement A/B tests with a focus on achieving a high level of statistical confidence. It may also be beneficial to consider conducting qualitative research or usability testing to gather more insights into user behavior and preferences before implementing changes on the website. This will help ensure that any future experiments are more impactful and effective in achieving the desired goals.

experiments.title: How adding in more copy on a recurring donation page affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in more copy for recurring donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the copy will provide some clarity about why they should donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 03/27/2025
For this test, we tested adding in some more copy on the monthly donation page as a way to provide more clarity and more value proposition to the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more copy to the recurring donation page did not have a positive impact on donation conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level below 95%. Therefore, we cannot draw reliable conclusions from these findings.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing hypotheses and variations on donation pages, but with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure more valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider qualitative feedback from users to understand their preferences and motivations when it comes to donating.

experiments.title: How a suggested gift amount on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a suggested gift amount callout for Easter Card donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics, primarily average gift because  it sets a clear expectation in the visitor’s mind about the suggested donation amount and reinforces the value of the Easter Cards they will receive. because   it sets a clear expectation in the visitor’s mind about the suggested donation amount and reinforces the value of the Easter Cards they will receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2025 - 04/01/2025
The Navigators ran an Easter card offer in Facebook this spring. The cards were free for requesters but there was a donation ask after the landing page. In an effort to boost our average gift on the donation page, we tested adding a minimum amount callout to the donation page copy.
The treatment included one sentence that said ” Would you consider making a $25 gift to the work of The Navigators today?” No other changes were made to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a suggested gift amount callout to the donation page resulted in a 55.7% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. This indicates that providing a specific donation amount suggestion can have a positive impact on donor conversion metrics.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 46.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these findings should not be used for future decision making.
While this test did not have the chance to validate, it does suggest that the callout increased both transactions, revenue and in turn, average gift. The average gift on the control was $6 and the average gift on the treatment was $17. This suggests that the callout helped visitors better understand the value of the cards before making their gift.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of suggested donation amounts on donor conversion metrics, but with a larger sample size and for a longer duration to ensure statistically significant results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the suggested amount callout and its placement on the donation page could provide further insights into its effectiveness in increasing average gift amount.

experiments.title: Will a personalized ask on a dear reader convert users better?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personal ask to the dear reader for the content pages during Spring Share will achieve more transactions because the user will be able to connect the ask with a person.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2025
Moody Radio hosts a Spring Share Fundraiser each year where a ton of traffic is driven tot he website from on-air, paid ads, and social.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could present an ask on the content pages that would support the campaign and convert users who are reading on the site.
In the past, we’ve seen that adding a dear reader to the content pages increases conversions significantly vs. not having one. For this test, we wanted to see if we could test adding different elements to make the ask as optimized as possible.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a personal ask to the dear reader for the content pages during the Spring Share fundraiser did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 17.1%, which is below the threshold of 95% for validity.

Furthermore, when breaking down the results by new or returning visitors, we're able to see that having the personalized ask actually works better for those that are new to the site.

We also saw that when only looking at desktop traffic, the personalized ask was more effective in converting donors (5 to 7); however, in the mobile view, the control was by far the champion. This could be because adding the image and extra copy caused extra donor friction on a smaller screen.

These results should not be used for future decision making. It is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to determine the true impact of adding a personal ask to the dear reader. Additionally, other elements could be tested to optimize the ask, such as different messaging or design elements. Future experimentation should focus on testing different strategies to effectively drive donations during the Spring Share fundraiser.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying the value proposition for perspective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they will understand what their donation will do for MAF missionaries and isolated people around the world. because they will understand what their donation will do for MAF missionaries and isolated people around the world.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 04/12/2023
MAF is running an offer through paid Facebook ads that asks people to leave a note of encouragement for MAF missionaries. On the landing page, they are provided with four pre-written messages that they can select from and then given an optional chance to write in their own note.
MAF wanted to see if we could increase the ICR on the donation page. After reading the value proposition on the control page, we found that the copy was confusing because it went from talking about the work that missionaries are doing to the work that pilots do for MAF. Because of this, there wasn’t clarity on what the user’s donation would do for MAF or isolated people.
To achieve this, we decided to run a radical redesign test and changed the following:
We removed “Thank you” from the headline because we hypothesize that this causes people to feel done with the process and leave the page.
We asked them to stay on the page by saying “Before you go, can we ask you something?”
We clarified the donation ask by stating A) Why missionaries are important B) That these missionaries can only continue this important work because of people who support MAF with a donation and C) What a donation to MAF will do.
We moved the ask for a donation to the end of the copy rather than asking for a donation first then explaining why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment A page saw a 51.2% increase in conversion rate with a 76.4% level of confidence. It also saw a 108.4% increase in revenue with a 89% level of confidence. The treatment B page (which had the same copy as treatment A) saw a 46.4% increase in conversion rate with a 71% level of confidence, and a 15.6% increase in revenue with a 60.5% level of confidence.

We believe that treatment page A had such an increase in conversion rate and revenue because people would more clearly understand what MAF was asking for on the donation page. Therefore we believe providing clarity increases donations and revenue.

We believe treatment B did not produce as much of a lift because donors didn't have a chance to make a more emotional connection by seeing isolated people in the header image.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a continuation headline for April TMS Sign Up Donation Page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the treatment allows the visitor to understand that there are more steps to take on the page therefore increasing engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/30/2025
Each month, The Navigators have been running scripture memory campaigns via text. After the user signs up to join the next text campaign, they are asked for a gift. We tested into this in a previous scripture memory campaign, but the results did not reach validity so we decided to test again.
Control: You’re in the challenge!
Treatment: You’re in! Now, would you consider making a gift so even more believers can experience this Scripture memory challenge?
The treatment was shown to 50% of traffic of the April TMS Sign Up Donation Page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included a continuation headline asking for a donation, resulted in a 6.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 25.6%, which is below the threshold of 95% required for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the treatment to see if a stronger impact on donor conversion metrics can be achieved. It is also worth considering other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign, the audience targeted, and any external factors that could have impacted donor behavior.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend towards increased donor conversion metrics with the treatment, the results should not be relied upon for making future decisions due to the low statistical significance. Further testing and optimization are necessary to determine the true impact of the continuation headline on donor conversion.

experiments.title: Will having headers on the campaign donation page help increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding headers to the doantion pages for the spring campaign will achieve more transactions because the headers will make the checkout process easier to follow.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
KCBI runs a Spring fundraiser each year where the on air hosts are fundraising and leading traffic to their page to give a gift or to call in.
For this campaign, we wanted to see how we could help increase conversions by adding headers or steps to the donation form. We tested this during CYE, but the results were not concrete. We wanted to see how this could perform during a different season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding headers to the donation pages for the Spring campaign actually led to a decrease in donations, instead of the expected increase. However, the results were not statistically significant with only a 77.9% level of confidence, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

With the steep decrease in transactions, we turned this test after just a couple of days. This test inadvertently could have caused more donor friction and anxiety by adding the headers. In future experimentation, it would be worth testing the language used.

experiments.title: Will a side by side layout work better for a donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the form to be a 2 column layout for the Spring Share doantion page will achieve more donor conversions because  the new layout has the potential to make the form easier to read and fill out.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/24/2025
Moody Radio runs a Spring Share fundraiser each year where tons of traffic from digital channels as well as on-air listeners are directed to a donation form.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the layout of the form had the potential to increase donor conversion on the form
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the form layout to a 2-column format for the Spring Share donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.3% decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. However, when you narrow down the results by traffic we can see that the mobile experience for this test is what caused the overall decrease. Looking at desktop-only results we are seeing a 9.6% increase in conversion at a 57% LoC. While not valid, the results of the desktop do not have a negative impact on conversion.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% in order to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further research to understand why the 2-column layout did not perform as expected. This could involve gathering feedback from users, conducting usability tests, or A/B testing other elements of the donation form to identify factors that may impact donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will a campaign countdown or a daily countdown resonate better with users

rows.name: Control - Wiki with Recurring Donor Countdown
Treatment - Wiki with Daily Recurring Donor Countdown

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing the campaign goal with a daily goal for the campagin wiki  will achieve more transactions because by breaking the donor goal into the day we can make the numbers small which can lead to an increased buy in from users.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2025 - 03/26/2025
Focus on the Family is a well known global ministry.
Right now, FOTF is in the middle of a recurring donor drive. To support this campaign, we wanted to add some elements to parts of the website that receives a lot of traffic.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could improve on our tried and true campaign wiki that sits at the top of the homepage by replacing the campaign goal to a daily goal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
- The hypothesis that replacing the campaign goal with a daily goal would lead to more transactions did not hold true in this experiment.
- The treatment, which displayed a daily goal instead of a campaign goal, resulted in a 12.0% decrease in Total Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
- The statistical level of confidence for this result was 0.0%, indicating that the results are not reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
Future Experimentation:
- When designing future experiments, it is important to carefully evaluate the potential impact of changes to the website, such as replacing a campaign goal with a daily goal.
- It is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration and with a large enough sample size to generate statistically significant results.
- Since the results of this experiment are inconclusive, further testing may be needed to determine the effectiveness of displaying a daily goal versus a campaign goal for donor drives.
- It is important to continue testing and iterating on website elements to optimize for user engagement and donation conversions.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for Avila’s donation page viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the audience will feel more aligned with the mission and goal of the Avila Foundation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2025 - 03/10/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether enhancing the donation page copy on their Ecce Homo offer with value proposition would increase donor engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Adding a Value Proposition Increased Conversions – The treatment led to a 16.6% lift in conversions, suggesting that highlighting Avila’s mission resonated with donors.
 	Higher Average Gift Amount – Donors in the treatment group gave more on average ($43.46 vs. $37.77), indicating that mission-driven messaging may encourage larger gifts.
 	Results Show Potential, But Need More Testing – The 57.4% confidence level suggests further testing is needed to confirm the impact.
 	Mission-Driven Appeals Strengthen Donor Alignment – Emphasizing Avila’s role in addressing cultural and spiritual challenges may have helped donors feel more connected.
 	Framing Matters – Positioning donor support as essential to the mission (rather than just covering costs) likely contributed to the increase in engagement.
Next Steps:
Test with a larger sample size to improve statistical confidence.
 	Experiment with different messaging elements (e.g., urgency vs. donor impact).
 	Consider adding donor stories or incentives to further boost conversions.

experiments.title: Does adding an image of a direct mail envelope impact donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an envelope image for direct mail donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
In March, the direct mail team wanted to test sending a new design of an envelope to donors as a remail strategy. We wanted to target this list with a digital test that looked at whether including the picture of the envelope in the email led to higher conversion rates.
Our hypothesis was that because the envelope was so different from what PTV usually uses, and could easily get lost in the mix, adding an image to the email would drive higher response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the envelope to the email for direct mail donors did not lead to higher conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a 29.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is also important to note that the true impact of this test will be whether it influenced offline giving at all; however, direct mail results won't be available for another month, so we are only reporting on instant digital transactions here.

This experiment also had a similar result when looking at it from a revenue perspective. The treatment version had 38% less revenue per visitor than the control, with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Can we increase donations from traffic on the homepage?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module to the her image for the spring fundraiser will achieve more transactions because the user will be able to start their gift on the homepage, taking a step out of the process and reducing friction.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2025
KCBI is in the middle of their on-air spring fundraiser, which drives a ton of traffic to their website.
For this campaign, and other previous catalytic campaigns, we’ve found that adding a sticky bar to the top of the homepage works well in converting users to donors.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could present the donation ask in a different way on the homepage and still convert donors as well as the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donation module to the hero image for the spring fundraiser did not result in an increase in donations compared to the sticky bar. In fact, the treatment saw a 5.8% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 37.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that the treatment version saw an increase in revenue by having an average gift of $177 vs the control $155. While overall transactions were down, the majority of these were due to recurring, when looking at one time gifts the donation module on the homepage had a slight lift in transactions.

In future experimentation, it's important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide meaningful results with a high level of confidence. This will help avoid drawing conclusions based on potentially unreliable data. Additionally, it's crucial to continue testing different strategies and elements on the website to optimize donation conversions. This experiment provides valuable insights that can be used to inform future tests and ultimately improve the effectiveness of donation asks on the KCBI website.

experiments.title: How rotating a “Gift Card” widget with a Tribute Thank-You message impacts clicks and tributes
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rotating a Gift Card widget in with the tribute Thank You card for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in Gift Card clicks/traffic without decreasing tribute giving because the additional CTA is only shown to those who’ve already donated, providing them with an additional next step rather than detracting from another important CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/10/2025
This experiment was a development upon a previous test to promote Gift Cards/Ways to Help as an additional CTA on user profiles. In the original test, the Gift Card widget had been swapped in place of the Tribute Widget on a rotating logic. Although this did dramatically increase clicks and traffic to the gift card page, it also resulted in a valid 30% decrease in tribute giving.
In this iteration, we hypothesized that only rotating the Gift Card in with the Tribute thank-you message would ensure that the additional CTA could be communicated, without removing another important ask, but instead, prioritizing the next step for people who’ve already donated. The logic for this setup was 50/50, half the time users would see the thank-you card for their tribute gift and the other half of the time they’d receive the additional Gift Card widget. Versus in the control, they were only shown the thank you message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Rotating a Gift Card widget in with the tribute Thank You card for users on a site profile resulted in a 17.5% increase in Send a Gift Card clicked for All Traffic. This indicates that the additional CTA was effective in generating more clicks and traffic to the gift card page.

2. The 78.1% statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid. This suggests that the increase in Gift Card clicks may not be entirely reliable.

3. However, another key aspect of this test was to incorporate the Gift Card widget without negatively impacting tribute giving. In this case, the treatment was a success. Reversing the original decrease seen previously, while also indicating that this treatment can be safely shown and likely will result in great education and clicks to Gift Cards.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of careful consideration and testing when introducing new elements to a site profile. By analyzing the results and refining future experiments based on these findings, we can improve the user experience and drive desired actions effectively.

experiments.title: What happens if we add a gift array to a sticky bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array for the homepage sticky bar will achieve more transactions because the user will be able to start their process right there after saying yes instead of being directed to the donation page first.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Care Net recently ran a recurring campagin to raise money for their new merger ICU.
In the past, we have tested into adding a sticky bar to the homepage. For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions by adding the gift array to the sticky bar itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 5.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive result, the statistical level of confidence was only 16.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

 

It is interesting to note that while we saw no difference in the total number of transactions completed, we did find a 25% lift in total revenue at a 56% LoC and a 45% lift in recurring transactions completed at a 53% LoC. While the gift array could have made this impact. I think the CTA had stake at play too and could be giving us a hint at what the audience prefers. In the control we saw a CTA of "activate my monthly gift" where as the treatment had microgoal "be 1 of 50". I think it is important to look into this microgoal to see if we can test this hypothesis out.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar, but additional testing is needed to draw conclusive results. This information should be used to guide future experimentation and help refine strategies for increasing donations on the Care Net website.

experiments.title: Will a personalized slide out be more effective at conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized ask on the slidout for the content pages will achieve more thansactions because it feels more human.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Recently, Care net ran a recurring campaign with the goal to acquire 50 new monthly partners.
In an effort to support this campaign we added a slide out that had a direct ask on all content pages (read anything other than the homepage and donation page). In the past we have seen this slideout work really well.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we made the generalized ask a little more personal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized ask on the slideout for content pages resulted in a 35.5% decrease in donations compared to the generalized ask. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

 

When breaking down the results a little further we saw the control - without the personalized ask have an 80% increase in revenue and a 100% lift in completed recurring transactions. Which is what this campaign was trying to achieve.

Similar to the sticky bar, I think we're seeing these results due to the CTA that is on the buttons. It seems like the audiences responds better to a microgoal "become 1 of 50" vs. "activate your monthly gift".

It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with variations of personalized asks to see if there are specific types of personalization that resonate better with donors. Continuous experimentation and data analysis are essential in order to make informed decisions and optimize donation strategies for better results.

experiments.title: Are we presenting an acquisition offer in the best way on the landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the landing page for the Lent offer in MMS will achieve more form submissions because the page will be optimized for a mobile experience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
For this test we wanted to see if we could change the landing page copy for the Lent offer in MMS and see more email sign ups.
We made several changes to the design of the page to make it more mobile friendly in terms of scrolling and skimming.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the adjusted landing page for the Lent offer in MMS did not result in more form submissions compared to the original page. Despite efforts to make the page more mobile-friendly for easier scrolling and skimming, the treatment actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 63.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test and iterate on the changes made to the landing page. It may be worth exploring different design elements, content, or CTAs to determine what truly resonates with the target audience and drives more form submissions. Additionally, ensuring a higher statistical level of confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision making.

experiments.title: How a grid layout for Watch Now website impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
a grid layout for desktop web traffic will achieve increase engagement because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling. because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling.
because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling. because the layout presents multiple viewing opportunities and less scrolling.</p.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to watch to past sermons and TV broadcasts.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling

experiments.title: How adding more prayer centered language on a landing page impacted sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing emotional appeal, personal transformation, and prayer for the landing page viewer will achieve will see an increase in email acquisition rates because it better resonates with the audience’s desires and challenges in developing a deeper prayer life..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2025 - 03/04/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether rewriting the copy on Avila’s offer, Into the Deep, to be more prayer-centered would impact email acquisition. Since this offer is about prayer, we wanted to try engaging the viewer with emotional appeal and frame prayer as a deep personal need and struggle, addressing common challenges like distractions, uncertainty, and the desire for true communion with God. Presenting more of a problem and solution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Takeaways:
The Treatment Showed Promise – The increase in email acquisition (+3.3%) and donations (+12.7%) suggests that the emotionally driven copy may be more persuasive.
Low Confidence Levels – The confidence levels (32.8% for email acquisition and 23.1% for donations) indicate high variability, meaning the test may need more traffic or a longer duration to reach statistical significance.
Next Steps:
Increase sample size – Running the test with more visitors could help clarify whether these trends hold.
Refine the treatment copy – Since the treatment showed promise, small tweaks (e.g., optimizing the CTA, adding social proof, or adjusting the donation appeal) could strengthen its impact.
Test a stronger CTA or incentive – A/B test a variation that further emphasizes urgency or value to see if it boosts conversions more reliably.

experiments.title: How introducing a new email sender in the beginning of an email appeal affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal for email subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will create a sense of novelty and engagement, capturing the recipient’s attention more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ kickoff email for their Sustainers Campaign, which aimed to acquire new recurring donors.
The test was designed to evaluate whether introducing a new sender at the beginning of the email appeal would increase donor conversion rates. Given that email engagement plays a crucial role in fundraising success, we wanted to explore whether a fresh or recognizable sender could capture attention, build trust, and drive more donations.
The decision to run this experiment was driven by the need to optimize email performance and maximize donor acquisition at the start of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates**: The treatment, which featured a new sender introducing themselves at the start of the email, resulted in a 41.8% increase in donations. This suggests that changing up the sender in email appeals can have a positive impact on donor engagement.

2. **Confidence in the results**: While the increase in donations was significant, the statistical confidence level was only 64.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered completely reliable or valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Continue testing with different sender introductions**: The positive impact of introducing a new sender indicates that further experimentation with different sender personas, introductions, and stories could continue to be beneficial. Testing a variety of approaches can help identify the most effective ways to connect with donors and drive donations.

2. **Increase sample size for higher confidence**: To ensure more reliable results in future experiments, it may be necessary to increase the sample size. This can help boost the statistical confidence level and provide clearer insights into the effectiveness of different email strategies.

3. **Iterate and refine based on learnings**: While the results of this experiment may not be conclusive, the learnings can still be valuable in shaping future email campaigns. Take the insights gained from this experiment, such as the impact of a new sender introduction, and incorporate them into future iterations of email appeals to continue optimizing donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image to a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the anonymous missionaries for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask Donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the image provides visual representation as to what an anonymous missionary is—therefore, helping visitors grasp the idea more quickly and evoking a stronger emotional connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 03/03/2025
To improve donor conversion rates on IMB’s Anonymous Missionary Direct Ask, we tested the impact of adding a relevant image to the donation page.
This specific offer can be challenging to communicate effectively, so our hypothesis was that an image depicting “anonymous missionaries” would provide greater clarity to visitors, ultimately leading to higher donor engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of anonymous missionaries to the donation page for Anonymous Missionaries Direct Ask did not lead to the expected increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid, but the results are trending in a negative direction.
Due to these results, we looked further into the where the traffic came from to see if it performed better or worse on different types of devices.
Sure enough - mobile users saw an almost valid 71.8% decrease in transactions (94.5% LOC). This indicates that the image negatively impacted potential donors who visited the donation page on their mobile device.  This could be due to the fact that image was somewhat larger and required more scrolling on mobile that desktop.
Desktop users saw an invalid 114.9% lift in transactions at a 47.4% LOC. In future testing, it may be beneficial to only test large images like this on desktop to minimize the amount of friction on the page for mobile users.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and consider the statistical significance before drawing conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that the hypothesis is well-grounded and based on solid evidence. Further testing may be needed to determine the true impact of adding an image to the donation page for this specific campaign. Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other potential factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as messaging, layout, or incentives.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. It is also crucial to carefully design the messaging and content of the email offers to resonate with the donor audience.
