experiments.title: How using No-primed questions in a survey impacted engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “No-primed” questions for a survey offer will achieve an increase in survey completions (email submissions) because respondents will feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers, which may lead to a subconscious desire to resolve with a “Yes” when asked to contribute later.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2024 - 11/27/2024
During high-urgency campaigns such as calendar year-end, we often use a survey as a tool to help “prime” Luther Seminary’s audience with knowledge about the problems that Luther Seminary is working to solve through their fundraising campaign. Typically, these survey questions are “yes-primed”, which means that they’re designed so that a Luther Seminary supporter would respond “Yes” to each question.
For Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test this “yes-primed” framing against a survey that was “no-primed”. Our goal was to understand how this “Yes” or “No” priming impacts engagement and downstream donor behavior.
Both survey funnels were identical, with the only change being the phrasing of the 5 survey questions. The Control survey used “Yes”-primed questions, which are designed to elicit affirmative responses from respondents. The Treatment survey, on the other hand, used “No”-primed questions, encouraging respondents to think critically about the topics and more likely to answer negatively.
This experiment aimed to explore the psychological principle of reactance theory, which suggests that when people feel their freedom of choice is being restricted or guided (such as through overly positive framing), they may react by resisting or disengaging. Conversely, by using “No”-primed questions, we hypothesized that respondents would feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers. The idea is that when people are encouraged to think critically and reject easy affirmations, they may feel more invested in their responses, fostering a stronger connection with the survey.
Additionally, answering “No” to these questions may create a cognitive dissonance effect, where respondents feel the need to resolve the negative stance with a positive action, such as donating after completing the survey. This subconscious desire for resolution may lead to higher engagement during the survey and increased donor conversions later in the funnel, especially when presented with an opportunity to act on their beliefs.
We split traffic to the survey pages evenly, and measured the # of survey submissions (emails acquired), as well as downstream donations on the post-survey donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test can be broken down into 3 key learnings:

 

#1 - Increase in Engagement with “No”-Primed Questions

The Treatment survey, which used "No"-primed questions, resulted in a 7.8% increase in form submissions compared to the Control survey. While this increase did not meet the 95% statistical level of confidence (79.1% LOC), it still suggests a promising trend toward higher engagement. This aligns with our hypothesis, which was grounded in reactance theory. By encouraging respondents to critically evaluate the questions and answer negatively, we likely fostered a greater sense of autonomy. Respondents who felt they were not being led to affirmations may have felt more invested in their responses, leading to increased engagement.

 

#2 - Potential Impact of “No” Priming on Conversion Behavior

Despite the increase in form submissions, the Conversion Rate for the Treatment group (2.41%) was slightly lower than the Control group (3.36%). This discrepancy suggests that while “No”-primed questions led to more people completing the survey, there might be a short-term resistance to following through with donations immediately after completing the survey. However, the cognitive dissonance effect mentioned in our hypothesis may still be at play—respondents who answered "No" to the questions may feel a subconscious need to resolve the negativity, and this could influence them to donate in the future as they reconcile their answers with the desire to support Luther Seminary.

 

#3 - Higher Average Gift in the Treatment Group

The Treatment group had a higher average gift of $32, compared to the Control group’s $23. This finding suggests that while the number of donors was slightly lower in the Treatment group, those who did choose to give were more likely to contribute larger amounts. This is potentially indicative of commitment consistency. Respondents who were engaged through the "No"-primed survey, and who felt more autonomous in their responses, may have been more motivated to make a larger, more deliberate contribution to Luther Seminary's cause. By thinking critically about their answers, they may have been more intentional in their giving.

 

The results of this experiment point toward the potential effectiveness of “No”-primed surveys for increasing engagement and stimulating larger gifts, even if immediate conversions didn’t significantly increase. Respondents who answered negatively may have felt more empowered and invested in their decisions, which could lead to higher-value gifts in the long term. Future tests could explore the extended impact of these psychological principles, with a focus on donor behavior over time and refining the optimal balance between engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: Should we be asking for a $10 gift or a $20 gift on web elements
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific ask amount of $20 for the slide in will achieve a higher average gift without losing transactions because the ask is specific and is still less than the overall average gift that is normally made to the Radio site during a CYE campaign.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
In previous campaigns we’ve added direct ask elements onto the site to see if we can increase donations. However, we wanted to see if we could increase the average gift amount without sacrificing overall revenue and/or donations.
For this test, we set up two identical slide ins. One contained a $10 ask and the other contained a $20 ask. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results show that incraseing the ask amount to $20 did in fact make us lose donations compared to having a lower entry gift.

However, we did not only want to look at transactions, we wanted to see if we could increase the overall gift, and thus the revenue.

By having the ask be $20 we saw a decrease in revenue by $8,000.. however, we will need to dig into the individual transactions to see if there are outlier gifts that are skewing the results.

It's also important to note that the average gift for both the control and treatment are way higher than the initial ask. This shows that even if we ask a lower gift of $10 people are giving more than that.

experiments.title: Will adding a timer to the dear reader increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer for the dear reader will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the urgency of the countdown convert donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For this test we wanted to build on our previous learnings to find the most optimized version of a dear reader.
This time we wanted to see if adding a countdown timer to the end of the campaign on a dear reader would increase the conversion rate. This test only ran for the last week of the campaign where urgency is at it’s highest point.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a countdown timer to a dear reader may not necessarily result in a higher donor conversion rate. In this particular case, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and consider factors such as timing, messaging, and audience preferences before implementing changes. It is also crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate and that the statistical confidence level is sufficient to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different variables to determine the most effective strategies for increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How is the best way to present an ask on the homepage during CYE?

Ended On: 02/18/2025
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar is really the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a high urgency campaign.
This test we took inspiration from the Chicago Sun Times and placed what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ element on the page. This test will only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lower third element on the homepage during a high urgency campaign resulted in a 9.1% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the sticky bar. This suggests that the lower third element may be more effective in capturing donor conversions. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.7%, which is below the recommended 95% threshold for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the design and placement of donation asks on the homepage. While the lower third element showed promise in this test, further research and testing may be needed to confirm its effectiveness. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is achieved in order to make reliable decisions based on the results.

Overall, the findings from this experiment can be used as a starting point for exploring new ways to optimize donation asks on the website, but further testing with a larger sample size is recommended to draw more conclusive insights for future decision making.

experiments.title: Is a specific ask better than an open ended ask?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific gift amount for a slide in ask on the internal pages of the website will achieve a higher donor conversion because having a suggested gift amount will decrease decision friction.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
During previous campaigns we have found that putting a slide in ask on the internal pages of the Moody Bible Institute (edu) website increases donations to the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase the average gift, while also not sacrificing donations, by asking for a specific gift amount of $60. To get to the $60 ask we looked at their overall average gift (excluding majors). Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a specific gift amount suggested in a slide in ask on the internal pages of the Moody Bible Institute website led to a significant decrease in donations. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the potential impact of suggesting a specific gift amount on donor behavior before implementing any changes. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that statistically significant results are achieved with a high level of confidence before making any decisions based on the outcomes of an experiment.

It is also recommended to consider conducting further tests or adjusting the approach to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to potentially uncover the most effective strategy for increasing donations without sacrificing donor conversion.

experiments.title: Will having shorter copy on the monthly donation page hurt or help recurring transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the monthly donation page will achieve more recurring transactions because the shorter copy reinforces the need to give without the extra copy that may create decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
Historically, the homepage monthly and episodic donation pages for Focus on the Family have been hard to get a valid test to help increase transition and/or revuene. Last Spring and through the summer we found that mentioning “Biblical Reosurces” on the pages had a positive impact on donations and revenue. We wondered, if we could build off of that finding to improve the donation page copy even more.
For this test, we shortened the copy on the monthly donation page to only communicate about the impact that a donor wold have in empowering families with biblical resources. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the copy on the monthly donation page did not result in an increase in recurring transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 7.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

Since the statistical level of confidence is only 20.7%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to run future experiments for a longer period of time to gather more data and ensure the results are statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies on the donation page to see what resonates most with donors. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other factors that may impact donation behavior, such as the design of the page, the suggested donation amounts, or the overall user experience. By continuing to test and iterate on different elements of the donation page, Focus on the Family can optimize for increased donations and revenue.

experiments.title: Will adding urgency to web elements increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing urgency for the lower third banner will achieve more transactions because the added urgency will motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
During CYE we went throughs several versions of web elements to see which one would be the best to show on content pages. We found that adding what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ banner to the bottom of the page increased transactions compared to a dear reader.
Our next step was to find out how to optimize the lower third to present it in the best way possible. For this test, we wanted to see if adding urgency to the lower third element would increase transactions. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding urgency to the lower third banner can potentially increase transactions. Although the treatment group saw a 4.5% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 19.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be deemed reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the concept of urgency and test different variations to see which one resonates most with the audience. It is also valuable to continue testing and iterating on different elements of the lower third banner to identify the most effective strategies for increasing transactions on the website.

experiments.title: How scarcity affects donor conversion rate during digital kick off campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing scarcity in the email will increase the donor conversion rate because framing the opportunity as rare and time-sensitive leverages the psychological principle of urgency and exclusivity, motivating recipients to act quickly to avoid missing out.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2025
The membership team tested language that focused on scarcity. Copy used words like “rare and fleeting” and “a unique chance” to evoke a feeling that the opportunity to donate would slip pass them if they didn’t act now.
Results varied between segments. Members saw an 8% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 40-50% LOC, but prospects saw a 16% lift in conversion rate with a 78% LOC.
Putting the results together the added copy did not seem to make difference. The test ran during a mid campaign deadline, which makes testing difficult because the donor intent to give is much higher during deadlines.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that emphasizing scarcity in the email did not have a significant impact on donor conversion rates. While the treatment did see a slight increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 85%, making the results inconclusive and not valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the context in which the test is being conducted. In this case, the mid-campaign deadline likely affected donor behavior, making it difficult to isolate the impact of the scarcity messaging. It may be beneficial to run similar tests outside of peak donation periods to get a clearer understanding of the impact of scarcity messaging on donor conversion rates.

Additionally, it is important to continue testing different messaging strategies and elements to determine what resonates best with donors. While scarcity messaging may not have had a significant impact in this particular experiment, it is still valuable to explore other tactics that may help increase donor conversion rates.

Overall, the key takeaway is that while scarcity messaging did not show a significant impact in this experiment, continued testing and experimentation are essential to optimize messaging strategies for increased donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding a countdown timer to a donation page effectively increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last week of the camapgin will achieve more transactions because the countdown timer will reinforce that time is running out and your donation is needed now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase urgency on the donation page by adding a countdown timer to when the campaign ends.
We started this test the last week of the campaign when urgency should be at it’s peak.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the donation page during the last week of a campaign can potentially increase revenue for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was at 0.0%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. It may be beneficial to run similar experiments with a larger sample size to accurately determine the impact of adding a countdown timer on donation page conversions.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to use caution in interpreting the findings and to prioritize statistical significance in making data-driven decisions for future campaigns and optimizations.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial to the sustainer donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the campaign donation pages will achieve more transactions because because the donor testimonial will lend to social proof and increase trust.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/19/2024
This CYE we wanted to see if we could present a donor testimonail on the donation page to increase social proof and trust, in turn increasing transactions.
For this test we added a box right above the giving from that had a donor testimonal in it. We added this to all of the main campaign giving pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the campaign donation pages did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It's also important to note that this test only ran for a couple of days due to the stark decrease in conversions. In the future, it would be worth testing this again to see if it just got off to a slow start.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring other forms of social proof or trust-building elements, such as testimonials from beneficiaries, impact stories, or trust seals. It is also important to ensure that experiments run for a longer period of time in order to gather more reliable and valid data for decision making.

experiments.title: How leading with supporting the mission over the book impacted donations for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting giving to the ministry for nondonors will achieve a higher impact in donations because the prospective donor can know what their money is going toward and what they will get out of it.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For the PTV Feb 2025 offer, we decided to test a “give then get” strategy. Where we emphasize more toward giving toward the mission and then receiving the book.
In previous strategies, we would focus primarily on the value of the book and giving to the ministry was a more secondary response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting giving to the ministry for non-donors resulted in a 39.6% decrease in donations for all traffic. Although this result did not reach a 95% statistical confidence level, it did show a significant impact on donation behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the balance between emphasizing giving to the ministry and the value of the offer. It may be beneficial to continue testing this "give then get" strategy with variations in messaging and targeting to determine the most effective approach. Additionally, conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes or different audience segments could help confirm the impact of highlighting giving to the ministry on donation behavior.

Overall, the results of this experiment indicate the importance of carefully testing and refining strategies to understand their impact on donor behavior and optimize fundraising efforts for Pathway to Victory.

The nondonor segment saw an increase from this strategy https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/9216

experiments.title: How removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email increase clicks and donations on a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing a a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks, emails, and donations because it shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “The Ten Causes of the Reagan Boom”. This specific email was to their donor audience.
Our hypothesis was that if we remove one of the links in the email it would increase clicks and email acquisition and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by removing a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover's donor audience, there was a 7.4% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that shifting the focus towards the content and value of the eBook before presenting the call-to-action may have a positive impact on engagement.
Another interesting thing to note is that the control version received 16 gifts, while the treatment received 12 gifts.
Here are some potential reasonings behind the increase in clicks:
1. Reduced Link Fatigue & Increased Focus
In the control email, the reader sees two instances of the eBook link. When people see the same link twice, they might mentally skip over it, assuming they've already processed the message.In the treatment, there’s only one link, making it stand out more and increasing the likelihood that the reader clicks it.
2. Building Anticipation
The control email presents the link early on, potentially causing some readers to click right away and miss the rest of the message. Those who don't click immediately might not feel the need to continue reading.The treatment removes the early link, keeping readers engaged until the end, where they see the CTA more prominently and with more context.
3. More Natural Flow & Call-to-Action Strength
The treatment version subtly shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA. This can make the final CTA feel more compelling, rather than transactional.The single call to action at the end could create a sense of exclusivity or urgency, reinforcing the importance of clicking.
4. Less Distraction, More Commitment
The control version interrupts the email with a link early on, which might cause some readers to skim or disengage.The treatment keeps the reader focused on the narrative before directing them toward a single action.
5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect
By not giving immediate access to the link, the treatment might make the reader feel like they need to read carefully to find out where to get the eBook. This small increase in effort can create a greater sense of investment, making them more likely to follow through.
6. Psychological Consistency (Zeigarnik Effect)
The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.

experiments.title: How removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email increase clicks and donations on a nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email for Hoover’s nondonor audience will achieve an increase in clicks, email acquistion, and donations because it increases engagement and reminds them of the CTA.
experiments.summary:

Here are some potential reasonings behind the increase in clicks:

Those who don't click immediately might not feel the need to continue reading.The treatment removes the early link, keeping readers engaged until the end, where they see the CTA more prominently and with more context.
3. More Natural Flow & Call-to-Action Strength
The treatment version subtly shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA. This can make the final CTA feel more compelling, rather than transactional.The single call to action at the end could create a sense of exclusivity or urgency, reinforcing the importance of clicking.
4. Less Distraction, More Commitment
The control version interrupts the email with a link early on, which might cause some readers to skim or disengage.The treatment keeps the reader focused on the narrative before directing them toward a single action.
5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect
By not giving immediate access to the link, the treatment might make the reader feel like they need to read carefully to find out where to get the eBook. This small increase in effort can create a greater sense of investment, making them more likely to follow through.

The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.
However, because of the low level of statistical confidence this will need to be tested further!

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar with a donor countdown on the episodic donation page impact transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar  for the campaign episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the sticky bar will increase the urgency and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if adding additional urgency/ motivation on the campaign episodic donation page would impact the number of transactions made.
We tested this on the monthly donation page and found that it did increase transactions and revenue. You can see the results of that test here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to the campaign episodic donation page can increase transactions, potentially due to the increased urgency and motivation it provides to donors. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 53.8%.
Similar to the test on the recurring donation page, we also saw an increase in average gift when the stickybar was present on the page. ($288/avg. gift vs. $388/avg. gift).
This campaign was ran towards the end of December when urgency is at it's highest. It's important to note that the treatment was winning up until the 28th (last day this was scheduled to run). In future testing it might be worth testing this approach earlier in the campaign and not at the end with the urgency for most people to give is already there.

experiments.title: How a headline on the donation page achieve a higher donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that saying ‘deliver hope’ instead of ‘give hope’ for the family episodic donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fotf audience across their website has responded better to it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/20/2024
In our testing series of figuring out if ‘deliver hope’ vs. ‘give hope’ resonates more with the fotf audience. We’ve found that on the homepage and monthly donation pages are showing promising results of the ‘deliver’ message being the more effective across their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the 'deliver hope' messaging did not result in a higher donor conversion rate on the family episodic donation page compared to the 'give hope' messaging. The treatment group actually experienced a 6.4% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.8%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to have a statistical level of confidence of at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions from an experiment.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is large enough and that the statistical analysis is robust to generate valid and actionable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to continue testing different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the Focus on the Family audience, taking into account the results of previous experiments to inform future decision-making.

experiments.title: Will changing the headline on the donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing ‘give hope’ to ‘deliver hope’ for the family monthly donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’ve seen the fotf audience respond better to deliver hope vs give hope.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/20/2024
Following suit in our first couple of experiments for CYE, we wanted to see if we changing out ‘give hope’ to ‘deliver hope’ would have the same effect as it did on the wiki header and the sohl monthly donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing 'give hope' to 'deliver hope' on the family monthly donation page resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 176.8% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the language used in donation appeals to see if 'deliver hope' consistently outperforms 'give hope' with a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to explore other variations of messaging to further optimize donor conversion rates. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments run for a longer duration to gather more data and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results.

experiments.title: How testimonials on the donation page increased donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they can clearly see the impact of their gift during CYE.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/16/2024
During the high-traffic month of December, we wanted to know if adding an “end user” testimonial would further inspire potential donors to give or give at a greater amount because they were reading about the impact of their gift at the time of decision. Similar to how an e-commerce page might use testimonials for a product, we wanted to know the impact of adding this additional copy from the end user to the Today in the Word CYE donation page would make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the main campaign donation page resulted in a 17.3% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that having end-user testimonials on the page can increase credibility and potentially encourage more people to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials or other forms of social proof on donation pages. By conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform our strategies for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a homepage banner performed against a sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a lower third ad for the homepage will achieve higher donor conversion because the lower third ad will be something new on the site for users to see, where the sticky bar could have banner blindness. It also gives the donor three specific gift amounts to choose from whereas the sticky bar is an open ended ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/14/2025
For this test we wanted to see if we could potentially test into a different element on the homepage besides a sticky bar. For this we created a bottom banner that slides up on the page that contains three different options to give.
We ran this test during a high urgency campaign where traffic to the homepage is higher than normal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lower third ad for donations on the homepage did not result in higher donor conversions compared to the sticky bar. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease in donations. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different elements on the homepage to find what resonates best with donors. It is important to consider the placement, design, and messaging of donation prompts to optimize conversion rates. Additionally, it would be helpful to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and longer testing period to ensure more reliable results.

experiments.title: How a consistent CTA throughout the donor funnel impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a consistent CTA for the housefile during a high-urgency appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action. because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a consistent CTA from the email to donation page copy and final “donate/give” button would help reinforce the desired action and increased donor conversion rate. In the control, the ask was presented with various ways of phrasing the call to action: “make a gift” “donate” “give” “match your donation” etc. In the treatment, we used “donate” consistently as the CTA in email copy and donation page copy while also changing the give button to “Donate.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a consistent call-to-action (CTA) does not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, using "donate" consistently as the CTA in both the email and donation page did not result in a higher number of donations compared to using various CTAs like "make a gift" or "match your donation."

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be worthwhile to test these in other funnels – besides just email (consistent CTA from a Sticky Bar or Facebook ad for example), in addition to swapping the consistency for a different CTA (using Give throughout rather than Donate throughout)

experiments.title: How adding a CTA to a Facebook Ad impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a CTA to the ad creative for Avila’s audience will achieve an increase in donations and click through rate because the purpose of the ad is clearer and therefore more motivated to download.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of Avila’s offers is Rule of Life a special guide to use as you chart out your spiritual “growth plan.”
We wanted to test adding the CTA on the ad creative as well as adding a colored background to see if this impacted the donation rate, click through rate, and email acquisition. By encouraging people at the beginning of the funnel to download the ebook – our hypothesis was that more people would click on the ad and therefore donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a call-to-action (CTA) to the ad creative can significantly increase donations and click-through rates for Avila Foundation. The treatment group, which had the CTA added to the ad creative, saw an impressive 81.4% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was at 79.3%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact from adding the CTA, they cannot be considered statistically valid and should not be used for making future decisions.
It also had an impact on how many downloaded the offer:
There was a 13.7% increase in signups on the treatment; with a 76.5% level of confidence.
Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other elements such as different CTA language, colors, or placements to further optimize donation rates and click-through rates.

experiments.title: How an identity call to action in email, emphasizing “become a donor,” impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps emphasize the user becoming a donor, speaking to their identity/what they’re joining, versus the action CTA of “make a gift”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that using an identity CTA for non-donors, emphasizing “become a donor” rather than just “make a gift” will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The hypothesis that using an identity CTA for non-donors emphasizing "become a donor" would increase donor conversion rate was not supported by the results.

2. The treatment group, which used the identity CTA, actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

3. The statistical confidence level of 66.4% is below the standard threshold of 95%, meaning that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is possible that for non-donors, the identity CTA framed the ask in a way that felt too big. As someone whose never given, "become a donor" may seem more intimidating than "make a gift" having a reverse effect given our hypothesis. It would be worthwhile to re-test this experience for higher validity during a period outside of the increased urgency.

experiments.title: How impacted gift array links in email impacts donor conversion during a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because it eliminates friction, pre-filling the selected amount on the donation page, while also reinforcing that their gift is being matched in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, we hypothesized that using impacted gift array links to drive urgency and eliminate friction during the 2-day match would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign led to a decrease in donations for all traffic. Additionally, the treatment saw a dropoff in average gift with $146 in the control versus $65 in the treatment.

Ultimately, the level of confidence was 78.9%, meaning these results are not valid for future decision-making. It would also be worth re-testing this experience for validity outside the window of a high-urgency appeal. Potentially, the treatment may have resulted in too much decision friction at the top of the funnel, whereas the links would have proved more effective simply by increasing clicks and greater traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: Does mentioning the reach potential of a recurring gift impact donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning the reach value for email subscribers will achieve a increase in donor conversion because people will get a tangible impact of their gift and know the number of lives they are impacting each month.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s January recruitment of Pathway Partners, PTV’s recurring donor program, we decided to test mentioning the reach value of a monthly gift for a year.
We calculated, based on Facebook CPM rates for 2024, that it costs PTV approximately $25 to reach 1,000 people. Therefore over the span of 12 months, a recurring gift of $25 could reach 12,000 new people for PTV’s mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning the reach value of a monthly gift can lead to a significant increase in donations for Pathway to Victory. The treatment version, which included information about how a monthly gift of $25 could reach 12,000 new people over the span of a year, saw a 61.2% increase in donations for All Traffic compared to the control version. While the statistical level of confidence was only 52.2%, indicating that these results may not be entirely reliable, the overall trend suggests that highlighting the impact of a donation can resonate with donors and encourage them to give.

For revenue, while $25/month was the amount mentioned in the email. The treatment saw a 43% increase with a 70% level of confidence. This is primarily due to a $50 gift given to the treatment version. Implying that this donor wanted to reach twice as many people mentioned in the email.

experiments.title: How a standard overlay compares to an overlay with a gift array during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array for site traffic during the final day of the holiday appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because the array prefills the selected amount onto the donation page, eliminating friction and providing users who are likely coming to the site in order to make their gift with a first step on page load.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2024 - 01/01/2025
During the final day of CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, half the traffic received a standard urgency overlay whereas the other half received an overlay with an interactive gift array. The frequency and amount the user selected would then be prefilled onto the donation page, eliminating friction and helping to encourage a higher gift amount. We hypothesized that the treatment would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue, providing users with a gift array on page load – assuming that much of the traffic will be coming to CaringBridge.org on the final day of the year in order to make their year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array, resulted in a 5.3% increase in donations for all traffic on the final day of the holiday campaign. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

It's important to note that this experiment was conducted during the final day of the year, where urgency is extremely high and uniquely motivated. As seen in the little to no difference between control and treatment, it is possible that users on the site at this time already know that they intend to make their gift – meaning that whether encountering version 1 or version 2 it doesn't significantly sway that decision negatively or positively.

experiments.title: How using a more provocative title for the subject of a survey impacts Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an more provocative name for the Administrative State for survey Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks on the ad because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions. because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 01/08/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a “Stop the Administrative State” survey and promoting through Facebook ads. In an attempt to increase click rates on the ads, they wanted to test whether using the more provocative term “Deep State” in place of the more straightforward “Administrative State” survey title would achieve this effect because people would be more provoked to take action against the subject when referred to as a more negative term.
The control’s Facebook ad image and copy referred to the Administrative State, while the treatment’s ad image and copy referred to the Deep State, and both received 50% of traffic. In addition, the control led to survey landing page with “Administrative State” language, and the treatment led to a survey page with “Deep State” language for continuity.
This test ran for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the term "Deep State" in place of "Administrative State" did not lead to an increase in clicks on the Facebook ad. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is only 15.9%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In addition to click rate, the treatment saw a valid 12.8% decrease in sign up rate on the landing page at a 97% level of confidence. This suggests that the term "Deep State" may have led to fewer people completing the survey.

Finally, the treatment saw a 8.5% decrease in donor acquisition rate on the post-survey landing page compared to the control, but at a 18.6% level of confidence, this result cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully design the study with a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical power. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variables or messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with the audience. Conducting thorough data analysis and ensuring statistical significance will help in making informed decisions based on experimental results.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the humanized sticky bar on the CYE Donation page for email 16 visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the personalized message boosts trust and creates a more personalized experience for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2024 - 12/30/2024
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we implemented a humanized sticky bar on the donation page as part of the strategy for Email 16.
This sticky bar ran for a full day, with the test limited to users who accessed the donation page via a link embedded in Email 16.
The sticky bar treatment was shown to 50% of the page’s traffic, allowing us to evaluate its effectiveness in driving engagement and conversions in this specific segment of our audience.
The control featured no sticky bar.
The treatment sticky bar featured Chuck Anderson, sender of Email 16, and had the following copy: “There’s only one day remaining to support IMB missionaries through your year-end gift to the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering®. As a reminder, your gift will play a crucial role in sharing the joy and peace of Jesus with those who have yet to hear His name. – Chuck Anderson”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the humanized sticky bar featuring Chuck Anderson on the donation page for Email 16 visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment saw a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 25.6%, indicating that the control group performed better.
It is important to note that the statistical confidence level is below the threshold of 95%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of humanized elements like sticky bars on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of humanized elements or personalize them based on donor preferences to see if this can lead to an increase in conversions. Additionally, ensuring a longer experiment duration and a larger sample size can help in obtaining more reliable and valid results for future decision making.

experiments.title: Does adding social proof on a landing page affect the conversion rate on an instant donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that adding social proof language (e.g., “___ people downloaded this ebook – Will you?”) to landing pages would increase donor conversion rates on the instant donation page by reinforcing the value and credibility of the content/Hoover.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on landing pages for content offers, focusing specifically on its effect on donor conversion rates. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer to determine whether this messaging could encourage more donations.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors during CYE will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitors to feel in control before being asked to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For IMB’s CYE campaign, we decided to test into donor autonomy on the homepage pop-up. We have had success with this concept with other clients, so we anticipated this concept would also perform well for IMB.
The hypothesis for this test is that having an option to say “yes” or “no” gives the potential donor a micro-decision to feel as if they are in control of the situation.
The control featured a single CTA: “Make my gift now »”
The treatment offered two options: “Yes, I’ll make a gift” and “No, not at this time” (this option closed out the pop-up)
We ran the pop-up from Dec 9 – Dec 27, 2025 with 50% of traffic receiving the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision on the homepage pop-up can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment, which included a "Yes, I'll make a gift" and "No, not at this time" option, resulted in a 25.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.7%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Despite the statistical level of confidence falling short, the experiment still provides valuable insights. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the concept of donor autonomy and how it influences visitor behavior. Additionally, it may be helpful to further refine the language and options presented in the pop-up to see if different variations can yield even better results.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment can inform future testing strategies and emphasize the importance of giving visitors a sense of control in their decision-making process on the website. This insight can be applied to optimize donation processes and potentially improve conversion rates in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Yes/No Slideout Compares to a Two-Step Slideout
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two-step slideout for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it provides the reader more autonomy answering the first question, and a clearer understanding of where the CTA will take them in the second.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/29/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a Yes/No slideout broken into two steps would increase donor conversion rate from homepage traffic. In the control, readers are presented with a Yes/No slideout, in which answering “yes” to the question of value then takes the user to the donation page. We believed that by breaking this question into a two-step process: “Have you been impacted by CaringBridge” and if so, “Will you make a gift?” will afford the reader more autonomy, while also better priming them for the main question of whether or not they will give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the two-step slideout for users on the homepage did not achieve the increased donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 18.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Based on these results, it is not advisable to make any significant changes to the donation process on the CaringBridge homepage. Future experimentation should focus on different variables or approaches to improving donor conversion rates. It is essential to carefully analyze the results of each experiment and ensure that the statistical significance is robust before making any decisions based on the findings.

experiments.title: Will adding a personal ask to a campaign wikibanner increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing the wiki banner ask CTA for the cye campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on this high traffic page because the donor will connect with Jim, which adds a personal element to the ask during this season of highly motivated giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2024 - 12/19/2024
For all Catalytic campaigns, FOTF has tested having a wiki banner on their home page. In previous campaigns, we have tested several different tactics and messages and have combined all of the winners to get to where the control is.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if personalizing the ask by adding an image of Jim and having the ask be from Jim would affect the donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding an image of Jim and having the ask come from him in the wiki banner for the CYE campaign led to a statistically significant increase in donor conversion rates compared to our control.

2. The treatment group saw an 11.8% increase in transactions for all traffic, indicating that donors may connect more with a personal appeal from a specific individual like Jim.

3. While the results were statistically significant at a 78.8% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity. This means that the results should be interpreted with caution and should be used in further testing.

4.This treatment approach was ran at a time of year where donor's motivation to give is high. Our team would like to retest during another campaign and during an "evergreen" month to see if the results would be similar.

5. Ordinarily, we'd test this test longer but during a high urgency campaign like CYE, we have a more aggressive testing schedule.

experiments.title: What’s the Impact of Adding Additional Giving Options on Content Pages Through a Lower Third Banner?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the lower third instead of a wikipedia ad for a direct ask on content pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the visibility of the ask will be greater and there are more options to give on this specific element..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2024 - 12/09/2024
During previous catalytic campaigns. FOTF has tested into having what we call “Wikipedia” ads on the top scroll of their content pages. We want to know if adding a new look to our CTA during CYE help draw more eyes to the opportunity to give and if having additional options to give increases donor conversion and to what degree.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which involved adding lower thirds instead of a dear reader for a direct ask on content pages, resulted in a 65.5% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is 0.0%, meaning that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could have influenced the outcome, such as the timing of the experiment, the audience segment targeted, or the messaging used in the lower thirds.

It is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and to take into account all possible variables that could impact the results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different variations of the ask, including both dear reader messages and direct asks, to determine the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rates on content pages.

experiments.title: How focusing on the impact of a donor’s gift affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the impact of the donor’s gift rather than mentioning the total financial goal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it emphasizes the immediate and tangible outcomes of their support, making the decision to give feel more personal and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ calendar year-end fundraising campaign. The experiment was conducted through an email campaign sent to The Navigators’ donor base. It involved A/B testing, where the donor audience was split into two groups: one receiving the Control email and the other receiving the Treatment email.
The experiment aimed to refine messaging by focusing on tangible outcomes that resonate with The Navigators’ mission-driven donor base. Highlighting direct impact was expected to feel more personal and actionable, avoiding the discouragement large financial goals can create, and ultimately improving conversion rates.
The goal was to test if shifting focus from organizational goals to donor impact would improve donor engagement and conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing on the impact of the donor's gift rather than mentioning the total financial goal can result in an increase in click-through rate, as evidenced by a 19.3% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. This indicates that emphasizing immediate and tangible outcomes of support can make the decision to give feel more personal and actionable for donors. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, meaning these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Additionally, the treatment group saw am invalid 16% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a 44% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing messaging that highlights the impact of donor support in a personalized and actionable way. By refining messaging to focus on tangible outcomes, we have the potential to improve donor engagement and conversion rates. However, it is essential to ensure that results reach a statistically significant level of confidence before implementing any changes based on experimentation. Additionally, exploring different ways to communicate impact and value to donors can further optimize messaging strategies for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: Does changing donation form copy to be more donor-focused affect conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor-focused language for users who downloaded the Fall Digest will improve conversion rates because emphasizing a sense of belonging and camaraderie taps into donors’ intrinsic motivations to contribute to a shared cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2024 - 01/11/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The experiment evaluated the impact of copy variations on the conversion rate of the Fall Digest instant donation page. The test focused on donor-focused language inspired by top-performing offers, with the goal of enhancing a sense of belonging and camaraderie to drive higher conversions. This experiment targeted users who downloaded the digital version of the Fall Digest and were directed to a donation page.

experiments.title: How testing a dual action slide-out on article pages impacts donor conversion during FamilyLife’s high urgency calendar year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation ask into two steps will increase conversion rates. By first asking whether FamilyLife has provided value and then following up with a donation request, we reduce cognitive load and create a smoother, less overwhelming user experience, which will lead to higher engagement and more conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, conducted this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign. FamilyLife uses a sticky donation slide-out that is always visible on article pages. This slide-out prompts users with the message: “Has FamilyLife given you and your family $25 worth of practical resources and biblical insights this year? If so, can you take a moment to donate to ensure that we’re always here for you when your family needs encouragement and support?” Users can then click “Yes” to be directed to the donation page, or “No” to close the element.
For this campaign, we wanted to test whether splitting the question into two parts would lead to higher conversion rates. In the Treatment, when the user first clicks on the sticky slide-out, they would only see the first part of the question—“Has FamilyLife given you $25 worth of practical resources and biblical insights this year?” If they click “Yes,” the second part of the message would appear, asking for a donation. If they click “No,” the element would close without prompting for a donation.
We believe that splitting the ask into two steps could improve engagement and conversion for several reasons. First, by breaking down the question, we reduce the initial cognitive load on users, making the first interaction feel less like a commitment and more like a simple, low-effort engagement. This might encourage more people to click “Yes.” Once they’ve answered affirmatively, they’ll then be presented with a clearer, more straightforward ask for a donation, which could feel less transactional and more natural. Additionally, by limiting the ask to one step at a time, we avoid overwhelming users with too much information or too big of a request at once, which could increase the likelihood of a conversion.
The Control group used the standard, single-message approach, while the Treatment group implemented the dual ask. This experiment was set up as a split A/B test with the traffic split 50/50 between the Control (standard slide-out) and the Treatment (dual ask). The goal was to determine if breaking the ask into two steps would create a smoother, more engaging donation flow, leading to higher conversions. Both the Control and Treatment groups used the exact same language in their messaging and was tested on the article pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 21 days. The Treatment (dual ask) led to a 50.9% increase in conversions, but with a 68.90% level of confidence, which means it didn’t fully validate. Despite not reaching the typical 95% confidence threshold, we decided to roll out the Treatment early due to the high urgency of the calendar year-end campaign.
The Control (standard sticky slide-out) generated 10 transactions, while the Treatment (dual ask) resulted in 15 transactions.
We believe the dual ask led to more conversions because it reduced cognitive overload by presenting a lower-effort, initial question. This likely made users more inclined to engage with the content, as the first interaction felt less like a commitment. Additionally, by breaking the ask into two steps, users had more time to process the request, leading to a higher likelihood of converting once they expressed interest in donating. The treatment also benefited from the fact that the second ask for a donation only appeared after the user clicked "Yes"—a step that could have made the donation request feel more natural and less transactional.
We recommend retesting the dual ask in FamilyLife’s next high-urgency campaigns to gather more data and validate its impact on conversions. Additionally, there may be an opportunity to explore this format as a permanent feature, especially if future testing confirms its effectiveness over a longer period and across various campaigns.

experiments.title: How will adding a visual cue to a donation page effect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a visual thermomether for the capaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the thermometer would add urgency to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2025
During CYE we know that there is a ton of traffic that make their way to the KCBI donation page on the last 4-5 days of the year. Due to this high volume of motivated donors to give to KCBI, but also to give before the tax deadline of December 31st.
For this test we wanted to see if we could add a visual cue that would show the amount of time left to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a visual thermometer to the campaign donation page resulted in a 29.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, these results should not be considered valid or used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further experiments or iterate on the design of the visual thermometer to determine its true impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: What happens when you replace an ad with a dear reader
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing an ad style cta with a dear reader for users that are on the article pages will achieve a higher donor converstion rate because a dear reader contains more value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2024 - 04/16/2024
Focus on the Family receives a ton of traffic to their article pages every day. In an effort to further engage this audience there are several ad style CTAs on the page to lead users to different video series or content offers. We wondered if we could test an “ad style” cta with a dear reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that replacing an ad style CTA with a "dear reader" message on article pages resulted in a 32.6% increase in revenue for all traffic. This indicates that although more people don't convert when seeing the dear reader, they do give more generously when they get to the donation page.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of the "dear reader" message against other CTAs or variations. By conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, more conclusive insights can be gained to inform decisions related to optimizing donor conversion rates on article pages.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience on the donation page convert donors better?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addinga. guided reading experience for the campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion because the copy makes it easier to understand what problem the organization is solving .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For the CYE campaign this year, we wanted to test into having a guided reading experience on the main campaign donation page. We’ve seen this work well in acquisition where it lays out the problem, the solution, and how the donor can help.
We wondered if running this during a high urgency campaign would work as well as it did during non campaign months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the guided reading experience on the campaign donation page resulted in a 23.6% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 46.0%, which is below the threshold of 95% needed to consider the results valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

While this test is more of a coin toss on whether it will help transactions, it did increase the average gift from $84 to $106 by adding in the subheaders to the copy.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the guided reading experience and iterate on the copy to better communicate the problem the organization is solving and the impact of donor contributions. Conducting more experiments with different variations and analyzing the results with a larger sample size will provide more reliable insights for decision-making in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a signer’s image in an email footer signature in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including Chitwood’s image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 will achieve an increase in clicks because the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message. because  the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to drive higher email engagement during IMB’s CYE campaign, we conducted a test incorporating an image of Paul Chitwood, IMB’s President, in the email sign-off. The hypothesis was that featuring Chitwood’s image would enhance the personal connection with readers, allowing them to associate a face with the name and fostering a stronger sense of trust and rapport. This, in turn, was expected to increase the likelihood of readers making a donation.
We implemented this test in Email 12 of the CYE campaign, with 50% of the traffic receiving the version featuring Chitwood’s image to assess its impact on engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including Paul Chitwood's image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 did not result in the expected increase in clicks. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 3.8% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Based on these results, it is clear that the impact of including Chitwood's image on email engagement is inconclusive. Therefore, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and ensure that the results reach a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes to the email strategy.
Furthermore, we saw a 24.5% decrease in transactions at a 61.4% LOC. While this metric did not reach 95% validity, it does suggest that the presence of Chitwood's image reduced the motivation to give once those who clicked landed on the donation page.
Finally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to enhance personal connections and trust with readers, such as through compelling storytelling, personalized content, or highlighting the impact of donations. By continuously testing and refining different strategies, IMB can better understand what resonates with their audience and optimize their email campaigns for improved engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: Does focusing on a physical premium on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by referencing the downloaded content offer and leaning into the electronic versus physical nature of the benefit, the test version would create a more seamless donor experience and increase conversion rates by making the Sustainer benefit more prominent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2024 - 11/09/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of tailored messaging on a content offer donation page for “The People’s Pharmacy.” The goal was to determine whether referencing the content of the offer and emphasizing the electronic nature of the benefit would enhance donor conversion rates by making the experience feel more seamless and highlighting the value of becoming a Sustainer.

experiments.title: Will having a guided reading experience on the donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a guided reading experience for the main campaign cye donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the copy will be laid out in a way that makes it easier to digest and explains how the donor makes a difference.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For our CYE campaign, we wanted to see if having a clear guided reading experience on the main campaign donation page would influence people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a guided reading experience for the main campaign donation page had a positive impact on donor conversion rates, resulting in an 8.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 24.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% needed to consider the results as valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to take into account the level of statistical confidence when interpreting results. While the increase in donations is promising, it is crucial to conduct further testing to validate the impact of the guided reading experience on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other aspects of the donation page and conduct A/B tests to optimize the layout, copy, and design for improved donor engagement and conversion. By continuing to experiment and gather data, Care Net can make more informed decisions to enhance its fundraising efforts and ultimately achieve greater success in driving donations for its campaigns.

experiments.title: Will having a specific donor goal increase the likelihood to give
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific donor goal for the homepage sticky bar on Giving Tuesday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a specific goal that is counting down shows the social proof that other people are giving and provides inclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For Giving Tuesday this year, we wanted to see if having a specific micro goal would increase conversion on the homepage sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a specific donor goal for the homepage sticky bar on Giving Tuesday did not increase donor conversion rates as hypothesized. The treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only at 31.1%.
