In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that there is a high level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the design of the sticky bar, the messaging used, or the timing of the campaign. It is also valuable to test different hypotheses and continue to learn from each experiment to optimize donation strategies in the future.

experiments.title: How highlighting the impact of a gift impacts click rate in a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the impact of the donor’s gift for Email 5 recipients will achieve an increase in the click through rate because it allows the donor to connect their action directly to meaningful outcomes.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For The Navigators CYE campaign, we tested into highlighting the impact of a donor’s gift within the email copy.
We tested this concept on Email 5 with 50% of email recipient’s getting the treatment version.
Our hypothesis was that doing so would connect their action (the gift) directly to meaningful outcomes, such as discipling young believers and strengthening faith communities. We thought the emotional appeal would be likely to resonate with recipients, inspiring a sense of purpose and urgency to give, compared to the practical framing of simply checking off a to-do list.
With this change, we expected to see an increase in the click through rate and as a result an increase in donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the impact of the donor's gift in the email copy for The Navigators' CYE campaign did not result in the expected increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 10.7% decrease in clicks compared to the control version at a 59.6% LOC.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Furthermore, we wanted to know whether this language produced more motivated clicks. The control produced 25 donations out of the 107 clicks and the treatment produced 19 donations out of the 115 clicks. Therefore, the treatment resulted in a 29.3% decrease in transactions at a 79.8% LOC. While again, these results are not valid, they do suggest that this type of language reduces the motivation to give for The Navigators audience.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing different messaging and approaches to determine what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring a combination of emotional and practical appeals to see what drives engagement and donations. Additionally, conducting experiments with a larger sample size and ensuring a statistically significant confidence level will provide more reliable results to inform future campaign strategies.

experiments.title: Will adding a humanized touch to an appeal increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making a humanized touch for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion because having the ask come from a specific person increases trust, and makes the ask a little more personal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For the CYE homepage sticky bar, we wanted to see if adding an image of Roland on the ask and positioning it to be from Roland will convert donors better, rather than having just a regular ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Adding an image of Roland on the homepage sticky bar increased donations by 24.0%.

- The statistical level of confidence of 71.8% is below the recommended 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid.

- The hypothesis that a humanized touch would increase donor conversion was supported by the results.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Consider factors that may have influenced the lower statistical confidence, such as sample size or variability.

- Repeat the experiment with adjustments to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

- Continue exploring the idea of a humanized touch in donation asks, as it showed potential for increasing conversions.

experiments.title: How using a gift array based on HPC impacted email donations during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a gift array based on highest previous contribution for donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. During their year-end campaign, we wanted to see whether basing a gift array on a donor’s highest previous contribution made an impact on conversion rates.
The control version had a standard gift array of $100/250/500/Other.
The treatment based the gift array on HPC, and included 1.5X HPC, 2X HPC, 2.5X HPC, and other.
This experiment was run across 3 of the last emails of the year (12/27, 12/30, and 12/31) to active and lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a gift array based on a donor's highest previous contribution can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment group in this experiment showed a 23.8% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that personalizing the gift array based on donor behavior may impact conversion rates positively.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size to ensure more statistically significant results. It would also be valuable to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the timing of the donation appeal, messaging, or donor segmentation.

Overall, while this experiment showed a potential impact of personalized gift arrays on donation rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: What’s the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a campagin?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the homepage ask in a different way than a sticky bar for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the popup will draw a users eye directly to it, where the sticky bar could have banner blindness.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
In the past, Care Net was tested into using sticky bars on their homepage. Each campaign season we try to test adding a new elements to the sticky bar and messaging to see what is the best way to present it in order to get donors to convert. This year, we wondered if there was an opportunity to try something new to see if the sticky bar was still the best method. For this we created a slide up gift array that will show on the homepage that contains copy, and three specific ask amounts for people to start their donation on the homepage itself. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the popup gift array did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the sticky bar for the CYE campaign. In fact, there was an 18.4% decrease in donations with a 39.8% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control (sticky bar) performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential for banner blindness with the sticky bar but also to acknowledge that new elements may not always outperform existing ones. It is crucial to thoroughly test any changes before fully implementing them, especially when the results do not reach a statistical level of confidence of at least 95%. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective method for increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding a humanized ask to a dear reader increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a picture of Roland for the CYE campaign dear readers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because their donor audience had previously responded well to personal asks from Roland.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
Earlier in our CYE campaign this year we found that adjusting the homepage sticky bar to be from a specific person increased conversions on the homepage. We wondered if the same would hold true for the dear readers.
For this test we created a dear reader that had the same copy and the same placement but added Roland’s image and signate to it. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment testing the addition of Roland's image to the CYE campaign dear reader showed a 32.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, compared to the control. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 63.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results are not considered valid for future decision making.

Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding Roland's image to the dear reader did not result in an increase in donor conversions, contrary to the hypothesis.

2. The previous success of personal asks from Roland on the homepage may not necessarily translate to other aspects of the website.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Conduct further tests with higher statistical confidence levels to draw more reliable conclusions.

2. Consider different approaches to personalization and messaging to determine what resonates best with the donor audience.

3. Continuously analyze and iterate on experimental results to enhance the effectiveness of donation strategies and optimize conversions.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonial on the donation page reinforce the need to make a gift?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating. because  the testimonial adds social proof and value proof and value proposition as to why others are donating.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could get more people to become donors on the giving page by having a donor testimonial on the page itself.
Adding in extra value proposition to a donation page is not a new thing to test out, but with this test specifically we wanted to see if we could present it in a different way where the testimonial stands out and is able to convert more donors because it draws the eye to it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the Giving Tuesday campaign donation page could potentially lead to an increase in donor conversions. While the treatment did see a 3.0% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 13.2%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95% for validity.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials on donation pages, but with variations in how they are presented or combined with other elements to determine the most effective way to leverage social proof and value proposition. Additionally, it would be important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to generate statistically significant results, which can be used with confidence to inform decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: How the placement of a Dear Listener message vs. an Impact Pop-up on a donation page impacts donor conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Impact Pop-up for FamilyLife’s calendar year-end campaign will increase donor conversion better than the Dear Listener message because the pop-up creates a more immediate sense of urgency and engagement, encouraging users to take action right away.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2023 - 12/16/2023
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, ran this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign, which began on December 2nd. Historically, FamilyLife has relied on a “Dear Listener” tactic at the end of their content—an approach that appeals directly to their audience and has proven successful in driving donations during previous high urgency campaigns. For this test, however, we sought to explore whether a different tactic could outperform this traditional method in terms of donor conversion.
To determine whether a more interactive and urgent format would yield higher conversions, we tested the Impact Pop-up against the “Dear Listener” message. The “Dear Listener” message was used in the Control group, maintaining the traditional approach where the appeal is made at the end of the content. In the Treatment group, we implemented the Impact Pop-up, which was triggered during the user’s session and emphasized the urgency of supporting the mission. The pop-up also featured an image of a family reading a Bible to reinforce the message and visually engage users.
This experiment was set up as a split A/B test in VWO, with the traffic being split 50/50 between the Control (Dear Listener) and the Treatment (Impact Pop-up). We tested these tactics on their podcast pages. Both versions used similar messaging, but the key differences were the format and the visibility of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran from December 2nd to December 16th, a two-week period, and although it did not reach the ideal 30-day threshold for full validation, the Impact Pop-up showed a promising 51.8% increase in conversions with a 67.6% likelihood of conversion (LoC) compared to the Dear Listener message. The Dear Listener generated 9 transactions, while the Impact Pop-up generated 14 transactions. While the result is compelling, it was still a bit too early to validate fully, but given the urgency of the campaign, the decision was made to roll out the Impact Pop-up.

The Impact Pop-up likely performed better because it created a sense of urgency by appearing during the user’s session, prompting immediate action. Unlike the Dear Listener message, which required users to scroll down the page to see it, the Impact Pop-up was triggered after 7 seconds of page engagement, ensuring that most visitors to the page would see it. This guaranteed visibility likely contributed to higher engagement and conversion, as users didn’t need to actively seek out the message. The emotional appeal of the image of a family reading the Bible further reinforced the urgency, making the call-to-action feel more relevant and personal for users.

Given the success of the Impact Pop-up in this initial period, we recommend rolling it out as the primary tactic for future high urgency campaigns. However, since the experiment didn’t run long enough to validate fully, we suggest retesting, continuing to monitor its performance closely, and conducting follow-up testing to further optimize the pop-up’s design and messaging. Additionally, testing with a larger sample size and longer duration could help validate its effectiveness across different user segments.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial box with a social proof headline impacts donor conversions during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial box with the headline “Make an Impact on readers like __” to the right side of FamilyLife’s donation page will increase donor conversions by providing social proof and illustrating the tangible impact of donations, thereby fostering trust and emotional connection with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2024 - 12/31/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing biblically-based resources and support to families. This experiment was conducted during their high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, a critical time for driving donor engagement and conversions.
Historically, FamilyLife’s donation pages have focused on clearly presenting the campaign’s messaging and donation options without additional elements like testimonials. For this test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating social proof could enhance donor trust and urgency.
To test this, we added a testimonial box featuring a real quote from a FamilyLife supporter, paired with a social proof headline: “Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!” This headline aimed to create a sense of community and inspire donors to contribute by showcasing the tangible impact of their support.
The Control was the standard donation page without a testimonial, and the Treatment included the testimonial box prominently placed near the donation form. This was a split A/B test built in VWO with 50/50 split traffic, ensuring an equal number of users experienced both the Control and the Treatment.
The goal of this test was to determine whether the addition of social proof could increase donor conversion rates during this critical fundraising period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Treatment featuring the testimonial box with the headline, "Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!", led to a 6.2% decrease in transactions compared to the Control at a 70% level of confidence (LoC). While the results did not validate due to insufficient data over the testing period, the downward trend suggests that the testimonial box may have negatively impacted conversions.

We hypothesize that the addition of the testimonial box could have distracted users from focusing on the primary goal: completing the donation. While the testimonial and headline aimed to build trust and provide social proof, it may have introduced unnecessary cognitive load or drawn attention away from the donation form itself. Alternatively, the headline or the quote could have lacked relevance or resonance for some users, reducing its effectiveness as a motivator.

Next Steps:

Given the negative trend, we recommend retesting this concept with adjustments:
Rework the Design: Explore a more streamlined and less visually dominant placement for the testimonial box.
 	Refine the Content: Test different quotes or headlines that may better resonate with the audience or align more closely with the campaign’s urgency.
 	Alternative Social Proof Strategies: Consider integrating testimonials in a subtler way, such as below the donation form or as part of an impact statement, to avoid distracting users.

experiments.title: How Positioning a Monthly Ask Effects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning a monthly ask to be more about the donor for the giving tuesday ask will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because positioning the ask to be more donor focused instead of transactional will create a sense of community.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
For our CYE campaign this year we decided to have Giving Tuesday be it’s own micro goal.
To make Giving Tuesday stand out we created a micro goal to get 50 monthly donors in one day. Since this was strickly a monthly ask, we wondered what the best way to position the ask would be. Will having the ask be more inclusive or transactional make a difference?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that positioning a monthly donation ask to be more about the donor for Giving Tuesday can potentially lead to a lower donor conversion rate. While the treatment did see a 1.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing with different messaging and positioning strategies to see what resonates best with donors. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to increase the statistical power and confidence level of the results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the ask, the target audience, and the overall campaign messaging can also impact the effectiveness of the donation appeal.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further testing and optimization of donation asks to maximize donor conversions and support for the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the gift array to IMB’s CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift array minimizes the number of steps that need to take place on the actual donation page therefore minimizing friction and decision making resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to boost IMB’s conversion rate from their CYE Sticky Bar, we opted to test into including a gift array on the sticky bar.
The gift array options were fully functional, and when clicked would auto fill the donation page with the applicable amount selected. We had three gift array options available on the sticky bar: $25, $75, and $250.
This test ran from Dec. 11 – Dec. 27th, 2024 with 50% of the traffic seeing the treatment version (gift array).
We hypothesized that adding the gift array would reduce friction on the donation page by minimizing the steps needed to complete a donation, ultimately resulting in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a gift array on the CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversions for IMB. In fact, the treatment version of the sticky bar with the gift array saw a 23.4% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
Despite the decrease in donations, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only at 66.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In terms of overall revenue, the control produced $15,686 and the treatment produced $10,930. Resulting in a 30.3% decrease in revenue at a 74.8% LOC.
We also looked further into the gift array amounts to see if one greatly outperformed the others.
$25 Button5 gifts$120 in Revenue$75 Button2 gifts$200 in Revenue$250 Button5 gifts$1,500 in Revenue
The remaining gifts came through the "More options" button that did not preselect a gift amount in the form meaning about half of users chose the "more options" button.
While these results are inconclusive, they do suggest that iMB's audience may react more positively to a standard sticky bar rather the gift array options.
In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and potentially tweak the gift array options or placement on the sticky bar to see if different configurations could yield more positive results. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is reached before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on A/B test results.

experiments.title: How including the gift array in a sticky bar affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a gift array on a sticky bar for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2024 - 12/27/2024
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including the gift array on the sticky bar of their website.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the sticky bar: the control version was a standard sticky bar with a button to take them to the donation page and the treatment version included the gift array in the sticky bar which took them to the donation page and pre-selected the amount they chose on the sticky bar.
Their hypothesis was that the gift array would increase donor conversion rate because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring.
By making the options immediately visible and actionable, we anticipate that users will be more engaged and motivated to complete their donation, leading to higher conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Including a gift array on the sticky bar did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment version saw a 0.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence was only 0.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This indicates that the results of the experiment are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. In this case, the experiment ran for 20 days, but it may have been beneficial to run it for a longer period or with a larger sample size.

- Consider other factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as the placement of the sticky bar on the website, the wording and language used in the sticky bar, and the overall user experience on the donation page.

- Continue to test different strategies and elements on the website to optimize donor conversion rates. While this experiment did not yield the desired results, it is important to learn from it and apply those learnings to future experiments to improve the effectiveness of donation strategies.

experiments.title: How using a donor “statement” for lapsed donors impacted donor conversions rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donor statement for lapsed donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/31/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
This year during their year-end campaign, Avila was promoting their largest-ever match, with a triple match as well. We wanted to see whether using a donor ‘statement’ at the top of emails to remind lapsed donors of their last gift date and amount would impact donor conversion rates. We ran this copy test on their active donor audience, through a total of 8 emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a donor statement for lapsed donors, resulted in a 16.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 46.6%, meaning that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.

3. Despite the increase in donations, the lack of statistical significance indicates that the effect may not be reliably replicable.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Ensure that the sample size is sufficient to detect statistically significant differences.

2. Consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

3. Test the impact of donor statements on different donor segments to see if the effect varies among different groups.

4. Analyze additional metrics such as open rates, click-through rates, and conversion rates to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences.

5. Consider testing other variables such as messaging, imagery, or incentives to identify factors that truly drive donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend in donations with the use of donor statements for lapsed donors, it is essential to gather more robust data to make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How including compelling images in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including compelling images in a high urgency appeal for CYE Email 14 will achieve an increase in clicks because the presence of the images allows the reader to visually connect withe the mission of the IMB.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To increase email click rates during IMB’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding compelling images to Email 14. The treatment was presented to 50% of the recipients of this email.
Our hypothesis was that incorporating these visuals would help readers establish a stronger emotional connection with IMB’s mission, thereby increasing the likelihood of clicking the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including compelling images in high urgency appeal emails may not necessarily lead to an increase in clicks for donation links. The treatment group, which received emails with images, actually saw a 2.9% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it would be important to thoroughly analyze the data and ensure that the findings are statistically significant before drawing any conclusions. It may also be beneficial to test different types of images or messaging to see what truly resonates with recipients and drives them to click on donation links.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy language during a year-end campaign impacts email revenue.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor autonomy language for active donors will achieve a change in email donation conversions..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/31/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
This year during their year-end campaign, Avila was promoting their largest-ever match, with a triple match as well. We wanted to see whether using donor autonomy language–allowing the donor to make a choice to give–throughout the entire campaign would make a difference. We ran this copy test on their active donor audience, through a total of 8 emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomy language for active donors may have a positive impact on donation conversions, as evidenced by the 4.3% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.9%, which is below the threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure a larger sample size to improve the statistical significance of the results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions among active donors, such as the timing of the campaign, the messaging used, or the specific calls to action.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit of using donor autonomy language, further research is needed to confirm these results and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: Does adding a gift array to a dear-reader effect transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a gift array to the “Dear Reader” message, instead of just a button, will result in more donations by providing clear giving options. This approach simplifies decision-making and appeals to a wider range of donors, encouraging both recurring contributions and one-time gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The goal of the test was to determine whether adding a gift array to a “Dear Reader” message, placed in the middle of articles, would lead to an increase in both recurring donations and overall contributions.
The body copy on both dear-readers remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment comparing the "Dear Reader" message to the "Dear Reader with Gift Array" approach showed that adding a gift array did not lead to improved performance in terms of donation rates or average gift amounts.

One-Time Donations:
The gift array version had a lower conversion rate (0.04%) compared to the standard message (0.06%), reflecting a 22.3% decrease with a 38.5% confidence level.
 	The average gift for the gift array was significantly lower ($17.25) than the standard message ($48.82).
Recurring Donations:
Both approaches yielded the same conversion rate (0.01%), showing no advantage for the gift array.
 	The average gift remained lower for the gift array ($17.25) versus the standard ($48.82).
Overall Transactions:
The gift array had a 27.4% lower conversion rate than the standard message, with a 51.0% confidence level.
 	The average gift for the gift array ($17.25) was considerably less than the standard ($48.82).
Key Takeaway:

experiments.title: How does adding value prop and description of a premium to the donation page impact conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop and description of the devotional for web visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because there is more information about the offer which leads to confidence in knowing what you will receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/06/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During November and December, PTV promotes their annual Daily Devotional. During this time, we decided to test adding copy to the donation page.
For this test, traffic came from the PTV website, email, and paid media channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop and a description of the devotional on the donation page did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group, although the statistical level of confidence was only 42.6%.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration in order to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment and the specific audience targeted.

When designing future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different versions of the value prop and description to see if there is a more effective way to communicate the benefits of the devotional. Additionally, exploring other elements of the donation page, such as the layout and design, could provide further insights into what encourages visitors to complete a transaction.

experiments.title: Which type of language (community membership or preserving free access) results in more donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that Variant 1 will result in more donations because it emphasizes the tangible perks and thank-you gifts that donors receive. The WUNC audience is often motivated by rewards and incentives, and offering gifts or exclusive benefits may create a stronger sense of immediate value and gratification.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The goal was to test which type of messaging language would make website visitors more likely to donate. Both messaging approaches were presented as slide-up prompts on the homepage of WUNC’s website.
Variant 1: Joining a Club
This approach emphasized becoming a Sustainer—a donor who gives regularly and enjoys special perks, such as thank-you gifts and prize entries. It targeted individuals who want to feel like long-term supporters of the station and value a sense of belonging to a community.
Variant 2: Keeping It Free
This approach focused on preserving WUNC’s free, paywall-free access to news and programming. It highlighted the station’s reliance on listener donations to remain freely available. The messaging aimed to appeal to individuals who value open access and might be motivated by gratitude to make a one-time contribution.
By testing these two distinct strategies, the experiment sought to identify which type of language—community membership or preserving free access—resonated more with potential donors and encouraged greater contributions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results of this experiment, Variant 1 (Joining a Club) outperformed Variant 2 (Keeping It Free) in terms of overall donation conversion rates. Variant 1 achieved a 2.6% conversion rate compared to Variant 2's 2.4% conversion rate, representing a 5.7% higher performance.
While Variant 2 emphasized preserving free access to WUNC's content, Variant 1's focus on perks and thank-you gifts appeared to resonate more with website visitors, leading to slightly higher donation rates. This suggests that offering tangible rewards and emphasizing membership benefits may be more effective at driving immediate contributions than messaging centered around maintaining free access.
Note: Although Variant 2 reportedly generated more recurring donations, this data is not validated and cannot be factored into the conclusions drawn from this experiment. Further research may be required to evaluate long-term donor retention strategies.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How a Slide-Up performs against an Exit-Intent during a Holiday Appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up with gift array for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because the experience is less intrusive than the pop-up, and the in-line array pre-fills the donor’s selected amount on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we conducted a re-test of the Slide-Up with gift array vs the standard Exit Intent pop-up. The goal was to determine whether there was a significant impact upon donations and revenue during a high urgency appeal – where motivation is significantly higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-up with gift array did not perform as well as the standard exit intent pop-up in terms of donations during the high urgency appeal. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 55.0%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

However, this test did help troubleshoot an earlier discrepancy between the slide-up and exit intent where traffic targeting was unfairly impacting results. In reality, when distributed to traffic properly 50/50, the two onsites performed similarly.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because because it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift during year-end would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within three emails they sent throughout their year-end campaign. This write-up shows the results of email 7 which was an appeal for education.
The control email presented the matching gift by stating that their gift would be doubled thanks to their year-end matching gift challenge up to $100,000. The treatment email presented the matching gift by stating that a generous donor wants to personally match their gift allowing them to have double the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a more personal approach to asking for a matching gift can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment email, which presented the matching gift as being from a generous donor wanting to personally match the contribution, resulted in a 33.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control email.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was 77.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and personal connection in donation appeals can motivate potential donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, particularly when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.

As for the effect on email click-through rate, the treatment email saw no difference in click-through rate. There was a 1.9% increase in clicks with only a 21% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of a personal ask for matching gifts with a larger sample size to see if the trend towards increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, considering other variables such as the timing of the ask, the messaging used, and the target audience could provide further insights into what drives donor behavior and how to optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock on a donation page in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to the donation page will increase the donation conversion rate because it creates urgency by highlighting the time remaining until the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/01/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including a countdown clock on the donation for the final 4 days of the campaign.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the donation page: the control version without a countdown clock and the treatment version with a countdown clock. Their hypothesis was that the countdown clock would create a sense of urgency, encouraging more users to make a donation before the deadline (When the countdown clock ran out).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a countdown clock on the donation page did not have the intended impact of increasing donation conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group, which had the countdown clock, experienced a 6.6% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the effectiveness of different strategies to create urgency or incentivize donations, such as limited-time offers or matching donation challenges, to see what resonates best with donors. Conducting further A/B tests with a larger sample size and varying durations may provide more conclusive insights for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page where people can make a gift in order to become a member of Heritage. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from on-site buttons and popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the gift amount displayed saw a 0.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control version, but at a 3.92% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing different variations of the submit button language and design to see what impact they have on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run experiments for a longer duration and with more traffic to ensure reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a quiz affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a quiz will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 10/15/2024
The Heritage Foundation has an offer where people can take a quiz to test their knowledge on Marxism and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight the Marxist movement in America today. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that decreasing the gift array amounts from $50, $100, and $250 in the control group to $25, $50, and $100 in the treatment group did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, but at a 3.6% level of confidence, this result is invalid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before drawing any conclusions or making changes based on the results. This can be helped by increasing traffic to the test page. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the messaging, imagery, or targeting strategies used in the donation process. Conducting further experiments with different variables and analyzing the results with a higher level of confidence will help in making informed decisions to optimize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How “Fighting the Negative” language on a donation page effects end of year giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the cta from a “fight the negative” perspective for prospects and members will achieve a lift in transactions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2024 - 12/16/2024
The team decided to test “Fighting the negative” copy against the more positive “Save the American Dream” copy. The treatment copy emphasized the impact of the radical Left’s bad policies and our need to not just overcome them but to utterly destroy and dismantle their grip on America. In the past because giving intent is high during end of year, it is difficult to get a valid experiment so the decision was made after week to end the test. The test showed a directional increase in transactions for the “Fighting the negative” version that did not mention fighting the “Administrative State” in the headline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the "Fighting the negative" copy without mentioning fighting the "Administrative State" in the headline led to a 16.4% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence, ideally above 85%, to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions. Additionally, we could consider further testing different variations of the copy to see if there is a consistent increase in transactions with the "Fighting the negative" messaging. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing the results of future experiments to gather more data and insights that can drive more successful outcomes.

experiments.title: How does customizing a gift array based on a donor’s giving history impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that customizing the gift array for donors will achieve a higher revenue per donor because the amount is specific to their giving history.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s year-end efforts, we decided to test the gift array on our donation page.
The control gift array for everyone is $25, $50, $100, $200
We used the donor’s HPC (highest previous contribution) or largest gift as the base for the treatment. Then we multiplied the amount by 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.
To execute this experiment
This organization uses HubSpot as its email marketing platform and Anedot as a donation platform, but this experiment is possible with other platforms.
First, we created new calculated contact properties within HubSpot, which took the largest gift amount and multiplied it by 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3. We also had the contact property round up to the nearest dollar so the donor would not see random amounts with cents.
Second, we reviewed Anedot’s documentation on how to pass the custom amounts.
This test can still be conducted for organizations that do not have these platforms.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the custom amounts and upload them to your email marketing platform as new fields. Then consult your donation platform documentation to see if/how to pass custom amounts.
If your donation platform does not allow custom amounts, you could break this test into buckets based on HPC. For instance, $1-50, $51-100, $101-150, $151-200, etc, or in whatever buckets best fit your organization. Then, you can AB test by sending half of the bucket to the control and half to a different donation page with a gift array that best fits that bucket’s range.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that customizing the gift array for donors based on their giving history can potentially lead to an increase in revenue per donor. In this particular case, the treatment with customized gift amounts based on the donor's highest previous contribution saw a 40.6% increase in revenue for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 79.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We will be conducting further analysis on this test to see how much more individuals gave based on the gift array.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal thermometer to a year-end donation form impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/30/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For their year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether using a donor goal thermometer on the year-end donation form will impact donor conversions. In order to hit the $320,000 goal, Avila needed 500 donors to step up and make a donation. In this experiment, the control had no thermometer; the treatment automatically updated based on gifts to that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors resulted in a 19.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. While the treatment group did outperform the control group, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.0%, falling below the typical threshold of 95% for valid results.

Moving forward, it is important to note that these results should not be used for future decision-making as they do not meet the standard threshold for statistical significance. However, the experiment does provide some insights into the potential impact of using a donor thermometer on donor conversions.

For future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the use of a donor thermometer but with adjustments to the implementation or design in order to potentially achieve statistically significant results. Additionally, considering a longer duration for the experiment and a larger sample size may also help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How sharing the impact of a gift to the mission of the organization impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers will achieve a higher click rate because there is greater correlation between their support and where it is going.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV emails Pastors Pen each month, a piece of encouraging content from Dr. Jeffress.
For this one, we decided to test the PS messaging.
The control thanks the recipient for supporting PTV, the treatment we included how financial support makes encouragement like Pastors Pen available to hundreds of thousands of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version of the email, which emphasized the impact of financial support on reaching hundreds of thousands of people with encouraging content, resulted in an 18.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.

2. Despite the increase in clicks, the statistical level of confidence of 63.6% is below the accepted threshold of 95%, rendering the results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision-making.

3. The hypothesis that expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers would achieve a higher click rate due to a greater correlation between their support and its impact was partially supported by the results.

4. Future experimentation should focus on refining the messaging and testing it with a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence for conclusive results.

5. It is important to continue experimenting with different messaging strategies to effectively engage email subscribers and drive desired actions without solely relying on the results of this inconclusive experiment.

experiments.title: How adding additional value prop on a donation page impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2024 - 11/11/2024
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding some additional value prop, especially around becoming a sustainer, impacted donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors showed a positive impact on donor conversions, with a noticeable increase in Transactions for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 27.6% is below the recommended threshold of 95%, indicating that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical power to generate reliable results. Additionally, clear and measurable key performance indicators should be established to accurately assess the impact of any changes or interventions. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and variability in donor behavior.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit from adding value prop to donation forms, further experimentation with a stronger design and methodology is needed to draw meaningful conclusions and make informed decisions for future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How does an impact call out versus a sticky bar impact donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact feature on instant donation page for paid media acquisition will achieve an increase in donations because it is a visual representation of their matched impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Last year for calendar year end we tested a sticky bar on instant donation pages that are within the funnel for new name acquisition content offers: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6937
This year we decided to test the sticky bar vs the Anedot donation platform feature of an impact call out. This feature shows the donor the “impact” of their gift, this case the double impact due to the year end match.
While the sticky bar explains the match and impact of the donation, our hypothesis is that the impact features visual will increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an impact call-out visual representation, resulted in a 2.5% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 8.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

3. Due to the low statistical confidence level, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the statistical confidence level is sufficiently high to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different variations of the impact call-out feature or other visual representations to determine their effectiveness in increasing donations. A/B testing can continue to be a valuable tool for Pathway to Victory in evaluating different strategies for optimizing donation pages and improving the overall fundraising process.

experiments.title: How including the reach of $20 in an email to existing donors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including a statement of reach for existing donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor can be confident of how their gift will be used and the impact. because the donor can be confident of how their gift will be used and the impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s final email of 2024, we decided to test adding in a how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry.
Our hypothesis is that by including this information, it would add confidence to the donor of how their gift would be used and the impact of it.
We calculated the reach by looking at the CPM for paid media channels, specifically Facebook as that is where PTV spends most of its acquisition media budget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on including a statement of reach for existing donors in the email were as follows:

1. The treatment group, which included information on how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry, saw a 4.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 12.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

3. While the treatment group showed a positive impact on donations, the results cannot be considered valid or reliable due to the low statistical confidence level.

4. Future experimentation should focus on increasing the sample size or making adjustments to the experiment design to ensure results are statistically significant and reliable before making decisions based on them.

5. The hypothesis that including information on how the donation would be used could increase donor confidence and donations needs to be further tested with a more robust experimental design to draw concrete conclusions.

experiments.title: How including the reach of $20 in an email to nondonors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including a statement of reach for nondonors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor can be confident of how their gift will be used and the impact..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s final email of 2024, we decided to test adding in a how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry.
Our hypothesis is that by including this information, it would add confidence to the potential donor of how their gift would be used and the impact of it.
We calculated the reach by looking at the CPM for paid media channels, specifically Facebook as that is where PTV spends most of its acquisition media budget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The inclusion of information on how far a gift of $20 would go toward growing the reach of the ministry led to a 21.5% increase in donations.

2. While the increase in donations is encouraging, the statistical level of confidence was only 58.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

3. This suggests that the results should not be used for future decision making, as the increase in donations may not be solely attributed to the inclusion of the reach information.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider increasing the sample size to improve the statistical significance of the results.

2. Continue testing the impact of including information on how donations are used, but also explore other variables that may influence donor behavior.

3. Analyze the data and results carefully to ensure that any conclusions drawn are based on valid and reliable statistical evidence.

4. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future testing strategies and make data-driven decisions to optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover affects donations during a Giving Tuesday campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a homepage takeover will increase donor conversion rate by reducing friction for motivated traffic during the Giving Tuesday campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/04/2024
For The Heritage Foundation’s Giving Tuesday campaign, they wanted to test the impact of a homepage takeover that would direct visitors to their homepage on Giving Tuesday to the campaign’s donation page with a link to go to back to their intended destination.
We believed that redirecting visitors to the donation page would reduce friction for highly motivated traffic that came to give a special gift and in turn increase donor conversions and revenue.
This experiment ran for one day. Each variant received 50% of traffic from mostly organic, direct sources to The Heritage Foundation’s home page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a homepage takeover to direct visitors to a donation page on Giving Tuesday can potentially increase donor conversions. We saw a 19.6% increase in transactions for all traffic during the one-day experiment. However, with a statistical confidence level of 56.7%, these results are inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

These results may indicate that site visitors may not have been highly motivated to navigate to the Heritage homepage in order to give a gift for the Giving Tuesday campaign.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar homepage takeover experiment over a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level. This can be accomplished by running another test over the last few days of the year during the calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: Does adding a scroll delay on a sticky bar make an impact on donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that delaying when a sticky bar renders for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/16/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
In the past, we’ve successfully tested using a sticky bar during a year-end campaign to help increase donor conversions on content offer donation pages. This year, we wanted to test whether the timing of when that bar shows up makes an impact. In this test, the control was displayed right when someone clicked onto that page; the treatment version had a 3 second delay and only showed up as people were reading the copy on the form.
Our hypothesis was that by timing the sticky bar, it would have more of an impact on donations if people had already started reading the copy on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The treatment, which involved delaying when a sticky bar renders for potential donors, resulted in an 8.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 57.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
3. Due to the lower statistical confidence, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
4. Future experiments should focus on ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on the results.
5. It is essential to continue testing different variations to determine the most effective strategies for increasing donor conversions, considering factors such as timing and messaging on donation pages.

experiments.title: How using a question for a landing page headline impacts email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a question headline for potential course takers will achieve a change in email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2024 - 12/04/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For one of their most popular free courses, we wanted to test whether using a question as a headline made an impact on email sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a question as a headline for a course did not lead to an increase in email signups. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 16.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to detect any meaningful effects and to have a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be helpful to consider other factors such as the wording of the question, the target audience, and the overall messaging strategy when testing different approaches to increasing email signups. Additionally, it is important to continue conducting experiments and gathering data to inform decision making and to be open to testing different hypotheses in order to optimize the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How referencing the new presidential administration in donation page copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating instant donation page copy to reference the new presidential administration for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because visitors will have increased context on the renewed opportunity for Heritage’s policy solutions to be implemented with the new administration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2024 - 11/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign where visitors could sign up to receive an eBook version of their Founders Almanac book. After signing up to receive the free eBook, visitors were redirected to a donation page where they could choose to give a gift.
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, and in response to the changing landscape of American policy following the presidential election, they wanted to test whether updated page copy referencing the incoming Trump administration and the work that Heritage can do because of it leads to this increase.
The control page contained copy that referenced the radical Left. The treatment page took the opportunity to take on a more hopeful tone of restoring the Founding Father’s vision that is more attainable with the newly elected administration.
The test ran for one week and each variant received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads and email prospecting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that updating instant donation page copy to reference the new presidential administration for eBook downloaders resulted in an increase in Transactions by 18.6%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.5%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

These results suggest that more timely copy referencing the new administration may not be as motivating a factor as focusing on the Left's destructive policies.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any potential fluctuations or outliers. It is also essential to carefully analyze the data and results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on them.

experiments.title: How adding transparency about the allocation of a donor’s gift affects conversion rates in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/16/2024
IMB tested how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals. We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
This experiment saw a 43.9% lift in donor conversion rate with a 90% LoC.
Because it did not validate, they wanted to test it again on one of their CYE email appeals. The set-up was the exact same as the GT appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding transparency about the allocation of a donor's gift can have a significant positive impact on the conversion rate.
2. The hypothesis that transparency builds trust and confidence in the organization, leading to increased donations, was supported by the results.
3. Despite the treatment showing a substantial increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence fell below the threshold of 85%, making the results inconclusive.
4. These results should not be used for future decision-making due to the lack of statistical validity.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Continue testing the impact of adding transparency about allocation on donation conversion rates to validate the findings.
2. Ensure that experiments are properly powered and have a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
3. Consider other factors that may influence donation behavior to develop a more comprehensive understanding of donor motivations.
4. Use the learnings from this experiment as a basis for formulating new hypotheses and designing future experiments to optimize donation conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How listing out bundles in bullet points inpact conversion rate for existing donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that listing out items in a bullet list for existing donor subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is easier to read and to see the value in each bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During late November and December, PTV promotes their brand-new Daily Devotional bundles. The bundles vary in the number of devotionals and if it is paired with additional items.
For this test we decided to test listing the bundles as a paragraph versus a bullet point list.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that listing out items in a paragraph form for Daily Devotional bundles resulted in a decrease in donations compared to listing them in a bullet point list. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 36.3%, which is below the 85% threshold for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different formats for presenting information to determine which method resonates best with donors. Additionally, ensuring a higher statistical level of confidence is crucial in drawing reliable conclusions from A/B testing results.

experiments.title: How listing out bundles in bullet points inpact conversion rate for nondonors
