experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that listing out items in a bullet list for nondonor subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is easier to read and to see the value in each bundle. because it is easier to read and to see the value in each bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During late November and December, PTV promotes their brand-new Daily Devotional bundles. The bundles vary in the number of devotionals and if it is paired with additional items.
For this test we decided to test listing the bundles as a paragraph versus a bullet point list.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to determine the effectiveness of listing out items in a bullet list for non-donor subscribers in promoting the Daily Devotional bundles for Pathway to Victory showed a 60.0% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 74.3%. While the control group performed better in this instance, the results are not statistically significant at a level of 85% confidence, and therefore cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of ensuring statistical significance in A/B testing results before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on them. In future experimentation, it is crucial to gather a larger sample size or consider other factors that may have impacted the outcome to ensure that conclusions drawn from the experiment are reliable and can be used to inform decision-making. Further testing may be warranted to determine the most effective way to present information about the Daily Devotional bundles to non-donor subscribers for optimal donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How sharing the impact of a gift to the mission of the organization impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers will achieve a higher click rate because there is greater correlation between their support and where it is going.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV emails Pastors Pen each month, a piece of encouraging content from Dr. Jeffress.
For this one, we decided to test the PS messaging.
The control thanks the recipient for supporting PTV, the treatment we included how financial support makes encouragement like Pastors Pen available to hundreds of thousands of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version of the email, which emphasized the impact of financial support on reaching hundreds of thousands of people with encouraging content, resulted in an 18.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.

2. Despite the increase in clicks, the statistical level of confidence of 63.6% is below the accepted threshold of 85%, rendering the results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision-making.

3. The hypothesis that expounding upon the mission of PTV for email subscribers would achieve a higher click rate due to a greater correlation between their support and its impact was partially supported by the results.

4. Future experimentation should focus on refining the messaging and testing it with a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence for conclusive results.

5. It is important to continue experimenting with different messaging strategies to effectively engage email subscribers and drive desired actions without solely relying on the results of this inconclusive experiment.

experiments.title: How shortening email copy for a content offer impacts donor signups and donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy for active donors will achieve a change in content offer KPIs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2024 - 09/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation, we wanted to test whether shorter, more-to-the-point email copy impacted clicks and email signups, as well as donation conversions.
This experiment result is for active donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the email copy for active donors resulted in a 6.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Moreover, when looking at downstream metrics:
The treatment version had a 13% decrease in email sign ups for the content offer, with an 80% level of confidence.
 	There was no difference at all when looking at donation conversions after the sign-up compared to total sent, but of those that signed up and THEN donated, the treatment had a 16% increase in donation conversions with only a 36% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does clarifying an offer through a headline on a landing page impact email signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to a headline for prospective content offer downloaders will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2024 - 11/07/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
For this experiment we wanted to test whether adding some clarity to a headline about what the content offer would help the prospect learn impacts email conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to a headline for prospective content offer downloaders resulted in a 0.5% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the treatment to enhance its effectiveness before conducting another experiment. It is essential to rely on statistically significant results when making decisions based on A/B testing to avoid drawing inaccurate conclusions.

experiments.title: How a humanized slideup impacts donor conversion rate during a high-urgency match
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized slideup for users on CaringBridge profiles will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the ask, and the letter-style sign-off presents the match opportunity from a person.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that an iteration of the slide-up where the ask is presented by a person, with a letter-style sign-off would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that introducing a humanized slideup with a letter-style sign-off on CaringBridge profiles resulted in a 25.0% increase in non-tribute donations. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance.
Ultimately, this experiment was ended early and should be re-tested before these results can be used for decision making

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout on the tributes donation array impacts conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic impact array for users on the tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it encourages interaction with the array and clearly outlines the impact of a donor’s gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that a dynamic impact array that automatically updated impact language based on the user’s gift selection would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamic impact array tested on the tribute donation page led to a 3.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 31.7%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is large enough to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more diverse data and account for any external factors that may impact the outcome.
While the initial results of the dynamic impact array seem promising, it is important to validate these findings with a more rigorous experiment before implementing any changes based on this data. Further testing with a larger sample size and a longer duration will provide more reliable insights into the impact of the dynamic impact array on donor conversion rate and revenue.

experiments.title: How changing ad copy impacted CTR on Facebook ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the copy to be shorter with called out value for Avila’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in CTR because the engager understand the value better.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/13/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke, Into the Deep. For this Facebook ad test, we wanted to change the copy to be shorter and focused on their relationship with the Lord. Describing more of the positive impact that the engaged would have with the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that updating the copy to be shorter with a focus on the value Avila Foundation provides for its Facebook audience resulted in a 7.2% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This indicates that the audience may have responded positively to the more concise message highlighting the relationship with the Lord that the course offers. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making.
The cost per registration/email acquisition had a 8.8% increase with a 46% loc.
Control:
sample size: 7271
emails: 99
Treatment:
sample size: 6951
emails: 103
In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of messaging that emphasizes the value of the courses in drawing Christians into deeper union with Christ. It may be beneficial to test variations of the copy further to see if a more significant increase in engagement can be achieved. Additionally, increasing the sample size or running the experiment for a longer period of time may help to gather more reliable data for making informed decisions about messaging strategies.

experiments.title: Does adding a sample of a daily devotional in the body of an email impact donations for an active donor audience?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a devotional sample for active donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2024 - 12/13/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
One of their most popular offers every year is a 265-page daily devotional that is sent to the donor as a thank you for their donation of any amount.
For this experiment, which was run on their housefile and targeted a donor audience, we wanted to see whether adding a sample passage from the devotional made an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a devotional sample for active donors led to a decrease in donations for all traffic by 8.9%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 54.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experimental design to increase the likelihood of obtaining valid results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcome of the experiment and take these into account when interpreting the results.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for high urgency campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running their yearly high-urgency President’s Challenge campaign. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was ultimately inconclusive. We learned that adding the donor's selected gift amount to the donation form's submit button may have a positive impact on donor conversion rate for high urgency campaigns. The treatment version, which included the gift amount in the submit button, saw a 1.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 12.5%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This means that we cannot confidently say that the increase in transactions was solely due to the change in the submit button language.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of including the gift amount in the submit button across multiple campaigns and audience segments to see if the results are consistent.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing is needed to confirm the impact of this change on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The treatment asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page did not lead to an increase in monthly gifts for all traffic.

2. Despite seeing a decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 8.3%, which is below the 85% threshold for results to be considered valid.

3. The experiment did not achieve the hypothesized increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions among members during Giving Tuesday.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to revisit the hypothesis and consider potential adjustments to the treatment based on the insights gained from this experiment. Further testing with larger sample sizes and potentially refined treatment variations can help validate findings and inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results cannot be referenced as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How does a slide out impact recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a slide out for homepage traffic will achieve a higher recruitment of recurring donors because the slide is a more visible method of recruitment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 12/03/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV would like to improve its recruitment of recurring donors (known at Pathway Partners) so we decided to test out a slide out on the homepage.
With the amount of traffic to the homepage we decided to make this a 3 way test:
control is no slide out
treatment 1 is slide out to become a Pathway Partner and receive monthly resources
treatment 2 is slide out to become a Pathway Partner by making a mission gifts
Currently to access the Pathway Partner page is a bit buried on the PTV site. Our hope is that the slide out on the homepage will achieve better results due to the homepage visibility
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a slide out for homepage traffic led to a 30.8% increase in Pathway Partner Revenue (Recurring) for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a high level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the reliability of the results.

Overall, while the initial results showed a positive impact of adding a slide out for homepage traffic, it is important to use caution in drawing conclusions from the data until a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for eBook campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate. because  the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2024 - 11/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running ads for their Election Fraud eBook offer, where visitors fill out a form to receive a copy of the eBook and then are redirected to a donation page. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for the eBook campaign did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 7.4%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
Therefore, we should not make any conclusive decisions based on the results of this experiment. It is important to recognize that sometimes variations in performance may occur due to random chance rather than a true impact of the changes made. In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used and that statistical significance is reached before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other variations or factors that could potentially impact donor conversion rates to gain a better understanding of what drives donor behavior.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have signed up to receive an eBook will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 09/12/2024
An organization has an offer where people sign up to receive an eBook and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the amounts on the gift array may have a positive impact on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had lower gift amount options of $10, $25, and $50, saw a 9.4% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 61.6% is below the threshold of 95%, meaning that these results are invalid and may not be reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, it could be useful to test different variations of gift amounts to see which combination yields the best results in terms of donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment suggest that lowering the gift array amounts can potentially increase donor conversion rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding credibility to a petition landing page impact email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a landing page like its a letter from an important figure for potential Pro-Life Petition signers will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it adds credibility and trust to the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/25/2024
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on ACI’s Pro-Life Petition landing page, we decided to run an A/B Test to lean into additional credibility through the presences of Dr. Ben Carson.
The treatment landing page copy was written as a letter from Dr. Ben Carson along with his signature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved presenting the landing page as a letter from Dr. Ben Carson, did not result in an increase in email acquisition for the Pro-Life Petition. In fact, there was a slight decrease in emails acquired compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment do not have a statistically significant level of confidence, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further refine the treatment and perhaps test different variables to determine what truly resonates with potential petition signers. It is also important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide more reliable results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, considering the sensitivity of the topic, it may be valuable to approach the messaging and presentation of the petition in a thoughtful and strategic manner to effectively engage the target audience.

experiments.title: How simplified value prop on a premium donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop for users on a premium donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it introduces them to the impact of their gift regarding the premiums specifically, rather than a more general “org-centered” value prop.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/23/2024
On Reasons to Believe’s premium donation pages, we noticed that multiple pages had an old value prop (one now replaced in newer copy) that outlined a series of bullet points demonstrating the impact of a gift. This language tended to be longer, more focused on how the org is impacted and the full mission of RTB, versus the newer premium specific value prop which simply talked about the impact of a donor’s gift regarding the premium specifically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that simplifying the value proposition on a premium donation page can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with a more focused and specific value prop regarding the impact of the donor's gift on premiums saw a 61.7% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 67.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to conduct similar tests with a larger sample size to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the value proposition to see if there are specific language or messaging that resonates best with donors on premium donation pages. By continuously testing and iterating on different approaches, we can gather more robust data and make more informed decisions to optimize donation conversions.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array options for monthly gift frequency affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because having different options for a one-time and monthly gift will reduce their decision friction and give clarity on the type of gift and amount they want to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/19/2024
For Reasons to Believe, they typically have the same gift array options for both one-time and recurring gift frequencies. For this test, we wanted to test changing the gift array options for the monthly frequency to have options that would be more suitable for a monthly gift. We also changed the placement of the options to select the gift frequency to be above the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options above the gift array did not have the desired effect of increasing donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.8% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic with a 5.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. Therefore, it is inconclusive whether the changes made had any impact on donation behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the design of the gift array options carefully and ensure that any changes made are based on solid research or data-driven insights. It may also be beneficial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How more applicable tab titles on a donation form + an action oriented statement impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donation form tab title from recurring to monthly and implementing action-orienting statements for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the word “monthly” will better resonate with their audience and the action-oriented statement prompts the visitor to take said action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2024 - 11/21/2024
The Navigators run a monthly Scripture Memory Text challenge. After participants sign up to join the challenge, they are asked for a gift.
We noticed that The Navigators tend to use the word “monthly” rather than “recurring” however their donation form says “recurring.”
We thought that this may be causing unnecessary friction on the donation page, and we saw it as an opportunity to also test into action statements for the donation form tab titles.
Control: One-time Gift & Recurring Gift
V1: One-time Gift & Monthly Gift
V2: Make a One-time Gift & Make a Monthly Gift
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the donation form tab title from "recurring" to "monthly" did not have a positive impact on donor conversion metrics for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, with a 21.6% statistical level of confidence.

These results suggest that the original tab title of "recurring" may have resonated better with the audience, despite the preference for using the word "monthly" in other communication from The Navigators. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that these results cannot be considered statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully consider the language used in communication and donation forms, taking into account the preferences and expectations of the target audience. Additionally, it is advisable to conduct further A/B testing with a larger sample size and potentially try different variations of the tab titles to determine the most effective messaging for donor conversion.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the presentation of a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to an increase in conversion rate as hypothesized. The treatment group saw a 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 15.5%.

Since the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making decisions. Therefore, it is important not to draw any definitive conclusions from this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies for creating urgency and a sense of immediate impact for donors. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is adequate and that the statistical analysis can provide reliable and conclusive results. Experimentation should continue to be a core part of The Navigators' strategy for optimizing their fundraising efforts, but conclusions should only be drawn from experiments with valid and statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How giving a monthly gift to a person instead of a fund impacts overall click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund will increase conversion rates because it creates a personal connection and fosters a sense of ongoing partnership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/06/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors. In the last email of the campaign, they wanted to test how their file would respond to having the option to give to a person rather than a fund of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund may have a positive impact on click-through rates for acquiring new recurring donors. This suggests that creating a personal connection and fostering a sense of ongoing partnership could potentially increase donor engagement.

However, it is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid for future decision making. The 16.6% level of confidence falls below the 95% threshold typically used to determine the reliability of A/B test results.

In the future, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to determine if offering the option to give to a person indeed has a significant impact on conversion rates. It is also important to consider other variables and factors that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging, design, and timing of the campaign.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing and analysis are necessary to make informed decisions about implementing this strategy in future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to the desired increase in conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 5.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85% (17.8%), which means that these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it might be worth exploring other ways to create a sense of urgency and make donors feel part of a tangible impact without solely relying on presenting daily goals. This could involve testing different messaging strategies, visuals, or other forms of engagement to see what resonates best with donors and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How will increasing the readability on the donation page affect conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the readability for the main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will give a better user expereience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2024 - 09/03/2024
The statement ‘many donors that make up the bulk of the program are over the age of 65’ reign true.
As we think about being user friendly and making the experience easier for users, it’s important to look at the demographic of those who frequent the donation pages.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we increased the readability of the donation page. For this we increased the font size, increased the margins, increased the line spacing, etc..
Here is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that increasing the readability of the donation page did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 95%, making these results inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

Therefore, it is important to be cautious when implementing changes based on assumptions without conducting thorough testing. Future experimentation should continue to focus on user experience and readability but should be done so with careful monitoring and analysis of results before making any significant changes. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor demographics and preferences to tailor the donation page for the specific audience.

experiments.title: How adding a popularity call out impacted email clicks and donations for a Donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that refreshing the email copy for Hoover’s Donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks and conversions because they see that people wanted this offer with the “30,000 people” callout.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/13/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. This experiment was for their Donor audience.
We wanted to update the copy to have these updates:
Shorter
Mentions well-known and respected Hoover fellow, Victor Davis Hanson, at the beginning of email
And have the callout “Already downloaded by more than 30,000 people in the last 6 months, you don’t want to miss your chance to learn from history’s most influential leaders from one of our country’s most esteemed historians.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the email copy for Hoover's Donor audience with a shorter, more engaging message mentioning Hoover fellow Victor Davis Hanson and including a callout about the popularity of the offer can lead to an increase in clicks. The treatment saw a 5.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the updated copy resonated better with the audience.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 40.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, while the results suggest a positive impact, they should not be used for future decision-making.

 

However, with the donations Version B showed a 11% increase in number of donations with a 34% level of confidence.

With total revenue being:

Version A: $1859 with 33 gifts

Version B: $2595 with 36 gifts

 

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the email copy to further optimize for engagement and conversions among Hoover's Donor audience. It's important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results, allowing for more reliable insights to guide decision-making.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for an existing donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more conversational approach for donors will achieve a change in clicks and email sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Income Inequality, we wanted to see if a more conversational approach to email copy made an impact on click throughs and email sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a more conversational approach in the email copy for donors, resulted in a 2.0% increase in clicks for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 17.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

3. When we look at full funnel statistics, we see the following:

The treatment had a 14% increase in email sign-ups, with an 80% level of confidence.

The treatment had also had a 28% increase in instant donations after people signed up to download the offer, with a 57% level of confidence.

 

Overall, having a more conversational approach to copy did result in increases across the board for an existing donor audience.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.

The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts active donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional reinforcement for active donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which included additional reinforcement about WUNC and its purpose, resulted in a 16.0% decrease in click-through rates compared to the control group.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.6% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for decision-making.

3. Moreover, the treatment version had a 50% decrease in donations.

This experiment shows us, based on a small sample size, that active donors to WUNC do not need to be reminded about what WUNC is and does to be motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts prospects from a political newsletter list during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop for political newsletter prospects will achieve a change in email clicks and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted prospects that had signed up for a political newsletter before the elections.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional call-out reinforcing the value proposition of WUNC did not have the intended effect on click-through rates and conversion rates during the Fall Fund Drive. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

Moreover, the donation conversion rate was also lower for the treatment option, with a 33% decrease in conversions and a 35% level of confidence. However, the sample size was extremely small (only 5 total donations).

This test bears refinement and repeating with bigger audience sizes.

experiments.title: Will having a guided reading experience improve transactions on a donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a guided reading expereince for the campaign donation page will achieve a higher number of conversions because the donation page will explain what the campaign proves to solve and how the donor can help in a easy to digest way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/01/2024
Each year this organization runs a a campaign that drives traffic to a main campaign donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we added a guided reading experience to the donation page that listed out the problem, the solution, and what you can do to help would increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a guided reading experience to the campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 64.3%. This indicates that the guided reading experience likely helped to better explain the problem, the solution, and how donors can help in a more easily digestible way, leading to higher conversions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of guided reading experiences on donation pages to further validate the initial results. It may also be beneficial to explore different formats or variations of the guided reading experience to see if there is an optimal design that maximizes conversions. Additionally, conducting further tests over longer periods of time with a larger sample size would provide more robust and reliable results to confidently conclude the effectiveness of the guided reading experience in driving donations.

experiments.title: Will adding in the value proposition copy from the donate button to the monthly donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding biblical resources for the monthly donation page will achieve will increase transactions because their is congruency between the donate button and the copy on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/29/2024
During our testing travels with the duel donation buttons in the main navigation bar we found that the value proposition of providing biblical resources led to an increase in clicks to the donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we adding that same value proposition to the donation page copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the value proposition of providing biblical resources to the donation page copy significantly increased revenue for all traffic. The treatment group, which included the added biblical resources copy, saw a 157.6% increase in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence, indicating that this change had a substantial impact.

This indicates that there is a strong congruency between the messaging on the donation button and the copy on the donation page, which likely contributed to the increase in transactions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to align the messaging throughout the donation process to maintain this congruency.

Additionally, it would be worthwhile to test other potential value propositions or messaging strategies on donation pages to see if they also lead to an increase in donations. By continuing to test and optimize the messaging and value propositions on donation pages, and can further enhance its donation performance and maximize its impact.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a non-donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy for a non donor audience will achieve a change in content offer performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. This experiment was for their non-donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy for a non-donor audience resulted in a 2.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. While this shows a potential positive impact on performance, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before making any definitive conclusions or decisions based on the results. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the variables being tested.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of copy focused on the offer and compare the performance results to further understand the impact on audience engagement and conversion rates. By consistently experimenting and analyzing the results with a critical eye on statistical relevance, Hoover Institution can make more informed decisions to optimize their email content and offers for better performance in the future.

experiments.title: Does Having a Dynamic Text Sticky Bar Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having dynamic text for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the dynamic text will decrease banner blindness and present more than one value proposition to become a donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
We have previously tested into the use of sticky bars during high-urgency campaigns for Moody.
For this campaign, we wondered if changing the text to be dynamic instead of static we could catch the eye of more users and ultimately lead them to convert on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a 19.7% increase in conversion at a 54% level of confidence with the first treatment of the dynamic texting. This suggests that our approach to incorporating dynamic text can be effective, especially when the copy is tailored to focus on the donor's perspective rather than the organization's. Conversely, the second treatment resulted in a 9% decrease in conversion, reinforcing the importance of donor-centric messaging.

From this, we can infer that dynamic text has the potential to significantly boost conversion rates when crafted thoughtfully. Specifically, emphasizing the donor's role, impact, or benefits appears to engage the audience more effectively than messaging that centers around the organization itself. However, to fully understand the scope and limitations of this tactic, it will be necessary to conduct additional tests across different placements and contexts. This further research will help us identify where and how dynamic text performs optimally, allowing us to refine our strategies and maximize conversions in various scenarios.

experiments.title: Would a virtual chat bot convert donors better than a dear reader?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing the dear reader with a chat bot for the stories pages during the on-air fundriaser will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the chat bot is a flashier tactic that would draw your eyes towards it and it’s a personal ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2024
For this test we wanted to see if we could try a different tactic on the story pages to convert donors. During KBCI Fall Fundraiser there is quite a bit of traffic pushed to the website from multiple channels.
In previous campaigns we have tested into having a traditional dear reader style ask on the story pages, however we wondered it it could be causing some “banner blindness” because it is used for every campaign. We decided to see if adding a chat bot message from a well known on-air host could draw more attention to the ask and then convert donors better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that replacing the traditional dear reader ask with a chat bot message from a well-known on-air host on story pages during the on-air fundraiser led to a decrease in donations by 42.8% for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 74.0%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the chat bot message or explore other creative tactics to make the ask more engaging and personalized for donors. It is also important to consider the overall user experience and ensure that any changes made do not negatively impact donation conversion rates. Continuing to test and iterate on different tactics will help determine the most effective approach for driving donations during fundraisers.

experiments.title: How an instructional headline effects email acquisitions
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that the instructional headline will lead to increased email acquisitions because it clearly communicates the benefits prospects will gain from signing up for the series, rather than simply stating the name of the series.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 10/22/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, focusing on a content offer landing page designed to encourage prospects to sign up for an email series aimed at helping them become more informed as the 2024 elections approach in North Carolina. We aimed to test different headlines to determine whether a more instructional would lead to higher email acquisition rates. The control group featured only the title of the email series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment remain inconclusive due to a low statistical significance level of 25.8%. While the treatment group, which featured an instructional headline, experienced a slight decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control group, this difference cannot be considered statistically significant.

We also observed a marginally higher average gift, but this finding is similarly inconclusive. This experiment serves as a useful starting point; however, our focus should shift to optimizing the body copy of the landing page to assess its potential impact on results. Currently, it appears that neither headline significantly influenced whether prospects opted to sign up for the series.

Given these findings, it is premature to draw any definitive conclusions or use these results for future decision-making. For subsequent experiments, it is crucial to ensure an adequate sample size to yield reliable and meaningful results. Additionally, testing different variations of the action-oriented headline may provide insights that could lead to a more significant impact on email acquisitions.

experiments.title: How including giving handles on a donation page during a recurring giving campaign impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including giving handles  for the Recurring Giving donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor. because  the gift’s impact will be more tangible for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/04/2024
During IMB’s Fall Recurring Giving Campaign, we decided to test including giving handles within the donation page copy.
Both the control and the treatment had the same gift array and the gift array aligned with the amounts within the giving handles. These giving handles ranged from $5 – $75.
This donation page did default to monthly, but one-time giving was still an option.
It is worth noting that this campaign was shut down early, so this test did not get enough traffic to validate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including giving handles in the donation page copy led to a 29.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 26.4% statistical level of confidence and a 10.1% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic at a 28.3% level of confidence. While neither of these results are trending in the direction we intended, we did see a 210.8% lift in recurring donations at a 93.4% level of confidence.
The recurring donation metrics was extremely close to validating which does suggest that the giving handles resonated well with monthly givers. It is worth testing further into how giving handles can compel IMB's donor base to give.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is enough traffic to validate the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of including giving handles with different messaging or positioning to see if there is a way to communicate the impact of donations effectively without negatively impacting conversion metrics. It is also crucial to consider the potential effects of messaging on donor behavior and to continuously iterate and test different approaches to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a Create a Post card with Ghost Text options impacts journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing host text options in the Create a Post card for authors will achieve an increase in new journal entries because it helps alleviate anxiety around what to post, while also eliminating friction by giving them a place to start.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 11/01/2024
In the next iteration of Create a Post testing, we hypothesized that the primary point of friction is that authors are not posting because they aren’t sure what to post on their page. We hypothesized that included a Create a Post card with a rotating option of Ghost Text to start their post would address these and increase journal entries. For context, Ghost Text options would be starting sentences like “A highlight to my week has been…” “Today, I’m grateful for…” “Something that’s been helping me lately is…”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Providing Ghost Text options in the Create a Post card for authors resulted in a 1.4% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on journal entries, the statistical level of confidence was only 44.0%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to think further about the types of prompts Authors are given, the callout Headline, and how the Create a Post card is shown with clear visibility. Additionally, we should also think about how the second opened card after they click aligns with messaging. Rather than "Create a Post" could it instead re-iterate the question, "How have you been?

experiments.title: How a one-time vs monthly ask impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly gift for October Scripture Memory participants will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the donation page will default to the monthly form and the visitor will be more likely to complete what is asked of them.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2024 - 11/01/2024
Every month, The Navigators run a Scripture Memory text campaign. At the end of the week long campaign, the participants receive an ask.
We tested this for the month of September, but the test was slightly biased in terms of value prop, so we retested it in the month of October.
Control: one-time ask in the donation page copy & one-time default on donation form
Treatment: monthly ask in the donation page copy & monthly default on donation form
The text copy was identical for both groups and they were asked to “make a gift” generally.
The donation page copy was identical except for a single statement asking for a one-time vs monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly gift in the donation page copy and defaulting to a monthly donation form resulted in a 58.3% increase in one-time donations for all traffic, but oddly had no impact on monthly giving. While this result is statistically significant at a 52.8% level of confidence, it falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

These results suggest that The Navigators audience preferred being asked for a monthly gift, but having the autonomy to make the decision to make a one-time gift.

Due to the invalid results and for the short nature of how long this test ran, we should not make decisions based on these results. However, it does open the door for more testing in terms of what motivates The Navigators audience to give whether that be one-time or monthly.

Overall, the results of this experiment should be viewed with caution due to the lower statistical confidence level, but they provide a valuable insight that can be used to inform future testing and optimization strategies for donation campaigns.

experiments.title: Will Adding “Moody Bible Institute” in an Appeal Increase Transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding Moody Bible Institute for a Fall Share appeal email will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because adding in Moody Bible Institute adds even more credibility to the organization and gives potential donors the chance to associate their gift with something bigger.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share each year and it reaches a lot of people on air and online.
For this test, we wanted to see if associating Moody Radio with Moody Bible Institute in an email will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding "Moody Bible Institute" to a Fall Share appeal email for Moody Radio resulted in a 10.8% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 18.6%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

It is important to note that this experiment did not validate for our original hypothesis, however we did see a directional increase in revenue. The treatment saw a 127% lift in revenue at a 85% LoC.

experiments.title: Rad: Does a raw link or a button create more clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a raw link for any email links will achieve a higher number of clicks because having a raw links makes the email feel more personal and not like a marketing email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share each year that includes a full email campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if the moody radio subscribers prefer a button in an email or a raw link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a raw link in an email campaign resulted in a 9.0% increase in donations compared to using a button. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 45.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different elements of the email campaign to determine what resonates best with Moody Radio subscribers.

experiments.title: Does a dynamic sticky bar convert donors better than a static sticky bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a donor countdown for the sticky bar CTA button will achieve a higher donor conversion because the donor countdown shows social proof that others are donating and can create a fear of missing out experience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/01/2024
Earlier in KCBI’s Fall Fundraiser we tested a static sticky bar vs. a rating sticky bar that showed 3 different value propositions to give. From that test we learned that their audience prefers the static sticky bar, however we wondered if we could improve on that version to maximise donations.
For this test we added in a donor countdown in the cta that added an element of social proof of fear of missing out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor countdown to the sticky bar CTA button led to a 10.4% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 59.5%, falling below the threshold of 95% needed to consider the results valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the results have a higher level of statistical confidence before making any decisions based on them. While the donor countdown showed promise in increasing donations, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm its effectiveness. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize donation conversions while ensuring the results are statistically significant.

experiments.title: How a slide out on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide out with supportive copy for authors will achieve an increase in co-author invitations sent because it addresses the felt need and highlights it as a call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/24/2024
For CaringBridge authors, a key call to action is inviting a co-author to help manage your page. In order to increase this, we hypothesized that a slideout  with supportive copy would increase co-author invitations (email sign ups).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved a flyout with supportive copy for authors, resulted in an 8.9% increase in Co-Author Invites Sent for All Traffic. This indicates that the treatment had a positive impact on the desired outcome. However, the statistical level of confidence for this increase was 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

It may be beneficial to further iterate on the flyout design and messaging to optimize the impact on Co-Author Invites Sent. Continue to monitor the engagement metrics such as Flyout CTA clicked to understand how users are interacting with the flyout.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is essential to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the effectiveness of the flyout in increasing co-author invitations.

experiments.title: Does include adding an image of the premium in email impact donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the premium for email subscriber will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is visual representation of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/16/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Every month, PTV promotes a different premium book; for October, PTV promoted Final Conquest. We had tested into removing images, but it had been a while, so we decided to run the test again.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of the premium book for email subscribers led to a 14.9% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that visual representation of the offer can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 41.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different types of images or visual elements to further understand their effect on donor conversion rates. It is also crucial to continue testing and refining hypotheses to gather more reliable and actionable insights for decision making.

experiments.title: Can a Donor Countdown Be More Effective than a Static Sticky Bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown for the sticky bar on the home page will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is something moving in the sticky bar so it’s eye catching and the donor countdown adds credibility and exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
During Fall Share we have tested into using Dynamic text over static. However, we had more questions on how to really optimize this element. We wondered, if having dynamic text was better than static, does it have to be all  of the text? Or could just one element being dynamic still have the same amount of influence?
For this test, we wanted to see if having a dynamic donor countdown would be more appealing to users than just have a regular static sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor countdown to the sticky bar on the home page did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. This suggests that in this particular case, the dynamic element did not have the intended effect of making the sticky bar more appealing to users.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of adding dynamic elements to key areas of the website. It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different types of dynamic content or in different placements to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that any findings are statistically significant and have a high level of confidence before making decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Does Having Dynamic Text Sticky Bar Have the Same Affect Post On-Air?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic text sticky bar for the homepage during post on-air will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the dynamic text is much more eye catching and it has clearer value proposition that is shortened down to one sentence.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
During the on-air portion of Fall Share we had a 3 way test to see which sticky bar would be the best performer (you can see those results here.)
In that test, we had two versions of the dynamic text sticky bar against the control and found that donor-centric language increased the likelihood for somebody converting by 19%. For this test, we wanted to see if these same results held true during the post-on-air portion of the campaign. Since we already know donor-centric language is better, we tested only that treatment against the static sticky bar. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that donor-centric language in the form of a dynamic text sticky bar for the homepage during the post-on-air campaign can lead to an 18.2% increase in donations compared to a static sticky bar. This confirms the earlier finding that donor-centric language is more effective in prompting conversions. However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means the results may not be entirely reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on using donor-centric language in our messaging and explore different ways to incorporate this into our campaigns. It's also important to ensure that our experiments have a high level of statistical confidence to draw more definitive conclusions. Additionally, we should consider testing other elements of the sticky bar, such as design and placement, to further optimize its effectiveness in driving donations.

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing a premium offer chat widget with a conversational tone and engaging design will increase donor conversion rates during the high urgency campaign by enhancing the visibility of the call-to-action and creating a more personal appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/22/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization committed to strengthening families through various resources, including their engaging podcast series. As part of their Fiscal Year End (FYE) campaign, we are testing two different messaging approaches on the podcast pages to encourage donations.
Historically, the “Dear Listener” message has proven successful in driving donations. However, we want to explore whether a more dynamic approach, utilizing a chat widget, might enhance engagement and boost donor conversion rates.
