This experiment follows a successful test conducted in July with the “Seeing Beautiful Again” campaign, which achieved a 187.9% increase in donor conversion. We aim to see if a similar impact can be replicated with a different premium during the FYE campaign.
We believe that the conversational and eye-catching design of the chat message widget will increase donor conversion during the FYE campaign by improving the visibility of the call-to-action (CTA) and humanizing the appeal.
The control version of the podcast page featured the traditional “Dear Listener” premium message, which included an image of the premium offer, Unoffendable. The treatment version showcased the premium message chat widget, featuring an image of a FamilyLife member. Most of the language remained consistent between the two versions, and traffic was split 50/50 to measure differences in donation conversion rates accurately.
We also tested this on their Article Pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment results indicated that the treatment group featuring the premium offer chat widget achieved a conversion rate of 0.39%, compared to the control group’s rate of 0.32%. This represents a relative difference of 24%. However, with a level of confidence of only 43.6%, these results were not statistically significant.

Despite the positive trend in donor conversion rates observed with the chat widget, the lack of robust statistical significance suggests that more data is needed to draw reliable conclusions. The test ran for 21 days, which may have limited the ability to fully assess the widget's effectiveness.

Next Steps:
Extended Testing Duration: Run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data, especially during different times of the month or year to account for seasonal variations in donor behavior.
 	A/B Test Variations of the Chat Widget: Experiment with different designs and messaging variations for the chat widget to identify elements that resonate better with the audience. Consider testing language, tone, and imagery to enhance engagement.
 	Analyze User Interaction: Utilize analytics tools to monitor how users interact with the chat widget (e.g., clicks, time spent, engagement) to identify any drop-off points or issues affecting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course will increase the donation amount because people won’t feel marketed to, but realize they have the power to make the decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/28/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible. To this aim, they offer free online courses. All their courses are funded by generous donors. After a person signs up for the Revelation course they are re-directed to an instant donation page. This page thanks them for signing up and then asks then to donate.
In an attempt to increase the donor amount on this page, they wanted to test whether donor autonomous language would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course can potentially increase the donation amount. While the treatment did see a 6.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing conclusions. It may be beneficial to repeat the experiment with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the donation page to further test the impact of donor autonomous language. Additionally, it could be useful to explore other factors that may influence donation amounts, such as the design of the donation page or the timing of the donation request. By continuing to test and analyze different variables, Dallas Theological Seminary can gather more reliable data to inform their decision-making process and optimize their donation strategy.

experiments.title: Does Adding a Sticky Bar to the Campaign Donation Page Increase Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher conversion rate because when a user lands on the page there is reinforcement at the top to give from the president of Moody Bible Institute.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
Moody Radio just completed its Fall Share drive, one of their main catalytic campaigns each year.
This campaign drives traffic from paid ads, broadcasts, and other places to the radio site and the main campaign donation page.
For this test, we wanted to see if adding a message from the Moody Bible Institue President, Dr. Mark Jobe, would influence more users to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In running this test we found that having the message from Dr. Jobe on the donation page actually causes a decrease in conversion rate by 11% at a 70% LoC. Because the LoC did not reach 95% this is not a valid test, but it is a directional decrease and should be tested more.

While this test did produce a decrease in conversions, we actually saw donors give more generously when the sticky bar is present on the page. We saw a $354 average gift with the treatment and a $293 average gift on the control.

experiments.title: Do Meta Suggested Image Optimizations really increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing Meta image optimizations for Facebook traffic will achieve higher engagement because Facebook said it would.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV runs regular content offers and direct asks on Facebook (Meta). Meta has been urging marketers to use its image optimization suggestions, which it promises will significantly increase engagement and, therefore, donations.
As experimenters, we are skeptical.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing Meta image optimizations for Facebook traffic did not lead to higher engagement for Pathway to Victory's content offers and direct asks. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 60.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 90% for validity.
Purchases saw a 8% increase but only 62% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important for Pathway to Victory to not solely rely on platform suggestions or promises when conducting experiments. It is essential to rigorously test any changes or optimizations before making decisions based on them. Additionally, future experimentation should aim to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the reliability of the results. It may be beneficial to continue testing different strategies and optimizations to find what works best for engaging with the audience and driving donations.

experiments.title: How additional cultivation and text appeals amid a High Urgency campaign impact donor giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending cultivation and appeals in an additional channel amid a high urgency campaign for Human Coalition’s housefile will achieve an increase in donor giving and revenue because it diversifies the communication and creates an additional reminder/opportunity to connect with the donor — beyond just their inbox. because  it diversifies the communication and creates an additional reminder/opportunity to connect with the donor — beyond just their inbox.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/08/2024
As Human Coalition expands into multi-channel efforts with Facebook retargeting and text appeals, we hypothesized that including additional touchpoints in text for the Back to School high urgency campaign will improve donor conversion rate and revenue. Our goal was to observe the impact on giving, not just from the text directly, but whether these additional touchpoints made donors more likely to give or more generous in their gift amount in other channels throughout the campaign.
The additional touchpoints sent by text were a launch day appeal, a cultivation piece with video, and a final day appeal – three texts in total.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:
1. The treatment group, which included additional touchpoints through Facebook retargeting and text appeals, saw a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 35.0%. This suggests that incorporating multiple channels for communication can have a positive impact on donor giving during high urgency campaigns.
2. Despite the increase in revenue, the statistical level of confidence was only at 75.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be relied upon for future decision making.
The key lesson learned is that incorporating multiple touchpoints in fundraising campaigns may have a positive impact on donor giving, but it is essential to gather more data and ensure statistically significant results before implementing any changes based on experiment outcomes.
Ultimately, a helpful takeaway is that receiving an additional communication in text does not negatively impact Human Coalition's audience, it doesn't appear to be an annoyance or a distraction. Rather, it would be worthwhile testing additional pieces in text that are "bonus" to the campaign. For example, rather than resending the video already included in email. What if readers were sent a new cultivation or video piece that they hadn't received.

experiments.title: Does having a specific ask help increase donor conversion on a dear reader?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific ask for the dear reader will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is less decision friction of having to think of a gift amount for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
In previous testing for Today in the Word, we have found that having a dear reader present on the content pages during a high urgency campaign significantly increases donor conversion.
For this test we wanted to see if adding a specific ask amount would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount for the dear reader did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, there was a 11.2% decrease in donations for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it will be important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be beneficial to test different specific ask amounts or other variables to see if there are any changes in donor conversion rates. Additionally, considering other factors such as timing, messaging, and audience segmentation could provide valuable insights for optimizing donation campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to a campaign donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for the Fall Share campaign donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because the need is reinforced on the page in a more eye catching way when somebody lands on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign each year. This campaign draws in a ton of traffic from ads, organic, and on-air listeners.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if adding a stickybar at the top of the campaign giving page would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a humanized sticky bar to the Fall Share campaign donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

However, we did see an in the average gift when the sticky bar was present. The control saw a $293 average gift while the treatment saw a $345 average gift!

Although we might not have increased overall transactions, by having the sticky bar present on the page we did see an increase in generosity. More testing needs to be done with this before it can be rolled out.

experiments.title: Will presenting a specific ask amount on a sticky bar increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting users with a specific ask for the recurring donor drive sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less decision friction on the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Today in the Word recently ran a recurring donor campaign. We had previously tested into different web elements on their site during previous campaigns, but wondered if we could iterate on previous learning to try to find a new control heading into CYE.
For this test we wanted to see if listing a specific gift amount would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting users with a specific ask for the recurring donor drive sticky bar resulted in a 59.2% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a promising result, the statistical level of confidence was only 59.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that although the treatment showed a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be used for future decision making at this time. It would be beneficial to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to further investigate the impact of presenting a specific gift amount.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing variations of presenting specific gift amounts to donors to see if it consistently leads to increased conversion rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as messaging, design, or timing of the ask could also be valuable in optimizing donor conversion rates for the recurring donor drive.

experiments.title: How an in-line prompt impacts new journal entries (non-clickable)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line Create a Post card for authors will achieve an increase in new journal entires because it prioritizes the CTA above others and makes it more visible..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/04/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
This was the first iteration in a testing series which presented an in-line card with a small clickable button – in the next test, the entire card was made clickable. Primarily, the impact of this change upon the first test was a significant decrease in clicks overall to the treatment, indicating that users did not know where to click or interact with the prompt.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both experiments within this series resulted in a similar increase in journal entries (3%), the non-clickable card impacted this treatment negatively in clicks where users did not know what step to take next.

It is worth noting that making this clickable change didn't impact our end metric ultimately (journal entries) but it did improve the user experience.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to an acquisition donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy on the donation page for the pledge will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because there will be clarity as to what the page is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2024 - 09/09/2024
Care net is a well-known organization that has multiple offers out in the market. One of these offerings is a pledge.
The offer is set up similar to any other, a user clicks on an ad, goes to the landing page, submits the form and then is redirected to a donation page. However, we were seeing a ton of people drop off once they hit the donation page. Could this be because they don’t want to give or is it because the page is not clear in what it is?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on the Pledge donation page are:

1. Changing the copy on the donation page can have a significant impact on donor conversion rates.

2. The treatment group experienced a 22.2% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

3. The statistical confidence level of 60.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be reliable.

4. The hypothesis that clarity in the donation page copy will lead to higher conversion rates was supported by the results.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

1. Continue testing variations in copy and messaging on donation pages to optimize for clarity and donor conversion.

2. Ensure that experiments run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future hypotheses and testing strategies for donation pages across different campaigns and offerings.

4. Consider additional factors such as design, layout, and call-to-action language to further improve donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment are promising, it is important to conduct future experiments with a higher level of statistical confidence to make informed decisions for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversions.

experiments.title: How an in-line, clickable “Create a Post” button impacts new journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable, in-line “Create a Post” prompt for authors will achieve an increase in journal entries because it prioritizes it as a primary call-to-action by making it more visible for the author..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2024 - 09/24/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The in-line "Create a Post" prompt led to a 3.3% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. This indicates that making the journal entry prompt more visible and clickable can encourage authors to post more frequently.

2. However, the statistical confidence level of 79.6% is below the standard threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and not completely reliable for future decision making.

We believe that these soft results indicate that the true point of friction hasn't been addressed by this treatment. A similar level of clicks between the cards and a similar conversion rate of journal entires may indicate that "where" to post or "how" is not what's preventing authors from doing so. Rather, it's likely a question of "what" to post. How can we make it easier for authors to share what's on their mind, even if initially they may believe they don't have anything to say.

experiments.title: How adding copy that emphasizes how many people downloaded the offer on an email to donors impacts click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding claims of popularity for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in email sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 09/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Trickle Down, we wanted to test whether adding copy that emphasizes how many downloads of the offer have happened in the past impacts sign-up rates. This email was aimed towards a donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding claims of popularity for content offer downloaders can potentially impact email sign-up rates positively. The treatment group, which included this additional copy, saw a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.
Moreover, when digging into downstream results, we see the following:
The treatment version had a 4% decrease with a 21% level of confidence. In terms of instant donor conversions, the treatment had a 55% DECREASE in donations, with a 95% level of confidence.
Future variations of this experiment could look at the congruency between email and landing page and donation form to make sure content offer downloaders see the same messaging across all points of the funnel.

experiments.title: How refreshing copy impacted click through rate on a recirculation offer for Donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy for Hoover’s donors will achieve an increase in click-through-rate because people will be more likely to read the copy if they’ve never seen it before.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 10/04/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. This specific test was on their Donor audience. They have enough offers to do different offers for roughly 3-5 months before the same offer is sent out. We suppress anyone who has previously downloaded this offer. So for this test, we decided to try new copy in the email to “refresh” the offer and hopefully increase the click through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy for Hoover's donors did not result in an increase in click-through-rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is only 56.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different variables such as formatting, visuals, or timing of the email to see what truly resonates with the audience. It is also important to continue monitoring and testing different variables to optimize performance and achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How the placement of the call to action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers will achieve more email acquisitions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS runs courses for email acquisition. For these courses, they use a course platform that has a template for what the landing page looks like. The template has the headline underneath the CTA. The user also has to scroll to see it. We wanted to test putting the headline above the CTA to see how it impacted email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers resulted in a 11.2% decrease in Email Acquisitions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any fluctuations in traffic or other external factors that could impact the results. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to consider other factors that could potentially influence email acquisitions, such as the design of the CTA button itself or the messaging around it.

experiments.title: How adding copy that emphasizes how many people downloaded the offer on an email to non-donors impacts click throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding claims of popularity for a non-donor audience will achieve a change in email sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2024 - 09/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Tricke Down, we wanted to test whether adding copy that emphasizes how many downloads of the offer have happened in the past impacts sign-up rates. This email was aimed towards a donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding claims of popularity to the email sent to a non-donor audience did not have a significant impact on email sign-up rates. The treatment group, which included claims of popularity, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 74.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
Moreover, when looking at downstream results, we see the following:
The treatment version had an 8% increase in email signups, with a 74% level of confidence.The treatment version had a 35% increase in donations, with a 60% level of confidence.
For non-donors specifically, adding in references to the popularity of the ebook increased all key metrics. Future testing would look at testing congruency on the messaging--does adding references to the popularity on the landing page or donation page impact performance metrics?

experiments.title: How adding an image to a premium offer impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the image of the large bundle for email subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is visual reenforcement of what all is included in the bundle.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/24/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a new book. Some of the emails also mention an upgrade to purchase a larger bundle of resources.
For this test, we decided to test adding an image of the large bundle
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding an image of the large bundle for email subscribers resulted in a 6.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 16.1%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity.

3. Therefore, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.

Specific to the PS, we saw a 120% increase in clicks to the PS link for the large bundle, however control and treatment each only received 1 large bundle donation.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is high enough to draw meaningful conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to continue testing the impact of visual elements, such as images, in email campaigns to see if they consistently lead to higher donation rates.

experiments.title: How shortening an email impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening the copy for email subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the CTA is short, to the point and no fluff.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/24/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month PTV promotes a different book, for September PTV offered a brand new book by Dr. Jeffress.
In an effort to increase donor conversions, we decided to test a shorter, more direct copy vs the standard longer version
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the shortened copy for email subscribers did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the standard longer version. While the treatment saw a 7.9% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 37.5%, which is below the threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the shorter copy to see if there is a more effective version that can be used to increase donor conversions. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign or the target audience. Overall, it is important to continue testing and analyzing different strategies to find the most effective approach for increasing donor conversions.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the donation page copy for Avila’s audience will achieve an increase in overall donations because they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission. because  they will feel more knowledgable and aligned with Avila’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/17/2024 - 09/02/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
They are currently running an offer, Root Sin. We decided to run a test changing the copy on the donation page. Recently, we updated their main donation page with more value proposition; we decided to add that to the Root Sin page to see if this would have a positive effect on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved adding a value proposition to the donation page copy, led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic by 36.2%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 24.3%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. It is also crucial to consider the timing and duration of the experiment to account for any external factors that could influence the outcomes. Additionally, further testing and iterations may be necessary to determine the impact of adding a value proposition to the donation page copy for Avila's audience accurately.
Overall, while this particular experiment did not yield conclusive results, the learnings can be used to inform future experimentation strategies and help optimize the donation process for Avila Foundation.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field to a donation form impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a phone number field to the donation page for donation page vistors will achieve a similar amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 08/09/2024
We wanted to start sending a thank you voicemail to donors after they donated, but to be able to do this we needed to start collecting phone numbers on the donation page. Before rolling out this change to the donation page, we wanted to make sure it wouldn’t impact the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a phone number field to the donation page resulted in a 7.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to adhere to a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an experiment. This will help to avoid making decisions that may not actually have a meaningful impact on the desired outcome.

experiments.title: How adding an icon to the donation page showing the number of gifts made so far impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in gifts and revenue to the tribute widget because it makes this tab more visible, highlights its importance, and drives more users to the widget..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 09/13/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile, it includes copy “Join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of [name].
Since migration to CB2, CaringBridge has seen low performance on this widget. We hypothesized that by driving more traffic to the tab, it would increase donor conversion rate and revenue. On the old site, this Tributes tab was it’s own designated page and the button to navigate to it included a badge icon with how many tributes were on the profile. We created two treatments, one that’s a static badge icon and another where the same badge icon animates and counts up to the total number of tributes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab on site profiles significantly increased Revenue - Tribute from the widget by 106.7%. Additionally, Tribute Tab clicks experienced a substantial increase of 9,5881%. These results were statistically significant with confidence levels of 95.6% and 100% respectively.

Moving forward, it is clear that showcasing the importance of the Tribute tab by making it more visible and engaging users with a badge icon can lead to a significant boost in donations and revenue. This insight should be taken into consideration for future experimentation and site optimizations. By continuing to test and iterate on features that drive engagement with the Tribute widget, CaringBridge can further enhance user experience and ultimately increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will Adding ACH as the Preferred Method of Payment Decrease Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by showing ACH as the preferred of payment for donors coming the monthly giving page will achieve longer donor retention in the long run because banking information expires with less frequency over credits cards. An added benefit is the reduction in credit card processing fees as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2024 - 09/23/2024
An organization worked on identifying ways to retain their monthly partners long-term. Because bank account information vs. debit/credit card information tends to change or expire less often than credit cards, will we retain our donors longer? Simultaneously, we also need to see if showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page hurts our current donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that showing ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly giving page resulted in a 6.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.2%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. This shows us an important finding. Including ACH as the preferred method of payment on the monthly donation page DOES NOT hurt donor conversion and can be considered for monthly giving pages as the preferred method of payment as it will help retain monthly partners longer.

experiments.title: How a dynamic homepage donate button on mobile impacts clicks and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the dynamic text on a donate sticky footer for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in button clicks and downstream revenue metrics because the dynamic, typing text is more eye catching to the user .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/10/2024
In an effort to push more of IMB’s homepage traffic to their main donation page, we decided to run a test of adding an additional “give now” button to the homepage.
Control: No changes
Treatment 1: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”)
Treatment 2: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”) + Dynamic Text (Types out “Send the Gospel”)
Our hypothesis was that the dynamic text would increase the number of clicks to the donation page because it was more eye catching than Treatment 1.
This test only ran on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate sticky footer on the IMB homepage resulted in a 42.3% increase in donations for all traffic. Whereas, the dynamic donate sticky footer actually resulted in a 13.5% decrease in donations at 34% LOC.

While neither of these metrics validated, it does suggest that the static donate sticky footer was helpful in pushing more of IMB's homepage traffic to the donation page.

We can also look in clicks based on each treatment.

Sticky Footer: 104 Button Clicks

Sticky Footer + Dynamic Text: 88 Button Clicks

This data suggests that the dynamic, typing text was not as beneficial as the static text in directing visitors to the donation page.

Due to these results, we have chosen to roll out the static button to all mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How a lower gift array impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for donation page visitors will achieve more transactions because the amount we are asking for is less.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/09/2024
We were running ads for an Education Survey on Facebook for ACI and noticed that people were selecting the two lower options in the gift array, people weren’t selecting the two higher options. We decided to test a lower gift array to try to increase the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that lowering the gift array options for donation page visitors resulted in a 3.0% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.5%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid. As a result, we should not use these findings for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to obtain statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to consider other factors that could impact transaction rates, such as the appeal of the donation page, the target audience, and the effectiveness of the ad campaign. By taking these factors into account and conducting well-designed experiments with higher levels of confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future strategies for increasing transactions on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding copy about giving monthly on a donation page impact recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy about why somebody should become a monthly donor for people who take the survey will achieve more recurring donations because it explains the value of becoming a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 05/08/2024
Care Net has a survey we were running in Facebook. The instant donation page for this offer didn’t have any language around why a donor should give monthly instead of once. We wanted to test how adding language about giving month would impact the recurring gifts made on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment version, which added copy about why someone should become a monthly donor, resulted in a 32.1% decrease in Recurring Donations for All Traffic with a 33.1% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the language used in donation appeals and how it may impact donor behavior. Further testing with different messaging strategies and potentially adjusting the copy to better communicate the value of becoming a monthly donor may help in identifying the most effective approach for increasing recurring donations.

experiments.title: How a callout on a gift array impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the gift array for donation page visitors will achieve more donations because it increases the value proposition. because it increases the value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 06/30/2024
DTS was running their annual FYE campaign. We wanted to test how a callout that added value proposition would impact donations and encourage people to give a gift of a higher amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a callout on the gift array for donation page visitors can potentially increase donations, as evidenced by a 10.4% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.2%, which is below the threshold of 95% needed for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test different types of callouts or messaging to see which ones resonate best with donors and lead to the most significant increase in donations. It is also important to continuously iterate and experiment with different strategies to optimize donation performance.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the call to action below a button impacts clicks to activate a free membership trial
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy below each button on the landing page for site visitors will achieve an increase in clicks through to the membership activation page because it reinforces the call to action that they will get a free 30 days of membership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2024
We noticed a decline in clicks through to activate a free NextAfter Institute membership trial, so we looked back at earlier, better-performing versions of our landing page and noticed there used to be copy reinforcing the CTA below each button on the page. So, we tested adding the copy back to see how that would impact clicks through to the activation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no distinct difference in the results between the control and the treatment. The test did not validate. We will test this again during a time where we are pushing more traffic to this page to see if we can validate a result.

experiments.title: Humanized vs. Premium: Which Slide-Out Drives More Transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that a slide-out showcasing a premium offer accompanied by a photo will drive more donations. This is based on the belief that prospects will be more inclined to donate when enticed by an attractive offer they can potentially obtain.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, sought to evaluate whether a slide-out featuring a compelling value proposition and photo or one highlighting a premium offer and photo would generate more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-out showcasing a premium offer accompanied by a photo did not drive more donations compared to the slide-out featuring a compelling value proposition and photo. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 29.4% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group outperformed the treatment group.
However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 77.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to increase the statistical power of the results. Additionally, it may be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the outcome of the experiment, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific wording and design of the slide-outs.
Overall, while this particular experiment did not yield definitive results, it provides valuable insights for future testing and optimization of donation strategies.

experiments.title: How a $25 callout on the Non-Tribute donation age impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $25 callout  for the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because it reinforces the impact of the user’s gift in a tangible way — powering a user’s site..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 09/06/2024
For CaringBridge’s non-tribute donation page (main donate) we hypothesized that including a $25 callout over the gift array, leveraging language that’s been used successfully in other locations/widgets on the site, would improve overall revenue and transactions on main donate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a $25 callout for the non-tribute donation page resulted in a 3.0% increase in revenue for all traffic but with a statistical level of confidence of only 54%.
It is likely that this callout is not making a significant impact either way, because in both the control and treatment, the average gift is already above the $25 callout.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiments hould not be be considered valid. It is essential to ensure that any future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the hypothesis and experiment design to potentially achieve more significant and reliable results. Testing different variations, such as adjusting the language or positioning of the callout, could also help to optimize future experiments.

experiments.title: How offering a digital version of Is Christianity the Right Religion impacts email acquisition rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a digital download for a Facebook audience will achieve a better email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 05/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
This is an extension of an experiment we tested with a single premium in April; for this experiment, we were testing the Is Christianity the Right Religion premium resources.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Pathway to Victory was able to 4.8% increase in email conversion rates; however the level of confidence is only 67%. Moreover, the cost to acquire an email address was 6% higher for the digital premium.

We believe this result is due to a combination of factors:
By offering up immediate gratification through a digital download, versus potentially waiting up to a month to receive your resource in the mail, the audience was much more likely to sign up to receive it.
 	By not requiring a full mailing address for the digital version, we removed some friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How mentioning reach on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by pointing out the reach due to the gift size for potential fiscal year end donors will achieve higher conversion rate because they know the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2023 - 10/02/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
In an effort to increase donor conversions for fiscal year end donations we decided to test calling out how many people would be reached with the gospel based on their gift size.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pointing out the reach due to the gift size for potential donors can potentially increase conversion rates. The treatment version, which included language highlighting the impact of the gift on reaching people with the gospel, saw a 15.1% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered completely valid.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of highlighting the reach of donations on conversion rates, but with a larger sample size and a longer duration. Additionally, it may be helpful to refine the messaging and design of the treatment version to further enhance its impact. Overall, this experiment provides a promising indication that emphasizing the impact of donations can positively influence donor conversions.

A break down on conversions

Only 24% of donations and 12% of revenue came from nondonors, implying that existing donors are more motivated to give during a FYE campaign.

Existing donors had an average gift of $84.38 while nondonors had an average gift of $41.26. 

Emails 1 and 5 (the final day) emails received the most donations of all the emails. Accounting for 55% of total revenue and 52% of gifts.

Total gifts: 328

Total revenue: $25,189.00

While the treatment for this test did not win out in a valid manner. This was the first full FYE campaign conducted by PTV and based on the results, we consider this a success.

experiments.title: Does Aligning the CTA and the Value Propostion Make a Difference in Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the cta and the value prop for the main donate buttons will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/22/2024
This is the third test in the series of the dual donate button test for FOTF.
The first test we wanted to see if aligning the CTA would make an impact. The second test we wanted to see if aligning the value proposition would make an impact (this test did!)
For the third iteration, we wanted to see if we could test “Give” vs. “Donate” as the CTA to see which language aligns better with the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using "Donate" as the call-to-action (CTA) for the main donate buttons resulted in a 11.9% decrease in donations compared to using "Give". However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.9%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity.
The key finding during this testing series is that the main CTA does nto make much of an impact, but using as much specificity as possible when describing the impact of a donor's gift - even at the button level - does.

experiments.title: How adding a video on a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a video to a landing page for Hoover’s audience will achieve in increase in donations because   they will be more likely to engage and feel connected to the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/21/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  For one of their most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals, we wanted to test whether adding a video that we were already running as a YouTube ad to the landing page would make an impact on donations. We hypothesized that by adding the video to the page, people would become more engaged and connected with the offer and would be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a video to a landing page for Hoover's audience resulted in a 5.2% increase in Revenue for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for valid results.
On the other hand, Email Acquisition experienced a significant 18.2% decrease with a high level of confidence at 98%.
These results suggest that while the video may have positively impacted the revenue, the decrease in email acquisitions raises concerns about the overall effectiveness of the video on the landing page.
In future experimentation, it is important to take into consideration both the positive and negative impacts of the changes made to the landing page. It is also crucial to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is higher before drawing any definitive conclusions. Additionally, further testing and analysis are needed to understand the full impact of adding a video to the landing page and to make informed decisions about its effectiveness in driving conversions.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline  for August Scripture Memory donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the visitor will be less likely to perceive the donation page as a confirmation page and understand there is another step to take.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2024 - 08/20/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a monthly text campaign to memorize scripture. Each month, participants are sent new methods to memorize a new verse. These have been really successful campaigns, but we wanted to continue optimizing the funnel process to see further growth. This test ran on the donation page directly after signing up to participate in the campaign.
For their August campaign, we decided on testing a continuation headline which removes the “Thank you” out of the headline. Our hypothesis was that removing this phrase and adding a “would you consider” would increase donor conversion metrics because the donation page visitor would not perceive the donation page as a confirmation page.
Control Headline – “YOU’RE IN! Thank you for joining us for our Scripture Memory Challenge!”
Treatment Headline – “You’re in! Now, would you consider making a gift so even more believers can experience this Scripture memory challenge?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included a continuation headline on the donation page without the "Thank you" phrase, showed a positive impact on donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in an 11.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 55.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As well as a 23.3% lift in overall revenue at an 86% level of confidence.
Although the results cannot be deemed statistically significant, it is still valuable to consider the potential impact of the continuation headline on donor conversion as well as the positive direction both of these metrics were heading.  In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the headline or explore other elements on the donation page that could enhance donor conversion rates. Additionally, increasing the sample size and running the experiment for a longer duration may help achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

experiments.title: Does using Yes/NO ads to promote a new premium offer impact donor conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using yes/no ads for a Facebook audience will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2023 - 11/06/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry arm of First Baptist Dallas, one of the largest Baptist churches in the country.
For a new resource that was created to respond to questions PTV had been getting with the chaos in Israel, we wanted to test whether using yes/no creative would make an impact on donor conversions. The yes/no concept is something PTV had used on organic posts; we had not tested this before for Facebook ads.
In this experiment, which we ran as adding an additional creative element to a campaign, not a straight A/B test, we tested the control which was an image of the book cover against the yes/no ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using yes/no ads for a Facebook audience did not result in a significant change in conversion rates. The treatment actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

While CPMs across both creative variations were the same, there was a slight decrease in CTR for the variant (1.09% compared to 0.93%). However the cost to acquire a donor on the yes/no variant was 16% higher.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests using different ad formats, messaging, or targeting strategies to see if there are any other variables that could impact conversion rates. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other channels or platforms to reach the target audience. Experimentation is crucial for understanding what strategies work best for increasing conversions, and it is important to evaluate results with a statistically significant level of confidence before making any conclusive decisions.

experiments.title: How adding project-specific value proposition language to a donation page affects donor conversion rate (Pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a donation page and making it a single column experience for prospective lottie moon donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 10/13/2022
When looking at IMB’s high traffic areas in their “Give” section on their website , we saw most donations go through the “Give Now” button on the Lottie Moon callout on this particular landing page. We noticed that when you click through this particular button, you’re taken to a generic donation page with no value prop language our mention of Lottie Moon. We hypothesized that if we added value proposition language that was specific to Lottie Moon, there would be more congruency in the messaging and prospective donors would be more motivated to give when they read about the impact of their Lottie Moon gift.
This is the second iteration of this experiment because we noticed that desktop experienced a large decrease in conversions. So, we hypothesized that we could use the same language but change the layout of the page so that it was a single-column experience so that the user could read through all the value prop language before making a decision
experiments.keyLearnings: Our first results showed a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. However our secondary hypothesis did not prove to be true. This experiment resulted in a 7% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 46% - meaning there is not statistical difference in the treatment. Upon closer examination it seems as if the donation form inadvertently got changed from the control's donation form. The header of the form changed and the gift designation selection was removed. These additional variables may have also impacted the results of our experiment.

experiments.title: How adding YouTube ad video on a landing page impacted instant donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding video to the landing page for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2024 - 08/03/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  For one of their long-term offers, Dueling Populisms, we wanted to test whether adding video-that we were already running as a YouTube ad-to the landing page would make an impact on instant donor conversions. Our hypothesis was that by adding the video to the page, and people ostensibly watching the video, they would be more likely to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a video to the landing page for content offer downloaders did not result in an increase in donation conversions. In fact, the treatment actually led to a 29.7% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 71.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

Tracking for email sign-ups was not added to this experiment, so we cannot analyze whether the video impacted email signups AS WELL as instant donations.

Therefore, based on these results, it is important not to draw definitive conclusions from this experiment. The findings should not be used for future decision-making as the statistical confidence level is below the acceptable threshold.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or potential adjustments to the video content or placement on the landing page. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more reliable and comprehensive data before making any strategic decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How bypassing the registration page impacted email signups and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction in the course registration process for landing page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because there will be less friction in the process and fewer clicks to achieve the desired action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2023 - 12/18/2023
After moving to a new course platform, we wanted to test reducing friction in the process by not having visitors have to click an additional button to begin the registration process. However, that page that preceded the checkout option is what communicated the value proposition of the course to the reader so we wanted to test before changing the process entirely. We set up an A/B test and split traffic to the registration page URL and the other 50% of traffic went to a treatment page which bypassed the registration page and went straight to the course checkout page. We ran the experiment for 2 months and observed how visitors behaved on the email acquisition page as well as downstream impacts to guide decisions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the registration page and reducing friction in the course registration process resulted in a decrease in email acquisition. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To determine the impact of reducing friction on the registration process and email acquisition, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help obtain more statistically significant results and provide more reliable insights.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that might have influenced the decrease in email acquisition. For example, the treatment page may have lacked important information or a clear value proposition, leading to lower engagement and conversions.

Future experiments can also focus on improving the treatment page by incorporating elements of the registration page that effectively communicate the value proposition of the course. This way, the experiment can gauge whether reducing friction while maintaining the value proposition can positively impact email acquisition. It is important to analyze and understand user behavior and preferences to optimize the registration process and maximize email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How enhancing the personal value for the user of a free online course impacted email registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the value to the user for potential course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because the course is then positioned more uniquely and personally as a solution to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2023 - 10/18/2023
When reviewing a course with low sign up rate, we wanted to propose a landing page treatment that would enhance the value of the course to the individual visitor. We put together copy that focuses more on the historical debates and questions about the return of Christ—addressing the questions in the minds of the visitors that would be answered or solved by signing up for the free online course. We attempt to tap into the curiosity of the visitor, positioning the course as a solution to those uncertainties. We also added clarity in the call to action to make it clear what next step the visitor should take before introducing the button to register. The treatment specifically mentions that by signing up, users will receive their first lecture instantly. This adds a sense of immediacy and instant gratification which might entice visitors to take action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the value of the course to potential users can result in an increase in email acquisition. This highlights the importance of positioning the course as a unique and personal solution to the uncertainties and questions that potential users may have.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on highlighting the value and benefits of the courses we offer. This could involve addressing specific pain points or uncertainties that potential users may have and demonstrating how our courses can provide the solutions or answers they are seeking.

Additionally, it is important to remember the significance of clarity in the call to action. Clearly stating the next steps for the visitor and highlighting any immediate benefits, such as receiving the first lecture instantly, can help to create a sense of urgency and incentivize users to take action.

To validate the findings of this experiment and make informed decisions for future experimentation, it is necessary to run additional experiments with a larger sample size and higher statistical level of confidence. This will provide more reliable and valid results that can be used to guide future decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer on a recurring campaign donation pages impacts monthly donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer for DTS’s North Texas Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion because the thermometer adds clarity on the number of people still needed to become a monthly donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/21/2023
Each year, Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) participates in the local giving day, North Texas Giving Day. During previous years, they’ve focused on increasing their number of monthly donors—Cornerstone Partners.
This year, they continued in this tradition, but wanted to see what optimizations they could make to their donation page to see if they could increase recurring donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version of the donation page, which included a monthly donor counter thermometer, resulted in a 23.2% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 64.4%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

3. The hypothesis that adding a monthly donor counter thermometer would increase monthly donor conversions was supported by the observed increase in recurring donations.

4. Future experiments should consider further optimizing the donation page by including a monthly donor counter thermometer as it showed promising results in increasing recurring donor conversions.

5. It is important to have a larger sample size and a higher level of statistical confidence to validate the findings and make informed decisions for future experimentation.

experiments.title: How does adding video on a landing page impact downstream donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding video to a landing page for content offer downlaods will achieve a change in instant donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2024 - 08/03/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  For one of their most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals, we wanted to test whether adding video-that we were already running as a YouTube ad-to the landing page would make an impact on instant donor conversions. Our hypothesis was that by adding the video to the page, and people ostensibly watching the video, they would be more likely to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a video to the landing page for the Victory and the Savior Generals offer did not lead to an increase in instant donor conversions. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions by 3.1% compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 13.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of this experiment are not reliable and should not be used for future decision making.

Tracking for email sign-ups was not added to this experiment, so we cannot analyze whether the video impacted email signups AS WELL as instant donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could have influenced the outcomes, such as the quality or relevance of the video, the placement of the video on the landing page, or the targeting of the audience. Conducting further testing with adjustments to these variables could provide more reliable insights into the impact of adding a video to the landing page on donor conversions.

experiments.title: How changing the picture of a premium resources impacted donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the picture on a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 07/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  Every quarter they publish the Hoover Digest, which is a collection of essays from 30 of their scholars. This offer is one of their best performing acquisition offers. We’ve tested into a premium test for the content offer where we ask donors for a gift of $100 or more to get all 4 editions of the Digest in the mail for the next year.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the image of the Digest itself to something that featured the current cover would make an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the picture on a premium offer for content downloaders led to a 29.6% increase in revenue for all traffic. This implies that the new image featuring the current cover of the Hoover Digest had a positive impact on donor conversions.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 71.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered entirely reliable and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of using updated images or visuals in premium offers to see if they consistently lead to higher conversion rates. It is also essential to ensure that experiments are run for longer durations to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, considering other elements such as copywriting, call-to-action buttons, and layout design in future experiments could provide further insights into what drives donor conversions effectively.

experiments.title: How adding a slide-out donation ask at the 2nd lesson in a course impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a donation earlier in the process for course takers will achieve a change in donation conversions. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2024 - 06/22/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke.
Traditionally, courses do not have any ask on them; there is an email at the end of the course that asks for donations, as well as a thank you screen.
We wanted to see whether using a slide out donation ask DURING the 2nd lesson of the course would generated more donations. Our hypothesis was that people would have received some value from the course already, and instead of waiting a week or 2 for people to go through the entire course, we might be able to get donation conversions earlier.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for a donation earlier in the course can potentially impact email acquisition negatively. Although the treatment group saw a decrease in email acquisition, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for significance.

Therefore, the results of this experiment should not be considered valid, and the hypothesis that asking for donations earlier in the process will increase donation conversions cannot be confirmed based on this data.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the timing and approach of donation asks within the course to test their impact on donation conversions effectively.

experiments.title: How using a static image compares to using a video for a course promotion on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the creative for potential course takes will achieve a change in sign-up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 06/30/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
