One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke. For this Facebook ad creative test, we wanted to see how the long-running control that featured a trailer from the course would perform against a static image with a ‘free course’ call out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which featured a static image with a 'free course' call out, resulted in a 76.0% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic compared to the control.
2. The Cost per Click experienced a 92% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and the Cost per Registration experienced a 185% decrease with a 100% level of confidence for the treatment.
3. Visitors in the 55-64 age group showed a 13% increase in complete registrations with a 96% level of confidence for the treatment.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for the increase in Complete Registrations is only 63.2%, which is below the threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the treatment with a larger sample size to determine if the increase in Complete Registrations is consistent and statistically significant. Additionally, exploring different creatives and messaging strategies could help identify the most effective approach for driving course sign-ups. As the Cost per Click and Cost per Registration decreased significantly for the treatment, it may be worth investigating why this occurred and if similar cost-saving measures can be implemented in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a slide-out donation ask during a course impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a donation earlier in the process for course takers will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2024 - 06/22/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke.
Traditionally, courses do not have any ask on them; there is an email at the end of the course that asks for donations, as well as a thank you screen.
We wanted to see whether using a slide out donation ask DURING the 2nd lesson of the course would generated more donations. Our hypothesis was that people would have received some value from the course already, and instead of waiting a week or 2 for people to go through the entire course, we might be able to get donation conversions earlier.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for a donation earlier in a course can potentially lead to an increase in donations. While the treatment saw a 43.8% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 31.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be important to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive conclusions. It may be beneficial to continue testing different strategies for donation asks at different points in the course to see if there is a consistent trend in donation conversions. Additionally, considering factors such as course content, audience demographics, and timing of donation asks could also help in designing more effective fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How pulling the home donate button out of the hamburger menu on mobile impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for mobile homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks on the donate button because the button will be more visible and accessible therefore reducing friction for mobile users. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/01/2024
We have seen a positive impact with other clients pulling their home donate button on mobile out of the hamburger menu to make it more visible to the visitor, so we wanted to test this on IMB’s homepage.
This test ran only on mobile with traffic equally split between the control and the treatment.
The treatment had a “Give Now” button pulled out of the hamburger menu, but we also left it within the hamburger menu to view clicks on both.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for the IMB homepage led to a 32.2% increase in clicks just on the donate button within the hamburger menu.

As well as, the new mobile donate button resulted in a total of 78 clicks. While the control button within the hamburger menu only had 22.

While this test is difficult to validate, it does not suggest that having the donate button more visible to the user on mobile devices is helpful in getting more users to the main donation page.

**Note: the 'Give Now - Mobile Header' button is tracking clicks on the NEW donate button that appeared outside of the hamburger menu. The 'Give Now - Mobile Menu' button is tracking clicks for both the control and treatment of the original donate button within in the hamburger menu.

experiments.title: How condensing a form and using more positive copy for a content offer impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the landing page form and using more positive copy for prayer calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the form will be perceived as less questions to fill out and the positive tone will resonate more with the visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s main Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a 3-way test. Treatment 1 condensed the landing page form so that it would be perceived as shorter for the user, and treatment 2 also condensed the form as well as used a more positive tone in the copy by changing the first line of the body copy.
Our hypothesis was that both of these changes would increase the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that both condensing the landing page form and using a more positive tone in the copy had little to no impact on the email acquisition rate for the Prayer Calendar landing page.

While these were not the results we were anticipating, it is interesting that the more positive copy did not have more of an impact on the landing page visitor.

Although we did not get any valid metrics from this test, it does tell us that the long form on the landing page does not negatively impact the visitor's motivation to complete the landing page.

We plan to continually run experiments on this funnel and test the motivations of IMB's audience.

experiments.title: How using a shorter more concise landing page for email prospecting traffic impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter and more concise copy for Pray for a Missionary landing page visitors acquired through email prospecting will achieve an increase in the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the page by getting to the purpose much quicker.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to build a funnel that resonated best with the traffic IMB was receiving from email prospecting efforts, we decided to test using a shorter and more concise landing page solely for individuals who received our Pray for a Missionary prospecting emails.
We used VWO audience segmentation to be sure that this test did not impact normal traffic to the landing page.
Our hypothesis was that using more concise copy would help visitors understand the purpose of the page quicker and therefore reduce friction on the page. We tend to think traffic coming in through prospecting channels want quick and easy information to consume so we wanted to put that concept to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using shorter and more concise copy on the Pray for a Missionary landing page for visitors acquired through email prospecting resulted in a 2.5% increase in emails acquired for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity, meaning that these results were ultimately flat.

Therefore we can not make any decisions based on this test. It would be interesting to test whether additional copy and value proposition for email prospecting traffic has a positive impact on email acquisition since our hypothesis was not proven with this test.

experiments.title: How switching the order in which information is presented affects conversion rate

rows.name: Switch order of the Main Donation Page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by switching the order in which information is presented for our donors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2021 - 10/24/2021
We tested this on FamilyLife’s main donation page. We noticed that when filling out your information, the last step to complete the donation process is adding your card information. We suspected that this could potentially cause friction due to all the internal decisions we are already making before hitting “make my gift”. Could be not having your card ready to fill out that information or perhaps your willingness to make that “commitment” decreases as you fill in other information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 57.3% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the impact of a gift impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including this statement of value proposition for visitors will achieve an increase in the number of transactions because the visitor will be reminded about the impact that their gift could make within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2023 - 11/27/2023
In an effort to increase the number of donations for ACI, we wanted to test adding additional value proposition within the donation form. We tested this on a multitude of pages with varying content and offers ranging from the homepage donation page, pro-life pledge donation page, education survey donation page, and a few others. With this being said, each different topic had a different value proposition statement that was relevant. Here are the statements that we used:
Pro-life: Your donation today will help fight for the rights of unborn children across the nation.
Education: Your ongoing support will help educate thousands of children on the true principles of America’s founding throughout the year.
General: Your recurring monthly gift will defend our nation’s guiding principles of life, liberty, community, and faith 365 days a year.
Little Patriots: The entirety of your gift will directly support our efforts to provide free curricula and cartoons that teach true American history through our Little Patriots program.
This statement was shown under the tabbed options of “One-time” or “Monthly,” and it was shown regardless of which tab the visitor had selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition statement within the donation form had a slightly positive impact on the conversion rate for transactions at 4.9%.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 67.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the impact of value proposition statements on donation conversions, using larger sample sizes and longer experiment durations. Additionally, it would be valuable to determine whether the recurring specific statements were more or less beneficial in the future.

By conducting more rigorous experiments and increasing the statistical level of confidence, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights on how to optimize the donation form and increase the number of donations for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion rate on a calendar year end page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for year end donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators were running a year-end campaign and wanted to test whether additional value proposition copy on the donation page would increase conversion. While the page already had visuals, a story, and value proposition copy at the bottom, they wanted to add it to the top of the page to motivate people who might arrive at the page unconvinced to give.
They drafted copy that outlined their plan for the next year, making a case for support, and tested the two pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no significant increase or decrease on the page. Early on, there was a notable increase—but as the month went on, and motivation (and conversion rate) increased, the copy had less and less impact, as the visitors were more motivated to give regardless of what was on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to the main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators historically had very little copy on their primary donation page. As they had set an initiative to generate more “where most needed” gifts (as opposed to staff gifts), they knew they might need to convince donors who arrived on the page to make a gift. However, they did not want to do this at the expense of staff giving. They developed a value proposition that would encapsulate the mission of the organization and placed it on the page. During the month of December they ran an experiment to see how the additional value proposition affected project giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though there was a slight increase in project giving (that was not statistically valid), the added copy to the page produced across-the-board decreases in overall giving, which led them to end the experiment. This showed that the additional copy might have confused staff donors, even if it did motivate more people to make one-time gifts.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 21.0% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.
Desktop visitors had a 4.9% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 7.4% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 7.1% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Direct Traffic visitors had an 11.8% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 12.8% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.7% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an outcomes-focused headline affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an outcome-based headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2021
The Navigators were running a landing page for an ebook and wanted to test if outcome-focused headlines have an impact in conversion email acquisition? For this test we decided to create two different headlines and keeping the copy intact. The control group receives a headline that incites a demand, while the experimental group receives a headline that incites an outcome-focused action.
Control group headline: Get the help you need to memorize Scripture!
Treatment group headline: Commit dozens of the Bible’s most helpful Scriptures to memory
We created this experiment in Unbounce.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not see a significant change between these two groups. however, it is worth testing how an outcome-based headline could potentially increase email acquisition conversion rate when focussing on positive gains (the benefit of getting the ebook, knowledge) rather than a negative one (like putting in some work, or perhaps is time consuming).

Moving forward we could try the above hypothesis.

experiments.title: How using a video on a high urgency campaign donation page impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the transcript of a video on a donation page for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/17/2022
Reasons to Believe launched their fiscal year end campaign and in their launch email they were leading their email recipients to a donation page with a video. Past experiments with other organizations have shown that a video on a donation page can actually add friction to the giving experience. The reason being that many people are unlikely to watch through a whole video and they’re more likely to read through some copy.
For this email we sent half of their list to a donation page with the video (the control) and the other half of the list to a donation page with a transcript of the content of the video (the treatment). Our hypothesis is that more people would convert on the donation page with the transcript of the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 56% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%. Something to note is that on the email itself, RTB used different copy on the CTA button. The control had "Watch my video" and the treatment had "Read my note." When looking at the click thru rate between each button we saw a valid 28% decrease in clicks with a level of confidence of 99%. In a sense, 2 variables were tested in this experiment: the call to action and the donation page itself.

It's likely that RTB's audience is conditioned to watch videos for their content and throughout their high urgency campaigns. However it seems as more traffic was pushed to the video donation page because of the different CTA. Watching a video may sound more appealing than to read something.

It's recommended that this is retested in a channel that gets more traffic and that has the same CTA button, like a sticky bar. This will isolate one variable and get us more definitive results.

experiments.title: How adding sticky donate button impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a step between clicking a button and giving for the sticky donate button will achieve a higher average gift or conversions because the prospective donor would be primed more to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page could increase the number of transactions or the amount of revenue that a donor would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page actually resulted in a decrease in donations as well as revenue. The yes/no question could have caused more friction in the process instead of being a micro yes.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary). On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary). On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation page for Giving Tuesday affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clarity on the donation page will increase donor conversion rate because people have a clear understanding to what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/04/2023
For Giving Tuesday this year, we wanted to test additional clarity on the donation page to see how IMB’s donor base would react.
The control offers an emotional appeal that focuses on the history of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering and emphasizes the tradition of the offering. On the other hand, the treatment page focuses on a time sensitive appeal by mentioning Giving Tuesday specifically, and lays out a more straightforward and urgent need for financial support.
We believe that the added clarity will allow for greater comprehension of the donation page leading to an increase in performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity on the donation page did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control page with a drop of 6.3% with 35% LOC.

Based on this outcome, it is necessary to retest this theory before implementing it. However, the current results suggest that IMB's audience does not respond positively to urgent time-bound appeals, but rather they prefer the emotional appeal and tradition of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering, as it seems to resonate more with the donor base.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that could potentially increase donor conversion rate, such as different messaging or design elements on the donation page. It is also important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is above 95% in order to make valid conclusions from the experiment results.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad with a brighter color background affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad instead of a darker color for Facebook users will achieve an increase in click through rate and decrease in cost per click because the brighter color background will create a sense of vibrancy and attention-grabbing effect, capturing users’ focus and prompting them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
We have recently tested Facebook ads for this ministry client, which was an experiment between organic vs. normal graphic ads. We found that the organic post led to a higher click-through rate and donor conversion. Knowing that, we then wanted to conduct an A/B test between two different organic Facebook ads. The control Facebook organic ad was the original organic ad, with simple white text on a dark-colored navy background. Our treatment for this experiment was the same simple white text but placed on a brighter color teal background. We predict that the brighter color background can create a sense of urgency and stand out to the Facebook user, leading to an increase in click-through rate. The copy on both ads is the same.
This was an A/B test with 50/50 split in traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad did not perform better in terms of Click-Through Rate (CTR) and Cost Per Click (CPC). The treatment group, which used the brighter color background, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this decrease in clicks was only 10.1%, which did not validate since it was not above a 95% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test other variables or factors that could impact CTR and CPC, such as ad copy - this can include ad copy length, font, size, and even emojis/symbols.

experiments.title: How concise copy and additional clarity impacted donor conversions for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points for visitors will achieve an increase in clarity and comprehension.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2024 - 01/16/2024
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar campaign, we decided to run a copy test to condense some of the copy. Our hypothesis was that the more concise the copy, the higher level of comprehension for the potential donor therefore, leading them to the donation form quicker.
We believe that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points will lead to increased clarity and comprehension. As a result, we believe the treatment version will outperform the control version in terms of donor understanding and response, leading to a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the language, reordering information for a more logical flow, and emphasizing key points in the copy had a very slight lift in the number of transactions. While the treatment version of the campaign showed a 1.1% increase in transactions, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.1%, falling below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

Furthermore, we saw a a drop of 35.3% in revenue between the control and treatment at a 12.1% level of confidence. While this did not validate, it is important to consider in future decision making that IMB's donors give less when they are given less value proposition, and more concise copy may lead to a lower average gift.

In future experimentation, it is important to carry out the A/B test for a longer duration to gather a larger sample size and increase the statistical power. This will help establish a more reliable and accurate conclusion about the effectiveness of the treatment version. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine and optimize the treatment version based on the insights gained from this experiment. Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used as a starting point for further iterations and testing to drive improved results.

experiments.title: How loss aversion language on a donation page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the emphasis on their contribution will create a stronger sense of urgency and connection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 02/02/2024
In an effort to increase our conversion rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar donation page, we wanted to test the inclusion of loss aversion language to see how their audience would respond.
Our hypothesis was that by leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion, we believe that potential donors will be more motivated to take action. Specifically, we hypothesize that framing the donation as an opportunity to prevent missed chances and emphasizing the tangible impact of their contribution will create a stronger sense of urgency and connection. This heightened emotional response is expected to increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leveraging the psychological concept of loss aversion did not lead to the desired increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which included loss aversion language, actually saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group as well as an even larger decrease in revenue and average gift.

While this experiment did not validate, it was trending enough in a negative direction that we decided to turn it off. It can be concluded that IMB's audience did not respond well to the loss aversion language.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies or messaging approaches to increase donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be helpful to conduct further research and analysis to understand why the loss aversion language did not have the expected effect. This will allow for more informed decision making and potential adjustments to the messaging strategy in future experiments.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a high-urgency donation page through a slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a slideout for Eagle Lake 2 donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/26/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 during June and July to raise money for their Eagle Lake camps.
We have seen success running social proof-based tests for other clients, so we were anxious to run it for The Navigators.
For this test, the treatment version displayed a pop-out of a statement that read “FirstName from State just donated $GiftAmount”
All the information displayed was data pulled from donors to the campaign. It is also worth noting, that these contacts were pulled manually due to our lack of ability to install a pixel to track conversions on their donation pages.
Our hypothesis was that the pop-out would increase the amount of social proof experienced by the visitor on the page which would then lead to an increase in our donor conversion metrics by increasing a sense of urgency and need to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding social proof in the form of a slideout for the Eagle Lake 2 donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 34.4%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid, and it would be unwise to make any decisions based on them. However, we can assume that the addition of social proof may have a negative consequence in terms of donor acquisition metrics. Therefore, we will need to test this concept again to come to a clear conclusion.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result. Additionally, it might be beneficial to conduct qualitative research to understand why the  slideout did not have the desired effect. This could provide valuable insights that can be used to design more effective experiments in the future.

experiments.title: How adding a slide out impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a step between clicking a button and giving for the sticky donate button will achieve a higher average gift or conversions because the prospective donor would be primed more to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page could increase the number of transactions or the amount of revenue that a donor would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page actually resulted in a decrease in donations as well as revenue. The yes/no question could have caused more friction in the process instead of being a micro yes.

experiments.title: Will a sticky bar with a micro goal increase monthly giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a microgoal of monthly donors for giving tusday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be a specific deadline involved.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For Giving Tuesday this year we wanted to see if we could achieve a higher number of monthly donors by creating a micro goal for the day. On Giving Tuesday we asked people to be one of seventy five monthly donors needed.
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the copy to say “Today is the day” would be more compelling to convert a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy to say "Today is the day" did not result in a higher conversion rate for monthly donors on Giving Tuesday. The treatment group actually had a 6.7% decrease in donations compared to the control.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up can effect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
We have previously conducted this experiment and wanted to see if we used a different image if it affects donor acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage increased transactions for all traffic by 25.6%. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.1%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
This experiment did result in a positive outcome when compared to previous experiments, indicating that using this image may be the way to go moving forward.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration or increasing the number of participants. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variables, such as using different images or messages in the pop-up, to determine their impact on donor acquisition rates.
Overall, while the initial results of the experiment were promising, it is important to prioritize statistical rigor in order to draw accurate conclusions and make informed decisions for future strategies aimed at increasing donor support for WUNC.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase sponsorship conversion by prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/03/2021
Save the Children has thousands of children that people can choose to sponsor at any given time. As users navigate throughout the site, they can sort and filter on certain variables to find the child they want to sponsor: location, gender, age, and by birth month. Many people used the search widgets to filter through children, because the number of children available was overwhelming—even after filtering through multiple searches. In an effort to make the decision process easier for an end user, we hypothesized that prioritizing a single child at the top of the page may increase conversion. To test into this, we developed a treatment that pulled out a single child, that remained static, that used personal language and information of that child. The variation said, “Sponsor Me!” and gave users the ability to quickly add that child to their cart to begin the sponsorship. All sponsorship cart functionality of the variation remained consistent with the control, the variation simply just visually highlighted one child at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in conversion from the treatment variation. Additionally, in the initial weeks of the experiment running, the results were, in fact, positively valid. As the experiment continued to run, the results remained positive, but loss the strength of its validity. That said, this experiment reveals something interesting about the sponsors: while we might think that they want more options to choose, they actually are more likely to choose a child when we choose the child for them. The is a result of removing friction to the decision process.

One other result of this experiment is that we also saw a directional lift in overall purchases from this page. Yes, sponsorship conversion is the primary goal of this page, but it is important to ensure that any tactic we would apply to the page to impact that metric doesn't negatively impact the overall conversion.

Because we initially saw valid results with this experiment, which then turned to directional, we believe that this tactic can improve sponsorship conversion, but the principle may need to be strengthened through the value proposition of the prioritized child. This is the next phase of testing for this feature in an effort to validate the directional results.

experiments.title: How using short, “concern”, communal language in a pop-up impacts email conversion

rows.name: YOU ARE RIGHT TO BE CONCERNED

experiments.researchQuestion: How will short, “concerned”, communal language impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 03/25/2021
In a continued effort to better understand the motivations of site users and people converting on the forced pop-up on the STC website, we tested communal language again – but this time is was much shorter, used “concern” language, and had a similar tone of the call-to-action. This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for nine days, there was no difference in conversion and no sign of the percentage of validity increasing either. The good news is that we didn't see any negative impact on email conversion. That said, the motivation of people seeing and converting on this pop-up is extremely high, and even when trying to reinforce how a person feels - that they are "concerned" - we aren't able to influence their decision to convert or get more people to convert.

experiments.title: Does adding a personal story to a dear reader connect with donors more?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized story for the SeeLife dear reader will achieve a higher conversion rate  because the story aspect to the copy will make donors connect more so than just with the campaigns copy. because  the story aspect to the copy will make donors connect more so than just with the campaigns copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2024 - 05/31/2024
During previous High Urgency Campaigns FOTF has tested into putting Dear Readers on their article pages.
In previous tests we have tried several different variations to get to what our control is now. However, we wondered if it could still be optimized. For this test we wanted to see if we could adjust the copy to be about “Rachel” to see if we could connect with donors in a different way than just creating urgency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized story about "Rachel" did not lead to a higher conversion rate compared to the original control with the urgent copy. The treatment actually saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, meaning these results are not considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the messaging and how it resonates with donors. While personalization can be effective in connecting with donors, it is crucial to test different approaches to see what works best for the target audience. Additionally, ensuring a sufficient sample size and statistical confidence level is important to draw valid conclusions from the experiment. This experiment should not be used to make any definitive decisions, but rather as a learning experience to inform future testing and optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How more relevant copy on a content offer landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using relevant and specific copy for Month specific Prayer Calendar landing page visiotors will achieve our email acqusition rate because there would be more relevancy and an inside look at what is inside the calendar for that month.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2024 - 08/05/2024
IMB has a Prayer Calendar offer that offers monthly prayer prompts. While we have used the entire calendar to market the product, we decided to focus on month-to-month as well to see if the relevancy would increase our email acquisition rate.
On the month-to-month landing page, we tested using a copy change that included the contents of the July prayer prompt versus the general copy used on the main funnel.
We ran this A/B test for about a month with the traffic split evenly between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while using relevant and specific copy for the Month-specific Prayer Calendar landing page seemed like a promising idea, the results did not support the hypothesis. The treatment, which included the contents of the July prayer prompt, actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 17.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and potentially refine the specific copy used for the Month-specific Prayer Calendar landing page. It may be worth exploring different approaches to see if there are ways to leverage the content of the calendar for increased email acquisition without negatively impacting the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as the design of the landing page, call-to-action placement, and overall user experience could also help in optimizing the effectiveness of the campaign.

experiments.title: Will a “Give” button have a higher donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing donate to give for the sticky button will achieve a higher donor conversion because give could be more cohesive with the messaging during year end.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the Donate sticky button on the side of the web pages to say “Give” would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the sticky button from "Donate" to "Give" did not achieve a higher donor conversion. In fact, it resulted in a decrease in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How an in-line banner ask on an article page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a wiki style banner for the article pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the additional value prop on the page can motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2023 - 01/01/2024
For a high urgency campaign we wanted to test if having an ask on an article page above the main hero image would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having the wiki style banner ask on the articles pages increased the total number of transactions by 6.6% but with only a 41% LOC. While the transactions did increase, the revenue actually saw a decrease by 7.6%. This leads me to believe that there could have been an large gift in the control that should not be taken into account when reviewing the results for this experiment.

experiments.title: How an educational tribute widget for new users impacts tribute gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an educational tribute section for new users on a profile without any tribute gifts will achieve an increase in tribute donations because it helps address confusion while also reinforcing what a tribute gift is and the impact it makes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2024 - 08/01/2024
When on a CaringBridge site, users can navigate to a profile’s “Tributes” tab that will catalog any tribute gifts made in honor of the profile. However, if a user does not have any Tributes gifts – this page would either be completely blank or present them with the normal Tribute Widget. Our hypothesis is that especially when thinking about new users, who may not be familiar with what a Tribute gift is, would be left confused on this tab. As a treatment, we developed an educational section that offered supporting copy on what the purpose of this tab was for, the impact of a tribute gift, and then presented the ask after this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the introduction of an educational section for new users on a profile without any tribute gifts led to a 2.5% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic. While this result shows a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the educational section, test different messaging or designs, and consider targeting specific user segments to see if the impact varies.

Overall, the results of this experiment provide some indication that educating users about tribute gifts can potentially increase donations, but further testing is needed to confirm and build upon these findings.

experiments.title: How a dear reader on political articles effects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the inclusion of a dear reader on WUNC’s political articles will result in a higher donor conversion rate because we are adding a ask to pages that have high traffic and could be motivated to give because the are already receiving valuable content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, and was tested on political articles on their website.
The control condition consisted of the articles presented without any additional content.
In the treatment condition, each article was followed by a personalized note from Colin Campbell, WUNC’s Capitol Bureau Chief, addressing readers directly at the end of the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dear reader, at the end of political articles led to a significant increase in Transactions for All Traffic. The treatment group experienced a 105.0% increase in conversions compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 44.9%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results cannot be deemed valid or reliable for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, conducting the experiment for a longer duration may help in capturing more data and improving the reliability of the findings. It is also important to consider other variables that may have influenced the results, such as external factors or changes in audience behavior.
Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further research is needed to confirm the impact of adding personalized notes in political articles on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a dear reader on Education Articles effects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the inclusion of a dear reader in WUNC’s educational articles will result in a higher donor conversion rate because we are adding an ask to pages that have high traffic and could be motivated to give because they are already receiving valuable content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, and was tested on educational articles on their website.
The control condition consisted of the articles presented without any additional content.
In the treatment condition, each article was followed by a personalized note from Brianna Atkinson, their higher education reporter, addressing readers directly at the end of the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on adding a dear reader to educational articles include the following:

1. The treatment condition showed a 103.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control condition.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 59.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.

3. As a result, the increase in Transactions cannot be considered statistically significant, and the results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficiently large to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to account for any potential fluctuations or seasonal variations in donor behavior. It is also crucial to consider other factors that could impact donor conversion rates, such as the timing of donation asks, the clarity of the call-to-action, and the overall user experience on the website. By addressing these factors in future experiments, WUNC can gather more reliable and actionable insights to inform their strategies for increasing donor conversions.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a sticky bar impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage pushdown/sticky bar.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with a yes and no option on the homepage pushdown/sticky bar can lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. The treatment version, which included both options, saw a 19.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 23.2%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%.

While these results are not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responds well to having autonomy and micro-decisions in the donation process.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of two-button options on our homepage pushdown/sticky bar to gauge how it impacts donor conversion rates. It's important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to ensure statistically significant results that can be used for future decision-making. Additionally, we should consider refining the language used in both the control and treatment versions to see how messaging impacts visitor responses. Ultimately, the key takeaway is that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision can positively influence their willingness to donate.

experiments.title: How using a personal image on a sticky bar impacts clicks to a webinar page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar that puts a face to the name of the presenter for our website traffic will achieve a higher click-through rate to the registration page because it adds authority to the ask and makes it more personal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2024 - 06/27/2024
Something we’ve tested into a lot with clients is a “humanized sticky bar.” In an effort to humanize our messaging more and improve our user engagement, I wanted to apply that concept to registration for a LinkedIn Livestream.
The design of the sticky bar stayed the same with the only difference being the addition of Nathan’s (the presenter’s) headshot.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate. We ran this during a very short window of time, so I would like to test this again during a longer period of time to see if we can get a larger sample size and get it to validate.

experiments.title: How including a staff testimonial video within an email impacts donor acquisition metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the staff testimonial video for Eagle Lake 2 email appeals will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion metrics because “people give to people” and the video helps establish a personable connection with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2024
For The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign this year, we wanted to test including a staff testimonial video within an email appeal to see how it impacted donor conversion metrics. The Navigators audience tends to respond more positively to more genuine communication and real stories.
Therefore, for email 7 of this high urgency campaign, we included an applicable video of an Eagle Lake Counselor sharing how God was working within the camp this year. We expected to see an increase in overall donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a staff testimonial video in email appeals for The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The treatment group, which received the email with the video, experienced a 44.5% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the video helped establish a more personable connection with donors, leading to a higher conversion rate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Further, we saw a 37.2% lift in overall revenue at a level of confidence of 78.2%. While this metric is also not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to the inclusion of the video.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of incorporating more person to person connection within appeals, but ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to test different types of testimonial videos or other forms of personal and genuine communication to determine the most effective strategy for engaging donors.

experiments.title: How reducing organizational references and using donor-centric language impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language for course subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is stronger appeal to give based on value and belief alignment with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2024 - 07/19/2024
When evaluating a donation page that appeared for course subscribers, we wondered if the language was too centered around the organization and not focused on the page viewer. We hypothesized we could convince more potential donors to say “yes” to the ask if we treated the donation page like a conversation and used the messaging to align with the page visitors’ beliefs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor-centric language for course subscribers can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion. The treatment group in the A/B experiment saw a 12.8% increase in one-time donations for all traffic, at 43% level of confidence. Results of past tests of this concept suggest that there is a stronger appeal to give when the messaging aligns with the page visitors' beliefs and values, but this test did not validate.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue exploring the use of donor-centric language and messaging that focuses on the values and beliefs of the potential donors. Additionally, incorporating feedback from donors and continuously iterating on the messaging based on donor responses could also help in optimizing donation pages for increased conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does calling out the CTA at the top of an email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by providing the CTA at the very top of an email for email subscribers will achieve a higher gifts and revenue because it highlights what is most important without much reading.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a different book in an effort to encourage donors to give as an incentive
For July, we released a brand new kids book by Dr. Jeffress.
We received an email from another company that used a text box at the top of the email that plainly and briefly gave the CTA in case the reader did not have time to read the entire email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing the call-to-action (CTA) at the very top of an email did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the CTA at the top of the email, saw a 24.8% decrease in revenue compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. This indicates that the decrease in revenue may not be solely attributed to the placement of the CTA at the top of the email.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies to optimize the placement of the CTA in emails. It may be beneficial to test variations in the messaging, design, or timing of the CTA to determine what resonates best with donors and drives the desired actions. Additionally, considering other factors that could have impacted the results, such as the content of the email or the timing of the campaign, could provide further insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How Giving Donor Autonomy in a Dear Reader Affect Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having donor automony for the dear reader donation ask will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donors will feel like they made the choice.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/24/2024
Today in the Word has a high level of success in converting donors.
For this test we wanted to see if having donor autonomy present in the ask could increase the conversion percentage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating donor autonomy in the donation ask did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate as hypothesized. The treatment group actually experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that these results are not valid and should not be relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results and that the statistical analysis is robust. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further evaluate the impact of donor autonomy on donor behavior in different contexts or with different messaging strategies to determine its effectiveness. It is important to approach experiments with a critical mindset and to carefully analyze the results before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on them.

experiments.title: How Adding an Additional Link in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional link for the fye appeal emails will achieve a higher click rate because there is an additional opportunity that wasn’t there before.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Mood runs a FYE campaign each year that relies a lot on email. For this test we wanted to see if adding an additional link in the copy would increase clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding an additional link does increase the click rate by 4%, it is only at a 75% LoC. Furthermore, looking down the adding an additional link does not negatively or positively impact donor acquisition or revenue.

experiments.title: How adding emphasis on recurring giving impacts recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing recurring giving for donation page vistors will achieve more recurring gifts because it encourages donors to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/18/2024
Luther Seminary was running their Fiscal Year End campaign. While writing copy for this campaign, we decided we wanted to test how emphasizing recurring giving on their FYE donation page giving impacted recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing recurring giving on the donation page led to an increase in recurring donations for Luther Seminary. Despite the positive outcome, the results are not statistically significant with only a 43.3% level of confidence. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. This will help in making informed decisions based on reliable data.

experiments.title: How Giving Donor Autonomy in a Dear Reader Affect Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having donor automony for the dear reader donation ask will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donors will feel like they made the choice.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/24/2024
Today in the Word has a high level of success in converting donors.
For this test we wanted to see if having donor autonomy present in the ask could increase the conversion percentage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating donor autonomy in the donation ask did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate as hypothesized. The treatment group actually experienced a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that these results are not valid and should not be relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results and that the statistical analysis is robust. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further evaluate the impact of donor autonomy on donor behavior in different contexts or with different messaging strategies to determine its effectiveness. It is important to approach experiments with a critical mindset and to carefully analyze the results before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on them.

experiments.title: How Adding an Additional Link in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional link for the fye appeal emails will achieve a higher click rate because there is an additional opportunity that wasn’t there before.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Mood runs a FYE campaign each year that relies a lot on email. For this test we wanted to see if adding an additional link in the copy would increase clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding an additional link does increase the click rate by 4%, it is only at a 75% LoC. Furthermore, looking down the adding an additional link does not negatively or positively impact donor acquisition or revenue.

experiments.title: How Adding Sharing Where Donations Are Being Allocated in an Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being realistic about the support that runs Moody Radio for the FYE campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it shows the user the impact that they will make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
During Moody’s FYE campaign we wanted to see if we added copy that said exactly what their donations are supporting could increase motivation to get to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding specific information about what donations are supporting can potentially increase motivation and click rates for the FYE campaign on Moody Radio. Although the treatment group did see a significant increase in clicks compared to the control group, the statistical level of confidence was below the desired threshold of 95%.

Results on the donation page show little to no impact on the amount of transactions or the average gift.

This indicates that while the results are promising, they are not conclusive enough to make a definitive decision based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this hypothesis with larger sample sizes or different variations in the message to determine if this strategy consistently leads to increased engagement with the donation page. It is important to remember that valid results with a high level of statistical confidence are crucial for making informed decisions based on experimental outcomes.

experiments.title: How Adding the Impact of the Individual Donor Makes Affects Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the impact that one donor makes for the FYE emails will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the solution is whittled down where the one donor is the hero.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For the test, we wanted to see if adding a paragraph saying the impact that the reader will make increases the motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a paragraph explaining the impact that one donor makes can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 40.7% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that donors may be more motivated to give when they understand the direct impact of their contribution. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 78.2%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of highlighting the donor's role as the hero in the narrative. It may also be helpful to explore other ways of emphasizing the donor's impact, such as highlighting specific success stories or showcasing the tangible outcomes of donations. Additionally, ensuring that future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence will be crucial in making informed decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How shortening copy on a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for instant donation page visitors will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because the donor will be able to see the donation form right away, which takes away friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 07/05/2024
ACI has a survey that has been running in acquisition for a while. It was preforming well, but we were wanting to refresh the copy. We decided to test the donation page, because the copy on it was long. We tested shorter copy, so it all could fit on the page without the user having to scroll. That way, when the user arrives on the page, they can see the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the copy on the instant donation page did not lead to an increase in conversion rate for one-time donations. The treatment group actually experienced a 6.3% decrease in conversion rate compared to the control group.

Since the statistical confidence level of 37.0% is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered reliable or valid for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variables or elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. By continuing to experiment and gather data, we can make more informed decisions on how to optimize the donation page for better conversion rates.

experiments.title: How Adding Urgency to a Dear Reader Affects Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown for the dear reader will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because adding the extra urgency on the page will also increase desire to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Today in the Word is a ministry of Moody bible Institute. Their mail mission is to spread the gospel through daily devotionals. Because they are a devotional ministry the majority of the contents on its website are article pages with previous daily devotionals.
We only ran this test on the article pages to ensure that we received enough traffic to be able to perform the test. Their audiences have seen multiple dear readers before, and their response has been overwhelmingly positive. For this experiment, we wanted to see if increasing the urgency on the dear reader would increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the article pages of Moody Radio's Today in the Word website showed that adding a donor countdown for the dear reader resulted in a 1.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 2.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding urgency to the dear reader message may have a positive impact on donor conversion rates.

2. A 1.0% increase in donations is promising, but the results need to be statistically significant to draw definitive conclusions.

In the future, we should consider testing this approach again to see the validity of the test can increase.

experiments.title: Does a Donor Countdown on a Sticky Bar Lead to More Donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown for the sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the countdown will create a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Today in the Word is a daily devotional website by Moody Bible Institute.. This website draws in a ton of traffic daily to digest its content.
During FYE, we wanted to see if we could acquire donors through a sticky bar. We have tested into sticky bars in the past so we know it does work for their audience.
For this test, we want to see if adding a donor countdown in the CTA will create more urgency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor countdown to a sticky bar did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

However, the treatment did see $400 more revenue. An assessment still needs to be done to see whether this treatment lead donor to give more generously, or if there are a couple of outlying larger gifts skewing the data.

experiments.title: How an embed form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an embedded donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an embedded form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the embedded form.
