experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an embedded donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

In terms of revenue, the control produced $3,555 in revenue and the treatment produced $1930. Therefore the control had an average gift of $148 and the treatment produced an average gift of $77. This produced a 44.8% drop in revenue at a 65.3% level of confidence, also considered invalid.

Due to the lack of validity in results, these results indicate that the embedded made little to no difference in the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience.

In future experimentation, we plan to continue testing ways to optimize IMB's donation process and increase our donor conversion metrics for the organization.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter with a dynamic ask impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 07/16/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we originally ran a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift. Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
This experiment saw a 7.8% lift in recurring donations with a 35% level of confidence. Additionally, we tracked the amount of “yes” clicks on the donation interrupter and found that 4.98% of people who originally initiated a one-time gift of $100 or less converted their gift to a $10 recurring gift.
In this new experiment, we wanted to test adding a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter to capture more donors who were giving gifts larger than $100. The dynamic interrupter ran as follows:
$100>: $10 ask
$100-$199: $15 ask
$200-$299: $30 ask
$300-$399: $40 ask
$400-$500: $50 recurring ask
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter did not achieve an increase in recurring giving as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 2.5% decrease in Recurring Donation for All Traffic with a low statistical level of confidence of 15.6%. This means that the control group actually performed better in terms of recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in recurring donations, we saw that 3.47% of people converted their one-time gift to a monthly gift based on the number of "yes" clicks on the pop-up.
Although this conversion rate it lower than what we saw in the first experiment, it is to be expected. We hypothesize that the conversion rate is the same as experiment 1 for donors who initiated a gift lower than $100, but expect the conversion rate to be a lot lower for people who initiated a gift larger than $100.
We believe this is because people who were making a gift larger than $100 had the intention to give a significant one-time contribution rather than committing to a smaller recurring donation. This suggests that while the dynamic ask amount may not have been effective in increasing overall recurring donations, it did still manage to convert a notable percentage of one-time donors to monthly contributors, particularly among those giving smaller amounts.

experiments.title: Does an Offer or a Direct Ask Popup Result in More Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a direct ask for the slide up will achieve a higher donor conversion because it is an ask that their audience is familiar with.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs an FYE campaign each year that brings in more traffic to their website. During this campaign, we wanted to present an ask on their website outside of a sticky bar.
For this test we wanted to see if the audience responds better to a direct ask or an offer that has an ask on the end of it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a direct ask for the slide-up did lead to a 19.4% increase in donations for all traffic. While this result is encouraging, the statistical level of confidence was only at 28.4%, which is below the minimum threshold of 95% needed for results to be considered valid.

Therefore, it is important not to make any significant decisions based on these results alone. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experiment design to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence. This will help in accurately determining the effectiveness of different approaches and making informed decisions for optimizing donation conversions during campaigns.

experiments.title: Does a Personalized Sticky Bar Impact Donor Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a humanized sticky bar for the FYE campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion because the ask is coming from somebody familiar, which can seem more personal .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that drives more traffic to their website.
In the past, we have put up sticky bars on their site when there is a high urgency campaign going on. For this test we wanted to see if having a normal sticky bar or a humanized sticky bar from Dr. Jobe would result in more conversions
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a humanized sticky bar from Dr. Jobe for the FYE campaign resulted in a 9.8% decrease in donations compared to a normal sticky bar. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 30.7%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of humanized messaging to see if there is a more effective way to personalize the ask for donations. Continual testing and iteration based on data-driven insights will help to optimize strategies for increasing donor conversions in the future.

experiments.title: Will a countdown clock on the homepage increase urgency and lead to higher donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because seeing a deadline of when the campaign ends increases urgency more than a donor countdown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/18/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that drives a ton of traffic to their website.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if we could test into which tactic works better to increase urgency: donor countdown or timer.
Below are the results that we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a timer for the homepage sticky bar led to a 47.0% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before implementing any changes based on the results. It may be beneficial to run the experiment again with a larger sample size or for a longer duration to obtain more reliable and conclusive results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different tactics and elements to optimize donation conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson for donation page vistors will achieve more transactions because there is social proof that what ACI does is approved by Trump.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2024 - 05/15/2024
ACI’s Dr. Carson has a relationship with former president Trump. Since ACI’s audience is mainly conservatives, we wanted to try using a quote from Trump about Dr. Carson on a few of ACI’s instant donation pages. We created a sticky bar that had the quote and a photo of Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson on the donation page did not result in an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only 38.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide valid and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the audience and their preferences when incorporating endorsements or quotes from public figures. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies or elements to understand what resonates best with ACI's conservative audience. A larger sample size and further testing could provide more conclusive insights for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How a embedded form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an IDonate embed donation form for the Bible Direct Ask Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because there would be less visual friction within the donation form. because there would be less visual friction within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 07/09/2024
IMB uses a custom frame donation form for all donation pages. In an effort to optimize their donation process, we decided to test using an IDonate embed form that did include the styling of their custom frame. Our goal was that by removing the styling, we would see an increase in donor conversion metrics due to the more natural-looking donation form process. We tested this split URL test on their Bible Direct Ask offer called “God’s Word for the Nations” with equal traffic going to both the custom frame and the IDonate embed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an IDonate embed donation form on the Bible Direct Ask Funnel resulted in only a 5.7% lift in transactions at a level of confidence of 15.3%, indicating these results are invalid.

These results indicate that the IDonate embed made little to no difference on the donor conversion metrics for IMB's audience. It is important to note that the revenue is not accurate in the above graph due to the fact that revenue from the IDonate Embed does not flow into Winston.

In future experimentation, we plan to continue testing ways to optimize IMB's donation process and increase our donor conversion metrics for the organization.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors resulted in a 4.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 27.3%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and potentially increase the validity of the results.

Overall, while the initial results show promise, it is essential to rely on statistically significant data to make informed decisions about implementing changes to the website.

experiments.title: How Adding a Content Offer Exit Intent Affects Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a second cta  for when a user abondends the page will achieve a higher email acq. rate because signing up for a content offer is a less committed ask than making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2024 - 06/19/2024
Focus on the Family ran a SeeLife campaign which drives a ton of traffic to their campaign donation page. When a user decides not to make a gift, we wanted to see if we could get them to convert on a content offer instead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The treatment, which involved offering a second call-to-action for users who abandoned the donation page, resulted in a 1.4% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic. This indicates that providing an alternative action for users who choose not to make a donation could potentially lead to more conversions.
2. The Email Acquisition metric experienced a significant 96.1% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that offering a content offer as an alternative to making a donation can be an effective way to acquire email addresses and potentially nurture leads for future donations.
3. While the results showed a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered statistically valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Continue testing alternative actions for users who do not convert on the primary call-to-action, such as offering content offers or other engagement opportunities. These alternative actions can help capture leads and potentially convert them into donors in the long run.
2. Pay attention to the statistical significance of the results. Results with low levels of confidence should be viewed with caution and further testing may be needed to validate the findings before making any strategic decisions based on the experiment.
3. Use the insights from this experiment to inform future testing strategies and prioritize initiatives that focus on email acquisition and lead nurturing, as these can play a critical role in driving conversions and donor engagement.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form tab affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s monthly gift tab and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2024 - 06/11/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
Wondering whether this concept would appeal to donors on another direct appeal donation page, they also tested this flag image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button, which led to a decrease in donations at a near-valid level of confidence.
As a follow-up experiment on the direct appeal donation page, they wanted to test whether the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, namely recurring donations.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's monthly gift tab and submit button did not lead to a valid increase in donations, specifically recurring donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 40.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of 78.8% is below the valid threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable.

The treatment also resulted in just a 0.17% increase in recurring donations at a 68.3% level of confidence. With such a small sample size, these results cannot be relied upon to inform future action.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a statistically valid result. Additionally, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as flag icons on donor behavior. Other ideas for follow-up testing include testing a different icon than an American flag or testing this concept on a donation page following a content offer.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount to a main donation page form submit button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for main donation page donors will achieve an increase in donation rate because increased clarity and donor autonomy will persuade donors to follow through with making their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase donation rate on their main donation page, The Heritage Foundation wondered whether adding the donor’s gift amount in the donation form submit button would increase clarity and donor autonomy and lead to this increase.
The control form submit button read “MAKE MY GIFT” while the treatment button read “MAKE MY GIFT OF $(amount)”.
Each variant in this A/B experiment received 50% of traffic which ran for 24 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button on the main donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.0%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Future experimentation should include running this test on a donation page that receives more traffic, such as a content offer.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the submit button text or other elements on the donation page to identify what truly resonates with donors and leads to an increase in donations.

experiments.title: How Changing a Headline to a Universal Value Proposition Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a universal headline that focuses on the value proposition for the GOAA/SOAA pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the headline will not be talking about the transaction, but rather the value that a person would get if they choose to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/31/2024
Focus on the Family has several GOAA/SOAA (gift of any amount/ sustainer of any amount) pages that they will drive traffic to each day from various sources. Each page is only advertised for a day or two. For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could create a headline that could lead people to convert more, but also make the headline broad enough that it could be used for all of the pages. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that creating a universal headline that focuses on the value proposition for the donation pages can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this particular case, the treatment version of the headline resulted in an 18.5% increase in Transaction - Recurring for All Traffic, although the statistical level of confidence was only 59.5%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing headlines that focus on the value proposition for donors rather than the transaction itself. This approach seems to resonate with potential donors and may lead to higher conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that the experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive conclusions.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small amount of people donate after requesting an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a free ebook offer for “Guide to the Constitution” for several years. Due to how long this offer has been in the marketplace, we’ve seen ICR slowly decline.
Because of this, we wanted to test highlighting the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take download the ebook are willing to take the next step and donate.” They’ve ran this experiment for other offers and have seen valid lifts in ICR.
This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the fact that only a small minority of people donate for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 32% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is only 66%, therefore these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing different messaging and strategies to determine what resonates best with potential donors. It is possible that highlighting the low donation rate may not have had the intended effect on the target audience. Future experiments could explore different ways to appeal to the ideal donor and emphasize the impact of their contribution, rather than focusing on the low donation rate. By continuing to test and iterate, The Heritage Foundation can refine its approach to maximize conversions and ultimately increase donations.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the higher contrast button did not have a positive impact on clicks or overall giving. In fact, all tracked metrics resulted in invalid decreases.

"Give Now" Button Clicks:

 	Treatment - 93 clicks
 	3.2% decrease in clicks, 17.6% LOC
Control - 362
 	Treatment - 335

experiments.title: How using a lighter background on a video series landing page impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a light background for the video series landing page will achieve an increase in clicks to the checkout page because the contrast from the white background will improve readability for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2024 - 04/25/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They created a landing page for a new paid video series and wanted to know if the current, black background of the page was harming the user experience.
We decided to test a light-background version of the page against the current dark background. Our hypothesis was that the white background would lead to more clicks to the checkout page because white backgrounds in web design are shown to enhance readability and reduce visual strain.
We ran this as a split URL test in VWO, with a random 50% of the traffic going to the dark background page. The other 50% were instead shown a light background version of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that changing the background color of the video series landing page from black to white did not result in the desired increase in clicks to the checkout page. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 2.4% decrease in visits to the Focus Store Checkout Page compared to the control group.

Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of this experiment were essentially flat and shouldn't be considered a valid test result.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that changing the background color alone may not be enough to drive increased engagement with the checkout page. Further research and experimentation may be necessary to identify the most effective strategies for improving user experience and encouraging clicks to the checkout page.

experiments.title: How does a video as creative in a Facebook ad impact donor conversion

rows.name: PTV DA Beacon of Hope Matching Challenge
PTV DA Beacon of Hope Matching Challenge video

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a video with captions for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because more information is provided in the video and a potential donor can have a higher motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2024 - 06/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each June and December, PTV conducts a matching challenge to encourage donors to give.
For the June 2024 campaign, we decide to run two different ad variants to see which would perform better, a static image vs a video with captions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a video with captions, resulted in an 11.2% increase in Purchases for All Traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that the treatment had a positive impact on donor conversion rates.

2. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 42.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and it is not advisable to make future decisions based on this experiment alone.

3. Clicks saw a significant increase of 152% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that the video with captions was more engaging and attracted more viewers to click on the ad.

4. The Cost per Click experienced a 65% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, showing that the treatment was more cost-effective in terms of generating clicks.

5. Female visitors showed a remarkable 657% increase in purchases with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the video with captions resonated particularly well with female audiences.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. Ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results with a confidence level of at least 85%. This will help in making more reliable conclusions.

2. Continue to explore the use of videos with captions in advertising campaigns, especially for targeting female audiences, as it showed promising results in this experiment.

3. Take into account all the metrics analyzed in this experiment, such as Clicks and Cost per Click, to gauge the overall effectiveness of different ad formats.

4. Conduct further research and experimentation to validate the initial findings and make informed decisions on the best strategies to improve donor conversion rates in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to an instant donation page through a FOMO slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a free ebook offer through multiple paid advertising channels.
They had previously tested a FOMO slideout on their ‘Renew Membership’ donation page but did not see any valid results in terms of donor conversion. They wanted to test how this concept would perform on an acquisition donation page.
The FOMO slideout pulled the most recent donations from that donation page and slid out every 10 seconds showing a different donor’s first name and state saying they just donated to The Heritage Foundation.
This test was ran for 20 days and traffic was split 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 2.3%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to detect any significant differences between the control and treatment groups. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the targeting of the paid advertising channels. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the FOMO slideout on different types of pages or with different messaging to see if it has a different impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline affect donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for ebook downloaders will increase the donor conversion rate because we’re providing clarity on their next steps and sparking curiosity by capitalizing on the visitor’s heightened attention and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously seen lifts in ICR when removing ‘thank you’ from the headline and instead, clarifying on what the users next steps are. Because of this, we wanted to test this concept on their Guide to the Constitution donation page. This instant donation page follows a free ebook download landing page.
Traffic was split 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a continuation headline for ebook downloaders may have a positive impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment version, which clarified the next steps for the user and prompted them to consider donating, saw a 2.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical confidence level of 6.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test this concept with a larger sample size or a longer duration to determine if the trend towards increased donor conversions holds true. It is important to always prioritize statistically significant results with a high level of confidence when making decisions based on experimentation outcomes.

experiments.title: How Adding More Value Prop to a Campaign Donation Page Affects Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the donaton page for the See Life campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion because the value of what is on the page will increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2024 - 05/15/2024
Focus on the Family runs a SeeLife campaign each year that receives a ton of traffic to its campaign donation page. The original campaign donation page was very short and did not have a ton of value proposition on it. We wondered if adding more value proposition, and length to the test could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more value proposition to the donation page for the See Life campaign did not result in a higher donor conversion. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 76.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the donation page with varying levels of value proposition to see what resonates best with donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donor conversion, such as the overall user experience, the donation process, and the clarity of messaging on the page. By conducting thorough and valid experiments, Focus on the Family can gather meaningful insights to optimize their donation pages and ultimately increase donor conversion in the future.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a gift impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact of a gift by restructuring the copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help direct the reader’s attention to it and place greater emphasis on the impact of the donor rather than the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2024 - 06/23/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion metrics on The Navigators Crossword Puzzles Content Offer, we decided to run an A/B test split 50/50. With previous offers, we have seen a positive lift in transactions and revenue when calling out the impact of a gift. Therefore, we expected to see similar results on this test.
The treatment reworked the structure of the copy to direct the reader’s attention toward the portion of the copy that emphasized the impact of their gift with the expectation that we would see a lift in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reworking the copy to emphasize the impact of a gift can have a positive effect on donor conversion metrics, specifically increasing transactions. In this case, the treatment saw a significant 69.0% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was 68.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, while the results suggest that emphasizing the impact of a gift can be effective in driving donations, they cannot be considered valid for future decision-making due to the low level of statistical confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before making any definitive conclusions or implementing changes based on the results. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data and increase confidence in the results. Additionally, we plan to further test this concept with The Navigators audience to help validate these findings for future offers.

experiments.title: How does mentioning the free resources a subscriber has received impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for nondonors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they reciprocate the value they have received.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December and June, PTV conducts matching challenges where a donor’s gift will have double the impact.
In an effort to convert nondonors to donors, we wanted to test mentioning that the subscriber has benefited from the free resources provided by PTV.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for non-donors led to a decrease in donations, contrary to our hypothesis. However, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the decrease in donations when mentioning the free resources. This could involve gathering feedback from subscribers or conducting additional tests to understand the impact of different messaging strategies.

Overall, while this specific experiment did not provide conclusive results, it highlights the importance of rigorous testing and the need to interpret results with caution before making any significant changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How does mentioning the free resources a subscriber has received impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for nondonors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they reciprocate the value they have received.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December and June, PTV conducts matching challenges where a donor’s gift will have double the impact.
In an effort to convert nondonors to donors, we wanted to test mentioning that the subscriber has benefited from the free resources provided by PTV.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning that the subscriber has received free resources for non-donors led to a decrease in donations, contrary to our hypothesis. However, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the decrease in donations when mentioning the free resources. Overall, while this specific experiment did not provide conclusive results, it highlights the importance of rigorous testing and the need to interpret results with caution before making any significant changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How a simplified and user-focused value proposition affects sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop about the user’s journey and desired impact for the DDJ landing page  will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because this will better communicate the value and focus on answering why they should be interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2023 - 08/16/2023
After a previous test on the DDJ landing page that tested into layout, The Navigators wanted to turn their attention to testing the elements themselves. In this copy test, we hypothesized framing the language around the user and simplifying the copy to their desired impact rather than the Navigators would increase sign-up rate. Rather than answering why the organization created Digital Discipleship, the treatment is focused on asking questions directly to the user and positioning the resource as the answer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifyign the value prop   did not achieve the desired increase in sign-up rate for the DDJ landing page. The treatment actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control, however this was not at a valid level of confidence. More testing is required.
Some questions to consider are the ways in which this framing shifted the responsibility onto the reader. Although the attempt was to not focus on why the organization created the resource so much as the user's journey and the impact they want to make — it's possible this put too much pressure on the reader. What if they aren't ready to disciple or commit to "come alongside someone?" The original used these terms, but only in the sense of whether the readers themselves has ever had someone do this for them, not whether they want to do this for someone else.
Future experimentation should continue to test different approaches to framing the language and offer on the DDJ landing page to determine the most effective strategy for increasing sign-up rates. It is important to gather more data and achieve a higher statistical level of confidence in order to make valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How prioritizing body copy over a header image affects landing page sign-up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing the body copy over the header image for users arriving on the DDJ landing page will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because headline and copy will better reinforce the value of why they should sign up than an image will. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2023 - 08/11/2023
The landing page for The Navigators’ Digital Disciple Journal is focused on name acquisition. In an attempt to increase sign-up rate on the page, we created two different treatments against the control. The first utilizes a smaller header image, our hypothesis is that this would prioritize the copy more and communicate more value when the user first arrives on the page versus only seeing the image and some of the headline. We theorized that especially in mobile, this would show the greatest improvement. For the second treatment, we eliminated the header images completely. When the user arrives on the page, they are immediately met with the headline and value copy of the body text. We belive both of these edits, though slight, would make an impact on sign-up by better prioritizing what the user sees and reads when they land on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that prioritizing the body copy over the header image on the landing page of the Digital Disciple Journal resulted in a very small different. The increase was only 2.5% with a level of confidence 26%. What this tells us is that despite rearranging the elements of the page, this is unlikely the reason why users are not converting on the page. Instead, it prompts us to turn our focus towards the elements themselves rather than their order. Whether they see the body copy when they first arrive or not, what does the body copy actually say?
In future experiments, it would be valuable to further explore these questions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to investigate other factors that may influence sign-up rate, such as the placement and prominence of the sign-up form, the call-to-action wording, or the use of social proof and testimonials

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.
Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on donation pages impacted revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our video series acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the smaller button will provide less of a competition for the donor’s attention as they’re making their gift, and this means that the friction will be reduced.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 06/26/2024
The team at Focus on the Family (FOTF) has been testing various elements of their donation pages to determine which factors could improve donor conversion and revenue. One element that we wanted to test was the Express Checkout button that appears in the donation form below the gift array. Users who click this button can choose to donate through Google Pay and the idea is that this makes the donation process easier and thus increases donor conversion. However, we wondered if this button could serve as a distraction during the donation process and actually negatively affect donor conversion.
FOTF first tested removing the Express Checkout button on their main donation page and the results were very flat. They then tested whether reducing the Express Checkout button’s size would drive a different outcome. This test found that the smaller button size led to a 21.3% increase in transactions, with a 95.7% level of confidence.
The purpose of this experiment is to see if that lift in transactions will occur when we test the smaller button on FOTF’s video series acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were completely flat; we didn't see any significant changes in revenue or transactions between the control and treatment. This means that, even though we didn't see any statistically-significant improvement on these metrics, the smaller button didn't hurt our donor conversion or average gift.

Because the smaller button saw a positive lift on Focus on the Family's other donation pages -- and did not harm their acquisition donation page conversion -- it's recommended that the smaller button treatment be rolled out on all of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How using clarity and an offer in a email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate

rows.name: Do you want to grow in your discipleship?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and incentives in the subscription for site visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because prospects will know what they’re getting and are further incentivized by receiving something of value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2023 - 05/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section to add clarity in the first variant. We also wanted to test using an ebook as additional incentive to sign up in the second variant. The use of these two different variants would give us some indicators as to what motivates people to subscribe to the Navigators’ communications.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment showed us that people were more drawn to take action when given clear proposition language over an ebook incentive.

Treatment 1 resulted in a 27% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 64% while treatment 2 resulted in a 5% decrease with a level of confidence of 15%. In order to get valid results, Navigators will ran this test again with a 50/50 split with the first treatment.

experiments.title: How Adding a Premium to the Winter Digest Affects ICR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium  for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate  because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 05/14/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The user gets a free digital version but this test upsells them on 4 physical versions for $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version, which offered physical copies of the digest for a $100 donation, resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 13.8% compared to the control version. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.
Therefore, based on these findings, it cannot be conclusively stated that the addition of a premium for donation page viewers led to a significant impact on conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the offer and possibly testing different variations to see if there is a more effective way to encourage donations without negatively impacting transaction rates. It is important to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficiently long period with a large enough sample size to obtain statistically significant results that can be used for making informed decisions.

experiments.title: How using a testimonial in a Facebook ad affects CTR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a testimonial for facebook users will achieve a higher click-through-rate because it adds credibility to the advertisement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2024 - 05/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. The Property Rights content offer has a low click-through rate, and the team wants to improve its performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which included a testimonial for Facebook users, resulted in a 12.7% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic with a 54.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

2. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 470% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment may not be cost-effective compared to the control.

3. The 25-34 age group saw a substantial 1000% decrease in clicks with a 98% level of confidence when exposed to the treatment. This suggests that the addition of a testimonial may not resonate well with this demographic.

Based on these findings, it is clear that the addition of a testimonial for Facebook users did not yield positive results in terms of Clicks for All Traffic, Cost per Click, and engagement from the 25-34 age group.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider refining the messaging of the testimonial, targeting specific audience segments, or testing different types of social proof to see if it positively impacts the performance of the Property Rights content offer. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical confidence levels are high enough to draw valid conclusions for decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for survey takers will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 05/14/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Voter Fraud Survey offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that clarifying the language in the tab titles of a donation form can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates, both for one-time donations and recurring donations. The treatment version, which used clearer language ("Make a One-Time Gift", "Make a Monthly Gift"), saw a slight 0.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 2.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% to consider the results valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Interestingly, the same treatment did lead to an increase in email conversion rate on a donation page following an ebook offer. This suggests that the treatment tab titles may resonate more strongly with people downloading an ebook as opposed to people who have taken a survey.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of language clarity in survey donation forms on conversion rates, ensuring that statistical significance is achieved to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the design, layout, and call-to-action language in donation forms could also be valuable for optimizing conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the Digest Premium Offer Affected ICR on the Trickle Down Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2024 - 05/17/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The NextAfter team tried this test on the Hoover Digest Donation Page, and it performed well, so it was implemented on other offers to test if it would improve ICR on more than just the Hoover Digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a premium offer on the donation page can potentially lead to an increase in transactions for the Hoover Institution. In this case, the treatment group experienced a 34.8% increase in transactions compared to the control group. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 74.0% is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

Therefore, while the experiment showed a positive outcome, it cannot be deemed as a conclusive result. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing the inclusion of premium offers on the donation page to see if the trend of increased transactions holds true with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other elements such as different types of premiums or messaging strategies to further optimize the donation page and ultimately improve conversion rates for the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How the Digest Premium Offer Affected ICR on the Dueling Populism Offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for donation page viewers will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because their donors want physical copies of the digests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2024 - 05/17/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank at Stanford University in California. The NextAfter team tried this test on the Hoover Digest Donation Page, and it performed well, so it was implemented on other offers to test if it would improve ICR on more than just the Hoover Digest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a premium to the donation page for viewers may not necessarily increase the instant conversion rate. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 60.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

Therefore, based on these results, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this particular experiment. It is important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and possibly different variations to truly understand the impact of including a premium on donation page viewers.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies for increasing conversion rates, while ensuring that the sample size is sufficient and the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. It is also essential to consider other factors that may influence donor behavior and tailor experiments accordingly.

experiments.title: How a multi-step form impact donation conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a single step form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversions because previous research suggests that well-optimized single step form out performs a multi-step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2024
In this experiment, George W Bush Presidential Center had just migrated to the iDonate platform. Their previous donation page used a long-form, single page donation form. When migrating to the iDonate platform, they opted to roll-out a more streamlined multi-step donation form—but they wanted to test the impact of the multi-step widget versus a single step form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that in this specific case, the single step form for main donation page visitors did not achieve an increase in conversions as hypothesized. But with a level of confidence of 52%, the likelihood is that there is no significant difference in performance between the control and the treatment.

Bush Center can move forward knowing the moving to a multi-step for is unlikely to harm results. In fact, there's a chance that it may be the more effective treatment.

With low conversion volume on the donation form, future experimentation should be focused on driving more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Pop-Up Impacts Clicks and Downstream Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a pop-up from the perspective of a real person and including their picture for users on the Human Coalition homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the pop-up and creates a more compelling experience having it come from a real person “in the field” .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, we hypothesized that a humanized pop-up written from the perspective of a Practice Manager at a center and the inclusion of her picture would generate greater click-thru and downstream donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved a pop-up written from the perspective of a real Practice Manager at a center and included her picture, resulted in little to no difference in downstream conversion. Flat results were also observed on click-thru rate as well:

Control: 1,538 | 15 Clicks | Click Rate: 1.0%

Treatment 1,552 | 13 Clicks | Click Rate .8% | -14.1% Difference | LOC: 31.4%

Although a greater sample size and further testing is required to achieve a valid learning, what this trend does indicate is that the humanized experienced made little difference for users on the homepage.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Letter Treatment on a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized treatment of the donation page that looks like a letter from a Practice Manager for users on the Mother’s Day campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it humanizes and personalizes the appeal to come from a real person who is working at the centers impacted. .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, we tested a humanized experience in various channels of the campaign. For the donation page, we hypothesized that updating the intro copy and end of the donation page to look like a letter from Amy, a Practice Manager, would help humanize the experience, personalize the appeal, and as a result improve donor conversion. It’s important to note that aside from the intro and sign-off, no other copy was changed on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The humanized treatment in the form of a letter from a Practice Manager on the donation page for the Mother's Day campaign resulted in a 27.8% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. While this humanized treatment showed promise in increasing donor conversion, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the results. Other metrics to observe are revenue and recurring.

Revenue saw slight improvements in average gift but below an 85% level of significance:

Control: $1,442 from 26 Gifts | $55 AVG Gift

Treatment $1,669 from 27 Gifts | $61 AVG Gift

Recurring:

Control: 716 Samples | 2 Recurring Donations | .3% Conversion

Treatment 655 Samples | 4 Recurring Donations | .6% Conv Rat | 118% Relative Difference | 63.9% Significance

experiments.title: How removal of an array in favor of a simplified ask in email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying an expanded gift array in email for consistent $26 ask in the copy for Human Coalition housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it eliminates decision friction and maintains the consistency of the $26 ask presented in all other parts of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, the main ask presented to the donor was that $26 help reach a mom seeking an abortion. However, in various email the reader would also be presented with an expanded array ask that requested $26, $52, $104, or their best gift in the email copy. Our hypothesis is that by simplifying this array to instead continue focus on the $26 ask would improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the gift array in email by consistently focusing on a single ask amount of $26 can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment group saw an 18.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 39.4%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Interestingly, it should also be noted that despite the treatment eliminating the expanded array of asks, ultimately average gift remained similar between the control and treatment.

Control: 112,985 Samples | 17 Donations | .1% Revenue Per Visitor | Avg Gift $78.22

Treatment: 112,131 Samples | 20 Donations | .1% Revenue Per Visitor | -.5 Relative Difference | 53.8% Significance | Avg Gift $62.56

Overall, the results of this experiment indicate that simplifying the ask amount in email communications can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates, but further testing and larger sample size is needed for validity.

experiments.title: How an Anti-Paywall Pop-up effects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/22/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a radio station based in North Carolina, aimed to explore the impact of introducing an anti-paywall pop-up with a value proposition on the homepage for incoming website visitors. The primary objective was to ascertain whether this could effectively stimulate increased donation rates. We sought to engage visitors immediately upon entry to the website, potentially prompting greater support for  WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the introduction of an anti-paywall pop-up on the WUNC homepage resulted in a significant decrease in revenue for all traffic, indicating that the treatment did not effectively inspire visitors to donate. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results are not valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of introducing pop-ups or other barriers to access on user behavior and website performance. Further testing with larger sample sizes and more robust data collection methods may be necessary to draw accurate conclusions about the effectiveness of such interventions.

experiments.title: Will a plain text email achieve a higher click rate vs a HTML style email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a plain text email instead of a heavily templatized email for HUC sends will achieve a higher level of engagement because the email will feel like it came from a person instead of a mass marketing email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending a plain text email instead of a heavily templatized email for house and HUC sends resulted in a decrease in clicks for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further investigate the preferences of the target audience to understand what type of email format resonates best with them. This can help in creating more personalized and engaging communication strategies in the future.

experiments.title: How replacing a button in an email for a hyperlink achieved a higher click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a huc campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it looks more personal and give the illusion of more trust because it feels like a 1-1 email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a HUC campaign resulted in a 4.5% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.

- The treatment group showed a higher click rate, which could indicate that using raw hyperlinks in emails may appear more personal and trustworthy to recipients.

- However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.8%, which means the results are not considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to:

- Conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical confidence level.

- Consider testing other variables such as email copy, subject lines, or sender name along with the hyperlink vs. button format to understand the impact of each element on click rates.

- Continue experimenting with personalized elements in emails to see if this leads to higher engagement from recipients.

- Keep track of statistical significance to ensure that results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

experiments.title: How clarity around the problem and expanded value prop on main donate impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increase clarity around the problem and expanding value prop copy to specify how RTB is the solution for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because users will not only have better clarity around the problem RTB solves but also the impact a gift makes towards solving it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/26/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s main donation page, we believe there was an opportunity to leverage the value prop language we’d seen perform well on acquisition donation page and in campaigns. We hypothesized that adding more clarity around what the problem is and better illustrating how a gift to RTB helps solve it would improve conversion rate. A few specific callouts are the more action-oriented headline versus “Support Reasons to Believe. The inclusion of details about what types of questions RTB answers, whose writing the resources, and finally what’s really at stake when these questions go unanswered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing clarity around the problem and expanding the value proposition copy to specify how Reasons to Believe addresses the users' needs can significantly impact donor conversion rates and revenue generated. While donor conversion rate remained similar between control and treatment, the latter did show a substantial 103.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.5%, below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing variations of the value proposition copy and messaging to determine the most effective way to communicate the impact of donations to Reasons to Believe. Additionally, conducting experiments with larger sample sizes or running the test for a longer period may help increase the statistical confidence level of the results. Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that focusing on clearly articulating the problem and how RTB provides a solution can have a positive impact on donor conversions.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the titles of a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for previous members will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a membership renewal page where previous members of the organization can renew their membership either through a monthly gift or a one-time gift.
They wanted to test adding clarity to the titles of the donation form to see if it would increase the recurring conversion rate and one-time donor conversion rate.
The control monthly tab is labeled “Join Leaders Club” and the one-time tab is labeled “Single Gift.” For the treatment, we clarified on the monthly tab that they could “Make a monthly donation” and for the one-time tab they could “Make a one-time donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding clarity to the titles of the donation form tabs did not have an effect on donor conversion rates for one-time or monthly giving. We saw that clarifying the monthly tab to "Make a monthly donation" led to an 11.8% increase in recurring transactions but with only a 19% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 5.4% lift in one-time giving, but with only a 13% level of confidence.

This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the donation form titles to further optimize conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results. By continuously testing and refining different elements of the donation process, the organization can work towards maximizing donor conversion rates and ultimately increasing support for its cause.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because   they will be presented with the opportunity to know what impact a monthly gift can have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page on their website where people can join the organization as a member by making a one-time or recurring donation, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through new memberships.
On the donation page, users have the option to make a one-time or monthly gift, but if the user chooses one-time and makes a gift under $100, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Make my gift” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what impact a monthly gift will have.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift had no effect on conversion rates for monthly or one-time giving. The treatment group saw a 32% decrease in Transactions - Monthly, but this decrease is only based on 5 recurring transactions and only had a 33% level of confidence. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.
Additionally, we saw a 15% lift in one-time giving but with only a 38% level of confidence.
The key takeaways are that the recurring interrupter will most likely not hurt one-time or monthly giving conversion rates, but we do not have enough data to know if it will produce a lift of any kind.
In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the donation interrupter with a larger sample size and over a longer period to determine its true impact on recurring donor conversion rates. Additionally, considering other factors such as user experience, messaging, and timing of the interrupter could provide valuable insights for optimizing donation processes and increasing donor engagement.

experiments.title: How does using ad set budget rather than campaign in Facebook impact conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by setting the budget at the ad set level for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because the budget is spend in a more controlled manner between ad sets.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2024 - 02/29/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV uses Facebook as a channel to retain donors, react to lapsed donors, and acquire new donors.
In an effort to increase Facebook performance we decided to test setting the budget at the campaign level vs the ad set level. Historically PTV has set the budget on the campaign level to allow Facebook optimization authority but we were curious if we could see better results with a more controlled approach with the ad set.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that setting the budget at the ad set level for Facebook resulted in a 3.7% increase in Purchases for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 25.1%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. This means that while the treatment version appeared to be more effective in terms of purchases, the results cannot be confidently attributed to the budget setting.
Additionally, the Cost per Click experienced a significant 105% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that setting the budget at the ad set level may have resulted in higher costs for each click, which is an important factor to consider when evaluating the overall effectiveness of the strategy.
