In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is sufficiently high to draw meaningful conclusions. It may also be beneficial to further investigate the impact of setting the budget at the ad set level on other key metrics, such as return on ad spend, to get a more comprehensive understanding of the strategy's effectiveness. Further testing with larger sample sizes or different variables may also help to validate the findings and inform future decision-making regarding budget allocation on Facebook.

experiments.title: How adding a headline impacts conversions pt2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headling for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because there is additional clarity in the offer. because there is additional clarity in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month, PTV promotes a different book with a gift of a generous amount.
For April, we decided to test adding a headline to the donation page. We have seen with other organizations that a headline can drive clarity and, therefore, drive results as it presents clear, brief, easy-to-read information.
In a previous experiment we saw a decrease in performance however we think that the headline was not appealing to the PTV audience. Therefore we are retesting this hypothesis but with a different headline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a headline to the donation page can potentially increase transactions, as evidenced by the 5.9% increase in donations for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 44.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid or reliable for making future decisions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the outcome, such as the appeal and relevance of the headline to the audience. Future experimentation should focus on refining the headline to better resonate with the PTV audience and conducting the test with a larger sample size to increase the statistical power of the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of headlines to determine which one is most effective in driving donations.

experiments.title: How more concise copy on a content offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the story from the donation page  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate  because we’ve streamlined the message to focus on The Navigators’ mission and purpose. Without the narrative, potential donors can swiftly grasp crucial information without distraction, reducing the risk of donor fatigue and ensuring a more prominent and compelling call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2024 - 05/06/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion on a content offer funnel, we decided to run a copy test on the donation page.
The control donation page features a story about the founder of The Navigators. While the story is relevant to the organization, we felt that it added unnecessary friction to the page because it was irrelevant to the offer.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the story from the donation page will increase the donor conversion rate because we’ve streamlined the message to focus on The Navigators’ mission and purpose. Without the narrative, potential donors can swiftly grasp crucial information without distraction, reducing the risk of donor fatigue and ensuring a more prominent and compelling call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the founder story from the donation page led to a 34.2% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control version with the story performed better. However, with a statistical confidence level of 85%, these results are not considered valid or reliable for decision-making.
While these results were not considered valid, they do suggest that The Navigators audience prefers receiving the backstory and the meaning of the organization even within content offer funnels which is opposite of what we initially hypothesized.
Due to the fact that these results were trending in a negative direction, we chose to end the test and keep the all traffic going to the control.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the impact of storytelling and personal connections in donation pages, as these elements can sometimes resonate strongly with potential donors and influence their decision to contribute. Further testing could explore how to incorporate storytelling effectively while still maintaining a clear and compelling call to action.

experiments.title: How does adding a dear reader on high traffic pages affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dear reader for high traffic pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the likelihood to donate when presented with with an opportunity after receiving content that is of high value is easier.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 04/10/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by adding an easy conversion opportunity right on the page.
We ran this experiment with Today in the Word, a daily devotional ministry. We only targeted the pages that contained the daily devotionals. These pages get a ton of traffic each day.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a donation opportunity directly on high traffic pages can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates.

2. The treatment group saw a significant 12.1% increase in transactions for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence was only 55.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for validity.

4. These results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

For future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 85% in order to draw reliable conclusions. It may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding donation opportunities on high traffic pages with a larger sample size or different variations to see if the results can be replicated and validated. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or any external events that may have impacted donor behavior.

experiments.title: Do prospects respond better to a content offer or direct ask?
experiments.researchQuestion: By leveraging the appeal of exclusive content related to the election series, we anticipate capturing the interest of a broader audience segment and ultimately converting them into donors through a more gradual and engaging approach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2024 - 05/03/2024
In collaboration with WUNC, a North Carolinian Radio Station, we initiated an experiment during their Spring Fund Drive to gauge the effectiveness of different approaches in soliciting donations. The core question revolved around whether a direct appeal or a more indirect approach through content offers would be more compelling to potential donors.
To execute this experiment, we implemented an “exit-intent” pop-up strategy. This feature activates when a visitor hovers their mouse over the left or right corner of the webpage, signaling an intent to leave the site. We devised two variations of the exit-intent pop-up to test our hypothesis.
The first variant presents a straightforward donation request, providing a direct link to the main donation page. Conversely, the second variant offers access to exclusive content related to the election series.
Drawing from our experiences with similar experiments in other nonprofit organizations, we’ve observed a consistent trend: prospects are often more receptive to content offers compared to direct donation appeals, even during high-urgency campaign seasons.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering exclusive content related to a topic of interest (in this case, the election series) can potentially be more effective in soliciting donations compared to a direct donation appeal. Although the treatment group saw a 2.8% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 22.6%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the level of statistical confidence when evaluating the results. While the initial findings are promising, they should not be used as a basis for definitive conclusions or decision-making. It would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the effectiveness of content offers in donation solicitation. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the timing of the campaign, the specificity of the content offered, and the audience demographics could provide further insights for optimizing donation strategies.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because asking for a smaller gift is appealing to the donor. . because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2024 - 05/07/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we decided to run a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. This test ran for a week and a half split 50/50 among traffic.
The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift.
Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the interrupter is converting some donors to monthly giving. Based on the results above, 11 people clicked "YES! Convert my gift to a $10 monthly gift." out of the 192 people who were shown the interrupter. This means that the interrupter converted 5.73% of people who saw it.
While we can't validate these results, it's clear that it is effective for at least 5% of individuals who see it. We also saw a 7.8% lift in overall recurring donations at a 35% LOC.
In future experimentation, we plan to extend the threshold of individuals who qualify to see the interrupter. Since the current cutoff is $100, we plan to extend this up to $500 with tiered levels of recurring asks accordingly and aim to get a valid lift in recurring donations.
Overall, it is encouraging to see the interrupter have a positive impact on gifts, and we hope to continually optimize this experience through testing.

experiments.title: How using shorter, more conversational language on a content offer instant donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter and more conversational language on an instant offer donation page will increase donor conversion by removing friction, particularly for mobile audiences.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2024 - 04/04/2024
Focus on the Family uses dozens of free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For the last year, their offer instant donation pages have all used the same template sharing “5 things you should know.” Reviewing this design, their team felt it could have been more conversational and shorter, especially on mobile devices.
We created a shorter, “3 things you should know” template used VWO to split donation page traffic for their popular Kingdom Mindset offer. The test ran a full month.
Our hope was to at least find no negative impacts from the treatment as it was preferred by Focus’ team and ours. Ideally, we want to see if we could validate the increase.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During the first three weeks, we experienced a significant increase in donor conversion, hovering around an 80% or greater confidence level. However, in the final week of the test, that confidence diminished.
When we examined the results, we saw that every visitor segment and device category preferred the new version to the control, but again, at a lower than 95% Confidence level. Mobile audiences saw the largest increase in donor conversion at 25% over control but there were only 5 donations in the mobile segment.
The Focus team decided to continue testing the new, shorter version and leave behind the longer one since they did not experience a negative result during the test and overall preferred the way the new template looked on mobile devices. We will continue to test and refine this new template.

experiments.title: How Changing the Headlines on GOAA/SOAA Pages to be focused on the value of the content vs the organization impacted donor transaction rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headlines to be more value focused for the GOAA/SOAA pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because traffic coming to these pages are more focused on receiving the premium being offered rather than the organizational value prop.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2024
Focus on the Family have GOAA/SOAA (gift of any amount, sustainer of any amount) pages where you can make a donation of any amount and receive the premium that is being offered. These pages are often called out on their broadcast for specific days. Usually the headline is focused around the organization. However, we wondered if we could change the focus of the headline to be focused on the value that they would be receiving.
Because these pages only draw traffic for a day or two we had to string multiple days together to be able to test. Each day offers a different premium, therefore no two days had the same headlines.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the headlines on the GOAA/SOAA donation pages to be more value-focused did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the value-focused headlines, saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.1%.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including gift handles with corresponding impact descriptions in a fundraising email may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease of 15.1% in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and elements in fundraising emails to see what resonates best with donors. This could include testing different gift handle amounts, impact descriptions, or even the placement of this information within the email. It's also important to ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to not solely rely on the results of one experiment, especially if the statistical confidence level is below the recommended threshold. By continuing to test and iterate on different strategies, we can gather more reliable data to inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Give Monthly Option to the Site Navigation Help with Monthly Donor Conversion During Calendar Year End?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a give monthly button to the main navigation on the website for people who land on the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/06/2023
For Giving Tuesday we wanted to test having monthly ask set up as a mini campaign within CYE.
To make it easier to make a monthly gift we wanted to test adding a monthly giving button in the main navigation next to the original give button.
experiments.keyLearnings: In conclusion for this experiment, adding a give monthly button decreased donor conversion by 34% with a 78% LOC. This could be due to several reasons. The button copy itself could use further testing between give and donate. Another possibility is people saw the monthly and that was too big of a commitment so instead of giving monthly, they gave a one-time gift.

experiments.title: How simplifying a landing page impacted donation conversion rates for a premium offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the calls to action on a landing page for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2024 - 03/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Most of the direct ask fundraising for this organization is done through premium offers; during March for Eater, PTV launched a brand-new coffee-table style book called Power of the Cross.
This book was promoted across all channels-mail, TV, radio, and digital. However, the landing page for the offer had a high level of friction, with multiple calls to action. As such, we wanted to test a simplified version of the landing page and see if impacted donation conversion rates at all.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the calls to action on a landing page for potential donors can lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment group, which experienced the simplified calls to action, saw a 5.8% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 61.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing different variations of the landing page while ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. By conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes, we can gather more reliable data to inform decisions about optimization strategies for donation conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will Adding Personalization to a Donation Page Headline Increase Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding someone’s first name on the instant donate redirect for those that signed up for Romans in Action will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will receive a personalized giving page upon submitting their first name for the resource.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2024 - 04/18/2024
The Next After Team was looking to find opportunities that helped donor conversion on KCBI’s acquisition offers — particularly on our higher-performing offers at the time of the design of experiments we produced. We decided to run this test on an eBook Bible study called Romans in Action. The sign-up rate for the resource was healthy but we saw there was an opportunity to grow donor conversion on the instant donate landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding someone's first name on the instant donate redirect for those that signed up for Romans in Action had a positive impact on donor conversion, resulting in a 11.8% increase in completed transactions for all traffic. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being 86.7%, these results cannot be considered valid.

We are going to take this test and try it on newer KCBI content offer that has more traffic and will most likely see a valid result. We're viewing this test as a clue to continue testing on an additional test idea that may result in a "new normal" for KCBI's acquisition offers.

experiments.title: Will Adding 5 Things You Should Know to the 6 Steps to Share Your Faith Giving Page Increase Donor Conversion Because the Case for Support is Easier to Understand?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that arranging the case for support language in 5 easy points for people who signed up to receive 6 Steps to Share Your Faith will achieve increased completed transactions because the copy on the page is arranged in an easy to follow list and conveys 5 important points about the eBook they are about to enjoy and receive value from.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2024 - 04/03/2024
KCBI’s donor conversion on it’s acquisition pages could see some improvement across all it’s offers. The team wanted to know — as seen with other clients — will arranging the case for support language on the giving page in 5 easy points to understand help convert more eBook sign-ups to become donors?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that arranging the case for support language in 5 easy points on the giving page for 6 Steps to Share Your Faith led to a 33.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 57.6%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%.

While the results suggest that the treatment had a positive impact on donor conversion, it is not statistically significant. We're going to retest this treatment on an offer that has higher traffic to see if we can get a valide result.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other methods of arranging the case for support language or testing different variations to see which one resonates best with potential donors. Regular experimentation and testing can help KCBI identify effective strategies for improving donor conversion on its acquisition pages.

experiments.title: How using a year-end specific sticky bar on acquisition pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates because the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines.. because  the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2023 - 01/01/2024
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
During the crucial year-end fundraising season, we wanted to see whether we could impact conversion rates on content offer instant donation pages by using a year-end specific sticky bar.
We ran this on all content offer instant donation pages during the last 10 days of the year, as that is when Hoover sees the most revenue come in online in December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders did not achieve the desired change in conversion rates. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions by 12.2% compared to the control group.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 79.8%, which is below the generally accepted minimum threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
While transaction conversion rates were lower for the treatment group, when looking at the impact on revenue, we see that the treatment version had a 104% higher average gift. Given the time of year this experiment was run, this is most likely due to a larger gift skewing results, but it is still interesting that the sticky bar with year-end messaging motivated that donor to make that large gift.

experiments.title: How including a gift array impacted clicks on a direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift array options for nondonor email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because there is greater clarity around the giving amounts and easier entry point to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
During a fall recurring campaign, we wondered if introducing the gift array options for monthly donors would drive additional clicks on an appeal by introducing the decision at an earlier stage in the funnel. We split the non-donor file 50/50 and included a clickable gift array for the treatment group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The inclusion of gift array options for non-donor email recipients did not achieve the expected increase in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 44.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. For future testing, we will consider testing different variations of the gift array options, such as the layout, wording, or placement within the email, to see if any of these factors may impact the click-through rates.

experiments.title: How reinforcing a pledge agreement impacted conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the agreement to the pledge on the instant donation page will cause an increase in donor conversion rate because of its emphasis on personal agreement, and detailed actions to combat cancel culture. The explicit call for agreement and clear steps for support may enhance engagement, making the treatment more effective in motivating individuals to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2024 - 03/25/2024
When looking to improve conversion on an instant donation page behind a pledge, we wondered if including additional emphasis on personal agreement and detailed actions to combat cancel culture would lead to higher participation on the donation page. We developed a treatment that reminded visitors of their shared commitment in the same space as asking them to make a gift to combat the issue they stood against. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for six weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included reinforcing the agreement to the pledge on the instant donation page with detailed actions to combat cancel culture, did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group. We believe this may be due to additional friction because of the length of the copy.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 22.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.

It may be beneficial in future tests to consider revisiting the design and messaging of the treatment to see if any adjustments can be made to improve its effectiveness in driving donor conversions. Additionally, testing different variables or approaches in future experiments can help in understanding what resonates best with donors and ultimately leads to higher conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a dear reader to a daily devotional page impacted donation conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dear reader style ask for the daily devotion pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we are adding a ask to pages that have high traffic and could be motivated to give because the are already receiving valuable content. because  we are adding a ask to pages that have high traffic and could be motivated to give because the are already receiving valuable content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Today in the Word Daily Devotion Pages receive a ton of traffic daily. We wanted to see if we could get some of this traffic to convert into being a donor by presenting a ask at the end of the devotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By presenting an ask at the end of the content we were able to see a 364% lift in conversion at a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment was a test of nothing vs. something (adding an ask), further testing can be done to see if we can increase the donor conversion or average gift by presenting the ask differently.

experiments.title: Is there a difference in conversion when asking for $10 or $25 gift from nondonors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for donations for $25 or more for nondonor subscribers will achieve a higher average gift because the based level giving has been raised.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2024 - 02/23/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV primarily raises funds through donations made for books written by Dr. Robert Jeffress. For Easter this year, PTV decided to create a premium offer specific to Easter and to support the mission of PTV, spread the Good News of Jesus Christ to a lost and hurting people, confront an ungodly culture with God’s truth, and equip the saints to apply scripture to their everyday lives.
Last year, PTV tested into asking nondonors for a gift of $10 or more and saw great results.
With this offer, we decided to test a higher gift ask of $25
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the donation ask from $10 to $25 for nondonor subscribers resulted in a 0.7% increase in clicks for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 4.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

Donations and average gift
Control: 15 gifts, $21.33 average gift
 	Treatment: 14 gifts, $21.43 average gift

experiments.title: How adding autonomy to a “Wiki” banner impacts revenue V.1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a decision point to make an impact for people who land on the homepage during a HUC will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2024 - 03/13/2024
Would adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact increase donor conversion on the homepage campaign wiki banner? By adding a level of autonomy to the donor experience and giving them “control” to say “Yes, I’d like to make an impact” or “No, I’m not interested today,” would we invite more participation in the campaign and insight additional donor conversions over our “regular” campaign messaging with a gift array?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact on the homepage campaign wiki banner resulted in a 21.5% increase in revenue for all traffic. This suggests that giving donors the autonomy to choose whether they want to participate or not can positively impact donor conversion rates. Further investigation will need to be done in order to see if the increase in revenue is actually related to the experiment or if there is a large gift that needs to be discluded.

experiments.title: Adding a Callout Above the Monthly Gift Frequency Button on the Campaign Giving Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages will increase the donor conversion rate because of the social proof and added exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 03/26/2024
By adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages, will this element help add additional direction to those motivated to give and who will have already landed on the campaign giving page? By adding a donor countdown animation within the message, we hope to add an additional layer of “encouragement” to give during the giving process by showing donors can be part of the answer and help reach an impactful goal through their gift by “becoming one of” the donors needed. We believe adding a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button will insight more donors to get involved and make a monthly donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button showed a 9.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 23% level of confidence. This test overall was pretty flat. In the future, we would like to rerun this test on a campaign-giving page for a longer amount of time to see if we can reach validity.

experiments.title: Removing Express Checkout on the Sustainer Campaign Giving Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing express checkout for HUC donation pages will achieve a higher percentage of conversion because there will be less decision friction once a donor hits the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 03/12/2024
On previous tests for FOTF, we’ve seen varying degrees of success when testing the removal or decrease in size of the Express Checkout method of payment on FOTF-giving pages. Would removing the Express Checkout on the Sustainer campaign giving pages also yield the same results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Removing the Express Checkout for the Sustainer campaign giving pages resulted in a significant decrease in Transaction - Recurring by 60.1%.
2. There was a 61.2% statistical level of confidence in the result for Transaction - Recurring, indicating that the control group performed better.
3. The Transactions - One-Time experienced an 861% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and Transactions experienced an 840% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Therefore, it is not recommended to make decisions solely based on these results. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to reconsider the hypothesis and perhaps conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to draw more conclusive findings.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy a wiki banner affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding autonomy language to the Wikipedia CTA will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we are giving the choice to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/02/2024
By adding an opportunity for FOTF article readers to determine whether they wanted to participate in the Sustainer campaign – giving them an opportunity to say “Yes, I want to strengthen our nation’s families” or “No, I’m not interested today” — would help inspire more readers to interact with the campaign Wikipedia buttons and insight more donors to give during the sustainer campaign. By adding this language approach, will we see higher donor conversion with this campaign treatment (Wikipedia campaign ad) we’ve seen do well in the past?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding autonomy language to the Wikipedia CTA did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a 5.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 25.0%. However, since the confidence level is below the standard threshold of 85%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different approaches to see what resonates best with donors. It's possible that the autonomy language did not have the desired effect in this particular context, but that doesn't mean it won't be effective in other scenarios. By conducting further tests and analyzing the results with a higher level of statistical confidence, you can gather more reliable insights to inform future campaigns and strategies.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated tabbed form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donor conversion because there is additional clarity in the process by adding frequency choice before the gift array choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 03/18/2024
When sending pledge signers to an instant donation page, we wondered if emphasizing the monthly gift option by creating a tabbed form experience would increase recurring donor conversion. We split traffic between the two versions and ran for the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the animated tabbed form for the instant donation page resulted in a 50.3% increase in recurring donation for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 47.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As a result, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to determine the most effective ways to increase recurring donor conversion. It may be beneficial to explore other design and messaging variations in future experiments to see if they have a significant impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated", did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 9.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic with a 44.1% confidence level, signaling that the control version was more effective at encouraging action. The conversion rate was also lower for the treatment email.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.1% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider the messaging carefully and ensure that it aligns with the organization's goals and mission. While emphasizing that any amount is appreciated may seem like a positive way to encourage donations, it's crucial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the audience.

2. Continue to experiment with different messaging, calls to action, and incentives to understand what drives donor behavior. By testing various approaches, we can gather valuable insights to inform future campaigns and optimize results.

3. Increase the sample size or test over a longer period to improve the statistical confidence level of the results. This will help ensure that the findings are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different strategies and messaging to determine the most effective way to engage supporters and drive action. By learning from these results, we can continue to refine our approach and improve the effectiveness of our campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more non-members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated," did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 4.2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic and a slight increase in conversions with a 58.4% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

2. The hypothesis that letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate was not supported by the results of this experiment.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of wording and messaging on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations of language to determine the most effective way to communicate the value of donations to non-members. Additionally, it is important to ensure that experiments are designed to achieve a statistically significant level of confidence to obtain reliable results that can inform decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding a banner to a donation page with a button to accelerate the process impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to begin the donation for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be reduced friction present for visitors who are already motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 03/04/2024
For highly motivated traffic coming to a main donation page from the homepage donate button in the top right corner, we hypothesized that giving them a chance to skip over the copy on the donation form and go straight to the gift frequency and gift array selections without having to read the donation page would reduce friction in the process that we know they initiated by arriving on this page. We had tested into having shorter copy on this page to help get the form above the fold, but wondered how else we could escalate the process and reduce the work involved for visitors here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to begin the donation process for main donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 6.7% increase in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 72.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results may not be reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore ways to reduce friction for motivated visitors on the donation page. This could include streamlining the donation process, providing clearer calls to action, or optimizing the layout of the page to make it easier for visitors to navigate and complete their donation. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to improve donor conversion rates, while also ensuring that the results are statistically valid before implementing any changes permanently.

experiments.title: How explaining the value of the membership card to non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the open rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value non-members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can potentially increase the click-through rates for donation emails, as evidenced by the 0.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical significance level of 11.0% is below the standard threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate increased in the treatment email, the conversion rate had a slightly lower conversion rate.

Moving forward, it is important to continue exploring the impact of including value proposition reminders in donation appeals, perhaps refining the messaging or placement within the email to maximize effectiveness. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the findings and make more informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Pop-Up on a Main Donation Page Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly popup with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the popup reinforces the impact of a smaller amount than their original gift made on a consistent basis. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2024 - 01/12/2024
To increase monthly gifts on CaringBridge’s main donation page, we hypothesized a pop-up that presents the user with an opportunity to turn their original one-time amount into a lowered monthly gift would increase recurring donations. This pop-up would appear when the user submitted their one-time donation. We hypothesized that by making this conversion easy, suggesting a lower amount than what the user was originally going to donate, and reinforcing the impact of that smaller amount on a consistent basis it would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which involved presenting a monthly pop-up with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation, resulted in a 25.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level of 77.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Our hypothesis for why this popup not only didn't increase monthly donations but in fact decreased donations overall is due to the way CaringBridge's form is preset. Monthly is already pre-selected as the default giving option, so to submit a one-time gift a user would have to intentionally click it, fill out the form, and press submit. Targeting that audience, who already has a specific motivation for making a one-time donation, with another popup likely made the experience more disruptive rather than presenting them with something they hadn't already considered.

In future testing, we will focus our efforts on that initial action — selecting one-time rather than monthly — at the top of the form so that we are meeting the donor at the point of consideration rather than at the end where they've already made a decision.

experiments.title: How reminding members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card did not have the intended impact on the click rate for the Members Card campaign. While the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, the statistical confidence level was only 22.3%, which is not sufficient to draw a valid conclusion.

Note: The treatment email also had a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the wording and placement of value proposition texts in emails. It is possible that the added text in the treatment group did not effectively communicate the value of the Members Card or may have distracted recipients from the main call to action.

To improve the effectiveness of future experiments, it may be helpful to test different variations of value propositions, incorporate feedback from focus groups or surveys, and ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other factors such as the timing of the email, the design elements, and the target audience can also impact the success of the campaign.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members did not increase the click-through rate. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: The conversation rate of the treatment email also experienced a slight decrease.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the matching gift offer within the email to determine the most effective position for maximizing click-through rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the messaging around the matching gift offer or the design of the email could also be valuable in optimizing fundraising efforts for lapsed members.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer earlier in a fundraising email did not increase the click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in clicks compared to the control. However, the statistical confidence level of 23.9% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate decreased in the treatment email, the conversion rate increased slightly.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the placement of incentives like matching gift offers in fundraising emails carefully. While the hypothesis in this experiment did not hold true, it does not mean that emphasizing incentives early in emails is ineffective across the board. It may be worth testing different messaging strategies, placements, or types of incentives to see what resonates most with members and drives higher engagement. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in results before concluding is crucial for making informed decisions based on experimentation.

experiments.title: How donor first party data through AVID Pathways impacted Facebook match rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that AVID Pathways connection  for AVID clients will achieve a higher match rate in Facebook because AVID has access to multiple sources of data. because  AVID has access to multiple sources of data.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
With the introduction of AVID Pathways, we decided to test the Facebook match rate of contacts uploaded via CSV versus that of a continuous AVID Pathways connection.
For the control of this experiment, we exported donors from the organization’s CRM and uploaded the list to Facebook as an audience. The data points provided included first name, last name, email address, phone number, city, state, and zip code.
For the treatment of this experiment, we created a segment of donors in AVID Pathways and then pushed that segment into Facebook as an audience. The AVID Pathways connection to Facebook is a continuous flow of information from the organization’s platforms, such as CRM, email, and donation platforms combined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the AVID Pathways connection for AVID clients showed a slight increase in match rate compared to the traditional CSV upload method. While this result suggests that there may be potential benefits to utilizing AVID Pathways for connecting with donors on Facebook, the statistical level of confidence at 15.9% is not strong enough to draw conclusive findings.
For this experiment, we utilized 2 different segments of donors based on the giving cohort:
Offline only donorsOnline only donors
For each segment, we saw the following results.
For offline donors, we saw a 1.7% decrease in match rate with a level of confidence at 82%For only donors, we saw a 1.2% decrease in match rate with a level of confidence at 99%
While these segments did see very slight decreases, we are confident that the continuous flow of information provide via AVID Pathways from multiple data sources will make up the decrease.
As AVID Pathways data continues to improve we expect these match rates to increase over time.
We will check back in on rate in a few months to confirm if match rates have improved.

experiments.title: How addressing one specific problem in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: Addressing a specific problem/issue will increase the click-through rate because members have a clearer understanding of the problem they will help solve.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if describing one issue America faces instead of three issues would encourage members to click. The control mentioned three issues while the treatment focused on one. The issues were mentioned in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing a specific problem/issue in an email campaign can potentially increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment focusing on one issue saw a 13.5% increase in clicks compared to the control that mentioned three issues. This indicates that providing a clear and specific understanding of the problem members will help solve can be more impactful in encouraging action.

Note: The donation conversion rate for the treatment email also experienced a slight increase.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is 57.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of addressing specific issues versus multiple issues in email campaigns. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations, we can gather more conclusive data on whether this approach effectively increases engagement and donations. Additionally, it may be helpful to explore other factors such as the framing of the issue, the language used, or the placement within the email to further optimize campaign effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email. The treatment mentioned the match in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.
Note: The treatment email also had a higher conversion rate.
However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 70.1%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email . The treatment mentioned in in the body of the email and in the call to action.

Overall, the results of this experiment should be interpreted with caution, and further testing is recommended to determine the most effective approaches for future email campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email . The treatment mentioned in in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.

Note: While the treatment email has a higher click-through rate it also had a slight decrease in the conversions.

However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 56.5%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How Autonomy Language in a website appeal popup impacts clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language in a donor appeal popup reinforcing a donor’s autonomy will increase clicks by empowering them to make an informed choice
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2024 - 03/25/2024
Buckner International had a popup on the website for visitors promoting an opportunity to give $15 to give shoes to a waiting child. We hypothesized that using language that granted the reader greater autonomy to make a choice would increase clicks on the popup by empowering them to make an informed choice.
For the Control popup, we led with a statement that the reader should know something urgent. In the treatment, we asked if we could tell them about something urgent. The button language reinforced their autonomy by saying, “Yes, I want to give shoes to a waiting child.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, there wasn't enough data in the experiment to validate the results. The popup was setup to only fire every two visits. Due to the traffic volume of Buckner's blog, that didn't provide enough data to validate the treatment.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked lapsed members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage lapsed members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the inclusion of text about being in a community of like-minded individuals did not have a significant impact on click-through rates for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control, with a statistical confidence level below the threshold for validity.
Note: The donation conversion rate experienced a slight increase in the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider alternative hypotheses and strategies for increasing engagement among lapsed members. This could involve testing different messaging approaches, incentives, or targeting tactics to better understand what resonates with supporters and drives them to take action. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are designed with a sufficient sample size and statistical power to draw meaningful conclusions.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of text promoting a sense of unity among supporters did not lead to an increase in click-through rates for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment group experienced a 12.1% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 70.6%.
Note: The donation conversion rate also decreased in the treatment email.
These results should not be used for future decision-making as the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%. However, it is important to note that the hypothesis about creating a sense of unity was not supported by this experiment.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different messaging strategies to understand what resonates best with supporters. It may be helpful to explore other psychological principles of persuasion and to consider the specific values and motivations of The Heritage Foundation's audience when crafting messaging for campaigns. Additionally, conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes and incorporating feedback from supporters could provide more reliable insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked non-members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage non-members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating messaging about unity and being part of a community of like-minded individuals can have a positive impact on engagement, as evidenced by the 2.7% increase in click-through rates for the treatment group. While the results were not statistically significant at the 85% confidence level, there is still an indication that this approach resonated with supporters.
Note: The donation conversion rate decreased by 5.9% with a 31% level of confidence in the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the concept of unity and community in messaging to see if it consistently leads to increased engagement. Additionally, conducting larger sample-size experiments could help increase the statistical significance of the results and provide more confidence in the findings. Overall, the principle of creating a sense of unity and belonging among supporters appears to be a promising strategy to continue testing and incorporating into future campaigns for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How 1 call-to-action vs 2 calls-to-action in an email impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of CTA for an email will achieve a higher click rate because the opportunity to click has grown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of calls-to-action (CTA) in an email can potentially lead to a higher click rate. In this case, the treatment with two CTAs resulted in an 18.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group with only one CTA. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.5%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. It may also be beneficial to explore the impact of different types of CTAs (such as buttons vs. hyperlinks) and the effect of using HTML vs. plain text emails. By continuing to experiment and test different variables, we can gather more reliable data to make informed decisions about optimizing email performance for Moody Radio.

experiments.title: How using continuity in the headline of a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a continuity headline for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will provide more clarity on the next steps for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2024 - 03/14/2024
The Navigators ran an acquisition offer this spring offering a set of Easter cards. In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate at the end of the funnel, we wanted to text additional continuity between the landing page to the donation page. The idea behind the test is that our donation pages may be perceived as confirmation pages when a visitor is working their way through a funnel. By adding a “continuation headline” we are able to better communicate the next steps to the visitor, therefore leading to the potential increase in donor conversion rate. We have seen success with other clients using this technique, so we wanted to see how The Navigators audience reacted to it. Our hypothesis was that the additional continuity would increase donor conversion rates.
Control:
Headline: Help us bring the disciple-makers to The Great Commission
Treatment:
Headline: Your Easter cards will be sent to you soon. Before you go, would you consider something?
Subheadline: Will you help us bring the disciple-makers to The Great Commission?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a continutation headline for potential donors can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this specific case, the treatment group saw a 14.0% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence of 44.5% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results not statistically significant and not suitable for future decision-making.
However, due to the slight increase, it would be important to retest this concept for The Navigators. It is important to note that we were unable to let this test continue to run and potentially validate due to a lack of inventory of the offer. Therefore, we may be able to get valid results on a test that can run for a longer period or on a more evergreen offer like an ebook.
While transactions did not validate for this test, it is encouraging to see a relative lift that may indicate their audience responds well to a continuation headline.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired actin is clearer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the call to action from a question to a statement did not lead to a higher click-through rate among members. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks and the number of conversions, although the results were not statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is important to note that small changes in wording or formatting may not always have the desired impact on member engagement. It is essential to conduct thorough testing with a significant sample size and statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence member behavior, such as the timing of the email, the content of the message, or the overall design of the communication.

Overall, the findings from this experiment should not be used to make decisions about future email campaigns, as the results do not provide a clear direction. Instead, it would be advisable to continue testing different strategies and elements to determine the most effective approach for encouraging members to take action.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for non-members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing non-members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask non-members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage non-members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the use of a statement as a call to action for non-members resulted in a slight increase in clicks compared to using a question. While the results showed a 0.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, the statistical confidence level was only 6.1%, which is not significant enough to make a valid conclusion.

The number of conversions for both emails was the same.
