In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to determine if using a statement call to action consistently leads to higher engagement from non-members. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other variations in the call to action to see what resonates best with the target audience. Overall, it is crucial to rely on statistically significant results before implementing any changes based on an experiment.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the benefits of a premium in a fundraising appeal may not necessarily lead to an increase in donor conversions. In this case, the treatment that focused on the benefits of receiving a book as a premium for becoming a monthly donor actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control, which highlighted the impact of the monthly gift on the organization. The treatment saw a drop of 44.1% in transactions at a 67.7% level of confidence, which is below the validity threshold.

This copy test saw a valid increase in clicks, but an invalid drop in transactions. Therefore, indicating that the copy was persuasive in getting potential donors to click the link, but their audience was less motivated to give.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different messaging strategies to understand what truly motivates the audience to give. This could involve testing different types of premiums, highlighting various impacts of donations, or even testing different audience segments with tailored messaging. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can gain a deeper understanding of our audience and optimize our fundraising strategies for better results.

experiments.title: How adding a side column ad in TDS articles affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a digital content offer in the sidebar of TDS article pages for TDS readers will achieve an increase in transactions and emails acquired because they are receiving relevant content that brings more value to their visit.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2024 - 03/05/2024
We added an ad for our Founders Almanac eBook offer in the side column of TDS articles. Our hypothesis was that adding an additional digital offer alongside our Guide to the Constitution Dear Reader ads would boost conversions for email sign ups and donations. There was a lift in email sign-ups but a non-validated decrease in transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The addition of a digital content offer in the sidebar of TDS article pages led to a decrease in revenue for all traffic by 18.4%, which was not statistically significant at a 52.1% confidence level. This means that there was not enough evidence to conclude that the digital offer had a negative impact on revenue.

2. However, there was a significant increase in Almanac Button Clicks by 1% and Submit Founders' Almanac Form submissions by 5441%. These results suggest that TDS readers were engaging more with the digital content offer.

3. The results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making due to the low level of statistical confidence (below 85%).

In future experimentation, it is important to conduct tests with a larger sample size and for a longer duration to ensure the results are statistically valid. Additionally, it would be beneficial to analyze user behavior and feedback to understand the impact of digital content offers on user experience and engagement. It is also crucial to continue testing different types of offers and placements to determine the most effective strategy for increasing email sign-ups and donations without negatively impacting revenue.

experiments.title: How adding an image of a thank-you gift impacted donations during a one-day fund drive.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/08/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day. This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding an image of the gift on the donation page made a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors resulted in a 13.6% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 33.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and improve the reliability of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments conducted.

experiments.title: How does receiving a premium compared to receiving personal recognition for their gift affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personal recognition for monthly partners will achieve an increase in clicks and donor conversion because donors would feel more valued by the organization and the personal touch of their name being added to the group.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test whether a premium or personal recognition for their recurring gift would be more effective.
The control focused on the same premium that had been used throughout the campaign, and the treatment focused on a more personal recognition for their monthly gift. The treatment also promised to send the monthly partner a picture of their name on the window with all of the other monthly partners.
We hypothesized that the treatment would greatly outperform the control due to the personal touch and recognition of becoming a monthly partner because recognition is a motivating factor for the majority of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which focused on personal recognition for monthly partners, did not outperform the control, which offered a premium for recurring gifts. The treatment actually saw a drop of 3.7% in clicks at a 21.6% level of confidence. These results are inconclusive; however, we also wanted to look into if this test impact donations rather than clicks.

The control had a total of 13 donations, and the treatment had 8 donations. The results in terms of transactions show a drop of 36.1% in transactions at a 71.3% level of confidence. While transactions also did not validate, it indicates that The Navigators audience did not respond well to recognition for their gift.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of personal recognition versus premiums for recurring donors, ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other variables or elements that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging or incentives, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of what motivates donors to contribute.

experiments.title: How including an image of a premium in an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including an image of the premium for email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is greater clarity about the children’s book and its intended audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
When offering to send a book as a premium for gifts of a certain amount, we wondered if having an image of the book being offered would have any impact on email engagement by generating interest and curiosity about the contents of the book as well as creating congruency between the email and the donation page, which also had the image of the book displayed at the point of transaction. Since the author also writes books for adults, we wanted to provide clarity at the point of decision-making that this book would be best suited for young readers or any children in the visitors’ lives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including an image of the premium in email communications might have a positive impact on engagement and click-through rates. Although the treatment group did see a 7.1% increase in clicks, the statistical confidence level of 64.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of visuals, such as images of premiums, in email communications to gauge their impact on recipient engagement. However, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating other elements such as A/B testing different visuals or messaging strategies could provide further insights into what resonates best with the audience and drives the desired actions.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Donate Button in Header and Navigation Menu

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because  more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons. 50% of home page traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.
Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language impacts clicks on a website popup during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a website popup during the year-end campaign will increase clicks by making the appeal more timely.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For Buckner International’s Year-End campaign, we wanted to test the impact of urgency language on a popup we were running to drive website visitors to the year-end donation page. Both popups would feature an image of a mother and child in need and invite users to make a year-end gift. The difference would be in the urgency of the appeal. The control would ask if the reader could “support a family in need this season?” The treatment stated, “Time’s running out to support a family in crisis.”
We hypothesized that the added urgency of the treatment would increase engagement by making the appeal more timely. To ensure that people didn’t see the popup every visit, we set it up to show once every two visits.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, website traffic was far too low during the campaign to validate this experiment. We had only 174 sessions that saw the popups and a total of 4 clicks. While both had a healthy CTR, there wasn't enough traffic. Future tests will need to either fire every visit and/or target higher-traffic pages on the site to be able to validate.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition of a sticky bar impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition for the course sticky bar will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases the clarity of what someone will receive when they sign up for the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2024 - 02/13/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary regularly runs sticky bars on their voice.dts.edu website to advertise new courses, surveys, and more. They’ve previously tested into language that acknowledges them as a friend of DTS and use this language regularly.
For their new course, we wanted to test different language angles to see if we could get more people clicking into the course landing page.
For the first treatment, we wanted to test language about starting off the new year with a new course as it launched in January.
For the second treatment, we focused on the felt need that the course addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results showed that the treatment version resulted in a 3.1% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.7%, falling below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, while the treatment won, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

The key learning from this experiment is that changing the value proposition in the sticky bar can impact email conversions. However, to draw more concrete conclusions, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be necessary. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence to make reliable decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact click-through rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner  for the donor will achieve an increase in click through rate because  of the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.
The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.7%, meaning the results cannot be considered statistically valid.
However, the treatment greatly outperformed the control in terms of transactions. The treatment saw a 161.3% lift in transactions at a 99.6% level of confidence, meaning that the results are valid!
Therefore, the key learnings from this experiment are that The Navigators housefile is much more motivated to give when exclusivity and incentives are coupled with an appeal. While the results showed that readers were slightly more inclined to click the link when benefits to the donor were included, the individuals who did take the next step within the treatment, were much more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to navigation giving buttons impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons for the site navigation will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it increases the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 02/23/2024
Care Net tested into adding a “Give Monthly” button in their main navigation next to their main donate button. While this increased clarity, we wondered if we could increase the appeal of giving while adding even more clarity by adding additional copy below the main button text.
For the Donate button, we added the text, “Save a baby today.” For the Give Monthly button, we added the text, “Save babies 365 days a year.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons in the site navigation can potentially increase recurring donations by making the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis more prominent. The treatment, which added the text "Save a baby today" below the Donate button and "Save babies 365 days a year" below the Give Monthly button, resulted in a 67.0% increase in donations for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the treatment showed a promising outcome, it cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding additional copy below the main donation buttons to confirm if it consistently leads to increased donations. It is important to ensure that any findings have a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes based on the results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the copy or placement of the text could help optimize the impact on donation rates.

experiments.title: How having multiple calls-to-action impacts the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of ctas in an email for the mass communication emails will achieve a higher percentage of engagement because there will be more opportunities throughout the email to engage with the content.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2024
This is the last test in our design of experiments to make a mass marketing email feel like a personal 1-on-1 email.
We started testing with HTML vs. Plain text and then pivoted to how we present CTA’s within the email (button or raw URL). This final test will be seeing how many cta’s we should put in the email itself. Will the Audience respond better to only having one or is it more ideal to have two?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of CTAs in an email can lead to a higher percentage of engagement, as evidenced by a 6.9% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 20.4%, which is below the typically accepted threshold of 85%. This experiment will need to be retested with a plain text email.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How an actionable and enticing subject line impacts clicks

rows.name: Connection with God, Neighbors, and Community
three steps to deepen your relationships…

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more actionable subject line in a cultivation housefile email will achieve more clicks because it create more curiosity .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2024 - 02/21/2024
Luther Seminary sent an article in a cultivation email about three steps to deepen your connection with God, neighbors, and community. We tested using a more actionable subject like impacted clicks to see if it encouraged more people to click to read the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a more actionable subject line, did not perform better than the control in terms of Clicks for All Traffic. In fact, it experienced a 2.5% decrease in clicks.

3. The statistical level of confidence for the Clicks for All Traffic results was only 22.9%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experiments, it is important to consider the following:

1. While creating more actionable subject lines may increase open rates, it may not necessarily lead to more clicks. It is important to strike a balance between creating curiosity and providing clear information in the subject line.

2. It is crucial to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making any conclusions or decisions based on the outcome of the experiment.

3. Further experimentation and testing may be needed to fully understand the impact of different subject line strategies on email performance.

experiments.title: How adding a “Most Popular Gift” call out on the gift array impacts one-time donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a “Most Popular Gift” call out for donation page gift arrays will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is.. because  it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2024 - 02/23/2024
Luther Seminary has seen a steady amount of traffic on their donation page. Conversions have been consistent, but the average gift has been lower. We wanted to see if we could get more conversions and also increase the number of gifts because we increase clarity of what the most popular option is for most page visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Most Popular Gift" call out to the donation page gift arrays led to a 22.0% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 77.3% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and account for any potential fluctuations.
Overall, the concept of highlighting a "Most Popular Gift" option shows promise in potentially increasing donor conversions and gift amounts. It could be worth conducting further experiments with adjustments to ensure reliable and conclusive results that can guide decision-making for optimizing donation pages in the future.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition on a donation page impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the value propostion for Education CYE main donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page. because having shorter copy, and copy that only focuses in on the year end goals can prevent friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2023 - 12/11/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to test if shortening the value proposition on the Education Donation Page to focus on one area instead of being very broad, could increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: By shortening the value proposition to only be positioned for year end, we where able to increase the donor conversion rate by 46.2% at a 75% level of confidence. While the result is not valid, it gives us a good baseline to expand on this test.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a sticky bar impacted donation conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the sticky bar will achieve a higher conversion rate because the appeal of the copy will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding value prop to the sticky bar could increase motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition to the sticky bar can increase motivation to give and result in a higher conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown timer to a slideout during a one-day fund drive impacted conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer for fund drive donors will achieve a change in donation conversion rates because of the urgency associated with the offer..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/07/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day.
This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding a countdown timer to a slideout featuring the image of the thank you gift would have an impact on donation conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to a slideout featuring the image of the thank you gift (a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug) resulted in a 13.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to consider testing the effectiveness of countdown timers in a more controlled setting, or perhaps with a larger sample size, to determine whether they do have a significant impact on donation conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that the language used in the treatment version aligns with the branding and tone of WUNC to maintain consistency and authenticity in communication with donors.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How does a humanized sticky bar on a donation page impact conversion rate for a direct appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the continuity from the email to the donation page will increase the personal connection and credibility of the sender.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/11/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal, we ran a 50/50 split in traffic to the donation page, half of which would see the normal donation page, the other half would see a humanized sticky bar featuring the the email sender on the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that individuals who saw the humanized sticky bar of Scott’s face, the email sender, would be more inclined to give a gift because it creates a more personal experience and increases the credibility of the sender.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with the sticky bar actually saw a decrease in transactions of 14.9% at a 38.2% level of confidence, compared to the control group as well as a slight decrease in revenue at 0.13% with a 46.3% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to design experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the appeal, the messaging, or the overall design of the donation page. It is also important to note that because there was no CTA on this sticky bar our results may be invalid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing the impact of humanized elements in donation appeals, but also to explore different variables and combinations to determine what resonates best with donors. We could potentially try a different email sender to see if a different person from the organization resonates with The Navigators audience more effectively.

experiments.title: How calling out security reinforcement impacts clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out the security of a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because it will reduce the anxiety of making a gift by emphasizing that their personal information will remain confidential.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the security of a gift within the email would increase the click-through rate as well as the donor conversion rate. This ran as a 50/50 split test to their housefile to test the effectiveness of security language for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email resulted in a 9.2% drop in clicks at a 60.2% level of confidence as well as a 72.6% drop in donor conversion rate with an 80.2% level of confidence indicating that the control email was more effective. Since both of these metrics fall below the validation threshold of 95%, we can not conclude that The Navigators audience does not respond well to calling out the security of a gift, and it may be worth testing again. It is possible that calling out the security of the gift before the potential donor landed on the donation page caused additional confusion and friction. Furthermore, this campaign sent people to a recurring only donation form which could have played a role in the lack of validation of the experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be interesting to test the impact of a security callout on the donation page as it may convert more donors as well as test this concept on an appeal where one-time donations are also accepted.

experiments.title: How focusing on impact rather than dollar amount impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the buttons on the gift array for people who are wanting to give on the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is more appealing for people to see the difference that they will be making.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/15/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by allowing a prospective donor to choose the impact that they wanted to make rather than a dollar amount that they would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that allowing prospective donors to choose the impact they want to make instead of a specific dollar amount might lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had the new buttons on the gift array, saw an 11.2% increase in revenue compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 66.1%.

experiments.title: How adding a “Support Honest Journalism” Sticky Button to TDS during EOY
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Support Honest Journalism” sticky button for new visitors to The Daily Signal will achieve a lift in conversion rate and average gift because media consumers are hungry for trustworthy news and will support a media outlet who represents independent and reliable journalism.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
We tested three different sticky buttons on The Daily Signal during EOY 2023. Two button’s were direct solicitations and one was a content offer for our Guide to the Constitution. The first button asked the visitor to “Support Honest Journalism” and the other asked them to “Donate”. Part of the experiment was to determine whether a more defined CTA like supporting honest journalism would create a lift in revenue over a tried and true content offer and a regular donate button. The donate button and Guide to the Constitution buttons resulted in a lower conversion rate and average gift, but the support honest journalism button saw a significant lift of lift in avg. gift and a slight lift in conversion rate. Looking at subsegments we identified that the primary lift came from New Visitors to the site which had an 18.77% increase in conversion rate, and a 98% increase in avg. gift. Returning visitors for the journalism button saw a net -58% drop in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. A more defined call to action, such as "Support Honest Journalism," can result in a lift in both conversion rate and average gift.

2. The "Support Honest Journalism" button specifically resonated with new visitors to the site, leading to a significant increase in conversion rate and average gift.

3. However, returning visitors did not respond well to the "Support Honest Journalism" button, resulting in a net loss in revenue.

4. The overall increase in revenue for all traffic was substantial but did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the "Support Honest Journalism" button has potential as a strong call to action for new visitors. However, it is important to consider the impact on returning visitors, as the negative response from this segment had a significant impact on the overall revenue.

For future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore how to tailor the messaging and call to action for different visitor segments. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments with a larger sample size to ensure statistical significance and validate the results before making any impactful decisions.

experiments.title: How providing form validation impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding validation that a field is completed correctly for web donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is visual confirmation of correct information.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2024 - 02/12/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
We decided to test and see if by adding validation on each form field would generate a higher conversion rate due to the confidence of completing the form correctly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding validation to fields for web donors may potentially increase the conversion rate. While the treatment group did see a 0.1% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is adequate before drawing any conclusions. This can be done by increasing the sample size or running the experiment for a longer period of time. Additionally, it would be beneficial to evaluate other factors that may impact conversion rates, such as the user experience, website design, and donation process flow. By considering these factors and conducting experiments with sufficient statistical power, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights for improving conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: How does adding a more personalized introduction affect email revenue on Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized introduction with rhetorical questions framed from personal experience relating to the project or topic being discussed for our email housefile will achieve a lift in revenue and an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2023
This test was run to our email housefile on Giving Tuesday 11/28/23 and was our first morning email. We tested a personalized introduction that featured rhetorical questions framed from the donors perspective and text specifically calling out bureaucrats and unlected agencies vs. a control without those additions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results recorded here reflect cumulative results for members (168K emailed) and nonmembers (790K emailed). Below are results for subsegments.
Member: treatment created a 58% increase in revenue with a 62.3% level of confidence
 	Increased conversion rate by 13.7% with a 64.1% level of confidence
 	Nonmembers: treatment created a 8% increase in revenue with a 52.3% level of confidence
 	Increase conversion rate by 24.8% with a 58.1% level of confidence
 	LC: Treatment created a 8.2% increase in revenue with a 52.2% level of confidence
 	No statistical difference between conversion rates
 	Lapsed Members: Treatment crated a -61.1% decrease in revenue with a 71.8% level of confidence
 	Decreased conversion rate by -26% with a 33.6% level of confidence

experiments.title: Does mentioning secure in a direct appeal email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the word “secure” for subscribers will achieve a higher conversion and revenue impact because of the additional perceived security.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a different premium to its housefile in an effort to acquire new donors and retain existing donors.
For this email, we decided to test adding the word “secure” when asking the subscriber to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the word "secure" when asking subscribers to make their gift can potentially have a positive impact on conversion and revenue. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid, they do show a significant increase in revenue for the treatment group.

Revenue impact, treatment saw an increase of 63%. 

Donation impact, treatment saw a increase of 33%.

The treatment version also saw more larger gifts made from the link within the PS (7 gifts vs 3 gifts)

Based on these findings, it would be recommended to further test the use of the word "secure" in future experiments. This could involve testing it with different segments of the audience, using it in different parts of the email, or testing variations of the word itself.

By conducting further experiments and gathering more data, it will be possible to determine with more certainty whether adding the word "secure" consistently leads to increased conversion and revenue. This will help inform future email campaigns and potentially increase the effectiveness of fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How call to action on a Facebook ad during a drive impacts donor conversions

rows.name: NA WUNC Spring Drive 2023 Learn More
NA WUNC Spring Drive 2023 Donate Now

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by using the Donate Now call to action for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because the call to action is clear.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. During the Spring Drive, we decided to test a Learn More vs Donate Now call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment is that using a clear call to action like "Donate Now" led to a 16.2% increase in purchases for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.4%, which is below the recommended 85% level, and thus, the results cannot be considered valid. Additionally, the experiment revealed that using "Donate Now" led to a significant increase in Cost per Click, with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while this clear call to action may lead to more conversions, it may also lead to higher costs.

In the future, WUNC should conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and higher levels of statistical significance before making any definitive decisions about changing their call to action. They should also consider the tradeoff between higher conversion rates and increased costs. It may be helpful to explore other variations of the call to action, such as "Donate Today" or "Make a Donation," to see if they lead to similar conversion rates without significantly increasing costs. Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of using data and statistical significance to validate results before making any changes to marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How invitational language on a landing page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by having language that is more invitational for potential acquisition individuals will achieve a higher conversion rate because the copy is more invitational and things to do with family and friends .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2023 - 03/30/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio news and radio station.
WUNC is currently running an acquisition offer around Things To Do in North Carolina. In an effort to drive conversion rates, we decided to test a more invitational language approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by WUNC with the hypothesis that an invitational language approach would yield higher conversion rates for email acquisition did not produce a conclusive result as the treatment showed a 4.9% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.6%, below the recommended confidence level of 85%, hence, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Despite this, the experiment identified a significant increase in email acquisition from Desktop visitors, with a 193% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This result suggests that future experimentation should focus on optimizing user experience for Desktop visitors specifically, perhaps by creating a more tailored approach to their specific user requirements.

Overall, the key learning from this experiment is the importance of having a sufficiently high level of statistical confidence to make valid decisions based on the experiment’s results. Moreover, it is crucial to carefully consider the target audience to optimize any experiment, as seen with Desktop users in this experiment. Thus, future experiments with invitational language should be run with a larger audience and a better targeted approach to maximize the benefit of such an approach.

experiments.title: How a headline on a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headline for donation page viewers will achieve a higher conversion rate because their is clarity on how their gift will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2023 - 03/30/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio station. For their main donation page, we decided to test adding a headline to the page to drive clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the donation page of WUNC, a nonprofit North Carolina Public Radio station, aimed to test the impact of a headline on the donation page. The hypothesis was that adding a headline would clarify how the gift would be used and increase the conversion rate. The experiment ran for 42 days and showed that the treatment had a 4.0% increase in revenue for all traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 57.5%, it cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

However, there were some significant findings from the experiment that should be taken into account for future experiments. Tablet visitors saw a 5922% increase in revenue, with a 98% level of confidence. On the other hand, organic search visitors experienced a 570% decrease in revenue, with a 96% level of confidence.

The key learning from this experiment is that adding a headline to the donation page can have a positive impact on revenue. However, more research is needed to determine the specific language and message that resonates with different visitor segments. It is also important to note that statistical significance is crucial in making data-driven decisions and cannot be ignored. Future experiments should aim for a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by removing gift amount options from a gift array affects donation rates

rows.name: 6 ask array choices for RD and 1X

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of ask arrays for potential donors will achieve greater conversion rates because we are eliminating options to decrease indecision and reduce friction from the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
We tested this on the GAF desktop on both monthly v. one-time gifts to see if decreasing the ask arrays would eliminate indecision and increase donor conversion since we are giving fewer choices to potential donors. For RD we removed the $75 and $100. For 1X – Removed $150 and $300, added $250.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (+12) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).

• Recurring donor conversion and RPV experienced directional declines.

• Recommendation is not to change the desktop ask array values.

• This test was the second iteration of ask array reduction tests. The first, tested mobile and yielded a strong statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How an animated donate button impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with an animated donation button in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/19/2021
With the high volume of traffic to the Save the Children website, we wanted to discover new ways to increase traffic to the donation page from the donate button in the primary navigation. To do this, we hypothesized that drawing more attention to the button might increase the number of people who would see the button and ultimately click and give. We set up a treatment version of the donate button that was animated. The animation was subtle with a motion going across the button coming from a heart icon. We tested this button against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated donation button ran for three weeks. While the goal was to increase donor conversion from the animated donate button - which we weren't able to do with valid results, we did see a significant lift in clicks from it (11%). Essentially, more people clicked to the page but only a minimal amount more donated.

When analyzing the donor conversion, we saw a higher conversion rate from site visitors if they landed on the donation page and saw the animated button versus landing on the homepage.

We also saw the greatest increase in conversion from mobile users. This is most likely due to the button being much more visible on the small device.

After running this experiment and seeing that it was likely not going to validate, we came away with two questions:
Will the subtle animation be a novelty feature that will fade with time?
 	Can we present the subtle animation to people when they are at a point in their experience when they are more likely to convert? This question leads us to our next experiment for this feature where we will trigger the animated donate button at a higher motivation point in their conversion pathway so we can not only get more people to the donation page, but get more people to give.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts

The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.

Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for nonmembers affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for nonmembers to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how nonmembers would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized and gratitude-centered approach used in the treatment email did not result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email. In fact, the treatment email saw a decrease in donations.

Based on this, it is important to recognize that not all alternative approaches will yield better results. In this case, the treatment email might have been perceived differently by the recipients, leading to a decrease in donations. It is also worth noting that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results are not valid.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and higher statistical confidence. This will help ensure more accurate and reliable results. Additionally, it might be beneficial to explore other approaches and messaging strategies that resonate well with the target audience.

Overall, this experiment emphasizes the importance of testing different strategies, analyzing the results, and using the learnings to inform future experimentation. It highlights the need for ongoing testing and optimization to effectively engage and retain members.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How communal language on a pop up impacts donor conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using communal language for the campaign pop up will achieve an increase in donations because it increases the appeal of making a gift by emphasizing the reader’s role in the work being done.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2023 - 01/01/2024
Luther Seminary ran a campaign during the month of December. For this campaign, we wanted to see how the concept of communal language impacted overall donor conversion.
For this test, we created a control pop-up that clearly outlines the problem and the solution that Luther Seminary provides. For the treatment, we emphasized that the reader was an important member of the Luther Community, reminding them that their support is essential to the work that Luther is doing. Then it explains what the donor’s gift will do.
Both pop-ups led to the same donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using communal language in the campaign pop-up resulted in a slight increase in donations compared to the control pop-up. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it would be important to run the test for a longer duration to collect more data and increase the statistical significance. This will ensure that the results are reliable and can be used for making informed decisions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the communal language to find the most effective messaging that resonates with donors.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for members to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which focused solely on the impact of The Heritage Foundation, did not have a significant impact on conversion rates compared to the control email.

The treatment email saw a .35% decrease in clicks for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence is only 3.4%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Although there was no difference in clicks, we did see an 8.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 77.4% level of confidence. This tells us that using concise copy for the members-only segment does not motivate them as much to renew their membership when compared to other segments like lapsed donors and non-members.

experiments.title: How does calling out an incomplete membership with a suggested ask amount for non-members affect membership conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and giving them a suggested gift amount  for nonmembers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it adds clarity to the ask which can cause Potential members appreciate knowing what is expected of them and how much they are expected to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to calling out in the top of the email that their membership is incomplete and providing them with a suggested gift amount to activate it. The callout also included the deadline of January 31st.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and suggesting a gift amount for non-members can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment group showed a 12.8% increase in donations compared to the control group but only with a 60.6% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 21% decrease in clicks on the email with a 99% level of confidence.
Because the statistical level of confidence of 60.6% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, this means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to increase the sample size or conduct the experiment for a longer duration to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. This will provide more reliable results and help make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.
Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations or combinations of strategies in order to identify the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rate. This could include testing different types of messaging, callouts, suggested gift amounts, or deadlines.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment is that highlighting membership incompleteness and providing a suggested gift amount may be a promising strategy for increasing donor conversion rate. However, further testing is needed to validate these findings and make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for lapsed members affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership reactivation rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment email, which employed a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, did not result in a higher membership reactivation rate compared to the control email.

2. The treatment email saw a 7.5% decrease in donations for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 21.6%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

4. The hypothesis that the treatment email would foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection was not supported by the data.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the personalized and gratitude-centered approach used in the treatment email did not have the intended effect on lapsed members. However, since the results are not valid, it is not recommended to make any decisions based on this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar test with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. Additionally, conducting user research or surveying lapsed members to gather insights on their motivations and preferences could help inform future experiments and improve the effectiveness of reactivation campaigns. It is important to continue testing different approaches to find the most effective strategy for reconnecting with lapsed members and increasing reactivation rates.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page. They also wanted to know if adding the image to the email would affect donor conversion rate since users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image to the email did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results should not be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experiment design to increase the statistical power and ensure valid results. The Navigators should also consider testing other variables or elements of the email and landing page to identify potential factors that may influence the donor conversion rate. It is important to continue conducting experiments and collecting data to make informed decisions and optimize the effectiveness of email campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How preselecting the first amount on the gift array impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting the gift array amount for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because there is enhanced cognitive momentum on the page reducing friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2023 - 12/28/2023
For a high traffic donation page, we wanted to test if pre-selecting an amount for the visitor would encourage more visitors to complete the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pre-selecting the gift array amount on the donation page may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. The treatment group, which had the pre-selected amount, saw a 12.8% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 59.2%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To further understand the impact of pre-selecting the gift array amount, it is recommended to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help to increase the statistical power and confidence level of the results, providing more reliable insights.

Future experimentation should also consider other factors that could influence the conversion rate, such as the visibility and placement of the pre-selected amount, as well as the impact on different segments of visitors. By conducting rigorous experiments and analyzing the results with statistical validity, the organization can make informed decisions regarding the implementation of pre-selected amounts for donation pages.

experiments.title: How a headline with more clarity and impact bullet points affected donation page performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of value proposition and impact for visitors will achieve an increase in performance on the donation page because visitors would have a more tangible understanding of the impact of their donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/06/2023
In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate on our Holy Lands funnel for IMB, we decided to run a 3-way split test. We ran the control page vs. a new headline, vs the new headline + impact bullet points.
Variation 1:
New headline: Reach hurting people in the Holy Land with the transformative power of the gospel
Variation 2:
New headline: Reach hurting people in the Holy Land with the transformative power of the gospel
Impact bullet points:
$10, you’ll purchase a Bible for one person in their local language.
$30, you’ll pay for one person to receive one hour of trauma counseling.
$100, you’ll pay for a hosted training event in trauma healing leadership.
$200, you’ll pay for one month of media outreach.
We thought that the headline would offer additional clarity and value proposition as well as the impact bullet points which detailed. out exactly what each gift amount would result in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning of this experiment is that the new headline had a negative impact of 47.2% on the number of transactions while the new headline and impact bullets had a very minimal increase on the number of transactions.
While these results are not what we hypothesized, neither of the variations validated, so we can not rule out the effectiveness of the headline change or addition of the impact bullets.
We did notice that the impact bullets were based on these amounts $10, $30, $100, $200, but the gift array on this donation page had these amounts pre-selected: $100,$250,$500,$1,000. It would be interesting to run this test again focusing only on the impact bullets, but match up the impact bullet amounts with the gift array. We hypothesize that the stark difference between the options given and the impact bullets could have caused additional friction.
An important note: revenue is not pulling into Winston
However, VWO is reporting that Variation 1 raised $5,125 in revenue and Variation 2 raised $1,055. Based on these results, we did make the decision to roll out the new headline to all traffic.
It is crucial that we retest the use of a clear but impact based headline as well as impact bullets.

experiments.title: How positioning the donor as the hero impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clarity on the donation page for donors and positioning them as the hero will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because they will have a clear understanding as to what their gift will do and how they will make an impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/30/2023
During The Navigator’s Giving Tuesday fundraising campaign, we wan a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. This copy test aimed to frame the donor as the hero to see how it impact the donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that making the impact of donation clear in the donation page copy would resonate more with potential donors, therefore, influencing them to complete their donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity on the donation page and positioning the donor as the hero can potentially increase donor conversion rate. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 32.2%. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. This can be done by increasing the sample size or conducting more iterations of the experiment.
Overall, while the results of this experiment are inconclusive, it does provide some indication that providing clarity and positioning the donor as the hero may be effective strategies. These strategies can be further tested and refined in future experiments to determine their impact on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a 2-Button CTA that Addresses Different Motivators Impacts Site Creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a 2-button CTA for users on the CaringBridge homepage will achieve an increase in site creations because it better addresses both motivations for starting a site and ensures users who are creating a site for a loved one don’t feel excluded by the single CTA..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2023 - 12/29/2023
On CaringBridge’s homepage, a user has two distinct motivations for creating a site. Either for themself or for a loved one. We hypothesized that a single CTA “Create Site” didn’t properly address both audiences and believed that by speaking to both “Create a Site” and a second a button “Create a Site for a Loved One” would result in increased site conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The introduction of a second CTA button for creating a site for a loved one did not result in an increase in site creations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.8% in New Site Created for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence for this decrease is 48.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for valid results.

Based on these learnings, it can be concluded that the addition of a second CTA button did not have the intended effect of increasing site creations.

In future testing, it would be beneficial to track clicks more closely and see how this impacts engagement even despite the relatively flat results in site creations.

experiments.title: How clearly stating the problem and positioning the organization as the solution affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment with its emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasis on the threat to democracy, will lead to a higher donor conversion rate because it’s focusing on the problem and how Heritage is the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey through paid acquisition channels to get the public’s opinion on voter fraud.
