However, the statistical level of confidence is only 61.1%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for the results to be considered valid and reliable. Additionally, we did not meet the recommended 50 transaction minimum even after running this experiment for a little over a month.
Therefore, while there is some indication that making the donate button more visible could increase donations, further experimentation is needed to confirm these findings.
Given when this test was ran (during fiscal year end), it's also likely that the motivation to give was too high and this treatment made no difference.
It's recommended we retest this concept with a different approach. We want to highlight the donate call to action but may need additional value proposition language to give the site visitor more context as to why they should give.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button color on a homepage affected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the donate button color in the menu for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because people will have less friction finding where to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 07/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to test changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu for this prison ministry client. The control color of the donate button is part of the color palette that this brand has. Our idea was to find a color that is different and would make the word “Donate” a lot more obvious to someone looking at the site. If the donate button stands out more, we believed that it would increase the conversion rate since the donor can see the button quicker and have less friction finding the donation button.
For this experiment, the only difference is that the control page has the original color donate button and the treatment has a new color. We split this test 50/50 as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu resulted in a 29.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.9%, which is not validated since it is below the 95% standard level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test a more radical design difference for the donate button to identify the most effective option. This could include shape, size, button animations, and font. By adding a more drastic design difference to the donate button, this could draw more donors which could increase the conversion rate. A/B testing should continue to be conducted, comparing different design elements or variations to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How Lowered Gift Array on Main Donate Impacts Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array and displaying it as low to high for users on the general donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it lowers the “level of entry” for general users on the main donate page who might not be familiar with RTB..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s Main Donate page displays a high to low gift, with $500 being the first option and the suggested low amount being $50. We hypothesized that reversing this, lowering it, and suggesting an amount of $25 will increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Changing the gift array from high to low and suggesting a lower amount did not result in an increase in donor conversion. The treatment version of the experiment saw a 16.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that the control version performed better in terms of conversion.
However, the statistical level of confidence for the decrease in donations for all traffic is 68.0%, which is below the 85% threshold. This was not the expected result, since this performed well on a Premium donation page and was expected to perform just well if not better on a general page. This assumption was partly rooted in that typically general donation page might have more "new users" but in reality the traffic to this page shows that RTB still receives a lot of returning visitors.
More testing is needed in order to achieve a validated learning from this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/29/2023
This test is a follow-up test to the “How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion” test recorded earlier this year.
We wanted to retest this concept because this topic ended up being an integral part of their Fiscal Year End campaign.
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high-urgency campaigns. This year during their fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner resulted in a decrease in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid. But because this was at the end of Care Net's fiscal year, we ended up ending the experiment early and rolling out the control to ensure 100% of the traffic saw the better-performing variant.

Based on this, it is not recommended to use the gift array in the home page sticky banner for future campaigns. Instead, it would be worthwhile to explore other strategies or elements to improve donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on testing different approaches and measuring their impact on donor conversion to identify the most effective strategies for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown impacts donor conversion on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s Spring Recurring Campaign, they rely on a daily donor countdown in sticky bars and website calls to action to increase urgency and drive traffic to their donation page. We hypothesized that reinforcing this countdown on the page itself would add urgency and increase donor conversion.
The message would indicate how many new monthly donors were still needed before midnight that night.  When the number was reached, it would change to say, “We still need new monthly partners before 11:59pm to be here for you and other families in need.”
We split traffic evenly between both versions of the page and ran it for almost three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page did not increase donor conversion during the high urgency campaign. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in FamilyLife - Recurring Donations compared to the control group.
While we could not validate this decrease, it was a sharp enough decline in conversion that we ended the experiment early so we wouldn't harm donor conversion during the critical campaign. It is possible that the message was more of a distraction than provided additional urgency to the page.

experiments.title: How does personalizing copy for new names impact donor conversion rates during a match for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing the way someone came onto the file for new names will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/04/2023
Pathway to Victory (PTV) is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
Part of the overall strategy for PTV is to acquire new names onto their file, both from new donors as well as people that sign up for some of their acquisition offers. New names tend to have a very low instant conversion rate; for this match campaign, we wanted to test whether adding language in the body of the email that referenced how they came onto the file would impact conversion rates.
This test ran across 4 different emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that referencing the way someone came onto the file for new names may have an impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which included language referencing how they raised their hand to seek spiritual help, saw an 8.8% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of the results is only 27.8%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experiments, placement of the copy, as well referencing the name of the offer they signed up for, will be an interesting test.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the value proposition of a donation with an impact statement on the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving will increase donations by helping to overcome hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the impact of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving can potentially increase donations and help overcome hesitancy at the point of completing a transaction. Although the treatment showed a 19.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence of 72.3% is below the threshold of 85% needed for the results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving. The results of this experiment provide promising insights that can be further explored and tested with larger sample sizes to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, it is important to evaluate different variations of the statement to identify the most effective messaging to drive donations.

experiments.title: How updating value proposition on acquisition donation pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net’s mission will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
In the last few months, there have been some major social movements in the pro-life space, including the overturning of Roe v. Wade and the federal government announcing that they will be developing a Federal Reproductive Health Care Hotline. The hotline in particular is direct competition to one of Care Net’s ministries: the Pregnancy Decision Line. We wanted to see how updating the language to center around this particular issue would impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the value proposition to be more timely and relevant to Care Net's mission resulted in a decrease in donor conversion and revenue. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on these learnings, it is important for Care Net to conduct further experimentation to validate the impact of updating the value proposition. The organization should explore other strategies and hypotheses to understand the factors contributing to the decrease in donor conversion. This can involve testing different messaging, design elements, or targeting strategies.

Future experimentation should be conducted for a longer duration to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical significance.

experiments.title: How an animated lock on a donation page affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security on the donation page for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor. because  there is a perceived increase in security and reduces anxiety for the prospective donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge already had an existing image of a security lock on their tribute donation pages.  We wanted to reinforce the perception of security even more by drawing attention to this part of the giving form. We made the lock an “animated” lock where it opened/closed every few seconds and we additional copy to remind the prospective donor that they are making a secure donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4.9% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 71%.
Reinforcing the perception of security on the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. An animated lock and additional copy reminding the prospective donor about the secure donation process can help reduce anxiety and increase donations. It's likely that because the security lock already existed on the page, security was already sufficiently highlighted for the end user.

experiments.title: How a slideout sticky donation prompt impacts giving on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky donate prompt for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in giving because they are reminded to make a gift with accompanying value prop language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge had run a previous experiment on their resource pages that tested using a sticky donate button and prompting people to give when someone scrolls halfway through the page. We wanted to run the same test on the homepage to make sure it would not produce a negative impact on overall giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not result in a negative impact to giving - which was the goal of the experiment. The slideout resulted in a 3% increase in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 31% which is no impact in giving. When looking at the actual donations generated by this experiment we saw 48 gifts come in totaling in $2515 in revenue. These are gifts that would have likely not been captured without the experiment. It's recommended that this treatment is rolled out to the homepage.

experiments.title: How prompting people to give through a sticky donate button affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
CaringBridge wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The first treatment would only slide out when people would click on it while the second treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version of the sticky donate button outperformed the control version, resulting in a 5.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85% (LoC 24%), indicating that the results may not be valid and should not be used for future decision making. The second treatment resulted in a 1% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6%. Overall, it seems as if there is no impact but when seeing how many gifts were actually generated through each treatment, we saw 2 transactions generated by treatment 1 and 10 transactions generated by treatment 2. This shows us that treatment 2 is actually more successful in increase donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How does adding a donor statement for lapsed donors impact donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor statement for lapsed donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because they will not have to read the entire email to see the last time they made a gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 06/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
For this June match campaign, we wanted to test whether including a donor ‘statement’ at the top of the email, versus an in-line last gift date, would make an impact on lapsed donor recapture. This was tested across all 5 emails in this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a donor statement at the top of the email might have a positive impact on lapsed donor recapture. The treatment group, which received emails with a donor statement, saw a 6.5% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 30.8%, which is below the desired threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid enough to make future decisions based solely on them.

We also received some feedback from potential donors on the statement, which considering the low level of confidence, means we need to refine and re-do this test for future campaigns.

Additionally, it could be beneficial to further refine the donor statement and test other variables, such as the wording or placement of the statement, to optimize its effectiveness.

Overall, this experiment provides some indication that including a donor statement may be a promising approach for lapsed donor recapture, but further research and testing is needed to confirm its effectiveness.

experiments.title: How does referencing a recurring donor’s status in email copy impact additional giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing a donors’ status for recurring donors will achieve a change in giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/01/2023
Pathway to Victory is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
For this June match campaign, we wanted to see whether we could get additional one-time gifts from Pathway Partners–who are recurring donors–by referencing their status in email copy and writing to them accordingly.
This test ran over 4 separate email sends.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Referencing a donor's status as a recurring donor in email copy has the potential to increase one-time donations.
2. The treatment group, which received emails referencing their status, saw a 20.0% increase in donations compared to the control group.
3. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 23.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for valid results.
Given the very small donation sample size, this experiment requires further refinement and re-testing during future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a campaign donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for users who are familiar with this organization will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because Jake is a familiar face to these subscribers which will increase the humanization of the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/01/2023
This ministry client was running a campaign for their fiscal year-end. We’ve seen humanized sticky bars increase donor conversion for other organizations when used for high-urgency campaigns, so we decided to test this on their main campaign donation page which received traffic from their housefile list and Facebook ads.
The sticky bar included a message from Jake Hennes, someone who the file is very familiar with and who they receive cultivation emails from often. We wanted the urgent, important message to come from someone they recognize. Jake is also the one who sent a majority of the campaign emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a humanized sticky bar featuring a familiar face can significantly impact donor conversion rates, especially for users who are already familiar with the organization.

2. The treatment resulted in a 25.6% increase in donations for all traffic, suggesting that the humanization of the donation ask through the use of a familiar face resonates positively with donors.

3. Female visitors, in particular, showed a significant increase in donations, with a whopping 956% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This suggests that the humanized sticky bar was especially effective in engaging and persuading female donors.

4. While the results of the experiment showed promising improvements in donor conversion rates, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, but due to the volume of gifts and difference in donor conversion, we believe this concept should be used in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a ebook acquisition page affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for online course enrollees will achieve a higher email sign up rate because the social proof shows the user that there are others who have downloaded the ebook and it offers them a chance to join a group of others with similar interests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client is offering a free online course on the book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, so we decided to test adding the concept of social proof. We added social proof by adding a floating white box that says “We’ve already had over 3,000 people sign up for this online course! Will you join the group of people who are expanding their knowledge of the Book of Psalms?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding social proof for the online course resulted in a 10.1% increase in email acquisitions with a 75.1% level of confidence. This indicates that social proof has a positive impact on encouraging users to sign up.

2. When analyzing the results based on gender, it was found that female visitors had a significant 681% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the addition of social proof had a strong and positive impact specifically on female visitors.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the impact of social proof on overall email sign-up rates.

experiments.title: How adding goal language to a sticky bar impacts donors conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more specificity in a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more urgency is communicated by the goal language .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
RTB was running a fiscal year end campaign and implemented a site-wide sticky bar to generate more gifts. In order to increase donor conversion, we added more specificity and urgency to the sticky bar by adding the goal amount and a time to the deadline. The hypothesis is that the urgency language and specific dollar amount will drive more motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more specificity and urgency to a sticky bar did not achieve the desired result of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version actually saw a decrease in donations. We saw a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below 85%, which means the results are not considered valid.
However, based on the data collected, it may be worth exploring other factors that could have influenced the decrease in donations. It is possible that the addition of the specific dollar amount and deadline in the treatment version may not capture new donors who need more context for the campaign. In fact, the control brought in 6 new donors while the treatment only brought in 2 new donors.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of the sticky bar, focusing on other value propositions for the campaign.

experiments.title: How adding urgency in a pop up during fiscal year end impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding language that communicates urgency for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the time-bound offer will drive donors to give now as opposed to later .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
During RTB’s high urgency campaign they wanted to drive urgency on the site by implementing a pop up to give during the last few days of the campaign. In order to drive the urgency we developed a treatment that used urgency language to drive fast action among prospective donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing language that communicates urgency did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate for the nonprofit's high urgency campaign. On the contrary, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations. There was a 37% decrease in donor conversion but only with a level of confidence of 59%.
Because this only ran for a few days and only generated 13 donations, it's recommended that this retested in a longer time frame.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2023 - 06/26/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings:
"Start a Page" resulted in a 27% decrease in site creation rate with a level of confidence of 75% while the "Start a Journal" treatment resulted in a 14% increase in site creation with a 40% statistical level of confidence, indicating a positive impact.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%.
Surprisingly, we did see a softness in response when we used "page." It's likely that some of the response is offer-specific but we did see a more positive response to using "journal." Which could indicate that journal lowers the barrier to entry for some reason.
It's worth to continue testing into this by reducing anxiety for the user by using supporting copy to highlight how easy it is to create a site.

experiments.title: How a Radical Redesign Impacted Staff Donation Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that re-positioning the donation page and better highlighting one-time vs monthly gifts for staff donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because moving the gift array to the bottom allows the user to read the copy and see the value first before being given an opportunity to give. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 04/06/2023
The Navigators’ saw tremendous success with a radical redesign of their instant donation pages that implemented a tabbed form and moved the gift array to the bottom of the page instead of the top. We hypothesized that creating the same treatment for Staff Donation Pages would result in a similar lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, we saw neither a valid increase nor a decrease in this experiment. The relative difference in conversion rate was only 2.8% with a low level of confidence at 27.7%. A learning here is that simply because a treatment worked on one page doesn't mean that it will generate the same results on another. The minimal difference also urges consideration on how this treatment was being applied on a very different page to an audience with potentially very difference motivations than in the original test. For Staff Donation Pages, users are more likely to already know in advance who they'd want to support. It's possible that because they are coming to the page with an intentional motivation of who they are or are not giving to, this treatment did very little to affect this audience's decision.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline prompting readers to read on impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a continuation headline  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will learn that there is more to their journey and will realize their opportunity to make a gift and why a gift is needed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/02/2023
DTS’ James course offer is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. When user’s sign up for the James online course, they are redirected to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to give. We want to see if we can get more people to give after signing up for the course.
We’ve built a donation page headline test that applies a continuation headline. The control donation page headline says “Thank you” and “Congratulations”, implying that their journey has ended and that nothing else if needed of them.
We want to change this implication to rather encourage users to continue on. That’s why we’ve built a treatment headline that reads as follows:
Before you go…
Your course material for week 1 is headed to your inbox – we’re excited for you to get started! But before you go, would you consider one more thing?
We believe this headline will encourage readers to read on, enabling them to engage with the value proposition copy and to see that donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings: After being able to run for only 8 days, the treatment saw a 2.9% decrease in donations with only a 4.3% level of confidence. It led to a more notable 40.9% decrease in revenue (77.1% level of confidence). As we were constrained by the sunset of the testing platform that this experiment was being run on, we will have to test this experiment once more on a new platform to give it more runway and to determine more trustworthy data outcomes.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 06/30/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Cherish your Spouse video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After three weeks, we were unable to show any statistically significant impact, positive or negative, from the treatment. Revenue showed the biggest impact, but it too wasn't statistically valid. After testing impact bullet points on three offers, we had one with a clear increase in donor conversion, one with a clear decrease, and this experiment with no significant impact. This shows that the success of emphasizing the value/impact of an offer vs emphasizing the content of the offer depends on the impact that is emphasized. For this offer, emphasizing that the videos will enable you to "feel the closeness that comes from cherishing each other and experience greater marital intimacy" weren't compelling enough impacts to significantly impact conversion.

More testing is needed here offer by offer to identify the best value impacts to emphasize in the landing page copy.

experiments.title: How donor-centric language impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-centric value proposition for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition and reasons to give will pertain to them on their impact rather than the organization’s.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/02/2023
DTS’ Genesis course offer is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. When user’s sign up for the Genesis online course, they are redirected to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to give. We want to see if we can get more people to give after signing up for the course.
We’ve developed a treatment which focuses especially on the donor. It includes much “you” language and focuses on the donor’s perspective and what they can help accomplish.
This differs from the control page, which uses much organization-centric language – “we” language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 8 days, the treatment produced a 45.1% increase in donor conversion rate with a 43% level of confidence. It also produced a 30.6% lift in revenue with a 65% level of confidence. Unfortunately, external factors prevented this experiment from running any longer.
Despite the limitations, this promising trend may prove this to be a experiment worth running for your organization.
The treatment was trending in the right direction, but before we can roll it out, we'll need to test it for a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How reinforcing security upon course sign up affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security for course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because there is reduced anxiety about how their information will be used .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 07/02/2023
When signing up for a course there may be added anxiety as to why we may be asking for their contact information. Adding security language below the submit button can let them know that their information is safe with DTS. Our hypothesis is that the addition of this language will encourage more people to fill out the form at the end and lead more people to the instant donation page (also resulting in an increase in donor conversion rate).
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 22 days, the treatment (security language implemented) produced a 1.6% increase in email acquisition rate with a 40% level of confidence. Given the flat lift and modest level of confidence, we cannot confidently roll out the treatment. It seems the added security language had little impact on a user's motivation and comfort to provide their personal information and sign up for the course. We may want to try a similar test on the instant donation page to see if it provides a more significant impact. This could be a more effective place to run the test given the additional information required from someone in order to complete a donation.

experiments.title: How a pulsing heart sticky donate button affected donations on blog pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a side sticky button for blog page viewers will achieve a higher donor conversion because the pulsing heart is eye catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donations from the Spiritual Directions blog pages we decided to test adding a side sticky donate button with a pulsing heart.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a side sticky button with a pulsing heart on the blog pages resulted in a 44.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the pulsing heart caught the attention of viewers and encouraged them to donate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 76.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure statistical validity. It would also be helpful to test different variations of the side sticky button design, such as different shapes or colors, to determine which design elements are most effective in increasing donations. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other factors that could influence donor conversion, such as the content of the blog posts or the placement of the donate button on the page.
When results of the blog page experiment is combined with the homepage results of the same test (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6140) we find a 66% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence

experiments.title: How an animated lock impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an animated lock  for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lock is eye catching and increases perceived security.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
In an effort to increase donations, and therefore revenue, we decided to test an animated lock on the donation page for the America is a Christian Nation donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the A/B experiment conducted on the America is a Christian Nation donation pages, the key learnings are as follows:
1. Animated lock impact: The treatment group, which had an animated lock on the donation page, showed a 2.5% increase in revenue for all traffic. This indicates that the animated lock had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the statistical level of confidence of 58.5% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, rendering these results invalid.
2. Audience segmentation: When the results were further analyzed based on visitor types, returning visitors showed a significant 720% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence, and other visitors had a 355% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the impact of the animated lock was more pronounced for repeat visitors and other visitor segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that there is potential for using an animated lock on the donation pages to increase donor conversion rates. However, to draw stronger conclusions and make informed decisions for future experimentation, the statistical validity of the results needs to improve by achieving a higher level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a donor countdown on a sticky bar impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by included a donor goal for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion of donors because their is an incentive for donors to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
For the June 2023 PTV Match Campaign we decided to test a sticky bar with a donor goal and count down.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a donor goal in the sticky bar on the website can potentially increase the conversion of donors. The treatment version, which included a donor goal, saw a 0.9% increase in donations compared to the control version. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% for valid results.
Based on this, it is recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the findings. It is important to increase the sample size, run the experiment for a longer period of time, and make sure that the statistical level of confidence is above 85% for conclusive results. Additionally, it may be worth testing different variations of the donor goal, such as different numbers or wording, to see if there is an impact on donor conversion.
Overall, the learnings from this experiment suggest that including a donor goal in the sticky bar could be an effective strategy for increasing donations. Further experimentation and optimization can help determine the best approach to maximize donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials in the donation page value proposition impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials through the value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the issue will feel closer to home for donation page visitors and they’ll be more inspired to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis instant donation page is producing a a low conversion rate. We wondered if enhancing the value proposition messaging through evidentials would better engage donation page visitors, as the messaging would feel closer to home and they’d therefore want to make a donation to help improve the border crisis situation.
The control uses more general value proposition while the treatment highlights specific threats to Texas citizens and examples of the harm the border crisis is causing to these citizens.
Let’s see which approach appeals more to our audience in terms of urging them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, the treatment resulted in a 45.2% decrease in donations with a 78.6% level of confidence. Potential donors did not respond to the treatment evidential-infused value proposition copy like we expected them to. Perhaps they already feel they have a strong grasp of the problem and therefore were not moved by the evidentials. In this case, we would want to hone in on the solution side of the formula more so than the problem side.
The treatment also produced a 29.5% decrease in revenue with a 73.6% level of confidence.
Returning visitors produced an 88% decrease in donations to the treatment with a valid 97.6% level of confidence - 1 donation to the treatment compared to 7 to the control. This provides further proof that our returning visitors already have a strong grasp of the problem.
Donors may care more about how their donation would make a difference in resolving the issues caused by the border crisis. Especially on this donation page where they're being asked to make a monthly donation, it's likely even more important to stress the reasons to give.
It would be interesting to run a test where we isolate the reasons to give between the control variant and the treatment variant to see how that impacts giving. In this instance, the treatment reasons to give focus more on stopping the bad guys (cartels) while the control reasons to focus more on implementing better policy. It would be interesting to isolate these to see which motivates our audience more to make donations.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is producing a low instant donor conversion rate after users sign the petition. In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their CEO’s picture and a message directing the reader’s attention to the donation opportunity below and reminding them of the impact that they can make. We believe this message coming from a person and prominent figure in the organization will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar with the picture of a prominent member of the organization and a personal message from that person can significantly increase the donor conversion rate - the power of personal touch. The treatment version of the donation page, which included the humanized sticky bar, saw a 164.8% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence is only 63.5% which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. This experiment had to end after 15 days, preventing the necessary data to reach validation. It also recorded only 4 total donations between the two variants, which is below the recommended 50 transactions to reach confident results.

experiments.title: How a value-driven headline on an acquisition offer landing page impacts email conversion, donor conversion, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating reader-focused value proposition language for visitors to the Cherish video series landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life. because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/10/2023
Focus on the Family is a Christian ministry that serves families worldwide. Every year, hundreds of thousands of people sign up for their free video series’ to learn more about strengthening marriages, families, and faith.
One of their most effective series is Cherish Your Spouse. In reviewing the landing page for this offer, we felt that the headline lacked a strong value for the visitor. We hypothesized that if we changed the headline to clarify the impact of the series on subscribers, it would increase the appeal of the offer and signups on the page, ultimately leading to more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for three weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant impact on email signups from the new headline. Downstream donor conversion actually trended down for the treatment page but we were not able to validate that decrease either. Future tests will need to change more than the headline to have a higher likelihood of resulting in a statistically valid result. Our next test will be to change the body copy to emphasize what subscribers will experience as a result of watching these videos.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in the gift array affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on the gift array for ebook downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there are fewer decisions to make at the point of conversion, overall reducing the amount of friction involved in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2023 - 06/09/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Revelation. Once users download the free study guide, they are redirected to the instant donation page where they are presented with value proposition asking for a donation to Luther Seminary.
Luther Seminary wanted to increase the instant conversion rate on this page so we decided to test only having one option on the gift array versus normally having four options. We believe this will decrease the friction involved in the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of options on the gift array for ebook downloaders did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate in this particular case. The treatment group saw a decrease of 33.4% in conversion rate with a level of confidence of 61%, making the results invalid. Because this experiment did not have 50 donations minimum and did not reach a valid level of confidence, it's hard to say how this would affect other offers and organizations.

In future experimentation, we need to test this hypothesis on an offer that receives more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How adding privacy language below a survey sign up submit button impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more formal privacy statement at the below the survey submit button will increase the education survey sign up rate because it will establish more credibility and trust in ACI and the survey itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/24/2023
ACI’s Education Survey is producing a 43.3% sign up rate over the last month. While this metric is performing above-benchmark, we believe there is room for improvement for this offer to match the sign up rates of other offers such as the Cancel Culture Pledge (59.5% sign up rate).
We recently tested applying privacy language below the survey sign up form on the Education Survey landing page, as we’ve seen this tactic have success across other similar organizations. The historical learning is that privacy language builds trust with our audience as many people can feel vulnerable giving away their information, so the implementation of privacy language (your information is secure with us) provides users more comfort as they make the decision to submit their survey and personal information.
Though, our recent test proved unsuccessful, producing a 4.3% drop in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence. You can review this experiment at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/Ewwm
As a second iteration, we think placing the privacy language below the form AND survey submit button will be the key to seeing a lift in email sign ups – this opposed to our previous experiment which placed the language between the from and the button.
Placing it below the button will avoid disrupting the sign up process and avoid making users think that they should think twice about submitting their information as they get ready to press the submit button below. Placing the text below the button serves more so as a supportive reminder.
Let’s see how our second iteration performs…
experiments.keyLearnings:
Iteration #2 did not produce a valid drop; however, it did produce very flat results. The treatment saw a 0.07% decrease in email acquisition rate with only a 3.51% level of confidence. This means that the data does not support the addition of privacy language being a part of the Education Survey sign up page.
There was no notable lift downstream or through any alternative segments, either.
After two attempts, adding privacy language does not seem to move the needle with our audience. Ultimately, this is a good thing as users seem to already trust the organization and do not need to be told that their information is secure in order to feel comfortable submitting a form.
We should move our experiment brainstorming to another element on the page to see if we can uncover a desired life in email acquisition.

experiments.title: How urgency-focused value proposition copy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that urgency value proposition language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because visitors will feel a sense of urgency to give and make an impact that they otherwise wouldn’t.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/23/2023
DTS is amid a high-urgency fiscal year-end campaign. As our org is pushing a large number of users to our fiscal year-end donation pages, we want to test into urgency-focused donation page copy.
As part of our FYE campaign, we launched a free content offer where users can sign up to receive the eBook, and once they do, they’re redirected an instant donation page. Our existing donation page deploys standard value proposition messaging through problem/solution copy and reasons to give; however, it does not include important FYE messaging including urgency language, the campaign deadline, campaign goal, or matching gift challenge associated with the campaign. The only urgent element it includes is a countdown clock, but it does not explain what the clock is counting down to.
We want to test urgency-focused messaging that does include these elements and mentions, and that grabs the user’s attention immediately, alerting them of this urgent opportunity to make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The urgency-focused language did not produce the results that we expected. After running for 17 days, the treatment resulted in a 9.6% decrease in donations with a 40.4% level of confidence. It also produced a 4% decrease in revenue with a 56.7% level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that urgency-focused language does not always lead to more donations. It can provide the urgency, the flash, and the untraditional that could inspire someone to give. Though, in this case, I wonder if the supporting value proposition copy was not as strong as the control. The control includes strong problem/solution copy and highlights specifically how a donor's gift will be used, while the treatment includes more general value proposition copy that never really explains the problem(s) that a donor's gift might help solve and how the gift would be used to solve them.
For a future iteration, it would be wise to blend the value proposition copy from the control with the urgency elements used in the treatment. This could be the formula for increase donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the Course on Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because it removes anxiety around giving up an email. because  it removes anxiety around giving up an email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/25/2023
The IMB’s Course on Prayer offer was experiencing a low sign-up rate on the landing page. We hypothesized that eliminating anxiety with a simple security callout at the bottom of the form would reassure users by clarifying what their information will be used for. We believe a reminder to the user of why their email is being requested (to receive their course lessons) will increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment should not be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Ultimately, a very small lift with a low level of confidence shows that this treatment did not cause much of a difference for users arriving at this landing page. More testing is required, but this also prompts us to explore what other factors could be causing anxiety/friction to email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to have a larger sample size and a higher level of confidence before making any decisions based on the results. It may also be beneficial to test alternative forms of security callouts or messaging to determine which resonates best with visitors.

experiments.title: How social proof vs. credibility impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of credibility for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will build trust in the value proposition and give the donor more reasons to believe our claims.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/22/2023
In a previous test, we saw that adding a quote from a current volunteer board member produced a lift in donations. We wondered if adding a statement of credibility using the same format would make any difference in conversion. We pulled a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization and put it in the same placement on the page as an element of social proof right before the donation form and split traffic 50/50, sending half of visitors to the control with Ken’s quote about donating and the other half to the treatment with the Forbes magazine call-out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to determine if adding a statement of credibility before the donation form would increase donor conversion. The treatment included a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization, while the control had a quote from a volunteer board member. The experiment ran for 28 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 53.8% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% and thus cannot be considered valid.

From this experiment, we can learn that adding a statement of credibility may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion. It is essential to conduct further experimentation to confirm the validity of the initial results. What we saw was the audience was more motivated by a donor-centric quote from a supporter regarding their experience with giving to the organization than they were by the organizational-centric quote from a review that gave an exclusive reason to support the organization. One reason for this could be the difference in the positioning of the language in the quotes "Let's go get 'em" which insinuates there is work left to be done versus the treatment that claims the organization has the "largest communication presence" which could lead donors to believe that the organization does not need their help or support since they are already doing the most/best with their existing resources and are already in a position to be successful without the donor making a gift to help.

experiments.title: How using language around gaining deeper knowledge and enhancing their faith affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language centered around gaining a deeper knowledge of the book of Psalms and enhancing their faith for potential online course signees will achieve a higher email conversion rate because it taps into the inherent desire for personal growth, intellectual curiosity, and a sense of accomplishment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/15/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a free online course of the Book of Psalms. They wanted to try and increase the email conversion rate on the sign-up page for the course, so we decided to test using language around gaining a deeper knowledge of this book of the Bible while enhancing their faith. We did this in two ways:
Emphasis on Knowledge and Understanding: The treatment page focuses on the idea of gaining deeper knowledge and understanding of the Book of Psalms. By highlighting the informative and insightful nature of the course, it appeals to individuals who are seeking to expand their knowledge base and develop a more profound understanding of the Psalms. This emphasis on learning and personal growth can be compelling for those who value educational opportunities.
Cultivating Personal Qualities: The first page mentions that studying the Psalms can help cultivate qualities such as a heart of worship, hunger for God, authentic prayer life, and a thankful heart. This emphasis on personal transformation and spiritual development appeals to individuals who are looking for practical applications and benefits from studying the Psalms. It positions the course as a means to enhance one’s relationship with God and deepen their faith.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the effectiveness of using language centered around gaining a deeper knowledge of the book of Psalms and enhancing faith resulted in a 6.5% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic with a 73.3% level of confidence. However, this result cannot be considered valid for future decision making as the level of confidence is below 85%.

The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing knowledge and understanding or cultivating personal qualities may not be effective for all visitors. Therefore, it is important to understand the audience and tailor language accordingly. Future experiments should continue to test various messaging approaches, target specific audiences, and gather feedback to determine what messaging is most effective in increasing email acquisition.

experiments.title: Site vs Page on Caring for Cancer eBook Confirmation
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the call-to-action as Start a Page for users who’ve just signed up for an eBook offer will achieve an increase in site creations because creating a page has less perceived commitment than starting an entire site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge’s primary ask is to have users create a site — either for themselves or for a loved one. After signing up to be sent an offer, the user is taken to a confirmation page that prompts them with creating a site. We hypothesized that this phrasing could be overwhelming to a visitor who perhaps isn’t familiar with what CaringBridge is but was just interested in the eBook. We believe that the treatment, which instead updates the page with a call to Start a Page will lessen the perceived barrier to entry and increase the number of site creations. This same treatment was used on a different CaringBridge offer with great results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The changed call-to-action from "Start a Site" to "Start a Page" did not result in an increase in website creations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in website creations. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.
Although this same treatment showed a huge increase in site creations on a different offer, the same did not result here. This might pose questions about how this offer and its audience may be different from the funnel where this treatment was last implemented. Ultimately, more testing is needed to create a valid result with a more significant sample size.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Callout in the Gift Array Impacts Recurring Giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the monthly giving option for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts because the copy reinforces the value of a monthly gift to the reader and drives home what a monthly gift accomplishes..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Sustainers appeal is The Navigators recurring giving campaign. With users arriving on the donation page from a variety of emails, ads, and onsite assets, we hypothesized that by calling out the monthly gift option on the donation page itself would help increase recurring gifts. We believed that a simple value prop callout reminding the user of need for monthly gifts specifically would help convert those who would have originally given a one-time gift or neither at all.
experiments.keyLearnings: The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigators' Sustainers appeal showed that highlighting the monthly giving option on the donation page did not result in an increase in recurring gifts with validity. More experimentation is needed, it also prompts questions about more effective ways to test this like having the value prop callout more prominently in the copy itself or a headline.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate

rows.name: Do you want to grow in your discipleship?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity  for site visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because there will be reduced anxiety when the provide their contact information .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 06/02/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment copy added to the footer section did not increase the email acquisition rate for the Navigators website. There was a 21% decrease with a level of confidence of 73%.
However, when looking at new vs returning users we saw the language resonate with returning users and new users saw a valid 41% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 95%. Our hypothesis is that the header language might be too high of a commitment for new users. We want to retest this concept and change the copy so that it's a lower barrier to entry for new users on their site.

experiments.title: How adding donor autonomy to a headline impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity and autonomy in the headline will increase instant conversion rate because people will be more likely to read the rest of the page when they are offered autonomy to help something as opposed to a command to join something.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 06/09/2023
When looking to improve donor conversion on an instant donation page, we wondered if the headline to “join the fight to eliminate election fraud” was too vague, too much mental work, and too commanding of a call to action. We hypothesized the headline needed additional specificity, clarity and autonomy to drive donor engagement. We developed a treatment that phrased the next step as an option or a choice rather than a direct instruction. We included “Here’s how” to drive curiosity from the reader and keep the communication in motion and guide the reader’s attention to continue down the page. We split traffic between the control “Your answers have been submitted! Now join the fight to eliminate election fraud in the U.S.” and the treatment “Your answers have been submitted–Now you can help eliminate voter fraud in the U.S. Here’s how:”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to test whether changing the headline's language to "you can help eliminate voter fraud, here's how" would increase the instant conversion rate compared to the control headline. Upon review, the treatment resulted in a 75% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the level of confidence was 75%, and the total number of donations evaluated was below 50, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.
Because we did not reach the needed sample size during the experiment's duration we will launch the next iteration of this test that will maintain the headline change in the treatment while also including additional focus in the body copy on what specific solutions to voter fraud issues that the donor’s gift would directly support.

experiments.title: How a donation exit intent popup on an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an exit intent popup prompting a return to the donation page will increase donations because potential donors will be brought back in by the ask and simplified value prop, and ultimately convert.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/17/2023
TPPF’s monthly donor conversion funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In an effort to increase conversion, we’ve implemented an exit intent popup that appears when someone tries to exit the page.
The popup will hit donation page visitors with one more ask with condensed value proposition copy in hopes that it will convince them to return to the donation page and complete a donation.
This approach could be effective in a number of ways:
It’s unconventional and it literally “pops out” to users
The popup will catch all users that try to exit, but could be especially impactful to the users that would bounce from the page immediately without reading the value proposition copy and realizing the donation ask
Perhaps the exit intent popup will help make the mind up of anyone that is on the fence about donating
Prompting visitors to second guess their decision to leave the page could lead some to return and convert – but will it be enough to produce a valid lift in conversion?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not produce the results we had hoped for. In fact, it only produced eight conversions in total and they were split between the control and the treatment (exit intent popup). Ultimately, the treatment had a 1.9% lift with only a 2.12% level of confidence.
This approach is worth testing again, but on donation funnel that has ample traffic and overall conversions. We simply don't have enough data from this experiment to prove any effectiveness or lack of effectiveness.

experiments.title: Will asking for a larger specific amount increase donations and average gift size
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking for a larger specific donation amount for a smaller donor set will achieve an increase donations and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2021
We wanted to test if sending a direct ask email mentioning a matching gift and asking for a small number of donors to donate a larger amount VS the standard direct ask mentioning the match would help increase the average gift as well as overall revenue.
So the control email mentioned the match and asked for a gift while the treatment mentioned the match and asked for 10 donors to make a gift of $1,000.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the control email performed well and received 54 transactions for $11,737 with an average gift of $217.35 the treatment email saw a smaller number of donations with only 20 transactions but saw a significant increase in revenue and average gift. The total amount raised in the treatment email was $16,745 for an average gift of $837.25!

From this, we see that asking for a smaller number of donors with a larger donation amount can provide exclusivity and an urgency to donate now.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a relevant image on an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will appreciate the image and gain inspiration from it leading them to make a gift to receive the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
In an effort to boost instant donor conversion rate through ACI’s Red, White, and Blue direct ask funnel, we want to test adding a relevant image to the donation page body section. The image is of Dr. Ben Carson reading the Red, White, and Blue: Our Flag Matters to Me and You book that he wrote to an audience of American children. We selected this image because it encompasses his image and likeness, his influence and care towards educating and engaging the American youth, and the book he is reading is the book someone can make a donation to receive on this donation page.
We believe that implementing this warm, inspiring, and relatable image as potential donors read through the value proposition will encourage more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of the image within the value proposition copy did not provide a boost in donor conversion like we hypothesized it would. In fact, the treatment led to a 14% drop in donor conversion with a 68.2% level of confidence. We saw a steeper 22% decrease in revenue with a nearly valid 91% level of confidence.
This is our second attempt at adding an image of Dr. Carson and the Red, White, and Blue book and both attempts led to a decrease in donations. It seems our audience does not respond well to images, or perhaps disruptions, as they are reading the value proposition. They may arrive at the page already motivated and the image could simply get in their way as they're working their way to the donation form. They may arrive at the page and are turned off by the image, as they may associate images with ads or blogs, or something that may turn them off when they're online.
It may be time to transition away from image implementation and try experimenting with other elements of the page.

experiments.title: How shorter, more congruent donation page copy impacted donations for a direct ask funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter, more congruent value prop copy will result in an increase in donations because the donor experience will be better-connected and remain on topic as they transition from the direct ask ad to the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
TPPF’s CAF-Focused funnel is producing a low instant conversion rate. In our analysis, we see that roughly 75% of our traffic for this funnel is coming from mobile and the mobile bounce rate is 95%. In evaluating the user experience, we hypothesize that the donation page copy is too lengthy – especially for mobile users – and that there is a disconnect between the direct ask Facebook ad creative and the instant donation page value proposition copy.
The Facebook ads are generating high click-through rates we believe primarily from the issue-specific yes/no ad graphic in which we ask users if they agree or disagree with a question that is relevant to the opportunity we’re providing to them. The ad copy also speaks specifically about one issue but then speaks much more generally on the donation page.
We need to find a way to maintain the momentum from the ad level to get more people to convert at the donation level.
Shorter copy should better engage our mobile audience and more congruent copy between the ad and the landing page should hone the value proposition copy and better motivate page visitors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment variant with the shorter copy and honed value proposition copy did produce more donations than the control, however, the difference was only one donation. The experiment produced only 5 conversions total: 2 to the control and 3 to the treatment. The 35.3% conversion lift from the treatment variant had only a 26.2% level of confidence.

Simply put, the experiment did not reach the conversion sample to validate one way or the other after running for 30 days. Thus, we cannot make any valid conclusions. We'll keep a look out for other opportunities to launch this type of test.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a donor’s gift before a call to action, affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treating the pushdown headline to first answer “why” for users on the homepage will achieve a higher conversion rate because leading with “what” a donation accomplishes for the user and “why” will be more compelling than the command in the control.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Navigators created a pushdown/sticky bar for their Eagle Lake campaign. This asset targeted the main homepage, and the button would take users to the instant donation page for the campaign. At launch, the pushdown was underperforming compared to its effectiveness on past campaigns—we hypothesized that this was due to copy itself lacking value prop. In the control, the main headline starts with a call to give even before the user has been given any reasons as to why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to improve the underperforming pushdown/sticky bar for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign by changing the copy of the headline to address the "why" of giving. The treatment resulted in a directional 13.9% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold for validity.

Though not a valid result, we did see that the treatment captured

Ultimately, Navigator's audience seemed to respond similarly to both control and treatment versions of the copy.
