Of course, the next steps could be testing into new copy treatments but the question is whether this is the most likely angle to move the needle for Navigators' audience given the subdued reaction here. Instead, we'd like to turn our focus away from testing into copy and instead determine whether a visual change, like a goal thermometer, is what will prove most effective in communicating value.

experiments.title: How adding specificity to your micro-value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing specific Psalms Study Guide information for course enrollees will achieve a higher conversion rate because readers have a clearer idea of what they will be giving to and receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 05/07/2023
This theological seminary client offers a few online courses as a biblical resource. On the Psalms Course resource, we wanted to test specific language in the copy of the donation page to see if it would produce a higher conversion rate.
The control donation page copy includes a donation ask that shares that the reader’s donation will help fund the next published biblical resource. We wanted to run an experiment that goes deeper into this copy and provides the reader with more information on what the following biblical resource is, ie. the Psalms Study Guide, and why it will help them, ie. “deepen their understanding of the Book of Psalms”.
This treatment experiment has been shown to 50% of the viewers and was run as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding more detailed language to fund their next biblical resource, the Psalms Study Guide, to the donation page copy did not produce a higher donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page saw a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with a 29.9% confidence level, so the experiment did not validate. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may help to include specific details about what the donor will read, ie. chapter topics, so the donor will have a better expectation of what they will be receiving if they donate.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline + congruent value prop messaging impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a continuation headline and more congruent reasons to give consistent with the theme of the funnel will achieve an increase in donations among donation page visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/04/2023
ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to similar offers. Click through rate at the ad level and petition sign up rate are very healthy, but we’re struggling to get donors to convert once they reach the instant donation page.
Reviewing the current donation page, two things stood out:
1) The headline comes off as conclusive and does not imply that there is another action the petition signer can take.
2) The reasons to give in the last paragraph are not on theme with the petition subject and therefore could be creating a disconnect that turns the page visitor away.
We developed a treatment that implements a continuation headline and closing value proposition copy (reasons to give) that align with the action they just took (signing a petition to stop judicial weaponization).
Let’s see how the treatment performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results were not what we anticipated. The treatment resulted in a 6.6% decrease in donations with a 20.1% level of confidence. Revenue to the treatment resulted in an 18% decrease with a 67.6% level of confidence. Ultimately, neither variant moved the needle.
Interestingly, there was a notable difference between mobile and desktop visitors:
Mobile: Treatment produced a 95.1% increase in donations with an 85.7% LoC
Desktop: Treatment produced a 42.5% decrease in donations with an 88.2% LoC
These outcomes came close to validating, however, it's difficult to put a finger on what led to these vastly different user responses given that the copy length stayed roughly the same and there was little difference in the actual visibility of the page.
We'll keep these results in mind and see if we can test this similar approach for other offers. If we do, we'll monitor to see if results are consistent or different to the ones we've recorded here.

experiments.title: How increased perceived security impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding additional copy for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion because there is perceived higher security.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 06/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
To increase donations and revenue, we decided to test increasing the perceived security of the donation form for PTV’s 18 Minutes with Jesus book
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we only saw a 4% increase with a 53% confidence level.
Due to the inconclusive results, we are going to test other methods of security such as an animated lock.

experiments.title: How adding appeal and exclusivity to course sign up value proposition copy impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity for landing page visitors will increase sign up rate because visitors will gain an improved understanding of what they’ll get out of the course and how the course will improve their relationship with Christ.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/29/2023
In an effort to increase sign up rate to DTS’ Intro to Prophets online course, we built a sign up page treatment focusing on enhancing the appeal and exclusivity with the value proposition copy. We feel these elements are lacking in the existing copy.
For exclusivity, we added language including “Don’t miss this rare opportunity”, “This one-of-a-kind course”, and “become part of an exclusive community of like-minded learners.”
For appeal, we transitioned the copy to focus more on the potential course participant and the course itself instead of the organization and the organization’s mission, and added more action sentences to hopefully invoke the action of signing up.
Let’s see how the adjusted value prop performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity can potentially increase the sign-up rate for a course. However, the statistical level of confidence of 32.6% is well below the threshold of 85% and the results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment produced a minimal 0.98% lift in course sign ups.
Downstream metrics were minimal as well, with the treatment producing a 5% increase in donations with just a 22.5% level of confidence, and revenue decreasing by 2.7% with a 55.7% level of confidence.
The added appeal and exclusivity did not enhance user motivation and increase course sign up like we hypothesized it would. We will need to revisit the value proposition copy to brainstorm alternative motives to increase sign ups. Or consider testing opportunities elsewhere on the sign up page.
Further experiments need to be conducted with a larger sample size and more significant changes to the treatment. Future experiments should focus on creating a strong value proposition that directly addresses the needs of the potential course participants and highlights the unique features of the course. Additionally, the experiment should explore other factors that could potentially impact the sign-up rate, such as the design of the landing page and the placement of the call-to-action button. Overall, the experiment provides a starting point for further exploration of how to increase sign-up rates for online courses, but a larger sample size and more significant changes are needed to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2023 - 05/29/2023
TPPF’s Pocket Constitution Booklet was getting a ton of traction as an individual content offer. We want to find out if taking that momentum by applying it as a premium as part of a new content offer funnel would produce a lift in donations for the new content offer (School Choice Survey).
We added an image of the Pocket Constitution to the School Choice Survey instant donation page, along with the following language at the end of the value proposition copy to clarify the premium opportunity:
“And if you make a donation of $10 or more today, we will send you a FREE Pocket Constitution for your home or classroom as a reminder of your commitment to better education in the Lone Star State.”
Let’s find out if the added premium offer increases donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test the hypothesis that adding a premium to an instant donation page would increase donations. The treatment included an image of a Pocket Constitution Booklet and an offer to send the booklet to anyone who made a donation of $10 or more on the School Choice Survey instant donation page.

The experiment ran for 32 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 17.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a 22.2% statistical level of confidence.

While the treatment did not produce a bump in donations like we had hoped, it did produce a large bump in revenue through a 645.8% lift with a nearly valid 84.8% level of confidence.

The average gift among donors to the treatment was $90 compared to an average gift of just $10 among those who donated to the control variant.

It seems the extra incentive provided by the Pocket Constitution Booklet premium made people want to give more, even though the minimum donation to receive the premium was $10.

This speaks to the "what do I get in return" mindset many people have. By getting an actual item in return for making a gift, it seems donation page visitors were willing to give more.

The nearly valid increase in revenue gives us the confidence to roll out the treatment, connecting all funnel traffic to the premium opportunity.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion

rows.name: Campaign Focused Donation Page - With Thermometer

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher subscribers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the goal thermometer on a donation page leverages psychological factors such as visual progress, social proof, competitiveness, accountability, and psychological commitment and engages potential donors on an emotional level and provides them with a compelling reason to contribute to the cause.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/26/2023
Luther Seminary is running its bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a goal thermometer to the donation page would increase donor conversion.
We added the thermometer to the treatment page from the start of the campaign and showed it to 50% of traffic. Users came to this donation page through email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that adding a goal thermometer to a donation page for Working Preacher subscribers resulted in a 4.6% increase in donations with a 42.1% level of confidence. While the experiment suggests that adding a goal thermometer may be effective in increasing donations, further testing is needed to confirm this.

We believe this did not validate as an increase in donor conversion because the Working Preacher subscribers are already comfortable and emotionally driven to support Working Preacher. Therefore they did not need the extra push that the thermometer provided.

experiments.title: How personalizing a donation ask with a signature impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing a donation ask to come from a well-known authority figure and adding his signature at the end of the ask will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will feel called upon directly by the authority figure to make an impact, and the signature will provide authenticity to the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2023 - 05/29/2023
For ACI’s Religious Liberty Survey funnel, we want to test a personalization approach for the offer instant donation page in which we will frame the value proposition to come from the organization’s leading authority figure. To execute this approach, we’ve:
updated the headline to call attention to the following message from the authority figure 
placed the message in quotations to show that it is being spoken/written from the authority figure
added the authority figure’s signature after the message to “authenticate” it
Our hypothesis is that this combination of personalization tactics will resonate more strongly with donation page visitors and lead to an increase in donations.
The control maintains value proposition language that comes from the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which presented a donation ask from ACI Founder Dr. Ben Carson with his signature added post-message, resulted in an 11.7% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.8%, below the required 95% for the results to be considered valid.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a well-known authority figure's signature to a donation ask can potentially increase donations, but further testing is necessary to validate the results.
In reviewing the treatment, we wonder if the "special offer" text next to Dr. Carson's signature on desktop and below his signature on mobile takes away from the personalization by introducing marketing copy between the message and the donation form. As a supplement test, we may want to experiment with removing or relocating the "special offer" text to where it does not disrupt the momentum the potential donor has going from the value prop to the donation form.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a newsletter subscription opportunity impacted email opt-ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to a newsletter subscription for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will gain clarity in what they’ll get from signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2023 - 05/21/2023
In an effort to get more people to sign up for IPI’s newsletter, we built a treatment that added value proposition copy above the newsletter sign up form. We highlighted what type of news coverage subscribers could expect, added more call-to-action language, and expresses the importance of being an engaged citizen.
The control version lacks each of these elements, so we’re interested to see if the treatment will result in an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, the experiment resulted in only one email opt-in per variant. Thus, the experiment had no winner and the lack of email conversions prevented any valid result. Value proposition does not see to be the issue. We should test different elements of the page to isolate the problem. Perhaps there was too much friction for visitors given the various check boxes they're asked to consider. A new test could be around simplifying the page to reduce decision friction.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring giving pop up during the giving process impacts monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring giving interruptor  for those making a one time gift on the main donate page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because we are adding clarity as to why someone should make a recurring gift instead of a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase the number of monthly donors, we wanted to prompt more people to give a monthly gift on the IMB’s main donation page. After the first step of the giving process (selecting one time + gift amount + selecting “Give Now”), they would receive a pop up that asked people to change their gift frequency to monthly. They would receive a custom ask amount based off of their original one-time gift amount. If their gift was over $200, they did not receive this prompt.
Our hypothesis is that this prompt would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase the number of monthly donors on IMB's main donation page by adding a pop-up interruptor that prompted people to change their gift frequency to monthly after the first step of the giving process. The hypothesis was that this would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
The experiment ran for 15 days and showed that the treatment group saw a 27.6% increase in Monthly Donations with a 39.6% statistical level of confidence. This means that this resulted in virtually no difference in monthly gifts.
But, we did see a valid decrease in overall gifts. A 35% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. This data point tells us that the pop up was not motivating enough  for people to make a monthly gift, but disruptive enough for people to potentially abandon the giving process.
This experiment will be retested in a different channel - an instant donation page in an offer funnel. It could be that motivations are different enough for prospective donors that we would see different results.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and exclusivity to a newsletter signup footer impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including value prop about why to sign up for communications from IMB for for users on the homepage will achieve an increased email conversion rate because people need to see value in signing up if they are going to provide their personal email because  people need to see value in signing up if they are going to provide their personal email..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 05/06/2023
At the bottom of IMB’s homepage is a call to sign up for communications and a form for users to submit their email.  We hypothesized that adding more value prop copy to the Sign-Up—verbiage about why should someone sign up, what will you receive, etc—would increase email acquisition. The control copy was shorter, but more general, leveraging with a grab about a newsletter with interesting stories. Alternatively, the treatment tested more copy, but conveyed more details about what you’d receive and why to sign up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The statistical level of confidence is not high enough to consider these results valid. Future experimentation should be conducted with a larger sample size given that the differences observed were under 1%. The experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in email conversion rate with a level of confidence of 33%.
One idea to execute a test of this hypothesis with a validating result is a more prominent treatment. This Sign Up form is located at the very bottom of IMB's homepage which limits the pool size only to those who scroll to the bottom. This same copy could be tested with a slideout or popup that appears on the first portion of the page.

experiments.title: How language specific to existing contacts impacted Facebook clicks

rows.name: FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member control
FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by making ad copy specific to FBD contacts for existing contacts on Facebook will achieve a higher conversion in clicks because they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas. because  they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 05/14/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Leading up to Sunday services, First Dallas utilizes Facebook to share what the sermon will be and invites people to join in person.
This campaign also targets existing First Dallas contacts through the HubSpot/Facebook integration. We decided to test and see if copy that is specific to this audience would increase clicks on the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test if making ad copy specific to First Baptist Dallas's existing contacts on Facebook would result in a higher conversion in clicks. The treatment resulted in a 2.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, but with only a 12.2% statistical level of confidence, the results cannot be considered valid.
During the experiment, the connection between HubSpot and Facebook was broken. This led to the experiment not spending its full budget or reaching its highest potential. We will rerun this experiment in the coming weeks to seek valid results.

experiments.title: How using support-focused language impacted gifts on an instant donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that focuses on how a donor can directly support an issue on a donation page will achieve an increase in revenue because people are more motivated to give if they feel that their individual support is needed and important.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 04/30/2023
In an effort to improve the instant donation rate of a survey offer, we tested how a more conversational tone and donor-focused language would affect donations.
The original donation page focused on what policy reforms the organization was promoting to impact fiscal reform. It also mentioned that a donor’s gift would help introduce over 50 specific policy recommendations that would affect the donor. However, we saw an opportunity to focus more on “you” language and putting the donor’s support at the center of the proposed solution. We believed that prospective donors would be more motivated to give if they felt that their support was integral to solving the problems outlined in the survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language that focuses on how a donor can directly support an issue on a donation page may not be effective in increasing donations. The treatment saw a very slight decrease in donations compared to the control, although the results are not statistically valid. The treatment saw a larger 55% decrease in average gift.

Reasons for this decrease could be:
the treatment's removal of a bulleted list in favor of only paragraphs, which can cause people to move on from without reading
 	the treatment's removal of specific policy recommendations could have led the viewer to not understand what actual, tangible solutions their donation would support

experiments.title: How staging a donation ask to come from an authority figure impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing a donation ask to come from a well-known authority figure for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will feel called upon by the authority figure to make an impact, and this version of a donation ask will hold more value and emphasis. because  potential donors will feel called upon by the authority figure to make an impact, and this version of a donation ask will hold more value and emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2023 - 05/04/2023
For ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition, we want to test two different copy angles to see which one produces the most donations. The control is our conventional approach, consisting value proposition speaking from the organization to the end-user. The treatment take a first person approach, presenting the donation ask from ACI Founder Dr. Ben Carson.
Leveraging Dr. Carson’s name and likeness has worked in many previous tests, so we want to try it for this offer funnel and donation ask.
We want to determine if a message communicated from Dr. Carson will resonate more with potential donors and lead to more donations. Will appreciate the personal message? Will they take action based on the message of “will you join with me?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that framing a donation ask to come from an authority figure may have a positive impact on donations. The treatment featuring Dr. Ben Carson as the messenger resulted in a 2.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence was only 25.5%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
The very slight life in donations does not lead us to confidently believe that the treatment is the optimal option moving forward.
Revenue resulted in a 5.1% increase with a higher but still invalid 66.5% level of confidence.
For a future test, we may consider emphasizing Dr. Carson in a more unique way, such as through a humanized sticky bar at the top of the page. This would enable users to hear from him even sooner.

experiments.title: How an animation on the monthly giving option impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an animated heart on the monthly donation tab for people who land on a video series acquisition donation page will achieve a higher number of monthly sustainers because the heart will draw the prospective donors attention to the monthly tab. because  the heart will draw the prospective donors attention to the monthly tab.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 04/13/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global ministry that receives tons of traffic through its acquisition video series. We wanted to see if we could increase the number of monthly sustainers that come from a video series by placing an animated heart on the monthly tab button on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an animated heart on the monthly donation tab on a video series acquisition page resulted in a 7.2% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.6% which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Further testing will need to be done to see how we can increase the number of monthly sustainers from an acquisition offer.

experiments.title: How presentation of a sender name impacted open rates on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization’s full name from the sender line for survey takers will achieve an increase in open rates because the email recipients will believe it is from a real person, not an organization.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2023
In previous testing we had seen a lift in removing the organization’s name from the sender line but we wanted to test this same concept with a larger audience. For the control, we included both title and organization name. For the treatment, we used the organization’s initials and the sender title.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's full name from the sender line for survey takers may result in a slight increase in open rates. However, since the statistical level of confidence is 31%, the results cannot be considered statistically valid. Therefore, it is recommended to conduct further testing with larger sample sizes to validate the results before making any decisions based on them. We did also see a 6.9% decrease in clicks on the treatment with 78.9% level of confidence, so we need to do further testing to see how the sender name could have impacted clickthrough rate conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because seeing the authority figure in action and in a natural, relatable manner will make them want to support the organization and receive the premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 04/29/2023
ACI’s Red, White, and Blue book instant donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. This is a direct ask funnel where users go directly from an ad to the donation page, where they can receive this book when making a donation of a certain amount. We wondered if implementing an image of the author of the book and the face of the ACI organization – Dr. Ben Carson – would help improve donor conversion rate. The book is written for children and the image shows Dr. Carson signing the book for a young kid. Will the relevance of the image and the inclusion of Dr. Carson make potential donors appreciate the opportunity more and convince them to make a donation to receive the book?
Let’s find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment (instant donation page WITH the image) resulted in a 7.5% decrease in donations with a 42.8% level of confidence.
Revenue did however produce a 33.7% increase with a 90.5% level of confidence. Additionally, new visitors produced an 86.4% increase in donations with a 78.% LOC compared to returning visitors producing a 5% decrease in donations.
The overall results tell us that there is potential with this concept. Overall donations did not increase like we expected, but revenue did and so did donations among new visitors. Potential deterrents could be the treatment page layout: The layout includes two images, both of Dr. Carson. The images split up the page copy and could really disrupt the flow of value proposition copy. We may want to try using only one image on the page or right aligning the book image in the main body to avoid disrupting the reader's flow.
And perhaps this specific image is not our most effective. We may want try implementing a different image but with the same intention. It may be beneficial to test different variations of images to see which resonates best with potential donors.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Do different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase downstream conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
Although there was no difference in clickthrough rate, treatment 3 (with the emotional appeal) produced a 67% increase in revenue through a combination of an increase in conversion rate and a simultaneous increase in average gift. This shows that while the initial metric was not affected, those who chose to click on the third treatment had a much higher motivation to give, which resulted in more gifts at larger sizes.
Treatment 3 offered something that none of the other treatments did—a way to show love and support for the journal author. While the other three treatments showed ways that the user could support the site and connected the site’s impact with the author, the third treatment gave them a direct connection to the person they care for, and proposed a donation as the way to show love and support. This produced an opportunity to give that no other treatment could match.
This experiment prompts more testing around this language throughout the site, as well as testing around how to increase clickthrough rate on the donate box through accentuated placement and visual UI.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.

The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They tested three different treatments to see if they could produce a lift in clickthrough rate:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
 	The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
 	The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
none of these experiments produced a significant lift in clickthrough rate

experiments.title: How offering a premium versus a direct missional gift impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a missional gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 04/23/2023
Pathway to Victory is the ministry of First Baptist Dallas, and fundraises through a premium-focused program.
As part of that program, they have a new name acquisition program where people are asked to provide an email address and contact information in exchange for a digital version of a resource offer.
In this experiment, we were testing whether asking people for a gift after that initial sign-up with a missional ask had a better conversion rate than with a resource-based ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a missional gift for potential donors might lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 19.8% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 39.6%, which is not high enough to be considered valid.
Since this is an ongoing acquisition effort, we will let this experiment run until for a bit longer; we will also test this approach on different acquisition offers to see whether it is the actual premium being offered that is helping skew results, or if it is truly missional giving that motivates new names to donate to Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How an urgent message presented as a sticky bar impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting relevancy and urgency for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because we increased the motivation to give by explaining in a relevant way why they should give their money to accomplish something specific to an area they care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
On a post-survey instant donation page, we wondered if we could increase the relevance and urgency of the donation page ask by calling the reader’s attention through a sticky bar at the top of the page. The first treatment was a general urgency around the need to fight against inflation due to a recent worsening of the problem. The second treatment took a more specific approach by highlighting current events in the newscycle that increased the need for action in this policy area. We split traffic 3 ways and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase donor conversion by highlighting relevance and urgency on the instant donation page through a sticky bar. The results showed an 11.6% increase in donations for all traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence of 36.2%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. However, the experiment did show significant increases in donations for Paid Social visitors, Male visitors, 45-54 visitors, and visitors over the age of 55.

The key learnings from this experiment are that highlighting relevance and urgency can increase donations, but there is a need for further experimentation to validate the results before implementing changes. Additionally, it is important to segment visitors based on demographics and traffic source to understand which groups respond positively to the treatment. Future experimentation could focus on refining the message and delivery of the relevance and urgency to boost donations across all visitor segments.

experiments.title: How simplifying the value proposition in a call to action impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying and focusing the value proposition language for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will add clarity to the call to action and reduce friction .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 04/11/2023
CaringBridge has a tribute widget on authors’ journal pages. This is an opportunity to make a donation as a tribute to keep the author’s site running. When looking at the original copy used in this tribute widget, we suspected that the value proposition used in the call to action might have competing motivations. It asked the person to be one X people who have already given and were given the suggested dollar amount to power the person’s site for an entire month. Plus, it asks if you can keep the site on for you and the author. Our hypothesis was that this call to action was actually adding friction to the decision making process. We wanted to simplify the call to action. Additionally we wanted to test if the removal of “CaringBridge” in the CTA would make the ask less organizational centric and clearer. The second treatment also focuses on just making the gift in honor of the person.
Treatment 1: A $30 donation to CaringBridge powers a site like Jan’s for one month. Will you join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of Laura with your donation today?
Treatment 2: X donors have made a donation in honor of Laura. Will you join them with your donation today?
The first treatment simplifies the ask so that it does not ask the person to keep the site online for them and for you. The second treatment simplifies the CTA even further by removing mention of CaringBridge and how much is needed to power a site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in no conclusive "winner" when looking at donor conversion rate. Treatment 1 resulted in a 2.8% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 31% while treatment 3 resulted in a 3.4% increase in donations with a 38.4% statistical level of confidence.
These treatments resulted in no change in conversion rate but there does seem to be a valid increase in revenue. Treatment 1 produced a 33% valid lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 98%. Treatment 2 produced a 19.5% lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 92%.
This implies that while the language doesn't inspire more people to give, it does motivate people to give even larger gifts. It's likely that the first treatment provided a larger lift in revenue than treatment 2 because it kept the specific $30 ask in the copy.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition language on a content offer landing page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because they know what they will be helping achieve by donating..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. As part of our efforts to drive new name and new donor acquisition through content offers, we wanted to run a test on landing page copy to see whether being more concise with what a donation would get would impact conversion rates. This test ran across all acquisition offers on all channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to potential donors may not necessarily result in an increase in conversions. The treatment resulted in a 6.2% decrease in donations with a 40.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better. However, since the statistical level of confidence falls below 85%, we cannot consider these results valid.
For future experiments, we can continue to test which specific points of clarity will help increase donor conversions. Since Hoover is a think tank, the immediate connection between a donation and a tangible impact is a harder case to make; continued language testing will help clarify that.

experiments.title: How communal language on a sticky bar affects child sponsor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. These sticky bars were placed on non-sponsorship pages, linking the child sponsorship page. Both sticky bars counted down the children left who needed sponsors. The control sticky bar had language focused on the urgency and the countdown of days. And the test had communal language around the reader being part of the mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were entirely inconclusive due to small sample size and no difference in sponsorships. Further testing on different audiences, such as their Restorative Justice page, where there is higher traffic and a more community feel would be an interesting iteration.

experiments.title: How does a stickybar with a countdown affect sponsorship acquisition during a campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: A countdown that increases urgency and expands on the value prop will increase sponsorships acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. The control had no sticky bar. The test counted down both days left to acquire sponsors and the children left who needed sponsors. It was placed on sponsorship pages to increase motivation and reinforce value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate due to sample size that was too low and not enough of a disparity between control and treatment. Our hypothesis is that this did not validate for two reasons:
The countdown clock did not create enough urgency or movement.
 	The high motivation during calendar year end typically decreases the effectiveness of a test, because those receiving the control are typically more motivated to give.
Organic Search visitors had a 481.1% increase in child sponsored with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 10.5% increase in child sponsored with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language on a sticky bar affects child sponsor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. These sticky bars were placed on non-sponsorship pages, linking the child sponsorship page. Both sticky bars counted down the children left who needed sponsors. The control sticky bar had language focused on the urgency and the countdown of days. And the test had communal language around the reader being part of the mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were entirely inconclusive due to small sample size and no difference in sponsorships. Further testing on different audiences, such as their Restorative Justice page, where there is higher traffic and a more community feel would be an interesting iteration.

experiments.title: Will adding a sponsorship countdown slider increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out on the main website to determine if adding a countdown clock vs. our original it would increase the conversion rate with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.

• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).

• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).

• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.

experiments.title: How adding a high-urgency sticky bar to a recurring donor campaign page affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high-urgency sticky bar on the donation page of a recurring donor campaign will achieve higher recurring donation rates by defining a tangible goal and more clearly communicating the problem being solved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/31/2023
During a recurring donor campaign, we wanted to know how adding a high-urgency sticky bar to the donation page would affect recurring donation rates. The sticky bar combined high-urgency language with a dynamic countdown which automatically refreshed to display how many donors were still needed to reach the campaign goal. We set this experiment up as an A/B test with traffic split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for 21 days, we observed a 5.6% increase in recurring donations produced by the treatment but with a low statistical level of confidence (36.1%). Despite the modest lift in recurring donations, we also observed that the treatment produced a sizable 21% decrease in overall revenue (with a 75.5% level of confidence), indicating that by putting more emphasis on the number of donations, the treatment may have unexpectedly had a negative impact on the amount that donors were willing to give. It may therefore be worth testing this tactic against a monetary goal in the future.

experiments.title: How addressing the felt need of the ebook recipient impacts account creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that addressed the felt need for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in site creation rate because the “why” of a site creation would be established before being asked to create one, thus reducing friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 04/09/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added language that addressed the felt need of the ebook recipient.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment invited the individual to “start a CaringBridge site” and was an explicit as to why someone would need a CaringBridge site. We believed that changing the language to address the felt need would decrease friction the person would experience in being asked to create a site right away.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the call to action saw a 7.6% increase in website creations for all traffic with a 21.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making. The results indicate that changing the language to address the felt need had little to no impact on the site creation rate. It is likely that the change in copy was not significant enough to sway the recipient to take action.
For future experiments, it is recommended to continue testing language that addresses the felt need of the audience for increased website creations. Perhaps a complete rewrite may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How adding statements of credibility to a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility language to the instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain belief in our organization and trust us to make a true impact with their contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 04/13/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis survey offer funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. We’ve run several tests on this page, including a recent test in which we added evidentials to the copy in order to make the problem more tangible and understandable. This test produced a drop in donor conversion rate. The result made us confident that our audience already has a strong understanding of the problem, and that the issue with conversion rate is elsewhere on the page.
So for this experiment, we’re testing adding credibility language with the hypothesis that it will convince our audience that we are capable of solving or improving the problems we face at the border. Perhaps the current donation page does not do a good enough job of separating TPPF from other organizations and selling us as the organization most capable of helping in this critical area.
Our audience understands the problem we’re trying to help solve, but do they trust us and believe that we can solve it?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding credibility language to the instant donation page for potential donors has a significant impact on increasing donations. The treatment group saw a 162.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a 76.6% level of confidence. We saw even greater results in revenue with the treatment producing a 614.2% increase in revenue for all traffic with a valid 96.5% level of statistical confidence. The average gift to the treatment was $20.40 compared to just $7.50 to the control.
Returning visitors converted four times through the treatment compared to zero through the control. This tells us that people who have experience with the organization and have engaged with it in the past appreciate the language separating us from other organizations.
Based on these findings, it is recommended that future experiments focus on improving the credibility and trustworthiness of the organization in order to increase donor conversions. Some future tests could include adding social proof, testimonials, and other forms of credibility language to the donation page.

experiments.title: How addressing a felt need in landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the felt need for prospective ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate  because relevancy to the individual’s needs strengthens the value proposition for the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/13/2023
IMB was experiencing a relatively low sign up rate on an offer landing page. To address this, we developed a treatment for this landing page that would better address a person’s felt need. The control for the experiment starts the page with a declarative statement to take action. The treatment invites the person to think about why they would want to sign up for this online course and provides ways that this offer will fulfill those needs. Our hypothesis is that the language that unpacks the “why” would result in more emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of prospective customers can lead to a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment that focused on the "why" behind signing up for the online course instead of just encouraging action saw a 4.4% increase in email acquisitions compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.3%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid for decision making.
Additionally, downstream we saw that donor conversion rate was negatively affected by the copy (though also not a valid result).
It's likely that this low impact is because the felt need is already addressed at the ad level (where most traffic to this page is coming from). Therefore, people weren't more motivated by this language on this landing page.

experiments.title: How stating the “deadline” in a subject line for a high urgency campaign impacted open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving clarity to an email subject line amid a high urgency campaign for email recipients will achieve an increase in email open rate because recipients will know their exact window to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/13/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute is amid a high urgency fundraising campaign. The campaign deadline is tomorrow, and we want to test how making that clear in the subject line of today’s email impacts open rate.
The control variant (subject line A) is “Urgent reminder” while the treatment variant (subject line B) is “Deadline tomorrow”. We’ve split these variants to where 50% of our recipients will experience one subject line and 50% will experience the other.
We want to find out how our recipients respond to each subject line in terms of choosing to open the email. We expect “Deadline tomorrow” to produce the most opens because it clearly communicates the deadline to recipients and the fact that the current opportunity will expire after tomorrow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment variant, which had the subject line "Deadline tomorrow", did not perform better than the control variant, which had the subject line "Urgent reminder". The treatment variant saw a 4.8% decrease in Opens for All Traffic with a 70.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, we cannot conclude that giving clarity to the subject line in a high urgency fundraising campaign leads to an increase in email open rate.
It's also worth noting that the treatment subject line produced a 32.3% drop in click rate with an 82.2% level of confidence.
The "Deadline tomorrow" subject line may have been less effective because recipients didn't know what the deadline was for. Instead of opening the email to find out, they may have decided it wasn't worth their time to find out. The "Urgent reminder" SL on the other hand, tells the recipient that they've been told about the opportunity before and they may then feel more inclined to open the email see what they need to be "reminded" about.
From a psychological standpoint, there is also the possibility that the word "deadline" is a turn off to recipients. Humans often view deadlines in a negative context because they're used to hitting deadlines at work or with tasks in life. They may not take kindly to the mention of another deadline.
This angle is worth testing again for a future high urgency email to get more data behind it.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations – attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will have an improved understanding of the impact their gift would have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 03/31/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
It’s important to note that we ran a previous expanded value proposition test on this main donation page roughly a month ago and it produced a 28% drop in donation conversion rate with a 85.9% level of confidence. The focus of that test was to bring attention to the “reasons why” someone should give. We laid out five reasons via bullet points but the approach was deemed unsuccessful per our audience’s response. You can view the experiment write up at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/pRdg
Our hypothesis stemming from our previous experiment’s results was that that because someone who clicks a “make a donation” button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to “stay out of their way” by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
For this experiment, we eliminated the “reasons why” someone should give – due to the high motivation someone has when clicking “make a donation,” we also hypothesized that they already have at least one reason to give in mind and therefore do not need more reasons presented to them. Here we cut down the copy from our previous test while still expanding the value proposition copy on the current control variant, this time focusing on a broader message of how their gift would help our nation today.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 30 days, this experiment produced flat results. The treatment resulted in a 0.27% decrease in donations with a less than 1% level of confidence. The drop was not as extensive as our previous donation page value proposition experiment, which further supports our hypothesis that the previous experiment's extensive copy was not well-perceived by our potential donors. However, the less extensive, but still enhanced value proposition we used for the treatment in this experiment still did not produce positive results.
Returning visitors saw a 73% increase in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence, while new visitors saw a 34.5% decrease in donations with an 83.3% level of confidence. It's difficult to explain the negative results of new visitors as that's the segment we would expect would benefit from expanded value prop and learning more about our cause.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page may not always be effective in increasing donations. It is important to consider that visitors who click on the "make a donation" button are already highly motivated to give, and adding more copy may create unnecessary friction.
The control variant continues to stand. For a next main donation page test, we may want to consider taking the existing language from our control and expanding specifically on it. The control mentions "America's founding principles" both in the headline and the main copy. Perhaps that phrase has great importance, clarity, and appeal to our audience, and instead of focusing on copy on something else like core pillars, we should use America's founding principles to expand on within our messaging.

experiments.title: How an exit-intent popup affects overall donation rates on a donation page during a recurring-giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing an exit-intent popup with a one-time ask for traffic attempting to exit a recurring-giving donation page will achieve higher overall donation rates by presenting a lower-commitment offer to leverage the reject and retreat principle of persuasion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 03/17/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on acquiring new monthly donors, we wanted to know how an exit-intent popup would affect overall donation rates.
As the name applies, the exit-intent popup would populate only as a prospect attempted to leave the donation page without completing a donation. The specifics of the popup were designed to leverage the persuasion principle of reject and retreat. This principle is based on the idea of reciprocity and states that when someone is presented with a perceived large commitment and rejects the offer, they may be more likely to accept a subsequent offer if it has a perceived lower level of associated commitment—due to the fact that the prospect will be motivated to reciprocate the retreat by accepting the smaller offer.
In this case, the exit-intent popup would provide prospects who are attempting to leave the page without becoming a recurring donor the opportunity to still make an impact by instead giving a one-time gift of the same amount.
For this experiment, 50% of donation page traffic was shown the exit-intent popup while the other 50% did not see anything when they closed the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment showing the exit-intent popup did produce a slight lift in overall donations, we are unable to draw any definitive conclusions about this experiment based on how the results were tracked.

Because the experiment session "fired" based on page load (rather than popup load) we are unable to determine whether it was the popup that persuaded more people to give or if these results occurred merely by chance. We were also only able to achieve a 39% level of confidence in these results. This does not mean that the tactic itself is invalid, just that in order to retest we will want to make sure the experiment "fires" only once someone see the popup and attempt to obtain a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How changing the language for existing donors impacts email conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing a donor’s status for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because we are showing them they are more than just an email address.. because we are showing them they are more than just an email address..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 04/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
One of their best-performing offers is a book titled Perfect Ending; this is an offer they run on multiple channels at least once a year. For this specific email appeal, we wanted to test whether recognizing the impact a donor has had on the ministry by changing the language in an email would impact their conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that by referencing that they were a donor, they would be more likely to make a donation. This test was run for the active donor segment only.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Changing the language in the donation ask slightly, using terms such as 'continue your support' and ' reinforce that you stand behind bold....' resulted in a slight decrease in conversion rates of 8.5% with a 49% level of confidence.

There was a bigger change when looking at the impact on revenue; a 10.9% decrease in revenue, with a 68% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to the fact that this experiment only changed a couple of words; the call to donate was buried at the bottom of the email, so potential donors to this campaign were already convinced to donate and get a copy of the book before they were even influenced by the donor-specific copy.

Future variants of this test will look at the placement of donor-centric language, as well as bigger copy tests.

experiments.title: How including student testimonials on a course landing page impacted signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page will increase course sign ups because it establishes more credibility for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2023 - 04/01/2023
In an effort to increase the number of course sign ups on the our Genesis course sign up landing page, we wanted to know how including testimonials of previous students on the course sign up page would impact the number of individuals who enrolled in our Genesis course.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment variants. The control version modeled the standard landing page format for all courses, while the treatment version included a text box of two different quotes regarding this course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment aimed to determine the impact of including student testimonials on the Genesis course signup landing page to increase course signups. Results showed that the treatment resulted in a 4.9% decrease in course signups with a statistical level of confidence of 63.7%. Although the control variant won, the results cannot be considered valid since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of setting a higher statistical level of confidence for more valid results, considering the impact on specific audience segments, and the need for further experimentation on the use of testimonials on course signup landing pages. Future experimentation could focus on testing different variations of testimonials, targeting specific audience segments, and testing other elements on the landing page that could impact course signups.
We chose to end this experiment before the 30 day mark due to results consistently being in the negatives.
The key learning from this experiment is that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page may not always be effective in increasing course sign ups, and further testing will be necessary.

experiments.title: How asking existing donors for a recurring donation on an instant donation page affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition to a recurring donation ask on an instant donation page for existing donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because these people are donors and have shown that they care about this organization in the past.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 04/01/2023
Luther Seminary is wanting to increase the number of recurring donors they have. In order to try and get existing donors to convert to recurring donors, we decided to target their existing donors with Facebook ads that advertised their free content offers. They typically offer study guides or online courses.
We decided that 50% of traffic would see the regular instant donation page that includes value proposition around funding the next resource, and the other 50% of traffic would see an instant donation page with value proposition centered around becoming a monthly donor with the recurring tab preselected.
We made sure to only target donors by setting the audience targeting to include traffic when the utm_content contains “donors” (the name of the asset).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring ask instant donation page saw a 3.8% lift in recurring conversion with a 69.2% level of confidence. But this is only factoring in one gift.

We did see a 74.4% decrease in overall giving with a 97.6% level of confidence for one-time donations.

Due to this experiment running for over a month, we decided to turn it off. The traffic to this experiment was very low because this ad was in a multi-offer campaign specifically targeting existing donors.

Although we did not see a valid increase or decrease in recurring giving, we do believe the recurring ask negatively affected giving all together. We would like to test this experiment again for an offer that is receiving more traffic.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving process from 2 pages to 1 page impacts donor conversion rate (pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experience to one step  for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because reducing steps to a process can reduce friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/09/2023
In a previous experiment, the Navigators successfully increased donor conversion rate by reducing the number of steps in the giving process from 3 to 2. Our hypothesis was that we would reduce even more friction in the giving process by consolidating the experience into a single page. Unfortunately, the organization’s current giving platform was not able to accommodate a single step giving experience. Therefore, we used a completely different platform for this experiment’s treatment. We kept the control as-is in its current platform and the treatment on a new platform as a single-page experience.
In a previous iteration of this experiment we saw a valid 55% decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that the user may have experienced a break in branding congruity. So, we rebuilt this experiment on another offer and made the donation page look more like the original donation page in terms of colors and aesthetics.
We only ran this experiment in one ebook offer funnel and most of the traffic was driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 73%, indicating that the results are not valid.
Though slightly better in results from our previous experiment, we're still seeing a decrease in donor conversion rate which was not expected.
Additionally, we thought that the control might be more successful in converting returning donors because there is a "log-in" feature in the original platform that may be a convenient way for pre-existing donors to give. However, when looking at new vs. returning donors in each segment, there is virtually no impact in conversion rate among each segment.
Ultimately, we believe that there are too many new variables introduced in a completely new platform that could've impacted giving. It may not be that reducing the experience down to 1 step is reducing conversion rate. Other features like not being able to personalize the first name, or including Anedot express giving/Google pay, are features we did not initially account for.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2023 - 03/31/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (showing the thermometer) resulted in a 7.8% increase in donations but with a level of confidence of only 34.8%. The treatment produced 64 donations to the control's 54. Revenue saw a 9% increase through the treatment with a level of confidence of 65.4%. The increases cannot confidently be rolled out for future high urgency campaigns, but it's definitely worth testing again to see if the experiment can reach a confident result.
It'd be interesting to see how the campaign goal factors into results. Would a campaign that nearly reaches the campaign goal be more motivating for users to give? In this case, the total revenue of the campaign was less than half the matching gift goal. So perhaps users weren't as motivated to give because they felt the goal was out of reach. The goal for the next campaign could be adjusted based on historical results to be more achievable.
Interestingly, the experiment produced contrasting results through desktop and mobile. Desktop saw a 24.3% decrease in donations from the treatment (86% loc) compared to a 58.7% increase in donation on mobile (91% loc). We should test this concept again, hopefully during a campaign that lasts longer than 11 days.

experiments.title: How an alternate motive conveyed in the course landing page headline impacted course sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the headline on personal application and self-improvement for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because visitors will be more interested in how the course can improve their life in faith rather than deepening their knowledge of a book of the bible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2023 - 03/26/2023
The Dallas Theological Seminary’s James course landing page is producing a 19.1% sign-up rate and $7.57 gross cost per name – both metrics are missing their benchmark. In an attempt to improve these results, we’ve developed a landing page test that tests what the user hopes to get out of the course.
Looking at the current headline, we wonder if it effectively speaks to the user’s desire. Do they want to deepen their knowledge of a book of the bible or do they want to take the overall message of the course and apply it to their life in faith? Are they looking for knowledge or self-improvement?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version that emphasized action and how the course could improve the visitor's life in faith resulted in a 3.5% increase in course signups, albeit with a statistical level of confidence of only 61.9%. This means that the findings cannot be deemed significant or conclusive for future decision-making.
Moving further down the funnel, the treatment lifted donation conversion by 6.2% but with a 16.6% level of confidence. And revenue experienced a 16% decrease through the treatment with a 70% level of confidence. None of the metrics validated through this test.
The key learning from this experiment is that a headline that emphasizes personal application and self-improvement could increase course signup rates, but the data overall is not consistent or significant enough to determine an optimal approach through this headline test. The collective audience in the test did not lean favorably in either direction when it comes to desirability or motivation to sign up for the course. The next test we run on this page should have more significant changes to trigger more conversions and a more significant level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a simplified Facebook image impacts click converstions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by simplifying the image and removing friction  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve higher click conversion because the ad will highlight the important aspects of the sermon.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks, the Facebook ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image that has Dr. Jeffress’s image removed and the sermon title removed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment tested the effectiveness of using an ad with a simplified image and message versus an ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress. The hypothesis was that the simplified ad would achieve higher click conversions by reducing friction. However, the results showed that the control ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress performed better, with a 4.3% increase in clicks for all traffic at a 61.1% level of confidence.
Since the level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, the results are not considered valid, and future decisions should not be based on them. There are some potential insights that can be gleaned from the experiment, such as the effectiveness of including details about the sermon and featuring Dr. Jeffress in the ad. Future experiments could build upon these insights to test different messaging and images to see if they can improve ad performance.

experiments.title: How positive messaging and imagery on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the dark photo and showing direct photos of multiple children for potential donors will achieve a higher conversion rate because we are sharing more descriptive and specific information about the children who are being served.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2023 - 03/06/2023
Prison Fellowship International works to transform the lives of prisons, their families and victims through a global network of community partners. In recent months we have been showcasing the support a child program for the children of prisoners that is more general that 1:1 child sponsorship. Programmatically it costs less to maintain while allowing more children to participate. The ads were not converting as many donors as we wanted to see. In an effort to increase the donations and revenue, we redesigned the donation page to remove a darker image of a child alone in a house and replaced it with child photos of them looking at the camera and gave details about children in the program.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing a darker image of a child alone in a house and replacing it with more upbeat photos of children and sharing details about their lives did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic. We learned that a significantly large gift had been given in error to the treatment, which means that the conversion rate was actually .60% which is a decrease of 25.8% with a 40% LOC and not .70% as shown above. It is important to note that there was only a 2 gift difference between the control and treatment and when the large gift is removed the treatment received a $30.80 average gift compared to $12.14 shown in the control.

experiments.title: How the Learn More vs. Donate Button on Facebook Led to a Higher Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the “Learn More” vs. the “Donate Now” button on Facebook for potential donors  will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because asking the potential donor to Learn More vs. immediately donate reduces friction..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2023
Prison Fellowship International works to transform the lives of prisons, their families and victims through a global network of community partners.
