In recent months we have been showcasing the support a child program for the children of prisoners that is more general than 1:1 child sponsorship.  Programmatically it costs less to maintain while allowing more children to participate.  The ads were not converting as many donors as we wanted to see. The current ads running on Facebook were asking the potential donor to select the “Donate Now” button to move to the Donation Page sharing additional information.  We have used the “Learn More” button in Facebook and achieved higher results.  We conducted an A/B test using the Learn More Button vs. Donate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that using the "Learn More" button instead of the "Donate Now" button on Facebook for potential donors may lead to a higher donor conversion rate due to reduced friction. The treatment resulted in a 14.5% increase in donations for all traffic, although the statistical level of confidence was 22% and below the 85% necessary to validate the experiment. Because the overall traffic is low, we recommend moving forward with the treatment as it did produce a higher conversion rate and we have used this as a control button on child sponsorship.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by shortening survey length impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the length of a survey will increase email acquisition rates by reducing friction and capitalizing on the cognitive momentum created by the ad that sends traffic to the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2023 - 03/17/2023
When reviewing results of a survey offer funnel, we observed a solid click-through rate, a low cost per click, and a fair instant conversion rate indicating that this is an offer and an issue people care to engage with. However, we identified lower-than-average email acquisition rates as an opportunity to improve overall results. After reviewing this campaign, we believe that the length of the survey was introducing significant friction and causing visitors to abandon. In all, the survey contains 20 questions. In a previous experiment, we were able to produce a sizable lift in survey completions by paginating the survey, rather than presenting all 20 questions on one page. However, over time, email acquisition was still struggling for this offer and we believe that its length was causing the cognitive momentum in landing page visitors to fizzle out. To test this theory, we built a treatment that truncated the survey to ~5 questions all presented on one page to reduce friction/cost of completing survey in effort to improve email acquisition. We split traffic 50/50 between the two versions and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month we saw a 0.86% increase in conversion rate with 12.4% level of confidence. However, we learned that not all friction is bad friction because we saw a significant decrease in Donations and Revenue, with a 100% and 98% level of confidence, respectively. This suggests that reducing the length of the survey may have a negative impact on the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. When reviewing downstream effects on donations, the treatment produced a 90% decrease in gifts with 99% level of confidence. We believe this happened because the more time that was invested in the survey, the more willing the visitor was to convert on the instant donation page. This means the control version with a lengthier survey was actually producing more qualified traffic to the donation page. Because the results were overall flat at the landing page level, we will need to do additional testing to improve email acquisition rate while still maintaining the quality of the visitor at the donation page level. In future testing, we could add additional elements to the process to let visitors know how many steps there are to complete the survey and display their progress along the way so they have more clarity around how long it will take them to finish the survey and submit their responses. It is important to note that we saw a significant increase in email acquisition rates for Housefile Email visitors, suggesting that the treatment may have a positive impact on visitors who are already familiar with our organization. Future experimentation should focus on finding a balance between reducing friction and maintaining the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. We can also consider targeting Housefile Email visitors with a truncated survey to capitalize on their familiarity with our organization and potentially increase email acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How increasing the median amount of a one-time array affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array amounts  for lamplighter donors will achieve an increase in overall giving without affecting recurring donors and overall donor conversions  because  the increased donation amount doesn’t affect their decision to give a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 02/23/2023
Potential donors for Prison Fellowship’s Lamplighters are presented with a tabbed donation form and given the option to give a one time gift or a recurring monthly gift.  The control donation form provides the gift arrays options of $15 (one prisoner)/$60 (4 prisoners)/$165 for an entire class.
We hypothesized that by increasing the median gift array option presented that there might be the potential to maintain the donor conversion rate and raise revenue without affecting recurring donors.   The tested gift array options for the treatment started at $30 to help 2 prisoners/$90 (6 prisoners)/$165 for an entire class.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the gift array options presented to potential donors of Prison Fellowship's Lamplighters program led to a decrease in overall number of donors; however there was essentially no effect on recurring donors in the control vs. the treatment.  As expected there was increased revenue for the treatment which presented the higher gift array including a large gift of $4,971.  When removing the large gift, the treatment received a 13% increase in revenue.
When separating out the one time and recurring donors and eliminating the large gift, the test validated for all donors and one time donors, but did not validate for recurring donors.  There was essentially no effect on recurring donors.  There were 14 recurring donors in the control and 12 in the treatment; the average gift for the recurring donors in the treatment was slightly higher - $37 vs. $33 in the control.
When focusing on one time donors we see that treatment resulted in 21% fewer donors with 20% more revenue.  Interestingly both the Control and the Treatment garnered 4 gifts of $165.  The Treatment did have 2 - $200 gifts which that plus 3 gifts in the $90 range resulted in the slight lift in revenue for the treatment's one time donors.
The higher conversion rate resulting in 25% more donors  indicates the control as the winner even considering the lower revenue that was generated.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to an online education program sign up page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a program sign up page for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will have a better understanding of the program and the value it provides.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 03/17/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute has a program called Little Patriots that offers lessons, activities, videos, cartoons, workbooks and more tailored toward children to teach them the story of America. Parents and teachers can sign up on behalf of their children to gain access to this content, and they would do so through the Little Patriots sign up page.
Currently, this page contains just a standard sign up form – it contains no value proposition copy and the headline simply says “REGISTRATION”. We wondered if the implementation of value proposition copy coupled with a descriptive call to action headline would lead to an increase in Little Patriots program sign ups. We’ve run countless tests that support this approach. Audiences typically respond well to value proposition and descriptive call to actions because the elements present value/benefit to the reader/prospect and gives them a clear path to the intended action. Let’s see how the approach works in this scenario…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 1.7% lift in email conversion rate with just a 22.6% level of confidence. The results do not dictate that the treatment should be rolled out or applied to the page moving forward. As the vast majority of page visitors come from the Little Patriots home page, there is a good chance they already have a strong understanding of the program and its benefits and do not need the help of value proposition copy on the registration page to convince them to register. In other words, the value is already clear in their minds and registering is just a formality.
The evidence is in the results. The treatment produced a 2.1% decrease in sign ups among returning visitors while new visitors produced a 1.9% lift. Still, the 1.9% lift only had a 22.5% level of confidence.
Paid social traffic produced a 9.6% decrease in email sign ups from the treatment, which could be explained by value being communicated to the user at both the Facebook ad level and the Little Patriots home page before they reach the sign up page.
Ultimately, value proposition may not be the most urgent focus for improving sing up rate. There are other approaches/elements we can try along the lines of social proof or credibility.

experiments.title: How conversational headers on a donation form impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating conversational headers for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because people will treat purely digital experiences like they are alive in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/09/2022
When reviewing a donation form post-petition, we realized the conversational nature of the donation page language ended abruptly once we began introducing different sections of the forms with headers. The form was broken up by standard titles “Donation amount” “Enter your information” “Credit card information” but we wondered if treating these steps in the process as a place to continue the conversation with the donor would help humanize the process and improve donor conversion on the page. We developed a treatment that guided visitors through each step on the page. We introduced the following treatment headers “Step 1: Would you like to make a one-time donation or support this work monthly?” “Step 2: How much would you like to give?” “Step 3: What is your billing information?” and “Step 4: What’s your payment information?” and split traffic 50/50 across a handful of donation forms.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that creating conversational headers on a donation page can result in a small increase in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for the overall traffic was not significant enough to be considered valid. A more significant learning is that the treatment had a significant impact on gender and specific visitor groups. Female visitors had a substantial increase in donations while other visitors had a significant decrease. This information can be used to inform future experiments by emphasizing conversational language for female visitors and potentially exploring other treatment options for other visitor groups. It is essential to note that further testing would need to be done to validate these findings and make data-driven decisions.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How eliminating a confirmation message post-survey impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that drawing attention to the donation page copy for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will keep their eyes on the donation page longer rather than leaving immediately upon arrival.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
After submitting a survey that led to an instant donation page, we wondered if people were reading the confirmation message “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” and automatically thinking they had no other action to take on the page and they could click to exit without reading the donation ask immediately following their submission. We developed a treatment that removed the confirmation message and instead led with the headline “One More Thing…” to indicate that their participation in the process was not fully complete. We still mention in the opening paragraph that they filled out the survey and thank them for their time and input. We ran these two versions side by side and measured the impact on donor conversion over a 10 day period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the A/B experiment showed that the treatment version, which removed the confirmation message and led with the headline "One More Thing", had a 10.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control version, with a statistical level of confidence of 40.2%. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results suggest that drawing attention to the donation page copy can increase donor conversion rate by keeping their eyes on the page longer.

In future experimentation, we can try to modify the treatment version and test it again to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence. We can also try to identify other factors that might affect donor conversion rate and run further experiments to optimize the donation process. It is essential to keep testing and optimizing the donation page and messaging to increase the number of donations and maximize the impact of the nonprofit's work.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How does removing an inline image from an instant donation page affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an inline image on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by eliminating distractions, reducing friction, and focusing the reader’s attention on the content of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2023 - 03/09/2023
This organization has traditionally included inline images on its instant donation pages. We wanted to know if this might be creating friction by distracting the prospective donor from the appeal and interrupting their momentum to make a donation—or if the image was effectively working as a supporting element to add context and value to the appeal.
We wanted to test this hypothesis on a single offer to see if we could obtain a large enough sample size to attain statistically valid results before applying it to other offers. This experiment was set up as an A/B test with both the control (with image) and treatment (sans image) receiving roughly 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 24% lift in conversions by removing the inline image. However, these statistical results are deceiving because that 24% lift accounted for only one additional donation since the sample size was so low. The low sample size also prevented us from validating our hypothesis, producing only a 35% level of statistical confidence in these results.
Nevertheless, it is worth noting that desktop visitors had a 5.6% increase in donation rates, with a 96% level of confidence, which suggests that removing the inline image may be more effective for desktop users. Conversely, removing the image resulted in a 40% decrease in donations for mobile users, with a 61% level of confidence.
Due to the very small sample size achieved during this experiment, and because the inclusion of an inline image on instant donation pages is something this organization applies across the board, we are for now going to leave the image in place and test this hypothesis at a later date in the context of a revamped strategy to test our hypothesis across all instant donation pages in an effort to produce larger sample sizes and obtain more statistically reliable results.

experiments.title: How a lock icon on a donation page impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the lock icon for prospective donors will achieve higher donor conversion because the perceived security has been increased.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2023 - 03/13/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
In an effort to increase donor conversion on donation pages, we decided to test the presence of a lock icon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lock icon to the donation page can lead to an increase in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence is only at 43.2%, which is below the threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not valid for future decision making. Therefore, it is necessary to run further experiments with an adequate sample size to validate these findings. Additionally, future experiments should consider testing other elements on the donation page that can lead to an increase in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a “donors needed” countdown affects donation rates during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with a “donors still needed” countdown timer during a recurring donor high-urgency campaign will achieve donation rates because of increased urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2023 - 03/11/2023
In the context of a high-urgency campaign focused specifically on obtaining recurring monthly donors, we wanted to know how a sticky bar that dynamically populated a “donors still needed” countdown timer to increase urgency would affect donation rates.
The countdown was designed to automatically update donor counts (recurring donors only). We wanted to know how this would perform against the “wiki-banner” containing a simplified donation form that they have traditionally used.
We set this test up as a multi-variant experiment testing to versions of copy in the “countdown sticky”, with the control and both treatments all receiving roughly 1/3 of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for eight days, treatment A produced an 8.7% decrease in recurring donations (with a 30.7% level of confidence) and a 2.1% increase in overall donations (14.7% level of confidence); while treatment B produced a 7.2% increase in monthly donations (with a 24.6% level of confidence) and a .78% increase in overall donations (5.5% level of confidence). Because of these flat results and the unlikelihood of this experiment validating, we decided to end the experiment after just over a week.
We believe the low level of confidence was likely a result of running two treatments, while the flat results indicate that the wiki-banner's ability to produce a micro-commitment by having the prospective donor begin the process before landing on the donation page was motivating more or at least as many people to give.
However, we did make a significant observation while analyzing these results: for direct traffic—which accounted for the majority of traffic—treatment A did produce a 50.7% lift in overall donations (86.6% level of confidence) while treatment B produced a 26.4% lift in donations (58.6% level of confidence).
Because of this observation, we do believe that the urgency created by presenting a countdown of 'donors still needed' does play a role in inspiring people to give by at the very least prompting them to make a lesser commitment in the form of a one-time gift.
Therefore, we are planning to follow this experiment up with an A/B test that tests the current wiki-banner w/ donation form against a treatment with the higher-urgency copy and countdown functionality to see if combining the two will produce a significant lift.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition to focus on “more content like this” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the value proposition on “more content like this” for study guide downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because specificity helps decrease cognitive friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2023 - 02/27/2023
Luther Seminary was getting great responses for their free study guide on the book of Revelation in terms of email signups. However, they wanted to see if they could increase their instant donor conversion rate.
In reviewing the value proposition on their donation page, they noticed that the reasons to give that were given were very high-level, wide-ranging, and broad. They hypothesized that if they focused the value proposition on just one element—particularly, producing more biblical resources like what the user had just signed up for, they could improve donor conversion rate.
So, they drafted a new donation page with this “micro-value-proposition” and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a month, the treatment page saw a 93.2% increase in donor conversion rate with a 76.9% level of confidence.

This experiment demonstrated that Luther Seminary might significantly increase its donation rate by focusing the value proposition on a single element — in this case, the biblical resources it promotes.

*However, it must be noted that this experiment was limited in its promotion due to budget, and thus only 10 total donations were included. While the gap in conversion rates was significant, it did not reach NextAfter's desired threshold of 50 donations.

experiments.title: How relevant images impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image from the landing page for petition signers will achieve an increase in signature conversions because there would be less friction on the page by removing a distracting or emotional element.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 01/15/2023
On a petition to repeal beer tax, we wondered if the image on the landing page was distracting or dissuading visitors from completing the petition. We created a treatment that removed the image altogether, and developed a second treatment that portrayed the politician the petition would be submitted to. We split traffic between the three versions and let the experiment run for 5 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing an image from the landing page did not result in an increase in signature conversions. In fact, there was a slight decrease in the number of sign-ups. However, this result cannot be considered valid due to a low statistical level of confidence. It is also important to note that there were significant differences in sign-up rates among different devices and traffic sources. Desktop visitors showed a significant decrease in sign-ups, while tablet and email visitors showed a significant increase.

In future experimentation, it would be important to examine these differences among devices and traffic sources and to tailor the landing page accordingly. It may also be useful to test different messaging strategies to see if there is a specific type of language or tone that resonates better with potential signers. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of careful testing and analysis to ensure that decisions are informed by valid and reliable data.

experiments.title: How a simplified image impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the content on the image for Facebook viewers will achieve an increase in clicks because it is less friction and simplified information.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image of only the sermon series title.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment indicates that simplifying language and images on Facebook ads may not be the best way to achieve increased clicks. Our hypothesis was that by reducing information on the image and content, Facebook viewer engagement would increase. However, as demonstrated in this experiment, clicks were actually 6.1% lower when a simplified image of only the sermon series title was used.
It is important to remember that although the control won in this experiment, the statistical confidence was too low to be considered valid. This means the experiment has not completely invalidated our hypothesis and the test of simpler ad copy and imagery may still be worth experimenting with again in the future.
As a result, the key learning from this experiment is that more experimentation should be done in regards to messaging, images, and ad copy in order to find out what performs best. It is important to conduct experiments often and on a consistent basis in order to stay updated on changes in Facebook ad performance and understand what works best at any given time.

experiments.title: How humanizing an appeal by presenting a specific child’s name, age, location and image for sponsorship affects conversion rates

rows.name: Slider with generic # of sponsors needed

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the featured child will outperform the slider  for users because they will identify with specific child details, more than a generic number of children in need of a sponsor, which will achieve a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2022 - 10/12/2022
We wanted to test a slide out with a specific number of children left in need of sponsorship by midnight vs. a (rotating) specific child above the search functionality that a potential donor could choose to sponsor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to declines in donor conversion and RPV.
• Donor conversion was lower for variant (-7%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-33%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slide.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy language to an acquisition offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement underneath the donation button for visitors to the donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because  the privacy statement adds perceived security to the form and reduces anxiety about how the donor’s information will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 02/24/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They run several different acquisition offers to attract donors on Facebook. We decided to run a series of tests to determine the impact of adding a statement promising to protect their privacy on the landing pages and donation pages.  This was the second test in the series where we examined the impact this disclaimer would have on their acquisition offer donation pages.
In our first test on the landing page, we saw that people who had the privacy and communication statement had a higher donor conversion rate than those who did not. We wanted to see if adding the same statement to the donation page would have the same effect. Could it raise donor conversion or even increase the average gift because of the added sense of security and transparency?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the privacy statement under the donate button resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion and revenue.
Donor revenue increased by 63% with a 94% Level of Confidence. This could be due to adding the perceived sense of privacy, the transparency of what the donors are signing up for, or both.
The donor conversion rate increased as well by 37% with a 75% LOC, this experiment will need to be retested to see if the donor conversion rate would have been able to reach validity with a larger sample size.
The increase in donations observed during this experiment suggests that adding extra assurances and anticipating objections pertaining to how sensitive personal information is handled at the point of the transaction can have a positive effect on donation rates by reinforcing the security of the donation and conveying transparency in how the donor's information will be used.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by adding a countdown timer to a slide-out affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out variant on the child list page on all devices to determine if adding a countdown clock to our original # of kids still needing to be sponsored would increase the conversion rates with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.
• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).
• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.
• We could potentially run this test during a high urgency campaign to see if it pulls a more valid response.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including the word “now” to a donate button affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve greater conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/19/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, there will be an increase in conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Click rate on the donate button was higher for the variant (11%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (24%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (95%).
• RPV was slightly higher for the variant (1%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (53%).
• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (79%).
• One-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (58%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (40%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (85%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (69%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (96%).
• As the hero already uses "Donate Now", we recommend not changing the main donate button despite the significant life in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How reducing the options on a gift array impacted response from non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array options for nondonors will achieve an increase in instant donor conversion because we are eliminating friction in the decision making process by offering one clear choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 01/15/2023
On an instant donation page during a year-end survey, we wanted to learn if eliminating friction by reducing the decision making process at the gift array level would help us get more non-donors to say “yes” when we asked for a gift. Rather than making them decide if they want to give $10, $25, $50, $100, $250, or “Other” we developed a treatment that removed all donation amounts other than “$10” and “Other” to make the ask more clear. We ran equal amounts of traffic through each experience and let the experiment run for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array options for non-donors may not achieve an increase in instant donor conversion. The treatment saw a 5.9% decrease in donations for all traffic with a 73.0% statistical level of confidence, and the results cannot be considered valid.

Moreover, the experiment showed that revenue experienced a 217% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the treatment's strategy has a negative impact on the organization's revenue, which is not ideal for a nonprofit.

While there was a 741% increase in donations from visitors aged 55-64 with a 99% level of confidence, this finding doesn't provide enough evidence that the treatment is an effective strategy. It's also unclear why this age group responded differently to the treatment.

Therefore, before making online donation changes, it's essential to consider that the gift array options available for donors may impact the organization's revenue negatively. We should test several options cautiously and analyze the data correctly. It's imperative to achieve higher statistical levels of confidence to ensure that the test's results are valid and reliable so that we can use them to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How offering one option on the gift array impacted gifts from nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that isolating the ask in the gift array to $10 for nondonors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the ask in the copy is more aligned with the options presented in the first step of the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2023 - 02/17/2023
In a series of testing gift array options, we wanted to better learn how the treatment was impacting conversion rate for non-donors so we continued to locate places to test this concept. One offer was an ask for 400 supporters to chip in $10 to help stop the home equity tax. Since the body copy mentioned specifically a need for $10 donations, we wanted to test if including $10 as the only option on the gift array field would help more non-donors convert to a new donor on this appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this A/B experiment conducted by the Canadian Taxpayers Federation are that isolating the ask in the gift array to $10 for non-donors did not achieve an increase in donor conversion. The treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic, including a 276% decrease for Mobile visitors and a 753% decrease for Direct visitors. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being below 95%, the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact on specific visitor segments, as seen in the significant decreases in donations for Mobile and Direct visitors. It may also be beneficial to test different gift array options, including the use of specific donation amounts versus a range of amounts, to see what resonates best with donors. It is important to continue testing and analyzing results to improve conversion rates and optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of decisions on a gift array impacted overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of decisions on the gift array for our French speaking audience will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be less friction in decision making for the visitors when they make their selection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 12/31/2022
While testing the impact of gift array choices, we wanted to see if the French-speaking audience saw similar or different results than the English-speaking audience. We created one treatment that kept the choice between one time and monthly giving yet reduced the gift array choice to just $10 or “Other” and we created a second treatment that removed the choice for monthly giving and just asked for one-time gifts of $10 using a single gift array. We split traffic 3 ways between the control and the treatment and ran the experiment for 9 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array options for French-speaking audiences resulted in an increase in donations. However, we cannot confidently validate this result as the statistical level of confidence is below 95%. Moreover, the experiment showed a significant decrease in revenue, which raises concerns about the overall impact of limiting gift array options. In future experimentation, we should consider testing different gift array configurations and analyzing the impact on both donation rates and revenue to make better-informed decisions about gift array options for different audiences. It is also important to ensure that experiments are run long enough to get reliable results and to always perform a proper statistical analysis to avoid flawed conclusions.

experiments.title: How reducing the gift array choices on a video campaign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the ask in the copy with the ask in the gift array for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the gift array matches up with the original ask in the body copy on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of ongoing testing, we wanted to find out if the amount of choices on a gift array was helping or hindering performance. In this case, we were asking for visitors to chip in $10 if they liked the video so it could be shown to 1,000 more people. Thus, the treatment we developed isolated the gift array options to just $10 or “Other” if they wanted to give more or less than choosing the $10 option. We split traffic between the control and treatment and ran the experiment for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the gift array options can potentially increase donor conversion. However, the statistically significant level of confidence of 45.8% is below the recommended level of 95%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, further experimentation and testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of simplifying gift array options. Future experiments should aim to increase the sample size and statistical power to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that could impact donor conversion, such as the messaging and design of the donation page.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How eliminating gift array choices impacted instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing decision fatigue for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the donor will have to do less work to say yes to what is being asked of them on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 01/15/2023
In a series of testing around gift array presentation, we wanted to know how results would differ for a post-petition instant donation page with mostly acquisition traffic compared to a survey donation page for primarily email visitors. We set up the same treatments for this segment that we had for the others to measure the overall impact of each version. The first treatment removed the extra gift array options, keeping only $10 and other. The second treatment removed the one time vs. monthly decision from the process and kept only the $10 or “Other” options in tact. Traffic was split equally across all 3 treatments and the experiment ran for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing decision fatigue may increase donor conversion rates for instant donation page visitors, but the results cannot be considered valid due to the low statistical level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment had a negative impact on donations from visitors aged 55-64.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential negative effects of simplifying donation options, particularly for specific age groups. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of gift array options and language to determine the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that experiments have a high enough statistical level of confidence to be considered valid before implementing any changes.

experiments.title: How asking for $20 vs. $10 in a single gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the amount of a single gift array choice for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because not only will more people will be saying “yes” and they will be saying “yes” to a higher amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2023 - 02/20/2023
Through previous testing, we were identifying some trends that offering donors one $10 gift array was increasing conversion in some instances so we were interested to learn if the increase in conversion was occurring because of the AMOUNT of the ask or purely the SINGULARITY of the ask. So we developed one treatment with the single array of $10 and created a second treatment that kept all elements the same except made the single gift array a $20 option instead. We split traffic equally across the 3 versions and ran the test for 5 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the amount of a single gift array choice in donation pages can result in a slight increase in donations. However, the results are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid. It's also important to note that the treatment resulted in a significant decrease in donations from male visitors and visitors aged 65+. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to test different gift array amounts and language to determine the best combination for increasing donations without negatively impacting certain visitor demographics. It's also important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to obtain statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How eliminating a confirmation message post-survey impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that drawing attention to the donation page copy for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will keep their eyes on the donation page longer rather than leaving immediately upon arrival.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
After submitting a survey that led to an instant donation page, we wondered if people were reading the confirmation message “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” and automatically thinking they had no other action to take on the page and they could click to exit without reading the donation ask immediately following their submission. We developed a treatment that removed the confirmation message and instead led with the headline “One More Thing…” to indicate that their participation in the process was not fully complete. We still mention in the opening paragraph that they filled out the survey and thank them for their time and input. We ran these two versions side by side and measured the impact on donor conversion over a 10 day period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the A/B experiment showed that the treatment version, which removed the confirmation message and led with the headline "One More Thing", had a 10.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control version, with a statistical level of confidence of 40.2%. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results suggest that drawing attention to the donation page copy can increase donor conversion rate by keeping their eyes on the page longer.
In future experimentation, we can try to modify the treatment version and test it again to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence. We can also try to identify other factors that might affect donor conversion rate and run further experiments to optimize the donation process. It is essential to keep testing and optimizing the donation page and messaging to increase the number of donations and maximize the impact of the nonprofit's work.

experiments.title: How removing the confirmation language for survey takers impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that continuing the conversation post-survey submission for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because more time will be spent reading the donation page language which makes the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
When sending survey takers to an instant donation page upon submission, we wondered if we were signaling in the initial headline “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” that the user did not need to take any further action and could exit the page without continuing to read the donation page language where we ask them to take the next step and make a gift. We removed the confirmation language in the headline and instead sent visitors to a page that said “One More Thing…” after they submit their survey results so they realize they have to continue to be involved in the process and they’re not necessarily finished yet. We did still recognize early on in the copy that they had filled out the survey and thanked them for their response. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 11 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that continuing the conversation with visitors after survey submission can result in an increase in donor conversion. The treatment version, which removed the confirmation language in the headline and directed visitors to a page asking them to take one more step, resulted in a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.7%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experiments, it is recommended to continue exploring ways to engage with visitors beyond the initial confirmation message to encourage more donations. It is important to ensure that statistical confidence levels are high enough to make valid decisions based on the results of any experiments conducted.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security at the point of transaction affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the perceived security at the point-of transaction on a main donation page (by explicitly stating how their payment details will be used) will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and increasing trust/transparency by reassuring potential donors that their sensitive information will be kept private.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 03/03/2023
We know that online security is highly important to general web users, especially when it comes to entering sensitive data like payment information online. So we wanted to test how increasing perceived security at the point of transaction would impact donation rates on a main donation page for a family ministry organization.
In order to draw attention to the security statement below the donate button, we animated the lock icon to catch the eye of potential donors.
We also included a more detailed security statement to communicate that the donation is not only secure but that the donor’s sensitive payment information will also be kept private and will not be used for any other reason than to process the donation.
For this experiment, we split traffic evenly between the treatment explained above and the control which featured a static lock icon and a brief security statement, “Your information is secure.” for a duration of 21 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning gained from this experiment is that making security assurances more visible can significantly increase donations, validating our hypothesis that online security is highly important to web users, especially when it comes to entering their payment information.
This experiment shows that people need to feel secure when making donations and that focusing on reinforcing the security of their transaction will result in higher donation rates. We believe that the treatment anticipated and preemptively addressed a common objection among potential donors, thereby increasing trust and the desire to give to an organization that cares about donors by taking data privacy seriously.
After 21 days, this experiment saw a 26.3% increase in overall donations (99% level of confidence), with a 74.1% increase in revenue.
The treatment also produced a 37.4% increase in donations among desktop visitors (100% level of confidence)--who make up the majority of this organization's donor base.
Significantly, we also observed a 28.2% increase in donations among new visitors (96% level of confidence) and a 31% increase in donations among visitors referred from organic search (98% level of confidence), highlighting the importance of online security to the general population and among traffic that may be less familiar with the organization.
We are unable to draw a precise conclusion about whether the animated lock icon or the more detailed security statement played a more significant role in these results, however both variables apply to the stated strategy of "increasing perceived security" so we do not feel this invalidates the learning.
That being said, future experiments could focus on isolating these two variables and then continuing to refine the design of the donation page to make donors feel confident and secure when donating.

experiments.title: How replacing an “emergency” banner with an “emergency” slide-in affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a greater response than the emergency banner  because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 03/09/2022
Adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage will outperform the emergency banner because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
Numbers in the slide-in module are dynamic and reset every hour. If a user clicks “donate now” and then returns to the homepage within the hour, the number will be lower than what they saw previously. The slide-in module is sticky and stays in view when the user scrolls down the page and can be removed by clicking on the “x” in the top right corner, similar to the emergency banner. Both of these CTA’s are driving to an undesignated GAF form. Copy approved by legal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall increase in donor conversion on the homepage for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (50%).
• Overall increase in RPV on the homepage for the variant (4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (80%).
• Undesignated donor conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.8%).
• Undesignated RPV declined for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (81%).
• Emergency donor conversion was higher for the variant (21%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• Emergency RPV was higher for the variant (14%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (98%).
• A few days into the test, there was an uptick in call center donors who requested their gifts be assigned explicitly to Ukraine.
• We shifted 100% of test traffic to the variant, as these users were more likely to give an emergency gift (potentially reducing call center calls). Shortly after, we shifted from Ukraine designated CEF, which made it difficult to measure the impact of the shift.

experiments.title: How including the word “active” in an email acquisition form headline affects sign-up rates

rows.name: "Join us to learn how you can help make change for children."
"Be an active supporter. Join below to lean more."

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language to “active” text above the homepage email capture module  for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate from users on the site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2022 - 09/06/2022
Changing the language above the homepage email capture module to see if adding active text increases email conversion from users on the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• No significant positive differences in email conversion (by channel, new/return visitor, or location).
• Variant directionally underperformed (-11%) compared to the control (69% LoC).
• Recommendation is not to change the language above the homepage email module.
• Overall email conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at anon-statistically validLoC (69%).
• Mobile email conversion was lower for the variant (-20%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (91%).
• Desktop email conversion was higher for the variant (23%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (65%).
• U.S. email conversion was slightly higher for the variant (7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (38%).
• International email conversion was lower for the variant (-51%) at a statistically valid LoC (99.7%).
• This test aimed to determine whether adding “Active” language above the homepage email module would increase email conversion. There were directional declines in email conversion across mobile, international, and new/ returning visitors. The only conversion lifts were seen for desktop (23%) and U.S.(7%) visitors, both of which were directional.
• We recommend not changing copy above the homepage email module

experiments.title: How simplifying an appeal by removing specific impact equivalences from a donation form affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation dollar amount equivalencies on donation forms for donors will achieve a greater conversion rate because  it’s more direct to the point.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2022 - 08/22/2022
This was tested on both desktop and mobile variants to determine if removing the presence of equivalencies would increase donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Overall donor conversion was lower for the variant (-2%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (32%).
• Overall RPV was higher for the variant (10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (79%).
• Mobile RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (96%).
• Future tests will examine removing the equivalencies exclusively for mobile users and changing them to reflect recurring values instead of one-time.
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (31%) at anon-statistically valid LoC (92%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (16%) at anon-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• One-time donor conversion was lower for the variant (-7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• One-time RPV was higher for the variant (+10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the variant (9%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (66%).
• Mobile RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (96%).
• Mobile recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (50%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (93%).
• Mobile recurring RPV was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (86%).
• Mobile one-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (13%).
• Mobile one-time RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Desktop donor conversion was lower for the variant (-10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (84%).
• Desktop RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (60%).
• Desktop recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).
• Desktop recurring RPV was lower for the variant (-1%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (51%).
• Desktop one-time donor conversion was lower for the variant (-14%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (92%).
• Desktop one-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (59%).
• The data shows that removing equivalencies specifically favored recurring donor conversion on mobile devices.
• Recommendation is not to remove equivalencies on donation forms.

experiments.title: How changing donate button copy to convey greater clarity and urgency affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve an increase in click rate because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 02/23/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, clicks will increase because donors need to act immediately. We examined how different CTA’s affected the click rate on the main donation button. There were four variants with differing language; testing the verbiage of donate v. give v. help in addition to temporal urgency now v. today. This test exclusively measured click rate and did not delve into conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped for lack of significant results after 2 1/2 weeks.
• Lowest performing treatment was "give now" (-1.6%) with a confidence of (9%).
• Best performing treatment was "donate today" (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence of (64%).
• Further tests will examine the impact on donor conversion and click rate.

experiments.title: How communicating the amount of a donation goes to the organization’s mission impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will including the percentage of a donor’s gift that goes directly towards the organization’s mission impact donor conversion and overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/26/2021
Focus on the Family was sending an email appeal and recently adjusted the percentage of a donor’s gift amount that goes directly to ministry versus overhead cost for their organization. Historically, they had never communicated that to their donors. With the new higher percentage of the donor’s gift going directly to their organization, they wanted to test adding this message to the body copy of the donation page to see if they might be able to increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there was no valid lift in donor conversion, we did see a directional increase in overall revenue of 33%! By communicating to people that we are being good stewards of their money and that their gift now goes even further towards ministry impact, donors were even more generous and gave larger gifts than when we didn't communicate that to them. This tells us that this principle is important to donors, but that it doesn't impact the number of donors who convert, but instead it does impact the generosity of their gift.

experiments.title: How adding value with more descriptive impact language affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that improving the value proposition with more descriptive language will achieve a higher click-through rate by clarifying the impact and outcome of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/31/2022 - 09/26/2022
We wanted to know if replacing non-descript, program-focused language with outcome-focused language affected would improve the value proposition in the minds of web traffic and give them more motivation to click through and learn more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that using more descriptive, outcome-focused language to promote a program has the potential to increase click-through rates (CTR) by communicating more value to prospects and thus giving them a better reason to learn more by clicking through.
Treatment one asks "Do you believe children deserve a better future? Join the team" This not only implies the outcome of joining but also aligns the offer with the prospect's values, further compelling them to act and producing a 36.4% increase in click-through rates with a 76% level of confidence.
Treatment two was less specific than the control, simply stating that a monthly donation can make a difference without communicating what that difference is or why it matters and producing an 18% decrease in clicks.
And while the 36.4% increase in clicks created by treatment one was not statistically valid, we know that there is little risk in rolling out a treatment that has shown a lift. We therefore recommend rolling this treatment out to 100% of traffic.

experiments.title: How a premium affects recurring conversion rate on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a premium for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it provides an incentive to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2023 - 02/02/2023
CaringBridge saw strategic value in growing their recurring donor program, and wanted to run an experiment on their main donation page. Through a partnership with Bicycle cards, they had the chance to offer a unique deck of playing cards in exchange for a recurring gift.
They wanted to test this concept to see if it would increase recurring gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 27 days and the results indicate that overall conversion saw a significant increase of 3.4% when adding the premium incentive. However, this result is too small to be considered statistically valid due to a 5.9% level of confidence.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment suggests that recurring donors do not respond positively when provided with incentives. Future experiments should focus on better understanding the motivation as to why people want to give recurring gifts, attempting to tailor incentives in a way that resonates most with their movement. This, in turn, will lead to sustainable increases in conversions and eventually, customer loyalty.

experiments.title: How “rally together” messaging impacted donations for a recurring giving program
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “rally together” messaging for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will want to unite against the threats to Texas independence and be a part of the efforts to combat those threats.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 02/17/2023
A public policy client has struggled to get traction on their recurring giving donation page. The page is producing a 0.29 instant donor conversion rate heading into this experiment. Assessing the current donation page copy, we wondered if it is too general and does not do a good enough job of inciting emotion among potential donors. We also wondered if page visitors would convert at a higher rate if we apply relevant language to the current public policy landscape in Texas and the threats to the people of the state.
We’ll develop a donation page treatment that uses “rally together” language, highlighting specific threats to Texas and its citizens and making it clear that these threats won’t seize unless we come together as one to fight for better policy and safeguard our personal liberties. The copy will reference current threats and conditions that are close to home and close to the heart of conservative Texans.
We expect this messaging to increase motivation and urgency to join the Come and Take It Society as a monthly donor and make a continuous effort to fight for independence in Texas.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither the control nor the treatment reached significance. The treatment led to 5 donations to 4 for the control. Revenue was a tick higher for the treatment compared to the control, but the increase in revenue only had a 67.1% level of confidence. It seems the "rally together" messaging did not motivate potential donors to convert to donors in the way that we hypothesized it would. The messaging could be worth testing through a regular offer in which someone can make either a one-time or monthly donation. However, for the recurring ask, the messaging did not do enough to make potential donors give recurring gifts at a rate in which we could trust rolling out the treatment.

experiments.title: How promoting a specific gift amount impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking existing donors for a specific gift amount for email audience will achieve higher conversion rate because the decision anxiety of how much to donate has been removed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 02/08/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas that shares the truth of God’s Word through radio, television, digital resources, and books.
Each month PTV offers a different book resource to their email file that provides the audience with an opportunity to make a donation and receive the resource as an appreciation gift.
Existing donors on the PTV email file historically receive a nonspecific gift amount asked. In an effort to increase donor conversion, we decided to test a specific ask amount for this audience.
We have conducted similar testing for the nondonor email segment and have seen positive results in providing nondonors with a specific gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On the email level, we did not see any significant or valid directional lifts. However, for donations and revenue, we see an interesting story...
For donations, the control saw 15 donations and 16 donations for the treatment. That calculates to a 5.2% increase with an 11.1% level of confidence.
For revenue, the control brought in $975 with an average gift of $65, and the treatment brought in $789 with an average gift of $49.31.
As a reminder, the treatment asked for a donation of $25 or more while the control was nonspecific.
The transaction break down for the two variants were
Control - nonspecific amount ask
1 - $10 gift3 - $25 gifts1 - $30 gift1 - $35 gift3 - $50 gifts1 - $75 gift3 - $100 gifts2 - $150 gifts
Treatment - $25 ask
2 - $7 gifts7 - $25 gifts4 - $50 gifts2 - $75 gifts1 - $250 gift
For revenue we saw a 13.3% decrease in overall revenue with a 61.6% level of confidence
Surprisingly, we saw that the control donors were more likely to make a gift of $25 or more without being prompted with 11 gifts. While the treatment audience was more likely to stay at the $25 level.
As this audience did not respond as well as to the specific ask as the nondonor segment has, we will begin testing into generic language around value prop and mission of PTV to see if a change will increase response.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the donate button on an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the donation button for instant donation page visitors will increase donations by reducing anxiety around sharing sensitive data online.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2023 - 02/10/2023
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the donation button would affect donation rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of giving would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to give.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to process your gift.”
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for just over two weeks, we observed that the inclusion of a privacy statement had very little impact on potential donors' motivation to give. Furthermore, the low sample size of donations prevented us from achieving an adequate level of statistical confidence in the experiment's results.
While the treatment produced a 5.3% decrease in donations (10% level of confidence), this amounted to just one less donation than the control. Because of the flat and inconclusive results of this experiment, we can determine that the statement's inclusion was not a significant enough motivating factor to move the needle, perhaps because the security of the transaction is already adequately conveyed with a visual indication shown around the payment fields.
Based on these results, we will look for other test ideas that may have a larger impact on potential donors' motivation to give.

experiments.title: How revamping the value proposition presented in the headline and body copy with specific, solution-focused language affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that improving the value proposition on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by more effectively communicating the impact of a potential donor’s gift and how it will be achieved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 02/06/2023
In an effort to improve conversion rates on an instant donation page for a public policy client, we wanted to know how stregthening the value proposition by adding a headline and solution-focused body copy would impact potential donors’ motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 14.3% increase in donations (30.7% level of confidence) and a 13.2% increase in overall revenue (62% level of confidence), however, we did not receive a large enough sample size of donations to reach statistically valid conclusions about our results. That being said, because the treatment did not have a negative effect on donations or revenue, we have recommended rolling out the treatment to all donation page traffic.

It is significant to note that among visitors obtained through paid social media (the primary acquisition source for this offer), we observed a 121.5% increase in donations with a 91.0% level of confidence.

This leads us to believe that the conclusion of a value prop-driven headline and body copy that is solution-focused and specific about why a donation is needed, what it will accomplish, and how increases the motivation for potential donors to give. Based on these results we will look for other opportunities to improve the value proposition on donation pages in an effort to increase donation rates.

experiments.title: How does more solution-focused copy on an instant donation page affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that solution-focused copy for instant donation page visitors will achieve higher donation rates because it provides a potential outcome for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2023 - 02/06/2023
We recently ran an experiment testing how agitating the problem on an instant donation page for an acquisition offer about Big Tech accountability would affect donation rates and found that the treatment did not outperform the control as hypothesized. So we wanted to follow up this test with a new treatment that instead focused on the solutions the donor’s gift will achieve.
For this experiment, we focused exclusively on solutions and gave specific examples of some of the initiatives that the donor’s gift would help support. This was set up as an A/B test with ~50% of traffic seeing the control and ~50% of traffic seeing the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed an 18.2% increase in donations—but a small sample size prevented us from validating these results, yielding only a 32.5% level of confidence. When looking at the results, we observed that the treatment only produced one additional donation while also producing a 25% decrease in revenue with a 72.4% level of confidence.
The low level of confidence coupled with the decrease in donations prevents us from drawing reliable conclusions about this experiment and we will therefore look for another opportunity to test this concept to see if we can get a valid learning.

experiments.title: How a lower monthly ask impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by decreasing the monthly amount to $10 for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because the lower amount is more attainable and less of a financial sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 02/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Church Dallas. Through radio, tv, digital efforts, and resources Pathway to Victory shares the truth of God’s word with people worldwide.
In January 2023, we emphasized Pathway to Victory’s Pathway Partner program. This is a recurring gift program where the donor makes a monthly or annual gift to PTV and receives resources each month and exclusive access to a library of sermons and other incentives.
Traditionally the entry Pathway Partner monthly amount has been $25. We decided to test an entry of $10 to see if this would increase the number of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall we only saw a 9.4% increase in conversions with a 47.2% level of confidence.
