In revenue, we saw a small decrease in the average gift but that is not surprising due to the lower ask of the treatment.
The average PTV audience falls within the 65+ age range, however we saw that 35-44 visitors had a 415.9% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence (1 vs 5 donations). While this was not the purpose of this test, activating a younger audience, it is interesting to see that impact.
We will continue testing into how we can convert more people to become Pathway Partners.

experiments.title: How modeling ad targeting after just your most qualified contacts impacts qualified lead generation results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that modeling after our most highly qualified contacts for our target audience will achieve an increase in qualified leads generated (and/or decrease our cost per new qualified lead generated) because it will be based upon a better source list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 01/31/2023
As a part of our recent experimentation with our own acquisition efforts, we decided to try specifically modeling a lookalike audience in Facebook (Meta) Advertising based upon our most engaged/best qualified contacts in the database, rather than our control approach (which is a mixture of interest-based targeting, lookalike models from our overall email database, and directly uploaded prospecting lists).
Would this improve the overall volume of the new qualified leads (and decrease the cost per qualified lead)?
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a month of experimenting with this hypothesis, we were unable to fully validate this concept, although we have some directional data that suggests that it is not necessary to model your prospecting audience within Facebook (Meta) Advertising solely based upon your most qualified contacts.
It's worth documenting some other movement on KPI's as a part of this study, although it did not validate.
Those are:
The control list had a 41% lower cost per qualified lead generated when compared to the treatment list.The control list generated 8% less "generic email domain" leads when compared to the treatment list.The control list did have a slightly higher cost per lead generated (+5.3%) when compared to the treatment list.
Conclusion:
Our hypothesis as to "why" this is that it could simply be due to pure audience size — whereas the "control" list was 41.9% larger than that of the "Qualified Contact Lookalike" list used within the control. This manifests itself in the CPM (or "Cost per Thousand Impressions Served") metric — which was 11.9% less expensive for the larger control list when compared to the treatment.
With all of that said, this evidence suggests that it's not worth the effort to model just off of your most qualified contacts within Facebook (Meta) Advertising, at least as it pertains to our own B2B marketing campaigns. It may differ within a donor program, and is worth further experimentation in that regard.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a premium of Navigating Genesis for users who already downloaded OT questions for free will achieve a higher ROI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2022 - 01/26/2023
RTB was running a free offer for their Old Testament Questions eBook, in an effort to improve the ROI we tested a premium offer for another ebook, Navigating Genesis on the instant donation page for Old Testament Questions. For a gift of any amount, the donor would receive a copy of this premium, Navigating Genesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Instead of an increased ROI, we actually saw a directional decrease in the average gift but with a total of only 9 donations, this experiment does not validate. A potential area to test is the exact wording of the premium offer. The options are either to be sent the Navigating Genesis offer, OR what is pre-selected is "No, I'd like to maximize my gift." My thought is that this creates conflict in the donor's mind of what their action should be.
On one hand, there's this generosity of the organization saying "we want to thank you with a gift," but also insinuating that they shouldn't accept it if they actually want their donation to be maximized. As a result, the donor begins weighing what they should actually do, deliberating over it too much and just deciding to exit due to the friction.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 75.4% decrease in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 73.0% decrease in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Email visitors had an 83.3% decrease in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How giving is impacted by the use of buttons in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation buttons from an email appeal for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
RTB historically uses multiple “Give Now” buttons in email appeals. During calendar year end we decided to run an experiment where we removed the buttons from the email appeal. The idea is that the removal of the buttons would actually humanize the communication more, make the email seem less promotion-like, and encourage people to read the entire appeal/case for support before deciding to give. This in turn, has the potential to increase donor conversion rate. We chose one email in the campaign to test this concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results in this experiment were flat. We saw .35% decrease in donor conversion rate with a .68% level of confidence. The average gift across both the control and treatment were also negligible (control: $250, treatment: $238) so the humanized treatment did not inspire people to give more either. A concern in removing the buttons from the email was that it would negatively impact click thru rate. However, results indicate that the removal of the buttons has no effect on click thru rate either. Click thru rate stayed the same at .4%.
Where we did see a noticeable difference was the impact on new donors. New donors experienced a 497% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 94%.  More non donors were activated to give through the treatment (no buttons) than the control. While it's not a large number of transactions, the organization should consider using this new layout (no buttons) knowing that it will not impact giving overall but may active more new donors. It's likely that non donors saw the email as less transactional/promotional and decided to read more of the appeal and were therefore more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How a forward style email in a high urgency campaign affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a forward style email  for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
In an effort to continue to humanize communications during a high urgency campaign, we wanted to use a “forward” style email in one of our email appeals. This email would refer to a previous email and make it seem as if the email was forwarded to the recipient. The control for this experiment had the intended, original email (a message from the President). The treatment was also a message from the President but it asked the email recipient to refer to the previous email sent.
In both of these emails previous donors were suppressed so as not to accidentally include those who may have given in the previous email. The email tested was an evening email on the last day of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 3.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6.69%. This means that this experiment had almost no effect on donor conversion rate. The control seemingly has a higher average gift that is skewed by a $3k gift. There is also no noticeable change in click thru rate. Our hypothesis is that because this was tested in an email that's in the "high urgency" phase of the campaign, motivation to give before the deadline is already high. It's recommended that this is retested at a different phase in a campaign to more clearly discern motivation.

experiments.title: How personalizing a Dear Reader during a year-end campaign impacted conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a dear reader for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 01/04/2023
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com and their blog pages, which were being promoted through a 3-times-a-week enewsletter, and see whether adding a Dear Reader donation ask would help drive more converisons.
For this experiment, we ran a 50/50 test to see whether a ‘Note to Readers’ versus a more personalized ‘Note from Dan (Burke, the founder of Avila) made a difference in conversion rates. The only difference between the two was the change in headline and adding the image and signature of Dan Burke.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we had previously tested whether adding a Dear Reader had an impact on donations, this test was to see whether a more personalized approach would increase conversion rates. And it did: the Note from Dan version, which read like a personal letter from Dan, had a 19% increase in donation conversions with a 72% level of confidence. While not reaching the level of validity to decisively call a winner in this test, the higher level of confidence does provide a directional result.
Our hypothesis is that because Dan is so visible and tied into the founding of Avila, readers of the blog posts were more likely to recognize him and respond to a personal appeal from Dan to support Avila during their year-end campaign.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to donation pages during a year-end campaign impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because it gives the organization a “face” which makes it more relatable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/23/2022
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and see whether adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page would impact conversion rates.
This sticky bar ran on all donation pages for 10 days; following this period we launched it across the entire site.
Our hypothesis was that by adding the personalized note from Dan, we would see a change in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment actually led to a decrease in conversion rates on the donation form, with 16.8% less donations and a 68% level of confidence. We believe that this was caused by a number of factors:
The placement of the sticky bar was not optimalThe copy on the sticky bar did not tie into the overall messaging during the year-end campaignIt potentially added a friction point for those donors who were ready to donate (since they already landed on the donation form)
Future iterations of this test will experiment with whether different variations of a sticky bar, placement, and styles, will have a more positive impact.

experiments.title: How adding a middle giving option to the gift array impacted donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a third giving option as an “in-between” amount for monthly and one-time giving will achieve an increase in donor revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/16/2022 - 01/25/2023
The PFI Lamplighters program gives people the opportunity to support prisoners in ways that help them become closer to God. The current one-time and monthly giving options for Lamplighters are $15 to support one prisoner and $165 to support an entire class. We hypothesized that adding a middle amount as a giving option would reduce giving friction, giving donors an additional option that might nestle in nicely between an amount that is too low or not impactful enough and an amount that is too high.
To test this hypothesis, we added a $60 giving option to support four prisoners. We tested this concept already but only for monthly giving. The test resulted in a valid lift in revenue for the treatment with an average gift twice the amount of the control. The average gift for the treatment was $61.52, implying that the added $60 giving option was a common selection among donors.
Let’s see how the concept performed when testing it for monthly AND one-time giving…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The impact on revenue was minimal this time, with the treatment experiencing a 0.71% decrease in revenue but with only a 51% level of confidence. The average gift for the treatment was $39.08 and $46.95 for the control. You'll recall that when we ran the experiment only for monthly giving, the average gift was just over $60.
This tells us that adding the $60 giving option to the one-time giving gift array did not influence as much giving at that giving level as when we added it as an option only for monthly giving.
However, we saw a much greater impact in overall donations. The treatment experienced a 21% lift in total donations at an 89.3% level of confidence. Interestingly enough, the experiment specifically for monthly giving resulted in a slight decrease in donations but a significant increase in revenue.
Having three giving options "low, "middle", and "high" amounts likely reduced donation friction as potential donors did not feel the pressure of giving too little in terms of impact and too much in terms of money. They had a middle option to provide comfort in choice (a happy medium), helping to reduce anxiety. This led to more donations.
Even if a donor chose to give less than $60, we believe their desire to give period was still stronger because they didn't feel as if their gift was "small" when comparing it to the next level up, which was $60. The psychological effect may be the opposite for a gift array that had only $15 and $165 options. In this case, potential donors may choose not to give at all because a $15 gift in their minds would be too small or insignificant when compared to the other amount of $165. Ultimately, $60 provides a nice reference point for potential donors.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 43.4% increase in revenue with a 95% level of confidence – which contributed the bulk of the experiment traffic.

experiments.title: How adding an additional and different CTA in the PS of an email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional call to action in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During calendar year end RTB wanted to be sure to let their constituents know about additional giving options for their 70+ aged donors. There was concern that too many CTA’s would interfere with donor conversion rate so we tested the P.S. message across two different emails in order to get more data around this decision. Our hypothesis was that an additional, different CTA would negatively impact donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not see a valid result from this experiment, we did see a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 70%. It's recommended that before rolling this out to additional emails that this is tested across several more emails to get a large enough sample size as the data seems to indicate a decrease in donor conversion rate when adding the different CTA in the P.S. of the email.

experiments.title: How does adding a personalized sticky bar to the donation page affect donation rates during a year-end campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar featuring a public personality to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing credibility and humanizing the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/28/2022
During a year-end fundraising campaign, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page would affect donation rates for a public policy organization, especially considering that the personalized message on the sticky bar would be coming from a public personality who is well-known and trusted by the organization’s target audience.
For this experiment, we created a treatment featuring a personal message and photograph of a public figure asking users to ‘stand with me’ by making a donation to the year-end campaign. We set this experiment up as an A/B test wherein ~50% of traffic would see the control (no sticky bar) and ~50% of traffic would see the treatment (including the sticky bar). The experiment ran for 23 days, throughout the bulk of the year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 23 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 12.7% increase in donation rates (with a 79% level of confidence) when compared to the control.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure who is widely trusted by the target audience was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and building credibility into the appeal. This may also have increased the believability of the outcomes being promoted on the donation page.
And though we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate this increase, we know that there is a relatively low risk associated with implementing a treatment that has shown an increase in targeted metrics, so we decided to roll this treatment out to 100% of traffic to wrap up the campaign.

experiments.title: How a countdown timer on a donation page affects donation rates during the end of a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last few days of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing the urgency for the prospect to respond to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how adding a countdown timer to the donation timer during the final four days of the campaign would affect donation rates. We hypothesized that the countdown timer would increase urgency and fear of missing out and encourage more prospects to give before time ran out.
For this brief 4-day experiment, we ran an A/B test with ~50% of traffic seeing the control (no timer) and ~50% of traffic seeing the treatment (countdown timer).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to the small sample size entering this experiment, we were unable to draw statistically valid conclusions about the efficacy of using a countdown timer in the context of a high-urgency campaign. Showing the timer produced a 5.4% decrease in conversion rates with only a 38.2% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that we may not be able to assume that adding a countdown timer to a donation page has a significant impact on donation rates. This could be because it is overdone in the nonprofit fundraising space (thus decreasing its impactfulness), or perhaps because it distracts from the content of the donations page. But due to the low statistical confidence of these results, these are only additional hypotheses and we are unable to draw definitive conclusions one way or the other.
Knowing that there is risk involved with implementing a treatment that produces a decrease in conversions, even if the result is not statistically valid, we will want to test this concept again in an effort to receive an adequate sample size to validate the efficacy of a countdown timer before rolling it out to all traffic during future high-urgency campaigns.

experiments.title: How removing thank you from the headline on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing thank you from the headline on the instant donation page for users who downloaded the free study guide will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2022 - 12/19/2022
Luther Seminary is offering a free study guide to learn about the Book of Proverbs. We had previously seen other organizations test the concept of removing “thank you” from the header of the donation page as that typically ends a user’s thought process when signing up for something or completing a purchase. Usually, when you see the thank you confirmation page, you think to yourself – Okay, I’m done now, so I’m leaving the page.
We predicted that users are leaving the donation page after seeing “thank you” at the very top, so we want to test changing that language to something that lets the user know that they are not done yet.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page that didn't state thank you in the headline saw a 17.8% lift in conversion rate with only a 38.4% level of confidence. Revenue saw a  10.6% lift with only a 60.8% level of confidence.
This experiment was running for over two months therefore we concluded that it was not going to validate either way. We believe this experiment tells us that removing thank you from the headline did not end the user experience like we initially thought it did.
We also ran this same experiment on another study guide for Revelation and saw similar results. Because this did not produce a decrease in donations or revenue, we rolled the treatment page out to 100% of traffic.

experiments.title: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed through our welcome series

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed (from an engagement status) once they made it through the welcome series of emails to onboard them as a new subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/20/2022
Recently, we experimented with using Facebook Lead Ads for our own acquisition campaign efforts, which we discovered that we could increase email signup rates by +131.5% (LoC: 100%), but the real question was “How would these leads perform downstream.”
We marked two check-in points, which were:
To review unsubscribe, bounce, disengagement, and never-opener rates after all leads generated in the experiment have “completed the welcome series.” And,
60-days downstream to see if either lead segment produced a more engaged subscriber well after the welcome series had ended, and they were introduced to 2-months of “live messaging.”
This study is focused on the first of these two check-in points, so we wanted to review how the leads performed now that all leads in the study had enough time to make it through the welcome series of emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that we're through the "welcome series" of messaging, we could not fully validate an increase in email engagement rates, but do see a slight improvement on subscriber engagement from the treatment segment.

This is actually very encouraging news, as it indicates that there is no discernible difference between using the control lead generation experience when compared to using the Facebook Lead Ad Form treatment approach.

To put it another way: The treatment experience has increased email signup rates by +131.5% (with 100% level of confidence), but HAS NOT produced a lower quality of email subscriber (at least through the point where all leads have successfully made it through the "welcome series" for new subscribers).

Perhaps this means that the hypothesis that Facebook Lead Ad Forms generate "bad emails" is debunked — at least in our own case.

We'll continue to monitor performance and look forward to checking back in at the 60-day mark from the point of when these emails were acquired to see if there is any noticeable difference between the quality of the two approaches to generating leads.

experiments.title: How using a micro goal impacts donations during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro goal for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 12/31/2022
During their calendar year-end campaign, the Avila Institute had an overarching goal of raising $300,000 in the month of December. In order to reach this goal, we wanted to implement a campaign-long test on the donor segments of their emails, where the experiment was to see whether asking for $300,000 or asking for 50 donors to step up today would have an impact on conversion rates.
We ran this test across multiple emails throughout the campaign (albeit, not every email sent has this test due to data issues).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our hypothesis, which we've seen in testing a micro goal across other clients, is that by giving a more 'reasonable' number for donors to reach (in this case 50 donors) rather than a larger goal ($300,000), conversion rates would increase.
In the case of this experiment, the impact was not very large, with a 13% increase in conversion rates overall when looking at the control versus treatment list throughout the whole campaign, and a 66% level of confidence.
In the next iteration of this test, we'd run it across every single email on the campaign, including resends, so that we'd get more volume.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer to a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a thermometer to a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
During Giving Tuesday, RTB wanted to include a progress bar on the on-site sticky bar. We wanted to test adding this thermometer in order to determine if it motivates more people to give through the sticky bar. The results of this experiment also determined if the organization would invest time and labor to manually update a thermometer in the sticky bar during the rest of the calendar year end experiment. If we saw significant gains by adding the thermometer, we would invest the time in manually updating the thermometer.
This sticky bar was live across the RTB site. Traffic was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: We initially believed that the thermometer would provide a visual representation of the organization's goal and would motivate more people to give. Results indicate that the thermometer did not have a significant impact. The experiment resulted in a 42% decrease in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 73%. There weren't many transactions through this experiment because it only ran for a couple dates for Giving Tuesday. However, we determined that because we saw a decrease (though invalid), we would not move forward in adding this feature in the rest of the campaign. It's probable that the goal seemed too large to the site visitor and ultimately made the goal feel unattainable by seeing such a large amount in the thermometer.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign name in an email appeal affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a familiar campaign name for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/30/2022
The IMB historically runs a Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign and they communicate this fund by name in all their communications. When developing emails for the year end campaign there was initially no mention of the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. We wanted to see if adding the name of this campaign would impact giving for IMB’s housefile list (who have historically known about this campaign). We tested this verbiage in one email appeal in their campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 15% increase in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of 35%. However, when looking at the impact on revenue, we saw a 360% directional lift with a level of confidence of 93%. However, there were several larger $1k+ gifts in the treatment. Nonetheless, this could be an indication that those who are familiar with the Lottie Moon offering may give larger gifts because of the familiarity of this campaign. It should also be noted that mid-level and major donors were suppressed from this campaign so it is likely that some donors upgraded their giving in this appeal and gave larger gifts than they typically would. It is recommended that this is tested across multiple emails in the future in order to confirm that while it may not affect donor conversion rate, it may impact the average gift in an appeal.

experiments.title: How a email subject line drives clicks

rows.name: 10 Core Beliefs for Standing Strong in a Shifting World
Brand-new book "What Every Christian Should Know"

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding clarity to the subject line for the housefile will achieve an increase in click conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth. In that effort, each month PTV shares a book or resource to equip Christians to share the truth of God.
The premium for January 2023 is a brand-new book by Dr. Robert Jeffress titled “What Every Christian Should Know”.
In an effort to drive premium purchases, we decided to test the subject lines in the initial kick-off email to see which resonated more with the individuals on the email list. For our treatment, we added clarity by announcing the release of the brand-new book, while the control stayed in alignment with the traditional approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this test, we saw 2 different results.
For the "10 core beliefs" subject, we saw a 5.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence in opens. However, we know that this is not a conversion rate that should be totally trusted due to various platforms "pre-opening" emails.
For the "Brand-new book" subject line, we saw a 10% increase in clicks with a level of confidence at 65.4%.
What we found interesting is that more people open the email with the subject line "10 core beliefs" however the higher click count occurred on the "Brand-new book" subject line. This leads us to believe that people are looking for information around the core beliefs but were not as interested in purchasing a book. Whereas the "brand-new book" subject line saw more clicks meaning that people knew what they were opening and were more interested in learning more about the book.
For this new book, we are going to continue testing copy and seeing which angle of this book resonates best in an effort to drive purchases.

experiments.title: How beginning an email with a declaration vs a personalized sentence affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a more personalized sentence rather than a declaration for email openers will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
Luther Seminary was running their year-end high-urgency campaign, and for the first two appeal emails of the campaign, they wanted to test if starting the email with a more personal sentence instead of a declaration would keep users reading longer which would ultimately increase the click-through rate.
The control email started off with an explicit statement that could be viewed as not personal or more “organizational”. The treatment email started with a personal statement that sounds more conversational or like you’re speaking with someone, not just an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email saw a 10.1% decrease in click-through rate with only a 57.7% level of confidence. Because of this, we can assume that starting an email with a declaration or personalized statement does not affect the click-through rate of the email. We believe this does not have as much of an impact because it's changing something very small in the email when compared to the overall value proposition the rest of the email provides.

experiments.title: How acknowledging someones previous gift history affects giving in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out previous giving history for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
For Luther Seminary’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to test how calling out the user’s previous giving history would affect the conversion rate. To do this, we split their file into four segments: never donors, lapsed donors, lybunt donors, and active donors. After those were segmented, we split traffic 50/50 where the control segment saw language around “someone who supports the mission of Luther Seminary” and the treatment segment saw language that specifically called out their last gift DATE and last gift AMOUNT. (Or for never donors, specifically calling out that they had never given a gift to Luther Seminary before.)
We ran this experiment on the AM and PM emails with the same segmentation for Giving Tuesday. In order to have the potential to reach statistical validation, all results have been complied into this one experiment write up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email saw a -.02% decrease in conversion rate with only a .05% level of confidence. These results tell us that there was no difference in giving because of calling out previous donor history.
We would like to test this concept on a different high-urgency appeal email during a time of year when the motivation to give is not as high.

experiments.title: How using a clear call-to-action focused on taking the next step of giving a donation in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using clear call-to-action language  for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2022 - 12/31/2022
As a part of Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test the concept of using a clear call-to-action to give a donation vs using a more vague call-to-action of “learn more” to see how it affects donor conversion from the email to the donation page.
We believe that using a clear call-to-action that focuses on taking the next step is most effective as focusing on something vague like “learn more” doesn’t set the right expectation on the next page (donation page).
For this experiment, we tested this concept throughout the final four appeal emails of this campaign in order to receive enough traffic to have the possibility of reaching a valid level of confidence.
The control emails used clear call-to-action language to make a donation and the treatment emails used vague call-to-action language around “learn more”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment emails using the vague call-to-action saw a 14% increase in conversion rate with only a 35.3% level of confidence therefore the clear call-to-action did not have an effect on the users decision to give.
We believe this happened because the motivation for year-end giving is already higher than all other times of the year. Because of this, using vague or clear language for the call-to-action did not effect whether someone was going to make a donation or not. It's also safe to say that there was not confusion between the vague call-to-action in the treatment email and the donation page.
We would like to test this concept again on another appeal email outside of calendar year-end.

experiments.title: How encouraging a user to read on in the headline of an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including language that encourages users to read more on an instant donation page following an online course registration will increase instant conversion rates by eliminating the cue that there is nothing else to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2022 - 12/23/2022
In an effort to increase donation rates on an instant donation page that is shown to users after they complete their registration, we wanted to know how including language in the headline that encourages users to keep reading would help increase donation rates.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control (featuring a headline that contained only the word “Congratulations”) and the treatment (which included confirmation language and the phrase, “Before you start…”) and ran the experiment for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, this experiment yielded flat results and did not receive a large enough sample size to validate the results (a 2.2% decrease in donations with only a 9.94% level of confidence), preventing us from drawing definitive conclusions on the efficacy of our hypothesis based on this experiment.
We may look to run this experiment for another online course in an attempt to validate results ... or we may look to test a more significant change to the headline such as presenting the value of making a donation front and center rather than one based so heavily on using confirmation language, i.e., giving the user a compelling reason to read on rather than using a phrase like "before you go"

experiments.title: How remove Dr. Jeffress’ image impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing Dr. Jeffress’ image from the year-end landing page increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/16/2022
PTV is a broadcast ministry with a premium-based fundraising program.
Dr. Jeffress is the host and preacher for PTV therefore his image is prominent in most promotions, landing pages, and websites.
Though his face is known to his TV audience, his radio, email and newly acquired names may not be familiar with his image.
For the year-end campaign, we decided to test the landing page without his image to see if this impacted donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a statistically valid increase of donations, we also did not see any harm with removing Dr. Jeffress' image.
On mobile, we had expected to see more of an impact of the treatment version. With the control version on mobile, Dr. Jeffress' image takes up the entire screen between the paragraphs of copy and the donation level options. So with the treatment version having the image removed, we expected an improved mobile experience and less friction however the data did not end up supporting that.
We will continue testing on this landing page to improve donor conversion as we are seeing higher than normal web traffic given the year end final push.

experiments.title: How anniversary language and monthly donor language affect conversion rate on a monthly giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding anniversary language and changing “partner” to “monthly donor” for returning visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
CaringBridge had used popups to ask repeat visitors to consider giving a monthly gift. While the performance had built a file of recurring donors, they wanted to see if they could increase the clickthrough rate and conversion rate to monthly donor. They formed two hypotheses:
Using language highlighting their 25th anniversary would increase relevance and clickthrough rate and conversion rate
Using “monthly donor” rather than “CaringBridge partner” would increase the clarity of the ask and increase conversion rate.
They decided to test this with an A/B/C test. Their first treatment introduced the 25th anniversary language, but kept the “partner” language. The second kept the 25th anniversary language, but introduced “monthly donor” in place of “partner”.
They launched this to determine a winning variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there weren't enough monthly transactions to validate the experiment, the second treatment (which included both the added relevance of the 25th anniversary language and the clarity of the term "monthly donor") showed a non-valid lift of 42%.
The low conversion rate made this unlikely to validate, as the difference was 0.00008205212361% for the control and 0.0001166588894 for the second treatment. However, it was clear that the first treatment underperformed, so they decided to run this experiment with only two variants to see if they could deliver a valid result.

experiments.title: How an urgent popup with a sponsorship goal and a 24-hour countdown impacted sponsorship conversion on children sponsorship pages.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgent popup noting a daily sponsorship goal and a 24-hour countdown clock for people visiting child sponsorship pages will achieve an increase in sponsorship conversions because the popup will create urgency and motivation, and stand as a special nudge to get people interested in sponsoring to sponsor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/06/2022
In an effort to increase sponsorship conversions on children sponsorship pages for a child sponsorship organization, we’re testing a popup that sets a daily sponsorship goal counting down from three and includes a countdown (hours, minutes, seconds) for the day. It also includes value prop as part of an urgent message. This popup does not include a CTA and does not redirect to another page when clicked. It’s tested to add urgency and motivation while a potential sponsor is already scrolling the children sponsorship pages. We’re running the popup only on pages that catalog the children available for sponsorship.
We believe that the popup will act as a special nudge to get people who are already considering sponsoring a child to convert into sponsors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for two weeks, the experiment did not gain much traction. The treatment resulted in a 16.8% decrease in child sponsorships with a 37.9% level of confidence. This tells us that the popup did not carry much weight in visitors' decisions to sponsor a child. The logic here is that they are already on the children page, so their intent is already established. Whether they saw the urgent popup or not, their mind was already made up when they reached the page.
We have another experiment running in which the popup appears on all website pages except for the children pages, and includes a call-to-action button that takes users to the child sponsorship page. In this case, we hypothesize that the popup will convince people who did not come to the website with the intent to sponsor a child to click the popup CTA, redirecting them to the child sponsorship page and converting them into a sponsor.
We will keep an eye on the experiment and see how it compares to this one in regards to motivation, urgency, and conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a statement of giving impacts lapsed reactivation rates in an email
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor statement for lapsed donors will achieve a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 11/28/2022
Giving Tuesday is generally a big day of giving for many non-profits. For the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate and their early Giving Tuesday email targeting lapsed donors, we wanted to see whether including a ‘statement’ of their donation history would help increase lapsed reactivation rates. This email was deployed on 11/28, with the only difference between the control and the treatment being the addition of a statement at the top of the email of the donor’s last gift date, amount, etc.
Our hypothesis was that by adding this statement at the top, it would serve as a visual reminder for lapsed donors of how long it had been since their last gift to the Oblates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the addition of a donor statement at the top of the email, the treatment version had a 39% higher conversion rate than the control. However, given the lower number of donations total for this lapsed audience, this experiment only had a 43% level of confidence.

Further testing throughout the year-end campaign is warranted to see if a long-term test would validate.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an incentivized item to an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing an image of the special CATIS coin in the welcome series email for potential CATIS members will achieve an increase in clicks because they’ll be impressed with the coin and gain a true feeling of possessing the coin.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/02/2022
As part of TPPF’s welcome series, new names are sent an invitation email to join the Come and Take It Society (CATIS) by making a monthly gift. When joining, members receive exclusive perks. One perk is receiving a special CATIS coin. This specific perk is mentioned in the email, however, the email does not include an image of the coin to give potential new members an accurate, tangible impression of the coin.
We hypothesized that adding an image of the coin would further pique the interest of email recipients, giving them a preview of the special coin they would receive, and therefore encouraging them to click through the email to the CATIS donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in an 89.9% lift in clicks - a 6.3% click rate compared to 3.3% for the control. Our hypothesis proved correct: Those who saw the coin were more likely to click within the email. The coin image gave these people a preview of the coin and provoked visions of what it would be like to possess the coin. The mere mention of it can only go so far. By just mentioning it, email recipients don't know what the coin looks like and if it'd be worth essentially paying for as part of a monthly donation commitment. They could envision the coin as appearing tacky and poorly made. Showing an image of the coin promotes something tangible and takes some of the guesswork out of the person's mind.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects recurring giving rates on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form will increase recurring giving rates on the donation page of an acquisition offer by increasing the visibility of the option to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 12/02/2022
In an effort to increase the recurring giving rate on an instant donation page of an acquisition offer, we wanted to know how increasing the visibility of the monthly giving option earlier in the donation process, with a tabbed donation form (which in the past has shown success in this regard), would affect recurring monthly giving.
We ran this experiment for 30 days as an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between:
A. the control that features a monthly giving checkbox beneath the gift array, and
B. the treatment that removes the checkbox in favor of a monthly giving tab above the gift array
In addition, the impact statement featured below the monthly giving checkbox in the control was moved above the monthly donation form in order to keep it in close proximity to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, the treatment featuring the tabbed donation form produced a 16.6% DECREASE in recurring giving rates with a 47.7% LoC. While the low statistical validity of this experiment prevents us from drawing definitive conclusions about these results one way or another, we did observe that perhaps the checkbox in the control may have offered a more logical "flow" during the donation process, prompting users to first select their donation amount, then select how often they want to give, then view the impact that monthly donation will make.
But because the results of this experiment were well below the 95% LoC that would make them statistically valid, and because we have seen tabbed donation forms produce lifts in recurring giving for other clients in the past, we will want to look for opportunities to test this treatment on a donation page that has an opportunity to produce a larger sample size and validate the results one way to the other.

experiments.title: How campaign first messaging impacts clicks from donors in Giving Tuesday emails
experiments.researchQuestion: What messaging will get a more positive response for Giving Tuesday in an email campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly premiums. For Giving Tuesday, they offered an exclusive bundle only available on that day.
As Giving Tuesday has grown exponentially around the world, we wanted to test how we could stand out during a small timeframe when all organizations are making an ask.
We tested this by using Giving Tuesday based messaging at the beginning of the email and then later talked about the mission of PTV for the control and developed a treatment using PTV mission-focused messaging at the beginning of the email and then later used Giving Tuesday messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive a sufficient change in clicks, only 2.5% increase with a 16.8% level of confidence. We did see a 9.2% increase in opens with a 99% level of confidence.
While email opens is no longer a reliable metric, due to Apple and other platform privacy rules. We did observe a high enough increase that warranted a deep look.
When the control email hit inboxes, the viewer saw the subject line and the first few words of the email which were "Hello [firstname], On #Giving Tuesday..." For the treatment, the viewer saw the same subject line but a different first few words. They saw "Hello [firstname], As you know Pathway to Victory..."
Our hypothesis is that the increase of opens is due to the initial response of the email not being Giving Tuesday focused like ever other email in the inbox.
As we did not see a valid response to this test, we will continue testing to see if the order of messaging can impact conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page affects donations during a Giving Tuesday Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure during a high-urgency campaign will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
During a Giving Tuesday campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test for one day with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for the length of the Giving Tuesday campaign—one day—we observed a 20.6% DECLINE in donation rates, with a 61% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment.
However, observationally, we can hypothesize that by being large and colorful, the sticky bar may have actually created friction by attracting users' eyes to the top page, rather than the headline and then the body copy, and then the donation form, creating friction during the conversion process.
Because we were unable to obtain a large enough sample size to validate these results, we will want to deploy a similar experiment during a longer-term high-urgency campaign in an effort to validate the results one way or the other.

experiments.title: How adding an offer to subscription form impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free eBook for signups on the homepage will achieve an increase in email acquisition..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/30/2022 - 10/13/2022
IMB’s homepage has an email signup form at the bottom of the page. It encourages users to Sign Up Today to receive emails with transformative stories and highlights of the work the organization is doing. We hypothesized that offering a free eBook in place of this would lead to an increase in email acquisition. This was for two reasons. First, the original signup copy can at first seem a little organization-focused. Is the user just signing up for a standard weekly update? Second, we believe the free offer communicates greater value immediately by providing them with a free ebook; it also makes the implication that signing up would ensure you receive future offers like this. Value is provided immediately, but there is an emphasis on the value of becoming a subscriber.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran 44 days, but despite a strong number of conversions for the data, ultimately the results did not validate with confidence — at only 38.7%
Regardless, the increase in conversion rate was very slight at 1.4% for all traffic. Notably, mobile visitors did see a valid 45.5% increase at a 97% level of confidence. Ultimately, more testing is required in order to determine results.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 45.5% increase in email acquisitions with a 97% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 65.2% increase in email acquisitions with an 89% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 103.1% increase in email acquisitions with an 86% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 75.6% decrease in email acquisitions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a first name personalization on an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a first name personalization for users who requested an offer will achieve an increased donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 10/13/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a free offer for their Genesis One eBook. After requesting the offer on the landing page, the user would then be taken to an instant donation page that reassures them their offer is on the way and then makes a donation ask. We hypothesized that personalizing this headline with the user’s first name would increase the donor conversion rate. For two reasons: first, using a first name establishes a more personal connection between the organization and the donor; second, it communicates more gratitude. Saying “Thank you,” and ‘Thank you, [First Name]” read very differently.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for nearly 90 days, but with only 31 conversions total the results could not reach validity. As a result, despite a slight decrease in donor conversion rate of -9.4%, the low confidence of 21.8% requires more testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
-9.4% decrease in conversion rate across all traffic.55-64 visitors had a 4.3% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a slide up CTA with a daily donor goal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up CTA with a daily donor goal will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/31/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to find ways of getting more visitors to become monthly donors. They had used sticky bars before and found success with these at acquiring donors from visitors to their website. They hypothesized that using a different style message, one that slid up from the bottom of the page, and that included a daily donor goal would increase donor conversion. They hoped that this message would, if not leading to more monthly donors acquired, at least lead to more donors acquired.
They used a bright yellow color design to catch the eye and a low appeal threshold of just $15/month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for just over a month, we were unable to validate an impact on donor conversion from the slide-up. Our sample size of gifts was just too small. We do however want to test micro goals presented in either sticky bars or slide up CTAs during future campaigns, particularly during seasons of high urgency when we are more likely to get enough data to validate the result.

It is possible that a transition to iDonate that took place during this test may have caused us to be unable to track donations made on the main donation page that were influenced by the popup rather than made through the popup.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site on the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to all non-homepages. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through anywhere but the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process.
This particular test focused on visitors to the main home page. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through the homepage page first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likely to complete a transaction, but they may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that came to the main page to make a donation, but abandoned for some reason, would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. Just like the test on the non-homepage version, When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $2k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive with an 11% decrease in the treatment, but with only a 53% Level of Confidence. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.

The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount), but only on the non-homepage version.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the ‘I’m new’ button for new website visitors impacted email acquisition rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlight a specific section for new visitors will achieve a change in click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
As one of the largest Baptist churches in the country, First Baptist Dallas drives a lot of new website traffic to it’s site every day. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether highlighting the ‘I’m New’ navigation menu in the header menu for new visitors only made an impact on email sign-ups on that specific page.
Our hypothesis was that by highlighting this section, any new visitor to the site would easily find information that was more relevant to them, instead of spending time searching through the site. New visitors were also asked to fill out a form on that page to receive more information; by reducing the amount of friction to find that form, our hypothesis was that email acquisition rates would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Highlight the 'I'm New' header navigation menu for new visitors to the site led to a small, 1.7%, increase in email acquisition rates, with only a 48.8% level of confidence.

While this shows a slight directional lift in email rates, this test warrants further refinement. For example, the form where people are asked to fill in their information is actually a plan your visit (for Sunday service) form; not all of the new visitors to the homepage went there with the intention of attending a Sunday service. The form itself is also at the bottom of the 'I'm New' page; further testing would place this form higher, potentially above the fold, and make it more appealing to new visitors by offering more than just Sunday service information in exchange for an email sign-up.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to an ebook instant donation page impacts revenue.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for donors will achieve a change in average gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 11/11/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
Every quarter, Hoover publishes a collection of essays and articles in the Hoover Digest. There is a digital version of this that Hoover offers as an email acquisition tool, and there is a physical version of the Digest that gets mailed to donors who make a gift over $100. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether including getting access to the physical version all four quarters of the Digest, for a gift of $100, would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment brought in a slightly higher average gift than the control version, with an increase of 17% and a 77% level of confidence. This result is in line with previous premium-focused experiments we've run on the Hoover Digest.
Our hypothesis is that the positioning of the physical versions of the Digest is not as appealing to digital donors as more traditional offline donors. Further testing might including positioning the Digest premium test as providing a 'gift' for friends and family.

experiments.title: How adding urgency with a sticky bar affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a sticky bar that conveys urgency during a fundraising campaign will increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 11/04/2022
During a special high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a sticky bar with a high-urgency message from the director of the project and including his image would affect donation rates.
The treatment including the sticky bar was run during the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of prospective donors who clicked through to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for just over 30 days, we observed a 5.3% decline in donation rates ... but with only a 49.4% level of confidence. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment. However, we can hypothesize that the sticky bar either created friction by attracting the viewer's eyes back up the page, rather than progressing down the copy to the donation form. It could also be possible that the particular language chosen for this sticky bar "this can't wait" was a bit too forceful and created resentment in viewers. But in either case, we cannot know for sure.

experiments.title: How detailing more value proposition on an instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition  for the Scouting survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2022 - 11/12/2022
The Boy Scouts of America were running a survey to all alumni and people associated with Scouting to gauge their thoughts on Scouting and to try and acquire more names of fellow Scouts.
Following the survey, we had an instant donation page that had minimal value propositions for why they should make a gift to BSA. Because the ICR was low on this page, we wanted to test changing the copy on the donation to copy that detailed their value proposition more and explained better what a gift to BSA would do for Scouting.
The copy we used in the treatment was copy similar to the copy we used on a high-urgency appeal the previous month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 41.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 62.2% level of confidence. We had to shut this test off before reaching proper validation because we turned off spend for this acquisition offer.
Because this did not reach validity and only had 11 total gifts, we would like to test this again on another acquisition offer's instant donation page.

experiments.title: How a give now button at the top of the donation page anchored to the form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a give now button at the top of the donation page anchored to the form will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2022 - 10/07/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if the length of their donation page was causing friction for donors already motivated to give and simply looking for the donation form on the page. During their Fall Campaign, they decided to test the impact of a “give now” button at the top of the page that was anchored to the form. Those coming to the page motivated by the email they read or the on-air appeal they heard, could click that button and drop straight to the form. They hypothesized that this could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this experiment for three weeks and were unable to validate the impact of the anchor button. When digging into the results by channel (note: the T1 space in the results window above is actually the control without the anchor button), we found that there was a 17% or so decrease in conversion on direct traffic, but an 11.7% increase on email traffic conversion. While neither of these segments were statistically valid, we hypothesized that there is a possibility that email was showing a positive result because the email traffic was primed to give by the appeal they'd just read and thus wanted the button and the decreased friction it provided. We saw that returning traffic was also showing a higher conversion rate (though not valid) and we believe it would be for the same reason.
We will not roll out a give now button but may test a similar approach on a future offer.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – non-homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site outside of the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to the main page. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process. This particular test focused on visitors to all other pages. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through non-homepage pages first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likey to complete a transaction, where as visitors that came to article pages or via a SEO search, may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test provided some interesting early data. Our hypothesis was that motivations for people visiting the site outside of the main page would be lower to donate than those from main site visits. By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that had less motivation would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. After about 10 days of running the experiment, the test actually validated (with a greater than 95% LoC) based on revenue. The test results also showed that average gift for the treatment was substantially higher (over $25 greater for the treatment). While these initial results were very encouraging, whenever we run a test on revenue lift, we always need to look for outlier donations that could potentially skew the data results. When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. There was a $20k gift on the control, and several $5k plus gifts on the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $1k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.
The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount).

experiments.title: How converting donation form into a multi-step form impacts mobile donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation form into smaller steps will increase donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2022 - 11/06/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They were wanting to test ways of increasing donor conversion, particularly for visitors using a mobile device. They hypothesized that the current form was too long for Mobile and caused visual friction and that a shorter form would lead to higher donor conversion for this traffic segment to the main donation page.
They ran the test for one month and found the following results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After one month, we were unable to validate the impact of a 3-step form. There just wasn't enough of an impact and enough gifts during the test to validate. We will not roll out a 3-step form but will instead test other ways of removing friction and increasing the value proposition of the page. For example, adding additional value propositions around the form submission button or testing the impact of asking for credit card fees on the form.
We should also test in the future a step one of X feature so they know what step they are on.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly donation button to the website’s main navigation menu impacts onsite donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donate button to the website’s main navigation menu will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 10/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on their website by adding a monthly donation button to their main navigation next to their one-time donate button. Monthly donors are an important part of their program and they hoped that this additional callout would increase monthly donor conversion.
When they first tested this approach (see prior experiment here), they found a significant increase in donor conversion. However, upon further analysis, that experiment changed multiple variables, so its results were not reliable. They then tested each of the individual variables:
Test the position of the one-time gift button (see experiment writeup here)
Test the copy on the one-time gift button (give vs donate) – see experiment writeup here
And finally, they tested the addition of a monthly donation button in the main navigation menu. This test ran more than two months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At no point did the additional button increase donor conversion. For several weeks, it appeared that there would be a valid decrease in donor conversion measured, but that too never reached a point of statistical validity. Ultimately, we were unable to measure a positive impact from adding the button so we will not be changing the navigation menu.
For future tests, we will not let an experiment run as long as this did because we will begin experiencing data pollution past 4-weeks of testing. This experience also showed the importance of not just taking an initial win in an experiment at face value, but ensuring that we are measuring each variable correctly and, if not, intentionally testing each and every variable so we know what is in fact impacting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding an additional ask in the P.S. line of an appeal email impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ask in the P.S. line of an email for email recipients will achieve more donors and revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/04/2022
The Navigators were running a short email campaign for their collegiate ministries. In order to increase donor conversion rate and revenue we wanted to see how adding one additional opportunity to give would impact giving. Typically, The Navigators’ have used the “P.S” line as a place to opt out of communications from that specific campaign. We wanted to test using the P.S. line as an additional ask with a link to the donation page. The control for this experiment used the P.S. line to opt out and the treatment used the P.S. line for a call to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the donor conversion rate, there was virtually no difference. We saw a .2% lift in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of .4%--making it a statistically insignificant difference. We did however see a 74% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 86.9%. While this seems positive, it is unlikely that an additional ask would sway a prospective donor to give significantly more than they were originally intending. Furthermore, we only had 29 transactions in this experiment and did not reach the ideal 50 transaction minimum. While there are no outlier gifts, it is more likely that the list was split in such a way that giving level wasn't evenly stratified and the treatment may have had larger donors or more key multiyear donors.

It is recommended that this is retested with an evenly stratified list across multiple emails to gain a more concrete learning.

experiments.title: How increasing the relevance of the problem to donors affected donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning the problem as something that would directly impact the donor’s life will increase donation rates on an instant donation page because it would increase the urgency and relevancy of the problem.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2022 - 09/30/2022
In an effort to improve donation rates, we wanted to know how “making the problem closer” than the donor might think would affect donation rates. We believed that by positioning the problem as something that could directly impact the day-to-day life of the donor (instead of speaking in generalities), we would be able to increase donation rates because donors would sense the problem as more urgent and relevant and thus boost their desire to support efforts that confront the issue. We split traffic between the control and the treatment 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed a 13.4% DECREASE in donation rates with only a 41% level of confidence. Because of our low level of confidence in these results, we are unable to validate any learning from this experiment.

However, with significant decreases in donation rates among some key demographics, it may be that further agitating the problem at this stage of the funnel may come across as redundant or even as fearmongering, potentially harming conversion rates.

Because we still desire to see an increase in donation rates related to this offer, we believe it may be more effective to focus the headline and the body copy on the solution to maintain momentum as potential donors engage with this offer while decreasing friction and even apathy by moving from the problem (already expressed in social media ads and the landing page) to a solution and inviting the donor to "take the next step".
