experiments.title: How shorter copy in a site-wide sticky bar affects donations

rows.name: Sticky Bar with Daily Donor Countdown
Sticky Bar with Daily Donor Countdown Shorter Version

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter copy for website visitors will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2021 - 09/04/2021
The team wondered if the long value proposition copy in a site-wide “Give Today” sticky bar was too long for people to quickly understand, and could be standing in the way of people clicking though to donate. So tested shorter value proposition copy to see if brevity would lead to more clarity and, ultimately, more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The shorter value proposition copy did not significantly impact donations, and for many segments appeared to lean towards a decrease in performance.
From a Bayesian perspective... Version B has an 80% chance of out performing the control. The likelihood of the short-copy outperforming the control is low, and could actually lead to a decrease in performance.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 72.9% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 46.3% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 49.4% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 41.5% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page from website visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar will increase clicks to the donation page for website visitors
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to test ways of improving visitor-to-donor conversion on their website. They hypothesized that having a sticky bar with a message from a child asking for a donation with the face of the child accompanying the message would increase donor conversion by “humanizing” the appeal.
People give to people and this would make it more of a conversational, person-to-person experience. They ran the test for the entire July matching donor campaign against their control, non “humanized’ bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we were unable to validate any impact on clicks or downstream donor conversion. While we couldn't validate a lift, the client did enjoy the interactivity of the treatment design and will continue to use it and test it on other offers and portions of their donor funnel. However, in doing so, the client is not expecting a lift in conversion from the new design.

experiments.title: How distinguishing between two topics in an email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that distinguishing between an offer and an incentive for Working Preacher donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how distinguishing between the two topics in an email (the match for the campaign first, then the free content offer, Sermon Brainwave next) would affect the conversion rate.
The original email seemed jumbled between the incentive (Serman Brainwave content unlock) and the offer (matching gift).
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, distinguishing between the offer and incentive did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 3.8% lift with only an 11.5% level of confidence.

We believe the Working Preacher audience either did not need the extra clarification between the offer and incentive in the email or we did not distinguish enough between the two topics which caused no change in the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How sticking to urgency in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sticking to urgency and removing extended value proposition for the final email in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how sticking with urgency and reducing the amount of extended value proposition copy in the final email would affect donor conversion.
We did this by removing the extra language from the control email centered around what their gift would do for Working Preacher, because these points had been conveyed heavily in every email in the campaign prior to the final email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, removing the extended value proposition and focusing mainly on urgency did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 6.6% decrease with only a 20.2% level of confidence.

From this experiment, we can assume that the difference in language did not affect the donor's decision to give at this point in the campaign. We believe this is because both emails had the urgency of that being the final day, and whether or not we included the extended value proposition, it did not affect the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How structuring the donation page copy in a user-friendly manner impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that structuring the donation page copy in a user-friendly manner and spanning the value prop across “three important things” for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/28/2022
TGC’s Digital Discipleship eBook funnel was producing a low instant conversion rate. Users that reached the instant donation page after signing up to receive the eBook were making donations at a rate well below the benchmark. We came across a TGC video that we thought did an excellent job of highlighting our cause and what we’re hoping to accomplish. So we transcribed it, and transferred the three important things stated in the video into the instant donation page copy for this offer. We wondered if diverting from the traditional copy format on the donation page and structuring the copy in a more user-friendly manner would increase donor conversion rate.
Thus, we updated the headline to invoke more excitement toward the step that the user just took. We then implemented the three important things from the video into the donation page copy, numbering them so they would be easy for the user to follow.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test resulted in zero donations for the control and treatment variants, so there was no learning. The Digital Discipleship eBook offer was not performing well at the ad level either, so we turned off the ad campaign we had running for it in Facebook. As a result, there was little web traffic actually getting to the instant donation page for the offer. This test may be worth running again if we can optimize the campaign at the ad level and generate more users to the instant donation page.

experiments.title: How emphasizing copy and expanding the value proposition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing key sentences within the copy and offering real-life application through the copy for page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2022 - 10/28/2022
TGC’s Secular Creed eBook funnel was lacking in instant donor conversion rate. Despite having a high sign-up rate for the offer, those who signed up were not converting at the end of the funnel by not donating through the instant donation page. We wondered if the subpar results were due to a lack of motivation. Therefore, we developed a copy treatment that expanded the value proposition, adding real-life application by talking about how resources like this one will help you resolve conflict and have impactful conversions with friends, neighbors, coworkers, and family members. We also bolded key sentences to get the message across in a stronger fashion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were inconclusive. Both the control and treatment led to 5 donations, and the treatment ultimately had just a 0.70% lift and only a 0.88% level of confidence. Potential donors gave little indication as to which donation page copy most resonated with them.

It's also difficult to know which components of the treatment helped or hurt donor conversion rate, as there were too many differences between the control and the treatment. The treatment had a longer headline, inconsistent bolding, and different copy overall. We may want to isolate these elements in future tests on this donation page.

experiments.title: How deriving impact back to the donor affects donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that deriving the impact back to the donor for potential donors apart of the Working Preacher campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how deriving impact back to the donor instead of the organization would impact the results of one email.
We A/B tested this concept on email 3 of the campaign by changing the email copy from language focused on what Working Preacher will do if they gain support to the specific impact the donor will have on pastors and Christians with their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not receive enough traffic on this email to see the validity, we saw a decrease of 24.1% in conversion rate with a 53.2% level of confidence. We believe this happened because Working Preacher donors are more inclined to give to this project when they know how the organization is providing and giving back.

By taking that concept away in the treatment and changing the narrative to what the donor will do with their gift, Working Preacher donors did not get the reassurance they needed to see what Working Preacher would do with their support.

In order to be confident in these results, we'll need to further test this concept with the Working Preacher audience.

experiments.title: How sending users to a campaign specific donation page during a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a campaign specific donation page for people in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2022 - 10/20/2022
Luther Seminary is running the bi-annual Working Preacher campaign.
Previously during high-urgency campaigns, they have always sent their users to the main donation page on workingpreacher.org which has many friction opportunities (navigation away from the page) and limited value proposition.
They wanted to test building a completely separate campaign donation that has more value proposition on why someone should give to the Working Preacher campaign and what Working Preacher will do for you (the donor).
We set up this experiment with the new copy and removed the extra friction (navigation) from the page as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for over 17 days with more than 630 samples to test with. All in all, we did not see any significant changes in performance between the control donation page and the campaign-specific donation page. We only saw a decrease in conversion rate of 3.8% with only a 29% level of confidence.

We believe this is because they have a very targeted and invested audience of Working Preacher members and donors therefore adding the extra value proposition or removing the extra friction did not make an overall impact on the users' decision to give to this campaign.

We will do further testing into the campaign-specific donation page by adding a countdown clock to provide more urgency toward the deadline.

experiments.title: How adding a deadline to a pop-up affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a deadline to a pop-up for users on the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2022 - 11/04/2022
Luther Seminary was running their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. We added a pop-up to the Working Preacher website and wanted to test how adding urgency / the deadline to the pop-up copy would affect donor conversion rate for users coming to the donation page from that source.
We added the deadline of October 31st to the headline and the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for about a month and saw an 8.7% lift in conversion rate with only a 60.5% level of confidence. We believe that adding the deadline to the pop-up provided more clarity for the users on the front end of what we were asking them to do and when we needed the donation.

Because this did not validate, we would like to further test this concept on pop-ups for future high-urgency campaigns.

Something else to note is that we should have tracked clicked conversion as well, to see if adding the deadline affected click-through rate. On our next test, we will need to track the clicks on the button as well.

experiments.title: How adding social proof affects ebook offer conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof by calling out the amount of people who have downloaded the ebook within the last month will increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their critical race theory ebook content offer. We wanted to test whether adding a visual element of social proof—that the ebook was downloaded more than 2,500 times in the last month—on the offer landing page would show that the resource was popular and inspire more people to download the ebook.
We added a grey box under the image of the book with the copy “This e-book was downloaded over 2,500 times last month!”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split test where 50% of traffic would see the control and 50% would see the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the social proof treatment decreased conversion rate by 2.2%, but these results did not validate. This experiment is also affected by the change in headline. A more clear learning could be achieved by isolating the two treatment edits, but we can still hypothesize a learning from the result.

Why didn't the treatment produce an increase in conversion rate?
The social proof box of text might have been distracting from the value proposition copy to the left or from the form itself.
 	Social proof might have dissuaded some from wanting to "follow the herd" and join in on what lots of other people have done.
New visitors had a 5.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Internal Links visitors had a 5.5% decrease in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 17.9% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 39.4% decrease in email acquisition with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Housefile Email visitors had a 16.2% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had an 11.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 10.7% decrease in email acquisition with an 88% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 42.6% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 17.2% increase in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding images of associated premiums impacted conversion rate and recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that putting the ebook premiums on display for potential donors to physically see will achieve an increase in donations and specifically recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 10/01/2022
TGC has a recurring giving funnel in which they offer premiums as an incentive for someone making a recurring gift. The premiums are clearly stated in the copy, however, there were no images of the premiums on the page. We thought that adding images of the premiums would perhaps increase donations and recurring donations as users would be able to see the premiums that they would be receiving when making a recurring donation. We added the images after mentioning the premium incentive and above the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The outcomes of the experiment were negligible. Neither the control nor the treatment led to a recurring donation, and the treatment resulted in a 20.5% increase in one-time donations (3 donations vs. 2) but with only a 16.5% level of confidence. The addition of the premium images carried led to a small and invalid impact.

We may want to push the experiment a step further by adding some sort of preview of the ebooks. This could consist of an image and a summary of the ebook (on-page) since we're trying to stress the premiums as an incentive to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How Calling Out The Name of a Monthly Giving Program on Tabbed Donation Form Affects Monthly Giving Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing generic monthly donation form tab copy with program-specific copy will increase recurring donor rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2022 - 10/02/2022
In an effort to combat ‘messaging fatigue,’ we wanted to know how replacing the generic monthly donation tab copy with language that specifically calls out the name of their recurring giving program will affect monthly giving. We replaced “Recurring monthly gift” with “Become a Cornerstone Partner” to see if we could increase exclusivity and a feeling of community by calling out the program by name. We ran this experiment during DTS’s annual North Texas Giving Day campaign, and split traffic 50/50
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we saw a 155% increase in monthly donation rates with a 75% level of confidence. Although there was a low volume of monthly gifts (restricted by the shorter length of a campaign), we were encouraged to see a significant lift in recurring giving via the treatment which included the "Become a Cornerstone Partner"--the name of Dallas Theological Seminary's monthly giving program.
We believe that calling the monthly giving program out by name was able to add value in the eyes of donors by conveying exclusivity and a sense of community/belonging. We also believe that breaking away from more generic 'give monthly' language helped the monthly giving option stand out by reducing "messaging fatigue" and thus doing a better job of catching the donor's attention.
Based on these results, we will look to validate this hypothesis by rolling the same treatment out to higher-traffic donation pages that are not as restricted by the truncated timeframe of a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How content preview impacted the click-through rate on a digest email

rows.name: Some cool new things for you, Vilavanh
Some cool new things for you, Danielle

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing preview content from a digest email for our general subscribers will achieve more click-throughs than a digest email with preview content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/31/2022 - 09/02/2022
We previously tested adding preview links to a digest email as a strategy to increase click-through rate on a digest email (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4515). In that test, there was a 30% increase in click-throughs on the treatment email.
To further test this, we wanted to go one step further and test using the preview links with our normal content previews beneath vs. just the preview links. We were curious to know if the extra content added value to digest recipients, or if it had no impact on their email engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings: Results did not validate. It seems removing preview content had no real impact on the click-through rate of the digest email, but results were trending in favor of the control version with preview content.

experiments.title: How creating intrigue in an email promoting new research impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that building intrigue for subscribers we are promoting new research to will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/08/2022
As a part of launching our new research study for how the industry handles a New Donor Welcome Series, we decided to experiment with a bullet list of questions that the research would answer, thereby increasing the “intrigue” factor for the reader.
Our thought was that this intrigue would drive more motivated clicks and increase email signup rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, this treatment did not have any substantial impact on the email signup rate. Although it shows a -7.6% email signup rate, the level of confidence is less than a coin flip probability to be repeated.

This means that the treatment did not create more (or less) motivation for a subscriber to get our new research.

This could simply be due to the fact that new research is just in such high demand that any lower-level experiments to manufacture "more demand" aren't necessary. On the other hand, it could be due to the fact that this approach was ineffective and should be retested in future opportunities to re-test the concept.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition language on the instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve clarity and cause an increase in ICR.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2022 - 09/18/2022
Luther Seminary has been running paid Facebook ads and email marketing for their content offer Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide. While the email sign-up rate was healthy around 48%, we wanted to try and increase the instant conversion rate on the donation page.
We hypothesized that the copy on the control page did not provide clarity on the impact a donation has for Luther Seminary and what the donor’s gift will do for the organization.
We believed that adding more value proposition-focused language to the page would provide clarity to the prospective donor for how their gift will be used in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the experiment were encouraging when it comes to lifts in revenue for this acquisition offer. We saw a 39.1% increase in average gift with a lift of 75.4% level of confidence. This experiment had been running for over a month and had not been validated therefore we moved traffic to the treatment page with confidence that the value prop copy will increase the giving amount on this page.
While we saw a lift in average gift, the results for donor conversion rate were flat at 4.3% lift with a 7.34% level of confidence.
We believe that the value proposition copied inspired potential donors more to give a higher gift, but was not enough to convince someone to give if they already decided against it. Although it did not negatively affect the decision to make a gift.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by changing the confirmation message at the top of the page to be more clarifying following the previous step in the funnel. We will still want to verify these results by running this experiment on another instant donation page while isolating the variables being tested.

experiments.title: The impact on donor conversion of adding language about protecting subscriber privacy to a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reassuring subscribers that their data will be kept private will reduce mental friction and increase downstream donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2022 - 09/26/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They use free content offers to acquire donors on Facebook. One of their most popular offers is 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Children in Your Life . They wanted to see if they could increase donor conversion by adding a sentence about subscriber privacy to the form.  in 2017, they tested this approach and found no increase in email conversion, however tests with other organizations found that this language would could increase downstream donor conversion.
They cloned the landing page and added this just below the form: “We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to deliver your devotion.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for two months there was no significant impact on downstream donor conversion by adding the privacy language to the landing page. When looking at email conversion there was also no significant difference between the control and treatment pages. While other organizations have found with their visitors that this language can increase donor conversion, the audience for this offer is not motivated either way by this additional value proposition.

experiments.title: How offering a survey about cards changed response rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for themed responses for survey responders will achieve change the response rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2021 - 10/29/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. They offer physical cards during Eastern and Mother’s Day; for this experiment, we wanted to survey their email audience to see whether people would respond more for an Easter ask versus a Mother’s Day ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both the Mother's Day control and the Easter treatment had essentially the same click-through rates to the survey, with only a 1.3% decline with the Easter treatment version, for a 33.6% level of confidence.

Since the results of this experiment were so close, this experiment will be tweaked and re-launched for next year.

experiments.title: How “hero-focused” language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging hero language for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
Donors are heroes. Or, so the common logic goes. CaringBridge had heard over and over again at conferences and speaking engagements how important it is to make the donor feel like a hero. So, they decided to test it themselves.
In their first appeal for their anniversary campaign, they had a very personal story from the campaign manager. They decided to replace this copy with copy that highlighted the donor as the hero.
They tested this by splitting their email file in half and sending one treatment to each half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results weren't statistically valid, there was a directional lift using the "hero" copy that focused the attention on the donors.

This will need to be re-tested, but signs point to the fact that giving the donor a sort of platitude that makes them feel good can increase results.

experiments.title: How the “fear of loss” positioning impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling someone they’ll lose something instead of telling them what they’ll get for prospective customers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2022
As a part of the marketing campaign to promote the launch of a new online course for NextAfter, we were promoting email signups to participate in our online “training cohort” program (for a guided tour through the course to maximize course consumption and learning retention).
The day before the course was launched, we decided to write a “last chance” (to signup) email and send it the day before the course went live.
In reviewing the copy for the course promotion, we decided to test using the “fear of loss” concept to see if it would drive a higher email signup rate for the training cohort/course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we did not generate enough results to scientifically validate the lift found in the treatment. With only a 54.1% level of confidence, these results are sadly no more reliable than a "coin flip chance" of producing the same lift percentages shown.
This was due to a much smaller audience size on the email resend, which ultimately just didn't produce enough samples.
This concept should be retested in the future to see if pairing "exclusive access" and "the loss of that exclusive access" if the reader doesn't take action can actually substantially lift response rates in the future.

experiments.title: The impact of Fiscal Year End Language on the donation page for the fiscal year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding fiscal year end language will increase donor conversion on the main donation page during the Fiscal Year-End campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2022 - 08/27/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were using a premium offer, Find Your People and a donor goal. They wanted to see if adding value proposition on the donation page surrounding Fiscal Year-End and the importance of ending the year “strong” would increase donor conversion on the donation page. No other elements included this language, just the main donation page. The control page would discuss the donor goal and the premium, the treatment would include language about the fiscal year ending and why that matters to the work FamilyLife does to strengthen families worldwide.
We split traffic 50/50 and ran it for just over two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 257 donations, there wasn't a clear improvement on donor conversion or revenue from the treatment. We did see an 80% Level Of Confidence for an improvement on revenue, but when digging into the segments, there were conflicting results between leading sources of traffic to their site and large gifts may have skewed these results. Due to the importance of ending the campaign strong and the desire to launch another test in the last days of the campaign (a sitewide popup that would prompt visitors to complete their donation) we ended this test.
We will see if we can test this approach again next year, but add the Fiscal Year End language to the emails as well as to the donation page to see if this holistic approach increases conversion by keeping that language consistent from email to page.

experiments.title: How adding COVID-19 language to a donation page impacted donor conversion results
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding COVID-19 language for donors will achieve a chance in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2020 - 06/04/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. As COVID-19 rapidly spread across the globe, we launched an experiment to see whether adding COVID-19 language to a donation form would make an impact on donation conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the pandemic rapidly spreading across the globe, our hypothesis was that by adding in COVID language more people would end up donating. However, there was a slight decrease in conversion rates of 1.3% on the COVID treatment page.

We believe that this was due to the fact that the pandemic, and it's impact on the people that the Oblates serve, was not yet widely known.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to the homepage during a fiscal year-end campaign impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that individualizing copy for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2021 - 07/02/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their standard high urgency campaigns takes place in June, as their fiscal year comes to a close.
In 2021, we wanted to test whether changing the language on the sticky bar to a more individualized approach–where by donating you are helping specific members of society–rather than a more generic ask, would have an impact on conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment were negligible, with only a 1.5% change in conversion rates from the control to the individualized copy version. With only a 6.4% level of confidence, this experiment needs to be re-run for a longer time period, during a campaign where there will potentially be more conversions to provide a valid test result.

experiments.title: How calling out the steps to take to make a donation impacted donor conversion rates.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Steps to take to make a donation

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding visual ‘steps’ for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/08/2020 - 09/09/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. As part of one of their acquisition offers which offered a download of a Lift the Spirit eBook, we wanted to test whether adding a visual call out for the different parts of a donation form impacted conversion results.
In this test, we added ‘Step 1, step 2, etc’ to each section of the donation form (gift array, billing info, etc) to test whether a guided process made an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding in the 'steps' for a donor to follow during their donation process led to a decrease in conversion rates of 16.7%. While the sample size for this experiment is not large--due to it coming from an acquisition experiment--there was a 68% level of confidence, indicating that continued testing and refinement would be needed in the future.

experiments.title: How increasing the suggested ask amount for a premium offer during year-end impacted revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the suggested ask for donors will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2021 - 01/04/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular offers during their end-of-year campaign is to donate a specific amount and have 4 advent candles lit for your intentions at the Shrine of Our Lady of the Snows.
Since year-end is where the majority of online revenue comes in for the Oblates, we wanted to test whether increase the ask for the Advent offer from $50 to $75 would make an impact on revenue from the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were interesting. Overall, there was a decline in revenue per visitor of 9.5% with a 73% level of confidence. That means that, despite a higher average gift on the $75 experiment version, total revenue was less with the higher ask.

However, the results also varied by the kind of donor that landed on the page. New visitors had a decrease of 33% in revenue with a 96% level of confidence; our hypothesis was that the $75 donation was just too high for new donors.

The story for returning visitors was different, however, with a 28.6% increase in revenue and a 89% level of confidence. Those that knew the work of the Oblates, and were interested in giving again to have advent candles lit for their intentions, were willing to donate at the higher ask.

This is an experiment we will continue to refine in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a visual call-out to for a monthly gift ask impacted conversion rates.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visual monthly call out for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether having a visual call-out for monthly gifts made an impact on monthly donor sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, there was a decrease in the number of monthly gifts that came in during this testing period of 28%, with a 78% level of confidence. While the total number of gifts is just barely over the number we like to have to validate an experiment (50), there were a couple of differences in the two forms that contributed to mixed testing results.
On the control version, there is a check box one needs to select in order to make your gift become monthly; for the treatment version we not only introduced the visual call out, but also added in a tabbed form. We believe that this contributed to the change in conversion rates.
For future tests, we will try and isolate just a single change on the form to get a cleaner test result.

experiments.title: How adding a ‘most popular’ treatment to a specific gift amount impacted revenue.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a most popular gift amount for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 04/07/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. As part of the run-up to their Easter fundraising campaign, we wanted to test whether adding a graphic treatment for the ‘most popular’ amount would have an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a slight increase in conversion rate with the most popular treatment option, as well as a slight increase in average gift. However, since it did not reach the level of confidence needed for a valid test result nor can provide any directional results, we will need to continue testing this approach to see if it has a long-term impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How adding a ‘handwritten’ call out for monthly donations impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Add handwritten note focusing on monthly donations

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a graphic call out for potential monthly donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2021 - 04/22/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment we tested whether a graphic call-out, in the form of some ‘handwritten’ text, focusing around the impact monthly donations would make, had an impact on monthly donor sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings: By adding in the 'handwritten' font emphasizing what an impact a monthly gift could make, there was a 21% increase in the number of new monthly donations converted on this experiment. While the level of confidence was only 56% due to the lower volume of recurring gifts, this result did indicated that further testing and refinement was needed.

experiments.title: How adding a $5 minimum gift requirement impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a minimum donation amount for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2020 - 04/17/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular digital acquisition offers is the Our Lady of Lourdes digital novena.
Traditionally, there was a premium associated with this offer in an effort to get ebook sign-ups to donate, with an offer of a vial of water from the sacred Lourdes grotto in France as a thank for a donor’s gift. For this experiment, we added a $5 minimum donation to the premium ask to see whether it would have an impact on donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in the $5 minimum gift, with language that made it clear the minimum was to cover shipping and handling costs, had a slight increase of 8% in terms of revenue per visitor, with a 72% level of confidence. Moreover, there was a 28% increase in overall donation conversion rates with a 98% level of confidence.

Our hypothesis is that by positioning the minimum gift requirement as a way to make sure the donor's donation is maximized (instead of spent on shipping and handling) led donors to be more generous with their gifts.

This kind of language merits continued testing throughout premium acquisition offers for the Oblates.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a premium landing page impacted donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop langauge for premium-resource driven donors will achieve a change in conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Their featured offer in May was Invincible: Conquering the Mountains That Separate You from the Blessed Life.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this specific offer, adding in value proposition language to the landing page for the book led to a decrease in both conversion rates and revenue per donor of 14.2% and 8.3% respectively. Despite an increase in average gift, the overall result was a decline in revenue for the experiment variant page.
Our hypothesis is that the majority of traffic to these book offer pages is driven directly from TV and radio programs promoting the book; those that reach that page want to purchase the book and don't have that much of an interest in value proposition.
However, it will be interesting to keep track of the 'value prop' donors long term, as our hypothesis is that by buying into the value prop of WHY they should donate to PTV instead of another ministry, they will become better long-term donors, and not just those looking to buy one book and that is the extent of their support.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity into a headline affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline of a content offer will result in a higher number of email conversions
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
I Am Second is committed to helping people be authentic and genuine—both with God and with others. For this experiment, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of emails they collect on a content offer landing page. They hypothesized that a headline that gave more clarity into what the subscriber would receive would yield a higher number of emails than the control that was good, but lacked full clarity into what the reader would actually gain by downloading the resource.
experiments.keyLearnings: As hypothesized, the headline clarity resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisitions. Although greater than the control, due to the experiment not getting at least a 95% LoC or greater, we cannot say with full certainty that the treatment had a true measurable effect. In addition, we normally only like to make one change to an experiment to isolate results...and this particular experiment changed both the headline and added an additional supporting sub-headine. While one could argue that the sub-headline further aligned with the headline, providing even more clarity...it cannot be proven as this test had too many variable changed. For future tests, it would be better only to change one element, or if time/urgency is a factor, do a three-way test that has a control, and two variants to see if both of these changes actually add to the increase. It is also important to note that this organization rarely gets enough traffic actually to validate tests with true statistical significance, but even still there are learnings to be had...as shown above.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity and recency copy to a headline on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into a landing page headline, and aligning the message with recent events will result in higher email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 08/27/2022
e3 Partners equips believers to take ownership of the Great Commission. They send believers into local and global communities to evangelize the lost, and they partner with believers to establish new churches across the globe. For this experiment, e3 wanted to test adding clarity and recency copy to their headline on an email acquisition landing page. The control headline had been used for a while and had good results, but after seeing other experiments that tested tried and true content offer copy, they decided to try it on their previously good performing content offer – prayers for the overwhelmed. Since this test ran during a period of societal uncertainty with recent tragedies in the news, inflation fears, and world unrest, we felt it was a prime opportunity to adjust the headline copy to reflect these worldwide events, while still remaining generic enough to stay evergreen.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not have enough traffic to validate the experiment, we did see a 206% increase in conversions for the treatment with a 69% level of confidence. This was a very small sample size, but we did not see a negative effect on any of the channels. Due to this, we decided to role this out oas the new control. The next steps in the experiment might be to test some tactical elements, like testimonials or social proof that aligns with the recency copy.

experiments.title: How adding more value proposition to the headline and body copy affect conversions for new users
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the headline and body copy of a landing page will yield better conversion metrics.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2022 - 08/27/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. For this experiment, they wanted to test if adding more value proposition into the headline and body copy of the email acquisition landing page for a popular content offer would result in a greater number of email conversions. While the control was very clear, the treatment alluded that the resource would provide a “blueprint for success”…thus potentially eliminating complexity. This came from comments from new readers that prayer life can sometimes be complicated, so we wanted to see how the treatment affected new visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had a very slight lift of just 3% with a 40% level of confidence. The results were inconclusive as the experiment did not have enough samples or LoC to validate. Since we did not see a directional lift, we cannot say if this experiment had any positive impact. As the experiment did have an adverse effect on returning visitors, future iterations of this experiment might be limited to just new visitors.

experiments.title: How adding more value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition statements into the body copy of a donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2021 - 01/22/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. For this experiment, they wanted to test if changing the body copy of the donation page to infuse more value proposition statements that align with the beliefs and values of the org would result in a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had a very small lift of 8% with about a 9% LoC. The results from this experiment were inconclusive and not valid due to a low LoC and small sample size. While we have plenty of experiments that validate for increased value proposition where we connect the "why", this [particular experiment probably had too many variables within the copy to have a bigger impact. Future versions of this experiment might be to shorten the body copy and to limit the amount of value proposition statements to focus attention on one thing.

experiments.title: How personalizing the name in the headline of a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding personalization to a donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher number of donations..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 08/27/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. They strive to make a personal connection to the donors that support them. For this experiment, they wanted to test if personalizing the name in the headline of an instant donation page would lead to a higher number of donations. I Am Second uses a CRM that allows variables like [first_name] to be passed from the CRM to the Donation page. For this experiment, the first name from the email landing page was passed to the donation page and added to the headline of the page upon a successful name and email acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a 2 week period and did not validate based on a low level of confidence and small sample size. However, even with a decrease in lift of 54%, we learned that certain segments of the audience had vastly different results. In desktop, there were 0 donations with very little traffic. In fact, every donation during the test period happened via mobile, with the majority of traffic. While this did not have the desired effect we were looking for, we did find some interesting learnings.
Mobile had 794 samples, where desktop only had 16 samples
 	Mobile received 4800% more traffic
 	Questions:
 	Since we are getting mostly mobile traffic, what can we do to convert more of it?
 	What is the experience like on a mobile device?
 	How can we reduce friction in a mobile environment to increase our chances at converting more of that traffic into donors?

experiments.title: How “hero-focused” language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging hero language for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
Donors are heroes. Or, so the common logic goes. CaringBridge had heard over and over again at conferences and speaking engagements how important it is to make the donor feel like a hero. So, they decided to test it themselves.
In their first appeal for their anniversary campaign, they had a very personal story from the campaign manager. They decided to replace this copy with copy that highlighted the donor as the hero.
They tested this by splitting their email file in half and sending one treatment to each half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results weren't statistically valid, there was a directional lift using the "hero" copy that focused the attention on the donors.

This will need to be re-tested, but signs point to the fact that giving the donor a sort of platitude that makes them feel good can increase results.

experiments.title: How would ordering the giving tabs with the monthly giving option showing first (the left tab) impact recurring giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping the giving tabs to show monthly giving first for page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2022 - 08/26/2022
With the goal to increase recurring giving to PFI’s Lamplighters program, we conducted an experiment in which the monthly giving tab (“Monthly Gift”) would show as the left tab and the one-time giving tab (“Give Once”) would show as the right tab on the Lamplighters giving widget. This order is the reverse of the existing setup (control variant) in which monthly giving shows on the right and one-time giving shows on the left. We ran the experiment on the Lamplighters landing page giving widget.
Lamplighters historically receives a lot of one-time gifts, so we want to see if this change will result in an increase in monthly gifts, as monthly gifts are much more valuable over time. The idea is that page visitors would read and notice the “Monthly Gift” option first, indicating greater importance and making it resonate with them ahead of the one-time giving option – prompting them to make a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variant resulted in a 34.4% increase in recurring giving conversions for all traffic with a level of confidence of 64.8%. Strictly for desktop users, the treatment resulted in a 187.2% increase in recurring giving conversions with a level of confidence of 89.4%.
Our hypothesis proved correct, that visitors noticing and reading the monthly giving option first instead of one-time giving would prioritize monthly giving in their minds and result in more monthly giving conversions.
Interestingly enough, total donations (one-time and monthly) showed 21 donations through the treatment and 18 through the control. So, despite the treatment placing a greater focus on monthly giving, users still made more donations overall when seeing the monthly giving tab on the left and the one-time giving tab on the right.
This concept is certainly worth testing elsewhere given the results and the long-term impact that recurring giving has on revenue.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding value prop before a petition impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating cognitive momentum with additional value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email signatures.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/16/2020 - 12/09/2020
We were running a petition and were looking for ways to improve email conversion rate on the landing page. Although the Facebook ad gave supporting reasons to sign the petition, we noticed the control landing page went straight into the petition statement without giving any additional context for why someone should add their name to the petition. We wondered if adding clarity and urgency for visitors would help continue the cognitive momentum from one step to the next and developed a treatment that carried over value proposition into a brief paragraph and an additional subheader before leading into the beginning of the petition statement. We split traffic to both versions and ran the test for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the test for 3 weeks, we saw a 1.4% increase in conversion rate with 45.6% level of confidence so the experiment results are not valid. We needed a greater sample size for further indication of whether or not the treatment version was working. Upon review, the new intro paragraph in the treatment used organization-centric language that could have led to flat results. In future tests on this page, we will optimize the copy to be more donor-centric while testing the same concept. We could also implement some design features on the page that more clearly separate the petition language from the introductory value proposition to reduce confusion and friction in the mind of the page visitor.

experiments.title: How an exit intent popup on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an exit intent modal for Lamplighters page visitors will achieve an increase in emails without a decrease in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2022 - 07/22/2022
Prison Fellowship International was running a lot of paid traffic to generate donations for their Lamplighters offer to share the gospel with prisoners. They knew that conversion rates were about 4%—and though they wanted to increase the conversion rate, they also wanted to capture the emails of people who were coming to the site but not giving so they could “waste fewer marketing dollars”.
However, they wanted to make sure that their exit intent popup didn’t reduce conversion, as it can sometimes be triggered when people are moving their cursor around without the intent to exit the page.
They built a popup in Unbounce and used Google Optimize to trigger it for half the visitors to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 32.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 42.4% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 52.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 145.5% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 46.5% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving experience from 3 steps to 2 steps increases donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the steps to donate for ebook downloaders will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2022 - 06/27/2022
Since changing giving platforms, the Navigators giving experience is now a 3 step process after requesting an ebook on a landing page. After a site visitor fills out the form to get an offer, they’re taken to a landing page with the value prop language and button on the bottom to give. When people click on the button they’re taken to a generic giving page with a gift array. They are then given the option to “add to basket” and are taken to a page to fill out their credit card information. Our hypothesis is that if we can reduce this experience down to 2 steps, site visitors will experience less friction and we’ll see a higher donor conversion rate.
To test this concept we ran this experiment through a specific funnel/offer: “Fight on my knees” ebook. Traffic to this funnel is mainly driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 65% lift in donor conversion rate but we only saw a level of confidence of 53.9% which means this experiment did not validate. Even after running for over a month, this funnel did not get enough traffic in order to acquire a large enough sample size. It is recommended that this either retested in a different funnel that has more traffic or we test using a 1 page donation process if at all possible.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a main donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for main donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/31/2022
In efforts to increase the donor conversion rate on the main donation page, IMB added a humanized sticky bar at the top of their page. This sticky bar would not have a call to action and would just be a preemptive “thank you” from Paul Chitwood (IMB’s President). Our hypothesis was that adding a message from Paul Chitwood would remind the prospective donor that they’re hearing from a person, not just an organization. This would in turn, increase donor conversion rate on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 20 days, the treatment made no impact on conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 57%. Meaning, half the the time it will result in a 6% decrease, and half the time it will not. Our conclusion is that those who are coming to the main donation page already have a high level of motivation. They're deliberately coming to this page to make a donation and therefore may not be impacted by a thank you message from Paul Chitwood. As a follow up test, we'd like to add a message from Paul Chitwood with value proposition-based language on the actual donation page.

experiments.title: How moving a premium offer above the fold impacted donations for Pathway to Victory

rows.name: Kids book above the fold on website

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an offer above the fold for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2022 - 06/06/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. This year’s June match campaign focused on the launch of a brand new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 challenge match.
Traditionally, the homepage of Pathway to Victory only shows an image of the featured monthly premium as a small image above the fold, and a larger focus below the fold. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether moving the image and description of the kids book above the fold would make an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the image of the new kids book above the fold had a meaningful impact on donor conversion rates and revenue, despite not reaching a 100% level of confidence. The 32.6% increase in donation conversions, with a 78% level of confidence, indicates that placing the image on an area where most of website visitors will see will lead to a change in donations.
Our hypothesis is that because the majority of traffic to the homepage is driven through radio and TV offers where they reference the premium offer, placing the image above the fold made it easier for potential donors to make a donation without having to search for where they can find the book.
Since Pathway to Victory is a premium-focused organization, this is a test we will continue to run with every offer.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacts donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/30/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether including a humanized sticky bar on the premium-focused landing page helped drive a higher donor conversion rate.
The sticky bar featured an image of Dr. Jeffress, the senior pastor for Pathway to Victory, and language that focused on the doubled impact a gift would have thanks to the match campaign. Since there were 4 separate ways to get the premium based on giving level, we ran this sticky bar test on all 4 donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The use of a personalized sticky bar did not have a very large impact on donor conversion rate, with only a 2% lift and a 46% level of confidence. Since the majority of the copy on the landing page was premium-focused, and did not really mention the match campaign, the sticky bar was the only, small, piece of digital real estate where donors could potentially be motivated more to make a gift because of the match itself, and not the premium offer.

experiments.title: How Moving the Global Navigation Donate Button Onto Its Own Menu Line Impact Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “Donate Now” on the global navigation donation button will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2022 - 07/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a prior test, they’d found that the addition of a “monthly” donate button to their global site navigation menu increased donor conversion. However, there were some issues with that test that made the results unclear. We had several factors that changed in the experiment (the placement of the buttons, the wording on the buttons, and the addition of a monthly donate button) that clouded the results. In order to understand WHICH factor led to the increase in donor conversion, we decided to test simply moving the “give now” button out of the menu line with the other options and onto its own line. Since this would only impact Desktop traffic, that is the only source of donors we examined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 50.9% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.
Direct visitors had a 56.2% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 37.6% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 6.7% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How redirecting mobile traffic to a specific section of a donation form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction in the donation pathway for highly motivated users during a high urgency campaign will result in greater donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/10/2022
Focus on the Family runs an annual high-urgency campaign for their pro-life movement called SeeLife. During this campaign, they run a sticky banner atop all of their pages that contains a specific call to action with a donation amount on the banner. There is little to no value proposition on this banner (especially on a mobile device), just a very simple donation ask, accompanied by specific donation amounts. When clicking on this banner, the user is normally redirected to the top of a campaign donation page where they can watch a video about the campaign, read more about it, then scroll down to make a donation.
For the purposes of this experiment, Focus on the Family wanted to test if redirecting the user to an anchored location on the donation form embedded on the campaign donation page would result in a higher donor conversion. This test would bypass the content at the top of the page, and give the donor the option to make their donation quickly…especially on a mobile device where screen real estate is limited. In addition, the team wanted to test if an anchored re-direct vs. a locked redirect (where the user had to initiate a force click out of the locked screen) would result in a higher conversion.
The working theory was that a user was already highly motivated to give due to the nature of the high urgency campaign, so removing as much friction as possible could lead to higher donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, the treatments for this user group experienced a lift. While the experiment did not validate due to a small sample size and a low level of confidence, the experiment did provide some good learnings. There was an 8.1% lift in conversion rate for the locked version with a 57% level of confidence, and a 3.2% lift with 25% level of confidence for the anchored version. Both treatments saw a positive lift over the control. However, we would like to try a similar experiment during a time when the user was not as motivated by a high urgency campaign to see if this would have a similar result. We would recommend rolling this out again for other high-urgency campaigns that have similar circumstances.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.2% increase with a 87% level of confidence.
Paid Search visitors had a 136.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for fiscal year end campaign visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/01/2022
DTS was running their annual fiscal year-end campaign and we wanted to optimize the standard donation page that is typically used during this campaign. We developed a treatment that incorporated the following elements: we updated the headline to include urgency by referencing the deadline of “before midnight on June 30”. We also used bullet points to demonstrate opportunities for impact in the body copy. The language also gave specific evidentials of how the donor’s gift would be used, stating that for every $75 you give, you can offer our online courses to 15 people. We also introduced some donor autonomy and re-highlighted the urgency of the deadline in the subheader leading into the donation form “If you want to support…please make your gift using the form below:”
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for the duration of the campaign was that the radical redesign treatment had a 5% decrease in conversion rate with 44% level of confidence so the results are not valid. Our hypothesis for the flat results is that 1) the donation page copy is longer than the control which could have introduced additional friction to the experience for those in the treatment group and 2) the donation page copy used in the treatment is still organization-centric and not donor-focused. All 4-5 paragraphs begin with the word "DTS" or "we" and the bullet points are organization-centric in terms of being presented as "here's what we are already doing" rather than posed as "what your gift will help accomplish". We also feel that the urgency could have been better communicated by asking for a gift "now" or "today" rather than any time before June 30. We need to better compel visitors to make their gift at the time of their visit to the page. There is no clear need or problem presented in the copy in the treatment for the donor to address or solve, which is something we will want to consider framing out for future tests.

experiments.title: How does adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affect instant donation pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page will increase instant donation rates for prospects who receive an ebook acquisition offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2022 - 08/01/2022
In an effort to increase donation rates for an ebook acquisition offer, we wanted to test if adding a humanized sticky bar to the instant donation would connect to prospects and encourage more people to give.
We ran this experiment for 36 days, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for 36 days, we observed a 3.7% lift in instant conversion rates with a 19.8% level of confidence, meaning that there were not enough gifts to validate these results.
It does not appear that in this instance adding a humanized sticky bar inspired significantly more prospective donors to give, however the low number of samples and the resulting low level of confidence in these results prevent us from drawing definitive conclusions one way or the other.
We will look for other opportunities to run this experiment in an effort to validate our hypothesis.

experiments.title: How splitting out a monthly donate button impacted monthly donor donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling attention to a monthly donor program for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether splitting out a donate and monthly-donate-specific button would have any impact on new monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had a 23% increase in the number of new monthly donors who were converted using the monthly call out button, but only had a 46% level of confidence given the lower total number of new monthly donors that came in during that time period.
Despite this experiment running during what is a traditionally a high-impact giving season (Easter), the number of donations was not enough to give us a valid result.
Nevertheless, this is a concept that we will continue testing on the Oblate's main website, especially given the importance of new monthly donors on their program overall.

experiments.title: How the “fear of loss” positioning impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling someone they’ll lose something instead of telling them what they’ll get for prospective customers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2022
As a part of the marketing campaign to promote the launch of a new online course for NextAfter, we were promoting email signups to participate in our online “training cohort” program (for a guided tour through the course to maximize course consumption and learning retention).
The day before the course was launched, we decided to write a “last chance” (to signup) email and send it the day before the course went live.
In reviewing the copy for the course promotion, we decided to test using the “fear of loss” concept to see if it would drive a higher email signup rate for the training cohort/course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we did not generate enough results to scientifically validate the lift found in the treatment. With only a 54.1% level of confidence, these results are sadly no more reliable than a "coin flip chance" of producing the same lift percentages shown.
