This was due to a much smaller audience size on the email resend, which ultimately just didn't produce enough samples.

This concept should be retested in the future to see if pairing "exclusive access" and "the loss of that exclusive access" if the reader doesn't take action can actually substantially lift response rates in the future.

experiments.title: How using a match-focused pop-up on the homepage impacted results for Pathway to Victory.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a pop-up to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether having a pop-up on the homepage with match copy made an impact on donor conversion rates.
Since the focus of the campaign was the children’s book, there was limited reference to the match campaign on the homepage of the site. This test aimed to see whether adding match language made any impact
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment failed to validate or provide any direction on whether a pop-up on the homepage during a match campaign would impact results. With a slight decrease in conversion rates of 2%, but only 17% level of confidence, this experiment needs further tweaking for future match campaigns.

Our hypothesis on why this had no impact is that the majority of people go to the homepage after watching a Pathway to Victory program on TV, or hearing one of the radio broadcasts. They are not necessarily driven to the site looking for the match campaign; therefore, offering them up language that a match was going on would not motivate them to donate.

experiments.title: How using an exit-intent pop-up during a match campaign impacted conversion results for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an exit intent for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/08/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether including an exit intent pop-up made an impact on conversion rates.
This pop-up was triggered when someone would mouse over the ‘back’ button on their browser; the copy emphasized the impact that making a gift during this limited-time match challenge would have on the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not reach the level of confidence where we would say it was valid, a 61% level of confidence in the 3.7% increase in donor conversion rates indicates that this was a useful tactic.
Since the donation page itself is very premium-focused with little reference to the actual match campaign, using this exit intent pop-up allowed us extra digital real estate to communicate the impact this campaign would have on the ministry.
This is a tactic we will continue testing during high donation frequency campaigns for this organizations.

experiments.title: How inviting donors to stand in the gap for those struggling with inflation impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that we can increase donor conversion on the main donation page by inviting donors to stand in the gap for those struggling with inflation and needing the inspirational content of KCBI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2022 - 07/04/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. Due to skyrocketing inflation, they were receiving word from a number of dedicated supporters that they would no longer be able to support the ministry. As donor response slowed, KCBI decided to air an appeal for donors to “stand in the gap” during this time to ensure that KCBI could remain on the air to provide the inspirational content families desperately need.
We wanted to test this approach on the donation page as well, so we created a radically different version of the donation page that discussed the difficulties communities are struggling with and invited the donor to “put your arm around a friend who cannot give” and ensure that “ KCBI can remain on air to provide inspiration and Bible-centered music, conversation, prayer and teaching during a time when we all so desperately need it.
We also acknowledged in the copy the struggles the reader may have been having with inflation and invited them to give if, “you’ve been blessed by KCBI and God has made it possible for you to give.”
We split the traffic to the donation page 50/50 and ran the test for 14 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We only ran the experiment for two weeks because we were not seeing any positive impact of the treatment copy. We may have been able to validate the experiment with a longer test, but the client did not want to lose more donations to achieve a valid decrease in conversion.
It is possible that talking about those who've had to stop giving due to inflation had the opposite impact of what we intended and caused visitors to reconsider their intention to give. They may have asked themselves, "Maybe I should save my money too!"
In retrospect, it would have likely been better to have included a microgoal and/or a smaller appeal amount like we've tested recently with other organizations that have discussed the impact of inflation on their donors. This may have overcome any friction added by discussing donors withdrawing their support due to inflation and given the visitor a clear way of getting involved while respecting their budget.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 51.1% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 42.6% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The impact on donor conversion of saying “Donate” vs “Give” on the global navigation menu donation button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “Donate Now” on the global navigation donation button will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2022 - 08/12/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a recent test, we added a monthly donation button to the global navigation menu and recorded a lift in onetime donor conversion. However, in examining the results of that test, we found that we had several changes between the control and the treatment, muddying the results. This prompted us to launch a series of new tests to determine what influence each of those changes had on donor conversion.
The last of these clarifying tests was to determine the impact of saying, “donate” instead of “give” on the button. In that original test, the control had “give now” and no monthly donation button while the treatment had “donate now” and “give monthly.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for approximately three weeks, we found no statistically significant impact on "donate now" vs the control, "give now" button. This showed us that the change from "give now" to "donate now" in the original test did not impact the donor conversion of visitors to the site. Now, we can retest the addition of a monthly donation button to see if it has a positive impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a video in an ad for Facebook users will achieve higher click rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
In an effort to increase click through rates on a FB ad promoting a survey, we wanted to know if presenting the issue through video format would provide a lift in click through rates. We hypothesized that by offering a different format that presented the narrative in video form, rather than text, more viewers would stop to interact with the ad and hopefully click through
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we were able to see a 49% lift in click through rates. This indicates that the video format stood out in people's feeds and presented the issue in a way that inspired viewers to click through.

However, due to the low number of impressions received by the two ads, we were unable to validate this experiment. The treatment received only $19.37. We also noted that the video format was not the only ad element that was changed. The body text was also shortened and revised to use more urgent language that compelled viewers to act.

In future experiments, we may want to distribute the spend between control and treatment more evenly and ensure enough spend is designated to provide sufficient traffic. If we can validate the lift provided by the video format, we can then test the copy variations against one another.

experiments.title: The impact of a floating shoe icon compared to a floating heart icon on donor conversion for traffic to the website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a floating heart icon will convert as many or more donors as a floating shoes icon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 06/17/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They’ve been testing into ways to convert more of the traffic to their website into donors. Recently, we tested the impact of a floating “shoes” icon that when clicked, led donors to their donation page to provide needy children with a pair of comfortable shoes and socks. We found that the floating icon did improve donor conversion. This led to a question about whether a heart icon leading to their general donation page would be more or less effective than the shoe icon.
We wanted to be able to drive traffic to their general page rather than just their campaign specific shoes page and hoped that this would be as effective or better than the shoes icon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, we were not able to validate any statistical difference on donor conversion between the two floating icons. The primary impact on donor conversion is not limited to a specific design, but rather the fact that there is a floating, eye-catching donation button on the page.
This allows Buckner to use a floating "heart" icon to drive traffic to the general donation page throughout the year and then use the shoes icon during high urgency campaigns raising money for shoes.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value of a premium offer impact donor conversion

rows.name: Reinforcing Value Preposition of the Book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the value of a premium offer on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 06/13/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. FamilyLife uses multiple premium offers on social media to acquire new donors. Their most popular premium is the Seeing Beautiful Again Devotional. They wanted to see if reinforcing the value proposition of the premium would increase donor conversion by making it more appealing to donors. They did this by creating a treatment that included social proof of what other donors said about the resource as well as changing the headline of the page to be more value driven. Instead of, “Seeing Beautiful Again” the treatment headline read, ” Gain healthier ways to process your pain through truth-based perspectives,” which we believed better emphasized the value the resource would bring to donors. We split traffic 50/50 between both pages and ran the test for 25 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The changes made to reinforce the value of the premium did not have a statistically significant impact on donor conversion or donor revenue. While we saw user segments having conflicting impact, higher donor conversion on new visitors vs returning visitors, no segment's impact was able to be validated. Instead of focusing on the value proposition of the premium, we will shift our strategy to testing tactical elements on the page to see if that will improve conversion.

experiments.title: How condensed copy affects conversion rates on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing copy on an instant donation page for a survey offer will increase conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2022 - 07/20/2022
We wanted to improve conversion rates on an instant donation page for a survey offer being run by Texas Public Policy Foundation. In order to do so, we decided to test condensed body copy that spends fewer words restating the problem, places a CTA in the headline, and calls attention to the benefits of joining the Come and Take It Society.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 34 days, we observed a 32.8% lift in conversion rates, with a 58.3% level of confidence. An insufficient amount of transactions completed during the testing period prevented us from validating this experiment.

We believe that reducing the copy may have helped provide this lift by becoming more action-oriented, reducing the scroll required to reach the form and by focusing on the impact a donor will have, sooner, while making the CTA clear in the headline.

While we were not able to validate this lift in conversion rates, the results are encouraging. Next, we will want to test different elements on the condensed copy version of this landing page to further this learning and continue to improve results.

experiments.title: How does removing intro language affect pledge participation for mobile visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing intro language for mobile users will achieve higher sign-up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/24/2022
In an effort to increase sign-up rates among mobile users who have been sent a pledge by The Heritage Foundation, we decided to test removing the intro language above the pledge to reduce friction by eliminating scroll and hopefully prompt more mobile users to complete the pledge and provide their email address.
We wondered if the introduction language was causing users to bounce because they had to scroll to see the pledge
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 90 days, we observed a slight 3.2% lift in sign-up rates, with a 74.3 level of confidence.

Despite being unable to validate our hypothesis, we were able to glean some interesting learnings from this experiment.

First, it seems that removing the intro language did not have a significant effect on sign-up rates, even though the intro language requires a user to scroll before seeing the pledge. This may be because the reduced scroll in the treatment is offset by a motivation to act caused by the problem-agitating into copy found in the control.

Second, as we drill down into the data we see that while the treatment (with intro language removed) did produce a modest lift in sign-up rates, it also produced a significant 27.9% decrease in donations (94% level of confidence) ... indicating that the inclusion of intro language that creates urgency around the issue at hand provides significant motivation for people to act downstream by making a donation.

One final metric of note is that removing the intro language did produce a 58.2% increase in email acquisition (100% level of confidence) for housefile email visitors, indicating that people already familiar with Heritage are more aware of the problem and thus require less motivation to complete the pledge.

Based on these learnings, it does not appear that there was a significant benefit to removing the intro language for mobile users in this offer. And because it produced a sizable decrease in instant donation rates, with only a slight lift in email acquisition, we can deduce that the benefits of including intro language outweigh the impact of reducing scroll to reach the pledge sooner on mobile devices.

experiments.title: How will injecting urgency into a sticky bar affect donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that injecting urgency into a sticky bar for prospective donors will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/08/2022 - 07/03/2022
During a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test two versions of a sticky bar to see which performed better in increasing donation rates on a campaign donation page.
For this experiment, we created two different sticky bar treatments and ran the experiment for the length of the campaign, 22 days
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 22 days, we observed a 41.5% lift in conversion rates produced by the sticky bar with urgency, however, we were unable to validate these results with a level of confidence of just 46%.

When reviewing the results, we also realized that the traffic who experienced each version was not evenly split ... with 58.6% of visitors experiencing version A and only 41.4% of visitors experiencing version B. Both versions earned 6 conversions, but it is not possible for us to know with certainty how this would have changed if a more even split of traffic had occurred.

We will need to test this hypothesis again in the future to find out if adding urgency in sticky bar language will improve donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a survey confirmation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for survey takers will achieve a lift in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2022 - 06/17/2022
Email conversion rates on a survey offer were performing exceptionally well but the instant donor conversion was sufferably low. We wanted to produce a radical redesign treatment to take advantage of the high traffic coming to the page and see if we could improve conversion. We added a personalized headline, and brought in recency and urgency to the timeline of the issue throughout the body copy. We also introduce the concept of donor autonomy by asking “Would you consider…” and saying “If you make a gift today…” and “Will you join?” Rather than using bullet points to highlight principles of belief like the control, we instead outline the ways their gift will be used in these bullet points demonstrating the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment we observed a 9.8% lift in donations with a 27.5% level of confidence so the test did not validate. When reviewing the results, we hypothesized that both the control and the treatment copy are long and required a lot of scrolling on the page to actually reach the donation form itself that could be causing unnecessary friction. For the next test on this page, we will develop a treatment that has shorter copy and leaves the donation form in view.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and more clarity to a landing page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the headline and adding social proof in the form of a testimonial would result in a higher number of email conversions for people that land on this campaign landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 06/28/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof in the form of a testimonial, and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around a 27% for the treatment.

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 28%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof in the form of a testimonial were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How aligning the messaging of a landing page to recent news events affects the conversion rate of page that has historically performed well
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding recency copy to the headline and body copy for an email sign up page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign had been steady for a long time, as this offer has been part of the Focus on the Family ecosystem for quite some time, but there is always room for improvement.
Due to recent tragic events that transpired at the time of this experiment, we felt we could align the messaging of this offer to the headlines of the day…without specifically calling out the tragedy itself. We wanted the messaging to remain evergreen, even if the treatment won out. We decided to test if adding recency copy to the headline and body copy would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a 38% lift in email conversions for the treatment, with a 52% level of confidence.
With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 52%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the recency copy that aligned with the news headlines of the time would have continued to outperform the control. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions during the short timeframe, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
The copy points, while aligned with the recent headlines, still remain evergreen enough to keep live, and could continue to see gains in conversions if we make this treatment the new control.

experiments.title: How aligning the graphic elements from an ad with the graphic elements on a landing page affect conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that matching the creative assets from the ad running on social media to the graphics on the landing page will result in higher conversions for visitors to the email landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs).
It is common for this team to update the ad creative in Facebook if we see that the ad conversion rates could use improvement. It is not as common to also have the graphics from the campaign match exactly the images on the landing page. While the change in creative helped the ad conversion rates at the “top of the funnel”, we noticed the signup conversion rates on this campaign (in the “middle of the funnel”) began to decline after making the creative switch. We felt this was an opportunity to test creative congruence between the new ad creative and the existing landing page.
For this particular experiment, in the treatment, we changed out creative assets on the email sign up page to match that of the new ad creative running on Facebook, and we made a slight tweak to the headline copy to add more clarity. The control was left as is.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw very little difference between the control and the treatment. In fact, the control (with a different graphic treatment between the ad and the landing page) was slightly ahead on email conversions (by just 3).

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and graphic treatment). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 10%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the graphic treatment caused the small decrease in conversions. We can say that we gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages. In the end, there were really no positive or adverse effects to making the creative match.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity to a landing page headline affects sign up conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline for readers that land on this page will achieve a higher number of email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw a decrease of around a 27% for the treatment, with a 27% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and increased emphasis on the call to action text - not shown in the screenshot).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 27%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the increased emphasis on the CTA were the cause of the drop in conversions. We can say that we saw a decrease in conversions and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and headline clarity affect email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and headline clarity  for readers of a landing page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof under a landing page image and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around 2% for the treatment with a 20% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 20%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How longer, value prop-based language impacts donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that longer, value-prop focused language on a donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/25/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for their Missionary Care fund. In order to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted to test longer, value prop-focused language on the donation page. Our hypothesis is that the longer copy will drive more donations because it is giving the prospective donor a fuller reason to give towards Missionary Care. Traffic to this donation page came from campaign emails and sticky bar traffic from their website.
experiments.keyLearnings: While initially the results seemed to indicate that the longer copy outperformed the shorter, direct copy, the experiment ultimately resulted in a 17% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 57%. The treatment made no substantial difference to the end results. This concept can be retested by moving the gift array to the bottom oof the donation page so that the site visitor needs to read through the copy before getting to the intended call to action. It's likely that because the gift array was at the top, some site visitors may not have scrolled down to read the intended copy.

experiments.title: How removing an image and adding a reader-centric headline affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image and providing a reader-centric headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2022 - 06/23/2022
The Navigators were running acquisition ads to promote their 7 Tips to Help Others Grow in Christ offer. As they tried to optimize ad creative, they also looked at the landing page to see if there was anything that might be hindering conversion.
They hypothesized that the size of the image might be pushing the form and value proposition too far down the page (especially on mobile), and that removing the image might increase conversion. At the same time, they also noticed that the headline simply declared the title of the offer, and didn’t offer any value to the reader. They decided to test this as well in a “C” variant, which compounded on top of the image removal.
They split the traffic three ways to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither experiment had a material impact on conversion, regardless of device type. This showed them that the image was not hindering conversion (and removal of it didn't help), and that the new headline did not increase motivation or clarity to accept the offer.
They wanted to try a radical redesign next that dramatically altered the layout of the page to see if that would move the needle.

experiments.title: The impact of reinforcing the value of a premium offer on donor conversion

rows.name: Reinforcing Value Preposition of the Book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the value of a premium offer on the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 06/13/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. FamilyLife uses multiple premium offers on social media to acquire new donors. Their most popular premium is the Seeing Beautiful Again Devotional. They wanted to see if reinforcing the value proposition of the premium would increase donor conversion by making it more appealing to donors. They did this by creating a treatment that included social proof of what other donors said about the resource as well as changing the headline of the page to be more value driven. Instead of, “Seeing Beautiful Again” the treatment headline read, ” Gain healthier ways to process your pain through truth-based perspectives,” which we believed better emphasized the value the resource would bring to donors. We split traffic 50/50 between both pages and ran the test for 25 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The changes made to reinforce the value of the premium did not have a statistically significant impact on donor conversion or donor revenue. While we saw user segments having conflicting impact, higher donor conversion on new visitors vs returning visitors, no segment's impact was able to be validated. Instead of focusing on the value proposition of the premium, we will shift our strategy to testing tactical elements on the page to see if that will improve conversion.

experiments.title: How using a homepage takeover vs a pop up impacted giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a home page takeover during a high urgency campaigh for website visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2022 - 07/04/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign (Fiscal Year End) and wanted to drive more donations during the last week of the campaign. In previous campaigns RTB has done a homepage takeover or a pop up at the end of the campaign to direct website traffic to the donation page. In order to discern which method works best for their audience we decided to run a test where half of their site visitors would get a homepage takeover and the other half would get a pop up. Our initial hypothesis is that more web visitors would convert to donors on a homepage takeover because it takes up more real estate and in turn draws more attention to the campaign. A pop up can be easily dismissed on a webpage.
experiments.keyLearnings: When looking at all traffic, this experiment resulted in a 21% lift in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 40%.  When we observe only new visitors we see a higher level of confidence: 82% lift with a level of confidence of 77%. While this did not validate, it is probable that new visitors to the site may have been confused by seeing an entirely new landing page (the Fiscal Year End donation page) and therefore responded more to the pop up. However, since this experiment only ran for the last week of the campaign, we did not see sufficient enough traffic to validate the results. It is recommended that this is retested over a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a ebook offer to the homepage affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a free ebook offer for site visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/20/2022
Reasons to Believe has had a generic newsletter sign up at the bottom of their homepage. In a previous experiment, we added more clarity in the language to increase sign up to their newsletter. When that did not result in a lift in email acquisition, we decided to try a more radical approach. In order to inspire more email subscriptions, we replaced the newsletter subscription with an ebook offer. Wanting to reduce friction, we embedded the actual sign up form in this content block so that the site visitor did not have to visit another page to get this offer. Once people filled out the form, they were directed to an instant donation page.
Traffic to this site was primarily organic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for nearly a month, we saw no real impact in email conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 1% decrease in email conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 6%. This means that the user was not motivated to give more based off of an offer. Given that the form as at the very bottom of the homepage could indicate that only those who are more invested in their site (enough to scroll all the way down) were already looking to take a next step--whether that was signing up for their newsletter or getting an ebook. We saw no difference in new visitors vs returning visitors. Nor did we see a difference in donation activity.
It's suggested that we optimize the newsletter sign up form by focusing on a different aspect of the value proposition, like exclusivity. In addition to that, we can test the offer once more but making it a slide out pop up so that the user does not have to scroll all the way to the bottom of the page to take advantage of this offer.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned landing page impacted email acquisition for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity and credibility through imagery for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in online course subscribers.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/21/2022
On a past eBook offer, we had seen a highly visual landing page increase email conversion so we wanted to test the same hypothesis on an online course offer. We developed a treatment that highlighted an image of Tim Keller, one of the instructors featured in the course, to add some clarity and credibility to the offer. We split traffic between the plain-text control vs. the radically redesigned layout of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw there was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rate with just a 23.3% level of confidence so the test is not valid and the results were flat. One interesting thing to note is when we looked downstream one step further into the funnel, donations experienced a 68.2% decrease with a 86% level of confidence in the treatment, indicating a directional decline. We believe this is because the highly designed landing page doesn't have enough synergy with the plain-text donation page that follows it, making the control landing page more congruent with the experience for each step. Next, we will test adding the Faith & Work course graphic on the plain-text page to see if adding a visual element will help indicate to visitors they are taking the correct next step to get the offer and increase email acquisition.

experiments.title: How aligning with a donor’s beliefs impacted conversion on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing donor alignment and donor autonomy for post-survey donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 03/27/2022
We were running a survey to the housefile and wanted to test the concept of introducing donor alignment and donor autonomy in treatment copy that would improve donor conversion. The control copy outlines the problem, gives urgency, and shares how the donor’s gift is a solution to the problem. But for the treatment, we developed a version that asks the donor to align themselves with specific beliefs in an attempt to get them to “nod their head” as they read along or give the opportunity for a step by step “micro yes” at each new paragraph on the page. We pose TGC’s beliefs and then ask the donor “Do you believe this as well?” We also ask a question that feeds off the momentum of the survey they just responded to: “If you believe TGC has made a positive impact on your faith and spiritual growth, would you consider giving back today?” Throughout the survey, the readers had a chance to tell us just how much value they had personally received from TGC resources. The donation page treatment introduces an opportunity for them to provide that same value to others. We open the donation page with a conversational question, “In today’s noise world what source can you trust for truth” as opposed to our control copy which leads with a statement “The digital world is a challenging place, full of unhelpful influences.” Rather than talking AT our donors, we made additional effort to talk TO them, and bring them into being a part of the mental conversation on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed was a 14.3% increase in conversion rate on the treatment with a 49.4% level of confidence so the test results are invalid. We did notice some directional increases and decreases for various audience segments: Returning visitors had a 40.0% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence. Email visitors had a 29.2% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence. 65+ visitors had an 18.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence. Finally, mobile visitors had a 67.7% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence. Because the confidence level for overall traffic is so low, we would need to re-run this test with more overt changes to be able to discern any true impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How removing a video on a donation page affects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a campaign donation page that does NOT feature a promotional video will convert more potential donors than a page that DOES feature a video.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2022 - 05/16/2022
Focus on the Family has an annual high urgency campaign called See Life to help fund their Option Ultrasound program. This annual campaign has a $4 million dollar donor match fund and attracts a highly motivated potential donor base. With this campaign, Focus on the Family wanted to test whether or not having a video on the donation page caused any additional friction for getting visitors to make a donation. Their control featured a 3 minute promotional video for the Option Ultrasound program just above the donation form. The treatment removed the video from the donation page all together.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for just over a month, and saw both an increase for the treatment (no video) and a decrease for the treatment over the course of 4 weeks, before finally settling on a 1.1% increase in conversion rate for the treatment by the time the experiment ended. Due to a lack of sample size (19k samples vs the required 491k samples) and a low level of confidence (21.4%), this experiment did not validate. This shows that having a video on this particular campaign donation page had no relative difference in the donation intentions for the majority of visitors.
Of particular note is that running this experiment for a campaign that has a very high donation intention could potentially skew the results. We suggest running this experiment again for a campaign that does not have a donor base that is as highly motivated as this one. This might provide better learning as to whether or not showing a video on a donation page either helps or hinders the number of donors that actually make a gift.
Also of note, while revenue experienced a 55.6% increase with a 99% level of confidence, this number was skewed by a couple of large individual donations.

experiments.title: How Asking Donors for a Donation Amount Based on Their Prior Giving Impacted Donor Conversion During a High Urgency Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking donors for a donation amount based on their prior giving will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/10/2022
Live Action is a pro-life organization that uses online content and breaking-news stories to change hearts and minds on abortion and reveal abortion’s impact on everyone involved. During their 5-day Spring Monthly Donor High Urgency Campaign, Live Action wanted to see if they could increase donor conversion by asking each donor for a specific donation amount that was based on their prior giving history. Donors receiving this treatment would be thanked for their gift and told that instead of that amount, they could have an even greater impact by giving this new amount on an ongoing basis as monthly donors.
The Control version would say we needed 250 new monthly donors by midnight on April 30th. The treatment version would use a chart of standards AND the donor’s prior gift to calculate a giving amount and donor goal. Example:
Friend of Life, this is why I’m looking for 100 donors to step forward and give $10 by Saturday, April 30th. Can you be one of these donors who saves lives every single day? I know you have given as much as $10 in the past, but today I’m not asking for that much this one time. Instead, would you consider giving just 10 to start your monthly partnership? 2,300 babies face death every day.
We hypothesized that this approach that combined a chart of standards AND donors prior to giving history would increase conversion. We ensured that the customized giving amount would push to the donation page and prepopulate the donor form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, due to the small sample size, we were not able to approach statistical reliability. However, we did validate this approach with the lapsed donor file during the same campaign.

People primarily give to people, not organizations or causes. By reminding the donor of their last gift and talking about how this new amount could extend the impact of their generosity further, we increased donor conversion. We will update this analysis with a breakdown of the reactivation rates by donor segment (broadbase and midlevel) to see if this approach had a greater impact on one audience over another.

We will also take every opportunity we can to give donors tangible appeals that are directly linked to their history of support and relationship with the organization.

experiments.title: How a “Learn More” CTA on a Facebook Ad Impacted Donor Conversion When Compared with “Donate Now.”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “Donate Now” CTA on a Facebook Ad will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2022 - 04/29/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas. For their spring fundraising campaign, they wanted to see if changing the text on the call to action button on an ad would increase donor conversion. Specifically, if saying “donate now” instead of “learn more” would increase donor conversion on the ad. When we tested “Donate Now” with another organization, it increased donor conversion, so KCBI hoped this would work with their audience as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, due to a low donor response, we really didn't have any statistical level of significance for donations or cost per click. We will keep this in mind and see if we can find further opportunities for testing this approach with their followers on Facebook.

experiments.title: How a Personal Introduction at the Beginning of an Appeal to Non-Doners Impacts Donor Conversion During a High Urgency Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal introduction at the beginning of an appeal will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2022 - 04/26/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas. For their spring fundraising campaign, they wanted to see if beginning an appeal with a personal story and introduction by the sender would increase donor conversion. When testing with another organization, we found that beginning an appeal like this increased engagement by humanizing the copy and building rapport. We know from research that donors give to people, not organizations, so we wanted to see if this would increase conversion during an urgent campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, while we had a great number recipients open both versions, our donations were too few to achieve statistical reliability. We will keep testing into more conversational and personalized intro copy to see if we can validate this approach with their audience.

experiments.title: How shortening the number of fields for a ‘plan your visit’ form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing form fields for a prospective visitor will achieve  a higher sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 03/31/2022
First Baptist Dallas has a ‘plan your visit’ form where they ask prospective visitors for Sunday’s church services a series of questions. The form had multiple required entries; we wanted to test whether splitting the form into two parts drove a higher conversion rate.
In our treatment version, the first part of the form just had basic questions (name, email), while the second part then asked the rest of the demographic questions that were present on the control form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the number of email signups was the same, the 2-step form had a slightly higher conversion rate than the control version, with a 10% increase in email acquisition.
Since filling out the form was not a requirement for attending church services on Sunday, the number of form fills was not enough to fully validate the experiment. Moreover, there was confusion on the back end with the 2-step form, as they were getting duplicate submissions (one with the name/email, one with the rest of the information asked).
For our next test of this, we will test streamlining the back-end process, as well as making the form fill a required part of church attendance. We would also test incentivizing form fills by offering a premium.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array from a donation page impacts revenue during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the gift array from the campaign donation page for page visitors  will achieve increased conversions and higher revenue per visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2021 - 07/09/2021
RTB’s donation pages always have a gift array to offer a preselected giving amount for the site visitor. For RTB’s 2021 FYE campaign, we wanted to test removing the gift array on the campaign donation page to see how this would impact giving (donations and revenue). The idea behind it is that visitors would give more without being guided by a gift array and having clear direction to choose their own giving amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While revenue only decreased 6.7% with a level of confidence of 55%, donor conversion rate had a directional decrease of 11.9% (LoC 88%). Though the data is not statistically valid, the results seem to indicate the the gift array is helpful in providing people a guide on how much they should give. If they aren't given pre-selected amounts, they are less likely to give on the donation page. It is recommended that further testing is done on the gift array. Changing the amounts, the ascending/descending order and the placement in relation to the one-time/monthly option are all potential next steps for testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 101.6% increase in revenue with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 139.4% increase in revenue with an 89% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 131.7% increase in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the gift array  for donation page visitors will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2021 - 07/29/2021
RTB had previously tested removing the gift array from a donation page during a high urgency campaign but the results were inconclusive. In order to try and garner valid results, they retested this concept again at another point of their high urgency campaign, on a different donation page. The hypothesis is that removing the gift array from the donation page may increase revenue and/or donor conversion rate as prospective donors feel like they have more autonomy in the giving process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the gift array resulted in fewer conversions and a decrease in revenue. The experiment results indicate that donation page visitors give more and give greater when they have a gift array available for guidance. A gift array takes some of the decision out of play for potential donors; instead of a donor having to think of a giving amount on their own, they instead have options presented to them, easing their decision-making.
Furthermore, removing the gift array resulted in lower revenue. This shows that a donor's "best gift" is often lower than what it would be if

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer and value proposition language impacts donor conversion in a higher urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity through value proposition language and motivation through a giving thermometer for potential monthly donors will achieve increased monthly donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2021 - 10/01/2021
RTB was running their monthly partner campaign in which their goal was to grow their number of monthly donors. We ran a test on RTB’s monthly partner donation page where two variables were tested.
We added value proposition language to each monthly giving button/option. The thought was that we could increase donor conversion rate by providing clarity on what their gift is going towards.
We also added a thermometer that indicated new monthly donors compared to RTB’s monthly partner campaign goal. We thought that adding clarity through value prop to what different levels of monthly giving can provide in addition to a visible goal that visitors can contribute to would lead to more monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in fewer conversions than the control; however, the difference was marginal.  This experiment resulted in a 68% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 70%. Therefore the results of this experiment did not validate. The run time of the experiment was only a week and could have validated given more time. However, there were two vastly different concepts/variables tested in this experiment. It is recommended that this is retested by isolating a single variable. From this experiment, we don't know if the fewer conversions were a result of the thermometer, added value prop, or both.

experiments.title: Does adding a security box around payment fields on a donation page affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a security box around the payment information section of the donation form for donation page visitors will achieve greater revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2021 - 10/30/2021
On RTB’s main donation page, we thought that adding a secure symbol and a security box around the payment information part of the donation page would encourage page visitors to give and to give more. While transactions to this page are already secure, this visual representation can encourage more donations as prospective donors feel like their payment information is safe with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a lengthy period of time, the results still did not validate. We saw a 14% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 65%.
The security box around the payment information portion of the donation form resulted in lower revenue/visitor and lower average gift. The appeal of a "secure" checkout did not encourage donors to give more.
One can conclude that RTB donors already trust the organization and adding a secure box did not persuasde or dissuade them enough to give.
An additional test idea would be to add a security box around the entire donation form. Perhaps if donation page visitors saw the secure symbol earlier in the process they would be encouraged to give and to give more.

experiments.title: How optimizing for a specific purchase event affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for a specific non-sponsorship action for Lamplighters prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2021 - 07/14/2021
Prison Fellowship International was promoting their Lamplighters donation opportunity and had their Facebook ads optimizing for the typical “purchase” conversion, which had proven to work better than an “email acquisition conversion” or Facebook’s default “donation” conversion.
Since they had sponsorships ($39/month gift) and purchases (one-time gift or recurring gift of any size) both reporting as purchases, they were curious to learn if optimizing for a more specific, non-sponsorship purchase would improve conversion.
They set up an ad set optimizing for non-sponsorship purchases and ran it against one optimizing for all purchases using Facebook’s experiment function.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no difference in conversion rate between the two optimizations. However, Facebook revealed that it was more efficient to optimize for non-sponsor gifts.
See the Facebook results here: https://capture.dropbox.com/y02dzi7qbfEGhhGE

experiments.title: How adding a humanized dear reader to blogs during a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized dear reader during calendar year end for blog readers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2021 - 01/06/2022
During calendar year end, Avila wanted to switch out their typical offer-driven dear reader with a direct appeal for the calendar year end campaign. However, we wanted to humanize this dear reader by adding the founder’s (Dan Burke) picture and signature so that the message and ask were much more humanized. The control of this experiment is written from the perspective of the organization, while the treatment is written from the prospective of the founder. These dear readers were placed at the bottom of the blogs and were live during the last couple weeks of the campaign.
All traffic to these pages were organic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment did not validate and did not indicate any kind of directional lift. The run time of this experiment was fairly short and did not reach the necessary sample size. It's recommended that this concept is tested again while using an offer in the dear reader. This would allow for more time for the experiment to validate and would give us more data on how their audience responds to hearing a message from the founder himself.

experiments.title: How a passive call to action on a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a passive call to action in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2021 - 01/04/2022
During calendar year end, Avila added a site-wide sticky bar to bring attention to their high urgency campaign. We wanted to make it clear that there was an urgent need for the organization during this time but wanted to test how a more passive call to action in the button would impact donor conversion rate. Would audiences be more intrigued to “learn more” on how they can contribute or would the be more motivated to give by being given a clear call to action to “give now”?
experiments.keyLearnings: Results did not validate for this experiment. We saw a 10% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 65%. This experiment did not have a long enough run time to gather the necessary sample size. It is recommended that this concept is retested during the next high urgency campaign and launched earlier in the campaign in order to garner a larger sample size.

experiments.title: How reducing the amount of information required on an event registration form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing form fill fields for event attendees will achieve a higher conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 04/02/2022
First Baptist Dallas was very excited to announce that Rebecca St. James would be the Worship Resident Artist for their Day One worship service. As part of the landing page providing details on the service, First Baptist included a form that had multiple questions. We wanted to test whether moving most of the questions to a 2nd page would impact form conversion rates. For the treatment, we just asked for first/last/email before directing them to a second form that asked the additional questions included in the control version. Our hypothesis was that by reducing the form-fill friction, prospective attendees would be more likely to fill out the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Reducing the number of fields that a website visitor had to fill out actually reduced the conversion rate by 11% for overall traffic. However, there were not enough conversions to provide a valid test result. Moreover, having two separate forms created some confusion for the staff that was managing entries, as they were receiving double form submission (one with the first/last/email, one with the rest of the information) for the treatment version.
We believe there was a decline in conversion rates as the people potentially attending the Day One service were used to filling out multiple fields from other events, as well as wanted to provide as much information as possible up front. Moreover, since filling out the form was not a requirement to attend, it was easy for potential attendees to just ignore it.
We will continue to test this concept, with future tests focusing on a reduction of friction and labor on the back-end, as well as making it clear what filling out the form would actually achieve.

experiments.title: How donor-centric language on a survey confirmation page can lead to increased giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that copy on a survey confirmation page that connects more deeply with the survey participants will lead to increased giving when presented the opportunity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2021 - 01/04/2022
The team at I Am Second ran a calendar year end survey to it’s email file. The survey is a common strategy used during high urgency campaigns, particularly during CYE, getting people to agree and affirm the mission of the organization. After successfully completing a survey, the survey recipient is taken to a confirmation landing page, where they are thanked for their responses, reminded of the mission of the org, and then presented with an opportunity to make a gift.
The team wanted to test language that was LESS about the organization, and MORE about a connection with the donor. The only changes to the treatment were the headline, body copy, and a more succinct CTA. The bullet points that detail how the gift will be used remained the same as the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the experiment did not validate due to a smaller than required sample size, and the level of confidence was below 95%, we did have some pivotal learnings.
While donations from all traffic saw around a ~37% lift with a 48.3% LoC, donations from NEW visitors saw ~ a 107% increase with a 78% level of confidence.
Key Takeaways: By including language that more deeply connects with the potential donor, I Am Second was able to increase their gifts from people that connected with their mission, using momentum to confirm those affirmations, and motivate the donor to take action immediately after survey completion. Also, the doubling of donations on the treatment for the new visitor segment may be a result of all the value prop work the team has been integrating for the new name acquisition file, thus showing that continued cultivation and value re-enforcement can lead to greater financial impact for the org.

experiments.title: How removing a step from the donation process affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a step (a user-initiated click and a secondary form page load) from donation flow for potential Lamplighter donors will improve conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2022 - 04/19/2022
We tested simplifying the donation flow on the main Lamplighters donation page. By including A) the full donation form on the test variant, B) removing a user-initiated click on donation call to action button and C)  removing  a second page load displaying the full form would reduce friction and improve donor conversion rate. Content flow was streamlined from two-columns to a single column, increasing user focus and reinforcing natural vertical page flow for users. Gift arrays within test variants more clearly defined to make it easy for donors to choose between 4 predefined options to support 1, 2, 3 or 4 prisoners. Despite this litany of changes, we did not experience a significant change in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment are inconclusive, with no meaningful change in overall conversion rate. We did see some interesting data across demos and device types, which are detailed below:
Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 334.8% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 64.8% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 149.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
One interesting takeaway here is that the 18-24 audience (a group we currently exclude from our Facebook acquisition marketing efforts) was our best-converting audience with a 4.4% CVR. The second best-converting audience was from the 25-34 age group with a 3% CVR, another audience we are not targeting with our FB ads. We are currently targeting ages 30+ across current Facebook paid social campaigns.

experiments.title: How changing the layout of a campaign donation page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a 2 column layout on a campaign donation page for potential donors visiting the campaign donation page during a HUC will achieve a greater number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2022 - 04/20/2022
During a high urgency fiscal year end campaign, e3 Partners wanted to test a 2 column layout on a campaign donation page to see if there was an appreciable difference in total donations. The control would have a single column layout with a donation form at the bottom. The treatment would have a donation form on the right side of the copy. It is worth noting that the mobile version would default to a single column layout with a donation form at the bottom to provide a better user experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key takeaways from this experiment were:
The timeframe was too short to get any statistical significance A small increase in conversions was not enough to make a judgement either wayIt is worth continuing to experiment with layouts
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 23.1% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding urgency copy w/ a specific ask affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a specific $ amount and specific number of donors for People visiting the website will achieve A higher number of donations than a less specific ask..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2022 - 04/04/2022
I Am Second wanted to see if changing the copy to have a sense of urgency with a specific ask would increase donations when added to a pop up. For the high urgency fiscal year end (FYE) campaign, IAS ran an experiment with 2 versions of a sticky bar pop up that asked for support.
In the control version, the copy asked if the potential donor would make a donation by Midnight on March 31st (IAS FYE). In the treatment version, there was specific copy that asked for “10 people to give $50 by midnight”.
The goal was to see if using a smaller believable amount of people and $ amount needed by a specific time would lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the experiment did not reach statistical validity due to not enough sample size, we did have some positive learnings. We saw a 106.7% increase in donations overall during this very short timeframe. While we saw a 77% Level of Confidence in these results, we did not have a large enough sample size to draw any concrete resolutions. However, we saw enough directional lift to say that this tactic would be worth experimenting with again on future campaigns.
Changes to future experiments may include a phased approach to urgency, where we start with a more generic ask similar to the control, then switch to a version similar to the treatment version with more specificity closer to the close of a high urgency campaign…to have more credibility. After-all…urgency cannot be sped up, only highlighted.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 275.3% increase with a 96% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 2.0% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How changing the content offer affects email acquisition for new visitors

rows.name: 5 Steps to a Better Prayer Life

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the content offer on a pop-up for new visitors will achieve a higher email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2022 - 04/20/2022
