This is the calculation of the total revenue brought in by each segment. These results were calculated by removing all test transactions and free tickets sold.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low percentage in confidence in these results, this hypothesis requires more testing.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a landing page with a seasonal offer impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2021 - 04/08/2021
In the weeks leading up to Easter, a sticky bar on the homepage was pointing visitors to a landing page where they could sign up for a free resource offer with Easter-focused family activities. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the landing page to see if we could increase overall email acquisition on this offer. We developed a treatment that removed the traditional footer with links to alternate pages on the site to avoid distraction from the intended call to action, which was to submit the email acquisition form and sign up for the free PDF.
We also revised the header on the page from “Adventures in Odyssey: Easter Faith Building Activities!” to “Get access to your free copy now and help your family celebrate Easter together with this free PDF of fun ideas for activities and devotionals to prompt meaningful conversations!” in an effort to make the offer less organization-focused and more focused on the page visitor.
The third element we changed for the treatment version of the page was enhancing the overall value proposition and added in language that helped address the why behind someone might benefit from the contents of the free guide rather than only sharing what was inside. We spoke directly to the felt needs of the audience and the emotions they might be experiencing as a parent and how this resource could alleviate some of the stress that often accompanies the anticipation of the Easter holiday.
Finally, we changed the CTA on the form from “To get your FREE download of Easter Faith Building Activities, fill out the form below” to “Tell us where to send your FREE copy of Adventure in Odyssey’s Easter Faith Building Activities:” and switched the button copy from “Download Here” to “Send my free copy »” to make this portion feel like less work for the user. We set up the control and the treatment to run as an A/B test and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we didn't have enough run-time to acquire the number of visitors to the page needed to validate results and we only reached a 35% level of confidence on the experiment. However, what we saw was a few observations on specific visitor segments that are outlined below. Even though we did not reach statistical significance overall, we can use these learnings to guide the development of language for future tests that target display/tablet visitors and speak more relevantly to females and those who are 65+. We could also use this knowledge to our advantage by keeping this audience in mind for introducing social proof on a landing page by adding a testimonial quote from a 65+ female who found the resource valuable. We can also test each of these items from the radical redesign individually on other free offers in the future to have more insight into which changes led to these increases and decreases per segment.
Desktop visitors had a 31.7% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Display visitors had a 42.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 34.9% decrease in outbound link click with a 94% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 33.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 28.5% decrease in outbound link click with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 32.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 39.5% increase in outbound link click with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 72.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had an 86.2% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 1,211.5% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 34.0% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Identity Language affects donor conversion on a fiscal year end donation page
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Bringing clarity to who the donation helps will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2021 - 07/09/2021
During the Oblates Fiscal Year End campaign, we wanted to test identity language on different elements of the campaign. We tested this language on the donation page during the last two days of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there was a distinct increase in giving from the email test, the donation page did not show as clear an impact. Our hypothesis is that the motivation was higher for all donation page visitors than for the people who opened the email. Further testing needs to be done for how we can use this strategy in the future.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security on a donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements to the credit card section increase perceived security and ultimately donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2021 - 07/21/2021
There are many elements of friction on a donation page. One of the most significant areas where friction can be found on these pages is the credit card section. Prospective donors are asked to provide their financial information in this section which can cause anxiety for people during the giving process. We hypothesized that visually telling the donor that their information was secure may impact their liklihood to complete the donation process. To test into this, Save the Children added a padlock image and border around the credit card section to visually communicate to people that their information is safe, it’s secure.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall transactions, there was no impact on donor conversion. It is important to keep in mind that this experiment was executed on the primary donation page where differing motivations from all channels types are pushing traffic. While there wasn't any impact - positive or negative - on overall donor conversion, specific segments did see a significant impact.
On mobile devices, which makes up 60% of Save the Children's traffic, the perceived security feature increased donor conversion by 10% at a 96% level of confidence. As we know, the donor experience on mobile devices is very different from desktop. With the smaller device, everything is a bit more prominent - which would make the visual elements added to the form even more apparent and easing the minds of people as they were giving on their phones. These results also bring up an interesting hypothesis that mobile users may be more concerned about the safety of their information than desktop users. This sounds counterintuitive, but so much is being done now on commerce sites to make purchasing so much easier that people normally don't even have to think about that kind of stuff. The visual reminder may have brought this to their attention and caused them to feel more at ease, confirming in their mind that this was, in fact, secure.
We also saw an increase in giving for returning visitors. There was a 13% increase in donor conversion at a 92% LoC. While this didn't validate for this segment, the results were directional and indicate that they will, more than not, have a positive response to the higher perceived security. It is interesting that new visitors weren't impacted in the same way. Without knowing if these return visitors were previous donors or not, what we can learn from this is that the visual security elements may have been the motivating factor to tip the balance for returning visitors in their decision to actually make a gift.
Because there is no negative impact on any segment or channel through this experiment, and some segments will - with confidence - see an increase in donor conversion, we propose rolling this feature out among all donation pages.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of the wiki banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can increasing the clarity of the value proposition on the wiki banner increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2021 - 07/15/2021
Focus on the Family uses a banner at the top of their website during high urgency campaigns to capture site traffic and convert them into donors. The messaging for this banner is always in alignment with the campaign messaging. It usually includes a headline and a direct call to action. We hypothesized that we could increase donor conversion by increasing the clarity of the value proposition in the banner. To do this, we developed a treatment that communicated the problem and presented a call-to-action as the solution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For all traffic, there was no positive or negative impact on donor conversion. That said, this neutral result is due to the significant differences in impact with opposing segments. Specifically, when looking at new visitors to the site, there was a 4% decrease in conversion; while with returning visitors, there was a 5% increase in donor conversion. What we can learn from this experiment is that the motivation of users on the site are wildly different and the additional value proposition is needed for some site visitors, but not all. This can be dependent upon other messaging they have seen in other channels, and even people's familiarity with the organization. One specific trend we saw in the data is that when people were coming from an outside channel where they may have read or seen messaging for the campaign, the increased value proposition helped to increase donor conversion when people got to the page because the message they had previously read was reinforced through the wiki banner on the top of the website.
Other observations of the impact on visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct visitors had a 14.7% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 16.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 47.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 74.8% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 35.7% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 36.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 4.1% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 4.7% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How presenting the gift frequency as a tabbed form on acquisition pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a tabbed form have a negative impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2021 - 07/11/2021
Focus on the Family has continued to test into the best way to present the gift frequency/gift type section on their donation pages. After learning that presenting it as a tabbed form increased donor conversion by 16% on the primary donation page, they wanted to test this method on other donation pages to ensure that it didn’t have a negative impact on other donation pages so that they could potentially roll this approach out across all donation pages. To test into this, they ran the tabbed form across some of their acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The tabbed form had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. This is a great learning because it confirms that the tabbed approach can be rolled out across all donation pages as the approach to use for presenting the gift frequency/gift kind section.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity that a content offer is free affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making it clear a content offer is free, while tying that to value proposition, will not decrease giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2021 - 07/29/2021
Focus on the Family had been receiving complaints that people didn’t know the video series was free and they believed they needed to make a donation to get the series. We tested one iteration of a donation page that increased the use of the word free in conjunction with the video series. That test decreased donations by 20%. We then tested another iteration of a donation page that focused on clarity and addressed the reader specifically, rather than increasing the reference to the series being free. This did not have a negative impact on donor conversion and only brought more clarity to the page – which was what we had hoped. To make sure that test wasn’t an isolated experience, we tested that exact same approach on one of the most popular video series to see the impact.
This was a 50/50 A/B test that ran for four weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: This iteration of the test didn't decrease donations while it increased clarity that the reader did not have to donate to receive the video series. These results are consistent with the same test we ran on another offer. We will roll this approach out to all acquisition pages.

experiments.title: How a redesign of a newsletter offer impacted clicks from the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks to sign up for a newsletter.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2021 - 08/02/2021
As we evaluated the Adventures in Odyssey homepage, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of people who were clicking on the link to sign up for the newsletter. We wanted to develop a treatment that increased clarity about what the visitor would receive through the newsletter, emphasized the appeal by highlighting the benefit and value of getting access to the content rather than not signing up at all, and indicated the offer was unique, exclusive, and why it is distinct from signing up for any other newsletter. We took the control headline “Sign up for our Newsletter” and replaced it with “Get access to the latest resources for your family”. We also revised the supporting body copy from “Sign up to receive the monthly Adventures in Odyssey newsletter directly to your inbox” to “We’d love to share our most recent and helpful resources with you and your family! Be inspired to grow in your faith through these exclusive updates you won’t find anywhere else!”. And the final element we changed was the button text which read “Subscribe Now” while the treatment version said “Get Access Now”. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and let the experiment run.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach statistical significance on the experiment, but the control saw a 20.8% increase with a 72.5% level of confidence when compared to the results of the treatment. What's particularly interesting is when reviewing the segments, we had a significant portion of samples that were visiting from mobile and who were labeled as "new visitors" but both of these segments show lower conversion rates, so future tests for this page could be more strategically designed to address these types of visitors since they make up the majority of the traffic volume. It also appears that returning visitors are more likely to click to sign up for the newsletter so we could potentially develop additional prompts such as a pop-up on someone's second or third visit that highlights the offer more prominently once we know they have shown a continued interest in the content. Phase two of this experiment involves testing the landing page copy where people end up once someone has clicked the button to subscribe so we can find out if people are actually converting and entering their email address to get the newsletter on the second step of the process after they have clicked through from the homepage.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 131.9% increase in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 97% level of confidence.Direct visitors had an 83.4% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in newsletter clicks with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an alternate form of social proof impacted email acquisition for free trial sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating additional social proof for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition for an offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2021 - 03/15/2021
The long-standing “winner” (in terms of highest conversion rates) sticky bar on the OA Club homepage included a testimonial quote from one user, so we knew that social proof works well for this audience, however we wanted to test if using a different form of the concept of social proof would hold this pattern. We thought that if we could broaden the social proof experiment to apply to more visitors and demonstrate stronger credibility through a third-party then we would see an increase in free trial signups. So we launched a treatment to the sticky bar that said “Join over 140,000 families who have helped their children grow in their faith and learn biblical truths over the past year with a free 14-day trial of Adventures in Odyssey!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we had to end the experiment early to launch another timebound offer on this same page so we did not have enough time to generate the volume of traffic needed to reach validity and saw a 2.8% decrease in conversion rate with just a 21.5% level of confidence. We will want to relaunch the experiment when we have the opportunity to leave it running long enough to get an appropriate amount of samples to analyze. However, over the 10 days the experiment ran, we did observe the following impacts on other visitor segments in the experiment that could help with more specific targeting on future treatments that are developed:
Female visitors had a 113.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 99% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 98.4% increase in begin free trial signup with an 88% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 79.1% increase in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a monthly donation ask email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for a monthly donor ask will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/11/2021
In this monthly donor ask email, Live Action tested an expanded value proposition to become a monthly donor over a shortened version that left out a lot of the benefits of becoming a Life Defender.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight lift in clicks from the expanded value proposition treatment, but ultimately this experiment proved invalid.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
To administer the test, we evenly split the file based upon (a) donor vs. non-donor statuses, (b) the donor’s highest previous contribution tier, (c) how long the person was on the email file, and finally (d) whether or not they were currently considered “engaged” or “disengaged” from the email program (where “engaged” was whether or not they had opened any of the last 10 emails that we had sent to them before splitting the email file evenly for this experiment).
The donor stories were sent from a “secondary sender” for the organization—someone who was planned to be used in two of the year-end campaign messages.
Finally, to ensure that the samples within the study are accurate, we looked at unique individual emails for both segments that received any year-end email (including the “donor story” emails for the treatment segment)
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 53.7% level of confidence, we observed that the treatment segment did not increase revenue in any significant way.

Although this treatment segment saw a decrease in the donor conversion rate of -23.2% (with a valid level of confidence of 99.4%) however, we don't believe that the additional donor story emails drove the decrease in donor conversion rate.

We did notice, however, that there was a +27.8% average gift value for those donors that did decide to give from the treatment segment over that of those in the control segment (who did not receive the donor stories).

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: How including author information affects video series sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Including information on the author/speaker will increase signups and email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2021 - 05/03/2021
Focus on the Family was releasing a new video series and they wanted to see what the impact of including information on the speaker would be on email conversion. The thought was that the broad audience that lands on the sign up page may not recognize the name of the speaker and so knowing more about them would convince the readers to sign up. This was a 50/50 split test and the primary traffic was from social media.
(N.B. The client set up the variable page and because of the nature of the test set up, the variable shows as the control.)
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means including information for the speaker is not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that the placing the information below the decision point of the submission button made the information a second thought and so did not have an impact.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: How specificity in a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: You should read this with your family
You should read this to your kids

experiments.researchQuestion: Will specificity in the subject line increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 11/24/2020
Care Net was sending a cultivation email to their file and wanted to test the element of specificity in their subject line. The control was “You should read this with your family” and the treatment was, “You should read this with your kids”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift from the treatment. This tells us that the specificity from the treatment minimally helped increase engagement, but wasn't a significant enough difference to have a greater impact.

experiments.title: FY21 – CYE Notecard Image Test
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image of the notecard for all donors will achieve higher net revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/08/2021
The test was run during December 2020 in the notecard, which landed in-homes around 12/15. The election was a prominent topic in the news at the time, most receiving it just after the electoral college cast their votes. We wanted to see if changing the image on the outer envelope made a difference. Three versions were tested:
1) The control was a stark, wintery image of SCOTUS
2) The first test was an image of a family with young kids walking in a snowy forest
3) The second test was an image of a wintery barn with an American flag
experiments.keyLearnings: First, image selection matters. Second, the image of SCOTUS, which has been our control, was the better route to go. This is likely because our donors care deeply about our work at the Supreme Court and it felt most like ADF.

experiments.title: How positioning what happens if a donor doesn’t give rather than what happens if they do impact donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning the “fear of loss” (eg what won’t happen if the donor doesn’t give right now) will impact donor conversion rate when soliciting donors within the context of a campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/10/2021
As a part of The Fund for American Studies’ recent Founder’s Day campaign, they were asking high capacity donors to contribute to building a matching gift fund that would be used to challenge other donors to join the movement to rally right now and do more to help more students and teachers in 2021.
We wanted to experiment with what would motivate donors to take action now: either (a) the hope of what could be achieved through their gift right now, or (b) what won’t happen without their gift right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We achieved neither the minimum number of samples we needed nor did we observe a wide enough variance between the two treatments to reliably validate that one variant over the other.

We knew that validating this experiment might be too difficult, considering we were running it against such a small universe of names (of very few high capacity donors on the email file).

For the sake of thoroughness, we decided to check the statistics related to a revenue lift study based upon these results — and we only observed a +6.5% increase in revenue for the treatment, but with only a level of confidence (LoC) of only 56%. Finally, the number of samples achieved on a revenue lift study was nowhere near where we'd need it to be, and it only achieved a "coin flip" likelihood to improve results by only a few percentage points.

All of this is to say: Further experimentation should be considered moving forward but on a much wider universe of available prospects by which we can potentially validate a lift for one variant over another.

experiments.title: How communicating what the benefits are to the donor impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the donor benefits impact conversion on a monthly donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2020 - 12/07/2020
Care Net has a stand alone monthly gift acquisition donation page. In an effort to increase the number of recurring gifts and the overall revenue, they tested adding copy to the page that communicated tangible benefits for the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was only able to run for a short time before traffic to the page was no longer being pushed to it. But during the week the experiment ran, there was no negative impact on monthly donor conversion and we even saw promising data that there could be an increase in overall revenue. While we will want to test this again, these initial data points tells us that the donor benefit language could have a significant impact on overall revenue; people like and want to know what they will receive by becoming a monthly donor and increases motivation to give and give more generously.

experiments.title: How does a cye campaign specific donation page affect revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Calendar year end specific language on a donation page will increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Bill of Rights Institute wanted to see if calendar year end specific language would increase donations from the direct traffic to their site. We ran a 50/50 split between their usual main donate page and their calendar year end campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings: We need to continue to test as the sample size was not large enough to reach validation. We will test this again during the next calendar year end campaign. We will also test longer value proposition in the body copy of their main donation page to see how much that increases donations and revenue.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift, increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.
When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.
When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.
It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.
Because of this, and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: How positioning what happens if a donor doesn’t give rather than what happens if they do impact donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning the “fear of loss” (eg what won’t happen if the donor doesn’t give right now) will impact donor conversion rate when soliciting donors within the context of a campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/10/2021
As a part of The Fund for American Studies’ recent Founder’s Day campaign, they were asking high capacity donors to contribute to building a matching gift fund that would be used to challenge other donors to join the movement to rally right now and do more to help more students and teachers in 2021.
We wanted to experiment with what would motivate donors to take action now: either (a) the hope of what could be achieved through their gift right now, or (b) what won’t happen without their gift right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We achieved neither the minimum number of samples we needed nor did we observe a wide enough variance between the two treatments to reliably validate that one variant over the other.
We knew that validating this experiment might be too difficult, considering we were running it against such a small universe of names (of very few high capacity donors on the email file).
For the sake of thoroughness, we decided to check the statistics related to a revenue lift study based upon these results — and we only observed a +6.5% increase in revenue for the treatment, but with only a level of confidence (LoC) of only 56%. Finally, the number of samples achieved on a revenue lift study was nowhere near where we'd need it to be, and it only achieved a "coin flip" likelihood to improve results by only a few percentage points.
All of this is to say: Further experimentation should be considered moving forward but on a much wider universe of available prospects by which we can potentially validate a lift for one variant over another.

experiments.title: How do campaign emails focused on specific outreaches compare to general campaign emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on specific outreaches individually will increase the donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/29/2021
In preparation for KCBI’s Spring Campaign, we were curious if listing all of the outreaches the an organization is fundraising for clouds the impact and value proposition for each individual outreach. In other words, does talking about keeping radio on the air, saving lives with ultrasounds, and bringing Bibles to prisoners all in one email lessen the value prop and so the donor response? Would it be better to talk about each individual outreach in an email bring more depth the the value of the individual outreaches and so improve donor response. So we tested this theory by splitting their housefile into two groups. One group got 4 emails that talked through all of the outreaches in all four emails. The other group got 4 emails that focused on each outreach individually (i.e. one email was focused on ultrasounds, one email was focused on prison ministries).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The outcome of this test was inconclusive, but we have a few hypotheses as to why.

First, it is important to note that there was no significant increase in the click rate on the different emails. There was a 13% increase at a 66% Level of Confidence, so nothing concrete or indicative of higher motivation either way.

Our hypothesis is that there was not enough distinction in the messages. Many of the test emails replace a paragraph with outreach specific message. If we were to retest this, we would make the emails more focused on the outreaches and make them more central to the message.

However, as we looked in Google Analytics, we found the level of traffic to the donation page was the same for the test and the control. Meaning the same number of unique people went to the donation page from both segments of the test. But, the conversion rate for the people who came from the control is higher. The donation page mentioned all of the outreaches, similar to the control emails. So people coming from the control would experience continuity of message. Whereas people coming from the test emails would then be presented with a donation page that lists all of the outreaches. Our hypothesis is that this created cognitive dissonance in the people in the test segment.

So if we test further, we would make donation pages specific to the outreach itself to take the test through the entire funnel.

experiments.title: How communal language on a primary donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying communal language for main donation page visitors  will achieve increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge had tested “communal” copy that reduced transactional language and attempted to build a relationship with the user first to great success on a series of popups throughout their site.
They wanted to test this copy on their main donation page during the holiday season. They changed the question-based headline to a complimentary headline thanking the donor for their consideration, and put the power to give in the user’s hands with “if it’s not too much to ask”. They also used language that had worked on the popup that removed the Board of Directors from a position of power and put them in a position of service.
experiments.keyLearnings: All in all, the communal language failed to have much of an impact at all in the time that it ran. Motivation is very high for this page, so it can be difficult to affect conversion rate. On further observation, the headline may be confusing for visitors, and that might need to be reworked.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that the placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How changing the email sender affects a time-sensitive membership renewal campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the email sender for the housefile email list will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
For an annual membership campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email envelope for The Heritage Foundation appeals. We tested the open rate for emails from two different senders. The control email included Maggy Smith – a representative from Membership Programs – as the sender, and the treatment email included Kay C. James – President of The Heritage Foundation – as the sender. The subject line and body copy was the same for each email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although there was a 1.6% increase in traffic, the experiment failed to validate at a 79.9% level of confidence. Users may be more inclined to open an email from the president of the company than a representative of Membership Programs; however, the data does not show a significant impact. This idea would need to be re-tested in the future.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift on a child’s future and emotional health will impact sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: That showing what the child’s life is and what life will be after the donors gifts, with all the emotions and impact of that gift, will increase child sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their child specific page, the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor this particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the increased traffic to these pages, there was no discernible difference in sponsorship. A 52% level of confidence is a veritable coin flip either way.
Our hypothesis is that copy alone is not enough to impact conversion one way or the other. Our next step is to test tactical page design test to keep folks on the child sponsorship page and make this the complete decision point. With the current design, users are presented the option to leave the page at several different points. In fact, twenty percent of the gifts that were made on the control were people who left this page to give a donation to the main PFI donation page.
Revenue did experience a 27.9% decrease with a 93% level of confidence. This is because more people were likely to give a one-time gift on the control.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift through feelings and outcomes impact conversion on a sponsorship page
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on the feeling and affects of the problem and showing the change that a donor’s gift will make will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their main sponsorship page, where the audience has the opportunity to see the different children who need a sponsor. It is important to note that this is not the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor a particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings: The outcome of this test showed that this communication tactic does not affect sponsorship conversion by itself. Our hypothesis is this is not a page where people make the decision to sponsor, but to see the different children that they could sponsor. Since this page is not a decision point, a treatment on this page will not have enough conversion to validate with this level of traffic. The next step is to focus on the child specific pages.

experiments.title: How personalizing the donation page with the reader’s first name impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that speaking to someone using their first name for visitors to the donation page will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2021 - 04/09/2021
Focus had a new offer running around Biblical truth in a broken world. It had a strong conversion rate and a lot of traffic. So we tested a personalization by passing the person’s first name from the landing page to the donation page and adding it to the header. Instead of just saying “Congratulations!” it said “Congratulations, John!” This has worked well in the past for other organizations and we wanted to test it for the Focus audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the opposite of expected. Using the reader's first name actually decreased donations by 36% and revenue by 37% with a directional decrease. This level of decrease held true across almost all devices and demographics.
Our hypothesis is that it caused more friction by the surprise of seeing their own name. The other possibility is that it made the Congratulations more noticeable and led them to believe their work on the page was complete and they did a good job and could now leave the page. We are now testing a form design on this page rather than a personalization, to see if making the choices in giving more clear and the process easier will increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How do campaign emails focused on specific outreaches compare to general campaign emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on specific outreaches individually will increase the donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/29/2021
In preparation for KCBI’s Spring Campaign, we were curious if listing all of the outreaches the an organization is fundraising for clouds the impact and value proposition for each individual outreach. In other words, does talking about keeping radio on the air, saving lives with ultrasounds, and bringing Bibles to prisoners all in one email lessen the value prop and so the donor response? Would it be better to talk about each individual outreach in an email bring more depth the the value of the individual outreaches and so improve donor response.
