Our hypothesis is that there was not enough distinction in the messages. Many of the test emails replace a paragraph with outreach specific message. If we were to retest this, we would make the emails more focused on the outreaches and make them more central to the message.

However, as we looked in Google Analytics, we found the level of traffic to the donation page was the same for the test and the control. Meaning the same number of unique people went to the donation page from both segments of the test. But, the conversion rate for the people who came from the control is higher. The donation page mentioned all of the outreaches, similar to the control emails. So people coming from the control would experience continuity of message. Whereas people coming from the test emails would then be presented with a donation page that lists all of the outreaches. Our hypothesis is that this created cognitive dissonance in the people in the test segment.

So if we test further, we would make donation pages specific to the outreach itself to take the test through the entire funnel.

experiments.title: How including author information affects video series sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Including information on the author/speaker will increase signups and email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2021 - 05/03/2021
Focus on the Family was releasing a new video series and they wanted to see what the impact of including information on the speaker would be on email conversion. The thought was that the broad audience that lands on the sign up page may not recognize the name of the speaker and so knowing more about them would convince the readers to sign up. This was a 50/50 split test and the primary traffic was from social media.
(N.B. The client set up the variable page and because of the nature of the test set up, the variable shows as treatment.)
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means including information for the speaker is not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that the placing the information below the decision point of the submission button made the information a second thought and so did not have an impact.

experiments.title: How a copy-heavy popup ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more value heavy ad for Top Nonprofits visitors will achieve more email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2020 - 12/20/2020
In this experiment, we were running a pop-up ad with a nonprofit marketing partner. For the sake of time, we whipped up version A with minimal copy and a very simple design.
But once the campaign launched, we wondered if running a very text-heavy ad would actually lead to emails since it communicated the value far more effectively.
We assumed we would get lower clicks, but we thought the clicks we would get would be more qualified – thus leading to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw the exact same amount of conversion from both ads. As predicted, version B had significant decrease in clicks (158 VS 67), but a much higher conversion rate for those that viewed the landing page (6.6% VS 15%).

In this case, the drop in clicks and increase in conversion rate meant that our overall acquisition was a wash.

This indicates to me that there may be a happy medium between these two ad variations. It would be worth testing a new ad treatment with some value prop copy to help qualify the click, but leave more to be desired to discover on the next page.

experiments.title: Will a more personal email inviting people to a webinar increase engagement and signups?

rows.name: How can you improve your online fundraising, Steph
How can you improve your online fundraising, Gianc

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a more human, personal email for email subscribers will achieve more clicks and therefore webinar registrants.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2021 - 02/05/2021
NextAfter does at least one webinar a month and sends an email to big part of their email file to invite them to it. Previous testing has shown that a personal style email with some design elements works best. These ‘personal’ emails are often from someone, in this case Brady, but written by someone else, in this case Riley. For this test, we wanted to see if an even more personal email from and written by Brady with more personal anecdotes and tone would increase engagement (clicks) and therefore webinar signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: In the end the added personalization didn't help or hurt. When we looked at conversions there was a 3.2% increase in signups (not significant) and a slight click to conversion rate increase (82% vs. 81%, also not significant) but neither were valid. When we looked at replies back there was also a slight increase in replies to the more personal email but also not valid. So the more personal email with Brady's tone had no significant difference in driving engagement, clicks, and signups.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a premium offer impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2019 - 12/16/2019
For the month of December, a special premium was being offered with a membership purchase. When evaluating the landing page copy, we wanted to develop a treatment that would increase conversions to the membership page. The control jumped right into the details about the premium and membership without giving a clear motivation for considering a Club Membership. We expanded the value proposition with a paragraph to enhance the value-add before launching into the and details of the Club benefits and premium information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we only had a short window to direct traffic to the treatment landing page. That being said, the experiment did not receive enough traffic to validate fully, but we did find that mobile visitors had a 20.1% increase in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence. We'll continue to find opportunities to introduce similar language on other landing pages that have a longer life span and see if we can observe a valid lift on conversions in the future on other premium offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 60.6% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 23.6% decrease in outbound link click with an 90% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 131.4% increase in outbound link click with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How interrupting the donation process with a value proposition question affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a slide-out question shown on the donation form improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2015 - 11/18/2015
Based upon the results of a previous experiment, we had learned that if we solicit a donor’s opinion while they are making their gift, it can actually improve conversion rate. Our previous test was visually similar to a gift designation question that is often seen on other organizations’ donation form but we framed in a donor-centric manner.
Our concern with this tactic is that by placing it inline with the rest of the donation form, it was adding an element of friction that may have adversely impacted a segment of the visitors that didn’t need the added value reinforcement. We decided to use the same question but move it into a window that slid out as the donor scrolled down the page. The hypothesis was that this would remove it from the donation form which would reduce the visual friction but would still have the same impact that was seen with the original test.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant difference between the form with the slide out question and our standard form that didn't ask the question at all. It would appear that the lift achieved with our previous test was, in part, due to how the question was positioned to the donor. By making it part of the donation form, it became a question that the donor had to answer before moving forward. This value that it reinforced the in donor's mind outweighed the added friction associated with the questions.

experiments.title: How the use of the word “you” affects email acquisition via an eBook offer on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: The use of the word “you” will increase conversion because it is addressing and engaging the reader specifically.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/23/2021
As we were launching a new eBook offer for Prison Fellowship International, the original language for the funnel copy included a lot of reader centered language focused on the outcome for the reader and the purpose for the reader, which often included the use of the word “you.” As that was not typically the communication style for PFI, they preferred to use more general language about how the eBook benefits people in general. To ensure that this did not affect the outcome, we wanted to test this in the Facebook marketplace.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the funnel copy focused more on the outcome for the reader did not affect the email conversion rate dramatically enough to continue using it or continue testing. However, there were two other key metrics that we needed to examine as well. First, the click through rate was 8% lower on the reader centered language. And that was a key metric for this funnel, because the cost per click was high, making the funnel more difficult to scale. Second, the ultimate goal was to acquire new donors. And the donor conversion for the more general language brought in two times more donors. For these two key reasons, we discontinued the test before reaching validation to place spend on the more efficient funnel.

experiments.title: How adding a blog description above an article can lead to an increase in clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the article descriptions above the article image  for the highlight section in the newsletter will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2021
At the bottom of RTB’s weekly newsletter email, they would have a “highlights” section where they would link to other articles/blogs. Below the articles, they would have a paragraph that would go into detail about what each blog/article was. The hypothesis was if we move the detailed description of what each article was directly above the article it would then lead to more clicks.
This was set up as a 50/50 split and was sent to their entire newsletter subscription file.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall this experiment was a wash. We saw a slight increase in clicks but this experiment was nowhere close to validating. While this experiment didn't validate we did see that adding the description above the articles didn't lead to a decrease in clicks so we are looking to test this again.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual those segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.

When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.

When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.

It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.

Because of this and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: How aligning the image and value proposition for a content offer affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the image with the offer and increasing the clarity of the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2021 - 06/12/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey resource through Facebook ads. Their cost-per-name was above what they projected, and they wanted to see how they could optimize their results. A previous test had shown that an image that was more specific to the offer greatly increased conversion rate and CPA, so they wanted to apply that to the landing page as well. Additionally, they wanted to test the content on the landing page to see if increasing clarity of the offer and removing vertical space could increase conversion.
They created two treatments and ran an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither landing page made a significant difference in conversion rate. There was directional evidence that the new image provided a slight bump to conversion rate, but it wasn't near significance.
The 29% conversion rate on the landing page is still low compared to other Navigators offers, but this test suggests that the issue is upstream from the landing page—perhaps in the targeting or in the advertising.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: LWF – Email Subject Line Test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the best results for LWF?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/15/2021
Testing subject lines and the preheader. The results on opens and clicks were very similar, however the treatment had a higher conversion rate when it came to donations. While not above the 95% confidence level it does give us something to build upon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that was tested may have done a better job of attracting people that wanted to get the series as it framed the content as what a person would get out of the book when they read it.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected housefile engagement for a cultivation email

rows.name: Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing “you” to add relevance in a subject line for a cultivation series to the housefile will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
We were noticing a recent decline in open rate for a weekly cultivation email series to the housefile so we wanted to test some subject line elements to see how we could improve how the audience was responding to different subject lines and increase engagement with the cultivation emails. We established a control—“An interesting interview”—using the element of mystery to invoke curiosity and a treatment—“Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?”—using the word you to create relevancy for the email recipients. We set up the A/B split test to see which of the two elements had better performance so we can continue to adjust subject lines for future emails in the series by using what works for this particular audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was set up in Hubspot to monitor results for the first 4 hours after the email test was sent to 50% of the total audience and then send the email with the most opens to the remainder of the file. Because of this, we did not have a large enough sample size to validate the experiment. The treatment saw a 6.3% decrease in traffic, but we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence to draw any conclusions based on these results. Moving forward, we will want to run a similar test as a split to the full housefile rather than just half of it so we can validate results and find out how to best optimize the subject lines for this audience.

experiments.title: How communal language on a campaign sticky bar affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on a sticky bar will lead to stronger donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/27/2021
During KCBI’s spring campaign, we wanted to test communal language on their campaign sticky bar. Their typical sticky bar language focused on the urgency of the need and the end of the campaign with a countdown sticky bar. We paired that urgency and countdown with language that focused on the common value proposition that brings the reader into the community of KCBI. This language matches much of the way they talk on air during their fundraisers and they talk to their constituents like family. So it was a natural fit for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language increased donations by 16% and revenue by 29%. And there were no demographics that experienced a valid decrease in conversion. Our hypothesis was correct that using communal language coupled with urgency would resonate with their audience. That is because the copy started by connecting the most important points of value for their readers, connecting those to the mission and the outcome of the campaign.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name in a membership email affects the open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a higher open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test different sender names to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect a different sender name would have on the open rate of an email. The control email was from “Maggy” while the treatment was from “Andrew.” The audience is accustomed to receiving membership emails from “Maggy” and weekly cultivation emails from “Andrew.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Though there was a .74% decrease in traffic this experiment does not validate because only a 47.3% confidence level was achieved. Further testing and a larger sample size would be needed in order to validate these results. We recommend sending from another person in the organization to see if that affects the open rate since the housefile is accustomed to seeing emails from both "Maggy" and "Andrew."

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affects the open rate of an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a greater open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test sender names to measure open and click response. We tested two different names from the organization in order to see how each name would affect the open rate of an email. We used “Maggy Smith” for the control email and “Andrew McIndoe” for the treatment. “Andrew” sends a weekly cultivation email while “Maggy” is the usual sender for membership card appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though we saw a 1% increase in traffic, this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 66.8%. However, a retest with a larger sample size is recommended in order to validate results. There was also no statistical difference in the click thru rates for each email. We recommend potentially using another person's name to see if it would significantly increase open rates since the housefile is used to seeing both "Andrew" and "Maggy" in their inbox.

experiments.title: How using communal language in a sticky bar impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language for website visitors will achieve increased traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2021
To highlight a Challenge Grant with an upcoming deadline, we added a sticky bar across Buckner’s website to draw additional clicks over to the donation page. For the control we used time-focused language to build urgency but we wanted to test incorporating communal language and remind visitors of their friendship with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the test for two weeks ahead of the Challenge Grant deadline but we did not receive enough traffic to validate the experiment and only reached a 55% level of confidence so we can't say for sure if this had any impact on donation page traffic. We'll look for additional opportunities to test into using communal language on housefile appeals where we have an established connection with the audience.

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the order of login options for mobile visitors will achieve an increase in user signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2021 - 04/03/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
The desktop version of this test showed a decrease in signups—however, the mobile showed promise. So they decided to isolate the mobile test to keep it running longer and cut their losses on desktop signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, this experiment failed to produce a winning treatment with any difference. While there was a small directional lift, it was not close to statistical validity. The team moved on to higher priority tests to drive more new users.

experiments.title: How a subject line test impacted open rates on a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new subject line for returning donors will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We developed a treatment that used more active rather than passive language. The control subject line asked the question “Are you ready?” while the treatment asked “Are you coming?” while the body copy remained the same for both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see any valid results from the experiment and only reached 50.2% level of confidence so this concept would need to be re-tested in the future. Looking at the body copy for the treatment, we would want to also test aligning the body copy with the subject line to make sure the original question is addressed or expounded upon to help the message flow more congruently.

experiments.title: How a more humanized subject line affects open rate on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized subject line for a housefile audience will achieve an increase in open rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect of a more humanized subject line vs. a standard subject line in an email. The control subject line read “Membership question” and the treatment read “I hope you will be joining us.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a .69% increase in traffic on the treatment but this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 49.2%. This would require retesting and tested with a prospecting email instead of a housefile email in order to test a larger sample size. We also recommend testing a more humanized subject line again using a first name in the subject line instead of "you" if testing the housefile email again.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affected the open rate on an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender for housefile email recipients will achieve increased open rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement, we wanted to test different email senders to measure open rate. We wondered if users would be more inclined to open an email from one sender than another. The control sender was “Maggy Smith” and the treatment sender was “Andrew P. McIndoe.” Maggy works in Membership Programs and Andrew is the Vice President of Development. Each email contains the same subject line and body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment resulted in a -0.59 decrease in traffic and a 39.0% level of confidence. Users engaged less with Andrew P. McIndoe than they did with Maggy Smith. A possible reason is users are more familiar with Maggy Smith, as they've heard from her before about membership relations. Also, an email from a representative of Membership Programs may be more engaging than an email from the Vice President of Development, assuming the user is familiar with each.

The next experiment could be to change the subject line to something more specific, such as "Renew your membership today" rather than "I haven't heard from you." The user may be more inclined to open the email because of a needed action, regardless of the sender.

experiments.title: How does focusing on a specific ministry over the full suite of programs affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing in on one ministry that the donor will be supporting rather than all of the organizations outreach will allow for a deeper explanation of value prop as well as a simpler ask, and that will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2021 - 03/29/2021
Prison Fellowship International launched its first content offer, Where Love & Justice Meet. Since this was a new initiative for them and they didn’t have many learnings from the market yet, we wanted to test which of their ministries were more likely to convert a donor. They have a mix of programs—prison bible studies, sponsorships for children of prisoners, and restorative justice projects. So to find out what converts a donor better, we did a 50/50 redirect split toward two different donation pages—The control, a donation page that showed how the reader’s donation would support their many prison ministries and 2. The Treatment, a page solely focused on the ministry and impact of a their prison bible study called The Prisoners Journey. The goal was to see which inspired more gifts. Because the copy on the donation page for The Prisoner’s Journey bible study was geared toward a monthly recurring donation ask, we made that form a tabbed donation form to highlight the recurring giving option, which was another variation in the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a full month of testing, the results are inconclusive. Neither donation page outperformed the other in donor conversion at any level that was valid. Our hypothesis is that both pages had equally strong value prop and simply delving into one ministry is not enough to move a needle on an acquisition offer.
Another key metric we were aiming for was acquiring recurring donors. The TPJ page acquired three monthly donors in that time span and the general ministries donation page acquired 2, so no meaningful findings there either.
Our next step is to focus on the page specific to the Prisoners Journey and learn how we can use their Lamplighter's program for monthly donors to TPJ as a greater incentive to give a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How using communal language in an email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: How will communal language impact conversion in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/08/2021
Care Net found very positive results in a previous experiment with using communal language. This experiment was run on the homepage of their website. They wanted to test this similar approach in an email appeal to see if they would see a similar response through this donor theory. To test it, communal language was added to the call-to-action of the email appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language variation had no impact on click rate or donor conversion. This is due to the low engagement rate of the email. We will test this approach again in an email appeal and incorporate more communal language throughout the email to see if we can increase engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that this placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How adding a donors first name to the instant donation page can increase conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the first name for those that visit the page will achieve an increase in both donations and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2021 - 05/01/2021
In this experiment, we added a donor’s first name to the instant donation page once they downloaded the eBook it said “Thank you, First name” while the control just said, “Thank you, friend”. This experiment was run specifically on Facebook and we had a 50/50 split on who saw the control vs the treatment.
The main metric we were looking to track on this experiment was the donor conversion rate. Previously with Hoover we ran this experiment and saw a lift but we didn’t launch it across the board so we wanted to test it again as they had also recently changed their donation platform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had to stop this experiment early so it did not receive the necessary traffic it needed to validate (67%LoC) but with it seeing a 12% increase in conversion rate and a 43% increase in revenue (99% LoC) we will continue to test this across multiple Hoover offers.
People enjoy seeing their name and it adds that personal aspect and helps reinforce that they completed the signup.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 43.8% increase with a 99% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had an 8.3% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 14.3% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 322.3% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How emotional impact language on the tribute widget affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding emotional impact language for personal home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2021 - 05/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute widget was a primary source of donations for people who visited their journals. Over the years, they had optimized the copy to maximize conversion.
However, as they reviewed the latest control, they wondered if they could better convey the emotional impact that a journal provides for someone who is going through a health journey, and if that would translate into more gifts or more revenue.
They created a treatment that made two tweaks to the copy. First, they changed the headline to include the impact, as well as the result. Instead of just “Help [FirstName] Stay Connected To Family and Friends”, they added the effect: “Help [FirstName] Experience the Comfort of Staying Connected To Family and Friends”. Then, in the body copy, they inserted language that referred to the potential feeling of loss the author might feel without Caringbridge: “A $30 donation to CaringBridge means that [FirstName] won’t lose this special connection to family and friends.
Then, they split traffic to see the impact of this new emotional impact language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, there was no impact on conversion rate. This suggests a few potential reasons why:
The reader might have already understood the emotional impact, and the specific callout only brought that to the surface—it wasn't missing. Any impact the emotional language might have had could have been blunted by the additional copy. The copy was ultimately not that different from the original.
Either way, this experiment failed to move the needle, and more radical approaches must be taken to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How longer value proposition on an acquisition donation page increases revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Expanding value proposition on an acquisition donation page will increase revenue by increasing the perceived value of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2021 - 05/09/2021
The Bill of Rights Institute had been running an offer for an eBook on Equal Rights for months. And the average gift was not as high as we believed it could be. So we decided to test including more copy on the donation page around the need for their gift and the impact it would make. The hypothesis was that this would lead them to give more.
This test ran for two months, longer than typically suggested, because we continued to see fluctuations in the results and wanted to give it a long time horizon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not result in valid findings. When looking at the different demographics. We found that it increased revenue with new visitors by 45% at a 87% LOC, and decreased the revenue from returning visitors by 24% at a LOC of 77%. The findings from these two segments of users negated the other when you view this test through all traffic.

Our hypothesis is that this test showed a directional lift in revenue for new visitors because it told them more about an organization that they are new to, and it taught them more about the value of the eBook they just received. But overall, this test showed that this copy alone was not enough to increase revenue significantly for a sustainable amount of time.

Despite the extra time, these findings were still not strong enough to roll out this test. We will be testing other avenues for increasing revenue.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement of a premium affects donor conversion for a postal chase donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visual reinforcement of a premium for postal chase visitors will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/22/2021
The Navigators were sending Easter cards as a premium offer to their direct mail file during the Easter season. They created a donation page for this campaign to receive traffic from the direct mail campaign and also as a landing point for Facebook ads which were shown to that audience to drive additional giving.
The donation page was very short and to the point. On reviewing it, the team wondered if adding a picture of the Easter cards (to reinforce what was sent in the mail) would increase conversion. They put an image of the card on the page, and added an additional paragraph of incentive-focused copy to remind them of the free premium they received.
They split the traffic to see which treatment converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment didn't validate due to low overall traffic, it reveals more tactics that can be used to directionally increase conversion on these pages.
While the same audience is targeted with both campaigns, we must remember that there is a time-delay between the direct mail piece arriving and seeing the digital ad, and must overcompensate to remind them of the piece they received in the mail (which could be one of many). When this reinforcement is done properly,

experiments.title: How increasing clarity that a content offer is free affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making it clear a content offer is free, while tying that to value proposition, will not decrease giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2021 - 05/20/2021
Focus on the Family had been receiving complaints that people didn’t know the video series was free and they believed they needed to make a donation to get the series. We tested one iteration of a donation page that increased the use of the word free in conjunction with the video series. That test decreased donations by 20%.
For the second iteration, we focused more on clarity and addressing the reader specifically, rather than increasing the reference to the series being free. You can see the enhanced test language below. This was a 50/50 A/B test that ran for four weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: This iteration of the test didn't decrease donations by any level of confidence while it increased clarity that the reader did not have to donate to receive the video series. We will be rolling this language out to their other instant donation pages.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: How prioritizing personalization on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing the first name of the connection for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2021 - 06/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute donation page is where the majority of their conversions happen. As people are reading journal pages, they are able to give to support the site of their friend.
This page had long had a “cause and effect” value proposition that had not been challenged in some time. In reviewing this copy, they noticed that the organization name came before the “familiar” name of the friend whose journal they were reading.
The team came up with two treatments—one that just reversed the order of the “familiar” and organization names, and then another that also made a more direct ask and included language about a “vital connecting point” to increase the strength of the case for support.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither treatment moved the needle. Both treatments had slight decreases in conversion rate that weren't going to validate, and slight increases in revenue (through larger average gifts) that weren't going to validate either.
With a 34-35% donor conversion rate, it might prove more fruitful to get more motivated traffic from upstream to funnel into this page.

experiments.title: How a form on the right side of the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: A right side form could harm conversion rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 06/04/2021
Focus’s web team wanted to roll out a new landing page format with the form on the right side of the page instead of the bottom. We wanted to assure that this did no harm to email acquisition. So we tested this on a new offer they were launching called Preparing Your Kids for the Teen Years.
experiments.keyLearnings: The majority of the traffic and conversions were on a mobile device. This test showed no results because on a mobile device it is almost exactly the same user experience. So we are going to retest this format on a high traffic page to only desktop users.

experiments.title: How making gift type the first decision point affects donor acquisition on premium offers
experiments.researchQuestion: Clarifying gift type at the beginning of the form will not harm donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2021 - 05/09/2021
FamilyLife found great success using tabbed donation forms on their main donation page. But before rolling this experience out to all of their donation pages, we wanted to ensure that this user experience did not not harm donor acquisition on acquisition offers. So we tested this on one of their most high performing premium offers, A Quiet Place. This was a 50/50 AB split test using an Optimize redirect. So half of the traffic saw the original format and the other half of traffic saw the tabbed form.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we were hoping, the tabbed form did not harm new donor conversion in any significant way. Neither revenue nor donations saw a valid decrease in this test. The next step is to roll this page out to their microsite.

experiments.title: How adding benefits of monthly giving affected clicks in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding benefits of monthly giving for a housefile appeal will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2021 - 03/11/2021
For a high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test if including the benefits of becoming a monthly donor would increase clicks to the donation page. This was the last email in the campaign and it was set up a personal note forwarding the previous email from earlier in the day with one final request to give before the deadline. The treatment version listed the benefits of becoming a monthly donor and included an image of the premium for monthly donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this email was sent to an audience limited in size, we were unable to validate the results of the experiment. We saw a 24.1% increase in traffic on the treatment but with only a 43.8% level of confidence so we cannot claim statistical significance. Moving forward, we'll plan to retest this same concept in future emails—as well as launch the test in other channels that may reach a wider audience —to see if there are any learnings to be applied.

experiments.title: How a new testimonial impacted clicks on a sticky bar

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that demonstrating tangible outcomes in the value proposition for parents considering a free trial will achieve an increase in free trial signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 03/06/2021
When reviewing surveys from people who had completed a 14-day trial, we found a testimonial that indicated the different skills their children built as a result of listening to Adventures in Odyssey episodes. We decided to include this testimonial as a form of social proof in the sticky bar to see if tangible outcomes in life skill areas such as “conflict resolution, compassion and addressing fears” would be motivating for parents considering beginning a free trial for their children. We split traffic between this treatment and the currently running testimonial that was implemented as the control after it had lifted results in a prior experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for a month, but we did not validate this test. While we saw varying results across multiple segments, only a few individual segments did validate. We'll look further into the mobile vs. desktop experience for future tests since it looked like these segments had opposing effects. We can also look into adding variable copy for segments like paid search visitors on any additional treatments.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 41.2% increase in begin free trial signup with an 87% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 20.5% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 2.7% increase in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.4% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 107.0% increase in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 36.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the sender of cultivation emails during a campaign performed when soliciting for gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the sender of cultivation emails during a campaign for campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
As the second part of this year-end experiment, we were interested to learn whether the second sender (who sent the donor stories) would increase the donor conversion rate and/or revenue when soliciting gifts from the treatment segment (that saw the donor stories) over that of the control segment (that did not).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.1% level of confidence, we observed a -12.3% decrease in revenue.

Similarly, we only observed with a 48.7% level of confidence a -13.6% decrease in donor conversion rate, as well.

Ultimately, we were unable to validate either metric through this experiment and further experimentation may be required.

experiments.title: Does the signer on a Dear Reader impact donation rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific signer on a dear reader for blog pages will achieve higher donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2020 - 12/31/2020
During KCBI’s calendar year end campaign, we added Dear Reader notes at the end of their blog posts asking for a year end donation. Originally the design was to have the note signed from “The KCBI Team”. But since the majority of their blogs are by one radio personality, we wondered if having her be the signer instead would increase donations. So we tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment or even show a directional lift. However, we did find that Dear Readers are a valuable source of revenue and that further experimentation is needed.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn’t do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a “turn off” for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer’s audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn't do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a "turn off" for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer's audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.

experiments.title: Does moving the form further down increase sign ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does moving the form further down the enrollment page allow the eye to more easily see the course descriptions, which may motivate them to enroll?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2018 - 04/19/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if changing the layout on the sign up page for their Constitutional Conversations course would impact conversions. They decided to test endorsements behind a white background versus a blue background, and made the other course description boxes larger on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The mobile versions of these layouts do not include the quotes or course descriptions, so both versions look identical. Even with a larger sample size, this experiment can’t be validated. In the future, any layout tests should exclude the any traffic that wouldn’t be impacted by the test. In the future, a new test to consider would be a treatment without course descriptions to see if they impact enrollment.

experiments.title: How including a hot button issue in the subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will mentioning free speech in the subject line improve the open rate of the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2018 - 07/14/2018
The Leadership Institute works with college students to help defend their free speech on campus. They wanted to see if including hot button terms like “free speech” and “the one percent” in the subject line would affect open rates. Their hypothesis was that the subject line about free speech would generate more opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rates for each email were no more than .4% deviations away from 20%. This experiment was not conclusive. It’s likely that the hot button issues did not affect open rate as much due to the specified name of the school in the subject line. A further experiment without a specified name may render desired results to confidently conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition and removing elements of friction impact donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How a membership offer impacts new visitors to ADF
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “membership” be a more appealing offer than “donating” to new visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2019 - 05/13/2019
As part of our efforts to evaluate the impact a membership program could have on the ADF fundraising efforts, we set up an experiment on the Thank You page of an email acquisition campaign. The visitors were coming from the blog after signing a Statement of Belief. We then created a new version of this page with the main modification being the final call to action at the end. We testing the concept of “membership” against the standard “donate” messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this experiment targeted at both new and existing visitors but, when digging into the metrics, we discovered that it had no significant impact on existing visitor conversion but it seemed to directionally impact new visitors likelihood to make a gift. This experiment had run for a full two months and would have required another month before validating so the decision was made to end this experiment to avoid being impacted by the “history effect.”
As next steps, we will roll this experiment out to a dedicated Facebook campaign where we will be able to send more new visitors to the treatment to see if we can replicate this impact in a statistically significant manner.

experiments.title: How the value proposition copy on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the value prop copy treatment that we validated on the Genesis course stand on its own in the Revelation course, or does it only work when coupled with the removal of the premium offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2019 - 04/06/2019
DTS validated a copy test on the Genesis donation page that increased donations by 248% (experiment id: 10582), but also included the removal of a high-dollar premium offer from the page. They wanted to test the separation of the copy from the premium removal, so they tested the same copy on the Revelation course donation page, while still including the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not reach statistical validity and only impacted conversion rate slightly. We are going to re-run this test with the removal of the premium to see if the same concept that worked on the Genesis course works here.

experiments.title: How personalization on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalization on an instant donation page after a content offer increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 10/02/2020
As part of a giving day campaign, DTS was offering a content offer and wanted to test using the donor’s first name in the headline to see if this would drive conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach validity with this experiment, when evaluating the results and drilling down even further by each segment, we noticed that while overall conversion rate decreased on the page, we saw an 89.4% lift in average gift—specifically with the returning visitors segment—with an 88% level of confidence. Across all traffic we saw a 40.8% lift in average gift with an 82% level of confidence. These findings in the directional lift in average gift will be used for future testing and will be useful for developing what language we present on donation pages to returning visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How a “monthly-only” form affects recurring gift conversion during a recurring gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a “monthly-only” form affect conversion during a recurring gift campaign?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
Hillsdale College was running a campaign to increase membership to their recurring gift club, the Liberty and Learning Society. Their call center had noted that many people in the past who made recurring gifts did so believing that they were one-time gifts, and called to cancel. Therefore, they suggested that the form be a “tabbed” donation form that they defaulted to a monthly gift, but still allowed one-time gifts. The team wanted to test this against a “monthly-only” form to see if the lack of a one-time option moved more people towards one-time gifts. Or, put another way, they wanted to see if having a one-time option acted as a bailout mechanism that gave people an “out” to give a short-term gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the monthly-only option, had no effect on the amount of recurring gifts submitted through the form. This indicated that, at least in the near term, there was no confusion about usage of the tabbed form. As an additional bonus, many people who did not make recurring gifts did make one-time gifts as well, leading to another source of revenue for the campaign, though it was not aligned with the intent of the campaign.
The caveat to this test comes in that it will take some time to see if the tabbed form generated as many downstream donor cancellation calls as the monthly form, though that data can be tied back to the original source form.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring donor ticker affected a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the live total number of donors acquired towards the overall goal increase conversion rate on a donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2020 - 09/24/2020
With a specific goal set for a high urgency campaign, we wanted to test if adding a ticker to the donation page in a sticky bar to count the number of monthly donors would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the minimum level of confidence needed for validity of the experiment and saw a slight decrease in the number of transactions, we did notice that the average gift increased on the treatment, which is something we would want to test further to evaluate. We also hypothesized that the results could have been negatively influenced since the campaign met its monthly donor goal ahead of schedule, there may have been a reduced sense of need or urgency when visitors arrived on the page and saw the goal had already been met before they decided to contribute. For future tests, we want to launch the sticky bar earlier in the campaign to see how it affects motivation and we also want to test replacing the text-only version sticky bar with additional visuals like a progress bar or thermometer to further add clarity to the progress of the goal and what is being counted.

experiments.title: Will sending a voicemail increase the liklihood of attending a webinar live?
experiments.researchQuestion: Are people more likely to attend a webinar live if they get a voicemail?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/30/2020
For a webinar in March, we wondered if a ‘thank you’ voicemail from Brady that reminded them of the date and time of the webinar would increase the likelihood of them attending live (as opposed to watching later or not watching at all). Using Zapier and Slybroadcast to automate the process, new webinar registrants would get a thank-you voicemail soon after they had signedup with the message of thanks and when/where to join.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no difference in performance

experiments.title: How adding an “About Us” section to the landing page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of an “about us” section to tell the organization’s history and beliefs to the landing page impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2020 - 10/03/2020
As a part of promoting a new content offer, The Fund for American Studies wondered whether telling more about their organization’s history would help boost email signups, but most importantly, whether or not it would increase the donor conversion rate after someone had signed up to request the resource they were offering.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 61% level of confidence, we observed a -23.7% decrease in donor conversion rate. This was subsequently driving a similar decrease in revenue, as well.
We did observe roughly a directional increase of +8.5% in email signup rates, but we decided to turn this off and focus experimentation elsewhere.
Further experimentation may be required, as we did notice an uptick in revenue from returning visitors with the treatment, but decreases in new visitors. This may be driven by new visitors largely being motivated by the offer, instead of the organization. Perhaps adding this description to the donation page may be a better place within the funnel for experimentation next.

experiments.title: Mug Signer Test Revenue (Low-Dollar Donor)
Messaging/OE Test – July 2014 – KYFAH Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new OE and slightly different appeal outperform the control?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/29/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the Outer Envelope from a photo of Obama, the ACLU, and the Bible with a red X through it which said: What’s behind the unprecedented attacks on your religious freedom … and what you can do about it!  To an OE with a photo of a door that says “Official Notice: Keep your faith at home – or else” with a teaser of: A message to Christians from the ACLU and the Obama Administration. We also varied the text in the appeal slightly to focus more on the ACLU Triple Threat.

experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a significant decrease in ROI due to a decrease in average gift, response, and an increase in costs. It is not recommended to use this kit.

experiments.title: How a dynamic donor countdown on a sticky bar impacts conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation at the end of a campaign by adding a sticky bar to the donation page to reinforce the value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2020 - 08/04/2020
In the final week of Focus on the Family’s summer campaign, they wanted to see if they would increase donor conversion on the donation page by adding a sticky bar to the top of the page that reinforce the need. On this sticky bar was also a dynamic countdown of how many donors were needed that day (this changed every day). They ran this test for a week against the control that didn’t have the sticky bar on it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in giving when the sticky bar was present on the page. The sticky bar increased motivation of the prospective donor by reminding them of the need and creating urgency around the goal of the number of donors needed to meet the need. This tactic has proven to work well for this organization and could be implemented in future campaigns without having a negative impact on giving.

experiments.title: How using social proof on a website can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can social proof help increase donor conversion on the FOTF website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2020 - 08/07/2020
During Focus on the Family’s summer campaign focused around their pro-life initiative, we wanted to see if we could impact donor conversion by using social proof to motivate people to give. To test this, we developed a slide in feature that appeared when a person visited the FOTF website that said, “[INSERT A PERSON’S NAME], from [INSERT CITY], made a donation to help save a child from abortion. You can help too>>”. The feature would show for 20 seconds and then disappear, but then reappear and refresh the name and city when/if someone on the website made a gift. We showed this feature on desktop and mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift (46%) in donor conversion when the social proof feature was present. We also saw the most significant increase occur on desktop devices and returning visitors. The great news is that this feature didn’t have a negative impact, even on mobile. What we can learn is that people can be impacted and motivated by others. Seeing that someone on the site had just made a gift to save a baby from abortion motivated others to do the same. We’d like to continue to test into this principle, run it for a longer period of time, and experiment with the kind of language used to see if we can validate the results and even increase donor conversion even more through this tactic.

experiments.title: How asking lapsed donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific amount impacted donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does communicating a specific quantity of donors we’re asking to make a gift relevant to the lapsed donor impact donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
As a part of The Heritage Foundation’s matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we wanted to experiment with the ask amount and approach. Typically, the organization has used “make your best gift today” kind of language within their control copy.
In an attempt to set a smaller, more reasonable target, we wanted to experiment with driving lapsed donors to be “one of just X donors we need today to make a gift of Y.”
The idea behind this is to (a) set a relevant ask amount for the donors, but also (b) allow them to see that they can help achieve a “small quantity” goal at that amount, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not validate an increase, we did see we did observe a +10.8% increase in lapsed donor conversion rate — albeit at only a 58% level of confidence.
Further, the revenue was relatively flat between the two segments (only showing a +7.2% increase, with a level of confidence of 60.9%). It was promising, however, that we also observed that 29% of the people in the treatment segment decided to give more than the amount that was asked for, while another 41% gave the exact amount that was asked of them.
Conclusion: Perhaps further experimentation with the ask amounts is advisable for lapsed donors, but this directionally showed a lift in revenue.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition on a specific gift handle can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the higher gift handle of $50 increase average gift or donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2020 - 08/13/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association wanted to find a way to increase the average gift for the donors that finished one of their pledges. It was hypothesized that one potential way to do that would be to increase the perceived impact of some of the higher dollar value gift amounts. To do that, they highlighted the $50 gift button which would have been a ~20% increase to average gift. This same gift handle was previously mentioned on the page prior to the ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month and a half, we did not observe a statistically significant change in average gift or conversion rate. One hypothesis behind this is that the value of the $50 was mentioned in the copy right before the form. As a result, this visual emphasis didn’t have the same level of impact it may have otherwise had.

experiments.title: How a different offer affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will lead to more emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
In this experiment, we had a list of attendees from an event we sponsored. We want more people to sign up for our own event, but ultimately, we just want to get them to opt-in to communication so we can develop a relationship.
So we wondered…is it more effective to send this list an email about our conference and ask them to register for a free ticket? Or should offer them something of immediate value like a research study?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page offer increased conversion by 72%, although it only reached a 78% level of confidence. Although this experiment didn’t reach the level of confidence we would like to see, it’s an indicator that NIO Summit might not be the best initial email acquisition point for an email prospecting campaign.
The donation page offer did increase clicks by 58% and reached statistical validity.

experiments.title: How increasing “involving” score affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing “involving” score affect email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2020
Hillsdale College was running a pre-registration campaign for their new course, “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They had run a test that determined that a “problem-solution” framework increased clickthrough rate, and thus signups and donors. However, they had been informed of a partner who had a method of testing copy on a 2×2 framework that indicated that increasing “narrative” score and “involving” score would increase fundraising conversion. The partner put together a treatment that, through their scoring mechanism, significantly increased “involving” score. They decided to test this copy to see if it had a positive effect on name acquisition, which would get more names into the donor conversion funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the higher “involving” score produced a slight, but not statistically significant decrease in email acquisition rate. When the team looked at the full funnel, there was a slight, but not statistically valid increase in donor conversion later in the process.
Ultimately, there wasn’t a significant difference at either step of the funnel. This may have been because of one of two factors:
The two-step soft offer of preregistration followed by ask might have muted the copy’s effect on donor conversion by placing the involving copy too far from the ask.
The copy that produced the higher involving score also lowered the narrative score, which may have had an adverse effect on conversion.
This shows a path to testing future direct ask copy with this method, provided that both involving and narrative scores both increase.

experiments.title: How does donor centered copy affect revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Donation page copy that speaks more directly to the donor rather than about the organization will increase donor
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/07/2020
BRI was offering a new eBook called Equal & Inalienable Rights. The donation page copy was very centered on the mission and impact of the organization. We wanted to test an approach that spoke more directly to the reader and drew them into the mission by focusing more on the benefits to them and to their impact. So we redirected 50% of their traffic to a new donation page with more donor centered copy centered around what they were receiving and why they should give. Donation page copy that speaks more directly to the donor rather than about the organization will increase donor

experiments.title: How bringing clarity to a homepage redirect experience impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by bringing clarity to a homepage redirect?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2020 - 07/13/2020
During Live Action’s high urgency campaign, we ran a homepage redirect where we redirected traffic that came to the homepage to the donation page for the campaign. We were curious what would happen if we brought more clarity to the experience and helped to orientate people with what was going on once they saw the donation page, so we tested language on the page that simply said, “Pardon the interruption but we wanted to be sure you saw this”. Our hypothesis was that people may be more likely to read what we were wanting them to see and do if we communicated that to them.
experiments.keyLearnings: Regardless of audience, or device type, there was no difference in donor conversion.
