Because this test was run on the very last day of the high urgency campaign, the motivation of donors was already high resulting in no difference in conversion between the control and treatment.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
*This was the 2nd of an identical test to see if greater volume would impact significance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3.9%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching statistical significance.

This was the 2nd of an identical test after seeing an “invalid” result the first time. We thought that more volume could give a stronger indication of impact. The combined results of this experiment yield the same result…a 3.6% decrease, but with an invalid result.

If we determine that the benefit of getting this user-reported revenue data more valuable than the slight risk of small decrease in conversion, we’ll roll this treatment out.

experiments.title: How a gift array with extended description affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with extended description affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/07/2020
Compassion International was running a campaign to ask existing sponsors to make an additional gift to help their fellow sponsors keep their children during the economic hardship caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. They had already learned that the value proposition of “helping a sponsor” versus “helping a child” was more impactful to conversion. However, they wondered if the suggested amount of $114 (which covers three months of sponsorship) was too nebulous and random for the visitor. They created a gift array that still suggested $114, but added a line of copy that explained that “$114 covers three months of sponsorship for a fellow sponsor”. Then, the array offered $74 (two months) and $38 (one month). They hypothesized that the array would help the prospective donor understand the reasoning behind the gift amount meant and make them more likely to convert. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, with the gift array and additional descriptions, produced a 7.3% decrease with no statistical level of confidence. This ultimately means that the array was ineffective at moving conversion at all.

experiments.title: How removing a video affects email appeal revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the video from the landing page (and thumbnail from the email) increase donation revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/04/2017
We saw a number of different factors and potential learnings at play this test.
First off, eliminating the softer CTA (“Watch Video”) in the email reduced the open-to-click rate by 34.9% in the non-video treatment.  However, there wasn’t a corresponding reduction in donations—likely suggesting that the larger audience clicking the control was less motivated to give and more motivated to watch the video.
At first glance when reviewing overall giving, we saw a 2.1% increase in conversions for the non-video treatment . . . but at a 28.8% decrease in revenue. Neither met our 95% confidence threshold, although the revenue decrease had a confidence of 90.9%.  For this reason, we scrutinized the data more closely to ensure that we didn’t miss a learning.
After reviewing the individual transactions from both variants, we found that the gift amounts had significant outliers due to $,1,000+ donations.  Knowing when to filter data is a difficult challenge of testing and optimization—but since we have repeatedly seen that higher-value gifts are less likely to be motivated by an individual element of a broad-base ask, we typically exclude these from revenue validation.  After making this adjustment, we see the smaller difference noted in the results table.
Here are a few thoughts on this result, and why it requires additional testing to validate:
Response sizes are too smallDue to a low conversion rate among both target segments, there isn’t enough data to back up the normal distribution of gift amount due to its existing variability.  This is especially true of a non-donor segment that was tested with similar results, where conversion rate was just 0.007%.  Further testing, including during higher response windows, may improve the actionability of results.
The video wasn’t the only thing testedWhen removing the video thumbnail from the email, it was replaced with a different image, which was also rotated.  The additional call to action button was also removed instead of being modified. Copy was added in that area, but from a different testimonial changing the value being conveyed.On the landing page, the same additional testimonial copy was used, but was added much later in the copy compared to the video.  When testing video as a medium, the core value proposition of the video should be represented in text—if not, you’re also testing between different value proposition or the removal of some of the value proposition presented completely.
There were significantly more outliers in the control segmentWhile excluding $1,000+ gifts from both segments made a difference, it’s difficult to tell what the normal distribution of gifts is and whether segmentation played a factor in the results.  Typically the only ways to answer these questions are to measure historical distributions to be able identify abnormal ones, or to have a larger number of results to help balance any small number of outliers.
Due to the significant impact we’ve seen when replacing video on appeal pages, we would recommend further testing to ensure a clear and applicable learning for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The team at Focus on the Family had seen experiments from other NextAfter partners suggesting that video on donation forms could actually be harming conversion.  With an appeal featuring video set to go out, they decided to test into this effect to see whether their audience would respond the same.  This particular test was against a segment of existing donors, although a non-donor segment was also tested.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate

rows.name: Progressive Form Field Asking for Organization Size

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2020 - 06/16/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching a significant level of confidence. It’s possible that running this for longer and getting more traffic to the page could prove that this decrease is in fact real – but the odds are low that this makes a significant impact over time.
We will continue to test the placement of this field, but knowing that the likelihood of a negative impact is very low. And the upside is that we get better segmentation and targeting for future outreach efforts.

experiments.title: How including a video thumbnail affects clicks in a cultivation email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video thumbnail outperform a raw link in number of clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/04/2020
TPPF wanted to test if including a video thumbnail would increase the number of clicks in a cultivation email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, this experiment did not get enough traffic to reach the level of confidence needed to validate.

experiments.title: How the call-to-action within an article offer impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action would increase reader response on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2020 - 06/13/2020
On the Focus on the Family marriage articles, they featured a video series called, Cherish. This video series was free for people to sign-up for and was presented at the bottom of every marriage article. The call-to-action on the offer was a button that would then take a person to the sign up page. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase conversion if we presented the sign-up form right there on the article. We split the traffic, 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for a month and we found that there was no significant difference in name conversion between the two different call-to-actions. This was a bit surprising as we have seen that reducing friction to the acquisition process can help conversion. Because the results were a wash, we hypothesize that the offer may have been over saturated on these pages since it had been running for months prior to this experiment. We hypothesize this because of the low conversion rate on the offer. We would like to test this tactic again in the future using a new, fresh, content offer on the article pages.

experiments.title: Will smart content on a general donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will referring to visitors by their first name and featuring their past giving history on the donation page result in greater donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2020 - 04/26/2020
We have been exploring a new donation page solution provided by DepositFix. These pages are built within Hubspot, so we can use Hubspot’s smart content on the donation page to reference a visitors name, past giving, etc. We decided to test a radical redesign of our general donation page featuring the visitor’s name and the amount of their last donation vs our current “static” donation page provided by RaiseDonors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
According to Hubspot, no one converted on the smart variations of the Deposit Fix page. The donations we acquired were on the standard/non-smart version which was seen by visitors not in Hubspot. Our hypothesis is that people were turned off when they saw their prior gift/first name on the deposit fix page leading to few donations.
We think the reason that the smart content on the page worked when coming from an email campaign is because donors had just read a personalized email, so then seeing a personalized donation page “made sense” in a way seeing a personalized page while just browsing the public site didn’t. We will continue to test these two solutions to see what more we can learn.

experiments.title: The impact of using a campaign graphic in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “plain” email without the Giving Tuesday Now! graphic convert more donors than one with it?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2020 - 05/08/2020
For the international Giving Tuesday Now! campaign, we decided to test the impact of using the campaign’s graphic in our digital appeal for the event. We wanted to see if adding it would increase or decrease donor response
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the version without the graphic had a 23% higher CTR on the donation link than the one with the graphic. However, those who clicked on the link in the email with the graphic were 45% more likely to convert to donors, meaning that our overall donor and revenue conversion was 41% higher for the variation with the graphic than our normal “plain” template.
We want to do further tests as our sample size was too small for confidence, however it seems that there was the potential for a directional lift in these results.

experiments.title: How unique email messaging to key audience segments affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending unique copy to key audience segments increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2020 - 05/12/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if sending uniquely tailored messaging to our key audience personas would impact their likelihood of registering for a webinar. The control used general messaging to a portion of the housefile. The treatment used tailored messaging to fundraising executives and fundraising practitioners.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it seemed that registration was trending up for the segmented messaging group, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95% or greater. This requires further testing, and leaning into the persona based messaging even more.

experiments.title: How unique email messaging to key audience segments affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending unique copy to key audience segments increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2020 - 05/12/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if sending uniquely tailored messaging to our key audience personas would impact their likelihood of registering for a webinar. The control used general messaging to a portion of the housefile. The treatment used tailored messaging to fundraising executives and fundraising practitioners.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it seemed that registration was trending up for the segmented messaging group, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95% or greater. This requires further testing, and leaning into the persona based messaging even more.

experiments.title: Will more text on a Facebook video ad lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more copy lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPR news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. They were running a campaign and wanted to focus on securing more monthly gifts and hoped a Facebook ad could drive more people to click and go on to make a donation. They’ve seen other Facebook advertising experiments where longer copy has led to increased donations and decided to test it for themselves. In each design, there is a video that was clickable but the main call to action and hope for the ad was to get people to click Donate Now and then visit the donation page where they could continue on to complete a recurring donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither the control or the treatment led to any donations and while the treatment had a slight increase in clicks, it was not significant. When looking at total unique clicks, the shorter ad (control) showed a statistically significant increase but the bulk of those were video views so, if the goal was to get more people to watch a video, it appears shorter copy is more effective. It should also be noted that the difference between the control and treatment was perhaps not great enough to prove the hypothesis. Also, taking a donor from Facebook ad to a recurring gift can be challenging.
So moving forward some ideas that require more testing and exploration are:
Shorter ads with videos where the goal is video views or just brand awarenessClearly longer ads where the goal is donations or clicks to a donation pageA short vs. long test with just an image and not a video that can be clickedDonation focused ads asking for a smaller one-time donation as opposed to a recurring gift

experiments.title: How a more personal tone in a direct mail letter can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more personal approach to Direct Mail impact donor conversion and overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2020 - 04/16/2020
Back to the Bible has shifted the tone and approach to their digital communication dramatically in the past year. The emails they send to people are very conversational, intentional, and personal. Their email file health has seen a dramatic improvement since this change was put in place. The focus of the emails has been to care for people and their spiritual life. Because of this responses digitally, we were curious how this approach might affect their Direct Mail strategy. To test this, we wrote two different Direct Mail letters. One letter was more traditional in nature – the call-to-action focused on the premium offer and included a P.S. at the end of the letter. The treatment was more conversational, soft, and personal in nature – more similar to the approach we’ve taken in their digital communication. We split their Direct Mail file in half and made sure there was an equal number of donors and nondonors for each variable.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After six weeks of the Direct Mail piece being in homes, we pulled the results of the experiment and found that there was no significant difference in donor conversion. We did see more overall revenue from the treatment, but this is mostly attributed to a handful of major gifts that came in from that variable. We will continue to monitor the results of this campaign over the next few weeks, but we'd also like to test this approach again and take an even more radical approach between the two letters. As you can see in the copy, the main body of the text was the same between the two versions.
Next, we'd like to test taking the digital appeal and just repurpose it for Direct Mail.

experiments.title: How mystery impacted open rates of a webinar invitation email to high profile donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an element of mystery to the subject line increase open rates from these high profile donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/13/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking to experiment with what ways they could increase high profile donor email engagement when promoting an upcoming webinar for them to attend. Because there was no way to register in the body of the email (but rather just “save the day/time”), the primary metric to measure was the open rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Regardless of which treatment these high profile donors were sent, the results were essentially identical, achieving only a 0.64% difference, with a 3.65% level of confidence (LOC).
At least when it comes to high profile VIP donors, that it doesn’t matter if you use mystery in your subject line to increase open rates.

experiments.title: How video ads on Facebook affect webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head video ad lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2020 - 03/12/2020
In a previous experiment, we saw indications that a “talking head” style Facebook ad video was performing better when it came to getting people to sign up for an upcoming webinar. But whenever we’re testing something brand new like this, it’s always a good idea to re-test and see if it holds up over time.
This can help fight against the novelty affect where we see an uptick for a time purely because something is new and different.
So for the control, we used the static title image for the webinar. And for the treatment, we used a video of me (Nathan) explaining what folks would learn in the webinar. All the rest of the ad was the same – copy, headline, cta, landing page, etc.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw mixed results from audience segment to audience segment, but no individual audiences saw a significant difference in performance when looking purely at registrations. Overall, an 11.6% increase was noted, but at an extremely low level of confidence.
But looking at cost per registrations paints a slightly different picture. Overall, the static image ad acquired webinar registrants for $7.58. But the video ad acquired registrants for just $4.70. That’s a 37% decrease in cost.
Now, a couple audience segments flipped the other direction in terms of cost and those will be noted for the future. But all in all, this video ad strategy seems to be performing better from a cost perspective, even if the conversion rates are the same as the static image.

experiments.title: How relevant, non-specific “crisis” language affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will relevant, non-specific “crisis” language affects conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2020 - 04/01/2020
When COVID-19 became a worldwide pandemic, the importance of CaringBridge became greater. As people were sheltering-in-place at their homes, the digital connections CaringBridge helped facilitate were more significant—which also increased the urgency to support CaringBridge as a platform. They wanted to find the right way to integrate this into their fundraising message, hoping that it would increase conversion rate. They created messaging that didn’t address COVID-19 specifically, as not to be exploitative, but addressed the larger sentiment. Then, they tested this in their highest revenue placement, the tribute widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After seeing an initial increase in conversion (that got as high as 15% in the first week), the data normalized at no significant increase in response. This could be because the copy was NOT specific to COVID-19, but could also reflect the fact that prospective donors did not share the sentiment that CaringBridge becomes more valuable in crisis times. There are two possible paths to take here:
Continue to test non-time-sensitive copy on the tribute widgetBe more explicit in the COVID-19 messaging to see if response can be increased with more specificity.

experiments.title: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?

rows.name: Fundraising Content You’ll Love… see what I did there…
Fundraising Content You’ll ❤️… see what I did there…

experiments.researchQuestion: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2020 - 02/28/2020
People use emoji’s all the time and we get asked how they can or should be used in emails and subject lines. We didn’t have any good experiments available to justify our thinking that it isn’t a worthwhile test so decided to test it for ourselves. We were sending a digest email on Valentines day so tied in the subject line to ‘love’ for the control and for the treatment we used a heart emoji in its place.
experiments.keyLearnings: Short answer: nope, didn’t do anything. With a 25% level of confidence, the emoji didn’t seem to have any impact on the likelihood of a subscriber to open.

experiments.title: Will bolding in an email increase click rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding some bold treatment throughout the email copy increase click rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2020
For event invitation emails we take a more long form approach as we’ve seen that be most successful in the past but are always interested in seeing how we can improve and not alienate scanners who aren’t looking to read. So for this experiment we bolded some of the key points of the email like what the event was and when, what it was based on, where to learn more, and evidentials like past event attendee survey responses.
experiments.keyLearnings: We sent to a targeted and smaller list so the result was difficult to validate but there was a directional decrease for the bold/treatment. There was no difference in unsubscribe rate so it doesn’t appear the bolding really upset people but could just be that it felt a bit more ‘marketing-y’ and didn’t get the same engagement. Possibly worth re-testing with a larger email list size but could be risky.

experiments.title: How using a smaller gift array impacted donor conversion on acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a smaller gift array increase donor conversion rate on acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2020 - 02/28/2020
In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate, Americans for Prosperity experimented with whether or not they were asking too much of 1st time donors. So, they wanted to test a smaller gift array to see if it would boost donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment didn’t validate (achieving only a 56% level of confidence), we noticed an 18% decrease in donor conversion with this experience.
We also noticed that revenue decreased proportionately to the decrease in the donor conversion rate, showing a 19% decrease, which was largely driven by less volume in the treatment experience, because the average gift was only about 2% lower for the treatment.
From here, we recommend further experimentation with variable gift arrays, but note that it may not have that much of a difference in terms of acquiring new donors.

experiments.title: How inviting donors to contribute to a matching gift affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift affects conversion versus a traditional matching gift?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2020
Leadership Institute was running a special “pre-Giving Tuesday” campaign as the initial part of their year-end campaign. The team wanted to test whether offering donors the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift would inspire more generosity, as opposed to a traditional matching gift as an incentive (as had traditionally been done). They developed two series of emails and split donors by previous giving amounts to run each through a three-email campaign leading up to Giving Tuesday to determine which approach resulted in more gifts and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: In terms of conversion, there was no statistical difference between the two approaches. Given the same size of housefile recipients, the incentive to “be the match” did not get more people to give.

experiments.title: How a new webinar landing page design affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a time/date bar on the landing page affect webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
One of the most common replies we get to webinar notification emails is people trying to clarify what time the webinar is actually at. Despite our best efforts to mention the time and time zone in every piece of communication we send, there still seemed to always be some level of confusion or clarity needed.
To accommodate for this, we added a time/date bar and widened the column of the template in the treatment version.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no change in registration rate as a result of this experiment. In this case, we see that as a success. We were able to make the time/date of the webinar much clearer and more apparent without diluting or distracting from the primary value proposition.

experiments.title: How a talking head video in a Facebook ad affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head style facebook ad lead to more webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
I see video-based Facebook ads all the time for different courses and trainings, and they always drive me crazy. But if they’re being used all the time, I wondered if they might just be more effective than static images. In this experiment, we tested using a static webinar title image (which has been our typical ad design for many months) against a “talking head” style video.
The video is of me in our webinar studio giving a high-level description of the webinar and what viewers can expect. It contained subtitles so that it can easily be understood with the sound off.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at results across all audiences, we saw a slight lift in conversion rate. But it did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence. However, we did find that the video ads delivered results that were 34% cheaper.

We will continue to test this in other contexts to see if the results hold up or perform better for specific audiences.

experiments.title: How a shortened digest email using bullets affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying the design and content of the digest email lead to more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2020
This experiment was inspired by a recent experiment from DTS. They took their designed newsletter email and simplified it down into a short intro blurb and a few bullet points. While we had previously tested into this more designed digest email format, it was only in the context of a long-form digest email with 6-8 different pieces of content to share.
For this experiment, we wondered if simplifying the design and content of the digest email down to a few bullet points could increase clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was a slight uptick in clicks, it wasn’t anywhere close to reaching a statistically significant result. As a result, it appears that this change in design made no real difference. It’s possible we might see a more significant result with a larger sample size.
This experiment shows us that both design styles may be acceptable and equally effective. This gives us more flexibility in how we write and craft these types of emails moving forward.

experiments.title: How lowering the donation ask for the Daily Prayer Book impacts transactions and revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will lowering the ask amount to $10 increase donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2019 - 11/15/2019
We have a page that offers a Daily Prayer book for $25. We thought that this could be a little to high.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no difference in the amount of donations when adding a lower amount. The control had an Avg. Order Value of $24.09 with $2,072 Revenue. The treatment had an Avg. Order Value of $20.91 with $1,798 Revenue.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer impacts the kind of donor acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer would be the best acquisition opportunity on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/01/2019
Through a series of tests, Focus on the Family discovered that segmented GOAA offers – based off of what people are consuming on the Focus website – results in the best conversion from article pages. That said, we were curious if the GOAA offer was the best opportunity to present on the article pages. To test this, we proposed testing the free video series content offer that also had a donation ask at the end of the name acquisition funnel against the GOAA offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment only ran for ten days and we saw no difference in donor conversion between the two offers. As we dug into the data a bit more, we learned a couple of valuable insights…
Our primary metric is donations and revenue, but what is not communicated in these results is that the video series also allowed us to acquire 472 emails addresses in addition to the donations and revenue. These emails also provide an opportunity for downstream donor conversion which isn’t measured from this experiment.
While donor conversion was not impacted by the kind of offer, revenue was. The average gift on the video series offer was significantly higher than the GOAA offer. In fact, revenue increased by over 600% with the video series offer. What this tells us is that we are able to acquire donors that have a higher value to the organization through the video series, and we are able to acquire “purchasers” through the GOAA offers. People viewed the GOAA offers as transactional, whereas the video series as an investment in the ministry and lives of other families like theirs.
The video series is the best method to acquire donors for the organization on the article pages. They provide the opportunity to not only acquire high value donors for the organization, but capture emails as well that could convert to donor downstream.

experiments.title: How the addition of a dynamic sticky bar during a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation with the addition of a sticky bar on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2019 - 01/02/2020
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations from their primary donation page by including a sticky bar at the top of the page that reminded people how many donations they needed to reach their goal. The sticky bar was dynamic and would count down as donations were being made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The sticky bar had no difference on donor conversion. These results were slightly surprising as we have seen this tactic help improve donor conversion for other organizations. That said, this experiment was performed during the highest giving season and donor motivation in a year. Our hypothesis is that we didn’t see a difference in conversion between the control and the treatment because donor motivation was already high. We’d like to test this same tactic when motivation is more neutral to see if we can create urgency during normal giving seasons.

experiments.title: Statement vs Question on a pop-up offer
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2019
Upon launching a new acquisition offer (in this case an eBook) we wanted to see what kind of language on a pop-up could generate more leads. Would a question to the reader pique more interest and lead to more conversions?
The test only ran for a week and therefore did not validate. However within the time frame we saw the treatment (question language) had a slight drop with a conversion rate of -9.6%.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that asking a question in a pop-up offer lead to a -9.6% drop in conversion rate. Because the experiment only ran for a week and had a small sample size, this experiment is worth testing again in a situation where we know we can have it validate.

experiments.title: How a post-donation popup ask affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a recurring popup ask after a one-time gift convert more donors to recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team wanted to get more recurring donations to their Survivor Hope Initiative to give it more regular funding so they could feed more Holocaust survivors. One tactic they wanted to try was asking donors, as soon as they had made a gift, to consider converting their onetime gift to a recurring gift with a single click. They created a popup that fired if someone
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, the popup did not have any impact on recurring donations. This could be because a higher-than-average percentage of donors selected a recurring gift in the initial step, or because of technical issues with the popup.
Future testing will focus on treatments that cater to the largely mobile audience.

experiments.title: How removing one of the payment plans can impact member conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase member conversion by removing one of the payment plans?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2019
Adventures in Odyssey offer people three different payment/membership plans. They wanted to simplify their membership process and hypothesized that if they removed one of them, they may be able to increase memberships, and ultimately overall revenue. To test into this, we removed the 6 month payment option during the second step of the membership process. We kept the 1 month and 12 month options for the treatment and split the traffic in half to test our approach
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for a month, we found that there was no significant difference in conversion. We had hypothesized that we might be able to minimize user decision friction by removing one of the options, but we learned that removing the six month option had no impact on conversion. We’d like to test into this more and see if we can eliminate decision friction in another way by emphasizing the most popular option.

experiments.title: How including an image influences conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a related image on the course registration page improve email acquisition rate for the Jane Austen online course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2019 - 09/17/2019
For Hillsdale’s free course offer, we wanted to test increasing clarity through providing an image related to the course subject that was used to promote the offer on the email acquisition page. The control version used the same copy and contained no image. Our hypothesis was that adding clarity and familiarizing the audience with the author of the works covered in the course content would increase the perceived value of the offer. We wanted to see if showing a black and white illustrated image would act as a distraction and introduce friction or improve conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we were not able to validate the results of the experiment and will need to pursue further testing with this hypothesis.
Adding some clarity to the offer—enough to remind the visitors of the topic that would be covered in the course and increase the perceived value of the offer—without acting as a distraction, did result in additional course registrations on the treatment page,

experiments.title: How simpliying donation page copy flow affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version of the donation page copy and format resonates with more donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2018 - 01/10/2019
Hoover institution was running their Dueling Populisms offer with their classic control instant donation page, with bulleted gift impact statements. They wanted to test a version that removed the bullet points and rearranged some of the original copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment did not validate, we did see a decrease in donation conversion from the treatment. This could be because the bulleted version lays out the reasons for giving in a more clear and concise manner.

experiments.title: How removing visual breaks in a page did not increase email signup rate on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the design elements and visual breaks on a pledge page increase email signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2019 - 11/08/2019
As a part of experimenting with what will increase email signups from the Freedom Pledge offer for Americans for Prosperity, we theorized that the visual breaks in the design may have been creating “mental hitches” for the reader that was limiting our ability to acquire emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for more than 2 weeks, there was not a significant enough difference between the control and treatment experience to validate this hypothesis, showing that there was a difference of less than 1%, with a level of confidence of only 6.75%, as well.
Further experimentation may be required.

experiments.title: How a “most popular” pre-selected value in the gift array did not hurt conversion or revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a visual treatment using that increases the visibility of the pre-selected value increase the average gift from instant donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2019 - 11/04/2019
In an attempt to increase the average gift for The Fund for American Studies’ acquisition campaign, we decided to experiment with a “most popular” treatment on the $50 amount in the gift array to see if it would increase the average gift amount from instant donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not see a validated increase in either donor conversion rate or revenue, we observed a directional lift in both metrics.
The key learning with this experiment was that after running for a month, this treatment DID NOT show, at any point, a decrease in either one of these metrics, which was enough for us to consider that the lifts we achieved were directional, and that there would not be risk in rolling this treatment out as the “new control” version of the donation page.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increases open rates for Daily Prayer
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2019
We’ve previously tested the sender name for other emails and found that using Fr. David increased open rates, but we wanted to see if it would do the same for our Daily Prayer that we send out every morning.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that using Fr. David did increase open rates, but not as much as our regular email did. We think because these recipients are so used to seeing the subject line Daily Prayer every morning that the sender name might not be as important to them.

experiments.title: How using a brand name in donor acquisition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will using a brand name in donor acquisition affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2019 - 08/04/2019
CaringBridge had long acquired many donors through their “tribute giving“ pages, which allow people to make a gift in honor of a friend or loved one using CaringBridge. The language on the donation page let the donor know that their gift made “this site” possible. The CaringBridge customer care team hypothesized that using the name “CaringBridge” instead of “this site” would increase clarity and decrease refund rate. The development team was open to this, but wanted to make sure that using the brand name didn’t negatively affect conversion. They developed a page with the same creative, but using “CaringBridge” in place of “this site”.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the brand name performed no differently than the “this site” language. This was a positive development as it cleared the way to test this downstream to increase clarity and reduce refund rate.

experiments.title: How changing Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to just Fr. David increases open rates for Daily Prayer
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2019
We’ve previously tested the sender name for other emails and found that using Fr. David increased open rates, but we wanted to see if it would do the same for our Daily Prayer that we send out every morning.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that using Fr. David did increase open rates, but not as much as our regular email did. We think because these recipients are so used to seeing the subject line Daily Prayer every morning that the sender name might not be as important to them.

experiments.title: How a dropdown on a donate button affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a drop down navigation option for one-time and recurring gifts increase overall (or recurring) giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2019 - 07/20/2019
For Caringbridge, the primary navigation has always had a prominent donation button in the top right corner. This button took all potential donors to a generic donation page that offered both one-time and recurring giving options.
There was a hypothesis that since a recurring donor and a one-time donor are two distinct segments, it may make sense to split this audience at the navigation level. Doing this would allow for targeted donation pages for each segment which may help increase the relevance and perceived value of the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months with over 10 million visitors, we found that the drop down navigation offer had no statistically significant impact on overall giving or the likelihood for individuals to give a recurring gift. This could potentially suggest that we're bringing this split too high up into the conversion funnel. Instead of the navigation, it may make more sense to emphasize this segmentation split (with associated value proposition) on the donation page itself. Similar experiments have been run using tabs on donation forms with success. That would be a natural next step.

experiments.title: How removing the banner of Evangelize emails improves clicks
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to increase clicks with our Evangelize emails.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/27/2019
We tested this with our monthly Evangelize emails. We believed that people may see our monthly newsletter banner and immediately lose interest and not scroll down to the links.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that this experiment had no impact on people clicking on the links. We will continue to for ways to improve our monthly newsletter emails.

experiments.title: Conversational “helpful” ad copy vs ad copy about a “war” on pro-life beliefs
experiments.researchQuestion: Which copy would convert more donors on our online course, copy that appealed to their feeling of being attacked by the media or copy that offered to help them overcome a longstanding fear?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2019 - 08/08/2019
We had a new online course, Defending Your Pro-Life Beliefs, that we wanted to advertise on Facebook. Our control ad focused on how pro-life beliefs are under attacks and how our course could help people defend their beliefs in the public square. We wanted to test this copy against copy that focused on how nervous people feel when they talk about being pro-life and how our course would help them be confident in these conversations. We wanted to see if appealing to the feeling among conservatives that their values were under attack would convert more donors than appealing to their desire to become confident.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The more conversational copy won across the board. The variation had a click to course signup rate of 23% vs 17% for the control. It had a Click to donation rate of 1.34% vs 1.13%. During the two week test, the control brought in $462 while the variation brought in $700.
We found that people were more likely to take the course and donate when we explained in the ad copy how the course would help them feel confident, rather than when we appealed to their desire to protect themselves from the attacks of the pro-choice Left.

experiments.title: How adding Facebook ads to lift awareness of an upcoming direct mail letter impacted letter response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding Facebook ads to promote direct mail letters impact direct mail responsiveness or giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2019 - 04/06/2019
Heritage Action wanted to experiment with whether or not adding Facebook Ads to half of the direct mail recipients in March would increase their letter response rates. Because of this, we built letter awareness ads that ran from March 1-March 16th, then switched to “urgency” ads which created an “artificial deadline” for letter recipients to “respond before it’s too late”.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant impact from the advertising. However, the experiment had to be called prior to the full data set being collected.

experiments.title: How intellectually-focused copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will intellectually-focused copy affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/20/2019 - 06/29/2019
Hillsdale College had developed copy for their fiscal year end campaign, designed to be donor-centric. However, as the copy went through their editing process, it was changed to include more intellectual language that elevated the reading level and put the College at the center, instead of the donor. Hillsdale wanted to test the “pre-edit” copy against the “post-edit” copy, and created two landing pages to split traffic to throughout the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The intellectually-focused copy produced a valid decrease of 12% early in the campaign, but later “high urgency” emails diluted both the impact and the level of confidence by increasing motivation. This resulted in a directional learning, but one that’s important to consider for future copy.

experiments.title: How indicating the time commitment of an email impacts open rate

rows.name: Why the "village" can't replace parents (4 min read)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the time commitment to the subject line increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2018 - 11/10/2018
For the weekly cultivation email sent out by Care Net, we wanted to see if we could increase the email engagement by letting subscribers know the time commitment required to consume the topic. This is something often seen on blog posts but we wanted to see if it would have a positive impact in email. To do this, we added ” (4 min read)” to the end of the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a nearly identical open rate between the two versions. After further reflection, it is our hypothesis that the longer subject line may have resulted in the “(4 min read)” not being shown in the inbox. Having this aspect cut off would have resulted in the two variants being identical.

experiments.title: How streamlining an admissions inquiry page affects completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding additional information to a landing page increase the likelihood of completion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2019 - 04/21/2019
Heartlight Ministries has an admissions inquiry page for anyone interested in getting more information about Heartlight Boarding School, that they wanted to increase conversion on. We hypothesized that adding additional information about Heartlight and answering FAQ’s on the landing page would do this. We also streamlined the page and removed extra navigation and sidebars to keep people’s focus on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version decreased conversion by 12% (not valid). It’s likely that people are so highly motivated to submit the form once they get there that additional copy actually adds friction to the page, instead of helping.

experiments.title: How “impact language” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “impact language” that describes the outcome of the gift increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2019 - 08/09/2019
CaringBridge’s main donation page had always been difficult to test, since it had a relatively low volume of traffic and a relatively high conversion rate. However, they didn’t want settle for “good enough”, so they kept testing. The control copy asked “What does CaringBridge mean to you” and had a highly personal appeal that proposed various emotions that might be brought to the surface when one uses CaringBridge. They wanted to test “impact language” that talked about what CaringBridge does instead of what it means to the individual viewer. They drafted new copy that very simply told the viewer what would happen when they gave—loved ones would be connected.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the treatment failed to produce any lift, most likely due to the high motivation of donors, which makes it difficult to increase motivation through copy. The next test on this page will be a recurring donation ask popup that seeks to increase lifetime donor value rather than conversion rate.

experiments.title: Will asking for a gift to help us reach more people improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking prospective donors to help us reach more people increase the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 08/26/2019
In an attempt to improve donor conversion rates for the Freedom Pledge, we wanted to experiment with asking the prospective donor to help us reach more people to sign the pledge they just signed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we saw a slight decrease in donor conversion rate before we moved away from the offer altogether.
With only 4 total gifts between the two variations, more experimentation with the motivation of “help us get more people with your gift today” is advised.

experiments.title: How clarifying the steps of a donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will modifying the form headers of the donation form improve donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/16/2019
The monthly recurring donor page for Focus on the Family had held a relatively low conversion rate when compared to other donation pages. After running more than five experiments, we had not been able to statistically improve overall results. After testing several value propositions, advertising to the page, and even adding additional functionality (live chat), we decided to shift focus to the form itself.
Based upon learnings we had seen with similar organizations, we decided to try modifying the form headers to add clarity on the purpose of each section. We also numbered the headlines to help move the user down the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for more than a month, we found that the numbered form headers improved donor conversion by 27%. The additional clarity of the headlines helped reduce the friction associated with the form. There is also potential that the mention of the form security in the final section helped to reduce anxiety as well.
The potential impact of this experiment can extend beyond just the Friend of Focus form. It would be our recommendation to test these changes on the main donation form and all “gift of any amount” forms.

experiments.title: How isolating an appeal from a newsletter affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will isolating the appeal from a newsletter increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a daily email newsletter that included an appeal, generally combined with a premium offer. However, they wanted to see if isolating the appeal section from the email would increase focus on that section and increase giving by presenting the reader with a “direct ask”. They created two unique emails and split the file, noting that half the file would not receive the news portion for that day.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The appeal-only email delivered 40% fewer donors, though the low overall donor conversion meant that it wasn’t statistically valid. This points to a few possible hypotheses:
First the lack of news content (if relied on by the prospect) could have lowered motivation to “return the favor” with a gift.
The steady drumbeat of news content could have reduced engagement by not offering what the prospect was used to.
This could have just been due to a random sample of the audience having more donor prospects in it than the other, for that particular offer on that particular day.
Ultimately, more testing must be done to increase donation results from the newsletter.

experiments.title: How more direct copy affected donations on a landing page

rows.name: Variant 1 -- copy test, more direct copy

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct headline that tells people what they just did (and more direct body copy) increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2019 - 08/26/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation sent chase emails to their file for a direct mail letter about ending the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare). They then versioned those chase emails to send to the rest of their file. They wanted to test the donation page copy to see if a more direct headline and explanation of what TPPF is doing to combat Obamacare would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: This copy experiment led to a 27.1% decrease in donations. However, there are several factors in the data that make these experiment results inconclusive. The sample size is too small, there is only a one gift difference, and the level of confidence is too low to come close to validating. Given this information, the experiment results cannot be used for future communications to the TPPF donor file.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a premium on the donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2019 - 07/11/2019
Focus on the Family was running a paid acquisition campaign through Facebook promoting a video series on intimacy in marriage. In a previous experiment for a different video series about parenting, they found that adding a premium offer on the donation page increased donor conversion. They wanted to test this approach with this marriage video series as well to see if they’d get the same/similar results. To test this, they added a premium to the donation page that people would see after they signed up for the free video series. The premium was the “5 Love Languages” book. They also added some copy about the premium to communicate the value of it to the end reader.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional lift of 18%. The results of this experiment are in line with what we saw from the previous experiment with the parenting video series. We would propose rolling this approach out to future acquisition efforts. The premium adds incentive to convert more new names into donors. What is important to note is that the content of the premium offer reinforced the content of the video series they just signed up for. The premium becomes the next logical step in the conversion process for people looking to improve their marriage.

experiments.title: How isolating one high-performing content offer over segmented offers impacts conversion on parenting articles
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the best performing segmented offer increase conversion if offered as a single offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2019 - 06/11/2019
Through a series of tests on the Focus on the Family parenting articles, we found that presenting segmented GOAA offers based on what people consume on the Focus website increases donor conversion over one single offer. That said, we found that there is one specific GOAA offer that performed best out of all of the segmented offers presented. To isolate this single offer and see if it could increase donor conversion alone, we tested it against the segmented offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the results. We found that the best performing segmented offer could not impact donor conversion on it’s own. We propose moving forward with segmented offers for all traffic.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the donation page impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the greatest impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 01/02/2017
Neither of the value propositions showed a statistically significant improvement over the other. With such a low instant donor conversion, is showing that the visitors were not highly motivated to give which makes finding the right language more difficult. The email offer is still receiving emails for a decent cost so we will try testing a new value proposition to improve donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: When launching the free eBook offer for The Jesus Agenda, we wanted to determine the right value proposition to use for the instant donor conversion opportunity. The initial thought was that the new subscribers may be motivated enough to make a gift to get the full version of the book. However, we also wanted to see if explaining the various aspects of Buckner’s ministry would increase the perceived value of the contribution enough to lift giving.

experiments.title: How we used A/A testing to validate the fix to an email acquisition form
experiments.researchQuestion: Is the conversion issue fixed for the NetCommunity landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2016 - 09/22/2016
At the launch of The Jesus Agenda eBook offer for Buckner International, we noticed a significantly low conversion rate that did not align with what we would expect for this kind of offer. After running a series of experiments, we did not see the kind of lift we were expecting. Not wanting to give up on the offer, we decided to launch an A/A test by created an exact duplicate of the email acquisition page in a different system. After launching this test, we noticed that the normal system was seeing a significantly lower conversion rate than the one set up in the other system.
After further investigation in Google Analytics, we discovered that a security error was being thrown for Android devices. We made a fix to the page and then relaunched this test to validate that it had fixed the issue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 4 days, this A/A test did not show a significant difference in conversion between the two systems. Based upon this result, we concluded the security warning had been the cause of the conversion problem and that it was now resolved.

experiments.title: How presenting segmented offers based on a person’s engagement on marriage articles impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering people targeted premium offers increase donor conversion on marriage articles?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 03/26/2019
Focus on the Family has done significant testing within the articles on their website. From testing the kind of offer to the placement of the offers, they have optimized and continue to optimize the conversion opportunities on the articles pages for the significant volume of traffic. Most recently they learned that presenting people a premium/book at the end of the articles, that readers could receive by giving a gift of any amount, had the most significant impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this learning, they hypothesized that presenting people with a targeted offer based on their engagement on the Focus on the Family website might increase donor conversion. Focus identified the top premium offers based on what is most requested and the kind of content that people read and consume from their site and offered premiums that closely aligned with those topics. This approach was tested against offering people one single premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we’ve continued to monitor and dig into the results of this experiment, we have discovered an interesting finding that is misrepresenting the results and learnings. A significant portion of the traffic on the article pages are either first time visitors to the Focus website, or this is the first article they’ve consumed from Focus. This audience has not been receiving targeted content offers based on cookies. Because of this, 69% of the donations from the treatment results have been receiving the single content offer/premium.
After discovering this and learning what was happening, we have paused the current experiment and relaunching it to target that audience with targeted content offers based on the current article they are reading. This small nuance to the experiment is critical in knowing whether a targeted content offer based on the kind of content people consume helps increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: Impact of changing the design of a banner ad and directing it to a monthly donation page

rows.name: Dear Reader to pledge to monthly gift

experiments.researchQuestion:
We wanted to learn two things:
Would changing the design of the ad so it was conversational and matched the look/feel of the site decrease visual friction for visitors
Would directing people to a monthly donation page increase donor revenue on the ads.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2019
For several years, we have been running banner ads on a popular pro-life website to try to drive traffic to become donors. Our most successful ads have been ads for our pledges, the most recent being one against infanticide. We wondered if making our ad match the look and feel of the site, rather than have its own design, would decrease friction for visitors. Additionally, we wanted to see how well conversational language would work in the ad. Our final desire was to see how well directing people to a monthly donation page vs a one-time donation would impact the revenue generated from the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for a month. Both ads had great engagement, however the ad on the sidebar got far more clicks than the dear reader variation. We also, as expected, got far more donations on the sidebar ad as it led to a one-time donation page. We brought in 19 donations and raised $633 on that ad. However, through the dear reader variation ad, we brought in 4 new monthly donors and raised $195.70 in their first months gifts. This will result in $2380 over the first year of their giving, 369.7% higher revenue than the control ad. 

Moreover, while the sidebar ad had far more clicks than our variation, it had a lower conversion rate to donors, even with it using a one-time donation page. .27% of clicks on the ad became one-time donors while .31% of clicks on the dear reader ad became monthly donors. While we didn’t have enough samples to validate this portion of the test, it is encouraging to see that we got higher donor conversion on the dear reader variation.

Our next test will be to change the sidebar ad so it drives traffic from a pledge to a monthly donation page. This will let us see which ad brings in more monthly donors.

experiments.title: Dear Reader footer ad vs a mid-page pledge ad (500% increase)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will bring in more monthly donors, the dear reader footer ad or the pledge ad?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/13/2019
In a prior test, we found that we could significantly increase the revenue from our Life News ads by creating a footer ad that looked like part of the site, using “dear reader” language on the ad, and linking it to a pledge and then to a monthly donation page. (the results of that test are here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2423) The “dear reader” ad had higher conversion and higher revenue than the pledge ad, but the pledge got more clicks. We wondered if we would bring in more monthly donors on the pledge due to its higher volume of clicks if we changed it so the pledge ad linked to the same monthly donor campaign as the dear reader one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the dear reader maintained a higher click to donation rate (16.67%), the pledge ad had 627% more traffic. This caused us to bring in 500% more monthly donors on the pledge ad than the “dear reader” footer ad. We also had a 76% higher email acquisition rate on traffic to the pledge ad vs the dear reader.
We aren’t sure how much of these results is due to the higher placement on the page of the pledge ad. We will likely test a “dear reader” version of this mid-page placement in the future.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page treatment impacts acquisition revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall revenue to the acquisition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2019 - 08/03/2019
After running for nearly six months, we had not been able to achieve a statistically significant lift to the donation page used with Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer. After multiple iterative approaches, we decided to test a radical redesign of the page. We combined multiple factors based upon positive results seen with similar organizations.
The down side to this approach is that it makes it difficult to isolate exactly what helped move the needle if a lift is achieved. However, the overall goal was to lift revenue and if that could be achieved then knowing the exact reason for the lift became less important.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a statistical level of significance, we did see an increased average gift from the treatment version. Initial results saw the treatment also have a higher conversion rate but this balanced out after a few weeks of running. This is a good reminder of why it is so critical to ensure there is both a significant amount of time and total conversions before making a call on any experiment.
Our current hypothesis for the increase in average gift is that the treatment makes a targeted ask of $50 while the control version asks for $25. However, given all of the other factors that changed, we will need to isolate this factor to prove it out in the future.

experiments.title: How consistency between the call-to-action and the body copy affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will consistent messaging of the value prop increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2019 - 07/21/2019
The Leadership Institute’s campus newsite CampusReform.org is proven to be major point of interest for donors and prospective donors. The Leadership Institute wanted to run a test to optimize the Campus Reform donation page. They wondered: will consistency between the call-to-action and the body copy using the word “expose” and a concentrated message of the value prop increase donations? They hypothesized that it would.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version of the donation page with concentrated copy led to a 42.5% increase in conversion rate. This experiment did not validate due to the small sample size and number of conversions. It appears that people prefer a more concise message that is consistent throughout the page. The Leadership Institute decided to roll out the winner of this experiment.

experiments.title: How the experience on a donation confirmation page impacts subsequent gifts and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase the number of subsequent gifts and overall revenue by improving the experience on the donation confirmation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2019 - 05/15/2019
A lot of focus gets put on the optimization and improvement of the actual donation process and little attention is put on the experience after a person makes a gift. We hypothesized that improving this experience and making the donor feel valued, celebrated, and affirmed in their decision to make a gift to Focus on the Family we might be able to impact and improve subsequent giving and overall revenue from the donor in the future. To do this, we developed a treatment experience on the donation confirmation page that thanked the donor by name, celebrated the donor in the copy we used, reinforced the value proposition of their gift by sharing two testimonials, and even had confetti explode on the page as soon as the donor landed on the page. We tested this approach for over six months to really assess the impact that we might be able to have on giving over a longer period of time. Half of the audience during those six months experienced the control confirmation page and the other half experienced the “Super, Happy, Thankful” treatment experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To analyze the winner, we looked at donors based upon the initial treatment they saw and removed any recurring donations since they were automated. We then looked at the likelihood of a donor to make a subsequent gift, the number of subsequent gifts, and their change in revenue (did their subsequent giving increase). In all of those situations, there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment.
Even though we didn’t see an impact on future gifts and revenue, the treatment did a better job in thanking and celebrating the donor – which is important. Maybe the one joyful, celebratory experience on the donation confirmation page wasn’t enough to impact giving, but could we create that same experience in a series of emails every single month for the donor and have an impact on giving? We’d like to test it.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up presented at the time of a donation being processed impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the recurring gift pop-up increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2019 - 06/21/2019
Back to the Bible wanted to increase the number of recurring donors coming from their primary donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that presented a pop-up at the moment a gift was being processed with the opportunity to convert their one-time gift into a recurring donation. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional increase in recurring gift conversion of 38% with the recurring gift pop-up. What this tells us is that people are willing to make a recurring gift if we clearly communicate the even greater impact they can have through their gift and give them the opportunity to do it. When analyzing all data associated with this experiment we also found that it did not significantly impact the likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an offer in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an image of an offer in an email affect click rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2019
Hillsdale College recently launched a new online course titled “The Second World Wars.” They are now offering this course as a DVD boxed set and are offering it as a premium offer to their housefile. They wanted to know whether or not adding an image of the DVD boxed set to the email would have any effect on click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 4% lift in the treatment version with the image of the DVD. The confidence level of this experiment was not at or above the 98th percentile, so this experiment shows no change in results. Even with a larger sample size, this test is not likely to validate. This is because there is such a small difference in click rate that a larger sample size will not determine any valid conclusion. This image test can be run instead on the landing page where people actually convert. We hypothesize that people like to see visuals with text as it helps them better understand what is being asked of them/offered to them.

experiments.title: How more mystery in a subject line affects click-rate

rows.name: The webinar recording is ready for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line with more mystery lead to more opens and clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2019 - 07/22/2019
In this webinar follow-up email, we had been using the same kind of subject line for a while. While the sample size is low and hard to validate on, we wanted to put a hypothesis to the test that a subject line with more mystery would lead to more opens and clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we assumed, our sample size was not large enough to validate the result, despite seeing an 11% increase in clicks from the treatment version. A more radical test will be required in order to see a statistically confidence result.

experiments.title: How linking one line of text instead of two affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will having only one linked line of text in email copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2019 - 07/10/2019
Hillsdale College ran a fiscal year end campaign. In this campaign, they versioned emails to lifecycle audiences: lapsed donors, active donors, and nondonors. In their email to active donors, they wanted to know if linking one line of text to a donation page instead of two or more lines would affect click rate. They decided to test this on their third email appeal in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 5.8% increase in clickthrough rate in the treatment with only one linked line of text to a donation page. However, this is not a large difference and the level of confidence is insufficient to conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: The impact of direct mail cultivation on overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending cultivation and stewardship-focused post cards increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2018 - 06/11/2019
For the complete writeup, visit https://app.winstonknows.com/e/z7Nd.
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to help retain existing donors. Knowing the importance of good cultivation and stewardship, we wanted to focus on methods that could improve the frequency and method for communicating impact and gratitude to the donors. One hypothesis was to send post cards throughout the year that both thanked them for their support and reminded them of the impact their gift.
To do this, we isolated 350K donors and split it randomly in such a way that there would be equal representation for each donor lifecycle segment. Half of these donors would receive four post cards throughout the year in addition to the normal communication stream. The control group would just get the standard communications.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant change to overall revenue. We did observe a 1.8% increase to donor retention / reactivation but the decrease in revenue per donor resulted in minimal difference between overall revenue.

experiments.title: How deploying a coaster premium affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of a premium incentivize more people to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2019 - 06/28/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation ran a campaign to fund a legal case to defend John Yearwood against the federal government. They had a coaster premium emblazoned with Texas’ famous motto “Come and Take It” that they had used in direct mail, and wanted to test it on the donation page to see if it would increase conversion. They created a simple treatment that added an image of the coaster to the page and split traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than two months, the coaster premium showed a directional decline in donations, though it wasn’t statistically valid and did not reach a valid sample size. Since this wasn’t able to move the needle, TPPF decided to go back to the drawing board and find a new test to deliver a radical result.

experiments.title: How a text endorsement compares to a video endorsement for conversion impact
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a text endorsement and/or video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 06/02/2018
This experiment was tested on the “Introduction to the Constitution” online course sign up page.
