We had found in previous experiments that videos negatively impacted a prospects likelihood to make a gift but we had seen some indication that they could help improve email acquisition if used in the right way. We had a video that provided an endorsement for the new online course and we wanted to see if it could help boost email acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, there was not enough difference in conversions to suggest that the video treatment made any impact. After running for a full month, we decided to move on to the next hypothesis since it did not see to make any significant improvements.

experiments.title: How positioning the printed guide impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion:
Would using the appeal of the ability to take notes in a physical book improve donor conversion rate?
5/16/2019 Note: As it stands currently, there is only a 1% difference in Stickybar clickthrough rate, but the donor conversion rate for the “take notes in a printed guide” message is proving to increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/23/2019
While continuing experimentation around what sticky bar message generates the most donors, we wanted to test the donation page headline (from the control) against a new message. We assumed that one motivation to receive the printed guide might be simply because the person like the benefits of a physical book. So, we played with the message to lean into that assumption.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a successful micro-donation acquisition campaign to raise money to feed Holocaust survivors. However, they wanted to see if they could increase conversion or revenue through additional landing page testing. They created a treatment that centered the value proposition at the top, included a picture of a Holocaust survivor, put the copy on a white background for more legible reading, and then reoriented the gift array. Previously, the gift array offered $3 and $90, with a pre-filled $30 ask in the open field. The treatment page offered the $3 gift, along with a $45 gift and a $90 gift. The hypothesis was that the landing page elements would increase conversion and the bracketing might increase average gift. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 67% increase in revenue that had a valid level of confidence, though with the small overall sample size of donors, this was not fully valid. This showed that the page elements did not, in fact, increase conversion, but that the bracketing did increase the average gift enough to validate the separation between the control and the treatment.

experiments.title: How Target ROAS settings in Facebook didn’t impact the email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would “Target ROAS” improve results for the offer in terms of leads generated, donors acquired and/or dollars raised?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 05/10/2019
When The Heritage Foundation launched a new ebook offer, we wanted to test the viability of increased ROI and donor conversion rate efficiency by installing a competitive “Target ROAS” campaign to split target audience traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 19.8% level of confidence, we were only able to notice a 1.6% increase in email registration rate.
Although the campaign served only about 1/3 the number of impressions and percentage-wise was about even with the “Lowest Cost” campaign type, we saw costs continue to scale with donation results flatline. For that reason, we decided to turn off this treatment to the traffic split and focus on creating the volume we needed at this time.
We may reconsider adding a “Target ROAS” element to this campaign in the future, but for now it appears that the best approach is to let the algorithm create results from the “lowest cost” setting.

experiments.title: How prompting for a reply impacts email engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will prompting the reader for a reply increase the email click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2019 - 05/21/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending out their weekly email cultivation email to a select group of subscribers. The primary goal of these emails is to increase the overall engagement of this group, as a result we wanted to test out tactics that could improve this metric.
Every email sends them an article to read on the ADF blog. In order to give them a reason to read the blog post, we wanted to try prompting the reader to respond to the email. The belief was that if we gave them a reason to read the article (in this case, to tell us their opinion), they would be more likely to consume the content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistically significant measurable difference between the control and treatment version of this experiment. However, one of the more challenging metrics to measure is the actual replies to this email. This would be a higher form of engagement than a click through rate but it is not easily captured.
This will need to be updated once we receive anecdotal evidence from the ADF team on the number of responses.

experiments.title: How a personal donation ask affects conversion on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value proposition in letter form from a known figure of the organization on a donation page increase donor conversion more than a traditional stepped approach with a premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2019 - 05/11/2019
Heartlight Ministries was offering a free online course called Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, and wanted to test a previous approach on the donation page that involved a letter from Mark instead of the original stepped donation approach with a premium. This letter from Mark was a much more conversational approach, that told the story of Heartlight and explained that the true goal of the ministry was to equip so many parents, that they didn’t need to run the boarding school anymore.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment option increased donations by 94%. Even though it wasn’t fully valid, this tells us that the message of Heartlight and Mark’s heart for the ministry came through in this copy enough to move people to want to give more than a traditional stepped approach, or the option to get a premium for a donation. This messaging angle, which we refer to as “close the camp”, is a powerful angle that emotionally connects with a potential donor. It is also written in Mark’s voice, which makes a strong impression on donors who heard from Mark in the Facebook ad, in the housefile email, and throughout the course, so they know him and trust him when he says these things.

experiments.title: How Faith-Driven Copy Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page more focused on the religious value proposition of “C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2017 - 08/31/2017
Due to lower traffic volumes, this test did not validate. Further testing will be necessary to provide an answer to this research question. However, Hillsdale can still consider the results that they found. Though there wasn’t a statistically significant difference, Hillsdale did notice that the faith-focused copy was approaching a valid decrease in donor conversion.
This decrease in donor conversion may suggest that the treatment page copy did not increase the clarity of the course’s value proposition. It may also indicate that visitors felt an increase in tension if they did not align with the overtly religious value proposition. New tests are being developed to provide a clearer answer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After noticing new room for improvement, Hillsdale College launched a series of new donation page treatments. “C.S. Lewis: Writings and Significance” is one of their most popular online courses. After reviewing their donation page, they hypothesized that emphasizing why they offer this course from a religious viewpoint would help clarify the course’s value proposition for visitors. They created a treatment and launched a test to find out.

experiments.title: How a homepage promotion for the membership program impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can a promotion for the membership program on the home page increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2019 - 04/04/2019
After launching a new Membership program, Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to promote the program on their homepage. We wanted to know how the introduction of the membership program overall from the home page would impact donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the home page promotion for over a month, we did notice a slight increase in donor conversion rate of +8.6%, but only achieved a level of confidence of 48.7%, which means that the experiment was not validated.
We did note that within that slight increase in donor conversion lift, that approximately 10% (13/130) gifts did come directly from the home page promotion.
In the end, we collectively agreed that the promotion itself needed a more “fair” treatment for the promotion of the control’s “donate” option.
For that reason, we ended the experiment and will be launching a new treatment which will more “fairly” promote the control’s “donate” path. We will also change the placement (from the right-side of the home page’s “hero image” area, as shown in the screenshot included to just below the “hero image” and the next section of content titled “Latest from the Blog”.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a main donation page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding third-party validation and more context about the organization in the copy increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) is testing their donation pages to increase conversion rates. They wondered if talking more about Texas and how TPPF can help Texans and the rest of America be more free would make a bigger impact on donations. They decided to take a radical redesign approach to their main donation page. They added a header with a call-to-action, more copy that explained what TPPF does and who they help, removed links that took people to a different page, and added an image and quote of a famous Texan for third-party validation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results from this experiment were surprising. The radical redesign caused a directional 38.4% decrease in revenue, but with no statistical validity. The original donation page had a better conversion rate, even with the less-inclusive copy and the links to another site. However, because the confidence level is not at or above 95%, this experiment is not statistically valid. TPPF should continue to run experiments on their main donation page, but overall conversions combined will make it difficult to validate a result.

experiments.title: How adding third-party validation to a donation page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a quote and image of a famed Texan increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) ran a petition in support of John Yearwood, a veteran who is working with TPPF to defend his land from the government. After the petition, petition signers have an opportunity to make a donation in support of TPPF and people like John Yearwood. TPPF wanted to run an experiment on the donation page where they tested the effect of third-party validation on conversion rates. They decided to use a quote and image of former Texas governor Rick Perry to see if it would lift conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The conversion rates for each of the two donation page versions during the one month that this experiment ran were the virtually the same, which meant that the photo alone had no impact on conversion rate. This means that TPPF must make bigger changes to impact conversion in this funnel.

experiments.title: How shortening a quiz’s first page content decreased email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter amount of text on the first (registration page) of a quiz improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2019 - 05/08/2019
When Americans for Prosperity launched their “Economic Freedom Quiz” offer, we wanted to experiment with what would boost email registration rates. In reviewing the first slide of the quiz, we noticed that there was a lot of copy, so we wanted to see if shortening it would keep more of the form on the visitor’s screen to improve the quiz start rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 69.4% level of confidence, we noticed a slight decline (-7.7%) in email registration rates.
Because this experiment did not validate and the difference between the two versions was so slight (< 10%), there is no key learning as a part of this experiment.
Further experimentation is advise.

experiments.title: How asking for support to extend our reach did not improve donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking Freedom Pledge signers to help us by funding our efforts to find more people like them outperform asking them to become a “donor activist”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2019 - 05/11/2019
As a part of Americans for Prosperity’s Freedom Pledge offer, we wanted to see if we could motivate donors to give towards helping us reach more people to band together with them to create a movement, as opposed to a straight-forward “become a donor activist” appeal to see if we could lift the donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that this version of the offer’s message is turned off, we turned off this experiment.
With only a 28.4% level of confidence, we did notice a developing trend of -14.3% on this approach. We did, however, also notice a +20% increase in revenue (with a level of confidence of 62%) with this approach.
Further experimentation may be required to determine the overall viability of asking this audience to fund reach, as opposed to asking them to become a “donor activist”.
It should be noted that this was related to the “original” Freedom Pledge positioning/language, which has been replaced, where overall, that messaging appeared to drive a higher level of instant donor conversion motivation when compared to the new messaging that we are required to use with the offer.

experiments.title: How switching the email subject line to place the words gift and prayer first increases opens
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Enroll a Loved One Today - Share the Gift of Prayer
Share the Gift of Prayer - Enroll a Loved One Today

experiments.researchQuestion: The question that we we’re wanting to answer is if this worked for every email. Would people be more willing to click if they noticed gift and prayer first in the subject line or would they still click on the email if that was towards the end of the subject line.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2019
We tested this through Mailchimp. We’ve noticed in previous tests and throughout our website analytics that most of the donors respond more when they see the words gift or prayer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key takeaway is that the results were almost completely identical. This could be because we also had words like Loved Ones at the beginning of the email that also usually generates a lot of interest with our audience. Moving forward when running a test like this we will have to make sure there is no words that could make an impact emotionally like Loved Ones is.

experiments.title: How removing a high-dollar premium from a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing a high-dollar premium from a donation page increase instant donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 05/03/2019
DTS was running a test on their Revelation donation page that utilized copy that had just increased donations on the Genesis course in a previous experiment, but also removed a high-dollar premium that they hypothesized was leading to fewer instant donations, because they thought it was possible that people didn’t want to give at the level necessary to get the book ($75).
experiments.keyLearnings: This test didn't validate, but showed a slight trend towards a lower instant conversion rate with the treatment. These results are likely to be better analyzed in future testing if the same copy is used on both control and treatment, but that the premium offer's placement on the page is an isolated variable.

experiments.title: How an updated value proposition impacted conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on children instead of the whole family and that incorporates scripture increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2019 - 05/02/2019
The text used on Buckner’s main donation page was created over three years ago and used messaging and tactics that we had long ago tested out of in our acquisition efforts. In an effort to boost the conversion to the page, we decided to test out an updated value proposition that incorporated scripture and focused more on the tangible impact to the children that Buckner serves instead of the holistic care being provided to all individuals.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant change in donor conversion from the treatment version of the donation page. It is possible that we did not identify the right value proposition for this audience. However, it is our hypothesis that it is likely the form itself that is causing a donor conversion rate lower than we would prefer. The next step in testing this hypothesis is setting up a new experiment that reduces the friction and anxiety associated with the form.

experiments.title: How a cultivation email prior to an appeal impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will long-form story emails positively impact overall giving to a campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2019 - 05/05/2019
In an analysis of prior appeals, Buckner International had discovered that their long story emails tended to be the lowest performing appeals. However, we had a hypothesis that while these emails may not perform well immediately, they may be influencing downstream giving. Given the time and effort required to write these emails, we decided to run a test to measure the impact of these “cultivation” style appeals when they are a part of a larger campaign.
To do this, we split the file in half and sent one segment a long story prior to the two appeal emails. The treatment just received the two appeal emails. We then monitored the donor giving across the entire campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: At the end of the campaign, we did not see any significant difference in donor conversion with the additional email. However, we did see a 50% increase to unsubscribes (with 100% level of statistical confidence) due to the addition of the extra email. Given that the additional story email did not have the positive impact on giving that we hypothesized, it would seem that we can reduce the number of emails in the appeal while negatively impacting giving.

experiments.title: How multichannel communication impacted a Giving Tuesday solicitation
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask made via a straight-to-voicemail call increase the likelihood of a donor to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 11/30/2017
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Live Action wanted to see if they could use multiple channels of communication to drive incremental giving. Using a new technology named SlyBroadcast, we recorded a “voicemail” from the organization’s founder, Lila Rose. SlyBroadcast was then able to send this message straight to the voicemail of several hundred of their donors.
The message itself started with stewardship and led to a direct ask for support on Giving Tuesday. We wanted to see how this would impact the donor’s likelihood to give.
To determine this, we divided all recent donors with an email and phone number in half. We then sent the SlyBroadcast message to one segment. It should be noted that not everyone in the treatment segment would have actually received the voicemail since it would only work with digital phone lines. We saw a 60% delivery rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment shows a percentage increase, it did not come anywhere close to statistical significance. This same test was performed with two other organizations at the same time and all had similar results. What this indicates is that while it didn’t hurt the chances of giving, it didn’t seem to help them either.
In the future, we will likely want to avoid using SlyBroadcast for direct solicitations. Instead, it should be reserved for stewardship and cultivation; basically, any communication where we are not making a call to action.

experiments.title: How targeting direct mail prospects with digital advertising affected direct mail revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does targeting direct mail prospects with branding ads and content offers via Facebook ads increase direct mail revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/17/2019 - 12/31/2018
Missionary Oblates had significant investments in their direct mail program and wanted to see if they could optimize the return by investing in digital marketing that would target these prospects in the time period that they received a direct mail letter.
They created a control audience that was excluded from seeing any ads, and a treatment audience that would see a rotation of brand and eBook offering ads (but no donation-centric ads) for a two week period before the mail piece hit mailboxes and for two weeks after, the prime time in which a donor would respond. They optimized the ads for reach—aiming to show ads to as large a percentage of the target group as possible. The goal of this test was to lift direct mail revenue—not to add an additional segment of online revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was not valid. We’d like to test this again at a different time of year, when the motivation of donors isn’t necessarily as high as it is at year end.

experiments.title: How text reminders impacted campaign giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will cultivating donors via text during a moneybomb campaign increase overall conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2018 - 04/24/2018
For the 2018 Tax Day Moneybomb, we decided to test out a next texting technology to see if reminding donors to give on the day of a big campaign would increase overall conversion. Since we did not have a significant volume of cell phone numbers, we had to append the numbers to donor records. We began the campaign with a cultivation text the day before the deadline and then a reminder to give on the day of the deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: When we compared those donors that were sent a text with those that could have received a text, we see that there was no statistically significant difference between the two segments. When we also factor in the time it took to set up the texting, the complaints received from members, and relatively low potential impact, this does not seem like a tactic that would be worth repeating for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a primary issue on the main donation page increased donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can moving a “defeat cronyism” -focused message into a single column layout outperform the two-column, generic message of “restore economic freedom” message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2019 - 04/21/2019
We wanted to experiment with improving the results of the primary donation form. In reviewing the “control” experience, we theorized that we could lift performance by addressing two issues:
Creating a more “issue-focused” message (defeating cronyism), and
Moving to a single column layout (instead of a two-column layout).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment ran for more than 3-months and we only achieved a 47.9% level of confidence, we did see a +15.0% increase in donor conversion rate.
We did notice that the treatment also saw a +60% increase in revenue (with a 71.1% level of confidence), as well.
Although neither treatment officially validated, we believe this information is directional enough (having run for 3-months) to move forward with the treatment donation page. It would appear that the primary issue the organization is largely focused upon solving (ending cronyism) is more inspirational, driving a higher conversion rate and average gift/revenue than the control message.

experiments.title: How offering more content options in an update email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending a newsletter style email with more content options lead to create click-throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2018 - 12/10/2018
In this experiment, we were testing a first iteration of a more newsletter-like email. We have more content than we are able to share through these long-winded personal emails, so we wanted to see if sending more content options in more of newsletter or digest format would affect our click-rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant difference in click between the two versions, indicating that this change in formate does not have a measurable impact the likelihood of someone visiting our website. That being said, option B allows us to share more types of content which could theoretically lead to greater engagement.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a premium added to a landing page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2018 - 06/01/2018
Compassion International wanted to test the impact of a premium on sponsorship conversion. They had a partnership with Chip and Joanna Gaines that allowed them to offer a book as a premium as a way to thank the donor for their sponsorship.
They added this premium to the sponsorship pages specifically around their remarketing campaigns driven from Facebook and launched an A/B test to determine the impact for conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the premium offer to the donation page produced a slight lift (8%) with no statistical confidence. This lack of variance seemed to indicate that by the time the prospect had reached the page, they had already made up their mind to give and weren’t affected by the premium.

The Compassion team decided to launch another test after this that moved the premium offer upstream in the communication chain to see if the premium would attract more traffic from the Facebook ad itself, and would result in a higher downstream conversion rate.

experiments.title: How matching gift language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will added matching grant language affect donor conversion after an online course signup?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/23/2017
Hillsdale College had received a matching grant of $400,000 to double gifts from their online courses. They wondered what the impact of adding this language to their online course donation pages would be—their hypothesis is that it would increase giving by giving donors an incentive to be generous.
They created treatments across three course donation pages that included the extra language for the matching grant and launched an experiment to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The matching gift language produced a slight, though statistically invalid decrease in conversion. A meta-analysis of Hillsdale’s online course pages showed that it was critical to not “break the spell” of excitement after signing up for a course.
Though matching gift language might work in other areas of fundraising, it might cause the prospect to think too much about the donation, thus breaking the spell and reducing conversion.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad image can affect conversion for a free online course offer [Lookalikes]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an image that more clearly conveys the title of the course result in greater registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2017 - 10/23/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign promoting our online course on Facebook advertising, we wondered if we could increase registrations by adding clarity to the Facebook ad image. We added a couple additional elements to the ad image that clearly showed the idea of “Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors.”
The results below are from the Lookalikes audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was ended early due to a large jump in cost-per-registration from the control. Although it did not reach full validity, the experiment was leaning towards the treatment as the winner. This indicates that the updated image brought additional clarity to the value proposition – something that helped this lookalikes audience understand the its relevancy.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Nonprofit News Sources]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting an aggregate audience including follower of the Nonprofit Times and Chronicle of Philanthropy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not reach validity based on conversion. The control ad was turned off due to a significantly higher cost-per-registration in the treatment.
Neither the control or treatment was appearing to approach validity, and so it appears that adding bullets into the copy had no significant effect. Although, a low statistical power implies that we may not have had a large enough sample size to detect an effect if there indeed was one to be detected.
In any case, it is possible that the audience by nature is more prone to reading with closer scrutiny given that they are followers of nonprofit industry news sources. If this is the case, they would have reached the same understanding of the value propositon, regardless of whether or not they saw the control or the treatment.

experiments.title: How a case study example in an email affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a follow-up webinar email that uses a case-study example increase registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2017 - 10/12/2017
When sending a re-targeted email to invite non-responders to register for an upcoming webinar, we wondered if there was a way to write a more appealing follow-up email than simply re-hashing the content of the prior email.
For the treatment, we thought that we could help the user experience the quality of the content we would be presenting by showing a real case study that applied to year-end fundraising.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no measurable difference in opens, clicks, or conversions between the control and treatment.
This tells us that neither method is better than the other to get more people to register for a webinar. Further testing would be needed to determine whether or not this type of email makes a difference for various segments of the non-responder audience (i.e. Non-opens VS Opens without conversions).

experiments.title: How suggesting impacting in the giving array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does aligning the gift array to specific impact increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2016 - 04/08/2016
For the Hillsdale moneybomb dedicated to raising funds for the DRTV campaign, we had a unique opportunity for the college. We were able to assign impact to the amount of money given; for $70, a donor could help secure a single DRTV spot.
Since we are not often able to assign specific impact to a gift, we decided to test what aligning the gift array to this impact would have on revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had a limited amount of time that we could run so we were not able to reach statistical validity. What we observed was that the gift handles that tied the gift to the DRTV spot received less revenue per visitor.
After examining the actual gifts given, we found that we lost smaller gift donors with the treatment. This would be the result of the gift array starting at $70 instead of $25 which is our control. This would not normally be an issue if we can significantly raise the average gift but, unfortunately, this treatment did not do that.

experiments.title: How a content focused homepage affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a content-driven homepage increase the number of clicks to additional content on nextafter.com?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2017 - 08/15/2017
The NextAfter homepage had been focused on the work of our consultancy since the web site was first created. Over time, we have started to produce more and more content based on our online fundraising research. We’ve realized that this content is most often the first point of contact that someone has with NextAfter. The perceived problem is that our content is not being featured on the homepage.
Elevating our top content to our homepage has the potential to improve SEO, improve a user’s ability to navigate to the content they’re looking for, and gain a stronger understanding of our research. A homepage structured around our content will also give us more flexibility in the future to produce, feature, and market new content types and offers.
We decided to test this content driven homepage to see 1) if it would improve or maintain bounce rates, and 2) improve or maintain conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The number of clicks to view other content on nextafter.com saw a 5% increase, although it only reached a 77% level of confidence. Additionally, we did not see a statically valid difference in conversion rates on the homepage.
We also evaluated the average session duration of the control and treatment, finding that the content driven homepage had an 18% increase in session duration with a 91% level of confidence.
With 2 of 3 key metrics trending towards an increase, and conversion rates having no statistically valid change, we will begin to roll out this new homepage. In this case, even having no change is a win for us because this new design and layout will help us craft and organize our future content more effectively.

experiments.title: How a stylized email template affects conversion rate for a webinar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stylized email template affect the conversion rate for a webinar?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2017 - 04/05/2017
We had tested out of using email templates with our nonprofit research partners, but we had never tested what affect an email template would have with our own audience.
So we set up a treatment for a webinar promotional email that included a branded header and footer, as well as an image of the guest speaker. We hypothesized that this change could increase the credibility of both the speaker and the event itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistically valid difference between the performance of the plain-text email and the stylized email template.
Moving forward, we will continue to use the plain-text version since it is easier to set-up, and it is consistent with what we recommend to our nonprofit partners.

experiments.title: How visually reinforcing a deadline impacts store conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will visually reinforcing the December 25th deadline increase the conversion rate of the site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2016 - 12/25/2016
One of the most successful fundraising campaigns for World Concern is their Global Gift Guide, especially leading up to Christmas. There is a high volume of traffic going to the site in the week prior to December 25th. With that as a natural deadline, we wanted to see if we could visually increase the urgency of the Christmas holiday.
We created an animated drop down that would appear when visitors first arrived at the site. It contained language that reinforced the overall value proposition of the organization and an animated countdown clock that would remind them of the Christmas deadline. There was no call to action since the Gift Guide has many options for people to choose.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a limited time span available leading up to Christmas, we were not able to validate any significant lift. However, it would appear the drop down may have increased conversion rate if there had been more time to collect traffic. In future iterations of this concept, it is recommended that we find a specific item or gift to highlight to give the dropdown a specific call to action instead of the more general value proposition.

experiments.title: Student vs. Celebrity 3rd Party Credibility
experiments.researchQuestion: Which 3rd party credibility source has the most influence on email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2016 - 11/18/2016
In the ongoing effort to improve the Constitution 101 email acquisition efforts, we wanted to see which source would be best to lend 3rd party credibility. To do this, we tested the standard Rush Limbaugh quote against quotes from students that reinforced the value of the course.
experiments.keyLearnings: The student quotes showed no significant difference over the control. Nothing to change here.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field affects name acquisition conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a required phone number field to the sign-up form affect the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2016 - 07/20/2016
At NextAfter, we have a monthly live broadcast in which we present findings from the research we conduct with nonprofits, break down tests and experiments, and explore trends that demonstrate what makes a donor give.
We wanted to add a phone number field to our live broadcast sign-up form, but not at the expense of losing attendees.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not reach a significant level of confidence to conclude that adding the phone number field made a difference in conversion. If anything, the results pointed to a higher conversion rate with the phone number.
Moving forward, we will keep the phone number field knowing that it does not create significant enough friction in the sign up process to affect our attendance numbers, and it gives us another way to keep in touch with our attendees.

experiments.title: How Donation Page copy test between pro-life vs. fiscal angle on defunding Planned Parenthood
experiments.researchQuestion:
Does including the term “pro-life,” mentioning that we work with “pro-life organizations” and that we are for stopping all abortions resonate with our donors and increase gifts and revenue?
OR
Does only focusing on goal of stripping federal taxpayer dollars from going to Planned Parenthood increase gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 -
We have some limited knowledge that those who want to defund Planned Parenthood don’t necessarily identify themselves as pro-life. They are fiscally conservative and not necessarily social conservative. We want to drill into this and see how much of our base identifies this way. This will help guide messaging moving forward- helping with what terms to use and what terms to avoid.

experiments.title: How a modified value proposition affected campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the biggest impact on revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 09/30/2015
In the final days of the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we were looking for ways to improve results. After reading the original copy on the page, we decided there were areas that we could add additional clarity to. Our hypothesis was that by adding the additional clarity, we could improve the understanding of the value proposition which would lead to increased conversion rate on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The modifications made to the copy on the page had no statistically significant impact on either revenue or conversion rate. In this situation, it would appear that the changes were not radical enough to make a major impact. It is also worth noting that at the end of a money bomb campaign such as this, the motivation of the visitors is often high enough that subtle changes will not have a major impact.

experiments.title: How the imagery of a page affected the campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: What impact does adding imagery to the page have on donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 09/28/2015
For the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we wanted to find a way to increase the overall performance of the landing page. We had the hypothesis that by adding supporting imagery that reinforced the messages being communicated on the page, we could make the content more engaging and easier to understand. In this situation, we were using references to the Constitution and quoting notable historical figures such as Thomas Jefferson.  We decided to set up a new treatment that would include images of those elements along with the text.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the imagery to the page had no significant impact on the overall performance of the page. We will often debate the amount of influence design has on the overall conversion of the page. In this situation, the design elements did not increase value proposition, lower friction, or reduce anxiety; as a result, that made no significant impact on the likelihood of a donor to convert.

experiments.title: How communicating value with the gift array affects revenue and member acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation form will produce the most revenue and new members for the organization?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2015 - 08/27/2015
The Texas State Historical Association is a membership-based organization with varying levels of benefits at each member level. The idea is to incentivize members to increase their giving in order to raise the average gift.
The most common donation form listed out each membership level and the associated benefits. This design led to a long donation form that was heavy on the copy. Our concern was that this structure produced additional friction to the giving process which would, in turn, lower conversion.  We wanted to find a way to decrease the size of the form while still communicating the value of each membership level. We decided to test a new form that utilized stylized buttons and a single, dynamic paragraph to describe the associated benefits of their selected level.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment with stylized buttons produced no significant lift to either average gift or conversion. We were able to reduce friction on the form but, by hiding the benefits of each membership level, we equally reduced the incentive. These two factors offset each other which resulted a no significant change.

experiments.title: How the length of page copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a shorter version of copy reduce page friction enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2015 - 08/30/2015
TSHA’s standard membership form utilized a long version of copy in order to fully explain the mission of the organization, the history, and the associated benefits of becoming a member. There was a hypothesis that this long copy may actually be turned off potential members due to the amount of friction it introduced into the process.
We decided to create a new version of the page that would dramatically reduce the copy in order to validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two landing pages had nearly identical conversion rates and there was no statistically significant difference between the average gift amounts. This result supports the conclusion that the length of copy on the page is not a significant factor in the overall conversion of the page.
In the future, it would be recommended that we need to find ways to add value for the potential member through different value propositions, incentives, and other page elements.

experiments.title: How the donation premium can affect both conversion rate and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Which premium will increase the overall revenue from existing members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 09/30/2015
As part of Texas State Historical Association’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to find a way to improve the average gift of existing members in order to boost revenue. The majority of existing members were acquired using the premium of the 2014-15 Texas Almanac. The update to this book was going to be released in the coming months so we had a feeling it would be a popular premium offer to use with the donor base.
However, this book is normally offered for a gift of $50 which meant it wouldn’t do much to raise the average gift. We decided to test another popular book as a premium and increase the minimum gift to $100 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Along Forgotten River premium offer reduced the donor conversion rate of the page by 50%. However, it was also able to increase the average gift by 64%. These two factors cancelled out the combined impact on revenue and produced no statistically significant change in either direction.  This does prove to us that if we can find the right premium offer, we should be able to increase the average gift enough to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How membership benefits affect conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does talking about member benefits on the year end landing page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/30/2015
During the fiscal year end campaign, the Texas State Historical Association had a landing page targeting non-members. It focused on the legacy of the organization and the work being done day-in and day-out. There was a premium for a gift of $50 or more but there were no other mentions of associated member benefits. These benefits had proven to be successful in other experiments so we decided to test them out in the year end campaign as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: The member benefits produced no statistically significant change in donor conversion. This would suggest that either they are not necessary during an urgency-based appeal like the fiscal year end or that the benefits did not add significant value for the potential donors. We will need to isolate these factors on a general donation form to determine which of these hypothesis are true.

experiments.title: How the landing page design can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another. Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as "approaching validity." This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How the premium’s minimum gift amount affects revenue and member acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will acquire the greatest amount of revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2015 - 01/11/2016
Prior to the move to the $100 minimum gift amount for the Texas Almanac, we wanted to get an idea of how a $100 minimum gift premium would impact the revenue and number of new members acquired. With the Handbook of Texas Music eBook offer, we had the opportunity to test this out by offering the Almanac for $50 and the Handbook of Texas Music for $100.  While not a true test of the impact of moving the impact to $100, the idea was that we could get an idea of the potential ramifications.
experiments.keyLearnings: With this experiment, we did not see any significant change in either conversion rate or average gift. Based upon these results, it would seem the premium was not the primary motivation for members to join.

experiments.title: How a high conversion rate affects visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which petition copy will acquire the most “signatures?”
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2016 - 02/01/2016
In response to statements made by Hillary Clinton about repealing the Hyde Amendment, a law that protects tax payer money from being put towards abortion, Susan B. Anthony List launched a petition. The goal was to collect enough signatures to send to the conservative candidates running for president so that they would understand the importance of this law to the American public.
To learn more about the motivations of the SBA List constituency, we developed two landing page treatments. The first positioned Hillary Clinton as the villain and urged the reader to sign the petition to help SBA list combat the attack. The second took a more educational approach in explaining the Hyde amendment but ended with how SBA List was going to fight back and why their signature was necessary.  We then split traffic between the two pages and drove traffic via email and Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither treatment produced a significant lift in visitor conversion or subsequent donor conversion on the next page. Both treatments received an 85% conversion rate which would indicate that the motivation of the visitors was high enough that the copy was not a major influence in the decision to sign. Given the exceptionally high conversion rate on this page, we decided to shift the testing efforts to the Facebook ads and donor conversion on the next step.

experiments.title: How member benefits affected donor conversion during a moneybomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will member benefits increase the donor conversion or average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/24/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find a way to incentivize subscribers to become a new member at higher levels. Their standard campaigns have historically not focused on the membership aspect of their gift and instead tried to sell solely the philanthropic aspect of the donation.
We decided to test out language around the benefits associated with membership and include a premium offer of a book that reinforced the messaging of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant lift for either treatment. Since the campaign was for a limited period of time, we decided to end the test in order to try a different treatment that might achieve a greater lift. It would seem these benefits do not carry the added incentive in the donors’ minds when we have a money bomb campaign such as this.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects revenue on a donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page will increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2016 - 03/08/2016
In response to Hillary Clinton’s plans to repeal the Hyde Amendment, SBA List created a petition in support of the Hyde Amendment. Traffic was pushed to the petition page through email and Facebook advertising. Once someone signed the petition they were taken to a thank you page with a donation ask.
SBA List wanted to do a radical redesign of the donation page to try and increase revenue. The control was a page layout they had been using previously. For the treatment, they tested a one column approach with a new headline and body copy that reinforced and clarified the value proposition. They also presented fewer giving arrays and a new call-to-action on the button. They did an A/B split test to determine which page would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings: They were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the test. Though the initial data they were able to get did show the one column treatment performing better because of a higher average gift, it is not enough data to come to a conclusion with those results. A similar test will be done for a future campaign with a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How creating unique landing pages for every email in a campaign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will creating unique landing pages to tie to each email theme increase the donors to a money bomb campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2016 - 04/12/2016
During a SBA List money bomb, we wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations by creating unique landing pages for each email theme. Each email focused on a specific theme taken from their all-encompassing general value proposition. Besides wanting to increase the number of donors through reinforcing the message and theme from email to landing page, SBA List also wanted to see which theme of their value proposition, donors would respond to most. We tested these unique landing pages against a general, all-encompassing value proposition landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the test, initial results showed that unique landing pages for each email theme did not help increase donations. The general value proposition landing page was able to convert donors at a higher rate. These initial findings tell us that SBA List donors aren’t more motivated by one theme over another and instead are motivated by all that SBA List does. These learnings were implemented for the remainder of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a text link vs a button link affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Do people donate through a text link or a button link in an email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 04/19/2016
Traditionally in SBA List appeal emails, a “donate” button is be placed at the bottom of the email. In one of the emails during a SBA List moneybomb they wanted to see the effect that a text link vs a button link has on donor conversion.  The email to the entire file had the same copy and featured both a text link and a button link. Are people clicking on the donate button more than the text link in the email and giving? They tested this to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though the test did not validate, in the initial findings more people gave through the text link than the button link. This could be attributed to a couple of things. First, the copy on the text link and button were different. The text link said, “Make a gift today” and the button said, “Donate”. The text link gave a more clear call-to-action. Second, the placement of the button was at the very bottom of the email after the senders signature. People may have already been motivated to give before they got to the button. Additional testing will be done to learn if continued use of button links is needed in appeal emails.

experiments.title: How the addition of value proposition copy affected donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition copy increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2016 - 05/19/2016
Susan B. Anthony list wanted to try and increase donations on their main donation page. In efforts to do this, they developed a treatment of the existing page. The existing page had minimal value proposition copy on it. For the treatment, they rewrote the existing copy, added value proposition language and a new headline and call-to-action that reinforced that value proposition. They split the traffic coming to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren’t able to validate the results for this test, the initial results tell us that the addition of the value proposition copy to the donation page actually caused a decrease in donations by 12.2%. The value proposition copy may have caused friction for those people who were already motivated enough to give once they came to the donation page.
The addition of the value proposition copy caused a decrease in donations for this test, but actually improved the second gift ask test we did on this same donation page. You can see that test here.

experiments.title: How two different value propositions on a donation page affected donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would motivate more donors to instantly convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 05/19/2016
In efforts to acquire names and email addresses, Susan B. Anthony List offered a free eBook called “Shutting Down Planned Parenthood”. After someone would request their free eBook they were taken to a confirmation page that had an immediate donation ask on it. SBA List wanted to test if they could improve instant donor conversion by testing two different value propositions. The value proposition for the control focused on getting the eBook in to the hands of more people and the treatment focused on advancing the pro-life movement and shutting down Planned Parenthood
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this test, the initial results told us that more people were motivated to donate to help put this resource into the hands of more people than they were to help advance the pro-life movement and shut down Planned Parenthood. The treatments value proposition may have seemed too big of a task for one person to do and accomplish through their donation. This could be why more people were motivated to convert with the control because it was more of a tangible thing that they could help do.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party endorsements impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd party endorsements improve the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2016 - 08/12/2016
On the main donation page for Susan B. Anthony List, we wanted to see if we could lift the donor conversion rate on the page. We decided to use some of the 3rd party endorsements that had been given to the organization by well known political figures.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we did not see any level of statistically significant difference between the two treatments. After further analysis, we realized that the treatment did not zero in on the 3rd party endorsements like we had intended. The headline modified the value proposition slightly, there were minor design tweaks, and the button copy was changed. Due to all of these factors, we are not able to say exactly what led to the insignificant result.

experiments.title: How the donation button design impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2016 - 08/12/2016
To optimize the donation funnel for the Susan B. Anthony List organization, we did an analysis to discover the biggest opportunities. What we found was that there were not enough organic visitors getting to the donation page to do any testing in that area. We decided to move our efforts forward in the funnel to try to drive more people to the page.
We had the hypothesis that if we made the donation page easier to find and put it into “first person” language, we could drive more visitors to the donation page. We decided to create an experiment to test the idea.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a nearly identical click through rate and donor conversion rate when compared to the control. The big take away from this experiment is that the button was not hard to find in the first place and that we did not add any significant value with the change in copy. Without those two factors influencing traffic, the treatment did nothing that would impact visitors making it to the donation page.

experiments.title: How the inclusion of graphical data in an email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a statistical image that reinforces the message increase the number clicks generated for the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 04/20/2015
As part of their monthly April appeal, Jews for Jesus wanted to validate the work being put into different aspects of the monthly fundraising campaigns.  One of these elements was a visual that was placed in the email to help drive interest in the topic and statistically reinforce the topic of the campaign. The goal of the experiment was to determine if the content needed the external reinforcement provided by the graph.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no statistically significant lift to the number of visitors to the page, it becomes evident that the image of the video and content of the email is enough to pique the interest of the email file.  The additional image of the graph did not have any influence on the visitors’ choice with regards to the call to action.

experiments.title: How the learnings from an invalid experiment affects both time and resources
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the length of the video and the amount of content shown in the video produce a significant lift in the overall donation conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 04/20/2015
As part of their monthly April appeal, Jews for Jesus wanted to validate the work being put into different aspects of the monthly fundraising campaigns. Many of these campaigns include a 60 second video with David Brickner, the president. Previously analysis has found that these videos are able to drive significantly higher amounts of traffic to the landing pages. What we wanted to determine is if we needed the full 60 seconds in the video or if a shorter version could be just as effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The 9.5% drop in conversions was not statistically valid which proves that the length of video does not have a significant impact on the success of the page. In future campaigns, we will be able to produce shorter videos (which take less time and money) and not adversely affect the results.

experiments.title: How quotes made along side the donation form affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd party quotes lower anxiety enough to improve upon the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 06/29/2015
During their a campaign to help make up for their financial shortfall, Jews for Jesus wanted to find a way to increase the conversion rate for the campaign landing page. A previous experiment had yielded a 252.6% lift by simplifying the donation form. It had reduced friction by removing key elements of the donation form which provided the lift.
For this new experiment, we placed quotes in support of Jews for Jesus from well known figures in the Christian community next to the donation form. By adding the quotes, we hoped to add 3rd party credibility which would, in turn, lower the potential donors’ anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, the 3rd party quotes did not have any statistically significant impact on the conversion rate of the page. It would seem that by the time donors made it down to the donation form, they had been significantly convinced of the credibility of Jews for Jesus that they did not need any additional information.

experiments.title: How high visitor motivation affects donation page experiments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will produce the greatest conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2015 - 07/20/2015
In an effort to lift the conversion rate and average gift of their primary donation page, Jews for Jesus launched a radical redesign of their existing design. The goal was to boost the value communicated on the page with additional copy, lower anxiety with quotes from 3rd party sources, and modify the donation form to reduce friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for nearly two months, there was no statistically significant difference between the two treatments when analyzing both conversion rate and revenue.
After examining the results in greater detail, we saw that both treatments had a conversion rate of ~65%; this was information not available before the launch of the experiment. A conversion rate that is this high is rare for an organization the size of Jews for Jesus. This would seem to indicate that only the most highly motivated donors are making it to the donation page.
Based upon these observations, the next logical step in optimization is to look earlier in the donation funnel to find ways to drive more visitors to the page.

experiments.title: How a quick donate button at the top of a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the “Quick Donate” button increase the number of donor conversions to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2015 - 07/01/2015
Through prior testing, we had learned that the Jews for Jesus visitors were highly motivated donors. As their shortfall campaign was coming to the end, we wanted to lower the barriers to these donors making a gift.  We decided to create an experiment that would place a “Make My Gift Now” button at the top of the page to jump the visitor down to the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite 28% of visitors clicking on the “Make My Gift Now” button having it didn’t significantly improve the overall conversion rate of the page.  However, it did seem to lower the average gift which may be attributed to roughly 1 out of 4 visitors skipping over the value proposition copy when they jumped down to the donation form.
By skipping over this copy, they did not read the reasons why their gift was needed which may have impacted how much they gave.  Again, this was not statistically proved and would require further experiments to validate the hypothesis.

experiments.title: How adding a login option back to the donation form affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will lead to the most revenue and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 10/26/2015
After two previous unsuccessful attempts to lift the revenue coming into Jews for Jesus primary donation page, we decided to take a step back and try to get a better understanding of the donors. The page converts at a high rate (~60% to ~70%) which means we are dealing with a highly motivated audience. It also means these visitors are likely existing donors which means they may not need as much convincing and may find value in elements that would cause friction with new donors.
After examining our previous treatment, we realized that by removing the login feature, we may have inadvertently made it harder for existing donors to give. On a donation page visited by primarily nondonors, this feature would have caused friction in the process but for existing donors, it would make it easier.  We decided to relaunch the previous value proposition test but add the Login feature back into the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our previous tests had decreased the conversion rate of the page. With this latest test, we were not able to statistically increase or decrease the results. What this means is that the login feature was impacting the donors’ likelihood to give.  It also means we have not yet discovered a value proposition that would prompt existing donors to give more generously.
In addition to our efforts to drive more traffic to this page, our next experiment will test a new value proposition in order to find a message that resonates better with the Jews for Jesus audience.

experiments.title: How a radical treatment to the primary donation page affects average gift size
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will produce the greatest lift to donor conversion rate or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2015 - 12/04/2015
On the Jews for Jesus main donation page, the default language is a simple headline with a call to action. We had the hypothesis that if we reduced the friction associated with the form, added language that expanded upon the primary value proposition, and inserted quotes from well known figures in the Christian movement, we could improve the already high average gift value.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment produced no significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue per visitor. It would seem that we have still not found a stronger way to communicate Jews for Jesus’s primary value proposition to their already highly motivated donors.

experiments.title: How adding a homepage overlay affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which homepage overlay treatment will produce the greatest lift to donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2015 - 01/04/2016
Jews for Jesus added a homepage overlay to their website during a year-end giving campaign. The overlay would pop-up once you came to the site to help drive traffic to the donation page. The hypothesis was that by removing the value proposition copy and adding a button, we could increase the donor conversion rate with this highly motivated audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment saw a slight increase in conversion rate, but the experiment didn’t validate so there was no real significance to that increase. However, it is worth pointing out that both treatments received a 7% conversion rate. This validates that the tactic (a homepage popup) is a good vehicle for driving donor motivated traffic to the right page. We just need to find if there are other options that would increase the perceived value of the gift.

experiments.title: How the addition of a button at the bottom of an email appeal affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a button improve click-through-rate in a monthly email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2016 - 03/12/2016
Jews for Jesus wanted to see if they could improve their click-through-rate on their monthly eAppeal by adding a button to the bottom of the email as the CTA. They traditionally used text links only in their eAppeals, but wanted to test that against the addition of a button.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the button increased the click-through-rate by 13%. This would suggest that the addition of a button in an email appeal brings clarity to the CTA. It visually drew a person’s attention and showed them where to click. Because this test didn’t validate, additional testing is recommended.

experiments.title: How length of copy and a soft ask affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email appeal would improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/14/2015
Each month Jews for Jesus sends an email appeal. They wanted to test the length of the copy and the call-to-action of their emails in an effort to increase the click-through-rate. Would a soft ask inviting people to “click here and find out more” motivate people to click-through to the landing page, or would a hard ask that was more direct improve click-through? They put it to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email that was shorter in length with the soft call-to-action had a 11.9% increase in conversion rate over the longer email with the hard ask. The short copy teased a story while the long copy told the entire story in the email with an ask for a gift.
People were more motivated to read more of the story than to give a gift. Though the longer copy had value proposition language supporting the ask, people weren’t ready to give a gift yet. This test didn’t validate and additional testing will be done.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition affects clickthrough

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a christ-follower

experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christian in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel.  With having two different value propositions and versions of emails, they tested them to see which would get a higher click-through.
They split-tested their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate these results, but what the initial data told us is that more people clicked through from the Israel outreach CTA than the control. This could be attributed to people being more motivated to give to a tangible need than a general value proposition and how the value proposition for the treatment was reinforced in the CTA.  Example: “Your gift will go immediately to direct, effective and unapologetic evangelism that is designed to present the Gospel in a way that Jewish people can relate to.”
In another test we did for this email, we found that the Israel outreach event increased donations by 47.3%. This audience is more motivated to give to a tangible event than a relevant value proposition for the organization. You can see the full details of that test here.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the action affects donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Does making a specific dollar amount ask in an email lead to an increase in the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/14/2015
As part of a follow up to a group of petition signers, the Media Research Center wanted to find what email call to action would lead to the greatest number of gifts.  The standard approach had always been to make a gift request with a link and compliment it with a Donate button.
We had a hypothesis that if we added specificity to the call to action and specifically told them what we would like them to give, we could get more constituents to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach statistical validity but the results would directionally indicate that adding the “Quick Donate” button motivated a greater proportion of the audience to give a gift. When we add specificity to an action, we are able to remove a portion of the decision making the donor has to do.
We will need to do further testing to validate this approach in the future.
Additionally, when examining the actual gifts made as a result of clicking the button, we found that even if donors clicked a certain amount in the email, approximately 50% ended up giving more on the actual donation form.  This indicates that the value proposition on the page was strong enough to motivate a larger gift.

experiments.title: How the copy on the page affects conversion of a highly motivated audience
experiments.researchQuestion: Will recognizing and thanking the visitors for their previous action increase the donations and revenue to the subsequent page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2015 - 09/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a second landing page and given the opportunity to make a gift. The copy on the page continued the messaging on the initial petition page and targeted the outrage the visitors would be feeling at the lack of media coverage for the Planned Parenthood videos.
After reviewing this copy, we realized that what the follow on page never did is acknowledge the visitor’s prior actions, specifically the signing of the petition. We decided to create a new treatment that would first thank the visitors for signing the petition and then lead in to what else they can do to help solve the problem (i.e. give a gift).
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running the experiment, the new treatment produced no statistically significant change in conversion or revenue. It is our hypothesis is that this lack of change goes back to a previous experiment where we learned the motivation of the visitors to this page was extremely high. The copy on the page was not the reason the majority of donors gave so any changes made would not have any significant impact.

experiments.title: How member benefits affected donor conversion during a moneybomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will member benefits increase the donor conversion or average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/24/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find a way to incentivize subscribers to become a new member at higher levels. Their standard campaigns have historically not focused on the membership aspect of their gift and instead tried to sell solely the philanthropic aspect of the donation.
We decided to test out language around the benefits associated with membership and include a premium offer of a book that reinforced the messaging of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant lift for either treatment. Since the campaign was for a limited period of time, we decided to end the test in order to try a different treatment that might achieve a greater lift. It would seem these benefits do not carry the added incentive in the donors’ minds when we have a moneybomb campaign such as this.

experiments.title: How a change in tone of the landing page affects the performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will yield the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find the best possible messaging tone to drive an increase in donations and revenue. A previous experiment using member benefits had not produced any significant lift so we decided to take a more radical approach at test the tone used on the page.
Our hypothesis was that landing page that more clearly laid out the problem and emphasized how the potential donor (instead of the organization) could help solve it would convert better.
