We also removed the 3rd party credibility indicators since, after further consideration, those may have confused the message.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither landing page was able to produce a significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue. This would suggest that the tone of the message was not influencing the campaign as much as the core message itself. For future campaigns, it would be best to take a more radical approach and test the entire messaging platform before getting to the final days of the campaign.

experiments.title: How the messaging approach affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will get the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
In the final days of the Media Research Center’s high urgency campaign, we wanted to see if a new approach to messaging could increase the conversion rate for the existing donor segment.  The existing copy took a stewardship approach of listing out the recent accomplishments of MRC and crediting the donor for making it happen.
The new treatment removed the accomplishments and instead highlighted recent examples of media bias.  It also added a premium offer for a gift of $100 or more and removed 3rd party credibility indicators that we hypothesized were actually working against us.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign of the landing page did not produce any statistically significant lift to donor conversion or revenue. Upon further reflection, the donor conversion rate of the page was near 25% for both treatments. By the time the test was launched, the visitor motivation was already significantly high enough that the messaging approaches didn’t have much impact.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form (Buttons vs. No Buttons)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding buttons with a predefined choice increase donations and donation/visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2015 - 11/12/2015
We were looking to increase donations on our main donation form. We believed that giving people a pre-defined choice helped decrease anxiety and thus lead to more donations & higher revenue/visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not achieve confidence on the increase in donations, although the numbers were clearly higher.  Donation/visitor was visibly higher during this test.

experiments.title: Prayer Points – Pic vs NO Pic
experiments.researchQuestion: Do pictures create friction or increase empathy?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2015 - 11/13/2015
We are looking to see if its more powerful to have images in the body of the email that will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: Whether we should include images in every prayer points email

experiments.title: Insider Insights – Positive (Us) vs Negative (InProv)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a positive spin, including a prayer, cause more people to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2015 - 12/19/2015
We were trying to see if creating a more positive spin of this week’s Insider’s newsletter would create more donations/ increase donation/visitor.
*Because this test was written internally, we did not have the ability to sign the email with Jonathan’s name. Therefore, the positive version was also written using “we” and “us” vs “I”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that the number of donors was down by a very small margin, but overall brought in more revenue and had a higher revenue/visitor.

experiments.title: Anita Dittman (Background vs. No Background)
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the background image cause friction which leads to less subscribers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2015 - 01/05/2016
We wanted to see what would happen if we had a background image on our landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: From this test we learned that people who did not see a background image were more likely to become a subscriber. We did not reach confidence, but were given enough confidence to test it again.

experiments.title: EOY Countdown Timer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a sense of urgency increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2015 - 01/07/2016
We wanted to test having a countdown timer on the landing page about two weeks before the end of the year to see if that would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We learned that the placement of the timer may or may not have had an impact on the number of donations. However, the average gift amount was $14 higher per visitor.
I recommend testing this element again next year, but placing it closer to the body of the copy rather than next to the amount they are choosing.

experiments.title: Confessing Hebrew Scriptures  (Background Img vs No Background Img)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a background increase friction so that less users complete the form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2016 - 01/19/2016
We wanted to see if adding a background distracted users from completing the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this test, we found that the background did not negatively affect the submissions. In fact, it increased submissions, but not to the level of confidence. We will need to add some more elements and/or try other background tests to determine if this really affects sign ups.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form Multivariate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new headline, copy, or new combination of copy elements product more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2016 - 01/27/2016
We wanted to test headline and copy elements in a multivariate to see how visitors responded.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that there was a slight variation between the original and variant #1 which combined a new headline with the old copy. The headline focused more on the value proposition, making it clear what we do that is valuable.

experiments.title: Shop vs Store on Main Navigation
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shop prompt more clicks than store?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2016 - 03/25/2016
We are looking at changing the navigation on our site to be more action oriented to give people multiple calls to action in the navigation. We decided to start this with the shop vs store test.
experiments.keyLearnings: Shop gave us a small lift, but after 5000 page views did not receive confidence. We will go back and add “now” to the shop button and see if that increases the lift to the point of confidence.

experiments.title: One Column vs Two Column Donation Form (WEB)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler form give us more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2016 - 04/11/2016
We are looking for ways to simplify our donation form to increase donations and revenue. This form was inspired by a form we found at another nonprofits website. Their form designed exactly the same was their highest performing form. We went with this design to see if it would outperform our current design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this form looked really nice and was modern in design, it failed to do better than our normal form. In fact, revenue for our regular form was double what this form pulled in.
A few things we see:
The picture could be distracting for people who just want to donate (causing friction)
A new form with this many changes could have made regular donors less apt to donate (anxiety)
The form was not simple enough. There might have been too much friction.
We did not add enough value. Although we added our value proposition and text that is known to convert, it did not add enough value.

experiments.title: TV Show (sidebar vs under)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will dropping the CTA button below the video negatively affect CTR?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2016 - 07/08/2016
We are redesigning the TV show page with Denver New Media and we wanted to see the affect of their design.
experiments.keyLearnings: It did affect it negatively, but only about 6% decrease in clicks. This was not confident, either.

experiments.title: Blue Links vs Red Links (Color of CTA)
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the color of a link play a part in the number of click throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 09/23/2016
Different colors elicit different emotional responses. We are looking to find which colors increase click throughs for our different email audiences/topics.

experiments.title: MEU Stylized vs Plain (CTR)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a plain, letter-style email get more clicks than a stylized email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2016 - 10/14/2016
Looking to see if MEU audience responds better to the regular email or to a plain, letter-style email that looks like it came from a real person.
experiments.keyLearnings: More testing needed.

experiments.title: E Appeal Stylized vs Plain (CTR)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will plain text get more clicks than stylized text.
experiments.keyLearnings: More testing needed.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the donation form radical redesign increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2016 - 05/23/2016
Jewish Voice Ministries was testing out a radical redesign of their standard donation page. They wanted to validate the impact of multiple factors on the conversion practice. For this experiment, they increased the amount of value proposition copy, moved to a two-step form, and switched to a two column layout to move the donation form closer to the top.
experiments.keyLearnings: After over a month of running, this experiment did not reach a high enough level of statistical validity. When analyzing the treatment, it is difficult to determine what led to this result given the variety of factors tested.

experiments.title: How membership benefits affect conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does talking about member benefits on the year end landing page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/30/2015
During the fiscal year end campaign, the Texas State Historical Association had a landing page targeting non-members. It focused on the legacy of the organization and the work being done day-in and day-out. There was a premium for a gift of $50 or more but there were no other mentions of associated member benefits. These benefits had proven to be successful in other experiments so we decided to test them out in the year end campaign as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: The member benefits produced no statistically significant change in donor conversion. This would suggest that either they are not necessary during an urgency-based appeal like the fiscal year end or that the benefits did not add significant value for the potential donors. We will need to isolate these factors on a general donation form to determine which of these hypothesis are true.

experiments.title: How using an image of the free resource offer in an email can decrease clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the image of the book help increase clicks to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2017 - 05/23/2017
Live Action created a new content offer to acquire new names and emails. They used this same content offer as a cultivation offer to their housefile. In an effort to increase click-through from the housefile email for the offer to the landing page, they put an image of the eBook in the email. They tested this against the same email without the image of the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate, but the initial data showed that the treatment with the image of the book slightly decreased clicks in the email. In a previous test we learned that their housefile responds best when the email from the sender is highly personal, casual and conversational. What the initial data in this new test suggests is that by having the image of the book in the email, it may not have felt as genuine or authentic that the sender personally sent them this email and eBook. This could have caused friction and resulted in less people clicking through to the landing page.

experiments.title: How positive or negative tone in donation page copy impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a positive or negative tone on a donation page motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2017 - 06/21/2017
Live Action was running a high-urgency campaign centered around the topic of cultivating a culture of life. They wanted to test whether a positive tone that focused on donor impact (“You can help create a culture of life”) or a negative tone that focused on media silencing of the pro-life voice (“Our voice is being silenced”) would motivate more people to give.
The elements tested were headline and body copy. All other elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though this experiment didn’t validate, there was still a 12.7% decrease in conversion rate on the negatively-toned variant. This is due to a couple of things:
People like to know that their gift will make a positive and direct impact. Positive and uplifting language empowers the reader and makes them feel like their gift is making a difference.
The negative-tone test was focused on things happening to Live Action (being silenced by the media), which was not as directly affective to the donor as the positive tone test- changing their culture. People aren’t as likely to feel they can really change that situation.

experiments.title: How the navigation’s donate button copy impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Which button’s call to action will drive the most donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/02/2017
On the Live Action News website, we wanted to see if we could apply the learnings we had seen with the LiveAction.org navigation test. In this test, we had found that a button that communicated more value than a simple “Donate” but was still was clear on the purpose, was able to increase donations.
For this test, we decided to test out two options: “Support the Preborn” and “Support Live Action.”
experiments.keyLearnings: None of the treatments had significant impact on the donor conversion rate. It is our hypothesis that this is likely due to the motivation of the audience. With Live Action News, the audience is motivated to read news and see uplifting stories. As a result, the language used around donating did not see much of an impact. In future tests, we will need to lay out the case for support more thoroughly, most likely in the form of the Dear Reader treatment.

experiments.title: How identifying the “next steps” impacts donor conversion after email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will explaining the “next steps” in the process increase the perceived impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2017 - 04/25/2017
For Live Action, there had been a significant emphasis on acquiring email addresses using their Pro-Life petition. However, they new subscribers had not been converting to donors at a significant level so we decided to launch a test.
Our hypothesis is that if we explain the process to the potential donors, they can understand how the next step they take (a donation) will play into the bigger picture of defunding Planned Parenthood. This would increase the perceived impact of their gift and, subsequently, the donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we were not able to see a significant impact to donation conversion for the treatment. Our hypothesis is this was likely caused by the low motivation of the audience. Petition signers are not often a good source of donors given the low barrier for entry (signing a petition). Since there is minimal value exchange, the donation ask is not as likely to happen.

experiments.title: How the donation premium can affect both conversion rate and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Which premium will increase the overall revenue from existing members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 09/30/2015
As part of Texas State Historical Association’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to find a way to improve the average gift of existing members in order to boost revenue. The majority of existing members were acquired using the premium of the 2014-15 Texas Almanac. The update to this book was going to be released in the coming months so we had a feeling it would be a popular premium offer to use with the donor base.
However, this book is normally offered for a gift of $50 which meant it wouldn’t do much to raise the average gift. We decided to test another popular book as a premium and increase the minimum gift to $100 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Along Forgotten River premium offer reduced the donor conversion rate of the page by 50%. However, it was also able to increase the average gift by 64%. These two factors cancelled out the combined impact on revenue and produced no statistically significant change in either direction.  This does prove to us that if we can find the right premium offer, we should be able to increase the average gift enough to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How mentioning a specific giving amount in the call-to-action of an email impacts click-rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email would increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2017 - 06/05/2017
Live Action sends monthly email appeals to their email file. In an effort to get more people clicking through to their donation page, they wanted to test if putting a specific donation amount in the call-to-action would help increase conversion. The control didn’t mention a specific giving amount and the treatment mention a specific giving amount of $50. All of the other copy in the email remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the treatment email decreased click rate by 10%. We will need to do more testing around this idea to know exactly the impact a specific giving amount has on click-through, but we could hypothesize from this initial data that mentioning a specific giving amount to people presents a point of friction in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How shorter copy in a “Dear Reader” experiment affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does shorter, value focused copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2017 - 06/20/2017
The Live Action team was running a “Dear Reader” experiment on Live Action news, and wanted to test if a shortened version of the copy would convert more donors. The new copy had more personal, value-focused copy on the benefits to the donor, rather than an overview of what Live Action News does.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were not valid, and the experiment was ended in favor of a new experiment before we could reach conclusive results on the effectiveness of the copy. However, the experiment trended in favor of the control, which could have increased the perception of the impact that Live Action News was having on the worldwide perception of abortion versus the shorter version that had fewer statistics. The statistics could have impacted the motivation of the giver more than the personal, donor-focused approach in this case.
Another factor that could have impacted giving was the stepped approach that the control took in leading potential donors through why they should give, rather than text blocks in the treatment.

experiments.title: How an extended value proposition on a main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an extended value proposition increase donor conversion on a main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 12/21/2017
Live Action wanted to re-test an extended value proposition on their main donation page. Earlier tests had revealed that extended value proposition decreased conversion, but due to technical restrictions and errors on the donation platform, these results proved to be faulty and not reliable. Because of this, they ran a new test on the main donation page, testing a new headline and body copy that had a longer explanation of the value proposition of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, as there was not enough traffic during the two months the test ran to get statistically sound data on a winning treatment. We recommend testing this approach in the future during a high-urgency campaign that will guarantee more traffic to the page in order to secure a valid learning on this experiment.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which countdown clock would increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2015 - 01/28/2016
As part of their year-end campaign, Online for Life wanted to find a way to boost the conversion rate on their donation page.
In a previous test, we had learned that the addition of the “Every 26 seconds” clock on the donation page had increased donor conversion rate. Now, we wanted to it against a treatment with a timer that counted down to the end of the year.
Our hypothesis was that people would be more motivated to give based on the urgency of the year-end. We ran a split A/B test. The control donation page had the”Every 26 seconds” clock and the treatment was the donation page with the year-end countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn’t reach a large enough sample size to validate the test. We were wrong in our hypothesis, but did learn that the “Every 26 seconds” clock had a greater sense of urgency and supported and reinforced the value proposition creating a stronger message. The strong value proposition not only convinced people to give a gift, but to give a higher gift amount as well. That is reflective in the increase of average gift.
Taking these learnings, the “Every 26 seconds” clock will be tested again on general donation pages.

experiments.title: How the stories told on a landing page affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Given the altruistic nature of volunteers, focusing on the impact they will have on others will appeal more to visitors than the impact the volunteering will have on them.  As a result, this new treatment will increase the number of names acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2012 - 10/03/2012
The Global Volunteer Network was looking for a way to acquire more applications for their volunteer program.  The existing landing page focused on the experience the volunteers would go through and how it would transform their lives. We wanted to discover what motivated volunteers to sign up so we created a new treatment that talked about the impact they would have on others instead of what volunteering would do for them.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach the required sample size or a statistical level of confidence in the time that this test ran.  While it would appear that talking about the impact they would have on others increased conversions, the lift was not significant enough to be valid.  We would recommend further testing of the idea before rolling it out across the site.

experiments.title: How running Facebook ads to Membership Card campaign targeting impacted donor conversion rates from non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a third channel in Facebook advertising (alongside email and direct mail channels) improve response rates or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 03/31/2019
We wanted to continue experimentation with running Facebook ads alongside focused campaign messaging used this time to promote their Membership Card campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 63.2% level of confidence, we were unable to validate this experiment.
Directionally, we did note a -7.8% decrease in non-member donor conversion rate.
Overall revenue decreased by -11.9% within this treatment, as well.
Our hypothesis is that the non-member segment splits may have more to do with the likelihood that a donor will be reactivated with the third channel of outreach (online advertising).
Note: Offline gifts were not included as a part of this assessment. This included online transactions only, and was run through the first half of the final day of the campaign. Additional updates will be forthcoming.

experiments.title: How multivariate copy changes on a primary donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing one set of copy variations against the control for primary donation page visitors will achieve a valid learning.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/16/2024
A public policy organization previously launched a multivariate test on their primary donation page in which 36 copy variations were tested in order to find the optimal combination of those changes. These variations included adding a “Your voice in Washington” subheadline, editing copy within a bulleted list describing what the organization does using the donor’s gift, adding “We fight for YOU” copy above the donation form, and more.
After running the test for 11 days, the organization ended the test and implemented changes on the donation page based on the combination of copy variations within the variant that led to the most revenue or donations during that time.
In an effort to solidify the learning on whether or not these copy edits led to a valid increase in revenue or donations, we created a new test with one control, the new donation page based on copy edits as a result of the multivariate test, and one variant, the original donation page copy.
We believe that by testing only one set of copy edits against the original copy, we will be able to reach a higher sample size for the variant and the test will be more likely to lead to a valid learning.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reverting to the original donation page copy led to a 21.3% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 79.3%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.
This means that the results of this experiment should not be considered valid or used for future decision-making. It is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or longer duration to obtain more reliable and conclusive results.
Future experimentation should focus on testing one variation of copy edits to identify individual changes' effect on donation page revenue or transactions.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s lapsed member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for reactivating lapsed members?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind lapsed members to renew their annual membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate for this donor segment, but directionally, we see alignment with the non-member and member segments that both validated towards increasing segment response rates when serving ads.
It’s worth noting that similarly to the non-member experiment we ran, the total cost to run these ads to lapsed members generated a 4.2 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the “with ads” segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an amount equal to slightly more than 3x the spend in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Further experimentation is required with future campaigns for the lapsed segment, but directionally these results align with active and non-member segments seeing an increase in response rates with ROI free and clear of the incremental expenses associated with adding the 3rd messaging channel of Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How the button text hurt email acquisition rate on a new offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Can something other than “get” in the button text improve email acquisition rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2019 - 04/12/2019
When launching a new ebook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test their way into improving email acquisition rates. For a long time, we’ve always used the term “get” in the titles and buttons within offer pages like this, so we decided to run an additional experiment to validate whether or not this old positioning statement still worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 64.6% level of confidence, we saw a -18.6% decrease in email acquisition rate.
Although the experiment didn’t yet validate, we decided to turn off the experiment due to the directional validation that the word “get” does indeed still perform best.
Continued experimentation with the offer should be made to improve email registration rates, but it would appear that the term “get” in the button is not where the testing should be.

experiments.title: How year-end specific language on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would year-end specific copy on the primary donation page increase donor conversion during year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 01/06/2019
During Back to the Bible’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the primary donation page if we used year-end specific language that communicated the overall value proposition of the organization instead of focusing on just one aspect of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment during the campaign. We hypothesize that this is because the motivation of donors during year-end is higher than other times of the year. We see that people are more motivated during the month of December than any other time of year to give and it is more difficult to positively or negatively impact that motivation.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates from The Daily Signal visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We had two questions:
How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates?
Which message would convert the most donors (stand with either (a) the president, or (b) Heritage)?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
On The Daily Signal’s year-end fundraising page in 2018, we installed an exit popup with two messages, where one was focused on “Stand with the President” while the other was “Stand with Heritage”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we gleaned some insight from the experiment.
Exit Popup Tactic Observations:
It’s also worth noting that the landing page secured a total of 27 transactions without firing the “exit popup”, and that the “exit popup” regardless of messaging variant secured an additional 42 gifts combined between the two message types. That means that the exit popup seemingly secured an additional 55% more gifts from donation page viewers who were attempting to leave the page. Subsequently, the revenue from the “exit popup audience” drove an incremental 58% in additional revenue to the audience that did not see the popup.
This supports the idea that the exit popup does indeed work to secure additional transactions and revenue.
Messaging Observations:
Although the “Stand with Trump” message actually had a 14% higher click rate on the affirmative button (40 / 350 = 11.4% CTR) compared to the “Stand with Heritage” message (which was 38 / 391 = 9.7% CTR).
However, we actually saw that the “Stand with Heritage” message drove an increase of 8.4% for donor conversions (5.9% vs. 5.4%).
Finally, The “Stand with Heritage” also had a 12.6% higher average gift value ($45.81 vs. $40.67) compared to the “Stand with Trump” message.
Final Conclusion:
With only 21% of the required sample size collected during this experiment, we propose continuing to test which messages work best to drive donor conversions, but that it would certainly appear that installing an exit popup on donation pages can have a profound impact as a tactic to convert donors and drive incremental revenue that otherwise would leave the donation page without interruption.

experiments.title: How a vanity URL impacted online giving from a direct mail appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will pushing donors to online giving increase revenue overall?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We tested adding a box to the bottom of the copy and RD that said: “Give ONLINE at: AllianceDefendingFreedom.org/donate”.
We also added the same message to the CRE.
OE

experiments.keyLearnings: We used the Vanity URL /Donate. I know this is a standard Vanity URL which makes me question if we properly tracked the donations from this campaign. We saw an insignificant decrease in response rate, which makes me question if these donors went online instead. Average gift and net income were also down.

experiments.title: How a higher gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with higher values affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2019 - 03/14/2019
CaringBridge had used a giving infrastructure that had separate mobile and desktop donation pages. As a result, certain optimizations had not been rolled out to both experiences, resulting in slight differences between the two pages. In previous tests, CaringBridge had “unified” the copy on the page by testing out of all negative impact.
However, the mobile version still had another gift array that started at $25, unlike the others that started at $50. As their final “unification” test, CaringBridge wanted to test the $50 array that was found everywhere else to make sure there was no impact on revenue.
The control had an array of $25, $50, $100, $250. They launched a treatment with the array found on the other pages: $50, $100, $250, Other.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than 700 transactions, the new gift array had no significant difference in conversions, which gave CaringBridge the all-clear to launch the unified experience. This process, though it took time, is the proof-positive way to launch new experiences. CaringBridge could have easily just launched the same copy and donation array everywhere, but there could have been serious ramifications in revenue. This series of unification tests shows that there was no risk at all.

experiments.title: How the emphasis on the power of prayer impacted sign up for a devotional
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shifting the benefits of the offer from their child to themselves increase the visitor’s likelihood to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2019 - 03/21/2019
Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had been running for nearly two years as a tool for email acquisition. The landing page focused on the need and benefits of this offer on the reader’s children. Based upon results we had observed at the ad-level, we had a hypothesis that we could see increased results by modifying the landing page to focus on the benefits to the reader. We also wanted to emphasize the power of prayer to reinforce the kind of good it can do in the individual’s life.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month and a half, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. It would appear, that shifting the focus entirely to the reader may have too far removed the primary purpose of the offer. As a result, we ended up not seeing any change in conversion. A next natural step would be a blending of these two treatments. By positioning the offer so that it applies to both the visitor and their child, we can potentially get the best of both world.

experiments.title: How a mid-dollar premium impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a $50 premium increase overall revenue to the page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
The instant donation page for Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had seen a significant increase in conversion with the implementation of Purchase targeting on Facebook and the switch to a Shoes for Orphan Souls as the donation ask. However, with the change to Shoes, we saw our average gift hovering around $25 (the cost to provide a pair of shoes.) In an effort to increase this average gift, we decided to run a test of offering a premium for a gift of $50 or more. Our belief is that by positioning this $50 ask as the first number they see, we would be able to increase overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we saw that the two pages had nearly identical conversion rates and the premium offer had a slightly higher average gift. However, this average gift was something that began trailing off over time for the treatment. Our hypothesis is that the premium (a calendar) became less relevant the further we got into the year. With the offer being less relevant, people started giving $10 gifts instead of the $50 which brought the average down to a similar level as the control.
A big takeaway from this experiment is that we were able to lift average gift when the premium is both relevant and timely without negatively impacting overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: The impact of Scripture in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using scripture in an appeal increase donor response rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
Buckner International had seen success with recent communications by leaning heavily into scripture. As a Christian organization, they would incorporate scripture verses into their cultivation emails and even had an acquisition offer that focused on scripture. With this in mind, we had the hypothesis that using scriptures in our appeals may increase the reader’s sense of duty which would then increase the likelihood to respond.
However, we wanted to be sure it wasn’t isolated to a single email. To do this, we split the house file equally among donors and nondonors and maintained this split through the whole campaign. We then three distinct emails, each with a scripture and non-scripture, version to measure the impact on the gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending three different emails with both scripture and non-scripture versions, we did not see any statistically significant increase in donor conversion. In fact, the only email that showed any increase in giving was the initial email sent to the file (shown above). It is also worth pointing out that the control emails did not omit mentions of God, they simply did not directly incorporate scripture.
This is a fascinating learning as it tells us that we do not need to directly reference scripture in order to invoke the donors’ sense of duty.

experiments.title: How adding a qualifying question affects conversion rate on an opt-in form

rows.name: Do you work for a nonprofit?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a qualifying question on a general opt-in form affect the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 03/14/2019
In a previous experiment on a webinar sign-up page, we learned that asking registrants if they worked for a nonprofit or not led to a significant increase in webinar registrations. We wondered if we could duplicate the same affect on a general opt-in form.
experiments.keyLearnings: The additional qualifying question made no significant impact on conversion rate. At first, this was a bit of let down seeing that it saw a 32% increase on a webinar page. But in reality, even seeing no change in conversion is a positive because it allows us to be more targeted with our segmentation and messaging without negatively impacting opt-ins.

experiments.title: How adding gift handles to a landing page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a suggested giving amount increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2019 - 03/02/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. They wanted to know if donations would increase on the landing page with the addition of a brief explanation of how many pocket Constitutions could be sent to children with a certain donation amount. They thought that if they suggested a giving amount next to how many pocket Constitutions could be sent with that amount, it would help the donor make a decision that they would otherwise have to make on their own. They hypothesized that telling people the impact a gift amount would make would inspire people to give generously, thus increasing donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Hillsdale team saw a 7.2% drop in conversion with the gift handle treatment. This was after sending all traffic from the second email of the campaign to the treatment landing page, which suggests that there would be an even larger decrease in conversion had the traffic been split evenly in that second email. After the test had run for three days, the Hillsdale team decided to end the test in favor of launching a new test against the control: the addition of a photo in the landing page. This new experiment can be found here.

experiments.title: How shorter value proposition on a donation page during year-end impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a shorter value proposition increase donor conversion for email traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/04/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the donation page for the people coming to it from email. To do this, we developed a shorter value proposition that didn’t remove the strength of the message, but just made the copy/content shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate this experiment, we did start to see a lift in conversion. Our hypothesis is that this traffic has already read and understands (through their email) the value proposition of the ask – they don’t need as much convincing and motivation to convert. The longer copy of the control may have even acted as friction in the giving process for these people.

experiments.title: How utilizing a longer funnel during early year end fundraising impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the video series offer result in more donations than a direct donation ask during year end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2018 - 12/21/2018
During the early part of December, Focus on the Family had found in previous years that the direct ask banner ads did not perform well with mobile visitors. During 2018’s year end campaign, they decided to test a tactic of presenting an offer for their new online video series. It is an email acquisition offer that presented new subscribers with a donation opportunity once they had finished the signup process. They launched this offer against their standard direct donation ask that runs throughout all of December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not reach a level of statistical significance with this experiment. We saw a difference of 12 gifts between the control and treatment. However, it is worth noting that the video series resulted in nearly 400 emails acquired for the organization. These emails have the potential to convert downstream and provide additional value.
In future campaigns, it may be worth testing an offer that is a middle-ground between an email acquisition and direct donation ask. For Focus, that would be a “gift of any amount” offer that offers a book or DVD when they make a donation.

experiments.title: How using a button instead of a raw link impacts conversion in a cultivation email
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a button instead of a raw link increase clicks in a cultivation email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2019
The Missionary Oblates had tested into using raw links for the hyperlinks in their email communication. This finding was over a year ago and they have continued to use raw links in all of their emails since then. As they were sending a cultivation email to their email file they hypothesized that maybe their file had grown used to the raw link and it wouldn’t have the same impact as it had when they had originally tested it. They developed a treatment that used a button instead of a raw link and split their file in half to see the impact on clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a directional lift of 5% from the treatment that had the button instead of the raw link. Because of the volume of emails that the Missionary Oblates send, we’d recommend testing this approach again in an effort to validate the approach and track conversions all the way through. We ultimately want to see the impact this kind of experiment has not only on clicks, but donations as well since the cultivation email offered people a free content offer that had a donation ask on the end of it.

experiments.title: How a “forwarded” email impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a forward email impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2019
Focus on the Family ran a year end campaign in 2018. They hypothesized that sending an email that looked like a “FWD” would increase engagement and conversion. They decided to test this against an email with a subject line that didn’t have “FWD” in it.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment is not valid because the level of confidence is only 51.2%. Having a forwarded email has no impact on conversion. The reason for this is because motivation is so high of the year. In the context of a year end campaign, a forwarded email has no impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How an email acquisition offer performs on a ‘Case’ page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the number of CTAs to a single action and utilizing the Statement of Belief as the offer increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 07/17/2018
In a recent data analysis, we discovered that ADF’s Case Pages see a significant spike in traffic when a case makes national news. Knowing that the results of the Jack Phillips case and NIFLA would be announced in the coming weeks, we decided to see if we could capitalize on that increase in traffic.
To do that, we created a treatment that added the already highly performing Statement of Belief to the Jack Phillips and NIFLA case pages. It replaced the existing call to actions which were buttons at the bottom for both a newsletter and a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant increase in the number of emails acquired when using the Statement of Belief. Our hypothesis is that the Statement of Belief was not the right offer to best match the motivations of those coming from organic search. Since, for many of them, they were just learning about Jack Phillips and NIFLA for the first time, they were likely not yet ready to sign on to a statement of belief.

experiments.title: How using exclusivity on a recurring gift page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donor conversion increase by appealing to potential recurring donors through exclusivity?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 02/26/2019
There has been significant testing done on Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page. The majority of this testing has been around strengthening the value proposition. We hypothesized that increasing the exclusivity on the page might increase donor conversion. To do this, we created a treatment that was more conversational and tried to appeal to recurring donors as a select, exclusive group of people, “the few”, that have been chosen or stepped up to give on an ongoing basis.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment ran for over a month and we weren’t able to validate the results. The treatment had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. When we analyze the experiment, we see that the donor conversion is really low on the page. What this tells us is that we would need a more radical version of the page to impact conversion, or look at testing higher in the funnel to get MORE people to the recurring donor page in an effort to increase recurring donors from this page.

experiments.title: How an exit popup did not impact donor conversion rates during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an exit popup to the Kickoff campaign landing page improve donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2019 - 02/25/2019
During the 2019 Kickoff Campaign for The Heritage Foundation, we tried experimenting with a more emotional appeal for the visitor to activate/renew their membership, but did not see any noticeable lift in the donor conversion rate. So, we wanted to test something more tactical by adding an exit popup to intercept the visitor before they left to ask them to reconsider.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 77% level of confidence, we only saw a negligible difference between the two treatments (-3%) in instant donor conversion rate.
Although the popup was displayed 1,847 times on the treatment, and 756 (or 41% of those shown the popup) of those people clicked on the button that read: “Yes, I will stand with Heritage!” — it somehow showed a decrease in instant donor conversion rates.
In digging into the data specifically, we noticed the following, as well:
Revenue/Visitor: +1.3% (68.9% LoC)
Avg. Gift: +3.79%
Even with those observations, it appears that the exit popup overall had a very negligible impact during the course of the campaign. The visitor motivation to give a gift on these pages is largely programmed via the emails (or ads) designed to drive them there. That said, experimentation with increasing motivation is likely not going to work, and tactical elements (like an exit popup) may not be effective to drive a noticeable split between the treatment and control during high conversion campaigns like this.
There are other tactical elements to experiment with during campaigns like this, but perhaps the most effective experimentation is likely going to be running tests with the motivation (which is driven from the emails / ads used to drive page traffic).

experiments.title: How budget strategy in Facebook affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does Facebook’s Budget Optimization tool create more opportunities to get instant donors than setting the budget at the ad set level?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2019 - 02/11/2019
Recent experiments had resulted in a significant improvement in Buckner’s active acquisition campaign known as “7 Scriptures.” The greatest lift was the result of technical optimization of the Facebook ads. With this in mind, we wanted to take this one step further and continue our testing of the various advertising techniques available to us through Facebook.
One of the ways ads are shown is based upon the method of bidding. Historically, we had set daily spending limits for each audience which equally spread the spend across each one. We had the hypothesis that if we allowed Facebook’s algorithm to determine the allocation of spend, we may see better results. To test this, we set the budget at the campaign level, using Facebook’s Budget Optimization tool instead of the ad set level (setting a daily limit per ad set). All ads and targeting remained the same.
At the time of the test, each campaign was spending the same amount per day ($83 and change).
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically valid, but the campaign that had daily limits set for each ad set did slightly increase instant donor conversion in the time span of the test. After further discussion, we may believe that setting daily limits for the Budget Optimization was not the best route since it still limited Facebook’s algorithm to daily amounts. Instead, we are going to next test setting a campaign spending limit that allows for the media to be spent whenever the algorithm determines it is best. This would potentially allow for increased spending on days that show increased conversion opportunity.

experiments.title: How the sender did not impact donor conversion rates for prospecting emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the sender of the Kickoff Campaign messaging impact donor conversion rates at all?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2019 - 01/31/2019
With the senders in the 2018 Kickoff Campaign messaging no longer with the organization, we wanted to identify who should be the primary sender for consideration on the 2019 Kickoff Campaign messages through the email prospecting channel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With regards to the Kickoff Campaign in 2019, we found that through the email prospecting channel, that the sender from Heritage played no role in donor conversion rates. As shown, we see a -1.3% difference between the two senders, but the level of confidence is only 7.52% — nowhere near the 95% validation that we’re looking for.
This was the case with the other performance metrics we would subsequently be interested in, as well, which were effected as follows:
Email Clickthrough Rate: +2.9% for Maggy (over Andrew), with a 41.9% LoC.
Email Open Rate: +0.5% for Maggy (over Andrew), with a 48.9% LoC.
Any additional experimentation was turned off, and Andrew was used for the remainder of the campaign sends within the timeline.
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the Heritage Foundation’s file is so accustomed to these cyclical campaigns, and that messaging for the campaign is very transactional in nature, which focuses upon the primary motivation of: “Renewing” or “Activating” your membership for 2019, that the sender, at least in the email prospecting channel, is not important.

experiments.title: How the framing of the transition on the instant donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does framing the transition into the donation ask differently in the headline + body copy affect donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2018 - 08/03/2018
For Dallas Theological Seminary, we wanted to see if we can reframe the donation page that follows the Genesis online course into a broader value proposition. This copy approach leads in more quickly into the donation ask and value proposition than the control, which focuses more on the next steps for the actual course, rather than the big picture value of these online courses to believers everywhere.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results were not statistically valid, but we did see a trend towards a 25% lift in donations with the treatment. Further testing necessary to validate this learning.

experiments.title: How a more emotional appeal impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more emotionally charged message about partnering with the president convert donors at a higher rate than an organizationally-focused message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2019 - 01/31/2019
As a part of the annual Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff campaign, we wanted to experiment with a more emotional message to stand as a partner with the president that has worked well in converting new donors from The Daily Signal against the more organizationally-focused, core control message that has previously been used.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As with previous experiments with the landing page language within The Heritage Foundation’s annual “Kickoff Campaign”, we were unable to substantiate a lift over the control language.
The key here is that the motivation is so high for members to go online and renew their membership that emotionally charged language cannot further lift their intent to renew, which is being programmed within the email language used to drive them online to give.
We ended this experiment early so that we could move on to experiment with tactics on the donation page and capitalize upon this unique high-traffic, high-donation period of time by identifying and/or capitalizing upon new opportunities.

experiments.title: How signifying a recommended gift amount affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does signifying a recommended gift amount and putting a value to that amount affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2019 - 01/31/2019
Buckner International was running their 7 Scriptures campaign and wanted to test if signifying the “most popular” donation amount, along with what that gift amount provides for a child, would affect donor conversion. They kept all copy  the same, and put a box and gift handle on the $50 amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not validate or produce any specific learnings. We’ve seen suggested gift amounts and “most popular” gifts work in the past but, in this situation, it would appear new donors were not as impacted by these suggestions. The next iteration of this experiment is to potentially increase average gift amounts by testing a premium with a minimum gift amount.

experiments.title: How noting the specific content offer type in the subject line affects conversion

rows.name: A full year’s worth of new fundraising ideas to test
A full year’s worth of new fundraising ideas to test [WEBINAR]

experiments.researchQuestion: Will noting that the email is about a webinar in the subject line increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/25/2019
In this experiment, we wondered if clarifying the type of content that the email was about in the subject line would lead to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This change had no affect on conversion. It’s possible that it may have had a more significant effect if placed at the beginning of the subject line, rather than the end. The end of the subject line could tend to get cut off on many devices and email platforms.

experiments.title: How using altruistic language for the benefits of a recurring gift impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will altruistic language on the recurring gift page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2018 - 01/23/2019
We had previously tested using altruistic language on the recurring gift pop-up but we weren’t able to impact donor conversion positively or negatively with it. Understanding this we hypothesized that the altruistic language might have a greater impact on the actual recurring page where the motivation isn’t quite as high and we might be able to impact donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for eight weeks and we weren’t able to validate it. What this tells us is that the altruistic language really had no impact on conversion. We hypothesize that the majority of people aren’t getting to the part of the page that communicates the altruistic benefits. Because of this, we propose a more radical experiment for the page that tests the overall value proposition.

experiments.title: How a simplified landing page and added imagery affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a radically redesigned landing page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2018 - 12/03/2018
Heartlight Ministries is running a free online course called Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, and they wanted to test a new copy approach as well as adding a photo of Mark to reinforce expertise and better connect with the reader. This test was a radical redesign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page did not get any valid results based on the low sample size, but the treatment showed a 26% increase in conversion rate and was therefore rolled out 100% to the page.

experiments.title: How a condensed version of a direct mail letter performs against a long version

rows.name: Fight "Fake News" and Stop Campus Indoctrination (short)
Fight "Fake News" and Stop Campus Indoctrination (long)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will long or short copy result in a greater amount of clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/01/2018
The Leadership Institute sends out a 7-12 page direct mail letter each month to its housefile list. They wanted to see if sending that same letter, digitally, would have an impact on clicks. They wrote a condensed version of the long letter to test against the original. Their hypothesis was that the condensed version would generate more clicks than the long version. They did not remove Word formatting for the long version.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was only a .04% difference in click rate between the two versions. There was insufficient data to determine if longer or shorter copy was more effective.

experiments.title: How requiring all survey questions impacted the survey completion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the survey response rate improve when all questions are not required?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2018
As a part of the 2018 calendar year-end fundraising efforts, we launched the National Concerned Citizen Survey, which was a 13-question survey designed to gather feedback from our donors, email subscribers and prospective donors regarding “how to align our priorities in 2019 and beyond.” We wanted to know if requiring all questions to be answered would negatively impact the survey completion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not validate and we decided to experiment with something that would likely more appropriately positively impact our ability to impact the survey completion (subsequently, the email registration) rate.
Further experimentation could be used to validate definitively, but it appears that requiring all fields on a survey of this length has a negligible of any impact on survey completion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a security lock to a donation page affects donor conversion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the appearance of increased security on the donation form increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/26/2018
Within our research library, we have validated several experiments with nominal increases in donor conversion rates when increasing the appearance of security at the point of the donation form where the donor will need to enter credit card information. Considering year-end efforts would drive a higher likelihood of traffic through the donation page, we decided to experiment with this treatment type for HAFA.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 15.3% confidence score, we did not fully validate the experiment. Further experimentation is required to fully validate whether the appearance of increased security on the donation form improves donor conversion rates.
We decided to pause the experiment to pivot towards different experimentation opportunities during this high-traffic time of the year. We did see a slight increase (+2.5% in donor conversion rates), and just assumed the treatment would improve conversion rates (like in the other experiments for other organizations), and rolled this treatment out as the new control without validating/substantiating what kind of lift it would produce.

experiments.title: How adding location to a seminar sign up affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the word “Constitution” and the location of the seminar in the sign up box increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2018 - 08/22/2018
Hillsdale College was promoting a Lifelong Learning Seminar. They wanted to increase sign ups, so they created an experiment to test adding additional information about the seminar — the descriptor “Constitution”, and the location of the seminar. They wondered if adding more information would make people more likely to sign up.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was insufficient traffic to the pages to validate this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding context to a video endorsement impacts sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding copy under a video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2018 - 06/27/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to know if adding copy to an endorsement video on the “Introduction to the Constitution” sign up page would increase the number of sign ups. They decided to test one sign up page with copy versus another sign up page with no copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment. However, with a conversion rate of 30.2% with the copy version and a 33.1% conversation rate with the version without copy, it’s likely that with more traffic, this experiment could validate in favor of no copy with video endorsements in the future. The test would need to run longer in order to prove this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How different endorsement methods impact sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a text endorsement and/or video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/08/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to know if adding an endorsement on the “Introduction to the Constitution” sign up page would increase the number of sign ups. They decided to test different endorsement styles, quotes vs videos vs a combination of both.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, there was not enough difference in conversions to suggest that the endorsement treatments made any impact. However, there was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment. With more data, this experiment could likely validate in the future.

experiments.title: How extra formatting in the email affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will extra formatting in the email affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 04/25/2018
This experiment was tested in the email copy. Hillsdale College wanted to see if adding bold text and two extra links in the copy would increase click rates. We hyperlinked two phrases in the original copy and added some boldness to the original text to see what impact would be made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a .47% click rate in comparison to a .44% click rate. We can conclude that there is no difference in the bolded version and the original version with no bold lettering. After a side by side comparison, the additional formatting was not remarkably different. As a result, it provided minimal impact on the readers likelihood to take action.

experiments.title: How longer form calendar year-end copy impacted donor conversion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will longer form copy increase donor conversion rate compared to the standard shortened version?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/17/2018
When building the landing page for HAFA’s calendar year-end efforts to finish 2018, we built the “control” from a close derivative from what was used in the previous year (with updates really to the issues that were pertinent heading into the upcoming year).
The treatment draft unpacked the priorities that were being addressed in a series of emails to increase relevancy between the messaging being delivered in the inbox, such that when someone clicked one of the emails (regardless of which one it was in the series), that there would be a bullet point on the landing page addressing that particular issue as a “top priority” for the upcoming year.
Because of this approach, it increased the amount of copy on the landing page by about 20-25%. So, we wanted to experiment with whether or not the longer form copy could perform similarly or better than the shortened control language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the experiment only achieving a 39.3% confidence score value, further experimentation is required to fully understand the impact of the longer form copy on donor conversion rates.
Even so, we did see a slightly higher donor conversion rate (a +5% increase) on the treatment draft.
Considering we didn’t validate on donor conversions, we certainly would not have validated this experiment for revenue lift, but it is at least worth noting that we did see an improvement in revenue for the treatment draft over the control by an increase of 19.5%, as well.

experiments.title: How presenting a gift of any amount instead of a direct ask impacts donor conversion on mobile devices.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion on mobile devices during year-end by presenting a gift of any amount over a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the homepage of mobile devices if we presented people with a relevant premium offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct year-end donation ask. We featured a devotional book for the treatment that included an image of the book and the appeal. When people clicked through to the donation page, there was an appeal for year-end for a gift of any amount to get the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate and we saw little to no difference in donor conversion with the treatment. This is due to the motivation of donors during this point in the campaign. The experiment was launched the week prior to the end of the campaign when motivation is much higher. We propose testing this approach earlier in the campaign and even during the normal giving season to see the impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a “Submit” button affects email acquisition on an email opt-in
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the word “submit” on a call-to-action button impact the likelihood of someone signing up for email updates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2018 - 01/14/2019
In this experiment, we wondered what affect the typical “submit” language on a call-to-action button would have compared to something that communicates more specifically what action is being taken. The control button said “Sign Me Up” and the treatment button said “Submit.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Submit” button had no significant impact on conversion. While we expected the submit button might lead to a decrease in conversion, it actually remained flat. It’s possible that neither of these call-to-actions are really communicating any distinct value. It’s also possible that we may have seen a more powerful result with a larger sample size. Follow-up testing will be needed to determine if a more value-focused call-to-action can increase conversion on this offer.

experiments.title: How different colored CTA buttons affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the color of the CampusWire CTA button affect the number of people who click on the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2018 - 09/07/2018
The Leadership Institute’s Campus Reform daily email, CampusWire, is sent from a template with bullet point lists of news stories. This email is a direct copy of what is sent each morning at 5am to major news outlets across the country. At the end of each email is a CTA button which leads to a donation page. They wanted to test different background colors on the CTA button because they didn’t want to deviate too far from the original template. They decided to test an orange button against the original blue button, and looked for the number of clicks on the CTA button itself rather than click through rate on the email, since there are several links in each email. The images below are just one of many emails that were sent during the experiment time frame. They hypothesized that the colors would make no real difference.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a .03% and .04% conversion rate on the CTA buttons, no real winner can be determined from this experiment when measuring only clicks. However, when comparing the number of clicks to the click through rate for each of the emails, the blue button did receive 35% more clicks with a 95% confidence rate. See the file “Test for CTA color on CampusWire stats” for details (https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nFI5H606QoeCdA3Kql90NftVcw02kIFXW4dWpzJvOqA/edit#gid=1283200889). Going forward, the Leadership Institute will use a blue background on the CTA for CampusWire.

experiments.title: How more visual and design elements on a landing page affect downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more design and visual elements lead to more downloads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
NextAfter produces a number of reports, studies, and eBooks each year and has a ‘template’ for the download landing page which is very simple and copy heavy. With The Canadian Online Fundraising Scorecard there were some strong visuals and infographics within the report so we wondered if we shared more of the visual components on the landing page and gave the landing page a bit more of an aesthetic that matched the report itself if people would be more willing to download the report.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a very slight increase in downloads on the more designed version so it is worth looking at. The treatment had a signup option right away as opposed to the control where you have to scroll/read before you provide your information which means you may choose to download, or not, before you experience the offer or value so we could keep more of the conversational flow similar while adding more visual elements to see if that makes a difference. The visitor also has a pretty high motivation when they hit the page so perhaps the quick download added value. Again, worth looking at in future experiments.

experiments.title: How indicating the number of steps for a single-step funnel impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding clarity by adding “step 1 of 2” above the form increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2018 - 11/15/2018
We tested adding “step 1 of 2” on our pro-life 101 free online course landing page and increased conversion by 22%. We wanted to test this on another offer and see if it had similar impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for three weeks, we were unable to validate. There was a slight increase, however it was not significant. We will continue adding the text to our landing pages, but any impact will probably be contingent on the specific offer and how traffic was driven there.

experiments.title: How the donation selection area can communicate tangible impact
experiments.researchQuestion: Will variable text around the donation amount field better communicate the impact of larger gifts and thus, increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2018 - 09/07/2018
For Buckner International’s Shoes for Orphan Souls fundraising campaign, we wanted to find the best way to visualize the kind of impact a gift will have. In this situation, $25 would fund a pair of shoes for a child in need. Historically, we had placed text on the donation buttons but the buttons were usually far lower than the average gift amount.
We had the hypothesis that if we removed the buttons all together and created variable copy around the textfield that visitors entered their information, we would be able to secure larger gifts. We decided to test this out during the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the entire campaign, we saw there was no statistically significant difference between either of the two forms with regards to conversion rate or revenue. It it likely that both treatments adequately communicated the impact of lower dollar gifts (less than $100) and that the larger dollar gifts are not as concerned about the tangible impact of their gift. They are giving because they believe in the overall mission.

experiments.title: How validating shared beliefs affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a section to validate shared beliefs affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a campaign to raise support for their Center for the American Future, which defended citizens against government overreach. They were running a petition of support for John Yearwood, a rancher who faced a threat from the government if he disturbed the habitat of a rare spider—one which he had never seen!
They ran an experiment on the petition page to see if adding a section that asked the reader to confirm their shared values—namely that Texas landowners should be able to use their land as they please, that the federal government should let property owners conserve their own land, and that the government should follow the 10th Amendment—would increase email acquisition. Previous testing had implied that these checkboxes could have an impact on email acquisition (more people would sign the petition) and donations (more people would give after signing). They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the shared values checkboxes had no effect on email acquisition. However, the downstream data must be examined further to understand how this is affecting follow-on conversion.

experiments.title: How adding background information in the copy affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a storyline and more information about what Mr. John Yearwood is up against increase giving?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2018
The Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a petition on behalf of a Texas citizen who was under threat of legal action from the federal government. After the petition, signers were given the opportunity to make a donation to this man’s legal fund. Donor conversion was below their benchmark, so they decided to run an experiment. The control copy assumes the reader knows the backstory of Mr. Yearwood. They hypothesized that because this case was not well known, that potential donors might need more  background information, including more on the challenges facing Mr. Yearwood.
