They created a treatment that ended up being quite long and included a thermometer to show progress and social proof. They launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 11 donations to the control and 12 donations to the treatment, they found that there was no difference in conversion between the two pages. While the sample size is not large enough to validate this experiment, current results indicate that even with more traffic, it is likely that there still won't be significant difference in donations between the two experiments.
This prompted them to run another experiment that heightened urgency to see if they could move the needle on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How body copy length on a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Are more people moved to give by the promise of future offers/resources, or by the overall mission of the organization?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2018 - 07/30/2018
Hoover has been running the Dueling Populisms content offer for some time, and wanted to test 2 things: a shortened mission-focused value proposition, and a more offer-centric rather than mission-centric value proposition on their donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but both versions of the shorter copy increased donor conversion rate. This validates the fact that a shortened, concise, and strong value proposition, whether it's mission-focused or offer-focused, is most effective for Hoover's audience. More testing is necessary to determine whether the mission-or-offer-focused value prop works better.

experiments.title: How adding an additional cultivation opportunity affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a checkbox to receive additional content at the end of a form affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2018 - 05/11/2018
Hoover Institution was running their Make America Exceptional Again offer, and wanted to know the effects of offering a checkbox to sign up for the HDR newsletter by the bottom of the form. All copy remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not experience a statistically valid lift. However, this experiment not statistically validating is just as important as if we had seen a lift. This means we can increase the subscribers to the newsletter without adversely impacting acquisition.
The goal of these email acquisition campaigns is to create a file of prospective donors that can also be converted downstream. The best way to do that is through ongoing cultivation.
By getting a new subscriber to opt-in to the ongoing newsletter cultivations, we are putting them into a communication flow that will increase the likelihood of downstream conversion. Since they are having to physically opt-in to the series, it will also increase their perceived value of the communications they will receive.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a person’s gift through a sticky bar can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase recurring donor conversion from the homepage by communicating the impact of a person’s gift on a sticky bar?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/31/2018
FamilyLife ran a high-urgency campaign during the month of May in an effort to increase recurring donor conversion. We hypothesized that we might be able to turn some of the website traffic into recurring donors by presenting a sticky bar on the bottom of the homepage that communicated how many families a person would be able to reach with a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate this experiment during the campaign. This experiment had an interesting concept and should be tested again. We propose testing this approach for one-time gifts during neutral giving seasons.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the donation page can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Could we increase donor conversion by expanding the value proposition and telling them the premium they would get with their gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/31/2018
On the Boys Town website, they have a “You can help” page that get significant traffic and tells people the different ways they can help the organization. One of the primary ways is to make a gift. During their year-end campaign, they wanted to test this donation feature by linking people to two different pages. The control page was a donation page that didn’t have a lot of value proposition copy on it and featured a banner with the primary message. This approach is more traditional to what Boys Town has done on their primary donation page. The treatment page did have extensive value proposition copy on it and featured the premium gift as well. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to impact donor conversion with this experiment. We hypothesize that because of the time of year that this experiment ran – year-end – motivation of donors was already high. It is difficult to impact donor conversion (positively or negatively) when motivation to give to an organization is already high. We propose testing this again when motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How social proof impacted the pledge signature rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing how many other people have signed the pledge increase or decrease the likelihood that someone else will sign the pledge?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2018 - 08/29/2018
We wondered if showing the total number of pledge signatures to-date would improve pledge signature (or rather, email registration) rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only 3% of the required sample size, we did not validate this experiment.
We stopped the experiment to pivot funds and focus upon a new opportunity to acquire names (SCOTUS confirmation efforts), which stopped traffic being sent to this page. Further experimentation is required to definitively answer whether social proof can accelerate registration rates.
It should be noted that the number of registrations (if perceived to be too few) may actually negatively impact the registration rates. Without being deceitful, it may be interesting to also test “large vs. small” social proof statistics and what lifts registration rates the most.

experiments.title: How presenting a gift of any amount instead of a direct ask impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would presenting people with a content offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct donation ask impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Focus on the Family’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on the homepage if we presented people with a relevant premium offer for a gift of any amount instead of a direct year-end donation ask. We featured a devotional book for the treatment that included an image of the book and the appeal. When people clicked through to the donation page, there was an appeal for year-end for a gift of any amount to get the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: The GOAA offer had little to no impact on donor conversion. This can be attributed to the motivation of users during the time the experiment was launched. We propose testing this approach again earlier in the campaign when donor motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How utilizing statistics in an email appeal influenced results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of statistics influence the results of an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2018
Buckner International was sending out their 2018 calendar year end campaign focused on the impact and need behind foster care and adoption. When discussing as a team, there was debate surrounding the use of statistics in the email appeals. There was published research indicating that statistics in an appeal could potentially lower response rates. We decided to test this theory with an email during the year end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that there was no statistically significant difference between either of the treatments with regards to both donations and clicks. It is worth noting that this is just one attempt at validating this theory. We would need to test it a few more times to truly understand the impact of incorporating statistics into our appeals.

experiments.title: How a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
CaringBridge’s donation process had always had a separate page for mobile devices because of some legacy technology. Instead of loading a responsive page, the CaringBridge site used redirects to send mobile users to a separate page entirely. Over time, this page had not kept up with all the optimizations that had been implemented on the desktop page. CaringBridge decided to test the two pages against each other to see the cost and benefit of rolling both pages into one responsive page, which would keep them from having to keep two pages updated and in sync.
The first major difference in the two pages was the gift array. This was tested independently, and had no impact on conversion or revenue. The next major change was the value proposition and layout of the form. The mobile page had always had a shorter value proposition because it was assumed that they had less “real estate” on the page to hold such copy. The team decided to test a mobile version of the exact same donation page that desktop users saw to see the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no change—and even a slight directional increase with the new copy. This gave CaringBridge the confidence to revert to a single, mobile-optimized donation page for their tribute funnel. Going forward, they will monitor desktop and mobile results independently to see how future experiments affect conversion on each.

experiments.title: How using a more personalized, urgent, and relevant message impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a more personalized, urgent, and relevant message impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the Dear Reader feature at the end of articles on the Care Net blog. Our effort to do this included making it more personal by adding a signer to the appeal and making it more urgent and relevant by including year-end focused language and a countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the changes we made had little to no impact on donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that this might be due to the motivation of the user. We launched this campaign towards the end of the month when donor motivation is much higher. When donor motivation is higher, we normally see that our effort to increase donor conversion doesn’t work or has no impact. We propose testing a similar approach when motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How a change in layout plus addition of video affects sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the layout and adding video to a page affect sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2018 - 04/20/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to increase conversions on their “Constitutional Conversations” online course sign up page. They wanted to know if removing endorsements and other offers from the enrollment page would impact conversions. They tested the control, which included endorsements and other offers, against a treatment with only the sign up form, a video, and short copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment versions in this experiment. What’s more, since there were multiple factors being tested, it is hard to know if one of the aspects (video vs. no video and endorsement vs. no endorsement) would have moved the needle by itself. In the future, we will want to isolate those individual variables as separate tests to understand their specific impact.

experiments.title: How adding an endorsement increases sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an endorsement increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2018 - 05/04/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if an endorsement on the sign up page would affect sign ups for the Founding Documents offer. This treatment was tested with the following signers: Laura Ingraham, Dennis Prager, Rich Bott, Rush Limbaugh, and Eric Metaxas. The individual endorsements were shown based upon the source of the traffic so that visitors that were directed by Laura Ingraham would see her endorsement.
experiments.keyLearnings: When analyzing the results across all pages, we found that removing the endorsement from the page resulted in no significant impact to email acquisition. All versions that removed a signer endorsement decreased except for Dennis Prager’s, which saw a 4% lift in sign ups without the endorsement (not statistically significant. This is an important reminder to always validate “best practices.” We had always looked for authoritative endorsement but, in this situation, it would appear not to be a factor in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How removing context of a video affects sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding information about video content impact sign ups? Are people motivated by curiosity, or do they like to know what they’re getting into?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2018 - 06/27/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to get more sign ups for their online course “Introduction to C.S. Lewis.” They wanted to know if providing information about the video on the page, rather than no context at all, would impact sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Insufficient data to produce takeaway. Need to push more traffic to validate.

experiments.title: How adding more information in the sign up section increases sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding information about the seminar location increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2018 - 08/01/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if giving more information in the sign up box — the location of the seminar — would lead to more sign ups. They added a treatment to see what would happen.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although there was not enough traffic to validate this experiment, the current conversion rates of 5.6% for the control and 4.8% for the treatment show that there was no statistically significant difference between adding the location of the seminar in the sign up box.

experiments.title: How problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: How will problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2018 - 12/30/2018
Hillsdale College was trying to increase conversion for their donation pages during their year-end campaign. They had previously seen that problem-centric language, or explaining the challenge they were trying to solve, had increased conversion in a fiscal-year-end campaign. However, they were unsure how that would affect results at a very different time of year, so they decided to test it.
They created a page that started with the problem, rather than positioning Hillsdale as the solution and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The problem-centric language produced an 8.4% decrease in conversion, though it didn’t reach statistical validity. However, due to the time-sensitive nature of the year-end ask, they decided to continue using the control as not to lose any revenue during peak times.
Previous testing has shown that problem-centric language can increase conversion, but a calendar year-end campaign might not be the best time to try it.

experiments.title: How a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius affected opens

rows.name: The roots of Jane Austen's genius

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius increase opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2018 - 04/20/2018
This experiment was conducted to see if a change in subject line between Jane Austen’s first novel and the root of her creative genius had any effect on open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rate for the control was 14% and the open rate for the treatment was 14.1%. This means there was no significant difference in responses to the subject lines. For future experiments, we will need to test more radical changes to the subject line.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a website affects email acquisition conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a sticky bar to our website with a content offer convert names from our web traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2018 - 02/19/2018
Buckner was wanting to diversify the ways they acquired emails through content offers, and to take advantage of their website traffic to convert names. They wanted to test whether offering the 7 Scriptures offer on their website as a sticky bar popup would convert people. The initial test measured clicks to the landing page and the tested variable was control copy vs. supporter copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 10 days of running the experiment, we only acquired 1 new name from the landing page. Because of this learning, we have concluded that other forms of acquisition are more effective than a sticky bar on the webpage.

experiments.title: Impact of using a baby image in a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Do images of babies perform better than images of pregnant women.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2018
We have an MLD pledge we use to introduce people to MLD and ask them to become volunteers. We wanted to increase the number of volunteers that sign-up. We knew that baby images were performing well in other tests, so we decided to try it in this Facebook ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: The image of the baby performed significantly better than the image of the pregnant woman. Next, we will test an image of a fetus against this baby image to see if it performs better.

experiments.title: The Impact of Making Two CTAs in one Appeal Pt. 1
experiments.researchQuestion: Would appealing to donors to “cut the cord” by cutting their entertainment bill (and its support of pro-choice programming) in favor of becoming a monthly donor increase the number of donations we received? What would the impact of making two CTAs in the same appeal be on donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2018
For months, Care Net has been brainstorming ways to increase the number of monthly donors, Caring Partners, that we have on file. We proposed testing our traditional appeal language against a new “Cut the Cord” campaign. This appeal would argue that money spent on entertainment providers like Netflix, Amazon Prime, Cable often supports pro-choice initiatives. By “cutting” some of our expenses on such entertainment and redirecting the money to Care Net’s work, lives would be saved and pro-choice orgs would lose funding. A win-win. We hoped that a pro-life person’s ire about pro-choice funding and programming would help motivate them to “cutting the cord” and saving lives.
The drawback to this approach would be that it would be essentially making two calls to actions: Cut expenses on entertainment and become a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the Cut the Cord email had the benefit of a new sender and a higher CTR, the donation rate was significantly lower. The results seemed to confirm that two CTAS in the appeal suppressed donor response as they had to agree to cutting back on something they were already doing and beginning something they weren’t yet doing. This was too much friction. However, we wanted to be sure. The variation had a 33% higher CTR. We wondered if we had too much copy on the donation page for the variation and that people dropped off who were prepared to make a gift. We decided to run an additional test to see if shorter copy on the donation page would cause Cut the Cord to perform better.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition to the offer influenced donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the application of Construal Level Theory increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2018 - 12/21/2018
For the instant donor conversion page on Buckner’s Advent Devotional, we wanted to test out a theory in conversion optimization known as Construal Level Theory. The theory states that an individual would be more likely to take an action that went against their own personal self interests if they felt it benefited a small group that they were a part of. In this situation, we were asking them to give a donation because they were part of a select group of people that wanted to protect vulnerable children this Christmas season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, we simply ran out of time before we could observe any significant influence of the treatment. We did not have enough visitors to the two variants to be able to measure the impact. Since this offer was only applicable during the Advent season, we had to turn if off two weeks into the experiment.
This is a theory worth retesting in the future when we have an offer that will see significant traffic volume or one that is being sent out to the email file.

experiments.title: How scripture and emphasizing offer importance impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will incorporating scripture and emphasizing why the offer is important increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2018 - 11/16/2018
In an effort to increase signups to their Parenting Video Series, Buckner decided to try a radical approach of putting a “Parenting Pledge” prior to the Video Series acquisition page. Visitors were asked to pledge to raise their children according to scripture and were then sent to a page with the video series that could help them do that.
After launching this pledge, we noticed that the conversion rate on the page was lower than we would like it to be. To try to improve that, we decided to test out a different approach with the pledge. We rewrote the copy to incorporate scripture and to focus on why signing the pledge is important. We then tested the original pledge against this new version to see which would acquire more emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a week, we did not see any statistically significant change between the two versions. It would appear that the incorporation of scripture and emphasizing why the offer was important was not important for the page. These were two elements that were included in the advertising; it would appear that using them in the ads caused them to be unnecessary on the page.
Given the high cost for acquisition, we decided to turn off this offer and focus on other means to acquire potential donors for Buckner.

experiments.title: Does sending a newsletter on Friday instead of Saturday morning improve open rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending a newsletter on Friday instead of Saturday morning improve open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/12/2018
AFP wanted to know if sending their weekly newsletter on Friday morning improved open rates over their standard Saturday morning delivery timeframe.
experiments.keyLearnings: Inconclusive. We need to create a larger sample and run the split again to conclusively declare a winner.

experiments.title: How personalization and value proposition affect recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does personalization (and the presence of value proposition copy in the first place) affect conversion for recurring donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2018
CaringBridge had recently reintroduced the recurring gift option in their tribute page donation process. While a previous test had showed that a single checkbox with value proposition copy beat a binary (monthly/one-time) option, they wanted to test the impact of personalizing the copy to the subject of the journal. While they were doing that, they wanted to also test a version that had no additional value proposition copy to establish a baseline conversion rate, since their first test was a radical redesign. They designed two treatments—one with personalized copy and one with just “Make this a monthly recurring donation” and launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there weren't enough conversions to validate any of the treatments, the final treatment with no value proposition copy showed a directional 40% decrease in conversion, with a growing level of confidence. This implies (though not statistically valid) that any amount of value proposition copy is better than no value proposition copy. There was no difference at all in conversion by adding personalization (which goes against the grain of previous testing), but also shows that we must work harder at this ask to increase results, as it is a secondary value proposition—asking the donor to "lift" a little more (with a monthly donation) when they are already making a lift as it is (with a single donation).

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video of Mark promoting the course convert more people than a graphic that looks similar to the course?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/15/2018
Heartlight was promoting their new free online course, Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, on Facebook. They originally launched ads with a graphic that matched the look and feel of the course, but wanted to test a video of Mark promoting the course to see if it increased conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although this test didn’t validate, we saw a 12% directional lift with the video creative. In addition to testing these two creatives, we also tested 3 other stock images and a longer version of the video, which all received a similar or lower conversion rate than these two options. Moving forward, we’ll be testing other elements of the ads to see if we can decrease cost and increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more donations for the non-donor audience?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with expanding the value proposition within the body of the email it was sending to raise funds for their Rapid Response Team to minister to victims of the disaster. This was the second email in a longer series and was intended to make a direct ask for support. Our hypothesis was that many of the non-donors may not yet understand the reason to make a gift to BGEA and would require more explanation to help them toward a donation. To test this hypothesis, a second email was developed that explained more about the history and purpose of the Rapid Response Team.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a ~60% level of confidence, we learned that an expanded value proposition centered around the history of the Rapid Response Team did not motivate more non-donors to make a gift. However, we also learned that the longer copy did not really negatively impact donor conversion given the low level of significance.
This would indicate the potential for further testing of the expanded value proposition concept but using different reasons to make a gift. There is potential to focus on the increasing need that comes from multiple natural disasters that are coming in the near future or how this ministry fits into the overall mission of BGEA.

experiments.title: How the donation page a pop-up links to impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the donation microsite impact donor conversion for the homepage pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2018 - 08/23/2018
On the Boys Town homepage, they had a pop-up appear once you had been on the site for five seconds. This pop-up links to a specific donation page for the value proposition of the pop-up. After just launching a new donation microsite and seeing that the donation microsite increased donor conversion by 21% on the primary donation page, we wanted to test the donation microsite against the original donation page for this homepage pop-up. Each donation page had identical copy on it, it was strictly just testing the donation experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The kind of donation page we sent people to from the pop-up had little to no impact on donor conversion. People coming from the pop-up were motivated enough to not be impacted by the donation experience. We would like to test this again with using just the banner image at the top of the page since we now know that that version of the donation microsite increases donor conversion most.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of the suggested gift amount impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2018 - 08/16/2018
Boys Town offers parents an eBook on how to teach your child to have integrity. This was a brand new offer and they wanted to use a suggested giving amount on the instant donation page for the ask. They hypothesized that by telling people the impact of the suggested gift amount, they’d might be able to increase donor conversion. The control only had a suggested gift amount and the treatment had a suggested gift amount and the specific impact it would have, “Your $50 gift will help us reach 2,000 more people with parenting resources.”
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for over three weeks and we weren’t able to reach validity. This is due to the really low donor conversion on the page. More radical experiments will need to be done to impact donor motivation and conversion.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a shortened value proposition impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2018 - 09/13/2018
In a previous test for the primary donation page, we found that an expanded value proposition didn’t convert as many people into donors as a version of the page that had an banner image at the top with no additional value proposition. Since we have seen in hundreds of other experiments that value proposition can increase donor conversion and average gift, we wanted to continue to test this approach. We hypothesized that by shortening the value proposition we might be able to increase donor conversion since a large majority of the traffic coming to this page is mobile.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for three weeks, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. That said, we did see a directional decrease with the treatment. This tells us that the banner image is still the best performing version of the primary donation page. The additional value proposition copy might actually be creating friction in the donation process because the people coming to the page are on mobile and are already highly motivated to give. Additional testing around the value proposition should be done to gain a better understanding of the donor’s motivation.

experiments.title: How a more organizational-centric value proposition impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Equip more parents like you with this resource
Invest in the lives of forgotten kids

experiments.researchQuestion: How would a different value proposition impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2018 - 10/08/2018
Boys Town offers an eBook on integrity. They promote the eBook through paid Facebook ads. It has a high appeal and they have been able to acquire new names really well with it. With the significant amount of people getting this eBook, that also means that they get a lot of traffic to the instant donation page that follow the eBook offer. The instant donor conversion on this page was low and they hypothesized that testing a different value proposition might increase donor conversion. They developed a treatment that focused more on the mission of Boys Town. It validated the parent reading the copy that they are a good parent for wanting to walk alongside their child and help them with their character. But then it talked about the kids at Boys Town who have been forgotten and aren’t as fortunate to have parents or loved ones who care about them. We tested this more organization-centric value proposition against the control that asked for people to make a gift so that other parents could be equipped with the resource that they just downloaded.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over a month, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. The motivation of donors on this page is extremely low and the kind of value proposition didn’t impact donor conversion. We will need to do a very radical redesign of this page to impact donor conversion.

experiments.title: How simplifying and streamlining the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and creating an ordered “next steps” list on the instant donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2018 - 08/23/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an email acquisition campaign around the upcoming Supreme Court nominations. After visitors signed an open letter to the Senate, they were presented with an instant donor conversion page. We had seen relatively low donor conversion so we wanted to test out a bit of a radical change to the donation page.
Based upon experiments with similar organizations, we had found success in creating an ordered “next steps” list that walked visitors through what to expect and why a donation to AFP was so important. We decided to implement this and then remove some of the details of the full “plan” to reduce the friction associated with reading the whole page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two pages. After further analysis of the two value propositions, one aspect of the pages that remained the same was the focus on the “product-level” value proposition of the efforts behind the Kavanaugh confirmation hearings. It may be worth testing a value proposition that is not so narrowly focused and, instead, emphasizes the overall efforts of the organization. This will be the next step in our efforts to optimize this campaign.

experiments.title: How a key insight in a Facebook ad affects eBook downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad focused on a key insight lead to greater eBook downloads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2018 - 08/15/2018
In this experiment, we wondered which ad approach would be most effective for the “Nonprofit News Sources” audience when promoting a recently published study about fundraising teams.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, we saw a contradictory result to the same experiment conducted with the Nonprofit News Sources audience; although, this experiment did not reach validity. I have two hypothesis as to why the results are different. First, it’s possible that the makeup of the lookalike audience is more practitioners than managers and executives, and the specific insight on the ad image was more of a turnoff than it was interesting – where as the nonprofit news sources audience may skew towards people in a leadership role.
The second hypothesis is that the book cover image portrayed more practical ideas of how to “optimize your team,” which may have been more attractive to our typical audience.
Although this experiment didn’t reach validity, there was a substantial difference in cost per acquisition. The control was acquiring emails for $3.17, and the treatment for $5.45.

experiments.title: How personal asks perform against persuasive asks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal ask outperform a persuasive ask?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2018
Leadership Institute was sending an appeal email to people who had donated to a matching gift campaign the previous year. They were curious whether these people, who had already absorbed and responded to the value proposition a year earlier, would need to be re-persuaded—or if a more personal request from an LI staff member would be more effective.
They had a control that sought to persuade through a story of impact, followed by an ask. They developed a personal treatment that very simply reminded the recipient that they had given last year, the need was still there, and requested a gift again this year. Though there was a small sample size to begin with, they launched a split test to see if they could validate a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The sample size was too small to validate, but the treatment delivered twice as many gifts as the control. This needs to be tested with a larger sample size, which means that the copy will need to be adjusted to include non-donors. However, this experiment does deliver a directional learning that can be tested with future donor-specific segments.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition on a pledge donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would expanding the value proposition increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 03/15/2018
Focus on the Family has a separate donation page from their primary page specifically for recurring giving. When looking at the copy on this page, we hypothesized that the copy led with a more transactional nature and that the messaging could be strengthened to better communicate the impact a person could have by becoming a recurring donor. We developed a treatment that reinforced the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for a month, we weren’t able to validate the results. This tells us that the expanded value proposition had no impact on conversion. Conversion rate on this page is very low. We will need to do a more radical redesign and address multiple friction points on the page if we want to decrease anxiety and increase motivation of people on the page.

experiments.title: How addressing a person’s fear to become a recurring donor impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would addressing a person’s potential fear of not being able to cancel their recurring gift increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2018 - 05/09/2018
On Focus on the Family’s recurring gift donation page, we hypothesized that people may have a fear of commitment when it came to becoming a recurring donor. To address this potential concern, we added copy to the page that clearly communicated to people that they could cancel their recurring gift at any time.
experiments.keyLearnings: We had hypothesized that addressing a person’s fear in becoming a recurring donor could dramatically impact donor conversion. What we found was that addressing this element of fear had no impact on conversion. This tells us that there is a different element of friction causing the low conversion rates. We propose potentially discovering this through an exit-intent survey asking users why they didn’t become a recurring donor.

experiments.title: How streamlining an acquisition page affects course signups
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more streamlined, value-focused acquisition page increase conversions on an online course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2018 - 06/08/2018
The Missionary Oblates were offering a free online course called Finding Your Prophetic Voice, and wanted to increase conversion on the landing page. To do this, they streamlined the page and used more value-focused body copy, along with a headline that better explained the value of the course to the taker. They also included testimonials from people who had taken another Oblates course to further explain the value.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was not statistically valid, and will require more testing to identify specific learnings.

experiments.title: How simplifying a landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does simplifying the copy on a page bring clarity to the value proposition and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2018 - 07/05/2018
Buckner International was offering a free parenting series course and wanted to try to increase conversions on the landing page. They hypothesized that simplifying the landing page by taking out some of the copy and clarifying the value proposition would increase conversion. They also took out many of the Buckner references and focused more on the parent the content was speaking to.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment produced a 5% directional lift, there was no statistically valid learnings from this experiment. Since the page was already pretty highly converting once people got to the page, we recommend testing other parts of the funnel to significantly alter conversion rate and cost.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition about a matching gift affects click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Does additional value prop about a matching gift increase clickthrough?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
Buckner International was running their first June solicitation email regarding a matching gift and the program it affected. They wanted to see if adding additional value prop about the matching gift would motivate more people to click the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically significant and did not produce a significant directional lift. If this test were to be repeated, more elements would need to be changed, or copy would need to be changed more drastically in order to see a true difference.

experiments.title: How a product-level value proposition performs on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a product-level value proposition, focused on the Open Letter, increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/15/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an open letter to the US Senate in regards to the upcoming confirmation hearings for Judge Brett Kavanaugh. After every signature, visitors were taken to a donation page for the option of making an instant donation. Given the emphasis put on this acquisition effort, we wanted to identify the optimal value proposition that would lead to the best overall donor conversion.
With this in mind, we wanted to test two different value propositions. The control focused more about the overall goal of the campaign and all of the effort that was going to go into getting the Senate to confirm Judge Kavanaugh. The treatment version focused more on the Open Letter. The hypothesis was that since the visitor had just taken part in the Open Letter, they may be more likely to support a value proposition that centered around it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, after running for over a week, we did not see a significant improvement in the donor conversion. Given the time-sensitive nature of this campaign, we decided to turn off this campaign and move on to another hypothesis.
While we did not determine, with statistical significance, whether the product-level value proposition would outperform something that was a bit broader, it was unlikely that the product-level value proposition would outperform the control. Based upon that, we decided to move to the next hypothesis.

experiments.title: How a banner image impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the banner increase the visual clarity of the headline leading to increased email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/17/2018
For the Open Letter to the Senate for the confirmation of Judge Kavanaugh, we had a highly converting email acquisition page. However, we had the hypothesis that it was possible the headline (which is one of the most important aspects of the landing page) was getting lost in the banner image at the top. To validate this hypothesis, we set up two versions of the page with the treatment including the exact same headline but placed inline with the copy with the banner removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant difference between the control and the treatment. In this situation, it would appear the headline was not as difficult to read as we initially thought it might be.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal affects donor conversion rate in a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the mention of a campaign goal in a high urgency campaign motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running their fiscal year end high urgency campaign and wanted to test whether the inclusion of a campaign goal motivated more people to click through to the donation page and give. They ran this on each email of the campaign , with copy in the emails being largely the same other than the inclusion or exclusion of the goal amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but we saw a directional decrease of 11% in donations with the removal of the campaign goal. This shows us that it’s likely more people will give towards a goal rather than an open-ended ask.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a redesigned donation page that removes header content increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running a high-urgency fiscal year end campaign and wanted to test whether the format of the donation page was causing friction for people coming to the page and giving them decision anxiety. They proposed a radical redesign that removed all other links from the header and top of the page and streamlined the form and body copy. The copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was not statistically valid, but we did see a directional lift of 6% with the redesigned page. This concept would be good to test with additional campaigns along with other elements of the page to produce a valid lift.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party credibility impacts conversion at the point of conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the 3rd party evidential of “3.2 million other Americans” decrease anxiety and, subsequently, increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2018 - 07/05/2018
Americans for Prosperity (AFP) was launching an open letter to the US Senate regarding the upcoming Supreme Court nominee as a means to acquire new emails and new donors. AFP has a unique aspect of their organization in that they have 3.2 million activists that are a part of their efforts. We had the hypothesis that including this fact near the point of conversion (the submit button) would help decrease the anxiety associated with giving their information. We decided to test this.
experiments.keyLearnings: After showing the two treatments to over 5,000 visitors, we did not see any significant increase in a visitors likelihood to convert. It is worth pointing out that with a 65% conversion rate on the page, the visitors are already highly motivated. It is our hypothesis that, given this high motivation, the visitors likely do not have a significant degree of anxiety which means the 3rd party evidential was not necessary.

experiments.title: How value proposition copy affects clicks on a donate button in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the additional value proposition copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/19/2018
Leadership Institute sent a daily email called CampusWire, updating their audience about developments through their campus network. At the bottom of the email, they had a simple button that asked the reader to support CampusReform. While this did produce donors, they wondered if adding value proposition to the button would increase clickthrough rate and thus increase donation page traffic. They crafted a treatment and sent it to half of the file for several weeks to see if it would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After several weeks, there was no statistical difference between the two treatments. Clickthrough rate overall was low, since this is at the bottom of an email designed to get people to click through to the site, but it showed that the additional value proposition was not significantly increasing motivation—which suggests that readers were already motivated to give just through the content.
This experiment deserves further review to see if donation page conversion improved. It also suggests that fundraising might increase if this value proposition was broken out into its own email, away from the context.

experiments.title: How pre-selecting the monthly recurring gift option impacts recurring donor conversion.

rows.name: Added Call to Action + Recurring Gift Default (1)

experiments.researchQuestion: Would pre-selecting the monthly gift option increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/02/2018
FamilyLife launched a recurring gift campaign. On the donation page they pushed people to, donors were given two options for their gift – a one-time gift, or a monthly gift. The primary goal of this campaign was to get more recurring donors. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase recurring donors if we get rid of the one-time gift check box and pre-select the monthly gift option.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did get a directional 33.7% lift in recurring donor conversion. This experiment ran during a high urgency campaign and what we found was that the increase in recurring donor conversion was much higher at the beginning of the campaign

experiments.title: How an open gift field for a recurring donation impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an open giving field increase donor motivation and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2018 - 06/29/2018
National Breast Cancer Foundation launched a recurring gift campaign asking donors and nondonors to become an ongoing supporter or their organization. Specifically, the recurring gift ask was about providing a mammogram to a woman in need. It costs $13 a month to provide one woman her mammogram for the year. Traditionally, NBCF used suggested giving arrays on their donation form. They hypothesized that an open giving field might inspire more generosity and allow the donor to decide exactly how much they wanted to give. In the body copy before the giving array and open gift field they had strong value proposition for the recurring ask and specifically mentioned the $13/month figure. One additional element tested with the open gift field was they reminded donors what their gift would do after they plugged in their gift amount. The copy would dynamically change depending on the amount of the gift. For example, if a donor typed in $13 for their gift amount it would say, “Your $13 monthly donation provides 1 woman with her mammogram this year.” Or if a donor typed in $40, it would say, “Your $40 monthly donation provides 3 women with their mammograms this year.” They ran this experiment for the duration of the recurring gift campaign that last three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional lift of 60.5%. This approach didn’t have a significant impact on gift amount, but it did increase donor conversion. What we learned is that by allowing the donor to decide how much they wanted to give and then reinforcing the value proposition of their gift, we were able to motivate more people to become a recurring donor.
To have a more clear understanding of the specific variable that increased conversion, you could isolate each variable to see if the open giving field or the reinforced value proposition was the motivating factor for donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a premium added to a landing page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2018 - 06/01/2018
Compassion International wanted to test the impact of a premium on sponsorship conversion. They had a partnership with Chip and Joanna Gaines that allowed them to offer a book as a premium as a way to thank the donor for their sponsorship.
They added this premium to the sponsorship pages specifically around their remarketing campaigns driven from Facebook and launched an A/B test to determine the impact for conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the premium offer to the donation page produced a slight lift (8%) with no statistical confidence. This lack of variance seemed to indicate that by the time the prospect had reached the page, they had already made up their mind to give and weren’t affected by the premium.

The Compassion team decided to launch another test after this that moved the premium offer upstream in the communication chain to see if the premium would attract more traffic from the Facebook ad itself, and would result in a higher downstream conversion rate.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects conversion on an “instant” donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will optimizing the value proposition on the donation page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2018 - 06/22/2018
Leadership Institute offered a free online video course, Conservatism 101 that they promoted through Facebook, hoping to use it to acquire prospects and turn them into donors. After users signed up, they were presented with a thank you page that asked for a donation. While this page had a conversion rate above 1%, which was the low side of the benchmark, they believed they could improve it through testing and optimization. They created a second donation page that took a more emotional tone.
The initial page went from the thank you message directly into tactical copy about how the course was delivered and why it was created. They reduced this “tactical” copy on the page in order to move more quickly into “why” the course was created, and the context of how it is currently taught on college campuses. The new copy tried to cast a vision for the course, and made a direct ask (in the form of a question) to fund it, finishing with a quantified ask of how $50 will help. Then, it preemptively thanked the donor before they made their gift. They launched an A/B test to determine which page delivered a better conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The second page produced a 97.5% lift, though the results were not statistically valid because of reduced media spend and low sample size. However, since a 75% level of confidence was reached, they felt the risk was mitigated to roll this out as the winner, increase the media spend, and develop a new treatment on top of this learning.

experiments.title: How the an individual sender impacted open rate for a cold list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an individual sender increase the open rate for a prospecting email list?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2018
Alliance Defending Freedom was in the process of cultivating a new list of email prospects with a series of emails. We had noticed a declining open rate so we wanted to see if there was a way to potentially boost this metric. We decided to try sending the emails from an individual (Michael Farris) instead of the organization. We had the hypothesis that people would be more likely to respond to an individual than an organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, we did not see a statistically significant difference between either sender. This is an interesting observation because with previous experiments for ADF related to sender name, we found that individuals will often perform better for their existing housefile. This would suggest that it isn’t just sending from an individual that moves the needle but sending from a known individual.

experiments.title: How adding an impact-focused value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/30/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Despite our best efforts, this experiment didn’t move the needle enough to be statistically valid. Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we should continue to test people’s motivation for providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well.

experiments.title: How email design impacted opens with a prospecting email list
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler email increase the likelihood of the email being opened?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/25/2018
ADF was using their existing email welcome series to “warm up” the purchased prospecting list from Axciom. The open rate of this list was significantly low compared to the standard house file emails so we decided to test some deliverability tactics to improve this metric. We had found the sender did not play a significant roll in increasing the opens so we decided to test the style of the email. The hypothesis was that a simpler email would not be placed in the “Promotions” tab of systems like Gmail and Yahoo which would increase the likelihood of being opened.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant different in open rate between both the designed and plain text emails. This is actually beneficial for ADF as their standard emails to their subscriber base have the more “designed” style which means this email file will fit well within their audience.

experiments.title: How the messaging approach for the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will targeting the visitors’ fear of loss increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2018 - 05/21/2018
The NIFLA statement of belief had received significant attention in our optimization efforts, especially at the top of the funnel. However, we had not yet done much testing in the final step of the process which was the donation page. This offer was going to be sent to the existing subscriber base so, with the potential influx of visitors, we decided to set up a test on this final page.
After reviewing the existing page, we had a hypothesis that we could improve the results by altering the delivery of the value proposition. Instead of focusing on the work being done, we shifted our message to focus on what could happen if we were to fail. Our hypothesis was that by focusing on people’s fear of loss, we could increase the urgency of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we did not see a statistically significant difference between the two variants. This would suggest the two tactics were not radically different enough to increase the urgency of the appeal. For future testing, it would be best to test a new tactic for increasing the perceived urgency of the appeal.

experiments.title: How an impact-focused value proposition on a donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move people to give more generously?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 06/02/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we did see a directional lift of 85% in average gift amount, which shows us that people are motivated by providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well. We should continue testing this type of value proposition in future messaging to see if this continues to be the case.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the Plugged In donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an expanded and strengthened value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/22/2018
On Focus on the Family’s Plugged In donation page, we hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on that page by adding additional value proposition for why a person should give and what their donation would help accomplish. We developed a treatment that not only strengthened the value proposition, but also connected Plugged In with the parent brand, Focus on the Family. We included Plugged In as an evidential for why a person should support Focus on the Family.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for three weeks, we saw no significant difference in conversion with the treatment. In looking at the data from this experiment, a clear insight from it is that the conversion rate on the page is extremely low. The low conversion rate points to low motivation of the end user. Not even strengthening the value proposition were we able to impact the motivation of the end user. The next approach in testing this page needs to be focused on a more radical treatment to better understand the motivation and kind of people visiting this page.

experiments.title: Does Adding the Word “Free” to a Landing Page Subhead Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding “free” language to the offer page subhead increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/17/2018
Our landing pages for The Hoover Institution’s ebook offers typically focused on simply explaining the benefits of the offer content rather than emphasizing that they are getting it the content for free. Since the typical Hoover donor is usually an older, intellectual, middle-to-upper class demographic, our assumption was that they would likely view a “free” offer as having less value than otherwise. However, we decided to test this hypothesis just in case our assumptions were incorrect.
experiments.keyLearnings: As there was virtually no statistical difference, we concluded that adding "free" language to the subhead was unnecessary to the offer's success. It was not necessarily hurting the value of the offer, but it was also not compelling enough to increase our conversion rate. In this case, we would rather stick with simply relying on the value proposition of the offer to sell itself.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an in-site ask affected clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of an end-of-article call-to-action affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/15/2018
Leadership Institute had seen a large jump in donation traffic through the implementation of an end-of-article call-to-action on their CampusReform website. They wanted to test to see if improved copy and layout could increase clickthrough rate from the site and hopefully increase overall donation revenue. They created a treatment that added increased persuasive copy, a third-party quote, and statistics to bolster the value proposition. They also made a softer ask in the button, asking the reader to “learn more” rather than “support Campus Reform now”
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no valid difference in clickthrough rate. After receiving several hundred impressions per CTA, the treatment showed a slight decrease in clickthroughs, but nowhere near validity. The team went back to the drawing board to try to find something more radical that would positively increase clicks.

experiments.title: How an additional content offer checkbox affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional content offer through a checkbox decrease conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2018 - 05/11/2018
Hoover Institution was offering an eBook, Make America Exceptional Again to build their email file. They also had the Hoover Daily Report, a daily newsletter as an additional content offer. They wanted to give their visitors the chance to opt in to receive the daily newsletter in addition to the eBook, but didn’t want to disrupt conversion on the initial offer.
They decided to set up the checkbox opt-in on a separate page and split the traffic to test the impact of the second offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced no significant impact—increase or decrease—in conversion. Further data analysis showed that 73% of people who saw the treatment also opted into the Hoover Daily Report, which provided additional cultivation and engagement. 

Ultimately, though there wasn't a lift, this test was a win—conversion rate was not affected, and most users chose to receive additional content from Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How an additional opt-in impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional opt-in option for have any impact on email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2018 - 05/03/2018
The Heritage Foundation did an analysis and discovered that subscribers that were not cultivated with their daily newsletter, The Morning Bell, were far less likely to convert to become donors. However, we had also found that The Morning Bell by itself was not an appealing enough offer to collected subscribers for a reasonable costs. With these two facts in mind, we decided to launch an experiment to see if we could acquire Morning Bell subscribers through other offers.
We decided to add a checkbox on the acquisition form for one of their eBook that would allow people to get both the eBook and the Morning Bell newsletter. We wanted to test this before rolling it out to measure the impact this checkbox would have on acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over a month, we did not see any statistically significant impact on email acquisition. Given the additional friction this checkbox could have caused, this is actually the ideal outcome since we are now able to acquire higher potential subscribers with no impact to conversion rate or cost.

experiments.title: How a targeted serial impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the messaging of the serial ask around the individual campaign increase overall conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2018 - 04/23/2018
For the 2018 Tax Day Moneybomb, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of gifts made to Heritage Action as part of the serial ask. In past testing, we found that a more relevant message was able to increase those giving to the serial ask. We decided to see if crafting this serial ask around the individual campaign would have increased results.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, we did not get enough donors to the primary ask for this campaign to be able to to validate a serial ask message. Since the samples for this treatment is based upon those that make an initial gift, we were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the experiment.

experiments.title: How reducing friction and aligning the CTA with the end result affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will reducing friction and aligning the CTA with the end result affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2018 - 04/26/2018
Compassion International has an option on their navigation bar to “select a child for me”. A popout picked a child from the database and gave them the “choose me” call-to-action. When the user clicked “choose me”, they were taken to the biography page of the child to learn more about them.
The CI team hypothesized that if they made “choose me” actually add the child to the user’s shopping cart, they would increase the number of people who followed through on their sponsorship.
They split their traffic and watched the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Over a two month period, this experiment produced a 12% lift in conversions, though it didn’t achieve statistical validity due to low traffic. In this case, a lift of this magnitude is worth rolling out without statistical validity, as the total traffic through this funnel makes the test unlikely to validate at any time of year.

experiments.title: How a more direct call-to-action affects conversion from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action increase online course registration conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/20/2018
We ran this experiment across several different audience groups in our Facebook advertising of our new email fundraising course. We wondered if a more direct call-to-action could be effective in driving higher conversions. The control used a call-to-action of “Learn More,” while the treatment used a call-to-action of “Active Your Free Online Course.” This experiment was run on the Nextafter.com traffic Facebook audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct call-to-action saw an increase of 186%, but it did not reach full statistical validity. We chose the treatment as the winner before reaching full validity since it had a 50% lower cost-per-conversion compared to the control.

experiments.title: How link placement in a course offer email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a link placed higher in a course registration email increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/11/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if having a link to the course registration page near the top of the email would increase clicks to the registration page. The control looked like our normal, longer form personal emails. The treatment naturally flowed into a link to the page early on, and the rest of the copy was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment didn’t significantly affect clicks to the landing page, indicating that link placement in a personal email like this does not affect the likelihood of getting a recipient to click. The value proposition is the primary influencer of the click.

experiments.title: How adding security indicators on a giving form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we be able to increase donor conversion by increasing perceived security on donation pages.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2018 - 04/11/2018
We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion on FamilyLife’s donation pages if we increased the perceived security near the credit card section of the donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that added a blue box around the credit card information, added an image of a lock in this same section of the form and said, “Your information is secured”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over two months, we weren't able to validate it, but we did see directional lift in donor conversion of 7%. What this tells us is that there is a mental cost to people when making a donation and that when we can relieve some of that friction - in this case by visually telling them that their information is secure - that we can decrease friction and increase conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and removing friction on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing friction and adding value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2018 - 04/13/2018
The Missionary Oblates wanted to increase donations on their Mother’s Day premium gift pages. They wanted to test if streamlining the page by removing additional ways to leave the page (a back button) as well as adding value proposition to the copy would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased donor conversion rate by 32%. The motivation of people coming to this page is relatively high and it seems that the added value prop copy actually added friction to the conversion process rather than motivate more people to convert. Although this experiment wasn’t statistically valid, it would be helpful to try testing this again with only one changed variable, so we can nail down the exact variable that decreased conversion.

experiments.title: How changing the layout of an acquisition page impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would laying the page out differently help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/11/2018
After a person signs a pro-life pledge, they are taken to a confirmation page for the pledge they just signed that is also an acquisition page for the Pro-life 101 online course acquisition page. Care Net hypothesized that simplifying the copy on that page into a stepped approach might help increase conversion of the online course. They developed a treatment that laid out the same copy from the control differently in an effort to make the page easier to read.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for over two weeks, there was no significant difference in conversion and had a low level of confidence. What this tells us is that a simplified, stepped approach has no real impact on this page and that a more radical redesign is needed to effect conversion.

experiments.title: How membership language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will membership language affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2018 - 04/09/2018
Illinois Policy Institute wanted to launch a membership program to better sell the benefits of a donating and increase retention. They were curious whether this membership language would present a stronger value proposition than the straightforward donation ask. They split traffic at the “donate” button and sent half the traffic to the donation page and half to the membership page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for a month, there was no statistical difference on

experiments.title: How shortening the funnel on the Dear Reader impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a link to the donation page in the mid-article teaser increase overall donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2018
The Daily Signal has been running an inline donation ask for over a year now. Through a series of iterations, we had found that moving the article to the end of the article was the ideal positioning. However, we had always kept a mid-article teaser that would link to the bottom of the article where the ask was found. It would hint about the need to take action. Given the high volume of traffic that clicked on the link, we decided to test if sending people directly to the donation page would increase our overall donor conversion. Our hypothesis was that, even if we sent a far lower motivated traffic, the overall increase would net out an increase in donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Including a link and a direct call to action in the mid-article teaser was able to triple the amount of traffic getting to the donation page but it had no statistically significant impact on donor conversion. It turns out that despite the significant increase in traffic, the visitors had such a low motivation that it was unable to move the overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the call-to-action impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing on the impact of the gift instead of the challenge aspect increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2018
For their March appeal, Alliance Defending Freedom was sending a follow up email explaining the outcome of their recent Supreme Court case. As part of the appeal, they were also announcing a matching grant and using that as one of the primary reasons to give a gift. Since it was a “challenge” gift, much of the language around the call-to-action was about “accepting the challenge.” We had the hypothesis that by focusing on the value of the gift (ie. to protect lives and freedom) instead of the challenge, we could get more visitors to the website and more gifts to the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant impact on overall gifts or clicks between the two treatments. It would appear that the value proposition of the various calls-to-action were not significantly different enough to see a measurable impact.

experiments.title: How making the daily resource less transactional on Adventures in Odyssey impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer increased conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2018 - 03/08/2018
The Adventures in Odyssey page gets significant traffic. In an effort to turn this traffic into donors, we hypothesized that turning the daily resource offer into something less transactional might increase conversion. To do this, we developed a treatment that told people the kind of impact they would have when they gave to Focus on the Family, and specifically when they supported Adventures in Odyssey. We also offered them a free 90 Day Devotional for Kids as an incentive for their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low conversion on this page, there was not enough data to validate or see a significant difference between the two offers. The majority of the traffic on this page is returning visitors. The motivation of people on this page is to get the audio. Knowing that, we will give them something of value instead of asking them to give and get to see if we can increase conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How modifying the donation pathway impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will linking to the donation widget increase overall donor conversion compared to linking to a separate donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2018 - 03/02/2018
Since the launch of their homepage donation widget (found in the middle of the homepage), Alliance Defending Freedom had seen a significant lift in the amount of revenue that was coming from visitors landing on the homepage. Historically, they had also included a donate link in the banner at the top of the homepage but his button linked to a separate landing page.
We had the hypothesis that linking to the widget may actually cause less friction than linking to a separate page. We decided to test this with one of the monthly appeals.
