experiments.keyLearnings: At the conclusion of this experiment, we saw minimal difference between the overall donations to the two variants. When digging into the details, we found that we were able to increase donations to the widget by 72% but this just shifted donations away from the normal donation page; it did not result in net new donations. This just proved that the jump did not eliminate friction within the donation process; it just provides an additional opportunity for giving.

experiments.title: How an additional incentive impacts webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an additional incentive increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2018 - 02/27/2018
For February’s webinar on email acquisition, we wanted to see if offering an additional resource would help incentivize people to signup for the webinar. In this case, we offered the 6 Ways to Grow Your File eBook since it was related to the topic we were covering on the webinar – email acquisition. We simply added an offer box highlighting the fact that when people signed up to attend the webinar they would also get the eBook for the treatment and the control only had who the presenter would be (as usual).
experiments.keyLearnings: The increase was slight and the sample size was too small so we cannot determine if the eBook incentive helped with webinar registrations. Worth testing again in the future.

experiments.title: How copy length in a webinar promotional email affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shorter and to-the-point email copy increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 02/14/2018
In this experiment, we were sending the initial promo for an upcoming webinar. We wondered if a shorter and more straight to the point email would affect webinar registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment made no significant difference in webinar registrations. What this means moving forward is that we don’t always have to write a long-narrative email to get webinar registrations. It’s possible to convey enough of the value proposition in a shorter email to move motivated people to the landing page to register.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition on the primary donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding value proposition to the primary donation page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2018 - 02/27/2018
In an effort to increase donor conversion on the Focus on the Family primary donation page, we hypothesized that adding value proposition to it would increase donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we developed a value proposition for the page that communicated why a person should give to Focus and what a person’s gift would go towards. We then tested this treatment page against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for four weeks, we turned it off because we were seeing minimal impact at a low level of confidence. Because the main donation page has high motivation of people, the additional value proposition isn’t impacting conversion.
When we looked at conversion based on specific devices, we noticed on mobile that the updated value proposition had a negative conversion. We would hypothesize this is due to the additional length that came with the copy on the treatment.
We propose continuing to test the value proposition on the primary donation page, but decreasing the length of the copy on the treatment. This would help to validate the hypothesis on copy length and the impact a strong value proposition can have.

experiments.title: How including the brand name impacts email opens
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the organization’s name in the sender information increase email opens?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2018
As part of a stewardship series for recent donors, Buckner was sending an email that included some stories of people that were impacted by the ministries. This email was going to donors from the last 12 months. We decided to run a quick test to see if including the organization’s name in the sender information would increase brand familiarity and, thus, boost open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no statistically significant difference in the sender that incorporated “Buckner International” into the sender. This tells us the brand doesn’t necessarily boost engagement but it also doesn’t get in the way.
We always strive to humanize the marketing emails that get sent out but, at the same time, we have to try different techniques to increase subscriber engagement. That is why experiments like this are important with “minor” sends like this. We can use these messages to determine what works best for the audience and can then apply it to more critical emails like those that are a part of fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding an acquisition opportunity to a homepage affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Is a sticky bar or an exit intent more effective in acquiring names on a website?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/20/2018
The Missionary Oblates were wanting to take advantage of the traffic to their website as a channel for email acquisition. To test the most effective way to do this, they tried two options for popups: an exit intent and a sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low traffic and conversion rate, this test won’t validate. We recommend putting one of these acquisition opportunities on the website since there currently isn’t an opportunity to turn site traffic into names and potentially donors for the organization.

experiments.title: How expanding a sub-brand’s value proposition to include the broader ministry brand impacts conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase conversion by expanding the value proposition on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2018 - 02/20/2018
On the Plugged In donation page we hypothesized that expanding the value proposition might help increase conversion on the page. We proposed testing two versions of the value proposition on the donation page; the current Plugged In donation page focuses on that particular sub-brand and the impact the program has. As a treatment, we developed a message around the broader Focus on the Family brand and value proposition. We then included language about Plugged In in that treatment copy but only as evidentials for how Focus is accomplishing the broader mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we weren’t able reach a significant level of confidence with the results (20%). We had trouble validating this experiment, not due to the sample size, but because the conversion rate on the page is so low. This tell us that the people coming to this page are not coming to give or are ready to give. No amount of value proposition will help increase the conversion rate on this specific page when we are asking for a gift. Instead, we need to be testing something higher in the conversion funnel. We propose testing a content offer on this page that then leads into a soft donation ask.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad’s inline call-to-action impacted results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action earlier in the ad increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2018 - 02/27/2018
While running ads for an email acquisition campaign for Alliance Defending Freedom, we wanted to see if we could increase our acquisition by adding a call to action link earlier in the Facebook ad. Normally, a FB ad would only have a button and an image that are linked but it is possible to add another link in the text above if it matches the same location that the other areas go to.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no difference between the two treatments when it comes to the number of emails acquired. We also analyzed the clicks and while the ad with an inline call to action did get a few more visitors, they did not convert on the page. This would indicate that making the call to action available earlier in the process only drove visitors without the proper motivation.

experiments.title: How “minor mystery” language in a headline affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will “minor mystery” language in a headline affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2018 - 01/14/2018
CaringBridge had run extensive testing on their “tribute widget”, where the majority of donations on their site begin. They wanted to run a headline test, with the knowledge that such a slight change might not produce any noticeable effect. They wanted to test the angle of a “minor mystery”, which had proven in other experiments to increase engagement (and subsequently conversion) by provoking the reader that they might not know something.
The headline on the tribute widget asked a simple question: Can you help power [tribute name]’s site? They proposed a test to the headline to introduce the “minor mystery”: Did you know you can help power [tribute name]’s site?
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant difference in the two headlines. This means that the next experiment needs to be more radical in nature to affect results.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a donation page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a premium to a donation page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2017 - 12/12/2017
NBCF was applying learnings from their previous premium tests and wanted to test a more focused experiment on the premium’s perceived effect on instant donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although this experiment did not validate, the 102% increase in donor conversion was high enough to implement this across all content offers.

experiments.title: How adding more text links affects clickthrough rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more text links increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2018 - 02/07/2018
In this experiment, we were getting ready to send a research update email featuring recent content and learnings. Our typical email includes only raw url links, and focuses in a select few pieces of content. We wondered if adding additional text links, and more in line opportunities to view content could increase total clicks from the email to the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although it didn’t reach the level of confidence we would like, the treatment using more text links was leaning towards the winner with a 14.2% increase in clicks. This indicates that we can drive more traffic to our website from email marketing when we included additional links and more varied content.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts call volume
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase calls?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2017 - 01/25/2018
One of the top webpages for the Pregnancy Decision Line is the “Types of Abortion” page. We hypothesized that a radical redesign of this page – removing the header image, adding value proposition to the headline and body copy and connecting personally with the end-reader through the tone of the copy – would motivate more people to call, which is the primary objective of the page. They split the traffic coming to this page and tested the two against each other
experiments.keyLearnings: After six weeks of testing this page, we weren’t able to validate the experiment. There was a slight decrease in calls from the treatment, but because of the test’s low level of confidence we aren’t able to pull any learnings from the experiment or know with certainty that the radical would decrease call volume. We will propose a different angle in testing to better understand the motivation of the end-user.

experiments.title: How asking for a recurring gift vs. a single gift affects conversion in a “Dear Reader” experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for a recurring donation affect conversion in a Dear Reader experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/25/2018
Through previous testing, Care Net found that by inserting a “Dear Reader” feature at the end of their blog posts, they were able to increase the number of people to their donation page. During the month of January, Care Net made an asserted effort to acquire more recurring donors. To support this effort, they tested adding a recurring focused ask in the “Dear Reader” feature instead of a one-time donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: You can see in the data that we weren’t able to validate the experiment in the short time that we ran it. We needed to turn off this experiment earlier than planned resulting in a small sample size. Because the Dear Reader feature gets more people to the donation page, we recommend testing this recurring gift focus again because of the potential revenue impact it could have.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn’t significantly impact conversion – positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.
You can see this fluctuation in the graph below where the lines cross paths multiple times.
This learning is valuable to know because of the cost associated with having the exit-intent pop-up on the site. Even though the test didn’t validate, we would recommend removing the exit-intent pop-up all together from the primary donation page – both during high urgency campaigns like year-end and during regular times of the year when motivation is neutralized.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts name conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2018
When evaluating the performance of the Pro-Life 101 online course, Care Net flagged the acquisition page as the biggest opportunity for optimization in the funnel based off of it’s conversion rate. They had been running ads that featured an image of a family. This same image was also featured on their acquisition page. After testing different images with their Facebook ads, we found that an image of a fetus increased click-through on the ads and ultimately people signing up for the course. They hypothesized that creating a radical redesign of the acquisition page featuring the same image of the fetus from the Facebook ad, changing and strengthening the value proposition and using testimonials of people who had already taken the course might help increase conversion. They A/B split the traffic coming to the page and tested this radical redesign.
experiments.keyLearnings: After the running the experiment for over a month and only reaching a 20%, there wasn’t a significant difference between the control and treatment. This suggests that a radical redesign of the page doesn’t impact conversion. That said, because the test had low power as well, we would need a much larger sample size the validate that claim. We propose testing more specific elements on the page to both increase conversion and understand the audience better.

experiments.title: How putting a countdown clock in the body copy of an email appeal impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a countdown clock increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2017 - 01/31/2018
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase motivation of their email file to click through to their donation page towards the end of the campaign by increasing urgency in the email appeal. To do this, they inserted an animated countdown clock in the body of their email copy and tested this against the control version that didn’t have the clock. The clock counted down to the end of the year and informed the end reader that their time was running out to make their year-end donation. All other copy remained the same. They split their file in half and tested this approach
experiments.keyLearnings: White we weren’t able to validate the experiment, there was a definite directional lift with the treatment of 54%. By adding a countdown clock to the copy, we were able to increase urgency to the message and motivate more people to click-through to the donation page. This tactic will be implemented moving forward for high-urgency campaigns in an effort to not only get significantly more people to click through to the donation page, but potentially give to their organization.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2017 - 10/23/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How adding a third party testimonial to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a testimonial from a well-known figure to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
Heartlight Ministries launched an offer called When Your Teen is Struggling, and wanted to test whether including a testimonial from a well-known Christian author, Joe White, would increase conversion on the landing page. The rest of the copy and elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The decrease of 4% on the treatment page was not statistically valid, but it’s possible that the placement of the testimonial distracted people’s eyes from the line of copy, or that the author of the quote was not influential enough to have a strong effect on conversion rate.
This concept would need to be tested again to be a statistically valid learning, and might be more impactful if multiple statements were used.

experiments.title: How hyperlink text color affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: In this experiment, we asked how the color of a hyperlink text in an email affects click-through rate? Our hypothesis was that changing the color from the traditional blue to red would grab the reader’s attention and increase incentive with a more urgent feeling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2017 - 08/09/2017
A study we saw indicated that changing the hyperlink text color from the traditional blue to red increased the click-through rate of the email by 52%. So we decided to test this on one of our small contact list chase emails.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate due to a minimal lift and the small sample size of the test. This does not give us confidence to change all hyperlink text colors going forward.

experiments.title: How the method of ad optimization impact offline conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Is it better for donors conversion to get more people to see the brand ads or is it better to get less visibility but more engagement with the ads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/20/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to increase the results in their direct mail program during the calendar year end push. One way they did this was by incorporating online brand advertising to the recipients of their direct mail letters. The donors and prospects were shown a series of brand ads that linked to select blog posts on the organization’s website. This methodology was based on past experiments where we found that brand advertising can influence offline behaviors.
For this campaign, we wanted to discover the most impactful method for displaying the ads. In Facebook’s platform, we have the ability to optimize the ad campaign for a handful of different metrics. In this situation, we wanted to know if it was better to optimize for impressions (i.e. get more people to see the ads) or for clicks (i.e. get more people to engage with the ads).
To determine this, we split randomly split the direct mail file and setups two ad campaigns that differed only in how Facebook was optimizing the ad spend.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Once the ads had finished running, we found that we were able to get several hundred more donors and prospects to click on the ad when optimizing for clicks and several thousand more ad impressions when optimizing for that metric. Despite these differences, we found that there was no statistically significant difference in performance between the two optimization methodologies. We also evaluated the segments on revenue and saw the same results.
It would seem that both methods of optimization allowed enough donors to see the ads to have the same impact on conversion.

experiments.title: How a video screenshot versus a raw link affects click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video screenshot or a raw link increase click rate in an email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2018
National Breast Cancer Foundation was running their year-end campaign, and wanted to test whether more people would click a screenshot of a video or the raw Youtube link of the same video. The rest of the email was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The raw link decreased clicks by 7%, but the test was not statistically valid. However, it’s likely that the image of the video supported the value proposition of the email more than the raw link with a line of copy before it. The image reinforced the emotional connection that people have with women in their lives.

experiments.title: How removing an extra decision point in an email affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing an extra decision point in the email increase webinar registration?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2018
In this experiment, we were promoting a new webinar on donor cultivation. The control version of the email asked a question in the middle of the copy before introducing the the actual call-to-action. We hypothesized that this question would create an extra decision point – forcing readers to say “yes” or “no” before actually presenting them with the call-to-action.
We thought that by removing this question, we might see an increase in webinar registration.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although we saw a 3.4% increase in conversion rate, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence for this to be definitive. It’s possible that this result could hold true, but we would need a much larger volume in order to validate this result.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock in an email appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a countdown clock in an email appeal impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2017 - 06/20/2017
While the experiment didn’t validate, the results were leaning towards being valid.
The treatment with the countdown clock decreased clicks by 5% and donations by 13%. What this initial data tells us is that the presence of the countdown clock didn’t motivate more people to convert, but possibly created friction in the giving process. There may have been a disconnect between the countdown clock counting down to Harvest America and the call-to-action of the email – which was to give to the fundraising campaign. The clock may also have taken away from the more casual, personal style and tone of the email that we know donors respond best to.
experiments.keyLearnings: Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency fundraising campaign prior to their  large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. They sent a series of emails to their house file to acquire donations and revenue. In an effort to increase clicks and donations from their email, they added a countdown clock in their email counting down to the end of the campaign. This element was added to create a greater sense of urgency and motivate more people to click-through and give. They split their email file and sent the control email without the countdown clock and the treatment email with the countdown clock.

experiments.title: How contextualization of a premium offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will contextualizing the premium offer increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was promoting their midmonth premium offer, which was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The landing page contained the heart of the value proposition and the call-to-action to get the book, which was divided from the rest of the copy by a horizontal line and a subheadline.
We hypothesized that the offer would be better contextualized if it was integrated into the copy to provide a seamless reading experience and decrease friction on the page. We created a treatment and ran an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The contextualized ask appeared to produce a 10.2% lift, but the experiment was launched late in the appeal and did not receive enough traffic to generate a statistically valid result. We will re-test this in the next premium appeal.

experiments.title: How adding a signer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing branding and adding a picture and signature increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2015 - 12/10/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium.
The control email was branded with a logo at the top and had no signature. They hypothesized that removing the logo to make the email more personal and adding a picture and signature would increase motivation and decrease anxiety, leading to an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Even though the email was more personal, there was no valid difference in conversion rate for the two emails. However, a closer look shows that the placement of the signature might have affected the results. Since a signature generally indicates the end of a letter, this would necessitate a second test where the signature was placed after the call-to-action button. Additionally, it might increase conversion to add the signer’s printed name and title as well.

experiments.title: How year-end messaging and urgency indicators affect donation page revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will specific year-end messaging and urgency indicators affect donation page revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Compassion International wanted to run a test on their main donation page at the end of year. Typically, people came to this page to make year-end gifts, with traffic and revenue ramping up sharply in December. However, the copy on the page remained the same all year, with nothing special done to reflect the year-end deadline.
They wanted to test copy and urgency indicators to see if they could positively affect motivation and generate more revenue from the page during the last half of December.
They crafted a treatment page with a new headline: “Make your year end donation today”. They added copy that referenced the December 31st deadline, added a story to show the impact of the donation, and added a countdown clock to visually show urgency in the sidebar.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The year-end treatment page showed a directional lift in revenue (14.1% increase), but it was unable to achieve statistical validity. This outlines the difficulty of testing at a time of the year where there is already high motivation. The treatment page might produce a lift, but because conversion is higher than normal on the control, there wasn’t enough separation to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How instrumentation error affects a donation button test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donate page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2015 - 11/18/2015
The Hillsdale online courses donation page currently averages a conversion rate of ~40%. While it is exciting to have this high of a conversion rate, what this implied to us is that we are only sending the most highly motivated visitors to the page.  As a result, we wanted to move our testing to earlier in the donor funnel to see if we could drive an increased volume of traffic to the donation page.
We decided to run a test on the donation button that is displayed across the online courses website. The current button uses “Donate” as the call to action and the color blends into the background a bit. We had the hypothesis that we could improve the click rate by making the button stand out more and changing the CTA to be a bit softer of language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test are invalid due to instrumentation error. It would appear that the VWO snippet was removed from many of the pages and blocked from several others.

experiments.title: How the length of copy affects donor conversion at the end of a money bomb campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the length of copy impact the donor conversion of a money bomb as it grows closer to the end of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2015 - 11/01/2015
As we grew closer to the end of the September money bomb campaign with Hillsdale College, we wanted to find a way to increase the conversion rate of the page. Our preview experiments had failed to move the needle in either direction so we decided to try a new tactic. We had the hypothesis that donors would be more highly motivated as the campaign deadline grew closer. As a result, we hypothesized that these donors would not need as much value proposition copy to convince them to make a gift.  We decided to create a treatment to reduct the amount of copy on the page in an effort to reduce the associated friction that comes with long copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter copy did not produce any significant impact on a donor’s likelihood to make a gift. We were able to reduce the length of the page but it was not significant enough to really impact the amount of mental friction associated with the length.

experiments.title: How personalizing the donation page headline affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the headline to include the potential donor’s name increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2016 - 08/02/2016
In an experiment with another NextAfter partner, we had found that personalizing the headline of a donation page increased the likelihood to give by 20%. With Con101 being the biggest source of acquisition for Hillsdale, we wanted to know if the same change would have the same level of impact for them. We set up a test where the only change on the page was customizing the headline to include the potential donor’s first name.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 2 months of running the test, we did not see any significant lift to revenue or conversion rate when we personalized the donation page headline. This is another good example that there is no such thing as “best practices” in the nonprofit world. What works for one organization is not necessarily applicable to everyone. This is why we must test each and every hypothesis to ensure and changes have the intended impact.

experiments.title: How donation page design affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the new landing page design affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/24/2016
Hillsdale College had recently redesigned their main website. They wanted to apply the new design, which was simpler and used new fonts, to the donation page. But they understood the importance of testing this first, and set up a test between the control design and the new design. The content on the pages stayed the same, which meant that this experiment was really to validate that there was no difference between the two.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically valid movement either way. In this case, it was a good thing — it meant that the new design didn’t affect donor conversion either way. Though we always like to see the big green arrow with the win, this is a critically important step that many organizations miss when they choose to launch a new design without validating the impact.

experiments.title: How a year-end premium affects conversion, average gift, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of a premium at year-end increase conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2015 - 12/26/2015
Hillsdale College was in the middle of their year-end fundraising campaign. A key part of the campaign was a survey about their flagship publication Imprimis, which ended with a donation ask. They were interested to see the impact of adding a premium (the Hillsdale calendar) to this message.
Premiums had proven to work in the past, but motivation is often much higher at the end of the year. They launched a test to see the impact of the premium on conversion and average gift, using “revenue per visitor” as the success metric.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there appeared to be a slight directional lift from the premium, it was not statistically significant and did not reach a valid level of confidence. This tells us that year-end might not be the right time to offer a premium — or the calendar was the wrong premium to add.
This will lead to further premium testing throughout the year to see if it can lift conversion during times where visitors might be less motivated to give.

experiments.title: How an additional priming question in a survey affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an additional priming question in a survey affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2017 - 12/27/2017
Hillsdale College sent a survey to donors during their year-end campaign that was designed to prime them for a gift to support Hillsdale as an independent educational institution that did not accept any federal funds. The survey guided the reader’s thought pattern to contrast Hillsdale with the vast majority of schools that are reliant on federal funds. It also made the case that
The team had an additional question at the end of the survey to directly ask for a donation within the survey process—”Will you stand with Hillsdale and make a tax-deductible gift today to help secure Hillsdale’s independence?”. They wondered if removing this would result in an increase in donations, as it didn’t tip the hand about what happened next—or, if this actually was a catalyst in post-survey donations.
They created a treatment that removed this final question (which also shortened the quiz by one question) to see how donor conversion was affected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The removal of the final question had no significant impact on donor conversion. This suggests a few things—that donors were already motivated to make a gift coming into the survey, and the direct ask did not increase that motivation, or that the initial questions were the actual motivating factors, and the final question had little to do with it.
Surveys have proven to be a powerful motivator for donation asks. This experiment prompts future testing for additional learnings.

experiments.title: How an optional phone number field impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the optional phone number field increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2017 - 02/08/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for ways to continue to improve their donation page. One of the factors that potentially hindered donor conversion is the friction associated with filling out the donation form. In an effort to reduce that friction, we decided to see if removing the phone number field would increase the likelihood of a visitor to make a gift. This field was already optional but the hypothesis was that by simply having this field as an option, it created mental friction in the mind of the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two weeks, we were able to determine that the phone number field did not add a significant level of friction to the donation process. This is actually great news for the college since they are starting up a phone program and collecting donor phone numbers is now becoming a priority. It is good to know that donor conversion does not suffer by simply having it available.

experiments.title: How varying types of third party credibility affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will student quotes outperform a endorsement from a former president?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2017 - 04/06/2017
Hillsdale launched a course designed to give students a better understanding of the role of the Executive Branch. Previous optimization efforts from a similar test on a different course had already shown that student validation outperformed the presence of a course schedule by 20.5%.  They were interested in discovering how student quotes as third-party validation would perform against an endorsement from former President Ronald Reagan. They hypothesized that student quotes would outperform an endorsement from a former president because it was more personal, experienced-based, and spoke from the point of view of the page visitor.
They launched an A/B test to find out which treatment produced the highest email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were statistically insignificant. Even though the treatment with student quotes increased conversion by a 2%, the test could not be validated, which showed Hillsdale that prospects were not heavily influenced by varying types of third party validation. This prompted a reassessment of which page elements should be further tested.

experiments.title: How a declarative value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a declarative value proposition produce an increase in donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2017 - 05/03/2017
Hillsdale College’s popular “Constitution 101” course had a donation page that had been through many iterative tests to optimize conversion. The control version (at the time) proposed a question to the reader:
We are often asked: Why does Hillsdale offer this course for free?
The copy then answered this question through three bulleted blurbs. Hillsdale’s team wondered if the rhetorical question-and-answer method might not properly motivate visitors to read through the copy. They developed a treatment that declared why Hillsdale taught the course for free, rather than proposing the question and answering it.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a slight lift, but was very far from statistical validity. This showed that there wasn’t any discernible difference at all between the two versions, and prompted more radical testing. It also might indicate that the motivation of the donor was high enough that the different styles of copy didn’t affect their decision to give one way or the other.

experiments.title: How an empty thermometer affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an empty thermometer affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2016 - 12/15/2016
Hillsdale College started their calendar year-end campaign with the intent to launch a thermometer on their donation page once they achieved a critical mass of donations toward their goal. Previous testing had shown that the ideal time to introduce a thermometer to maximize conversion was when the goal was at least halfway met. However, the team wondered if putting the empty thermometer on the page would disincentivize visitors from giving.
They launched a treatment with the empty thermometer and closely monitored the results to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The page with the empty thermometer failed to reach statistical validity, which showed that it did not disincentivize donors from giving. However, Hillsdale chose to remove the empty thermometer from the page to maximize the “reveal” of the thermometer once significant progress had been made toward the goal. This result may have been clouded by the fact that these donors were the first to respond to the campaign, and tended to have the highest motivation.

experiments.title: How Premium-Focused Copy Affects Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a premium-focused “Constitution 101” donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2017 - 08/31/2017
Hillsdale College has a premium offer for their most popular online course, “Constitution 101.” Everyone who donates  $100 or more receives a free copy of The U.S. Constitution: A Reader. They wondered if donor conversion would increase if they focused more heavily on The U.S. Constitution: A Reader and it’s benefits to the student. They created and launched a treatment donation page that focused more on the premium to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale found no statistical difference from their control and treatment donation pages for this test. However, it appears the more premium-focused treatment page was trending towards a decrease in donor conversion. Though this is not statistically valid, we can consider a few reasons why this may be the case.
It’s possible that visitors to this page experienced a decrease in motivation due to the transactional nature of the treatment page. Though the value proposition of Hillsdale College is still present, the treatment page focuses heavily on the content and benefits of the The U.S. Constitution: A Reader instead of the actual course itself. This may have muddied the true value proposition of Hillsdale College and their free online courses. However, further testing is required to validate that hypothesis.

experiments.title: How the Favicon impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a favicon increase email acquisition rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 10/26/2017
We had found in a previous experiment that added a favicon (the little image seen in the browser tabs) increased email acquisition. With this being such a minor factor in the page, we wanted to revalidate this experiment to see if this same result would hold up for other pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With this experiment, we did not see a statistically significant difference between the control and the treatment. Logically, this was our expected result given how minor the change was in the scheme of the page. In all likelihood, the last experiment that targeted the favicon fell into to the experiments that produced a false positive.
When we seek to achieve a 95% level of confidence, what this means is that there is a 5% chance the same effect could occur if both pages were actually the same. This demonstrates the importance of re-testing important test wins where we see a result that is unexpected.

experiments.title: How hint text forms impact email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of hint text forms increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to increase their online course signup rate for their popular course ‘Western Heritage.’ After reviewing other similar course signup pages, they found that ‘hint text’ forms were a common practice. They wondered: Will using hint text forms on an email acquisition page reduce friction and increase email acquisition?
They launched an A/B test on their ‘Western Heritage’ course signup page to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment with hint text forms did not have a valid impact on email acquisition rate. Therefore, the results of this test are not conclusive. However, we can take away something important from this experiment. It’s important to note that this test didn’t have a significant impact, despite the difference in design. Though most would agree that visually the hint text version is more visually appealing and less cluttered, it ultimately did not impact the visitors motivation to sign up for the course.
Further tests are being conducted on similar course signup pages, in regards to connection between the visual and functional components of these pages.

experiments.title: How premium-specific donation page copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing more specifically on The U.S. Constitution: A Reader increase donor conversion on the ‘Constitution 101 Thank You!’ page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for a new way to enhance the experience of those who sign up to take ‘Constitution 101,’ while increasing donor conversion on the ‘Constitution 101’ donation page.They know that the The U.S. Constitution: A Reader is a helpful guide to students looking to get the most out of this course. They wondered: Can we increase donor conversion by adding more specific language about the benefits of The U.S. Constitution: A Reader. They created a treatment and launched a test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Hillsdale College found that this treatment version did not significantly increase or decrease donor conversion on the ‘Constitution 101’ donation page. It’s apparent that this change in language was not dramatic enough to change the actions of the page visitor. Even though this test wasn’t statistically valid, we can still look at the trend of the test results for clues. We see that the donation page copy that focused more heavily on the premium offer, rather than the value proposition of Hillsdale College, was actually experiencing a lower conversion rate. This is interesting to remember as we develop more tests with donation pages that contain a premium offer.

experiments.title: How simplifying a donation page and removing designations affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will removing designation options and reducing visual friction affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2017 - 12/21/2017
Though Compassion International’s fundraising focus centered around child sponsorships, they do have a general fund that is collected on their donation page. This page offered donors the opportunity to contribute to a wealth of funds—from HIV to education to clean water.
Their team wondered how all of these designation options affected conversion. Did they present too much decision friction? Did they cause donors to think too much about their gift size?
They wanted to test a single donation ask with one place to enter a gift. Their hypothesis was that this would increase conversion by reducing decision friction. At the same time, they wanted to test the impact of reducing visual friction by moving the copy from being overlaid on the image to a white background, reducing the size of the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistical difference between the control and the treatment. This tells us two important facts—removing the list of gift designations didn’t increase giving by reducing decision friction, but it also didn’t decrease giving by offering fewer options to give. Thus, the amount of giving options was neither the problem, nor the solution.
This indicates that the primary factor affecting conversion on this page might not be friction. Instead, this indicates that the value proposition is the element which should be tested and strengthened. This page’s conversion rate of nearly 50% shows that users are quite motivated when they show up. If we can successfully increase the force of the value proposition, then we have a chance to increase that conversion rate.

experiments.title: How clarity of navigation buttons impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shortening the Renew Membership link text in the navigation impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2016 - 02/06/2017
Prior to the launch of the new Heritage homepage, we wanted to test out if the new proposed navigation would have an impact on donor conversion. The navigation was trying to limit the number of words for each link to just one. As a result “Renew Membership” would be shortened to just “Membership.”
Our concern was that this change may decrease the motivation of the people clicking on the link which would, in turn, decrease the total donor conversion from the homepage. We set up a test to validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Shortening the navigation link to just one word did not have any significant impact on the overall donor conversion of visitor coming from the home page. We did see a 53% increase in traffic from the shortened link but since these were less motivated visitors, the additional traffic did not impact the conversion in either direction.
The good news is that it does not appear that changing the navigation will significantly impact overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the gift array options impacted Leaders Club memberships
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a lower gift array of $3, $10, & $15 increase the conversion rate and overall revenue to the Leaders Club program?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 11/10/2016
For a recent Leaders Club campaign, we wanted to test the standard gift array of $5, $15, & $25 to see if we could lower the mental friction associated with the high monthly ask. Our hypothesis was that if we can reduce the initial ask amount, we could get more donors which would lift the overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: The lower gift array did not have any statistically significant impact on a donor’s likelihood to join the Leaders Club or their gift amount once they joined. It is recommended that we maintain the current control of $5, $10, and $15 for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How the style of donation form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a traditional form design increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2017 - 03/28/2017
The Heritage Foundation underwent a website redesign for their primary website, Heritage.org. In order to maintain visual congruence, we updated the design of the donation forms to match the look and feel of the new site. Part of that new design incorporated a more modern style of form fields and buttons.
There was concern that this new style for form fields may cause confusion for older Heritage donor base. We decided to test these form elements against the more commonly used designs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon these results, the design of the form fields did not have any significant impact on donor conversion. It would seem that the more modern style of form fields were clear enough that they did not cause any friction or anxiety.

experiments.title: How visually highlighting security impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will visually highlighting the security of the credit card section reduce anxiety enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2017 - 02/08/2017
Based upon experiment results from similar organizations, we decided to launch an experiment across the primary donation forms for Heritage to see if visually reinforcing the security of the credit card form was able to lift donor conversion. The hypothesis was that by visually separating the form from the rest of the page, we could reduce the anxiety associate with entering a credit card online.
experiments.keyLearnings: This change did not seem to have any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a gift. It may be possible to increase the visibility and the language around the security but, given this experiment result, it does not seem like it would be significant lift even with those changes. Given the current schedule of experiments, we feel that we would be better spent testing new technologies and radical changes than iterating on this concept.

experiments.title: How the primary donation value proposition is impacted by relevance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will produce the greatest donor conversion for Daily Signal readers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2016 - 12/15/2016
Our last test on The Daily Signal found that a value proposition that talked about the quality of journalism produced by TDS was the highest performing with regards to donor conversion. To continue that line of testing, we wanted to incorporate the relevant news of Donald Trump’s election as president and Heritage’s role in that transition.
With that in mind, we rolled out the Journalism value proposition as the winner and created a new treatment around the Donald Trump transition plan.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not produce a significant lift for either treatment. Our hypothesis is that these results are less about the value proposition itself and more about the tactic used to deliver the language. Including this snippet of text at the bottom of each article results in it not getting seen by a large proportion of the audience and it may not be standing out. Subsequent tests will focus on the tactic used to deliver the message.

experiments.title: How a homepage donation ask impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition and additional calls to action to the homepage increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2017 - 03/16/2017
At the launch of the new Heritage.org redesign, we wanted to find a way to add emphasis to the Donate and Renew buttons that were commonly found in the navigation. The belief was that the navigation would be too hard to find which would result in a reduction in donors coming from the homepage.
To alleviate this potential drop in traffic, we created a treatment that overlaid a donation ask with value proposition copy on the primary banner. The goal was to create an easier to find pathway for visitors to give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The additional callout and buttons were able to drive 20% more traffic to the various donation pages. However, the increased traffic did not statistically increase the overall donor conversion on those pages.

experiments.title: How addressing High-C subscriber in their style of communication impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will speaking to subscribers in their preferred DISC style increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2017 - 08/11/2017
In a follow up experiment during the 2017 Board Match, we wanted to test writing an appeal email with associated landing page in the style of a high “C” DISC profile.
Using the data that we appended from the CrystalKnows website, we segmented the subscriber list by both donor status and primary disc style. We then took the Control version of the email and landing page (written in the I style) and recreated it in the style of a high C.
experiments.keyLearnings: The high-C treatment showed no significant difference in conversion rates between the control and treatment. Interestingly, the treatment which was written in the high-C format, decreased the conversion rate for the high-I group by 56%. It had no significant impact on the other groups.

experiments.title: How a phone number field on a donation form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a phone number field impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2017 - 10/31/2017
The Heritage Foundation had started using phone numbers to reach out and thank their donors for their gifts. Historically, the phone number was something that only came from direct mail donors. In order to increase the number of donors that had an available phone number on file, we wanted to see if adding a phone number field to the donation form would have an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, the insignificant result was exactly what we were hoping for. This proves that there is no significant impact to conversion by adding a phone number field and making it optional. Since it doesn’t impact conversion and provides us with additional data that will be helpful downstream, this has since been rolled out to all donation forms.

experiments.title: How the membership offer impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a membership offer increase donor conversion on those that just subscribed to The Agenda?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2017 - 11/22/2017
In a recent meta-analysis, we discovered that when it comes to new donor acquisition, the membership offer generally outperforms a straight donation ask. We had recently seen success promoting a subscription to The Agenda but it was using a direct ask on the confirmation page. We decided to see if a membership offer would be right for this audience too.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant level of improvement with the membership offer. While there was a slight improvement in performance, the conversion rate and traffic levels were too low to statistically validate. This speaks to the low donor motivation of this audience and the need to shift efforts to boosting traffic by optimizing the earlier steps in the funnel.

experiments.title: How a Facebook headline affects direct-ask donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the headline ‘Donate now’ or ‘Become a member’ drive more donor conversions on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 12/12/2017
The Heritage Foundation noticed an opportunity on Facebook to retarget visitors who viewed their online donation pages. They created to direct ask Facebook ads that invited anyone who visited the page but did not complete the form to return and support the Heritage Foundation. In the first ad, they simply asked all visitors (including members who visited the page) to ‘Donate now.’ In the second ad, the asked all non-member visitors to join the Heritage Foundation by ‘Becoming a member today.’ They launched a test to find out which approach would be more effective at converting donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: They found that that the results were inconclusive. Due to a low overall donor conversion rate, The Heritage Foundation decided to pause this campaign and assess the results. The first ad did receive 1 donation, but this was not enough to validate this approach over the other. Using the urgency of the calendar year, They decided to launch and run a similar test that is directly tied to their CYE theme.

experiments.title: How the order of a donation ask impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What type of offer will lead to the greatest return in terms of email subscribers and donations acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 09/05/2017
In a previous test, we had found that an inline donation ask, was able to increase the number of donations that were received from readers of Heritage.org articles. However, despite the percentage increase, the total number of donations was still not very high.
We had the hypothesis that this audience might not yet be ready for a donation ask. Instead, we wanted to see we could get significantly more email addresses that would outweigh the immediate value received from the donations. We would set up the email acquisition offer to also have an instant donation ask for immediate conversion and we also know that we can convert 1% to 5% of all emails acquired downstream. Based upon these factors, we wanted to see if offering an email acquisition offer before the donation ask would achieve better long-term results.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that there was no statistically significant difference in donations between the two treatments. However, during this same period, the email offer also collected nearly 1,000 new subscribers for the house email file. Given the long term conversion that is likely to happen with these new subscribers, this makes the email acquisition offer a clear winner.

experiments.title: How membership benefits impact instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will membership benefits increase donor conversion for signers of the petition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2016 - 05/03/2016
For the instant donor conversion page that followed the petition against nominating a Supreme Court justice, we wanted to determine if membership benefits would be a strong incentive that could increase the overall donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we did not find that membership benefits had any significant impact on donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that this is the result of a low motivated audience; not that the offer did not work. We have found the petition signers do not make the best donor prospects given the low barrier to entry onto the file.

experiments.title: How a membership offer impacted instant donor conversion on a premium donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing membership language on an instant donor conversion page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2016 - 06/10/2016
On the instant donor conversion page for the email acquisition offer “Guide to the Constitution,” we wanted to test whether the membership language was creating significant enough friction to the process to be negatively impacting conversion. We decided to test removing this language to see what kind of impact it would have on the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for one month, we did not see a significant difference from removing the membership benefits language. In this situation, it is our hypothesis that the value added by the membership was equal to the friction associated with the additional copy. In future tests, we should try testing a shorter version of the membership information to see if it has the same impact.

experiments.title: How a button to receive the “incentive” impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “accepting the challenge” button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2016 - 07/28/2016
During the 2016 Heritage Board Challenge moneybomb, we wanted to test a tactic that we had observed on several large ecommerce sites. In these sites, the visitors had to click a button to receive an incentive. Our belief was that this action increased the perceived value of the offer that they were receiving which would positively impact conversion.
During the Board Challenge, the incentive is the match from the board. We created a button above the donation form that was a way for the visitor to “Accept the challenge.” Once pressed, it told them their gift would be doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant impact from the button after running for a full week. This would indicate that it did not increased the perceived value of the matching incentive as we hypothesized that it would.

experiments.title: How a donation ask based upon the content of the article impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the content of the ask to the content of the article increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2017 - 09/11/2017
The “Dear Reader” ask had become a significant source of revenue for The Heritage Foundation. Over a span of a year, we had tested a variety of aspects include positioning, aligning with year end, and individual value props. However, with the diversity of content on The Daily Signal, we had the hypothesis that if we aligned the content of the ask to the content of the article, we could increase the relevance of the ask which would impact donor conversion. We decided to test this out by writing 8 different value propositions that would be shown based upon the article the visitor was reading.
experiments.keyLearnings: After over a month of collecting data, we did not see any statistically significant impact come from targeting the content of the ask to the content of the article. This mirrors the findings that we had seen with similar tests run on the Heritage.org homepage when we tried to align the message to popular topics in the news. In those instances, the topical asks either had negative or no impact on donor conversion when compared to the standard organizational value proposition.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock added to an email appeal impacts click through
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of a countdown clock in an email help get more people to click through to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 12/31/2016
In the final days of a year-end campaign, urgency plays a key role in people making gifts to an organization. On the second to last day of NBCF’s year-end campaign, they hypothesized that they could increase the urgency of the ask by adding a countdown clock to their emails. They developed a treatment that had a countdown clock added to the top of the email copy and test this against their control that didn’t have the countdown clock on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Because NBCF’s email file is still in it’s infancy, there was low engagement and we weren’t able to validate the results. From the data that we were able to collect, the addition of the countdown clock received less clicks than the control that didn’t have the countdown clock.
We recommend testing this approach again once the email file has been cultivated more and there is a large enough sample size.

experiments.title: How donor-centric copy affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donor-centric copy increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2016 - 10/14/2016
The National Breast Cancer Foundation was giving away a free eBook, What Every Woman Needs To Know during Breast Cancer Awareness Month. They featured this eBook on their Breast Cancer Awareness Month page, with a form right on the page to download it.
The team at NBCF hypothesized that using copy that addressed the reader directly would increase email acquisition rate. They created a treatment that personalized each element: rather than “Get the free breast health guide”, the treatment offered the visitor the chance to “Get your free breast health guide”. Additionally, they revised the paragraph copy to show how the content of the eBook allows the visitor to be proactive about their own health. Finally, they added the value proposition element of “free” into the call-to-action.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced no statistical lift or decrease in email acquisition—in short, it made no difference. This revealed a few key insights:
It’s possible that visitors who were going to download this eBook were highly motivated and the slight changes in copy were not enough to affect that initial motivation.
It’s possible that the copy changes were too slight, and need a more radical treatment.
The copy may not be the primary factor affecting email acquisition—it may be location on the page.
This prompts future tests to see how the eBook can be better leveraged for name acquisition.

experiments.title: How showing impact of a gift on a donation page affects revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the layout of the donation page affect donor conversion, and revenue per donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2017 - 12/05/2017
Buckner International wanted to increase instant donations on their 7 Scriptures acquisition offer, and wanted to test how redesigning the donation page would affect donor conversion- and also if people would give bigger gifts if they more clearly understood the impact.
The elements tested were the design of the confirmation of offer download and how it flowed into the body copy, as well as a rewritten headline and some body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment did not validate at a level of confidence of 95%, we saw a trend towards higher donor conversion and higher revenue per visitor with the redesigned page. This is likely due to a more user-friendly format as well as a clearer value proposition in the headline and body copy on the page.

experiments.title: How the cover artwork for an eBook offer impacts name conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which eBook cover would be more appealing to people and acquire more names?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2017 - 03/14/2017
The Missionary Oblates set up an acquisition offer for Lent. They were offering people an eBook of Lenten Prayers. The eBook was a resource they already had created, but we hypothesized that something visually brighter and more modern might be more appealing and potentially acquire more names. To test this hypothesis, we developed a new cover for the Lenten Prayers eBook and set up two different Facebook campaigns – one with the original cover that looked more traditional, and one with the new cover that was more modern.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two covers were drastically different in appearance, there was no signifiant difference in conversion. This experiment didn’t validate, but what it tells us is that the cover of the eBook had no impact on name conversion for the offer. This is great insight to have as we continue to launch new offers using content and artwork that is already developed.

experiments.title: How ordering and numbering the content on the donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the simplified donation page acquire more donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2017 - 04/09/2017
The Missionary Oblates developed an Easter Novena as an acquisition offer. Once a person had signed up for this content offer, they were taken to a donation page. The Missionary Oblates hypothesized that they might be able to increase donations on this page if they simplified the content. They developed a treatment that took the same content as the control and ordered/numbered it on the page. They split the traffic coming to this donation page and tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the test didn’t quite validate, the initial results showed that by simplifying the content on the donation page, we were able to increase donations by 52%. What we can learn from this is that we are able to not only simplify the content on a donation page by ordering/numbering it, but we were able to mentally walk a person through the steps that we wanted them to take on the donation page resulting in more people converting as donors!

experiments.title: How length of copy affects conversion rate in a Facebook ad
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does longer, more in-depth copy about an offer in a Facebook ad increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2017 - 06/07/2017
The Missionary Oblates were running a campaign promoting their new email acquisition offer, a Daily Prayer via email. They wanted to test whether a more in-depth value proposition in the ad copy converted more people than shorter, more concise ad copy. All other elements of the ad remained the same.

experiments.title: How a stepped approach on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a stepped approach on a donation page increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2017 - 06/07/2017
Though this experiment did not validate, there was a 26% lift in conversion rate from the stepped approach treatment copy. The stepped approach allows the Oblates to walk the viewer through the value proposition and why they should give to their mission, which increased clarity in what their gift will accomplish.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates launched a 9-day Novena offer for Mother’s Day, and wanted to increase conversion on the donation page. They ran a test to see if a stepped approach performed better than paragraphs in the body copy of the page.

experiments.title: How an additional donation ask impacts revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking for an additional gift right before checkout increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2017 - 05/19/2017
In an effort to optimize the Oblates online store, we had reached out to the store developers, DonorDirect. In our communication with them, we asked about a variety of features that were on our “wish list” of actions we’d like to be able to take. One of these actions was the ability to turn on an additional ask right before checkout. It turns out that not only was this possible, it was easy to do!
Before moving ahead, we wanted to measure the impact this kind of feature would have on giving. We decided to split test visitors to ensure the additional gift would not have any negative impact on overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a full 12 months, we were not able to increase revenue by a statistically significant amount. We did see a directional lift of 16.6% and it did not have any significant impact on the conversion rate. Based upon these results, we have rolled out the feature to the entire audience.
This test taught us to important concepts:
Just because we ask, doesn’t mean people will give. We need to provide a compelling reason to provide an additional gift and we need to make it an easy process to complete.
The motivation of the audience as this stage of checkout is high. The conversion rate of this page is 90% which means we can test out some long, value-heavy copy that may increase the length of the page without having a significant impact on conversion giving the motivation of the audience.

experiments.title: How navigation impact donor conversion rate on landing pages
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing navigational elements increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2017 - 07/18/2017
With the launch of the Oblates Celebrating Mercy online course, we wanted to test out some to the more tactical elements of their donation page. Specifically, we had the hypothesis that the navigational elements that were a part of the standard template were causing significant friction to the donation process. We decided to test removing the elements to see if that increased instant donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month an a half of running, we found that removing the navigation did not have any statistically significant impact on donor conversion. When digging deeper into these results, one of the elements that jumped out at us was the overall low instant donor conversion. Based upon the low rate for both the control and treatment, it would seem that we needed to shift our focus away from reducing friction and begin testing into the right value proposition.

experiments.title: How user experience improvements impacted donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an updated user experience increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2017 - 09/26/2017
In an effort to improve the instant donor conversion on the Missionary Oblate’s Celebrating Mercy online course, we wanted to see if we could improve the overall user experience for the donation page. This involved making it mobile-friendly, removing the navigation on the page, and making the text easier to read and follow.
To validate the impact of this change, we set up a test of this new experience vs. the standard store checkout page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At first glance, it would appear the changes had no statistically significant impact on conversion. However, when we started analyzing the data, we realized this test may have been skewed by some of the technical difficulties experienced during the time period. There was an issue with Facebook that prevented ads from being run for a month and a technical issue that crashed the website for a week.
With the significance of these outside factors, we decided to relaunch this test to see if they had any impact on conversion.

experiments.title: How evidentials of success impacted subscriber click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shorter copy increase the subscriber click through rate to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2016 - 01/12/2017
For the first email in their year end campaign, the Illinois Policy Institute wanted to determine what depth of explanation yielded the highest level of engagement from their subscribers. They set up two different emails, a short and a long version. The long version was nearly identical to the short with the exception of including bullet points in the copy with evidentials of their accomplishments in 2016.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the shorter version did see a 23% increase in the number of clicked, it was not a statistically significant lift. With such a low level of confidence (77%) it would be dangerous to try to make too many assumptions about the impact and results of this experiment.
This test does go a long way to proving that we need to create a more holistic reporting loop so that we can tie donation results back to an individual email. It would be a pretty safe assumption that a shorter email will increase the number of people clicking through to the web page but the behavior on that page is what is important to monitor. If the longer email sends less subscribers with a higher motivation, that may have a better impact on donation results.
For future appeals, it is recommended to utilize UTM tracking to allow attribution back to the individual email.
