experiments.title: How the call-to-action of the button impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call to action increase the motivation and subsequent donor conversion of website visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 04/14/2017
A previous analysis of the Dear Reader experiment found that we were able to drive a significant volume of donor traffic to the donation page but were seeing an 8% conversion of those visitors. Given the low conversion of the traffic, we developed a hypothesis that the call-to-action “Support Illinois Policy Institute” may not be clear enough. We decided to test the language on the button using a more direct “Donate Today” call-to-action. Our hypothesis is that this direct statement would receive less overall traffic but more donor motivated traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 2 weeks, we had not yet reach statistical validity. Based upon the progress of the test, we expected it would take another full month before we would know with certainty the impact of the experiment. We decided to move on to another experiment that we felt could have a higher potential impact and roll out the “Donate Today” language for future iterations.

experiments.title: How an ad’s targeted value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a journalism focused value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2017 - 05/08/2017
In an effort to boost the performance of the existing Dear Reader treatment, we wanted to try a modification of the existing value proposition. The current Control focused on the independent journalism aspect of the organization but also brought in some of the other work being done politically. Our hypothesis was that by focusing solely on the journalism aspect of the work and making the call-to-action softer, we could make the ask more relevant given the delivery mechanism of the test (the news site.)
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, we did not see any statistically significant increase in clicks or donor conversion of either treatment. As a result, we decided to move on from this test.

experiments.title: How a campaign-focused ad impacted donor conversion during a money bomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a specific campaign with highlighted deadline increase conversion rate over the general value proposition offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2017 - 07/03/2017
The short time frame of the experiment with minimal difference in conversion rate did not allow us to reach statistical validity for the experiment. When analyzing the data, we did discover a spike in giving on June 30th for both the control and treatment. It would seem that the campaign had an impact on giving even if visitors didn’t see the campaign-specific treatment. These visitors were likely exposed to the messaging through email which increased their motivation when they came to the site.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the June money bomb campaign, we wanted to see if we could utilize the Dear Reader treatment that is shown on all article pages to drive additional donations for the organization. The hypothesis was that the additional urgency associated with the campaign would increase the likely conversion of the visitors.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page redesign can affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a mission-focused page improve donor conversion against a premium-focused page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2016 - 03/28/2016
Illinois policy Institute was promoting their free e-book about Mike Madigan to several Facebook audiences, and then asking for a donation on the following page. They had a premium-focused page that was not producing donors, and wanted to test a more mission-focused page. They create a treatment that removed the premium (in case that was “getting in the way of the donation”) and focused on the value and the efforts that they are undertaking to remove Mike Madigan from office.
They set up an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The mission focused page failed to produce a donor as well, which fosters a few possible conclusions:
The offer might not create enough perceived value to solicit a donation.
 	The audience might not have enough time to digest the offer before making the decision to make a gift.
 	The offer might be targeted at a less philanthropic audience.

experiments.title: Personal Note email vs. Quick Donate email

rows.name: Urgent message with quick donate links

experiments.researchQuestion: The question we set out to solve was whether or not our control of a personal casual note (in this case a text message) from the CEO outperforms an urgent message with “quick donate” links that include suggested gift amounts starting at $15 going to $300.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2015 - 07/24/2015
We have had much success in email copy tests with person casual tone of voice email being the winner in renewal emails, solicitation emails, and new membership emails. We have also had success with using the “quick donate” tactic on emails that are responses to what’s happening Hill and how Heritage Action is engaging in the fight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We sent two versions of the email as a test. One had a soft ask to find out more. The other had a series of hard asks that said “quick donate $15; quick donate $25; etc”.
As expected, the soft ask sent more than 4x more traffic: 916 visitors vs. 208. But the quick-donate version had a conversion rate nearly 5x higher: 19.7% vs. 4.0%.
Result: more gifts and more revenue from the appeal with dramatically less traffic.
Moving forward we will continue testing the quick donate tactic.

experiments.title: How the length of copy affects the follow on conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will acquire the most donors for a follow on ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2015 - 09/25/2015
One of the best fundraising methods for Heritage Action for America is a follow on ask for Heritage donors. After a Heritage donor has completed their gift, they are prompted with a second ask for their sister organization. Friction for this ask has been dramatically reduced by requiring them to only enter the amount they want to give.
The copy associated with this follow on ask is relatively long since the value proposition changes between the first ask and the second. We had the hypothesis that this longer ask might not be necessary for donors and may actually be hurting our conversion. We decided to simplify the language and test the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After three months of testing, we discovered a handful of learnings.
The first is that we did not have enough traffic to this follow on ask to validate a small change like reducing the copy.  We either needed to expand the area of the website being tested or need to develop a more radical change.
The second is that the friction associated with the length of the copy did not impact the overall conversion of the page.

experiments.title: How refining the value proposition affected the follow-on HAFA gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will produce the best conversion rate for follow-on Heritage Action gifts?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2015 - 11/18/2015
The follow-on ask for Heritage Action that occurred on Heritage forms had become one of the largest sources of gifts for HAFA. In an effort to continue to improve upon the success of the page, we wanted to see if we could refine the stated value proposition. We set up a test across all donation forms so that we could test it with all completed transactions in order to reach validation quicker.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment with a refined value proposition was not able to significantly impact the conversion rate one way or another. With an average conversion rate of ~24%, it would appear that we are going to have to take a more radical approach in order to improve the results.

experiments.title: How a targeted HAFA secondary ask impacted donor response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a HAFA value proposition used in the secondary ask that is targeted to the topic of the specific campaign lift secondary asks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2016 - 11/10/2016
After a gift is made to The Heritage Foundation, the donor is immediately prompted with the opportunity to make a donation to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. Historically, we have used the same copy for the secondary ask on every donation page.
As part of the SCOTUS Money Bomb campaign, we had the opportunity to test a secondary ask for Heritage Action for America that was targeted to the theme of the campaign. Our goal was to determine if it is worth the time and effort to craft a unique value proposition for the HAFA ask for each campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant impact on donor conversion for the targeted secondary ask. It would appear that there was a slight improvement but it would have required significantly more donors to the Heritage campaign before this could be validated. In short, it would seem that it is not worth the time to develop targeted secondary asks for individual campaigns.

experiments.title: How the gift array impact a recurring giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a blank gift array impact average gift and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2017 - 07/19/2017
Unfortunately, with this experiment, we did not see enough conversions to draw any meaningful analysis from the results. However, this does seem like an interesting test given the low average gift for recurring donors. It would be worth repeating with a future campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the Liberty Brigade acquisition campaign, we wanted to see what kind of impact the giving array had on the likelihood to join and the average gift. We had the hypothesis that using a blank array would allow people to “choose their own generosity” which would result in a higher gift.

experiments.title: How the headline value proposition affects acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: By stating what the visitor will “get” within the headline, we can improve the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/14/2014
The audience was already highly motivated and the ads had conveyed what would be received on the subsequent page. As a result, the headlines did not produce a significant lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of the calendar year end campaign, DTS produced an eBook that they gave away free to their supporters. To expand the impact of this free offer, we also created landing pages that were advertised to Facebook audiences. These pages offered the book for the price of an email address.
With the influx of traffic, we used the opportunity to test the impact that a headline can have on conveying the value proposition of the page.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for a year-end donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign impacts conversion on the year-end donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
During the Focus on the Family year-end campaign, we hypothesized that we might be able increase conversion by testing their current year-end donation page with a radical redesign. The radical redesign included removing the video on the page, adding a countdown clock on the page, increasing value proposition and added a giving thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We weren’t able to validate this experiment but initial data shows us that there was no real significant difference between the control and the treatment. With such a low level of confidence we can hypothesize a couple of take aways and next steps:
The more we learn about the Focus on the Family audience, the more we are realizing that this audience has a very different motivation and we must take a different testing approach.
Normally, we can dramatically impact conversion with radical redesigns. But with the Focus on the Family audience, we want to learn more specifically about what impacts donor motivation and conversion. To do this, we need to isolate specific variables in our testing instead of doing radical redesigns.

experiments.title: How adding an incentive to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding an incentive to the donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/16/2017
FamilyLife was running a name acquisition offer that included a summer reading list for dads. They wanted to test if adding an incentive to the donation form would increase donor conversion- in this case, one of the books mentioned in the offer. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment didn’t validate, we saw a trend towards a significant increase in donor conversion if the test had continued to run. This could be due to the perceived value of the incentive, and/or because it increased the value of the offer itself by giving readers a headstart on the summer reading list.

experiments.title: How the type of ask affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a direct ask or a soft ask in the call to action produce better donor conversion in a high urgency campaign email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 08/25/2017
FamilyLife was running a high urgency campaign and wanted to test a direct ask vs. a softer ask in the call to action of email #3 to see which motivated more people to click through to the donation page and give.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a trend towards higher donations from the soft ask version of the email. This is likely due to higher clickthrough rate which led to higher overall donations, but the same conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a headline on a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused headline on a banner increase donations more than a match-focused headline on a banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 12/27/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that led with value prop headline first instead of match language would increase donations more than a match-focused headline on banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, likely because there was not enough difference in the variables tested against the control version to statistically affect conversion rate. The timing of the test was also during the end of their year-end campaign, when many people were already highly motivated to give once they got to the website.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacts donor conversion during year-end.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign motivate more people to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2018 - 01/27/2018
For FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, they had a special matching gift they offered to people in an effort to motivate more people to give during this critical fundraising campaign. The donation page was focused on this matching gift and encouraged people to give before December 31 to take advantage of this incentive. We hypothesized that while people will be motivated by this incentive, they might be more motivated if we increased urgency and reminded them of what their gift will be used for. We developed a radical redesign of the donation page that led with a FamilyLife value proposition, increased urgency with a countdown clock and also included the match incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment didn’t validate, but we did see a directional lift in conversion of 10%. An incentive like a match is great, but we are able to motivate more people to convert if we can also remind them of what their gift is going towards and increase urgency visually. People don’t give to give, they give to have an impact and we must communicate what that impact is.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click thoughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.

experiments.title: How value proposition conveyed through headlines affects signups
experiments.researchQuestion: Headline’s emphasizing what will be gained by giving their email address will have a higher conversion rate than framing it as a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/21/2015
During the Christmas season, DTS creates a devotional series that runs between December 1st until Christmas. As a way to broaden the audience that receives the devotionals and collect new email addresses, we created a landing page to be used for Facebook advertising. We then created a new treatment to find the message the resonated most with the Facebook audience
experiments.keyLearnings: Given the minor difference in acquisition rate, it would appear the two treatments were not distinct enough to elicit an increase in response.

experiments.title: How the headline value proposition affects acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: By stating what the visitor will “get” within the headline, we can improve the conversion rate on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2014 - 12/14/2014
As part of the calendar year end campaign, DTS produced an eBook that they gave away free to their supporters. To expand the impact of this free offer, we also created landing pages that were advertised to Facebook audiences. These pages offered the book for the price of an email address.
With the influx of traffic, we used the opportunity to test the impact that a headline can have on conveying the value proposition of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The audience was already highly motivated and the ads had conveyed what would be received on the subsequent page. As a result, the headlines did not produce a significant lift to conversion rate.

experiments.title: How the cost of a premium affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a premium boost the overall conversion and revenue of a donation landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 06/24/2015
As part of the a name acquisition campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary was giving away a free eBook in exchange for a visitor’s email address.  After the successful email acquisition, visitors were sent to a contextualized donation page.  In an effort to boost conversion on this follow up donation ask, we wanted to see if offering a relevant premium (a hardcover book on a similar topic) would boost conversion.
As a result, we created two landing pages, with one that had a premium offer for a gift of any amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page with the premium offer did not have any statistically significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue. Since this book had an associated cost to the seminary, it didn’t make sense to continue offering the premium to donors.
For many organizations, premium offers will often have a positive impact on donor conversion and average gift but there are times where, as in this case, the impact was negligible. This is why it is so critical to test any changes to a donation form to ensure the impact with worth the cost.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition delivers a higher donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 10/26/2015
DTS launched a resource center for biblical perspectives on today’s issues. The goal of this resource center was name and donor acquisition. Immediately after the signup page was a donation ask. DTS wanted to test messaging on the donation page to determine which value proposition was stronger: an ask for an investment in a student or an ask centered around teaching biblical truth.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for several months, with no clear winner. We determined that for this resource, there was no clearly superior value proposition. This resulted in the launch of a new, more radical test.

experiments.title: How suggested giving amounts on a donor acquisition page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving type would increase donor conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2015 - 01/28/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary offered an a free online course. The goal of the free online course was for email acquisition and donor acquisition. Immediately following the course sign-up page was a donation ask.
On the donation page, a premium was offered with an open giving field. We wanted to test that page with a treatment that had suggested giving amounts next to the premium. Our hypothesis was that by adding suggested giving amounts to the donation form we would show the value of the premium and be able to increase donor conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS ran this test for a couple of months, with no signifiant results. We determined that for this donation page we would need to do a more radical test that included suggested giving amounts as one of the variables.

experiments.title: How the length of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 06/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course studying Genesis. After a new user signed up, they were presented with an opportunity to make a donation supporting the course and receive a companion book.
The value proposition on the page laid out a clear reason to support the course, but the team hypothesized that two changes might increase conversion. They changed the headline from “Thank you” to “Your enrollment in Genesis is confirmed” to entice more people to read the copy. Then, they shortened the body copy to attempt to increase the force of the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no valid lift or decrease in the conversion rate for the treatment. This tells us that DTS donors did not prefer one treatment over another, and that we must launch more radical experiments to increase the force of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How a time-sensitive value proposition affects conversion rate for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a matching challenge ask outperform a general value proposition ask during a time sensitive campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a fundraising campaign for North Texas Giving Day.They had previously tested into a value proposition centered around their mission: growing the kingdom. However, they wanted to test to see if a very time-sensitive specific matching challenge ask would be a greater motivator for donors and nondonors. Since the two segments had different levels of affinity and motivation, they wanted to test them separately. They created a treatment page and showed it to both donors and nondonors independently, with a 50-50 split to try to determine which value proposition was a greater motivator for this specific campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 9.5% decrease in donor conversion rate although this was not statistically valid due to low traffic for this campaign. Because it was not statistically valid, these numbers cannot be interpreted to infer that the new value proposition was less motivating to nondonors. In fact, this shows that there is practically no difference between the two. These results show that DTS must either take a more radical approach or generate more traffic in order to validate a test like this.

experiments.title: How a time-sensitive value proposition affects conversion rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a matching challenge ask outperform a general value proposition ask during a time sensitive campaign for a donor segment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a fundraising campaign for North Texas Giving Day.They had previously tested into a value proposition centered around their mission: growing the kingdom. However, they wanted to test to see if a very time-sensitive specific matching challenge ask would be a greater motivator for donors and nondonors. Since the two segments had different levels of affinity and motivation, they wanted to test them separately. They created a treatment page and showed it to both donors and nondonors independently, with a 50-50 split to try to determine which value proposition was a greater motivator for this specific campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 23% decrease in donor conversion rate although this, like the nondonor test, was not statistically valid due to low traffic for this campaign. Because it was not statistically valid, these numbers cannot be interpreted to infer that the new value proposition was less motivating to donors. This test was closer to validity than the nondonor test, but even when combining the data from the two tests, there was not a clear winner or statistical validity.

experiments.title: How reduced copy and a more direct headline affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reduced copy and a more direct headline positively affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2016 - 01/05/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary offered a free online course called How to Read the Bible Like a Seminary Professor to allow their donors and prospects to experience the value proposition of the Seminary. Although they had a good donor conversion rate, they wanted to see if they could improve email acquisition by making the value proposition on the signup page more appealing.
They created a treatment with a more direct headline: “Learn to read the Bible like never before”. Then, they removed the bulleted list from the copy to reduce the overall length of the copy, hypothesizing that it was not core to the value proposition on the page.
They split traffic to the page and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version produced a slight decrease in email acquisition, though it was far from valid. This shows that more radical changes need to be made to the page to significantly increase email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How placement of a call-to-action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the placement of the call-to-action button increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2016 - 03/30/2016
CaringBridge developed an email appeal for their March 2016 fundraising campaign. They had traditionally put the call-to-action buttons in the body of the email, giving the donor a large clickable area to click through and make their donation. However, since their email had a postscript, they hypothesized that putting the call-to-action at the end of the email might make more sense, as it allowed the user to digest all the copy before taking action.
They split their email file 50/50 to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment did not produce a significant lift. This shows us that the placement of the button does not have a significant effect on the clickthrough rate. Subsequent tests showed that removing the button actually improved results, so this element does not appear in current emails.

experiments.title: How prioritizing match language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does putting match language before a story affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2016 - 01/17/2017
CaringBridge had a matching gift offered during their year-end campaign from a user who had personally experienced the value proposition of the organization. They knew that stories had been powerful vehicles for fundraising campaigns, but this story had the added power of a matching gift.
They deliberated whether to put the match announcement at the beginning of the email or after the story. Putting it at the beginning would allow people who were already motivated to give to make their donations right away. But putting it at the end might allow people to be drawn in by the story first and potentially result in more donations once the powerful story was augmented by the match. They decided to put it to a test and split their file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which led with the matching gift announcement, produced a 32.7% lift in conversion, although the experiment did not reach statistical validity. This suggests that the directness of the match language was a powerful motivator, or that it possibly framed the story and resulted in more donors being led to take action.
However, this test will need to receive more traffic to produce a valid result.

experiments.title: How a signature in an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a signature image to an email affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/17/2017
CaringBridge was sending a personal email from a development team member as a follow-up to a matching gift announcement. They wondered about the inclusion of an actual signature in an email. People don’t normally sign emails, but many organizations brought this tactic over from direct mail appeals.
Typically, their emails did not include signatures, but they created a treatment to see if this small change added validity to the sender or decreased the personal feel of the email.
They split their email file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the signature produced a directional 32.9% lift in donor conversion—which meant that despite it being a non-native email element, it appears to have positively affected conversion. However, at only 72.9% statistical confidence, it needs to be tested with a larger audience to reach validity.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.
The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They wanted to test different messages to see if they could radically increase clickthrough rate, and with that increase in traffic, increase conversion rate and revenue.
They developed three treatments:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
Since CaringBridge has large amounts of traffic, they launched a 4-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After several weeks of testing, none of the copy variants produced any significant lift over the control. While this was disappointing, a further review of the data found that one of the variants produced a significant increase in revenue after the click. This showed that the value propositions might not produce a result immediately, but might inspire a higher motivation to give.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a donate button affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donate button inside the secure box increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/15/2017
CaringBridge had performed extensive testing on their “tribute” donation form, which produced the majority of their online fundraising revenue. One experiment was able to increase the conversion rate on the page by increasing perceived security, adding a box around the credit card information. However, this box left the donate button outside of the box. Their team wondered if this was leaving conversions on the table due to the button being perceived as “insecure”.
They launched a version of the page that moved the donate button into the “secure box” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test failed to produce a valid result in either direction. While the secure box produced a lift, simply moving the donate button inside the box was not enough of a difference-maker to affect conversion positively or negatively.

experiments.title: How direct copy affects donation page conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does more direct copy increase the force of the value proposition and result in more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s primary donation page had proven to show better results with a broader value proposition, as opposed to the highly personalized value proposition presented on their tribute donation pages.
In a copy review of their tribute donation page, they wondered if the question posed in the headline: “What does CaringBridge mean to you?” added cognitive friction as people tried to answer this question for themselves, instead of continuing to ingest the value proposition.
They created a treatment that instantly offered an opportunity to the reader—”You make CaringBridge better.” The treatment copy removed questions and made statements, telling them that if they valued CaringBridge, their donation was vital.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not produce a statistically significant result either way, which showed that more radical changes need to be made to the page to deliver a result. Since this page has a high donor conversion rate and low traffic, it might be difficult to prove a winning treatment due to higher visitor motivation…but we will continue testing.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on mobile devices affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an open field on a donation form affect donor conversion rate on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2017 - 06/15/2017
CaringBridge had seen a significant lift in donor conversion on one of their donation pages when they removed the gift array and simply offered an “open field” that allowed donors to pick their own donation amount. However, their donation page setup split out desktop pages and mobile pages, so they wanted to test the exact same premise on their mobile pages.
They removed the array on a treatment page and left an open field, then launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though the experiment didn’t validate, CaringBridge saw quite the opposite result on mobile devices—a 10.5% decrease in conversion when the open field was presented. This could be due to the fact that the buttons become larger on mobile devices and are much easier to use on a small screen. It could also be related to the situational difference between desktop (likely seated at a desk) and mobile (out and about, with many more possible distractions).

experiments.title: How emotion-driven copy affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive copy increase donation revenue on a tribute donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2017 - 08/31/2017
CaringBridge had previously run an experiment on the journal pages of their site that produced a big lift  in revenue when “emotive” language was shown. They wanted to run an additional test to see if the same sort of emotive language would produce an increase in revenue on the tribute donation page. Since the the winning copy from the previous experiment wouldn’t deploy for a few weeks, they had a chance to test the copy on the tribute page independently. They hypothesized that if they could also get a lift on the donation page, then they might be able to have a compound lift from the two areas of improvement.
They created a treatment donation page that utilized the “love and support” language and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page failed to produce any significant difference from the control donation page. This shows that while the emotive language might be a great motivator to start the gift process, it doesn’t have a significant effect at finishing the donation process. It also may show a relationship between the emotive language in the donate box and the standard control language on the donation page—these two copy variations may be working in tandem to produce a higher revenue total.

experiments.title: How using clarity in the call-to-action can impact traffic to a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving button copy would increase traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2017 - 10/11/2017
Care Net wanted to increase the volume of traffic going to their primary donation page. One of the ways to try and do this is to test the kind of copy that was being used on the “donate” button of their homepage. Their current donate button currently used value proposition focused language, “Save Babies Now”. They hypothesized that a treatment version with language that more directly reflected where the button would take them once they clicked on it might increase traffic to the donation page and ultimately have a positive impact on donations and revenue. They treatment button copy said “Support Care Net”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy had a slight increase in conversion, but the results weren’t statistically valid. The experiment ran for over a month and would need a more radical treatment with a larger sample size to validate.
Even though the test didn’t validate, we can hypothesize that with the slight increase in conversion from the button that said “Support Care Net”, the copy presented more clarity with the end-users action. They knew exactly where they would be taken if they clicked on the button. By saying “Save Babies Now” in the control, the copy may have been too vague and created a higher level of friction in the mind of the end-user – where would they be taken if they clicked on that button? What if they weren’t ready to “save a baby now”?
When we test copy in a call-to-action, it is good to keep in mind that clarity trumps persuasion.

experiments.title: How an offer with low appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will receive the highest amount of revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2016 - 08/19/2016
During Buckner’s Shoes for Orphan’s Souls campaign, we ran a series of experiments with the goal of boosting donor conversion. One of the experiments was to run Facebook ads asking directly for a gift to the campaign. The normal flow for Facebook acquisition is to offer a premium in exchange for an email address followed by a donation ask. However, with this specific campaign, we thought a gift may be an appealing enough offer that the initial email acquisition was not necessary.
To make sure we got the most out of this campaign, we created two landing page treatments to test for the offer. The goal was to ensure we were going with the right message for the audience. The control included some additional language around Buckner International and the broader mission while the treatment focused specifically on the Shoes aspect of the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment spoke more towards the general appeal of the offer than the individual results of the pages. After spending $200 on ads, we did not net a single donor. As a result, we shut down the offer before we could achieve a valid sample size and result. This concept, “general Buckner value prop” vs. “Shoes specific value prop” is a concept worth testing again next August.

experiments.title: How 3rd party quotes impacted the email acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will positive reviews of the book increase the appeal of the offer and, subsequently, the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of the promotion for their free eBook offer, Buckner International wanted to determine if adding book reviews increased the appeal of the offer. We were able to pull quotes from the book’s Amazon page and add them directly on to the page before the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full 2 months, we did not see any statistically significant impact of the reviews. The page was converting at a ~40% conversion rate and a small adjustment like this did not seem to have any influence on the visitor’s likelihood to submit their information.

experiments.title: How the imagery of the page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a flat image or an eCard video produce the best email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of the December acquisition campaign, Buckner International was launching a dedicated eCard to be sent out to all foster and adoptive families. We then launched Facebook ads to collect “signatures” for the card. During our initial conversations, we had a debate on the best way to show the “card” that they would be signing. Initially, it was believed that a flat image would be the least distracting but then the hypothesis of a video was raised since it was believed that it would be more engaging.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this offer had a limited time period that it could be reasonably run, we were not able to validate the experiment. However, it appears that the animated eCard video produced a lift over the flat card. It would have taken several hundred more visitors to statistically validate this result but this is definitely worth remembering for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does added value proposition copy on a donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2017 - 07/10/2017
Though this experiment did not validate, they experienced a 10% lift in donations from the added treatment copy. This could be due to a more readable format (a bulleted list) for people to make a decision to donate, or perceived added value of their donation to the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International released a new offer giving parents a series of prayers to pray over their children. They wanted to test if adding additional value proposition and a bulleted list of what Buckner does to the donation page would increase donations. The form and other elements remained the same.

experiments.title: How transcribing a video on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the transcription of a video convert more donors than a video alone?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running a video ad on Facebook targeting their donors and housefile, with a video of Dr. Reyes explaining the impact that their gifts had in 2017 on children and families that Buckner served, and inviting them to make a year-end gift.
The video linked to a landing page with the video on it and the opportunity to make a gift. They wanted to test whether transcribing Dr. Reyes’ message in the video on the page would have a greater impact than just re-watching the video.
Other elements of the donation page, including the form, remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The motivation of visitors to the page and traffic to the page was too low to make any definitive assessment of which was the better treatment. This makes sense given the reason they clicked to the page (either to watch more of the video or for more information).
It’s likely that not enough people were motivated to give because of the source of the traffic (Facebook) which has traditionally been harder to convert with direct ask ads. Also, people may have been confused by the lack of context on the donation page that only showed the video, without supporting text.
The concept of testing video vs. text treatments should be re-tried with a page that gets higher traffic to guarantee a more valid learning.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy policy to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding privacy language to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/22/2017
Buckner International was running an email acquisition campaign with their 7 Scriptures offer. They wanted to see if adding privacy language around the conversion form would increase conversion rate due to a higher perceived sense of security for downloaders. All other elements of the landing page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that conversion rate stayed pretty constant between the two landing pages, with the privacy policy page showing little to no difference in conversion rate. It’s possible that a privacy policy does affect people’s perception of the security of the offer, but not having one does not deter people from downloading either.

experiments.title: How more concise value proposition affects email conversion rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2017 - 09/25/2017
Buckner International was running a test on an acquisition offer that offered free scriptures to pray for your kids. They wanted to test if a shortened, more concise value proposition on the landing page would increase conversion rate. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, and we saw only a minor decrease in conversion rate from the shortened copy. This could be tested further with a more radical test to figure out if people are deterred by length of copy, or more encouraged to download with a more built-out value proposition for the offer.

experiments.title: How urgency on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does increasing urgency on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2017 - 09/29/2017
Buckner was testing more urgent copy for an immediate need on the donation page of their content offer, 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life. The immediate need focused around Hurricane Harvey and the Buckner staff and families affected by the disaster. The ads and acquisition page for the offer remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not reach 95% validation or reach statistical power of over 80%, so the test results were not valid. However, we did see a trend towards a higher gift amount on the treatment page, likely due to the urgency of the need. It is also likely that the time window for donations to Harvey had passed once the treatment began, which could have had some effect on conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 11/06/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How adding region-specific wording to an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does making copy more personal to the reader increase click rate in an email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 11/28/2017
Buckner International was giving a free offer to people in their email file as a gift for Giving Tuesday. They wanted to test whether adding region-specific copy in the email would increase clickthrough rate. The control had generic language, whereas the treatment would use contact-specific wording and apply their most local Buckner branch’s name in that copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The region-specific copy increased clicks by 3%, however, this experiment was not valid. A more radical test should be performed to increase learnings about conditional copy to the reader.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a year end donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding gift array buttons to a donation form affect donor conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test how adding a gift array to their year-end donation page would affect donor conversion and gift value. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 10% decrease to conversion rate (not statistically significant) but a 42% increase to revenue per visitor when we removed the buttons during year end (not statistically significant). Given the low significance, this is something that would need to be re-tested in future appeals before making any definitive statements. However, based upon these results, it may make sense to segment high dollar donors ($100+) and provide them with a different giving experience than lower level and non-donors. Basically, use the buttons to remove friction for the less motivated audience and then allow the higher donors to choose their own level of generosity. Again, this is a hypothesis that will need to be revalidated with a future test.

experiments.title: How high visitor motivation affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Stand Up Headline with Matching Grant

experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment is going to produce the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 05/31/2015
The standard appeal for Alliance Defending Freedom will begin with an email to their main constituency that contains a strong call-to-action to give a gift. In an effort to increase the conversion rate of the page, we wanted to try several changes add additional value to the potential donors’ gift and reduce friction and anxiety.
We created a treatment that dramatically expanded upon the value proposition being discussed on the page both in the main page copy and in the button at the bottom. We also moved the matching gift incentive to the end of the copy to focus on the primary reasons to give to ADF. We also expanded elements that emphasized the security and financial accountability.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not reach significance with either sample size or level of confidence, but we did learn something about the motivation of the donors.
The initial driver of traffic (the email) was crafted in such a way that only the most highly motivated visitors made to the page. We realized that in order to improve the overall response rate of the campaign, we needed to move the optimization efforts back in the donor funnel to the email itself.  If we can increase the number of visitors to the page, we can then impact the overall results of the campaign.

experiments.title: How taking a softer call to action affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call-to-action within the email will drive more traffic to the website which will, in turn, convert a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/27/2014
As it was getting closer to the end of the year, Heritage wanted to test a messaging approach with their fifth email in their year end campaign. Toward the end of urgency-based marketing campaigns, it is common practice to make a direct ask in the email.  The question was how early can that direct ask be made and have it still be effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer language in the email increased the number of visitors by 41% but it actually converted fewer total donors in the test.  At this stage in the campaign, there does not seem to be any impact to response rate by utilizing a softer call to action.

experiments.title: How will just adding the calls-to-action affect donations and email acquisition

rows.name: Calls to Action at the Bottom

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the email capture and donation button to the page increase the number of people taking one of those two actions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2015 - 10/28/2015
The Alliance Defending Freedom blog receives high volumes of traffic each day, largely from social media. We wanted to find ways to motivate this audience to take one of two actions: either subscribe to the newsletter or make a donation.
There is a component found on many other areas of the website that gives both of these options but it was not available on the blog. As the first experiment to increase either of these two behaviors, we decided to simply put this widget at the bottom of the page to see if that lifts either the number of donations made or email addresses acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: Just adding the calls to action to the bottom of the screen did not inspire the blog readers to do either of the two actions. In future experiments, we need to find ways to contextualize the calls-to-action so it fits within the content of the blog and we need to emphasize a primary CTA to make the desired action clear to the ready.

experiments.title: How a shift in copy tone affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: An email talking about how Heritage impacts the constituents’ family life will drive more clicks than one talking about how they stand for the constituents’ principles.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
In the fourth email in the Heritage year end campaign, we tested what messaging point (principles or family) would drive the most traffic to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two treatments spoke to different prospect level value propositions, the copy did not seem to be different enough to see any significant impact on click throughs or donations.

experiments.title: How a Faith and Justice offer impacted the blog subscriptions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the incentive of Faith and Justice lift the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2016 - 03/31/2016
In our continued efforts to increase the conversion rate for visitors on the blog, we decided to isolate the Faith and Justice offer to see what impact that would have in visitor conversion. In past experiments, we discovered that Faith and Justice by itself decreased conversion so by adding incremental language to the slide out that offered the subscription we were hoping to see if the added incentive increased the value in the visitors minds.
experiments.keyLearnings: With statistical validity sitting at 73%, we discovered that Faith and Justice did not increase the value of the email acquisition offer in the minds of the visitor. It did not detract from it but we learned that it would not serve as an added incentive for subscription.

experiments.title: How the positioning of the offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing the slide out offer in the middle of the blog post increase the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2016 - 04/20/2016
Our most successful acquisition devices for the ADF blog is currently a slide out offer that appears once the visitor reaches the end of each blog post. The position of the offer was chosen based upon the opinion that people that finish the content would be more likely to convert. We decided to put this hypothesis to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings: Initially, we saw positive results with the slide out offer appearing in the middle of the page but over time, these results diminished until there was no statistical difference. It would appear that there is no major impact between showing the slide out in the middle or at the end of the page.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects visitor motivation on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2016 - 06/08/2016
In a recent experiment, we discovered a new value proposition that offered a significant lift to the email acquisition rate on the blog. In prior experiments, we had learned that blog traffic had differing motivations than those coming to the home page. As a result, we decided to test this new value proposition on the home page to see if it would have the same impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant change to email acquisition with the new treatment. This experiment is another reinforcement that the visitors to the blog have very different motivations than those visiting the home page. In this case, the visitors already had a better understanding of what ADF does so the “updates” and “latest news” did not require any additional validation or explanation.

experiments.title: How donor page redesign affects visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will result in the greatest conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2016 - 06/13/2016
After the Safe Bathroom campaign initially launched, we noticed that the donation page had a relatively low conversion rate of 1% to 2%. After examining the donation page, we decided to run a radical redesign that would reduce the length of the page while reinforcing the reason they initially signed the pledge that led them to this page.  This radical redesign would also bring in imagery from the original page to try to reinforce that original value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment is a good example of why it is necessary to give experiments enough time before making any quick decisions. At the initial launch of the experiment, the Control seemed to have a significantly higher conversion rate but, as time went on, this difference leveled out. By the time we ended this experiment, there was less than a 70% level of confidence in either treatment meaning neither showed significant signs of improvement over the other.
This test largely focused on tactical aspects of the page such as form length and imagery. In the end, those changes were not enough to sway the visitors to give a gift.

experiments.title: How the “Next Article” button impacted engagement – V2
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Next Article” button to the blog increase the percent of people that explore the ADF site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/03/2017
We had previously validated an experiment that proved a “Next Article” button at the end of a blog actually decreased the amount of people that read the next article. We had doubts about the validity of this experiment due to a technical issue so we decided to run the experiment again to see if the same results would be achieved.
experiments.keyLearnings: This proved what we expected about the previous test; there was a technical issue that had invalidated the results. This experiment proved the simple act of adding the button did not impact the likelihood of the visitor to read the next article.

experiments.title: How reducing visual friction on the donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a donation page with reduced navigation and a smaller donation form increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2017 - 03/20/2017
We conducted a series of experiments on a handful of email list rentals prior to rolling out the message to a wide audience. Once we were confident in the tactic and messaging approach of the appeal, we rolled it out to a larger set of lists.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After the appeal was rolled out to five different email lists, we were still not able to achieve statistical significance with the test treatment. It would seem that the audience had such a high motivation that the navigational elements and form fields were not a significant source of friction.
However, it is worth pointing out that the desktop treatment saw a 14% lift in conversion (not statistically significant) while the mobile treatment was nearly identical to the control. This would seem to indicate that the navigation and form were more of a source of friction for desktop visitors than mobile. This makes sense given that the mobile design already has reduced navigation and the form would be nearly identical due to the responsive design.
To validate this hypothesis, we would need to test this again with a larger audience of desktop visitors.

experiments.title: How a Topical Inline Email Offer Impacts Email Acquisition Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing someone an email acquisition offer that is specific to the article they are on increase the likelihood of conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom has identified their blog as a high-traffic section of their site. They noticed, however, that too many mobile visitors were leaving without signing up to receive more Alliance Defending Freedom content. To assist in email acquisition, they decided to add an inline offer for mobile visitors that prompted them to ‘stay informed to changes to your religious freedom,’ by signing up to receive the latest generic updates from Alliance Defending Freedom. After noticing that this inline offer increased email acquisition, they decided to create more specific inline offers for each major topic: Sanctity of life, religious liberty, and marriage and family. They set these treatments up against the more generic control, and documented the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test did not validate. However, we can still draw some conclusions from the results. The addition of the specific inline offer did not dramatically increase or decrease email acquisition for mobile visitors. This may indicate that blog visitors were not drawn to one particularly topic but rather Alliance Defending Freedom’s high level value proposition. It may also indicate that the more generic inline offer had a great perceived value (i.e. updates on sanctity of life vs. updates on sanctity of life, religious liberty, and marriage and family). Though not statistically valid, this test gave Alliance Defending Freedom a head start in understanding the motivations and behavior of mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How a ‘Blog Topic’ Survey Impacts User Engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Blog Topic’ survey increase user engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2017 - 08/11/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom found in a previous test that offering blog readers additional relevant content at the end of the article increased engagement. Since this discovery, they have looked for new ways to (a) further increase engagement and (b) gather feedback on which topics they should cover. With these goals in mind, they launched a ‘Blog Topic’ survey that prompts the reader to select which topic they would like Alliance Defending Freedom to cover next.
After the user made a selection, they were offered one of two different options: additional articles to explore related to their selection or an email acquisition offer. Both of these actions are metrics that we are trying to increase for blog readers so we wanted to see if this style of engagement could impact either one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither of the two survey options had any significant impact on either of the two tracked metrics: reading more articles and acquiring more emails. The data set above is for the email acquisition rate.
Though this test did not show a statistical difference in the primary metrics, we can draw one conclusion from the results. The ‘Blog Topic’ survey did not have a negative impact on email acquisition or engagement rates. Because of this, we know that Alliance Defending Freedom will be able to gather insightful data from the blog users. This data–collected with no net impact on engagement–can help them determine strategic focuses in the future.

experiments.title: How aligning the content of the email offer to the article impacted acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offers that better relate to the content on the page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
On the mobile version of the Alliance Defending Freedom blog, we wanted to see if we could better align the value of the email offer with the content on the page. The belief was that people reading an article on “Sanctity of Life” or “Culture” would be more likely to respond to an email offer on the same topic.
To do this, we created four different versions of the email acquisition offer targeted at the four categories of blog posts on ADFLegal.org’s blog.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment did not produce any significant lift to the email acquisition rates when it aligned with the content of the article. After running for a full month, it seemed to be negatively impacting the results so we decided to shut it off before it validated. (It wasn’t worth running for another month).
This reinforces something we’ve seen in the past with ADF. Supporters connect with the organizational value proposition of defending religious freedom. Whenever we try to target a specific aspect of that proposition, it negatively impacts results.

experiments.title: How an ‘Up Next’ slideout affects email acquisition rate on Desktop
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase clickthrough rate on desktop by providing a single article that is “Up Next”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2017 - 10/02/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently conducted an experiment  on mobile devices, where they asked: Is it possible to increase clickthrough rate by providing an ‘Up Next’ article? The result was a valid 29% increase in traffic without a valid decrease in conversion. They wondered if they would see the same results if they conducted this experiment with desktop users as well. They launched a desktop-only test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom’s experiment did not achieve validity. Though the results were inconclusive, this test may tell us an important thing or two about desktop users. Behavior from one device to another can change significantly. We know that those who view a blog post on a mobile device are already more likely to read more than one article at a time. Due to the differences in motivation and the likely environment of mobile and desktop users, it’s important that we test each separately. Another variable that makes this test different than the mobile-only version is the ‘Up Next’ content the desktop viewer saw. It’s possible this piece of content was less compelling to viewers than what was shown as a treatment in the mobile test.
Further studies are being conducted to determine whether desktop visitors benefit from an ‘Up Next’ slideout or not.

experiments.title: How a case-specific exit intent offers impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an exit intent offer geared towards a particular legal case increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2017 - 10/17/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was looking for a new way to improve the exit intent offer that visitors see before leaving their site. They wondered if a more specific call-to-action would cause visitors to take action with more urgency. They decided to test an offer around one of their largest legal cases right now: The defense of Jack Phillips. So they created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, this test did not validate for Alliance Defending Freedom. While they did begin to see a lift with the more case-specific messaging, when compared with the mission-specific messaging (the control), the end results were inconclusive. This has prompted further test ideas regarding the nature and offer of exit intent offers.

experiments.title: How case-specific value proposition copy impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an newsletter signup offer geared towards a particular legal case increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/13/2017 - 10/17/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently conducted a test with their exit intent offer, asking: Will a more case-specific offer increase email acquisition? The results of that test were inconclusive but prompted a similar test on their ‘Jack Phillips’ information and email acquisition page. They asked: Will a case-specific newsletter offer increase email acquisition? They created two versions and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Again, Alliance Defending Freedom found that the results were inconclusive, since the test did not reach a statistical significance of 95% and a statistical power of 80%. We would naturally believe that aligning the newsletter offer with the related information on the page would increase email acquisition. And perhaps, it can if given enough time and traffic. However, this test cannot confirm that hypothesis, and thus, requires further testing to prove.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the urgency of a campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donation page copy that emphasizes the relevance and urgency of Jack’s case increase donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2017 - 11/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was looking for new ways to optimize the performance of their Jack Phillips donation page. They wondered: Can we positively impact visitor motivation and by increasing the relevance and urgency donating to support the Jack Phillip’s case? They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom found that this test produced no statistically significant results. Therefore, the impact of the treatment version is inconclusive. Still, the fact that this page did not produce a valid result does tell us that the copy used on the treatment page to emphasize the urgency of this case did not increase visitor motivation.

experiments.title: How a banner header image impacts article engagement for desktop users
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the header image on blog pages increase reader engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2017 - 11/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was interested in knowing how blog page design impacted reader engagement on their site. They wondered: Will removing the header image on blog pages decrease friction and increase reader engagement? They created a trackable goal that measured clicks from one blog to a second article. They then created a treatment version that removed the header image and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the removal of the blog header image did not significantly impact reader engagement on their blog. The treatment page, in other words, did not produce a statistically significant increase or decrease in reader engagement for desktop users.
This test does, however, seek to address an important question–one that further testing will continue to try and answer. That question is: How does design impact page functionality and usability? This is an important, ongoing question that every nonprofit marketer should be motivated to answer for their organization because both form and function must exist in a healthy balance.

experiments.title: How a supporting image affects email acquisition rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing a supporting image increase email acquisition rate on a landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/19/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently launched a campaign to rally support around Jack Phillips–one of the clients they are defending at the Supreme Court. They found that adding additional copy to their facebook ads did not increase or decrease clickthrough rate. They wondered: Will removing the image of Jack Phillips on the email acquisition page increase the overall email acquisition rate? They created a treatment version (without the image of Jack) and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the the treatment version actually had no statistically significant effect on email acquisition. In other words, we saw no difference in conversion on the page that lacked the supporting image. This is an interesting learning for all of us to consider, as we build new campaign pages. This test suggests that the presence (or lack thereof) of a smaller image doesn’t have a dramatic impact on visitor motivation.
However, it’s also important to note that the conversion rate for this page was much higher than the average acquisition page (converting over 70% of all visitors). Motivation may have been so high, in fact, that it overpowered any page elements that would have otherwise affected page users. Thus, Alliance Defending Freedom will continue to test the image element in order to be certain of its impact.

experiments.title: How exclusivity and impact factor into donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will exclusivity or impact have the greatest influence on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2017 - 07/14/2017
During the acquisition campaign for Heritage Action’s Leaders Club, we were trying to acquire a new group of donors for the organization’s recurring giver club. As we were reviewing past campaigns, we realized that our historical messaging had focused on the exclusivity of joining the club. However, we had the hypothesis that donors would more likely join due to the impact their gift would have over the exclusivity of the club. We decided to test these two approaches against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It would seem that both factors were equally important in the donors’ minds. There was no statistically significant difference between the two versions.
Now, it is worth pointing out that both versions incorporated both exclusivity and impact into the copy; this test was focused on determining which one of these factors should be led with.

experiments.title: How the addition of Apple Pay impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of Apple Pay decrease the friction associated with the donation process enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 04/18/2017
The Heritage Foundation is consistently looking for new ways to make the donation process both easier and better for their members. The Apple Pay option had recently gone mainstream with its incorporation into both iPhones and Mac web browsers. This one-click giving option made it significantly easier for visitors that had the service set up; for those that did not, they would not have seen any difference between the forms.
However, before rolling out the new feature, we wanted to measure its impact on the donor audience. Since this new feature was only applicable to Safari users on a Mac, we targeted the experiment to that audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new Apple Pay feature did not have any statistically significant impact on donor conversion or revenue. Our hypothesis is that this is likely due to the slow adoption of Apple Pay for Mac and Apple users. The portion of users that will be implementing Apple Pay will only be increasing in coming month so we decided to roll out the new form for visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How a new landing page design impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the new redesigned page and donation form impact donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2016 - 11/10/2016
The Heritage.org website is in the process of a redesign. As part of this transition, it was suggested that the existing donation page design be migrated to the new format. This would include the transition to a new look and feel for the donation form itself. Before making this change, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on the performance of the donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for the length of a money bomb campaign, we determined that the new design had no statistically significant impact on the performance of the campaign. While this is reassuring, it has led to two more questions:
How does the donation form design specifically impact conversion? We want to isolate this factor to ensure the more modern design is not having a negative impact on the older donor base.
Once the new site launches, will this new design actually increase conversion by maintaining visual congruence.
Both of these questions will be tested in upcoming campaigns.

experiments.title: How the function of the form elements affects conversion (and the “most beautiful form in the world”)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a most condensed donation form that utilized more modern labeling techniques increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
