Timeframe: 08/03/2016 - 09/12/2016
In an effort to continue to iterate on the general donation form used throughout the Heritage properties, we developed a test that would incorporate the modern web technique of adaptive placeholders. The goal of this technique is to visually condense the form by using placeholder labels inside of the text fields but to then move those same placeholders to the top of the field once a visitor focuses on it so the purpose of the field is clear. This is a technique that was taken from some of the larger eCommerce sites like Hotwire.com.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new form with adaptive placeholders did not produce any statistically significant results. While the form was more condensed, it looks like it was not a significant enough change to increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the general donation page affects member conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a general donation page perform better than a campaign specific page for new members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2016 - 02/03/2016
For the Heritage 2016 January membership campaign, we created a campaign specific landing page that introduced all of the benefits of Heritage membership and made a pitch to join to kick off 2016. We had the hypothesis that we were overcomplicating the process with additional language that was unnecessary. Since the membership offer was relatively simple, we decided to test the campaign specific page against the general donation page made a clear and concise pitch for membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither of the pages produced a significant improvement in conversion over the other treatment. Our hypothesis is that by the time this test was launched, the end of the campaign was near enough that the campaign deadline became a significant motivating factor. With the added motivation, the reduced friction became just as important as the added value the additional copy provided. This test would need to be repeated earlier in a campaign to validate this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How low visitor motivation affected value proposition arguments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2015 - 07/03/2015
Heritage was running an email acquisition campaign using a petition against the government’s Common Core initiative. As part of the process, signers to the petition would be sent to a donation page following their signature.  The standard practice for these types of campaigns was to create a landing page thanking them for their signature and asking them to make a gift to Heritage based upon the high-level value proposition of Heritage.
We had a hypothesis that if we could focus the language on the page on how Heritage is specifically impacting this topic (Common Core), it would lead to a better conversion rate.  As a result, we created an experiment to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: By the time the campaign was ended, we did not have enough conversions to be able to reach a statistically significant conclusion.  In this situation, there was a low motivation to give a gift for visitors coming to the page. As a result, neither of the value propositions tested were able to significantly impact the overall conversion of the page.

experiments.title: How Imprimis’s value proposition affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will inspire the most donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2015 - 08/17/2015
The standard Imprimis donation page does not receive enough traffic each month to validate any tests. However, with the Imprimis survey that was sent out in August of 2015, we were able to drive enough visitors to possibly validate an experiment.
We created an experiment that tested two unique value propositions. The first is the current control which talks about the mission of the college and how Imprimis is connected to that. The new treatment utilized the cost metrics of Imprimis and framed it in terms of what it costs to send to both the donor and other like-minded people.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither treatment created enough of a lift to validate the experiment. This proved to us that both had equal appeal in the mind of the donor. We will continue forward with the existing control.

experiments.title: How getting visitors to identify with the target audience affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will identifying the target audience for the course increase the number of email addresses acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2015 - 09/01/2015
As part of the continued optimization of the Constitution 101 email acquisition page, Hillsdale College wanted to find a way to boost conversion. We had a hypothesis that visitors might not be converting because they didn’t know if the course was right for them.
We decided to create a new treatment that identifying the “target audience” for the course. This would let the visitors mentally bucket themselves in one target audience which would increase overall name acquisition efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both treatments produced nearly identical email acquisition rates which resulted in no statistically significant lift. Based upon these results, it would seem that the visitors not converting with the control version where not questioning whether they were the right audience for the course.

experiments.title: How speaking to donor passion points affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A call to action derived from Hillsdale’s financial independence from the federal government will get increased donors and average gifts when compared to a message involving their work teaching the principles of freedom.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/25/2014
As a way to thank their donors, Hillsdale produced a video close to Christmas. In addition to the stewardship aspect of the video, it offered a view of the Hillsdale campus and student body that donors rarely get to experience. To get more out of this video, we developed a landing page to send out to the full email file and included a contextualized donation form below.  This donation form offered an opportunity to test different aspects of Hillsdale’s value proposition to see what messages resonated best with their donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Given the nature of the passive ask and the inability to test the entire audience, we were unable to produce conclusive results. Further testing with more direct asks will be necessary to gain a better understanding how the donors view each of the elements of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding a video screenshot in an email affections visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email treatment will produce the highest click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2015 - 05/30/2015
With the landing page containing a video of Dr. Arnn, we wanted to see if that video was a motivation to visit the page. We created two versions of the email, one with a screen grab of the video and the other without.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having a video in the email did not prove to increase the engagement with the Hillsdale audience. Typically having an video will increase the click rate, but in this instance the video did not add enough perceived value to cause an increase in the number of recipients to click the link.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift amount impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which price point for the premium will result in the greatest lift to revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2016 - 07/26/2016
In one of the more successful donor acquisition campaigns for Heritage, we were offering a copy of The Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100 or more. To see if we could improve the revenue made from the campaign, we created a treatment that offered the same premium for a minimum of $125 gift. We targeted the offer at some rented lists.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 20% decrease to conversion rate and a 25% increase to average gift as a result of the higher minimum gift amount. This meant slightly more revenue (not statistically significant) but since this campaign was targeted at primarily donor acquisition, it did not make sense to make slightly more revenue while losing 20% of new donors. As a result, we then moved on to testing a $75 minimum gift to see how that would impact revenue and donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition impacted member renewal
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will result in the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2016 - 04/26/2016
For the member renewal campaign, we wanted to see if we could add some relevance to the appeal by talking about the upcoming challenges. We tested this expanded value proposition against the original control that had worked in the previous campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant difference in donor conversion or average gift. This would seem to imply that the two different value propositions were not different enough to have much of an impact on a donor’s likelihood to give.

experiments.title: How taking advantage of high visitor motivation affects follow on donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving friction to the front of the petition process increase the likelihood to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2016 - 04/27/2016
The Heritage SCOTUS petition had been running for a while and was acquiring email addresses for a incredibly low gross cost. However, we were getting very few instant conversions in the follow on ask.
We noticed that the email acquisition page was acquiring emails at a 60%+ conversion rate so we decided to try a test to take advantage of the high motivation. We changed the email acquisition page to require the visitors’ address fields in addition to just name and email. We knew this would decrease the initial conversion rate, however, we could then use this information on the tail end of the process to pre-fill the donation form. Our hypothesis was that if we reduced the friction on the second step, it would increase donor conversion which was ultimately what we were wanting.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not achieve significance before the campaign was shut off, we did notice that the front-loaded friction was able to increase donor conversion by 60%. The campaign still did not receive a high enough donor conversion to make it worth continuing but it is a good learning to utilize moving forward. When we see a high-converting email acquisition page, the decrease in email acquisition may be worth the subsequent increase on the donor acquisition page.

experiments.title: How creating a secure donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will not having a security certificate impact the conversion rate on a donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2016 - 03/16/2016
Hillsdale College was in the process of rolling out a new series of landing page for their email acquisition efforts. This new system was hosted on a new sub-domain that did not have a security certificate installed. This means that we were unable to add the https in front of the URL. It was our hypothesis that the lack of certificate would add anxiety in the mind of the visitor which would negatively impact conversion.
Since there was an added cost to installing a security certificate, we decided to validate this theory on a domain that had a certificate installed. We showed half of the visitors a donation page with https and half without it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Surprisingly, the security certificate did not have an impact on the likelihood of a donor to convert on the page. We may partially removed anxiety by including the word “secure” in the domain name itself but, regardless, the https, did not make any significant difference. This allowed us to save costs by not installing the certificate on the new domain.

experiments.title: How membership benefit language affects the conversion of existing members
experiments.researchQuestion: How will listing membership benefits on the page impact the likelihood of an existing member renewing their membership?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2016 - 01/20/2016
During the January membership campaign, we had the hypothesis that the associated benefits may not serve as much of an incentive to existing members. We decided to create two versions of the existing member page, one with a handful of benefits listed out and another without. We then split the existing member traffic between the two pages and monitored conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to produce a statistically significant result in either direction. It would seem that a reminder of their benefits does not have any impact on the likelihood of an existing donor to renew their membership.
Interestingly, we performed this same experiment for new members and it produced a 20% increase when we added the benefits. The benefits would appear to have a higher perceived value with those that have not yet experienced them.

experiments.title: How the load time of a page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a faster loading page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2016 - 01/20/2016
We noticed a decrease in the landing page conversion in the last Heritage year end campaign. One of the hypotheses for the cause was that the load time of the new server infrastructure may have been adversely impacting the conversion.  To test this hypothesis, we created a flat version of the same landing page that did not require any database calls, had a reduced amount of styles and code, and excluded several elements for tracking users.  The net result of these changes was a 47% decrease in the load time of the page (3.23 ms vs. 1.70 ms).
We then tested these two pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach any statistically significant results with this experiment. Directionally, it would seem the faster page gave a minor boost to conversion but the initial load time of 3.23 milliseconds may already have been fast enough that it was not as impactful to the conversion process as we initially envisioned it would be.

experiments.title: How seasonal imagery affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will seasonal background imagery impact the conversion rate on the page during the holiday season?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/28/2015
We had a hypothesis that if we could subtly incorporate seasonal imagery into Heritage’s calendar year end campaign, we would be able to encourage the spirit of giving that comes with the holiday season. We created two landing page treatments, one of which incorporated a bokeh image (blurred Christmas lights).
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment produced no significant impact on conversion rate or average gift. It would appear that either the image was too subtle and didn’t remind visitors of the Christmas season or the imagery played such a small part in the likelihood to convert that a change to this area didn’t make much of a difference.

experiments.title: How the imagery of a page affected the campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: What impact does adding imagery to the page have on donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 09/28/2015
For the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we wanted to find a way to increase the overall performance of the landing page. We had the hypothesis that by adding supporting imagery that reinforced the messages being communicated on the page, we could make the content more engaging and easier to understand. In this situation, we were using references to the Constitution and quoting notable historical figures such as Thomas Jefferson.  We decided to set up a new treatment that would include images of those elements along with the text.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the imagery to the page had not significant impact on the overall performance of the page. We will often debate the amount of influence design has on the overall conversion of the page. In this situation, the design elements did not increase value proposition, lower friction, or reduce anxiety; as a result, that made no significant impact on the likelihood of a donor to convert.

experiments.title: How a modified value proposition affected campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the biggest impact on revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 09/30/2015
In the final days of the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we were looking for ways to improve results. After reading the original copy on the page, we decided there were areas that we could add additional clarity to. Our hypothesis was that by adding the additional clarity, we could improve the understanding of the value proposition which would lead to increased conversion rate on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The modifications made to the copy on the page had no statistically significant impact on either revenue or conversion rate. In this situation, it would appear that the changes were not radical enough to make a major impact. It is also worth noting that at the end of a money bomb campaign such as this, the motivation of the visitors is often high enough that subtle changes will not have a major impact.

experiments.title: How an alternate call-to-action, positioned at the right time, affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an alternate call-to-action impact the visitor’s likelihood to give a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 10/28/2015
With an upcoming radio membership campaign, we wanted to find a way to get more out of the traffic being driven to the landing page. We had the idea of offering a free subscription to the Morning Bell when people showed the intent to leave the page.  The hypothesis is that this would not interfere with the giving process but would still allow us to get capture visitor information for those who decided not to give.
We decided to test this hypothesis to ensure that A) we did not impact donor conversion and B) the offer was strong enough that people would give their email address.
experiments.keyLearnings: We knew that by offering the opportunity for a visitor to give their email address would acquire more emails than not even giving that option. What we learned in this experiment was that there was no significant impact on donor conversion by having the secondary call to action.  We subsequently rolled out the exit intent offer to 100% of the traffic.

experiments.title: How the landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another.
Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as “approaching validity.” This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How the landing page imagery affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the use of imagery help boost donor conversion on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2015 - 08/31/2015
In a previous experiment, we had validated the messaging approach to use for a money bomb campaign. Because it involved a physical building, we felt this campaign would lend itself better to images to help convey the message. This is a different approach than we normally take with money bombs so we wanted to be sure to validate the impact these images had on the overall campaign performance
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the images to the page created no significant lift to either donor conversion or average gift. They made the page more visually appealing but this didn’t impact the donors’ likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How limiting copy length affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which copy treatment will produce the highest conversion rate during a money bomb campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 06/24/2015
During the 2015 fiscal year end money bomb campaign, we decided to run a test at the start of the campaign to see if the campaign theme needed added copy to increase the strength of the value proposition. Our goal was to validate this experiment so that we would have an optimized donation form by the end of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found no statistical improvement in the short or long copy treatment on the donation form. It would seem the length of the copy was not as important as the concept itself. Both treatments adequately laid out the need and the reason to give which is what produced no significant difference between the two.

experiments.title: How adding a Donate Now button at the top affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a donate button at the top of the page for returning visitors increase their likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 07/01/2015
For their fiscal year end campaign, Hillsdale College wanted to find a way to make it easier for their donors to make a gift.  The landing page being used contained a large amount of copy in order to fully communicate the college’s unique value proposition however this copy also added friction to the page.
We had a hypothesis that if we could add a way for returning visitors to skip this copy, by placing a Donate Now button at the top, we could reduce the overall friction of the page.  The concern with this approach is that this may ultimately reduce the average gift of the donations since they are not seeing the full value proposition.
We decided to run an experiment targeting just return visitors to the page and testing two different copy versions of the button versus no button at all.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Showing the buttons to returning visitors did not make a significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue.
When digging deeper into the results, we found that only 2% to 5% of returning visitors even clicked on the buttons.  This may speak more to their location outside of the eye path of the page than their effectiveness at reducing friction for the donor.  It may be worth running this experiment again but placing the button more in the eye path of the visitors.

experiments.title: How the minimum gift amount affects donation form results
experiments.researchQuestion: Which minimum gift amount will result in the greatest amount of revenue and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2015 - 06/24/2015
For the Heritage Foundation, the minimum level for membership technically begins at $25. However, the renewal page for their members has a minimum donation amount of $2 which is often utilized by visitors.  In an effort to lift the average gift, we decided to implement a new page that would make the minimum gift amount $25 to match the membership levels.  When doing this, we want to be sure to monitor both donor conversion and overall revenue to ensure this new $25 minimum didn’t significantly impact the number of donors acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both revenue per visitor and conversion rate of the two different pages were nearly identical and neither achieved statistical validity. We learned from this test that, with regards to membership renewal, the minimum gift did not factor into most donors’ decision to give.

experiments.title: How the placement of elements to add urgency affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which placement of the countdown clock and thermometer of the donation goal will have the greatest impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2015 - 06/04/2015
Previous experiments had taught us the positive impact increasing urgency and progress toward a goal can have an a campaign. As part of a moneybomb campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine if the placement of the visual elements designed to promote these two factors would impact the donor conversion.
For the campaign, we designed two landing pages: one with a thermometer at the top and a countdown clock at the bottom and another with these two visual elements flipped.
experiments.keyLearnings: Regardless of whether the countdown clock or thermometer was displayed at the top of the page, the pages had nearly identical conversion rate. From past experiments, we know both of these elements will boost campaign performance. What we learned here is that their placement is less important than simply being included in the page.

experiments.title: The impact of the messaging approach for the same value proposition of Imprimis
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to donors, advancing the principles of liberty or helping to distribute Imprimis to more Americans?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2015 - 04/30/2015
In this first experiment for the newly created Imprimis donation page, we tried to find a which approach to the core value proposition (advancing the principles of liberty) would be most appealing to the donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant different between either of the treatments.  In the end, both were promoting the core value that Imprimis embodies which is promoting the knowledge of American liberties. Both treatments approached it from different angles but in the end, the value propositions were not different enough to have a significant difference between the two.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.

experiments.title: How taking a softer call to action affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call-to-action within the email will drive more traffic to the website which will, in turn, convert a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/27/2014
As it was getting closer to the end of the year, Heritage wanted to test a messaging approach with their fifth email in their year end campaign. Toward the end of urgency-based marketing campaigns, it is common practice to make a direct ask in the email.  The question was how early can that direct ask be made and have it still be effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer language in the email increased the number of visitors by 41% but it actually converted fewer total donors in the test.  At this stage in the campaign, there does not seem to be any impact to response rate by utilizing a softer call to action.

experiments.title: How a shift in copy tone affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: An email talking about how Heritage impacts the constituents’ family life will drive more clicks than one talking about how they stand for the constituents’ principles.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
In the fourth email in the Heritage year end campaign, we tested what messaging point (principles or family) would drive the most traffic to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two treatments spoke to different prospect level value propositions, the copy did not seem to be different enough to see any significant impact on click throughs or donations.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action to “Stand with Heritage” will increase the click through rate which will increase the number of donors that convert on the page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2014 - 12/22/2014
On the third email in Heritage’s year end campaign, we ran a test asking constituents to either “Stand with Heritage” or “Make a Donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Even though the softer call-to-action drove 436% more visitors to the landing page, the traffic was not motivated enough to have a significant number of those visitors convert to donors.  On offers like this in the future, we may consider special messaging or landing page to help continue them down the donor funnel.

experiments.title: How removing Challenge Gift language affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will not including a challenge gift with the campaign have minimal impact on the fundraising campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/28/2014
As part of their year end campaign, the Heritage Foundation secured multiple gifts from major donors to serve as challenge gifts to their members. It has always been said that Challenge Gifts can increase conversion rate and average gifts but we have never known how much the increase is. We decided to test this well-known tactic to determine how much impact a Challenge Gift can have on a fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight increase in conversion rate for those that received the version with language about the challenge gift but it did not reach significance with sample size or lift. This would suggest that the challenge gift may not have the level of impact that best practices tells us it has. However, this test would need to be run with a larger segment to validate that conclusion.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.
experiments.keyLearnings: The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.

experiments.title: How speaking to donor passion points affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A call to action derived from Hillsdale’s financial independence from the federal government will get increased donors and average gifts when compared to a message involving their work teaching the principles of freedom.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/25/2014
As a way to thank their donors, Hillsdale produced a video close to Christmas. In addition to the stewardship aspect of the video, it offered a view of the Hillsdale campus and student body that donors rarely get to experience. To get more out of this video, we developed a landing page to send out to the full email file and included a contextualized donation form below.  This donation form offered an opportunity to test different aspects of Hillsdale’s value proposition to see what messages resonated best with their donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Given the nature of the passive ask and the inability to test the entire audience, we were unable to produce conclusive results. Further testing with more direct asks will be necessary to gain a better understanding how the donors view each of the elements of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How suggested donation amounts affect conversion rate and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: How will different giving arrays impact donor conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2015 - 12/21/2015
The gift array is a standard tactic used with most donation forms to limit decision friction and to offer the visitor suggested gift amounts.
Hillsdale College wanted to validate this best practice by putting its own default giving array, found on their main donation form, to the test. They created two treatments based upon experiments in the NextAfter Research Library. In one experiment, we increased the first suggested amount in the array which was able to increase the average gift. In another experiment, we found that not including an array was able to increase both donor conversion and average gift.
We set up two new treatments and split organic website traffic between the three versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the various giving arrays had no significant impact on either donor conversion and average gift. It is our hypothesis that the visitors coming to this general donation page were existing donor that did not need the suggested gift amounts. It did not hinder them to have it but, since they already were coming with an amount to give in their mind, the suggestions were largely ignored.

experiments.title: How we ensured a smooth transition from Kimbia to Stripe
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the new Stripe form have any impact on the donation conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2016 - 02/08/2016
When The Heritage Foundation decided to move their payment processing and form generation from Sage/Kimbia to Stripe, we wanted to have a way to do a “soft-launch” that would ensure a successful roll out. Specifically, we wanted to be able to test the form to ensure there were no unintended bugs and that the form did not possess some unseen factors that would have hurt the donor conversion rate.
We decided to do a split-test between three different donation pages (the main donate page, the renewal page, and the Join Heritage page). We tested these pages using a Kimbia form and a Stripe form and split traffic equally between them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment resulted in no significant impact in either direction. In this situation, that is actually what we were hoping for. We wanted to ensure that Stripe didn’t negatively impact the conversion rate. While a lift would been nice, since the forms were nearly identical, we didn’t expect that to actually happen.

experiments.title: How the placement of an impact statement affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What copy treatment will produce the highest conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/25/2015 - 06/30/2015
In Hillsdale’s fiscal year end campaign, we were able to utilize a matching gift as a way to inspire donors to give more. The grant was talked about in in the copy above the donation form but we also wanted to find a way to incorporate it into the form itself.
As a result, we created a treatment that added an impact statement to the donation form which reminded a donor how their gift would be worth due to the matching grant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test proved to be inconclusive. After further reflection, we would hypothesize that the impact statement appeared after the donor had already made their decision as to how much money to donate.  As a result, it didn’t really impact their decision on how much and whether to give.
To make better use of the matching grant in future campaigns, the impact language needs to be higher up on the form so it affects the donors’ decision prior to selecting a gift amount.
